topics.title: Awareness on IS Codes - IS 732
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Awareness on IS Codes - IS 732

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an IS Code?

An **Indian Standard (IS)** is a technical document issued by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** — the national standards body of India, established under the **BIS Act, 2016** (formerly under the BIS Act, 1987). Every IS code is a formally published consensus standard that codifies design rules, safety practices, testing methods, material specifications, and installation practices for a specific engineering discipline.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** is the sole national body responsible for the harmonious development of standardization, marking, and quality certification of goods. It is the Indian counterpart of the British BSI, German DIN, or American ANSI.

## 1.2 What is IS 732?

**IS 732:1989** — *Code of Practice for Electrical Wiring Installations (System Voltage not exceeding 650 V, including 250 V)* — is the **principal Indian Standard governing low-voltage electrical wiring** in residential, commercial, and industrial premises.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 732 is to an electrical engineer what the National Building Code (NBC) is to a civil engineer** — it is the foundational, mandatory reference for *all* low-voltage electrical installation work in India.

The current edition is **IS 732:1989 (Reaffirmed 2019)**, with **Amendment No. 1 (2002)**, **Amendment No. 2 (2004)**, and **Amendment No. 3 (2007)** incorporated. The 1989 edition superseded the older 1963 version and harmonized Indian practice with **IEC 60364** — the international wiring rules.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Driving Rulebook"

Imagine you have just learned to drive. Before you can legally drive on Indian roads, you must follow the **Indian Motor Vehicles Act** and the **road sign system**. The Act tells you *which side to drive on*, *speed limits*, *when to give way*, and *what protective gear to wear*. You don't invent these rules yourself; engineers and safety experts studied accidents for decades and codified the safest practices.

**IS 732 plays the exact same role for an electrical wiring installer.** It tells the engineer:

- *Which type of cable* to use in a given environment (conduit, armoured, flexible)
- *How to size* the conductor (current-carrying capacity, derating)
- *Where to place* protective devices (MCBs, ELCBs, fuses)
- *How to earth* the installation safely
- *What clearances* to maintain between wiring and other services
- *How to test* the finished installation before energizing

Just as ignoring the Motor Vehicles Act causes accidents and legal liability, **ignoring IS 732 causes electric shocks, fires, equipment damage, and legal non-compliance** — and no insurance company will honour a claim on an installation that does not follow the code.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** This is an *awareness* topic — students are not expected to memorize every clause number. They must understand the **scope**, **philosophy**, **key provisions**, and **relationship to other IS codes** relevant to electrical system design and estimation.

## 1.4 Scope of IS 732

The standard applies to **electrical wiring installations in buildings and premises** for:

| Parameter | Specification |
|---|---|
| Nominal system voltage | **Not exceeding 650 V** (including **250 V** single-phase and **415 V** three-phase) |
| Frequency | **50 Hz** AC, and DC systems |
| Premises covered | Residential, commercial, industrial, institutional, agricultural |
| Exclusions | Heavy industrial HT installations, traction, ship wiring, aircraft |

## 1.5 Why IS 732 Matters in Design & Estimation

When a student of **Electrical System Design and Estimation (PCEET602)** prepares a bill of quantities (BOQ), selects cable sizes, or estimates labour, the **basis of every line item must trace back to IS 732 (or its referenced companion codes)**. An estimator who does not know the code will:

- Underestimate conduit length by ignoring looping and junction-box rules
- Miss mandatory protective-device provisions
- Use wrong cable grades for the ambient conditions
- Fail to include earthing material quantities

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU 2024:** Module 1 lays the *standards foundation*. Modules 2–5 build the actual design and estimation methodology **on top of** IS 732 and its family of codes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Standardization Ecosystem in Indian Electrical Engineering

Before diving into IS 732, a clear understanding of the **standards hierarchy** is essential. Indian electrical engineering practice is governed by a layered set of documents:

| Layer | Document | Issuing Body | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 — Statute | Electricity Act, 2003 | Parliament of India | Legal framework |
| 2 — Regulation | Central Electricity Authority (CEA) Regulations, 2010 (Measures relating to Safety & Electric Supply) | CEA | Statutory safety rules |
| 3 — Code of Practice | **IS 732**, IS 3043, IS 5216, etc. | BIS | Detailed engineering rules |
| 4 — Product Standard | IS 694 (PVC cables), IS 1554 (armoured), IS 8828 (MCB) | BIS | Equipment specifications |
| 5 — Installation Rule | IE Rules 1956 (amended 2010) | Govt. of India | Legal wiring rules |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy reminder:** *IS codes are advisory in nature unless adopted by statute.* However, **IS 732 is given legal teeth** because the **Central Electricity Authority (CEA) Regulations 2010**, the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016, Part 8)**, and most **State Electricity Licensing Boards** explicitly reference and adopt it.

## 2.2 The Five Pillars of IS 732

IS 732 organizes its technical content around **five fundamental design objectives**. Every clause ultimately serves one or more of these:

1. **Safety of Life** — protection against electric shock and fire
2. **Safety of Property** — protection of the building and equipment
3. **Functional Adequacy** — the installation must *work* reliably
4. **Maintainability** — easy inspection, testing, and repair
5. **Future Expansion** — provision for additional load (typically **20–25% spare capacity**)

## 2.3 The Fundamental Rules of IS 732 (Cl. 3)

The code opens with **seven "Fundamental Rules"** that an installation *must* satisfy. These are the philosophical bedrock:

| Rule No. | Principle (simplified) |
|---|---|
| 1 | All equipment shall be of **good construction and proper material**, free from defects |
| 2 | Conductors, accessories, and apparatus shall be **of adequate rating** for the load and fault conditions |
| 3 | All joints and connections shall be **mechanically and electrically sound** |
| 4 | Every installation shall be provided with **means of isolation** (switches/MCBs) to cut off voltage |
| 5 | Adequate **protective devices** (fuses, MCBs) shall be provided to clear overcurrent and short-circuit faults |
| 6 | Efficient **earthing** shall be provided for non-current-carrying metal parts |
| 7 | The installation shall be **tested before being put into service** and at regular intervals |

> [!NOTE]
> **For examinations:** The "Fundamental Rules" section is a high-yield, frequently asked area. Memorizing the *principle* (not the exact wording) of each of the seven rules is enough for a 3-mark answer.

## 2.4 Key Technical Provisions of IS 732 (Major Clauses)

### 2.4.1 Classification of Voltage (Cl. 4)

IS 732 recognizes three voltage bands:

| Classification | Voltage Range | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Extra-Low Voltage (ELV)** | Not exceeding **50 V AC** or **120 V DC** | Telephone, bell, ELV lighting |
| **Low Voltage (LV)** | Exceeding ELV but **not exceeding 250 V AC** | Domestic single-phase 230 V |
| **Medium Voltage (MV)** | Exceeding 250 V but **not exceeding 650 V AC** | Industrial 415 V three-phase |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **230 V single-phase / 415 V three-phase** Indian supply falls under **Low / Medium Voltage** as per IS 732, with the entire installation designed for these nominal levels.

### 2.4.2 Permissible Wiring Methods (Cl. 5 to 8)

IS 732 permits several wiring systems, each suited to specific environments:

| Method | Typical Use | Conductor Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Conduit wiring** (surface or concealed) | Residential, commercial | PVC insulated (1.1 kV grade) |
| **Armoured cable wiring** | Industrial, outdoor, underground | XLPE/PVC armoured |
| **Lead-sheathed wiring** | Damp locations (obsolete, rarely used) | Lead-sheathed |
| **Cable tray / ladder** | Industrial, large commercial | Armoured / flexible |
| **Busbar trunking** | Heavy current distribution | Copper/Al busbars |
| **Cleat wiring** | Temporary installations | Tough-rubber or PVC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern practice:** Conduit wiring (PVC conduit) is the most common in residences. **Armoured cable** is mandatory for industrial and outdoor runs. The estimator must know the **per-metre cost and labour-hours** of each method.

### 2.4.3 Conductor Sizing (Cl. 9 & Tables)

The selection of conductor cross-section is governed by:

1. **Current-carrying capacity** of the conductor (from IS 732 Tables — derived from **IS 3961**)
2. **Voltage-drop limitation** — typically **3%** for power circuits, **5%** combined (from supply terminal to the farthest load)
3. **Short-circuit withstand** — the cable must carry the prospective fault current until the protective device operates
4. **Derating factors** for grouping, ambient temperature, thermal insulation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Estimation tip:** When computing the cable BOQ, the estimator must add **looping lengths at switch boxes and junction boxes** (typically **0.5 m to 1.0 m extra per point**), per IS 732 conduit-fill recommendations.

### 2.4.4 Protection Against Electric Shock (Cl. 11)

IS 732 mandates **two independent protective measures**:

- **Basic Protection** (against direct contact): insulation, enclosures, barriers
- **Fault Protection** (against indirect contact): earthing, automatic disconnection, RCDs/ELCBs

A residual current device (**RCD/ELCB**) with a rated residual operating current **not exceeding 30 mA** is recommended for **all socket-outlet circuits** in residential premises (per IS 732 Cl. 11.3, and also mandated by NBC 2016).

### 2.4.5 Earthing (Cl. 12 + IS 3043)

IS 732 requires:

- **Two separate and distinct earth electrodes** for every installation
- Earth-electrode resistance **not exceeding 5 Ω** for domestic, **1 Ω** for large industrial
- Earth-continuity conductor (ECC) to be run **alongside every phase and neutral conductor** inside the same conduit

The detailed earthing design is governed by the companion code **IS 3043:2018 — Code of Practice for Earthing**.

### 2.4.6 Testing of Installation (Cl. 14)

Before energizing, every installation **shall be tested** for:

| Test | Acceptance Criterion |
|---|---|
| Insulation resistance | **≥ 1 MΩ** (500 V megger for LV) |
| Earth-electrode resistance | **≤ 5 Ω** (domestic) |
| Earth continuity | **≤ 1 Ω** end-to-end |
| Polarity check | All switches on phase line |
| Operation of protective devices | Trip within specified time |

## 2.5 Companion IS Codes Frequently Co-Referenced with IS 732

An estimator must be familiar with the *family* of IS codes that IS 732 invokes:

| IS Code | Title | Used For |
|---|---|---|
| **IS 3043:2018** | Code of Practice for Earthing | Earth-electrode design |
| **IS 694:2010** | PVC insulated cables (up to 1.1 kV) | House-wire specification |
| **IS 1554 (Pts 1 & 2)** | PVC insulated (heavy duty) armoured cables | LT power cables |
| **IS 7098 (Pts 1 & 2)** | XLPE insulated cables | Industrial cables |
| **IS 8828** | MCBs for LV installations | Circuit protection |
| **IS 5216 (Pt 1)** | Guide for safety procedures | Work permits |
| **IS 3961** | Current-carrying capacity of cables | Cable sizing |
| **IS 2309** | Lightning protection | Building surge protection |
| **IS 1646** | Code of practice for fire safety | Coordination with fire code |
| **IS 4237** | Switchgear for domestic purposes | DB and MCB selection |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the single most important revision sheet for this topic. Memorize the *logic* and the *limits*.

| Item | Standard Value | IS Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Nominal LV supply | **230 V single-phase, 415 V three-phase, 50 Hz** | IS 732 Cl. 4 |
| Voltage drop limit (power) | **3%** of declared voltage | IS 732 Cl. 9.4 |
| Voltage drop limit (lighting) | **5%** combined (3% + 2%) | IS 732 Cl. 9.4 |
| Earth-electrode resistance (residential) | **≤ 5 Ω** | IS 3043 / IS 732 |
| Earth-electrode resistance (industrial) | **≤ 1 Ω** | IS 3043 |
| Insulation resistance (LV) | **≥ 1 MΩ** | IS 732 Cl. 14 |
| RCD sensitivity (sockets) | **30 mA** | IS 732 Cl. 11.3 |
| Spare capacity (future load) | **20–25%** | IS 732 guidance |
| Maximum no. of cables in conduit | **4 to 6** (derated) | IS 732 Cl. 7 |
| Conduit loop length per point | **0.5 to 1.0 m extra** | IS 732 Cl. 7 |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Application of IS 732 in Design & Estimation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because this is a *code-of-practice* topic (not a derivation-heavy physics topic), the "analytical content" in this section is framed as **worked-out decision procedures** that an estimator must perform *step-by-step* on any real project. These are the exact reasoning chains that fetch full marks in a 14-mark KTU question.

## 3.1 Application Procedure 1 — Selecting the Wiring Method for a Premise

**Scenario:** An estimator receives a project brief — *"Design the wiring system for a new 2-storey residential building, plus an attached single-storey workshop."*

### Step 1 — Read the building use and environment

- **2-storey residential** = dry, indoor, aesthetic concern → use **concealed conduit (PVC)** in walls and ceilings.
- **Workshop** = dusty, possibly damp, mechanical abuse → use **surface conduit (PVC) or armoured cable on tray**.

### Step 2 — Match to IS 732 Cl. 5–8 (permitted methods)

IS 732 permits conduit and armoured-cable methods. Both are admissible.

### Step 3 — Cross-check with IS 732 Cl. 5.1.1

The wiring system must suit the **environmental conditions**:

- *Ambient temperature* — PVC conduit is rated up to **70 °C conductor operating temperature**; beyond that, mineral-insulated or XLPE is required.
- *Presence of water* — IP-rated accessories required.
- *Mechanical risk* — armoured cable for floor or outdoor runs.

### Step 4 — Cross-check with **NBC 2016, Part 8 (Building Services, Section 2 — Electrical)**

NBC mandates concealed conduit for residential. Surface conduit is not prohibited but discouraged in living spaces.

### Step 5 — Finalize and record in estimate

Decision: **Concealed PVC conduit for residence; surface PVC conduit + armoured cable for workshop.**

This decision, **with code clauses quoted**, must be written in the design basis report. The estimator's BOQ will reflect the different per-metre rates.

## 3.2 Application Procedure 2 — Sizing a Conductor per IS 732

**Scenario:** A single-phase 230 V, 50 Hz feeder is to be run for a load of **4 kW** at a distance of **35 m**, in **PVC conduit on wall**, with **2 other circuits** sharing the same conduit, in an **ambient temperature of 40 °C**.

### Step 1 — Compute full-load current

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{FL} &= \frac{P}{V \times \cos\phi} \\
       &= \frac{4000}{230 \times 0.85} \\
       &= 20.47 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply diversity (if applicable)

For continuous load, IS 732 Cl. 9.3 specifies that the conductor be rated for **125% of the full-load current** for loads running continuously for more than **3 hours**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{design} &= 1.25 \times I_{FL} \\
           &= 1.25 \times 20.47 \\
           &= 25.59 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Look up current-carrying capacity in IS 732 Table (or IS 3961, Pt 2)

For **PVC-insulated copper**, **2-core + ECC, 1.1 kV grade, in conduit**:

- **4 sq mm Cu** → **27 A** (in conduit, single circuit, 30 °C ambient)
- **6 sq mm Cu** → **34 A**

### Step 4 — Apply derating factors (IS 732 Cl. 9.5)

- **Grouping factor** (3 circuits grouped): **0.70**
- **Ambient temperature factor** (40 °C, PVC insulation): **0.87**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{derated} &= 27 \times 0.70 \times 0.87 \\
            &= 16.44 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **less than 25.59 A** → **4 sq mm is inadequate**.

### Step 5 — Re-select with 6 sq mm

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{derated, 6sq} &= 34 \times 0.70 \times 0.87 \\
                 &= 20.71 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

Still slightly less than 25.59 A. Try **10 sq mm**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{derated, 10sq} &= 46 \times 0.70 \times 0.87 \\
                  &= 28.02 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**10 sq mm Cu** satisfies the design current.

### Step 6 — Voltage-drop check (IS 732 Cl. 9.4)

For single-phase 230 V, **3% limit = 6.9 V**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{drop} &= \frac{2 \times I \times L \times \rho}{A} \\
         &= \frac{2 \times 20.47 \times 35 \times 0.0172}{10} \\
         &= 2.46 \text{ V} \quad (\text{at full load, DC resistance approx.})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\% V_{drop} = \frac{2.46}{230} \times 100 = 1.07\% \quad \text{(well within 3\%)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Voltage drop = 1.07% < 3%** → the 10 sq mm selection is confirmed. If the design distance were **80 m**, voltage drop would become the binding constraint, not current-carrying capacity — the estimator would have to upsize the cable further.

### Step 7 — Document the result

Selected conductor: **10 sq mm PVC-insulated copper, 2-core + ECC, 1.1 kV grade**, in 25 mm PVC conduit, as per IS 732 Cl. 9 and IS 694.

## 3.3 Application Procedure 3 — Earthing Design as per IS 3043 (referred by IS 732)

**Scenario:** A small workshop with a 25 kVA three-phase 415 V transformer.

### Step 1 — Decide number of earth electrodes

IS 3043 + IS 732 Cl. 12 → **two distinct earth electrodes**:

- **Equipment earth** (for transformer body, DB enclosure)
- **System earth** (for neutral point of the transformer)

### Step 2 — Size the earth conductor (IS 3043 Table)

For a 25 kVA transformer (full-load current ≈ 35 A on LV side):

- **Earthing conductor (Cu)**: **6 sq mm** (minimum)
- **Earth lead (Cu)**: **16 sq mm** (from electrode to first point)

### Step 3 — Specify earth electrode

**GI pipe electrode, 50 mm nominal bore, 3.0 m long**, driven vertically with alternate layers of **charcoal and salt** (for low-resistivity soil).

### Step 4 — Test and verify

Earth-electrode resistance after driving: **target ≤ 5 Ω** (per IS 3043 Cl. 9). If higher, add water/salt or use multiple electrodes in parallel.

## 3.4 Application Procedure 4 — Mandatory Provision Checklist for an Estimate

Before signing off on a residential wiring estimate, the estimator must confirm the following **IS 732 items are present in the BOQ**:

| Item | Quantity Basis | IS Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Main isolator / MCB (at intake) | **1 no.** per service connection | IS 732 Cl. 3.4 |
| ELCB / RCD (30 mA) for sockets | **1 no. per DB** | IS 732 Cl. 11.3 |
| Earth electrodes | **2 nos.** (equipment + system) | IS 732 Cl. 12 |
| Earth-continuity conductor | **Along every sub-circuit** | IS 3043 |
| Loop length at each switch box | **0.5 to 1.0 m extra per point** | IS 732 Cl. 7 |
| Conduit fill | **≤ 40%** of internal cross-section | IS 732 Cl. 7.3 |
| Spare capacity in DB | **20–25% spare ways** | IS 732 guidance |
| Testing instruments | **1 set (megger + earth-tester + multimeter)** | IS 732 Cl. 14 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Hierarchy of Electrical Standards in India

This diagram shows the **standards pyramid** that IS 732 sits within.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STATUTE: Electricity Act 2003] --> B[REGULATION: CEA Safety Regulations 2010]
    B --> C[CODE OF PRACTICE: IS 732 and family]
    C --> D[PRODUCT STANDARD: IS 694, IS 1554, IS 8828]
    D --> E[INSTALLATION RULE: IE Rules 1956 amended 2010]
    C --> F[NBC 2016 Part 8 Section 2 Electrical]
    F --> G[STATE ELECTRICITY BOARD RULES]
    C --> H[ESTIMATION AND DESIGN: PCEET602 Course Content]
    H --> I[BOQ and Site Execution]
    I --> J[TESTING and COMMISSIONING per IS 732 Cl 14]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Internal Structure of IS 732

A **block-level functional architecture** of how the standard's clauses are organized.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[IS 732 1989 Reaffirmed 2019] --> L[Section 1 Scope and Definitions]
    K --> M[Section 2 General Requirements and Fundamental Rules]
    K --> N[Section 3 Classification of Voltages]
    K --> O[Section 4 to 8 Wiring Methods and Installation]
    K --> P[Section 9 Conductor Sizing and Voltage Drop]
    K --> Q[Section 10 Protective Devices Fuses MCBs]
    K --> R[Section 11 Protection Against Electric Shock]
    K --> S[Section 12 Earthing cross reference to IS 3043]
    K --> T[Section 13 Special Locations Bath Outdoors]
    K --> U[Section 14 Testing of Installation]
    O --> O1[Conduit Conduit Type Conduit Fill]
    O --> O2[Armoured Cable Tray and Ladder]
    P --> P1[Overcurrent Coordination]
    P --> P2[Short Circuit Withstand]
    R --> R1[Basic Protection Insulation Enclosures]
    R --> R2[Fault Protection Earthing RCD]
    T --> T1[Insulation Resistance Test]
    T --> T2[Earth Electrode Resistance Test]
    T --> T3[Polarity and Continuity Test]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Decision Tree for Selecting Wiring Method

This is the *practical* flow an estimator follows when choosing a wiring system for a premise.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Identify Premise Type] --> B{Is premise residential}
    B -- YES --> C{Is aesthetic priority}
    C -- YES --> D[Use Concealed PVC Conduit]
    C -- NO --> E[Use Surface PVC Conduit]
    B -- NO --> F{Is premise industrial outdoor}
    F -- YES --> G[Use Armoured Cable on Tray or in Ground]
    F -- NO --> H{Is premise damp wet}
    H -- YES --> I[Use Weatherproof IP 55 Conduit with PVC Cable]
    H -- NO --> J{Is premise commercial large floor area}
    J -- YES --> K[Use Busbar Trunking or Cable Tray with Armoured]
    J -- NO --> L[Use Surface PVC Conduit]
    D --> M[Verify with IS 732 Cl 5 to 8]
    E --> M
    G --> M
    I --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Document in Design Basis Report]
    N --> O[Prepare BOQ per IS 732]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Companion IS Codes Linked to IS 732

This shows the **family relationship** between IS 732 and its most-frequently-referenced companion codes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IS 732 Code of Practice for Electrical Wiring] --> B[IS 3043 Code of Practice for Earthing]
    A --> C[IS 694 PVC Insulated Cables 1.1 kV]
    A --> D[IS 1554 Armoured Cables 1.1 kV]
    A --> E[IS 7098 XLPE Cables]
    A --> F[IS 8828 Miniature Circuit Breakers]
    A --> G[IS 3961 Current Ratings of Cables]
    A --> H[IS 2309 Lightning Protection]
    A --> I[IS 5216 Safety Procedures]
    A --> J[IS 1646 Fire Safety]
    A --> K[IS 4237 Domestic Switchgear]
    B --> L[Design Earth Electrode Resistance]
    C --> M[Select Conductor for Sub Circuits]
    D --> N[Select Conductor for Feeders]
    F --> O[Select Protection in DB]
    H --> P[Protect Building from Surges]
```

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Workflow: From Code to Commissioned Installation

This is the **end-to-end process flow** showing where IS 732 enters at every stage of an electrical project.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Brief from Client] --> B[Consult IS 732 for Scope and Voltage Class]
    B --> C[Select Wiring Method per IS 732 Cl 5 to 8]
    C --> D[Calculate Loads and Diversity]
    D --> E[Sizing Conductors per IS 732 Cl 9 and IS 3961]
    E --> F[Select Protective Devices per IS 732 Cl 10 and IS 8828]
    F --> G[Design Earthing per IS 732 Cl 12 and IS 3043]
    G --> H[Layout Drawing Single Line and Plan]
    H --> I[Prepare BOQ using IS standard item descriptions]
    I --> J[Tendering and Vendor Selection]
    J --> K[Site Execution under Supervision]
    K --> L[Testing per IS 732 Cl 14]
    L --> M{All Tests Pass}
    M -- YES --> N[Energize and Hand Over]
    M -- NO --> O[Rectify Defects and Re Test]
    O --> L
```
