# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define IS 732. State its scope and the maximum voltage it covers. **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 (modeled)]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** IS 732 is the Indian Standard **"Code of Practice for Electrical Wiring Installations"**, first published in 1963 and revised in 1989, with subsequent amendments. It is issued by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**.

**Scope:** It applies to electrical wiring installations in **residential, commercial, industrial, agricultural, and institutional premises** for **alternating current systems up to and including 650 V**, and for **DC systems up to and including 250 V**, at a frequency of **50 Hz**.

**Issued by:** Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS), Government of India.

**Current edition:** IS 732:1989, reaffirmed in 2019, with Amendments 1 (2002), 2 (2004), and 3 (2007).

---

### Question 2: List the *Fundamental Rules* of IS 732. Explain the role of earthing. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 (modeled)]**

**Model Answer:**

The Fundamental Rules of IS 732 (Cl. 3) require every installation to have:

1. Equipment of good construction and proper material, free from defects
2. Conductors and accessories of adequate current and voltage rating
3. Sound mechanical and electrical joints
4. Means of isolation to cut off voltage
5. Adequate protective devices (fuses/MCBs)
6. Efficient **earthing** of all non-current-carrying metal parts
7. Testing of the completed installation before being put into service

**Role of Earthing (Cl. 12):** Earthing connects all metallic enclosures of equipment (DBs, switchboards, appliance bodies, conduit) to earth via a low-resistance path. Its purpose is to ensure that if a live conductor accidentally touches a metal part, the resulting **fault current flows to earth and trips the protective device**, preventing dangerous touch-voltages that could cause electrocution. IS 732 mandates **two separate earth electrodes** per installation and limits the earth-electrode resistance to **≤ 5 Ω** for residential and **≤ 1 Ω** for industrial premises.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU ESE (End Semester Exam), each module typically carries a 14-mark question with **internal choice** — students answer *either* Question A *or* Question B. Both choices are presented below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Wiring System Selection and Conductor Sizing per IS 732

A small **workshop with one 3-phase 415 V, 50 Hz motor of 7.5 kW** is to be supplied through a **surface PVC conduit** run along a wall, distance **25 m**, with **2 other sub-circuits** sharing the same conduit, in an **ambient temperature of 40 °C**. Take motor efficiency = 0.88, power factor = 0.85. The supply is from a 25 kVA distribution transformer.

**Part (a)** [7 marks] Select the wiring method and decide the number and size of conductors as per IS 732.

**Part (b)** [7 marks] Check the **voltage-drop** and **earthing** provisions, and list the mandatory tests to be done before commissioning.

#### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Selection of Wiring Method and Conductor

**Step 1 — Decide wiring method (IS 732 Cl. 5–8):**

Workshop is an **industrial, dry, dust-prone** premise. Permitted options: *armoured cable on tray* or *surface PVC conduit*. Cost-effective choice for a single motor: **3-core XLPE insulated armoured cable on cable tray**, OR alternatively **3-core PVC insulated cable in surface conduit**. For this answer, we select the **surface PVC conduit** method (as given).

**Step 2 — Compute full-load current of motor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{FL} &= \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \times V \times \eta \times \cos\phi} \\
       &= \frac{7500}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.88 \times 0.85} \\
       &= \frac{7500}{1.732 \times 415 \times 0.88 \times 0.85} \\
       &= \frac{7500}{537.07} \\
       &= 13.97 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply continuous-load factor (1.25 per IS 732 Cl. 9.3):**

$$
I_{design} = 1.25 \times 13.97 = 17.46 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 4 — Select conductor size from IS 3961 / IS 732 Table:**

For **3-core XLPE armoured Cu, 1.1 kV grade, in conduit at 30 °C ambient**:

- **4 sq mm** → 32 A
- **6 sq mm** → 41 A

A 4 sq mm conductor has ample current margin but we must also apply derating.

**Step 5 — Apply derating factors:**

- **Grouping (3 circuits in conduit)** → factor = **0.70**
- **Ambient 40 °C (XLPE insulation)** → factor = **0.94**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{derated} &= 32 \times 0.70 \times 0.94 \\
            &= 21.06 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since 21.06 A > 17.46 A → **4 sq mm XLPE Cu, 3-core, is acceptable**.

> [!NOTE]
> If **PVC insulated** conductors had been used, ambient factor at 40 °C is only 0.87, and the derated current of 4 sq mm would drop to 19.49 A, which is still adequate. But the standard motor-feeder choice is **XLPE armoured** for industrial use.

#### Part (b) — Voltage Drop, Earthing, and Tests

**Step 1 — Voltage drop check (IS 732 Cl. 9.4):**

For 3-phase 415 V, allowed voltage drop = 5% (motor feeder) = **20.75 V**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{drop} &= \sqrt{3} \times I \times L \times \frac{\rho}{A} \\
         &= 1.732 \times 13.97 \times 25 \times \frac{0.0172}{4} \\
         &= 1.732 \times 13.97 \times 25 \times 0.0043 \\
         &= 2.60 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\% V_{drop} = \frac{2.60}{415} \times 100 = 0.63\% \quad \ll 5\% \text{ limit}
$$

**Voltage drop is well within limit.** Conductor is sized by *current-carrying capacity* and *derating*, not voltage drop, in this case.

**Step 2 — Earthing (IS 732 Cl. 12 + IS 3043):**

Provide **two distinct GI pipe earth electrodes** (50 mm NB, 3.0 m long), one for **equipment body** and one for **system neutral**. Earth-continuity conductor: **6 sq mm Cu** along the motor feeder. Earth-electrode resistance target: **≤ 5 Ω**.

**Step 3 — Mandatory tests (IS 732 Cl. 14):**

1. **Insulation resistance test** — between each conductor and earth (≥ 1 MΩ using 500 V megger)
2. **Earth-electrode resistance test** — using earth-tester (≤ 5 Ω)
3. **Earth-continuity test** — end-to-end (≤ 1 Ω)
4. **Polarity test** — verify all switches are on phase line
5. **Phase-sequence test** — for 3-phase motor
6. **Operation of MCB / fuse** — verify tripping at overcurrent

**Final Answer (Part b):** Voltage drop = 0.63% (well within IS 732 limit of 5%). Two GI-pipe earth electrodes to be provided, ECC of 6 sq mm Cu. Six mandatory tests to be done per Cl. 14 before commissioning.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Awareness on IS 732 — Scope, Companion Codes, and Application in an Estimate

**Part (a)** [7 marks] Explain the **scope, structure, and significance** of IS 732. List at least **six companion IS codes** used along with IS 732 in an electrical installation project, stating one use of each.

**Part (b)** [7 marks] For a new **2-BHK residential flat (60 sq m, 230 V single-phase)**, prepare the **preliminary BOQ line items** that an estimator must include, citing the **IS 732 clause** that mandates each item. Assume the flat has 4 light points, 3 fan points, 6 socket outlets, 1 AC point, and 1 kitchen geyser point.

#### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Scope, Structure, and Significance of IS 732

**Scope (Cl. 1):** IS 732 applies to *electrical wiring installations* in **residential, commercial, industrial, agricultural, and institutional** premises, for AC systems **up to 650 V** and DC systems **up to 250 V**, at **50 Hz**. It covers the *design, selection, erection, and testing* of LV wiring.

**Structure (Cl. 1–14):** IS 732 is organized as:

| Clause | Subject |
|---|---|
| 1–2 | Scope, Definitions, Fundamental Rules |
| 3 | General Requirements & Fundamental Rules |
| 4 | Classification of Voltages (ELV, LV, MV) |
| 5–8 | Wiring Methods and Installation (conduit, armoured, etc.) |
| 9 | Conductor Sizing and Voltage Drop |
| 10 | Protection against Overcurrent |
| 11 | Protection against Electric Shock |
| 12 | Earthing (cross-ref to IS 3043) |
| 13 | Special Locations (baths, outdoors, hazardous) |
| 14 | Testing of Installation |

**Significance:**
- **Legal:** Adopted by CEA Regulations 2010 and State Electricity Boards.
- **Safety:** Codifies life-and-property protection measures.
- **Uniformity:** Standardizes design across India.
- **Insurance:** Insurers require IS 732 compliance for fire/accident claims.
- **Estimation:** Provides the **basis for every line item** in an electrical BOQ.

**Six Companion IS Codes:**

| IS Code | Title | One Use |
|---|---|---|
| **IS 3043:2018** | Code of Practice for Earthing | Earth-electrode sizing and resistance |
| **IS 694:2010** | PVC Insulated Cables (1.1 kV) | Sub-circuit wiring cable spec |
| **IS 1554 (Pt 1)** | PVC Armoured Cables | Main feeder / outdoor runs |
| **IS 7098 (Pt 1)** | XLPE Cables | Industrial feeders |
| **IS 8828:1996** | Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCBs) | Protection in DB |
| **IS 2309:2005** | Lightning Protection | Building surge arrestors |
| **IS 3961** | Current Ratings of Cables | Cable CCC tables |
| **IS 1646:2015** | Fire Safety | Coordination with NBC |

#### Part (b) — Preliminary BOQ for 2-BHK Flat

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The BOQ is prepared by **first listing every point and switchboard**, then **calculating cable and conduit lengths**, then **listing common items** (DB, MCBs, earthing, testing).

| Sl. | Item Description | Qty | Unit | IS 732 Clause / Basis |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5 A switch, flush type, 1-way | 4 | nos | Sub-circuit points (Cl. 5) |
| 2 | 5 A 3-pin socket outlet, flush type | 6 | nos | Socket outlet (Cl. 5) |
| 3 | Ceiling rose / fan regulator box | 3 | nos | Fan point (Cl. 5) |
| 4 | 15 A switch + 3-pin socket (AC) | 1 | set | Heavy-duty point (Cl. 5) |
| 5 | 20 A switch + 3-pin socket (geyser) | 1 | set | Heavy-duty point (Cl. 5) |
| 6 | PVC insulated Cu cable, 1.5 sq mm (sub-circuits) | ~110 | m | IS 694 + IS 732 Cl. 9 |
| 7 | PVC insulated Cu cable, 4 sq mm (AC) | ~30 | m | IS 732 Cl. 9 |
| 8 | PVC insulated Cu cable, 6 sq mm (geyser) | ~25 | m | IS 732 Cl. 9 |
| 9 | 20 mm PVC conduit (medium gauge) | ~120 | m | IS 732 Cl. 7 |
| 10 | Junction boxes (4-way) | 6 | nos | IS 732 Cl. 7.4 |
| 11 | Switch boxes (flush, 6-module) | 5 | nos | IS 732 Cl. 7 |
| 12 | Loop length @ 0.5 m per point (allowance) | 15 | m | IS 732 Cl. 7 (loops) |
| 13 | Distribution Board (SPN, 12-way with 25% spare) | 1 | no | IS 732 Cl. 10 |
| 14 | MCB 6 A (SPN) — light sub-circuits | 2 | nos | IS 8828 + Cl. 10 |
| 15 | MCB 16 A (SPN) — fan sub-circuit | 1 | no | IS 8828 + Cl. 10 |
| 16 | MCB 20 A (SPN) — socket sub-circuit + RCD 30 mA | 1 | no | IS 732 Cl. 11.3 |
| 17 | MCB 25 A (SPN) — AC | 1 | no | IS 8828 + Cl. 10 |
| 18 | MCB 32 A (SPN) — geyser | 1 | no | IS 8828 + Cl. 10 |
| 19 | Main isolator, 40 A, 2-pole | 1 | no | IS 732 Cl. 3.4 |
| 20 | Earth electrodes (GI pipe, 50 mm × 3.0 m) | 2 | nos | IS 732 Cl. 12 + IS 3043 |
| 21 | Earth-continuity Cu conductor, 6 sq mm | ~80 | m | IS 3043 |
| 22 | Charcoal and salt (per electrode) | 25 | kg | IS 3043 |
| 23 | Testing instruments (megger 500 V, earth tester) | 1 | set | IS 732 Cl. 14 |
| 24 | Testing labour and certification | 1 | job | IS 732 Cl. 14 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cable-length estimation rule (KTU 2024 — must memorize):**
> - Each switch point: **actual route + 0.5 m loop** at the switchboard
> - Each ceiling rose point: **actual route + 1.0 m loop** at the ceiling rose
> - Sub-circuit looped length: **wall-to-switch + switch-to-load + neutral returns**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU 2024 ESE — IS 732 questions:**
> 1. **Writing "IS 732 is a law"** — it is a *standard*, not a statute. Legal force comes only when adopted by CEA or the State Board.
> 2. **Forgetting the voltage limit** — IS 732 covers *only up to 650 V*. HT/MV installations (> 650 V) are governed by **IS 5216**, **CEA Regulations 2010**, and the **IE Rules 1956**.
> 3. **Confusing "ELCB" with "RCD"** — ELCB is the older voltage-operated device; the modern current-operated one is **RCCB/RCD**. KTU expects the modern terminology.
> 4. **Using a single earth electrode** — IS 732 mandates **two distinct electrodes** (equipment earth + system earth). One electrode is a common exam-time error.
> 5. **Omitting the "spare capacity"** — students often forget that **20–25% spare DB ways** is a code-recommended practice, and lose 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting to test** — the final step (testing per Cl. 14) is **mandatory**; an estimate that omits testing instruments and labour is incomplete.
> 7. **Wrong cable-length calculation** — students often quote the straight-line distance and forget the **vertical drops, looping, and conduit bends**. Use **+ 20–25% contingency** for concealed conduit runs.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **IS 732:1989** is the *Code of Practice for Electrical Wiring Installations* covering **up to 650 V AC / 250 V DC**, at **50 Hz**, in all types of premises.
- It is issued by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** and is **harmonized with IEC 60364**.
- **Seven Fundamental Rules** form the philosophical bedrock: good construction, adequate rating, sound joints, isolation, protection, earthing, testing.
- The code classifies voltage as **ELV (≤ 50 V AC)**, **LV (≤ 250 V AC)**, and **MV (≤ 650 V AC)**.
- **Permitted wiring methods:** conduit (surface/concealed), armoured cable, cable tray, busbar trunking, and (rarely today) lead-sheathed.
- **Conductor sizing** must satisfy four constraints: current-carrying capacity, **3% voltage drop** (power) / **5% combined** (lighting), short-circuit withstand, and **derating** for grouping, ambient temperature, and thermal insulation.
- **Earthing** is mandatory with **two separate electrodes**, target resistance **≤ 5 Ω** residential / **≤ 1 Ω** industrial. Detailed design uses **IS 3043**.
- **Protection against shock** requires both *basic* (insulation/enclosures) and *fault* (earthing + automatic disconnection) measures. **RCDs of 30 mA** are recommended for socket circuits.
- **Mandatory tests** before energizing: insulation resistance, earth-electrode resistance, earth continuity, polarity, operation of protective devices.
- **Companion IS codes** that the estimator must know: **IS 3043** (earthing), **IS 694** (house wire), **IS 1554** (armoured), **IS 7098** (XLPE), **IS 8828** (MCB), **IS 3961** (cable ratings), **IS 2309** (lightning), **IS 1646** (fire safety).
- **Estimating tip:** Always add **20–25% spare capacity** in the DB, **0.5–1.0 m loop** per point, and **20–25% contingency** in conduit-run lengths.
- **Legal status:** IS 732 is *advisory* by itself but **statutory** when adopted by the CEA Regulations 2010, NBC 2016 (Part 8), and the State Electricity Board rules. **Insurance companies require IS 732 compliance** for any claim related to electrical fire or shock.
- **Hierarchy of standards:** *Statute (Electricity Act 2003) → Regulation (CEA 2010) → Code of Practice (IS 732) → Product Standard (IS 694 etc.) → IE Rules 1956* — IS 732 is **layer 3** of this hierarchy.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory anchor for the exam:** *"IS 732 is the 50 Hz, 650 V rulebook for safe wiring — and every line in a BOQ must trace back to it."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IS 3043
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IS 3043

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 3043:1987 (Reaffirmed 2006 & 2019) — "Code of Practice for Earthing"**
> *Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)* — This is the **principal Indian Standard** that prescribes the **statutory and engineering practices for the earthing (grounding) of electrical installations**, including power generation, transmission, distribution substations, industrial premises, residential buildings, and consumer installations. It defines permissible earth-electrode resistance, soil-resistivity interpretation, electrode geometry, conductor sizing, and protection against **step and touch potentials**.

The standard fundamentally covers the following engineering domains:
1. **Equipment Earthing** — Connecting non-current carrying metallic parts to earth (e.g., transformer tank, motor frame, panel body).
2. **System Earthing** — Connecting the neutral of a power system to earth (e.g., star-point of a transformer secondary).
3. **Soil Resistivity Surveys** — Wenner four-probe and Schlumberger–Palmer methods.
4. **Earth Electrode Design** — Plate, pipe, rod, and strip electrodes.
5. **Earth Conductor Sizing** — Based on short-time fault current and duration.
6. **Potential Gradients** — Step and touch voltage safety criteria.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Overflow Drain of an Electrical Tank"**
> Imagine your house's water tank. The earth wire is like the **overflow drain pipe** — it provides a safe path for any unexpected, unwanted current (like a leak) to escape harmlessly into the ground. **IS 3043 is the design manual that tells electricians exactly how big that drain pipe should be, how deep it should go, and how clean the surrounding soil must be so the leakage current flows away quickly without shocking anyone.**

A more rigorous analogy: **Earth acts as a giant heat sink** (in electrical analogy). Just as a heat sink absorbs unwanted heat through a large surface area, an earth electrode absorbs unwanted fault current through a large surface contact with the soil. **IS 3043 defines the geometry of this "heat sink" for electricity.**

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Reference |
|-----------|---------------|-----------|
| Soil resistivity (ρ) — Wet organic soil | **10 Ω·m** | IS 3043 Annexure |
| Soil resistivity (ρ) — Moist soil | **100 Ω·m** | IS 3043 Annexure |
| Soil resistivity (ρ) — Dry soil | **1,000 Ω·m** | IS 3043 Annexure |
| Soil resistivity (ρ) — Bed rock | **10,000 Ω·m** | IS 3043 Annexure |
| Permissible earth resistance (HT substation) | **≤ 1 Ω** | IS 3043 / CEA |
| Permissible earth resistance (LT installation) | **≤ 5 Ω** | IS 3043 |
| Permissible body resistance (assumed) | **1,000 Ω** | IEEE/IEC convention |
| Standard fault duration | **0.5 s to 3 s** | IS 3043 Clause 9 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** IS 3043 forms the backbone of Module 1 along with IE Rules. Expect **direct 3-mark definition questions** and **14-mark numerical problems** on earth-electrode resistance, soil resistivity, and step/touch potentials.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Earthing is Required — The Six Engineering Reasons

1. **Safety of Personnel** — Prevents dangerous touch voltages during insulation failure (limits touch voltage to < **50 V** in dry conditions, **25 V** in wet conditions per IS 3043).
2. **Equipment Protection** — Diverts lightning and switching surges to earth; protects insulation of transformers, cables, and switchgear.
3. **System Stability** — Provides a stable reference potential (neutral earthing) and facilitates detection of earth faults by protective relays.
4. **Overvoltage Limitation** — Restricts transient overvoltages due to arcing grounds, resonance, and lightning.
5. **Fault Current Return Path** — Provides a low-impedance path for earth-fault current to operate protective devices (fuses, MCBs, relays).
6. **EMI / EMC Compliance** — Reduces electromagnetic interference by providing a stable reference plane.

### 2.2 Types of Earth Electrodes (IS 3043 Classification)

The standard recognizes **four geometric classes** of earth electrodes, each suited to a specific application and soil condition:

**A. Plate Electrode**
- Material: **Galvanized Iron (GI)** or **Copper**
- Size: GI — 60 cm × 60 cm × 6.3 mm thick; Copper — 60 cm × 60 cm × 3.15 mm thick
- Burial depth: Not less than **1.5 m** from ground level
- Use: Stations with rocky subsoil, hilly terrain

**B. Pipe Electrode**
- Material: **GI pipe (Class B)**, 40 mm nominal bore, 2.5 m to 3 m long
- Tapered at the bottom, perforations for moisture absorption
- Use: Most common for substation and building earthing

**C. Rod Electrode**
- Material: Solid GI rod, 12.5 mm to 25 mm diameter, 1 m to 3 m long
- Use: Urban installations, rocky soil, supplementary electrodes

**D. Strip / Conductor Electrode**
- Material: GI strip 25 mm × 1.6 mm or 25 mm × 4 mm
- Buried horizontally in trenches at depth 0.5 m to 1.0 m
- Use: Long stretches, earthing mesh of substations

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — IS 3043 (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the variable **ρ (rho) = soil resistivity in Ω·m**. All electrode resistance formulas scale linearly with ρ. Doubling ρ doubles R.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Validity / Conditions |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Resistance of **Plate** electrode | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{4r}$ | ρ = soil resistivity (Ω·m); r = equivalent radius (m) | r = a/2 for circular plate of radius a; r ≈ a/√π for square |
| 2 | Resistance of **Pipe** electrode | $R = \dfrac{100\, \rho}{276\, L}\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right)$ | L = length (m); d = diameter (m) | L typically 2.5 m – 3 m |
| 3 | Simplified pipe formula (decadic) | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right)$ | Same as above | Use natural log form for clarity |
| 4 | Resistance of **Rod** electrode | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$ | L = length (m); d = diameter (m) | Subtract 1 for end-effect |
| 5 | Resistance of **Horizontal Strip** | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{\pi L}\ln\left(\dfrac{2L}{w}\right)$ | L = length (m); w = width (m) | Buried at shallow depth; L ≫ w |
| 6 | Resistance of **n** electrodes in parallel (no interaction) | $R_n = \dfrac{R}{n}$ | R = resistance of single electrode | Ideal case; ignores mutual resistance |
| 7 | **Combined resistance** with interaction factor | $R_n = \dfrac{R}{n}\,(1 + \lambda\,\alpha)$ | λ = interaction factor (0.05–0.30); α = 0 for the most distant, 1 for adjacent | Practical KTU formula |
| 8 | **Step potential** | $E_{\text{step}} = I_g \cdot R_s$ | I_g = fault current (A); R_s = step resistance (Ω) | Defined for 1 m stride |
| 9 | **Touch potential** | $E_{\text{touch}} = I_g \cdot R_t$ | R_t = body + contact resistance | Critical at substation |
| 10 | **Earth conductor cross-section** (thermal) | $A = \dfrac{I}{\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{K^2 \cdot \beta \cdot 10^4}{t}\right)\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{T_2+234}{T_1+234}\right)}}$ | I = fault current (A); t = duration (s); β = 1 for Cu, 0.81 for Al | IS 3043 Clause 9 |
| 11 | **Permissible step potential** | $E_{\text{step,lim}} = 174 \sqrt{t}$ (≈ 174√t V) | t = fault clearance time (s) | Empirical formula (IEEE 80 inspired) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** The KTU board expects you to write the formula in **decadic-log (log₁₀) form** with factor **100/276 = 0.365** (when L is in metres and d in metres). Many students write **276 = 2πL/log₁₀(e)** in the derivation — this is the high-mark fetcher!

### 2.4 Soil Resistivity — The Master Variable

Soil resistivity **ρ** is **not a constant** — it varies with:
- **Moisture content** (most dominant factor — drops by 100× when wet)
- **Temperature** (rises drastically below 0 °C as water freezes)
- **Salt content / mineral composition**
- **Seasonal variation** (summer vs. monsoon)
- **Compaction and grain size**

**Measurement Methods (IS 3043):**
1. **Wenner Four-Probe Method** (most common)
   $$ \rho = 2\pi a \cdot R $$
   where **a** = probe spacing (m), **R** = measured resistance (Ω).
2. **Schlumberger–Palmer Method** — for deeper investigations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** Always use **ρ in Ω·m** (S.I.) in numerical problems. The IS 3043 formula uses natural-log form. If the problem gives d in **mm**, convert to **metres** before substituting.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Substation Earthing Mesh** (e.g., 33/11 kV): Designed using IEEE 80 / IS 3043 to limit touch and step potentials to safe values.
- **Building Earthing** (residential): Single pipe electrode with earth resistance ≤ 5 Ω.
- **Tower Footing Resistance** (transmission): 33 kV line tower — ≤ 10 Ω; 220 kV — ≤ 1 Ω.
- **Computer / Data Centre Earthing:** Single-point grounding with low-impedance (≤ 1 Ω) dedicated earth.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION_3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Resistance of a Hemispherical Earth Electrode (Foundation for Plate Electrode)

We begin with the hemispherical model because **any plate or pipe electrode can be approximated as an equivalent hemisphere of radius r**, from which IS 3043 derives all practical formulas.

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry.**
Consider a hemispherical electrode of radius **r** buried with its flat face flush with the earth surface. Current **I** flows radially outward from the electrode through the surrounding soil of resistivity **ρ**. At any radial distance **x ≥ r** from the centre, the current flows through a hemispherical surface of area:

$$ A(x) = 2\pi x^{2} $$

**Step 2 — Write the elemental resistance of a thin shell.**
Consider a hemispherical shell of radius **x** and thickness **dx**. The resistance of this shell is:

$$ dR = \frac{\rho \, dx}{A(x)} = \frac{\rho \, dx}{2\pi x^{2}} $$

**Step 3 — Integrate from r to infinity.**
The total resistance is the integral of elemental resistances from the electrode surface to infinity (since the soil extends effectively to infinity):

$$ R = \int_{r}^{\infty} \frac{\rho}{2\pi x^{2}} \, dx $$

$$ R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi} \int_{r}^{\infty} x^{-2} \, dx $$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$ R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi} \left[ -x^{-1} \right]_{r}^{\infty} $$

$$ R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi} \left[ 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{r}\right) \right] $$

$$ R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi r} $$

**Step 5 — Convert to the IS 3043 plate form.**
For a **circular plate of radius a**, the equivalent hemisphere radius is **r = a**, so:

$$ R_{\text{plate}} = \frac{\rho}{2\pi a} $$

Using **ρ/4r** form (where r = a/2 is the plate's geometric radius):

$$ R_{\text{plate}} = \frac{\rho}{4r} \quad \text{with } r = \frac{a}{2} $$

For a **square plate of side a**, the equivalent radius is $r = a / \sqrt{\pi}$:

$$ R_{\text{sq-plate}} \approx 0.8 \, \frac{\rho}{a} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters:** KTU examiners frequently award 4–5 marks (out of 7 in part (a)) for the **derivation of the hemispherical model**. It demonstrates that you understand the physics, not just the final formula.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Resistance of a Pipe Electrode (IS 3043 Standard Form)

**Step 1 — Start from the rod-electrode field equation.**
For a vertical cylindrical rod of length **L** and diameter **d** (driven fully into the soil with its top at earth surface), the IS 3043 expression begins from the line-source approximation:

$$ R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \ln\!\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) $$

**Step 2 — Convert natural log to log₁₀.**
We use the identity:

$$ \ln(x) = 2.3026 \, \log_{10}(x) $$

Substituting:

$$ R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \times 2.3026 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) $$

**Step 3 — Combine constants (the famous 2π / 2.3026 = 2.728 ≈ 276/101).**
For the specific case where L is in **metres**, d in **metres**, and R in **ohms**, with ρ in **Ω·m**, the constant is:

$$ 2\pi \times 2.3026 \approx 14.466 $$

IS 3043 chooses the form:

$$ R = \frac{100 \, \rho}{276 \, L} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) \quad \text{(ohms)} $$

(where the 100/276 = 0.365 ≈ 1/2.74, accounting for the conversion chain and the empirical rod-end correction).

**Step 4 — Apply the end-effect correction (for rod only).**
For a rod whose tip is below the surface, an empirical correction of **–1** is added in the natural-log form:

$$ R_{\text{rod}} = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\!\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right] $$

For a **pipe electrode** (hollow cylinder with perforations), no end correction is needed (open perforation behaves similarly to a flat-ended hemisphere), so:

$$ R_{\text{pipe}} = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \ln\!\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) \quad \text{or} \quad R_{\text{pipe}} = \frac{100\, \rho}{276\, L}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) $$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Examples for Numerical Mastery

#### Example 1 — Single Pipe Electrode (Classic 7-Mark Problem)

**Problem:** A GI pipe electrode of length 3 m and diameter 50 mm is buried vertically in soil of resistivity 80 Ω·m. Calculate the earth-electrode resistance. Take d = 0.05 m, L = 3 m.

**Step-by-step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify formula (IS 3043):

$$ R = \frac{100\, \rho}{276\, L}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) $$

**Step 2:** Substitute ρ = 80, L = 3, d = 0.05:

$$ R = \frac{100 \times 80}{276 \times 3} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4 \times 3}{0.05}\right) $$

**Step 3:** Compute numerator and denominator:

$$ R = \frac{8000}{828} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{12}{0.05}\right) $$

**Step 4:** Evaluate argument of log:

$$ R = 9.66 \times \log_{10}(240) $$

**Step 5:** Evaluate log:

$$ R = 9.66 \times 2.3802 $$

$$ R = 22.99 \;\Omega \;\approx\; 23.0 \;\Omega $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is too high!** For an LT installation (≤ 5 Ω required), a **single** electrode is insufficient. We must use **multiple electrodes in parallel** (see Example 2).

---

#### Example 2 — Multiple Electrodes in Parallel (Killer 7-Mark Problem)

**Problem:** A substation requires an earth resistance ≤ 1 Ω. The soil resistivity is 100 Ω·m. If pipe electrodes of 3 m length and 50 mm diameter are to be used, how many electrodes are required? (Use interaction factor λ = 0.20.)

**Step 1:** Single-electrode resistance (from Example 1, scaled for ρ = 100):

$$ R_1 = \frac{100 \times 100}{276 \times 3}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4 \times 3}{0.05}\right) $$

$$ R_1 = \frac{10000}{828} \log_{10}(240) = 12.08 \times 2.3802 = 28.75 \;\Omega $$

**Step 2:** Effective combined resistance formula (with interaction factor):

$$ R_n = \frac{R_1}{n}(1 + \lambda\,\alpha) $$

Taking worst case α = 1 (all adjacent):

$$ R_n = \frac{28.75}{n}(1 + 0.20) = \frac{34.50}{n} $$

**Step 3:** Set R_n ≤ 1 Ω and solve for n:

$$ \frac{34.50}{n} \le 1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad n \ge 34.50 $$

**Step 4:** Round up:

$$ n = 35 \text{ electrodes (minimum)} $$

**Step 5:** Practical recommendation: space electrodes at **6 m** apart (≥ 2 × length), use **40** electrodes for design margin.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU examiners love this problem.** It tests both the pipe formula AND the parallel-with-interaction concept in a single 7-mark question.

---

#### Example 3 — Plate Electrode Resistance (7-Mark Question)

**Problem:** A square plate electrode of side 60 cm is buried vertically in soil of resistivity 200 Ω·m. Calculate the earth resistance. The plate is 1.5 m below ground level.

**Step 1:** Convert dimensions: a = 0.6 m, ρ = 200 Ω·m.

**Step 2:** Equivalent radius for square plate:

$$ r = \frac{a}{\sqrt{\pi}} = \frac{0.6}{1.7725} = 0.3385 \;\text{m} $$

**Step 3:** Apply hemispherical formula:

$$ R = \frac{\rho}{4r} = \frac{200}{4 \times 0.3385} $$

$$ R = \frac{200}{1.354} = 147.7 \;\Omega $$

**Step 4:** Apply burial-depth correction (IS 3043 multiplier):

For depth = 1.5 m, IS 3043 recommends multiplying by 0.85:

$$ R_{\text{corrected}} = 147.7 \times 0.85 = 125.5 \;\Omega $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Using **r = a/2** (radius of inscribed circle) instead of **r = a/√π** (equivalent radius of the actual plate). This gives a wrong answer by 17%.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION_4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of an Earth Pit (IS 3043 Standard Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["EQUIPMENT SIDE"]
        eqp1["Transformer / Panel Body"]
        cnd1["Earth Conductor (GI Strip / Cable)"]
    end

    subgraph B["EARTH PIT ASSEMBLY"]
        pit1["Inspection Chamber (Brick Masonry)"]
        pit2["GI Pipe Electrode L=3m, d=50mm"]
        pit3["Charcoal + Salt Layer (Coke Bedding)"]
        pit4["Funnel for Watering"]
        pit5["GI Strip 25x4 mm (Earthing Lead)"]
    end

    subgraph C["SOIL MEDIUM"]
        soil1["Backfilled Soil ρ = 80-500 Ω-m"]
        soil2["Moisture Zone (Radius ~ 1.5 m)"]
    end

    eqp1 --> cnd1
    cnd1 --> pit5
    pit5 --> pit2
    pit2 --> pit3
    pit3 --> soil1
    pit2 -.current flow.-> soil2
    pit1 -.houses.-> pit2
    pit4 -.water supply.-> pit2
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow — Selection of Earth Electrode Type (IS 3043 Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["Start: Earthing Required for Installation"]
    q1{"Soil Type?"}
    q2{"Available Space?"}
    q3{"Required Resistance?"}

    a1["Loose / Sandy / Rocky Soil"]
    a2["Compact / Clay / Moist Soil"]
    a3["Open Land Available"]
    a4["Constrained / Urban"]
    a5["< 1 Ω (HT / Substation)"]
    a6["< 5 Ω (LT / Building)"]

    p1["Use PIPE Electrode (3 m, 50 mm GI)"]
    p2["Use PLATE Electrode (60x60 cm GI)"]
    p3["Use STRIP Electrode (Horizontal Trench)"]
    p4["Use ROD Electrode (Driven)"]
    p5["Use MULTIPLE Electrodes in Parallel"]
    p6["Use Single PIPE + Chemical Treatment"]

    start --> q1
    q1 -- Sandy --> a1
    q1 -- Clay --> a2
    a1 --> q2
    a2 --> q2
    q2 -- Open --> a3
    q2 -- Constrained --> a4
    a3 --> q3
    a4 --> q3
    q3 -- High --> a5
    q3 -- Moderate --> a6
    a5 --> p5
    a6 --> q1
    p5 --> p1
    p6 --> p2
    p3 --> p4
    p4 --> p3
    p1 --> p2
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Earth Resistance Components (Series-Parallel Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1["Soil Contact Resistance (Rc)"] --> step2["Spread Resistance of Soil (Rs)"]
    step2 --> step3["Resistance of Earth Electrode Itself (Re)"]
    step3 --> step4["Resistance of Earth Conductor (Rcond)"]
    step4 --> step5["Contact Resistance between Conductor and Electrode (Rcc)"]
    step5 --> step6["TOTAL EARTH RESISTANCE (RT)"]
    step1 -- in series --> step2
    step2 -- in series --> step3
    step3 -- in series --> step4
    step4 -- in series --> step5
    step5 -- in series --> step6

    step6 -.dominant term.-> dom["Spread Resistance (Rs) ~ 90% of RT"]
    style step6 fill:#FFD700
    style dom fill:#FF6347
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** The block diagram above clarifies a frequently-misunderstood concept. The earth resistance is **NOT** just the resistance of the metal electrode. The **dominant component (90%+) is the spread resistance of the surrounding soil**, which is why soil resistivity (ρ) and moisture control are the most important design variables.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION_5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Define earth resistance and state the permissible values of earth resistance for (i) a 33/11 kV substation, and (ii) a residential LT installation, as per IS 3043.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Earth resistance** is the resistance offered by the earth electrode to the flow of fault current into the surrounding soil, measured between the electrode and a distant remote earth (true ground at infinity).
>
> **Permissible values as per IS 3043 / CEA Regulations:**
> - **33/11 kV substation (HT):** Earth resistance **≤ 1 Ω** (must be maintained year-round).
> - **Residential LT installation:** Earth resistance **≤ 5 Ω** (single-pipe electrode typically suffices).

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Differentiate between "system earthing" and "equipment earthing" with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | System Earthing | Equipment Earthing |
|--------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Definition** | Connecting the **neutral point** of a power system (e.g., star-point of transformer) to earth | Connecting the **non-current-carrying metallic body** of equipment to earth |
| **Purpose** | Stablise phase voltages; provide fault-current return path | Safety of personnel; prevent shock during insulation failure |
| **Example** | Star-point of 11 kV / 433 V distribution transformer grounded | Metallic enclosure of an induction motor connected to earth |
| **Typical Resistance** | **≤ 1 Ω** | **≤ 5 Ω** (LT) |
| **IS Reference** | IS 3043 Clause 3.1 | IS 3043 Clause 3.2 |

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT mix units** — convert d from mm to m before substituting. A common error is writing d = 50 directly (in mm) into a formula expecting d in m. This gives an answer off by **a factor of 1000**.
> 2. **Always specify the formula's domain** — IS 3043 formula $R = 100\rho/276L$ requires **L in metres** and **d in metres**. Using cm will yield a wrong R.
> 3. **In parallel-electrode problems, never ignore the interaction factor λ.** Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this omission.
> 4. **The constant 276 in the denominator is 2π·log₁₀(e)·100** — do not confuse it with a stray dimensionless number.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** A **GI pipe electrode of length 2.5 m and diameter 50 mm** is buried vertically in soil of resistivity **150 Ω·m**. Calculate the resistance offered by the electrode to earth. State the IS 3043 formula used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Wenner four-probe method** for measurement of soil resistivity. How is the value of soil resistivity computed from the measured resistance? List **three factors** affecting soil resistivity. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

Step 1 — **Identify formula (IS 3043 Clause 11):**

$$ R = \frac{100\, \rho}{276\, L}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) \quad [\Omega] $$

Step 2 — **Substitute values:** ρ = 150 Ω·m, L = 2.5 m, d = 0.05 m.

$$ R = \frac{100 \times 150}{276 \times 2.5}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4 \times 2.5}{0.05}\right) $$

Step 3 — **Compute numerical values:**

$$ R = \frac{15000}{690}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10}{0.05}\right) = 21.74 \times \log_{10}(200) $$

Step 4 — **Evaluate logarithm:**

$$ R = 21.74 \times 2.3010 = 50.02 \;\Omega $$

**Answer:** R ≈ **50.0 Ω** (this is far too high for any practical use — multiple electrodes required).

---

**Part (b):**

**Wenner Four-Probe Method (Soil Resistivity Measurement):**

The method uses **four equally-spaced electrodes** (probes) driven into the soil in a straight line, with equal spacing **'a'** between adjacent probes (typically 2, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30 m).

**Procedure:**
1. Drive four probes P₁, P₂, P₃, P₄ into the soil in a line, equally spaced at distance **'a'**.
2. Pass a current **I** through the **outer probes** P₁ and P₄ using a current source.
3. Measure the potential difference **V** between the **inner probes** P₂ and P₃ using a high-impedance voltmeter.
4. Compute the resistance **R = V / I**.
5. The **apparent soil resistivity** is calculated using the Wenner formula:

$$ \rho = 2\pi a R $$

where **a** = probe spacing (m) and **R** = measured resistance (Ω).

The probes are then re-driven at a different spacing 'a' to obtain resistivity at different depths (effective depth ≈ spacing 'a').

**Three Factors Affecting Soil Resistivity:**
1. **Moisture content** (dominant — resistivity drops drastically as moisture increases).
2. **Temperature** (rises sharply as soil freezes below 0 °C).
3. **Salt/mineral content and soil composition** (clay < sand < rock; salt contamination reduces ρ).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and laying procedure of a plate earth electrode** as per IS 3043. State the materials and dimensions recommended. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A substation requires a **combined earth resistance of 0.5 Ω**. The soil resistivity is **200 Ω·m**. Pipe electrodes of **3 m length, 50 mm diameter** are used. If the interaction factor λ = 0.15, determine the **number of electrodes** required when spaced at adequate distance. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Plate Earth Electrode (IS 3043 Specification):**

**Materials & Dimensions:**
- **Material:** Galvanized Iron (GI) plate — 60 cm × 60 cm × 6.3 mm thick; **or** Copper plate — 60 cm × 60 cm × 3.15 mm thick.
- **Earth lead:** GI strip 25 mm × 4 mm or copper strip of equivalent current-carrying capacity, bolted to the plate using two bolts.

**Construction & Laying Procedure:**
1. Dig a pit of size approximately 1.5 m × 1.5 m × 3 m deep (or up to permanent water-table level, whichever is shallower).
2. At the bottom, place a **layer of charcoal and salt mixture** (ratio ~ 6:1 or 5:1 by volume) of about 25 cm thickness — this maintains moisture around the plate.
3. Place the plate **vertically** at the centre of the pit, with the earth lead welded/bolted.
4. Backfill with alternate layers of charcoal/salt and soil, with each layer watered and compacted.
5. The earth lead is taken through a GI pipe (for mechanical protection) to the equipment/panel.
6. The top of the pit is sealed with a **brick-masonry inspection chamber** with a cast-iron cover for periodic watering.

```
         Surface (Ground Level)
         ┌──────────────────┐
         │ Inspection Chamber│
         │   ┌──────────┐   │
         │   │Earth Lead│   │
         │   │  (GI Strip)│ │
         │   └────┬─────┘   │
         │        │         │
         │   [Plate 60×60]  │
         │   (vertical)     │
         │   ┌────┴────┐    │
         │   │Charcoal │    │
         │   │ + Salt  │    │
         │   │ Layer   │    │
         │   └────────┘    │
         │  Backfilled Soil │
         └──────────────────┘
              Depth ≥ 1.5 m
```

**Part (b):**

Step 1 — **Single-electrode resistance** (ρ = 200, L = 3, d = 0.05):

$$ R_1 = \frac{100 \times 200}{276 \times 3}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{12}{0.05}\right) = \frac{20000}{828} \times 2.3802 = 24.15 \times 2.3802 $$

$$ R_1 = 57.48 \;\Omega $$

Step 2 — **Combined resistance with interaction factor** (worst case α = 1):

$$ R_n = \frac{R_1}{n}(1 + \lambda \alpha) = \frac{57.48}{n}(1 + 0.15) = \frac{66.10}{n} $$

Step 3 — **Set R_n ≤ 0.5 Ω:**

$$ n \ge \frac{66.10}{0.5} = 132.2 $$

Step 4 — **Round up to integer:**

$$ \boxed{n = 133 \text{ electrodes (minimum)}} $$

Step 5 — **Practical recommendation:** Use **140 electrodes** spaced at 6 m apart in a grid for design margin and to handle seasonal variation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — IS 3043**

- [x] **IS 3043** = Code of Practice for Earthing (BIS, 1987; reaffirmed 2019).
- [x] **Permissible earth resistance:** HT substation ≤ **1 Ω**; LT installation ≤ **5 Ω**.
- [x] **Four electrode types:** Plate, Pipe, Rod, Strip — pipe is most common.
- [x] **Dominant variable:** Soil resistivity **ρ** (Ω·m); measured by **Wenner 4-probe** method: ρ = **2πaR**.
- [x] **Plate formula:** $R = \rho / 4r$, where r is equivalent radius of the plate.
- [x] **Pipe formula (IS 3043 standard form):** $R = (100\rho/276L)\log_{10}(4L/d)$.
- [x] **Rod formula (with end correction):** $R = (\rho/2\pi L)\left[\ln(4L/d) - 1\right]$.
- [x] **Horizontal strip formula:** $R = (\rho/\pi L)\ln(2L/w)$.
- [x] **Parallel combination with interaction:** $R_n = (R/n)(1 + \lambda\alpha)$, where λ = 0.05–0.30.
- [x] **System vs. Equipment earthing:** Neutral earthing vs. body earthing — different purposes.
- [x] **Earth conductor sizing:** Based on short-time **fault current and duration** (thermal criterion).
- [x] **Step potential** — voltage between two feet (1 m apart) on the ground surface.
- [x] **Touch potential** — voltage between hand and feet at the equipment location.
- [x] **Soil treatment:** Charcoal + salt bedding around electrode maintains moisture and reduces ρ.
- [x] **Unit discipline:** Always L in **m**, d in **m**, ρ in **Ω·m**, R in **Ω**.
- [x] **Classic 14-mark numerical pattern:** Single electrode R → convert to "n electrodes" problem → interaction factor → spacing criteria (≥ 2L).
- [x] **Wenner 4-probe** probe spacing 'a' corresponds to effective depth 'a' of measurement.
- [x] **Practical spacing rule:** Maintain distance ≥ 2L between adjacent electrodes to minimise λ.

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — IS 3043**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IS 2026, IS 3646 (Part 1 & 2), IS 5216 (Part 1 & 2)
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IS 2026, IS 3646 (Part 1 & 2), IS 5216 (Part 1 & 2)

# 📘 KTU Module 1 – Indian Standards (IS Codes) for Electrical System Design

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are IS Codes?

Indian Standards (IS) are technical documents issued by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** that establish uniform engineering criteria, methods of test, safety requirements, and design guidelines. For the electrical estimator and designer, these IS codes act as the **legal and technical "rulebook"** that governs how systems are designed, installed, tested, and commissioned.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the outcome-based NEP 2020 framework, students must be able to identify the *correct IS code applicable to a specific electrical design scenario*. Questions in ESE frequently test your ability to recall code numbers, their scope, and key clauses.

---

### 1.2 IS 2026 – Power Transformers

**Formal Definition (as per BIS):**
IS 2026 is the Indian Standard for **Power Transformers**, which lays down the requirements, ratings, tests, temperature-rise limits, insulation levels, and connection symbols for power transformers (oil-immersed type) used in power generation, transmission, and distribution networks.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🏗️**
Think of a power transformer as a **"Voltage Translator"** in a massive highway system. Just like highway speed limits and bridge-load ratings must follow national specifications so that vehicles can travel safely from one state to another, a transformer must be built to IS 2026 so that it can be safely connected to the national grid. When you estimate a substation, IS 2026 tells you *exactly what to specify* — kVA rating, voltage class, impedance, oil type, and cooling class.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Coverage of IS 2026 (multiple parts):**
> - **Part 1:** General requirements and definitions
> - **Part 2:** Temperature-rise requirements
> - **Part 3:** Insulation levels and dielectric tests
> - **Part 4:** Terminal markings, tappings, and connections
> - **Part 7:** Loading guides for oil-immersed power transformers
> - **Part 10:** Determination of sound levels

---

### 1.3 IS 3646 – Code of Practice for Interior Illumination

**Formal Definition:**
IS 3646 (in multiple parts) provides a comprehensive code of practice for **interior illumination** in various types of buildings — offices, schools, hospitals, factories, and residences. It defines illumination levels, glare control, colour rendering, lighting layouts, and calculation methods.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 💡**
Imagine walking into a room where you can barely see your own hand versus a room flooded with the warm, even glow of a cloudless afternoon. The *quality* of light in a room affects everything — productivity in an office, accuracy in a factory, recovery in a hospital. IS 3646 is essentially a **"recipe book"** that tells the lighting designer exactly how much light (lux), of what quality (CRI), and from which arrangement (direct/indirect) is required for each type of space.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Coverage of IS 3646:**
> - **Part 1:** Principles of good lighting and lighting design data (general principles)
> - **Part 2:** Schedule of illumination levels (luminance values) for various building types

---

### 1.4 IS 5216 – Safety Procedures and Practices in Electrical Work

**Formal Definition:**
IS 5216 provides recommendations on **safety procedures and practices** to be followed during installation, operation, maintenance, and inspection of electrical equipment and installations. It is a critical document for ensuring human safety, prevention of electric shock, fire hazards, and ensuring statutory compliance.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition ⚡**
Think of electrical safety as the **"seatbelt rulebook"** of the electrical world. Nobody plans to crash, but the rulebook exists for the day something goes wrong. IS 5216 tells the engineer *exactly* which precautions must be taken — earthing, isolation, permit-to-work, PPE, lockout-tagout — so that the person doing the work tomorrow is not the victim of a poorly-designed system today.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Coverage of IS 5216 (multiple parts):**
> - **Part 1:** General safety precautions and procedures
> - **Part 2:** Specific safety procedures for particular equipment and installations (e.g., switchgear, transformers, cables, overhead lines)

---

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value / Unit | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Standard reference temperature for transformer design | **75 °C** (oil) / **98 °C** (winding hot spot) | IS 2026 Pt. 2 |
| Reference ambient temperature (India) | **45 °C** (max), **35 °C** (avg daily) | IS 2026 Pt. 2 |
| Standard frequency | **50 Hz** | IS 2026 Pt. 1 |
| Preferred illumination for general office | **300 lux** | IS 3646 Pt. 2 |
| Minimum safe approach distance (11 kV) | **2.5 m** | IS 5216 Pt. 1 |
| Body resistance (assumed) | **1,000 Ω** (dry skin) | IS 5216 Pt. 1 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 IS 2026 – Detailed Theoretical Framework

IS 2026 governs the **design, manufacture, testing, and selection of oil-immersed power transformers**. For estimation purposes, the designer must specify transformer parameters strictly conforming to this standard.

#### 2.1.1 Rated Power (kVA) and Voltage Classes

The transformer rating in **kVA** (kilo-Volt-Amperes) is standardized according to IS 2026. The standard preferred kVA ratings for distribution transformers include:

$$\text{Preferred kVA ratings} \in \{5, 10, 16, 25, 63, 100, 160, 200, 250, 315, 400, 500, 630, 1000, 1250, 1600, 2000\}$$

**Why this matters for estimation:**
When you prepare a cost estimate, you *cannot* quote an arbitrary kVA. The estimator must choose from the standard ratings, otherwise the manufacturer will refuse or charge a premium for non-standard units.

#### 2.1.2 Temperature Rise Limits

IS 2026 (Part 2) specifies the maximum permissible temperature rise over an **ambient of 45 °C** for oil-immersed transformers:

| Component | Max Temperature Rise | Absolute Max Temperature |
|---|---|---|
| Top oil (by thermometer) | **50 °C** | **95 °C** |
| Winding (by resistance) | **55 °C** (avg) | **100 °C** (avg) |
| Winding hot spot | — | **115 °C** |

#### 2.1.3 Impedance Voltage (%Z)

The percentage impedance determines the **short-circuit level** and **voltage regulation** of the transformer:

$$\%Z = \frac{V_{sc}}{V_{rated}} \times 100$$

Typical values per IS 2026 for distribution transformers (up to 2500 kVA, 11 kV/433 V):
- **4.5 % to 6 %** for ratings up to 630 kVA
- **6.25 % to 9 %** for higher ratings

#### 2.1.4 Vector Group

IS 2026 standardizes vector groups such as **Dyn11**, **Yyn0**, **YNd1**, etc. The common distribution transformer vector group in India is **Dyn11**, which provides a 30° phase shift and allows for better handling of unbalanced loads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Dyn11 is the *most preferred* vector group for 11 kV / 433 V distribution transformers in India because the delta on the primary side suppresses third-harmonic currents, and the star point on the secondary (with neutral) allows single-phase loads to be connected.

---

### 2.2 IS 3646 – Interior Illumination Framework

IS 3646 is the foundation of all **lighting design** for interior spaces. The design process follows a structured methodology.

#### 2.2.1 Key Lighting Terms (Defined by IS 3646)

- **Luminous Flux (Φ):** The total light emitted by a source, measured in **lumens (lm)**.
- **Luminous Intensity (I):** Light emitted per unit solid angle, measured in **candela (cd)**.
- **Illuminance (E):** The amount of light falling on a surface.

$$E = \frac{\Phi}{A} \quad [\text{lux or lm/m}^2]$$

- **Luminance (L):** The brightness of a surface as perceived by the eye, in **cd/m²**.
- **Luminous Efficacy (η):** Light output per unit power input, in **lumens/watt (lm/W)**.

$$\eta = \frac{\Phi}{P} \quad [\text{lm/W}]$$

#### 2.2.2 Recommended Illumination Levels (IS 3646 Part 2)

| Type of Interior | Recommended Illumination (lux) |
|---|---|
| Staircases, corridors | **100** |
| General offices | **300** |
| Drawing offices, typing rooms | **450–700** |
| Hospital operating theatres | **1,000+** |
| Fine machine work, inspection | **700–1,500** |
| Classrooms | **300** |
| Reading rooms / libraries | **300–500** |
| Factory rough bench work | **150** |

#### 2.2.3 Glare Control & CRI

- **Glare Index (GR):** Must be limited to ensure visual comfort (typically GR ≤ 19 for offices).
- **Colour Rendering Index (CRI):** For general interiors, CRI ≥ **80** is preferred; for critical visual tasks, CRI ≥ **90**.

---

### 2.3 IS 5216 – Safety Procedures Framework

IS 5216 establishes the **hierarchy of safety** that every estimator must incorporate into a design.

#### 2.3.1 Five Golden Rules of Electrical Safety (per IS 5216 Pt. 1)

1. **Disconnect** the supply completely.
2. **Secure against re-energization** (lockout-tagout).
3. **Verify the absence of voltage** using a voltage tester.
4. **Earth and short-circuit** the conductors.
5. **Provide protection against adjacent live parts** (barriers, warning signs).

#### 2.3.2 Earthing Resistance Limits

The maximum permissible earth-electrode resistance (per IS 5216 / IS 3043) is:

$$R_{earth} \leq \frac{50}{I_{fault}} \quad [\Omega]$$

But subject to:
- Major substations: **≤ 0.5 Ω**
- Transformer neutral: **≤ 1 Ω**
- Equipment body (LT): **≤ 1 Ω**

#### 2.3.3 Safe Approach Distances (per IS 5216 Pt. 1)

| Voltage Level | Minimum Safe Approach Distance |
|---|---|
| Up to 415 V | **0.5 m** |
| 11 kV | **2.5 m** |
| 33 kV | **3.0 m** |
| 66 kV | **4.0 m** |
| 132 kV | **5.0 m** |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression / Value | Unit | Code Reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transformer rated current | $I = \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V}$ | A | IS 2026 |
| 2 | Percentage impedance | $\%Z = \dfrac{V_{sc}}{V_{rated}} \times 100$ | % | IS 2026 |
| 3 | Temperature rise (oil) | 50 °C over 45 °C ambient | °C | IS 2026 Pt. 2 |
| 4 | Winding absolute max temp | 100 °C (avg) | °C | IS 2026 Pt. 2 |
| 5 | Luminous flux | $\Phi$ | lm | IS 3646 |
| 6 | Illuminance | $E = \Phi / A$ | lux (lm/m²) | IS 3646 |
| 7 | Luminous efficacy | $\eta = \Phi / P$ | lm/W | IS 3646 |
| 8 | Office illumination | 300 | lux | IS 3646 Pt. 2 |
| 9 | Earth resistance (LT equipment) | $\leq 1$ | Ω | IS 5216 / IS 3043 |
| 10 | Safe approach (11 kV) | 2.5 | m | IS 5216 Pt. 1 |
| 11 | Safe approach (415 V) | 0.5 | m | IS 5216 Pt. 1 |
| 12 | Standard frequency | 50 | Hz | IS 2026 |
| 13 | Reference ambient (India) | 45 | °C | IS 2026 |
| 14 | Vector group (distribution) | Dyn11 | — | IS 2026 |
| 15 | Body resistance (assumed) | 1,000 | Ω | IS 5216 |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of These IS Codes in Engineering

| Code | Practical Application |
|---|---|
| IS 2026 | Used by **transformer manufacturers (BHEL, Crompton, Schneider)** to design and test products. Estimators use it to draft transformer specifications in tender documents. |
| IS 3646 | Used by **lighting designers (Philips, Havells, Wipro)** to plan illumination for malls, hospitals, and offices. Crucial for green-building LEED/GRIHA certification. |
| IS 5216 | Used by **safety officers, electrical contractors, and site engineers** to establish safe work permits, lockout-tagout procedures, and PPE protocols. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Mapping IS Codes to Design Decisions

The following matrix shows how each IS code directly drives a specific design decision or estimation step:

| Design Stage | Applicable IS Code | Decision Influenced | Typical Output in Estimate |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specification of transformer | **IS 2026** | kVA, voltage ratio, %Z, vector group | Datasheet, item rate in BOQ |
| Selection of lighting fixtures | **IS 3646** | Lux level, lumens, fixture layout | Number of fixtures, wattage |
| Earthing design | **IS 5216 / IS 3043** | Earth-pit resistance, conductor size | Number of earth pits, GI strip quantity |
| Cable sizing | **IS 3961 / IS 1554** | Current rating, derating factors | Cable cross-section in mm² |
| Switchgear rating | **IS 13947 / IEC 60947** | Short-circuit capacity (kA) | Breaker rating in BOQ |
| PPE in estimate | **IS 5216** | Safety equipment allowance | Lump sum / line item |

---

### 3.2 Derivations and Worked Numerical Analysis

#### 3.2.1 Transformer Full-Load Current Derivation (IS 2026)

**Problem:** A 500 kVA, 11 kV / 433 V, three-phase, 50 Hz distribution transformer is to be specified per IS 2026. Calculate the rated full-load currents on HV and LV sides and the short-circuit current on the LV side, given %Z = 5%.

**Step 1: Full-Load Current on HV (Primary) Side**

The general formula for three-phase line current is:

$$I_{rated} = \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{L}}$$

Substituting for HV side (S = 500 kVA, $V_L$ = 11,000 V):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{HV} &= \frac{500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 11{,}000} \\
&= \frac{500{,}000}{19{,}052.55} \\
I_{HV} &= 26.24 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Full-Load Current on LV (Secondary) Side**

Substituting for LV side ($V_L$ = 433 V):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{LV} &= \frac{500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 433} \\
&= \frac{500{,}000}{750.13} \\
I_{LV} &= 666.50 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Short-Circuit Current on LV Side**

Using the %Z of the transformer (5%), the short-circuit current is the inverse of the per-unit impedance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{sc, LV} &= \frac{I_{LV}}{\%Z/100} \\
&= \frac{666.50}{0.05} \\
I_{sc, LV} &= 13{,}330 \text{ A} \approx 13.33 \text{ kA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result Interpretation (Design Use):**
- HV side rated current ≈ **26.24 A** → use a 63 A MCB/MCCB.
- LV side rated current ≈ **666.50 A** → use a 800 A ACB.
- Short-circuit level ≈ **13.33 kA** → ACB must have breaking capacity ≥ **25 kA** (next standard rating).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Expected Skill:** The student must demonstrate the link from IS 2026 (%Z) to actual switchgear selection — a common ESE Part B question.

---

#### 3.2.2 Number of Luminaires Calculation (IS 3646)

**Problem:** A general office of dimensions 20 m × 15 m is to be illuminated to **300 lux** (per IS 3646 Pt. 2). Use LED panel lights, each rated 40 W, emitting **3,600 lumens**. The utilization factor (UF) = 0.6, maintenance factor (MF) = 0.8. Calculate the number of luminaires required.

**Step 1: Calculate Total Area**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= L \times B \\
&= 20 \times 15 \\
A &= 300 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Total Luminous Flux Required**

The lumen formula per IS 3646:

$$\Phi_{total} = \frac{E \times A}{UF \times MF}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_{total} &= \frac{300 \times 300}{0.6 \times 0.8} \\
&= \frac{90{,}000}{0.48} \\
\Phi_{total} &= 187{,}500 \text{ lumens}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Number of Luminaires**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{\Phi_{total}}{\Phi_{per\,luminaire}} \\
&= \frac{187{,}500}{3{,}600} \\
N &= 52.08 \approx 53 \text{ luminaires}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Total Connected Load**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{total} &= N \times P_{luminaire} \\
&= 53 \times 40 \\
P_{total} &= 2{,}120 \text{ W} = 2.12 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result Interpretation (Estimate Impact):**
- 53 nos. × 40 W LED panel = **Item 1 in BOQ** with unit rate and quantity.
- Wiring circuit design will follow from the 2.12 kW connected load (e.g., circuit loading, MCB sizing).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Many students forget to apply **MF** (Maintenance Factor) — which accounts for lamp lumen depreciation and dirt accumulation. The standard MF of **0.8** is mandatory for industrial/commercial design as per IS 3646.

---

#### 3.2.3 Earth Resistance Verification (IS 5216 / IS 3043)

**Problem:** An equipment has a body fault current of 50 A. Per IS 5216, the body voltage must be limited to **50 V (safe touch voltage in dry conditions)**. Calculate the maximum permissible earth resistance.

**Step 1: Apply Ohm's Law to Earth Path**

The earth electrode must ensure that the touch voltage (body voltage) does not exceed 50 V:

$$R_{earth,max} = \frac{V_{touch,max}}{I_{fault}}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{earth,max} &= \frac{50}{50} \\
R_{earth,max} &= 1.0 \; \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Verify Against IS 5216 Limits**

IS 5216 specifies that for LT equipment, $R_{earth} \leq 1 \, \Omega$ — the calculated value **just meets** the requirement. In practice, designers target $R_{earth} \leq 0.5 \; \Omega$ to provide a safety margin.

**Step 3: Determine Number of Earth Pits**

If a single 40 mm dia., 3 m long GI pipe earth electrode gives a resistance of ~20 Ω in ordinary soil, the number of pits in parallel (assuming no interference) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{pits} &= \frac{R_{single}}{R_{target}} \\
&= \frac{20}{0.5} \\
N_{pits} &= 40 \; \text{pits (ideal, no interference)}
\end{aligned}
$$

In practice, due to mutual interference between electrodes spaced less than their length, this is reduced to **~10–15 pits** spaced 6 m apart, supplemented by chemical earthing or GI strip ring.

---

#### 3.2.4 Safe Approach Distance Derivation (Conceptual)

The minimum safe approach distance (per IS 5216) is derived from two factors:
1. **Human reach** (anthropometric data) — typically **0.5 m** for an arm's reach.
2. **Electrical clearance** — based on the *flashover voltage* of air (≈ **3 kV/cm** or 30 kV/m for uniform fields, but reduced to **10–15 kV/m** in non-uniform field at altitudes).

For an 11 kV system (line-to-line), the peak line voltage is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{peak} &= V_{LL} \times \sqrt{2} \times 1.1 \; \text{(safety factor)} \\
&= 11{,}000 \times 1.414 \times 1.1 \\
&\approx 17{,}106 \; \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

The required air clearance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{air} &= \frac{V_{peak}}{15 \text{ kV/m}} \\
&= \frac{17.1}{15} \\
&\approx 1.14 \; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding the human reach (0.5 m) and a safety margin:

$$d_{safe} = 1.14 + 0.5 + \text{margin} \approx 2.5 \; \text{m} \;\; \text{(matches IS 5216)}$$

This is why IS 5216 prescribes **2.5 m** for 11 kV.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Functional Architecture of IS Code Application in Estimation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Receive Electrical Design Brief] --> B[Identify Project Type]
    B --> C{Is Transformer Involved?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Apply IS 2026 - Transformer Specs]
    C -- No --> E{Is Lighting Design Required?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Apply IS 3646 - Illumination Levels]
    E -- No --> G{Is Safety Compliance Needed?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Apply IS 5216 - Safety Procedures]
    G -- No --> I[Apply Other IS Codes - 732, 3043, 3961]
    D --> J[Compile Technical Specification]
    F --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Prepare BOQ and Cost Estimate]
    K --> L[Review Against IS Compliance]
    L --> M{Compliant?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Finalize Estimate]
    M -- No --> O[Revise Design]
    O --> J
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Lighting Design Workflow per IS 3646

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1: Determine Room Dimensions] --> B[Step 2: Identify Task Type per IS 3646 Pt 2]
    B --> C[Step 3: Select Lux Level E]
    C --> D[Step 4: Select Luminaire and Lumens per Lamp]
    D --> E[Step 5: Apply UF and MF Factors]
    E --> F[Step 6: Calculate Total Lumens Required]
    F --> G[Step 7: Compute Number of Luminaires N]
    G --> H[Step 8: Layout Verification and Glare Check]
    H --> I[Step 9: Finalize BOQ Item]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block: Transformer Selection Logic per IS 2026

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Calculate Connected Load in kW] --> B[Step 2: Apply Diversity Factor]
    B --> C[Step 3: Maximum Demand in kVA]
    C --> D[Step 4: Add Future Expansion Margin 20 to 25 percent]
    D --> E[Step 5: Round Up to Standard IS 2026 kVA Rating]
    E --> F[Step 6: Select Vector Group Dyn11 for Distribution]
    F --> G[Step 7: Specify Percentage Impedance from IS Table]
    G --> H[Step 8: Add to BOQ with Accessories]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block: Safety Hierarchy per IS 5216

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IS 5216 Safety Framework] --> B[Personal Safety]
    A --> C[Equipment Safety]
    A --> D[Operational Safety]
    B --> B1[PPE Specification]
    B --> B2[Body Earthing Requirement]
    C --> C1[Earthing Resistance Limits]
    C --> C2[Insulation Coordination]
    D --> D1[Lockout Tagout Procedure]
    D --> D2[Permit to Work System]
    D --> D3[Safe Approach Distances]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the scope and purpose of IS 2026 with respect to power transformers used in distribution networks.*

**Model Answer:**

IS 2026 is the Indian Standard code of practice for **oil-immersed power transformers**. Its scope includes:
- General requirements and definitions (Part 1)
- Temperature-rise limits and insulation classes (Part 2)
- Insulation levels and dielectric tests (Part 3)
- Terminal markings, tappings, and connection symbols (Part 4)
- Loading guides for operational transformers (Part 7)
- Sound-level determination (Part 10)

**Purpose:** To provide a uniform specification framework so that transformers manufactured by different vendors (BHEL, ABB, Siemens, etc.) are interchangeable in rating, performance, testing, and safety — thereby enabling fair tendering and reliable grid operation.

**[3 Marks]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the recommended illumination levels (in lux) for the following interiors as per IS 3646 Part 2:*
*(a) General office, (b) Hospital operating theatre, (c) Factory rough bench work, (d) Reading room.*

**Model Answer:**

As per **IS 3646 (Part 2) – Schedule of Illumination Levels:**

| Interior | Recommended Illuminance (lux) |
|---|---|
| (a) General office | **300 lux** |
| (b) Hospital operating theatre | **1,000+ lux** (special high-level lighting) |
| (c) Factory rough bench work | **150 lux** |
| (d) Reading room | **300 to 500 lux** |

**[3 Marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each) – Module Internal Choice

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
*(a)* Discuss in detail the salient provisions of **IS 3646 (Part 1 & Part 2)** related to interior illumination. Explain the terms: luminous flux, illuminance, luminous efficacy, utilization factor, and maintenance factor. **[7 Marks]**

*(b)* An office hall of size **24 m × 12 m × 3.5 m (height)** is to be illuminated to a standard service illuminance of **300 lux** using twin 36 W fluorescent tube luminaires (each tube gives 3,250 lumens). The utilization factor is **0.5** and the maintenance factor is **0.8**. Calculate the **number of luminaires** required and the **total connected lighting load**. Layout the luminaires in a regular pattern. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Solution to Q.A (a):**

**1. IS 3646 Part 1 – Principles of Good Lighting:**
IS 3646 (Part 1) lays down the fundamental principles governing interior illumination design, including:
- **Quantity of light:** Adequate illuminance on the task plane.
- **Quality of light:** Distribution, glare control, colour rendering, colour appearance.
- **Visual comfort:** Absence of veiling reflections and excessive contrast.
- **Aesthetic considerations:** Harmony with architecture and interior design.
- **Economic considerations:** Initial cost vs. running cost vs. maintenance.

**2. IS 3646 Part 2 – Schedule of Illumination Levels:**
This part tabulates the recommended **illuminance values (lux)** for different types of interiors and tasks — e.g., offices 300 lux, classrooms 300 lux, drawing offices 450–700 lux, hospital operation theatres 1,000+ lux, etc.

**3. Definitions:**

- **Luminous Flux (Φ):** Total quantity of light emitted by a source per second, measured in **lumens (lm)**. It represents the total "amount" of light regardless of direction.

- **Illuminance (E):** The luminous flux incident per unit area of a surface.

$$E = \frac{\Phi}{A} \quad [\text{lux} = \text{lm/m}^2]$$

- **Luminous Efficacy (η):** The ratio of luminous flux emitted to the power consumed.

$$\eta = \frac{\Phi}{P} \quad [\text{lm/W}]$$

- **Utilization Factor (UF):** The ratio of luminous flux reaching the working plane to the total flux emitted by the lamps. It depends on room geometry, reflection factors of walls/ceiling/floor, and luminaire distribution. Typical UF = **0.4 to 0.7**.

- **Maintenance Factor (MF):** The ratio of illuminance on the working plane after a stated period of use to the initial illuminance. It accounts for lamp lumen depreciation, dirt accumulation on luminaire, and room surface darkening. Typical MF = **0.7 to 0.8**.

**[7 Marks]**

---

**Solution to Q.A (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Room dimensions: L = 24 m, B = 12 m, H = 3.5 m
- Mounting: Suspended 0.5 m below ceiling → mounting height ≈ 3.0 m, working plane 0.85 m above floor → mounting height above working plane = 3.0 − 0.85 = 2.15 m
- Required illuminance: E = **300 lux**
- Luminaire: Twin 36 W fluorescent → flux per luminaire Φ = 2 × 3,250 = **6,500 lm**
- UF = **0.5**, MF = **0.8**

**Step 1: Area of the room**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= L \times B \\
&= 24 \times 12 = 288 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Total flux required**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_{total} &= \frac{E \times A}{UF \times MF} \\
&= \frac{300 \times 288}{0.5 \times 0.8} \\
&= \frac{86{,}400}{0.4} \\
&= 216{,}000 \text{ lumens}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Number of luminaires**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{\Phi_{total}}{\Phi_{per\,luminaire}} \\
&= \frac{216{,}000}{6{,}500} \\
&= 33.23 \approx 34 \text{ luminaires}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Layout (regular pattern)**

For uniform distribution, use a 6 × 6 grid → 36 luminaires. This slightly overshoots the design value, which is acceptable as a small safety margin.

- Spacing along length: 24/6 = **4.0 m**
- Spacing along width: 12/6 = **2.0 m**
- Spacing-to-height ratio check: S/H = 4.0/2.15 ≈ 1.86 (acceptable for twin-tube fluorescent, recommended SHR = 1.5 to 2.0).

**Step 5: Total connected load**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{total} &= 36 \times (2 \times 36) \\
&= 36 \times 72 \\
&= 2{,}592 \text{ W} = 2.59 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** **34 (design) / 36 (layout) luminaires**, total connected load ≈ **2.59 kW**.

**[7 Marks]**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*(a)* Describe the salient features of **IS 5216 (Part 1 & Part 2)** with respect to safety procedures in electrical work. List the **five golden rules of electrical safety** and the **safe approach distances** for 415 V, 11 kV, and 33 kV systems. **[7 Marks]**

*(b)* A **500 kVA, 11 kV / 433 V, three-phase, 50 Hz** distribution transformer conforming to **IS 2026** has a percentage impedance of **5%**. Calculate: **(i)** the full-load current on HV and LV sides, **(ii)** the short-circuit current on the LV side, and **(iii)** the rating of the LV-side ACB needed (assuming next standard rating above fault level). **[7 Marks]**

---

**Solution to Q.B (a):**

**1. IS 5216 Part 1 – General Safety Precautions:**
This part covers general safety procedures applicable to **all electrical installations and work**, including:
- Precautions before commencing work (issue of permit, isolation, earthing).
- Use of personal protective equipment (PPE): insulated gloves, safety helmets, safety shoes, arc-flash suits.
- Tools and equipment safety.
- Safe handling of live and de-energized equipment.
- First aid and emergency procedures.

**2. IS 5216 Part 2 – Specific Safety Procedures:**
This part addresses safety procedures for **specific equipment** such as:
- Switchgear and control gear
- Power and distribution transformers
- Cables and overhead lines
- Rotating machines
- Storage batteries

It also covers **permit-to-work systems**, **lockout-tagout (LOTO)**, and **restricted access** for authorized personnel.

**3. Five Golden Rules of Electrical Safety (per IS 5216 Part 1):**

1. **Disconnect** the electrical supply completely before any work.
2. **Secure** the supply against inadvertent re-energization (lockout-tagout).
3. **Verify** the absence of voltage using an approved voltage tester.
4. **Earth and short-circuit** the conductors.
5. **Protect** against nearby live parts using barriers, screens, or warning notices.

**4. Safe Approach Distances (per IS 5216 Pt. 1):**

| Voltage Level | Minimum Safe Approach Distance |
|---|---|
| Up to **415 V** | **0.5 m** |
| **11 kV** | **2.5 m** |
| **33 kV** | **3.0 m** |

**5. Additional Provisions:**
- **Earthing:** All exposed metal parts must be earthed per IS 3043.
- **First aid:** An authorized first-aider and a shock-treatment chart must be available at every electrical site.
- **Fire extinguishers:** CO₂ / dry-powder extinguishers must be provided near switchgear.

**[7 Marks]**

---

**Solution to Q.B (b):**

**Given:**
- Rating: S = 500 kVA
- HV voltage: $V_{HV}$ = 11 kV = 11,000 V
- LV voltage: $V_{LV}$ = 433 V (line-to-line)
- Frequency: f = 50 Hz
- Percentage impedance: %Z = 5%

**Step (i): Full-Load Current on HV Side**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{HV} &= \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{HV}} \\
&= \frac{500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 11{,}000} \\
&= \frac{500{,}000}{19{,}052.55} \\
I_{HV} &= 26.24 \; \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (i): Full-Load Current on LV Side**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{LV} &= \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{LV}} \\
&= \frac{500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 433} \\
&= \frac{500{,}000}{750.13} \\
I_{LV} &= 666.5 \; \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii): Short-Circuit Current on LV Side**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{sc,LV} &= \frac{I_{LV}}{\%Z / 100} \\
&= \frac{666.5}{0.05} \\
I_{sc,LV} &= 13{,}330 \; \text{A} = 13.33 \; \text{kA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (iii): ACB Selection**

- Required current rating ≥ $I_{LV}$ = 666.5 A → next standard ACB size = **800 A**.
- Required breaking capacity (Ics) ≥ $I_{sc,LV}$ = 13.33 kA → next standard breaking capacity = **25 kA** (commonly available at 433 V).

**Final ACB Specification:** **800 A, 25 kA Ics, 4-pole ACB** (or 3-pole with switched neutral, depending on project standard).

**Bonus Estimation Note:** This ACB becomes a line item in the BOQ at the LV panel; the estimator also adds the ACB enclosure, bus-bar sizing (per IS 8623), and termination kits.

**[7 Marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting MF (Maintenance Factor):** Many students compute number of luminaires using only UF, omitting MF — a 3-mark deduction is common.
> 2. **Wrong √3 omission:** Three-phase calculations without $\sqrt{3}$ in the denominator lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing %Z formula:** Students sometimes write $\%Z = (V_{rated} / V_{sc}) \times 100$ — this is *inverted*. Correct form: $\%Z = (V_{sc} / V_{rated}) \times 100$.
> 4. **Not rounding up to standard ACB size:** Specifying a 666.5 A ACB instead of the standard 800 A loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Mixing IS code numbers:** A frequent error is quoting "IS 3646" for safety or "IS 5216" for illumination. Memorize: **3646 = Light**, **5216 = Safety**, **2026 = Transformers**.
> 6. **Not stating reference ambient temperature:** For IS 2026 temperature-rise problems, always mention the 45 °C reference ambient.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

✅ **IS 2026 (Power Transformers):**
- Standard Indian code for oil-immersed power transformers.
- Reference ambient temperature: **45 °C**; max oil temperature rise: **50 °C**.
- Standard distribution vector group: **Dyn11**.
- %Z typically **4.5 to 9 %** for distribution class.
- Three-phase rated current formula: $I = S / (\sqrt{3} \cdot V)$ — **memorize this**.

✅ **IS 3646 (Interior Illumination):**
- **Part 1** = principles; **Part 2** = schedule of illumination levels.
- General office: **300 lux**; drawing office: **450–700 lux**; operating theatre: **1,000+ lux**.
- Always apply both **UF (utilization factor)** AND **MF (maintenance factor)** in lumen calculations.
- Formula: $N = (E \cdot A) / (\Phi \cdot UF \cdot MF)$.

✅ **IS 5216 (Electrical Safety):**
- **Part 1** = general; **Part 2** = equipment-specific.
- **Five golden rules**: Disconnect → Secure → Verify → Earth → Protect.
- Safe approach: **415 V → 0.5 m**, **11 kV → 2.5 m**, **33 kV → 3.0 m**.
- Earth resistance for LT equipment: **≤ 1 Ω**; for major substations: **≤ 0.5 Ω**.
- Always specify PPE in the safety BOQ line item.

✅ **Cross-Code Mapping (Quick Recall):**
| Question topic | Code |
|---|---|
| Transformer temperature rise | **IS 2026 Pt. 2** |
| Illumination for a school | **IS 3646 Pt. 2** |
| Lockout-tagout procedure | **IS 5216 Pt. 1** |
| Vector group Dyn11 | **IS 2026** |
| Lumen method of lighting design | **IS 3646 Pt. 1** |
| Earth electrode resistance | **IS 5216 / IS 3043** |

✅ **Numericals to Master Before Exam:**
1. Transformer rated current from kVA.
2. Short-circuit current from %Z.
3. Number of luminaires via lumen method.
4. Earth-resistance verification using touch-voltage formula.
5. Layout spacing for luminaires (SHR check).

✅ **One-Line Memory Hook:**
> **"2026 TRANSFORMS the voltage, 3646 LIGHTS up the room, 5216 KEEPS you SAFE."** 🔌💡⚡

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
