topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electricity Supply Code-2014

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Electricity Supply Code, 2014** is a regulatory document issued by the **Kerala State Electricity Regulatory Commission (KSERC)** under the powers conferred by Section 50 of the **Electricity Act, 2003** read with Section 86(1)(h) of the same Act. It codifies the rules, standards, rights, and obligations governing the relationship between the distribution licensee (primarily **KSEB Ltd.** in Kerala) and the consumers of electrical energy within the State of Kerala.

The Code supersedes all previous supply codes, manual instructions, and circulars that were inconsistent with its provisions. It is the **primary legal and engineering reference** for designing, estimating, executing, and commissioning any electrical installation that is to be connected to the KSEB distribution network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PCEET602 – Module 1):**
> The Supply Code defines the **standards of performance** of the licensee, the **conditions of supply**, the **classification of consumers**, the **technical limits** of load that can be connected at various voltages, the **metering code**, the **billing and payment** mechanism, the **disconnection/reconnection** procedure, and the **safety standards** that every electrical installation must satisfy before it can be energized.

## 1.2 Scope of the Code

The Code is structured into the following major chapters (sub-divisions):

1. **General Provisions** – Short title, extent, definitions, and applicability.
2. **Classification of Consumers and Voltage of Supply** – LT, HT, EHT categories.
3. **Limits of Supply** – Maximum load on single-phase, three-phase, LT, HT, etc.
4. **Application for New Connection / Additional Load** – Procedure and documentation.
5. **Connection Agreement** – The legal contract between licensee and consumer.
6. **Service Line, Meter, and Consumer's Installation** – Ownership, responsibility, and safety.
7. **Metering** – Type of meters, ownership, testing, accuracy class, and seals.
8. **Billing and Payment of Charges** – Billing cycle, methods, surcharge, and dispute.
9. **Disconnection and Reconnection** – Conditions and procedure.
10. **Standards of Performance** – Guaranteed standards and overall standards with compensation.
11. **Quality of Supply** – Voltage, frequency, harmonics, and unbalance limits.
12. **Safety Standards** – Earthing, clearances, and statutory clearances.
13. **General Conditions** – Power factor, harmonics, standby arrangements, etc.
14. **Schedule of Charges** – Schedule of General and Miscellaneous Charges.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU examination purposes, a design engineer is expected to know the **classification, voltage standards, limits of supply, metering rules, and the safety/earthing provisions** because these directly influence the **estimation of materials and the sizing of components** in any design problem.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the Supply Code as the **"Highway Traffic Code"** of the electrical network.

| Traffic Code Analogy | Electricity Supply Code 2014 Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Vehicle registration (Type of vehicle) | Classification of consumer (LT/HT, single/three-phase) |
| Speed limit sign | Limits of supply (Maximum kW that can be connected) |
| Lane discipline and width of road | Voltage of supply (230 V / 415 V / 11 kV / 66 kV) |
| Traffic police inspection | Inspection and testing of installation before energisation |
| Toll collection rules | Billing, tariff, and payment schedule |
| Suspension of driving licence | Disconnection of supply for default |
| Emission norms for vehicles | Harmonics and power-factor standards |
| Insurance and accident rules | Safety standards and earthing requirements |

Just as a driver and a vehicle owner must obey a common code to use public roads, the consumer and the licensee must both follow the Supply Code to use the public electrical network.

> [!TIP]
> When solving KTU numerical problems, always **read the question to identify which "class" of consumer** is involved (e.g., domestic LT single-phase, commercial LT three-phase, industrial HT). The answer (e.g., the applicable supply voltage, the maximum permissible load, or the applicable tariff) is determined entirely by the consumer class as defined in the Supply Code.

## 1.4 Key Definitions (As Per the Code)

| Term | Definition (Simplified) |
|---|---|
| **Licensee** | The KSEB Ltd. (or any other authorised distribution licensee in Kerala). |
| **Consumer** | Any person who is supplied electricity by the licensee. |
| **Sanctioned Load** | The maximum connected load (in kW) approved by the licensee for a consumer. |
| **Contract Demand** | The maximum demand (in kVA) agreed in the connection agreement for HT/EHT consumers. |
| **Connected Load** | The sum of nameplate ratings of all equipment installed at the consumer's premises. |
| **Maximum Demand (MD)** | The highest load drawn by the consumer during a billing period, recorded by a demand meter. |
| **Power Factor** | The cosine of the angle between voltage and current; a measure of how effectively electrical power is being used. |
| **LT (Low Tension)** | Voltage levels up to **650 V** under the Code. |
| **HT (High Tension)** | Voltage levels above **650 V** and up to **33 kV**. |
| **EHT (Extra High Tension)** | Voltage levels above **33 kV** (typically 66 kV, 110 kV, 220 kV). |
| **Service Line** | The line/cable from the distribution main to the consumer's point of supply. |
| **Point of Supply** | The point where the service line terminates and ownership of the installation transfers. |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Consumers

The Code classifies consumers based on **two main criteria**:
1. **Voltage of supply** (LT / HT / EHT).
2. **Purpose of use** (Domestic, Commercial, Industrial, Agricultural, Public Lighting, etc.).

A design engineer must first identify the consumer class because it dictates:
- The applicable **voltage of supply**.
- The **tariff category**.
- The **metering arrangement** (whole-current vs CT-operated).
- The **type of connection agreement**.
- The **statutory clearances** required (e.g., Electrical Inspectorate clearance for HT).

### 2.1.1 LT (Low Tension) Consumers

- Receive supply at **single-phase 230 V** or **three-phase 415 V** at **50 Hz**.
- Meters used are **whole-current (WC) meters** for lower loads and **CT-operated meters** for higher LT loads.
- Connection agreement is relatively simple.
- No separate Electrical Inspectorate clearance is required for loads below the HT threshold.

### 2.1.2 HT (High Tension) Consumers

- Receive supply at voltages such as **11 kV** (most common) or **22 kV** in Kerala.
- **Contract Demand** is specified in the connection agreement (in kVA).
- **CT/PT-operated metering** is mandatory.
- **Compensating capacitors / APFC panels** are mandatory to maintain the power factor above the prescribed limit.
- **Harmonic filters** may be required if non-linear loads are present.
- **Electrical Inspectorate clearance** is required before energisation.
- **Dedicated bay** at the licensee substation may be needed for higher demands.

### 2.1.3 EHT (Extra High Tension) Consumers

- Receive supply at **66 kV, 110 kV, or 220 kV** (rare; typically for large industrial consumers or bulk power consumers).
- Very high Contract Demand (in MVA).
- Subject to stringent power-quality, telemetry, and protection requirements.

## 2.2 Voltage of Supply — A Critical Design Parameter

The Code prescribes the **standard voltages** at which supply is to be given to different classes of consumers:

| Category | Standard Voltage | Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| Single-phase LT | **230 V** (between phase and neutral) | **50 Hz** |
| Three-phase LT | **415 V** (between phases) | **50 Hz** |
| HT (preferred) | **11 kV** or **22 kV** (between phases) | **50 Hz** |
| EHT | **66 kV / 110 kV / 220 kV** | **50 Hz** |

> [!NOTE]
> In India, the standard frequency of supply is **50 Hz** with a permitted variation of **±1 %** (i.e., 49.5 Hz to 50.5 Hz) during peak conditions and **±3 %** (i.e., 48.5 Hz to 51.5 Hz) in extreme emergencies, as per the **Indian Electricity Grid Code (IEGC)**. The Supply Code mandates that the licensee maintain this frequency and voltage within the limits specified by the Central Electricity Authority (CEA).

## 2.3 Limits of Supply (Maximum Load at Each Voltage)

This is the most heavily tested sub-topic in KTU examinations. The Code prescribes the following maximum loads:

| Category | Maximum Permissible Connected Load / Demand |
|---|---|
| Single-phase LT (230 V) | Up to **10 kW** (typical engineering practice in Kerala) |
| Three-phase LT (415 V) | Above 10 kW, up to approximately **150 kVA / 100 kW** |
| HT (11 kV / 22 kV) | Above LT limit, typically up to **5 MVA** at 11 kV |
| EHT (66 kV and above) | Above 5 MVA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The exact numerical limits may be revised by KSERC from time to time through amendments to the Code. For KTU examinations, the student is expected to use the **values prescribed in the latest syllabus notes or as specified in the question**. The principle to remember is: **higher the sanctioned load, higher the voltage of supply** — this is to keep the line current low and reduce the $I^2R$ losses in the distribution network.

## 2.4 Why Voltage Steps Up With Load

Consider a consumer with a sanctioned load of **$P$ kW** supplied at voltage $V$ (line-to-line) with a power factor of $\cos\phi$.

The line current drawn from the supply is:

$$
I = \frac{P \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \, V \, \cos\phi} \;\; \text{(in Amperes, for a 3-phase system)}
$$

For a 3-phase LT system at 415 V, $\cos\phi = 0.85$:

$$
I = \frac{P \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.85} \approx 1.637 \, P \;\; \text{Amperes}
$$

For a 3-phase HT system at 11,000 V, $\cos\phi = 0.85$:

$$
I = \frac{P \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 11000 \times 0.85} \approx 0.0617 \, P \;\; \text{Amperes}
$$

**Observation:** At 11 kV, the current is **26.5 times lower** than at 415 V for the same kW. This drastically reduces copper losses and the size/cost of cables. Hence, the Code mandates HT supply for higher loads.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table is the **cheat-sheet** for all numerical problems on this topic. Memorise these formulas and their units — they appear in almost every KTU examination.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Three-phase line current | $I = \dfrac{P \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \, \cos\phi}$ | $I$ in A, $P$ in kW, $V_L$ in V |
| 2 | Single-phase line current | $I = \dfrac{P \times 1000}{V_{ph} \, \cos\phi}$ | $I$ in A, $P$ in kW, $V_{ph}$ in V |
| 3 | Three-phase apparent power | $S = \sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I$ | kVA |
| 4 | Three-phase active power | $P = \sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I \, \cos\phi$ | kW |
| 5 | Three-phase reactive power | $Q = \sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I \, \sin\phi$ | kVAr |
| 6 | Power triangle relation | $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$ | Universal |
| 7 | Power factor | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P}{S} = \dfrac{\text{kW}}{\text{kVA}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | MD as % of Sanctioned Load | $\text{MD Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Maximum Demand}}{\text{Sanctioned Load}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 9 | Diversity factor | $D_f = \dfrac{\text{Sum of individual Max Demands}}{\text{Maximum Demand on System}}$ | $\geq 1$ |
| 10 | Energy consumed | $E = P \times t$ or $E = \sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I \cos\phi \times t$ | kWh |
| 11 | Line voltage $\to$ phase voltage (Star) | $V_{ph} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ | V |
| 12 | Phase voltage $\to$ line voltage (Delta) | $V_L = \sqrt{3} \, V_{ph}$ | V |
| 13 | Star line current | $I_L = I_{ph}$ | A |
| 14 | Delta line current | $I_L = \sqrt{3} \, I_{ph}$ | A |
| 15 | Power-factor correction capacitor | $Q_c = P (\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$ | kVAr |

> [!NOTE]
> **Strict Table-Syntax Rule:** In the formulas above, the absolute-value or parallel symbol has been written using **\vert** or **\mid** (LaTeX) so that the markdown table structure is **not corrupted** by the bare `\|` character, which markdown would otherwise interpret as a column separator.

## 2.6 Connection Procedure — A Designer's Perspective

The Code prescribes a **time-bound procedure** for granting a new connection. As a designer, you must understand this because your **estimation schedule** must include the time required for sanction, inspection, and energisation.

| Step | Action | Time Limit (Typical) | Designer's Responsibility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Application with documents | Day 0 | Prepare the **load list** and **single-line diagram (SLD)** |
| 2 | Site inspection by licensee | Within 7 days | Provide **site plan** and **route plan** for service line |
| 3 | Estimate and demand note | Within 15 days | Verify the **estimate** for accuracy |
| 4 | Payment by consumer | Within 30 days | Budget for the **security deposit** |
| 5 | Execution of service connection | Within 30–60 days | Coordinate with licensee for **meter and service line** |
| 6 | Inspection by Electrical Inspector (for HT) | As per IE rules | Ensure the **installation complies with IE Rules 1956** |
| 7 | Energisation | After inspection | Provide the **test certificate** and **completion certificate** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Electricity Act, 2003, Section 53** mandates the licensee to supply electricity within **one month** of receiving a complete application in areas already electrified. The Kerala Supply Code reinforces this with specific Service Connection Regulations.

## 2.7 Power Factor Standards and Penalty

The Code prescribes that **all consumers must maintain a power factor of at least 0.85 (lagging)** at the point of supply. Failure to do so attracts:

- A **penalty** in the form of a reactive-energy charge (kVArh billing).
- A **bonus** for maintaining a power factor better than 0.95 (this has been part of KSEB tariff orders).

The reactive-energy charge in kVArh is computed as:

$$
\text{Reactive charge} = \text{Energy in kVArh} \times \text{Tariff rate (Rs/kVArh)}
$$

Hence, the design must include a **capacitor bank (APFC panel)** to compensate reactive power and maintain the power factor within the prescribed band.

## 2.8 Safety Standards — Earthing and Clearances

The Code mandates:

1. **Two separate and distinct earth electrodes** for every consumer installation — one for the system earthing (neutral) and one for the equipment earthing (body). The resistance must be **less than 1 Ω** for HT/EHT and **less than 5 Ω** for LT installations.
2. **Minimum vertical clearance** for overhead service lines as per Indian Electricity Rules (now Central Electricity Authority (Measures relating to Safety and Electric Supply) Regulations, 2010).
3. **Minimum horizontal clearance** between HT and LT lines, and from buildings.
4. **Earth-leakage protection** (ELCB/RCCB) is mandatory for all LT installations.

These safety requirements **directly impact the estimation** of materials — number of earth electrodes, quantity of earth-pit charcoal and salt, length of earth strip, and the rating of RCCBs/ELCBs.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Determination of Supply Voltage from Sanctioned Load

**Problem Statement (Worked):** A commercial complex has a sanctioned load of **85 kW**. The power factor is **0.85 lagging**. Determine the supply voltage (LT or HT) and calculate the line current at the appropriate voltage.

**Step 1 — Identify the consumer class.**
A commercial complex with **85 kW** falls in the **LT three-phase** category (since the load is between 10 kW and ~150 kVA). The standard LT supply voltage is **415 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz**.

**Step 2 — Compute the apparent power in kVA.**

$$
S = \frac{P}{\cos\phi} = \frac{85}{0.85} = 100 \;\; \text{kVA}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the three-phase line current at 415 V.**

$$
I_{LT} = \frac{P \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \, \cos\phi}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{LT} &= \frac{85 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.85} \;\; \text{A} \\
&= \frac{85000}{1.732 \times 415 \times 0.85} \;\; \text{A} \\
&= \frac{85000}{611.022} \;\; \text{A} \\
&\approx 139.11 \;\; \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the 11 kV HT option (for design perspective).**
If the same load were to be supplied at 11 kV:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{HT} &= \frac{85 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 11000 \times 0.85} \;\; \text{A} \\
&= \frac{85000}{1.732 \times 11000 \times 0.85} \;\; \text{A} \\
&= \frac{85000}{16189.22} \;\; \text{A} \\
&\approx 5.25 \;\; \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclude.**
The current at LT (139.11 A) is huge and would require large cross-section cables. As the load increases, the licensee will insist on **HT supply** at 11 kV to reduce the current to manageable levels (5–10 A range). Hence, for loads above approximately **100–150 kVA**, the supply voltage steps up to **11 kV** under the Code.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power-Factor Correction Capacitor Sizing

**Problem Statement:** A factory has a sanctioned load of **500 kW** at 0.75 power factor (lagging). The licensee requires a power factor of **0.95 lagging**. Calculate the rating of the capacitor bank (in kVAr) that must be installed.

**Step 1 — Identify the initial and target power factors.**

$$
\cos\phi_1 = 0.75 \implies \phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.75) = 41.41^\circ
$$

$$
\cos\phi_2 = 0.95 \implies \phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.95) = 18.19^\circ
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the initial reactive power.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\phi_1 &= \tan(41.41^\circ) \approx 0.8819 \\
Q_1 &= P \times \tan\phi_1 = 500 \times 0.8819 \\
&\approx 440.95 \;\; \text{kVAr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the target reactive power.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\phi_2 &= \tan(18.19^\circ) \approx 0.3287 \\
Q_2 &= P \times \tan\phi_2 = 500 \times 0.3287 \\
&\approx 164.35 \;\; \text{kVAr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the capacitor rating required.**

$$
Q_c = Q_1 - Q_2 = P \, (\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_c &= 500 \times (0.8819 - 0.3287) \\
&= 500 \times 0.5532 \\
&\approx 276.6 \;\; \text{kVAr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Practical selection.**
A standard 300 kVAr APFC (Automatic Power-Factor Correction) panel with 12 stages of 25 kVAr each would be specified. The design would also include detuning reactors if harmonic content is high.

> [!TIP]
> The derivation above is the **exact pattern** expected in KTU Part B questions. Always show the tan values from the standard trigonometric table (or compute them with a calculator in the exam and write the result to two decimal places).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Diversity Factor and Sanctioned Load

**Problem Statement:** A residential apartment complex has **20 identical flats**. Each flat has a connected load of **5 kW**. If the diversity factor for the complex is **2.5**, calculate the sanctioned load and the maximum demand.

**Step 1 — Compute the total connected load.**

$$
\text{Total connected load} = 20 \times 5 = 100 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the diversity factor to find the maximum demand.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MD} &= \frac{\text{Sum of individual Max Demands}}{\text{Diversity Factor}} \\
&= \frac{20 \times 5}{2.5} \\
&= 40 \;\; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sanctioned load calculation.**
The sanctioned load is typically set at **80 % of the MD (or rounded up to the next standard kW value)**:

$$
\text{Sanctioned load} = 1.2 \times \text{MD} = 1.2 \times 40 = 48 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

(Rounded up to 50 kW, which is a standard kSEB slab.)

**Step 4 — Conclude.**
The licensee will provide **three-phase LT supply (415 V, 50 Hz)** for this complex because the sanctioned load is greater than 10 kW. The **metering will be CT-operated** at 415 V (whole-current meters are typically limited to 100 A).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Consumer Class vs. Design Requirements

| Consumer Class | Typical Sanctioned Load | Supply Voltage | Meter Type | PF Requirement | Special Requirements |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Domestic (BPL/Ordinary) | 0.5 kW – 5 kW | 1-φ 230 V | Whole-current (WC) | Not enforced | RCCB mandatory |
| Domestic (High-end) | 5 kW – 10 kW | 1-φ 230 V | Whole-current | Not enforced | RCCB + adequate earthing |
| Commercial (Shops, Offices) | 5 kW – 50 kW | 3-φ 415 V | Whole-current / CT | $\geq 0.85$ | Earth-pit, MCB distribution |
| Commercial (Malls, Hotels) | 50 kW – 150 kW | 3-φ 415 V | CT-operated | $\geq 0.90$ | APFC panel, dedicated transformer |
| Industrial (Small) | 100 kW – 1 MW | HT 11 kV | CT-PT | $\geq 0.85$ | APFC, EI clearance, harmonic study |
| Industrial (Large) | 1 MW – 10 MW | HT 11 kV / 22 kV | CT-PT | $\geq 0.90$ | APFC, EI clearance, dedicated bay |
| Bulk Consumer | 10 MW+ | EHT 66/110/220 kV | CT-PT with telemetry | $\geq 0.90$ | SCADA, ABT metering |
| Agricultural | 3–10 HP pumps | 3-φ 415 V | Whole-current | Not enforced | Special tariff, seasonal connection |
| Public Lighting (Street) | 0.5–2 kW/light | 1-φ 230 V | WC with timer | Not enforced | Astronomical timer, LED preferred |

## 3.5 Sample Numerical — Harmonic Distortion and Total Demand Distortion

The Code (and IEEE 519) limits the Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) at the point of common coupling. For an HT consumer, the THD in voltage must be below **5 %** and the Total Demand Distortion (TDD) in current must be below **8 %** for $I_{sc}/I_L \leq 20$ (typical HT consumer).

**Sample calculation (illustrative):**
A 500 kVA HT consumer has $I_{sc}/I_L = 25$. The allowed TDD is **7 %** (interpolated from IEEE 519 table).

$$
I_{TDD,max} = 0.07 \times I_L
$$

If the consumer's load produces harmonics such that the measured TDD is **9 %**, the consumer is **non-compliant** and must install a **harmonic filter** (passive or active) to bring the TDD within the limit.

The Code's stand on this is clear: the licensee can **disconnect or refuse to connect** an installation that violates the quality-of-supply standards, after giving due notice.

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — New Connection Procedure

The diagram below shows the **interactions** between the consumer, the licensee, and the Electrical Inspectorate as per the Supply Code and IE Rules.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant C as Consumer
    participant L as KSEB Licensee
    participant EI as Electrical Inspectorate
    participant M as Meter and Service Crew

    C->>L: Step 1 Submit application with load list and SLD
    L->>C: Step 2 Acknowledge receipt and assign application number
    L->>L: Step 3 Site inspection within 7 days
    L->>C: Step 4 Issue estimate and demand note within 15 days
    C->>L: Step 5 Pay estimate amount and security deposit
    L->>L: Step 6 Design service line and prepare material schedule
    L->>M: Step 7 Dispatch crew to execute service line
    M->>C: Step 8 Install meter and service line at point of supply
    C->>C: Step 9 Complete internal wiring and obtain test report
    alt If HT Installation
        C->>EI: Step 10 Request inspection and clearance
        EI->>L: Step 11 Issue energisation approval
    end
    L->>C: Step 12 Energise the installation and seal the meter
    L->>C: Step 13 Issue first bill at the end of the billing cycle
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Earthing Configuration as Mandated by the Code

The diagram below shows the **mandatory two-earth-pit configuration** that must be provided for every consumer installation. The two pits (system and body) are kept physically separated to prevent dangerous touch-voltages during faults.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["DISTRIBUTION TRANSFORMER 11kV / 415V"]:::src
    meter["METER BASE AND SERVICE CABLE"]:::meter
    eb1["MAIN EARTH PIT 1 SYSTEM NEUTRAL RES LESS THAN 1 OHM"]:::earth1
    eb2["MAIN EARTH PIT 2 EQUIPMENT BODY RES LESS THAN 5 OHM LT"]:::earth2
    eb3["STRUCTURAL EARTH PIT METALLIC FRAMES AND PIPES"]:::earth3
    db["DISTRIBUTION BOARD MCB RCCB ELCB"]:::db
    load1["DOMESTIC LOADS LIGHT FAN SOCKET"]:::load
    load2["POWER LOADS GEYSER AC OVEN"]:::load

    src --> meter --> db
    db --> load1
    db --> load2
    meter -- NEUTRAL WIRE --> eb1
    db -- BODY EARTHING --> eb2
    load2 -- ADDITIONAL BODY EARTHING --> eb2
    load1 -- METALLIC FIXTURE EARTHING --> eb3

    classDef src fill:#003366,color:#ffffff
    classDef meter fill:#fff2cc,color:#000000
    classDef earth1 fill:#d5e8d4,color:#000000
    classDef earth2 fill:#d5e8d4,color:#000000
    classDef earth3 fill:#d5e8d4,color:#000000
    classDef db fill:#f8cecc,color:#000000
    classDef load fill:#e1d5e7,color:#000000
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — APFC Panel Configuration for HT Consumer

The diagram below shows the **Automatic Power-Factor Correction** configuration that an HT consumer must install to comply with the Supply Code's PF standard of 0.85–0.95.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mains["INCOMING 11 kV OR 415 V SUPPLY"]:::mains
    ctt["CTs AND PTs FOR METERING"]:::ctpt
    busbar["MAIN BUSBAR"]:::bus
    load["INDUCTIVE LOADS MOTORS FURNACES TRANSFORMERS"]:::load
    ctrl["APFC RELAY POWER FACTOR CONTROLLER"]:::ctrl
    cap["CAPACITOR BANK 12 STAGES 25 kVAr EACH"]:::cap
    cont["CAPACITOR DUTY CONTACTORS"]:::cont
    det["DETUNING REACTOR 7 PERCENT"]:::det
    fuse["HRC FUSES"]:::fuse

    mains --> ctt --> busbar
    busbar --> load
    busbar --> fuse --> cont --> det --> cap
    cap -.FEEDBACK.-> ctrl
    ctrl -.CONTROL SIGNALS.-> cont
    ctt -.PF MEASUREMENT.-> ctrl
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1: Define the term "Sanctioned Load" as per the Kerala Electricity Supply Code, 2014. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Model Answer (3 marks):**
"Sanctioned Load" means the **maximum load (in kW)** that the licensee has approved for a consumer to be connected to the distribution system, based on the consumer's application and the technical feasibility of supply. The sanctioned load is recorded in the **connection agreement** and forms the basis for:
- Determination of the **applicable tariff slab**.
- Sizing of the **service cable, meter, and protective devices**.
- Computation of the **security deposit** to be paid by the consumer.
The consumer must obtain prior permission from the licensee before exceeding the sanctioned load.

### Question 2: List any **three** safety standards that a consumer's installation must satisfy as per the Supply Code, 2014. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Earthing:** The installation must have at least **two separate earth electrodes**, one for system (neutral) and one for equipment (body) earthing. The earth resistance must be **less than 1 Ω** for HT and **less than 5 Ω** for LT.
2. **Earth-Leakage Protection:** A **RCCB/ELCB** of appropriate rating (typically 30 mA for domestic, 300 mA for commercial) must be provided at the distribution board.
3. **Clearances and Wiring:** All wiring must be carried out as per IS 732 and the National Electrical Code, with proper conduit/ casing-capping, and adequate clearances between HT and LT lines.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): *Limits of Supply and Voltage Selection*

A new industrial unit is to be set up near Ernakulam. The estimated load details are as follows:

| S.No. | Equipment | Qty | Rating (kW) | Power Factor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Induction Motors (3-φ) | 10 | 22 | 0.80 |
| 2 | Lighting Load | – | 30 | 0.95 (unity on resistive) |
| 3 | HVAC Units | 4 | 15 | 0.85 |
| 4 | Welding Transformers | 2 | 12.5 (each) | 0.60 |
| 5 | Office Equipment | – | 25 | 0.90 |

**Solve the following sub-parts:**

#### (a) Calculate the total sanctioned load in kW and kVA, and recommend the appropriate supply voltage and metering arrangement as per the Kerala Electricity Supply Code, 2014. [**7 marks**]

**Step 1 — Active Power (kW) of each category.**

$$
P_1 = 10 \times 22 = 220 \;\; \text{kW (motors)}
$$

$$
P_2 = 30 \;\; \text{kW (lighting)}
$$

$$
P_3 = 4 \times 15 = 60 \;\; \text{kW (HVAC)}
$$

$$
P_4 = 2 \times 12.5 = 25 \;\; \text{kW (welders)}
$$

$$
P_5 = 25 \;\; \text{kW (office)}
$$

$$
P_{total} = 220 + 30 + 60 + 25 + 25 = 360 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the kVA of each category using $S = P / \cos\phi$.**

$$
S_1 = 220 / 0.80 = 275 \;\; \text{kVA}
$$

$$
S_2 = 30 / 0.95 = 31.58 \;\; \text{kVA}
$$

$$
S_3 = 60 / 0.85 = 70.59 \;\; \text{kVA}
$$

$$
S_4 = 25 / 0.60 = 41.67 \;\; \text{kVA}
$$

$$
S_5 = 25 / 0.90 = 27.78 \;\; \text{kVA}
$$

**Step 3 — Total kVA (arithmetic sum is approximate; weighted method is more accurate).**

$$
S_{total} \approx 275 + 31.58 + 70.59 + 41.67 + 27.78 = 446.62 \;\; \text{kVA}
$$

**Step 4 — Determine the overall power factor.**

$$
\cos\phi_{overall} = \frac{P_{total}}{S_{total}} = \frac{360}{446.62} \approx 0.806
$$

**Step 5 — Recommend supply voltage.**
Since the **kVA demand (~447 kVA) exceeds the LT limit (~150 kVA)**, the Supply Code mandates **HT supply at 11 kV**. The metering will be **CT-PT operated**, with a **Contract Demand** specified in the connection agreement. An **APFC panel** is mandatory to improve the power factor to at least 0.90–0.95.

#### (b) Compute the line current at 11 kV, the size of the CT/PT required, and the rating of the capacitor bank needed to improve the power factor to 0.95 lagging. [**7 marks**]

**Step 1 — Line current at 11 kV, three-phase.**

$$
I = \frac{P \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \, \cos\phi_{overall}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{360 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 11000 \times 0.806} \\
&= \frac{360000}{1.732 \times 11000 \times 0.806} \\
&= \frac{360000}{15354.99} \\
&\approx 23.45 \;\; \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — CT/PT selection.**
Standard CT ratio: **30/5 A** (or 50/5 A for safety margin).
Standard PT ratio: **11000/110 V** (i.e., 100:1).

**Step 3 — Capacitor bank rating for PF correction to 0.95.**

$$
\cos\phi_1 = 0.806 \implies \phi_1 = 36.32^\circ, \tan\phi_1 = 0.7355
$$

$$
\cos\phi_2 = 0.95 \implies \phi_2 = 18.19^\circ, \tan\phi_2 = 0.3287
$$

$$
Q_c = P \, (\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2) = 360 \times (0.7355 - 0.3287)
$$

$$
Q_c = 360 \times 0.4068 \approx 146.4 \;\; \text{kVAr}
$$

**Step 4 — Practical selection.**
A standard **150 kVAr APFC panel** with 6 stages of 25 kVAr each is recommended. Detuning reactors (7 %, 189 Hz tuned) are advised to avoid resonance with the welding-transformer harmonics (3rd, 5th, 7th).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often **forget to compute the kVA from each load category separately** and instead divide total kW by an **average power factor**. This gives a wrong kVA total. Always compute the kVA per category and then sum them, or use the weighted formula:
> $$\tan\phi_{avg} = \frac{\sum P_i \tan\phi_i}{\sum P_i}$$
> Avoid mixing PF bases. Each load's PF must be used with its own kW.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): *Power Factor, Sanctioned Load and Sanctioned Demand*

A residential complex has the following load pattern. **Solve the following:**

| S.No. | Block | No. of Flats | Connected Load per Flat (kW) | Diversity Factor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Block A | 12 | 4 | 1.8 |
| 2 | Block B | 16 | 5 | 2.0 |
| 3 | Block C | 20 | 6 | 2.4 |

#### (a) Calculate the total sanctioned load of the complex, the overall diversity factor, and the maximum demand in kVA assuming a power factor of 0.85. [**7 marks**]

**Step 1 — Total connected load per block.**

$$
P_A = 12 \times 4 = 48 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

$$
P_B = 16 \times 5 = 80 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

$$
P_C = 20 \times 6 = 120 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum demand per block (MD = connected load / diversity factor).**

$$
MD_A = 48 / 1.8 = 26.67 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

$$
MD_B = 80 / 2.0 = 40.00 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

$$
MD_C = 120 / 2.4 = 50.00 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Total maximum demand.**

$$
MD_{total} = 26.67 + 40.00 + 50.00 = 116.67 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Sanctioned load calculation (typically 1.2 × MD).**

$$
\text{Sanctioned load} = 1.2 \times 116.67 = 140 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kVA at 0.85 PF.**

$$
S = \frac{P}{\cos\phi} = \frac{140}{0.85} = 164.71 \;\; \text{kVA}
$$

**Step 6 — Recommend supply.**
Since the sanctioned demand is approximately 165 kVA, the supply will be **3-phase LT at 415 V** (just under or near the LT-HT boundary). The licensee may provide HT 11 kV supply with a step-down transformer to keep the LT cable current low.

#### (b) If the licensee insists on HT 11 kV supply, calculate the line current, the size of the cable at LT side, and the savings in copper compared to a pure LT 415 V supply. [**7 marks**]

**Step 1 — Line current at 11 kV (HT side).**

$$
I_{HT} = \frac{140 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 11000 \times 0.85} = \frac{140000}{15864.45} \approx 8.83 \;\; \text{A}
$$

**Step 2 — Line current at 415 V (LT side, after transformer).**

$$
I_{LT} = \frac{140 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.85} = \frac{140000}{611.022} \approx 229.13 \;\; \text{A}
$$

**Step 3 — Cable size selection.**
- HT side: A **3-core 11 kV XLPE cable of 16 mm²** is sufficient (ampacity ~95 A, much higher than 8.83 A).
- LT side: A **4-core 1.1 kV XLPE cable of 95 mm²** is required (ampacity ~260 A, derated for grouping).

**Step 4 — Copper savings.**
Since copper area is proportional to current carried (for a given voltage drop and length), the ratio of copper required is:

$$
\frac{\text{Copper at LT}}{\text{Copper at HT}} = \frac{I_{LT}}{I_{HT}} = \frac{229.13}{8.83} \approx 25.95
$$

Hence, the HT scheme uses **~26 times less copper** than the LT scheme. This is the **primary engineering reason** for stepping up the supply voltage to HT for large loads.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse **Connected Load** with **Sanctioned Load** and **Maximum Demand**. The relationship is:
> - **Connected Load = Sum of nameplate ratings** (always $\geq$ MD).
> - **Maximum Demand = Connected Load / Diversity Factor.**
> - **Sanctioned Load = Round-up of (1.0 to 1.2) × MD.**
> - **Contract Demand = kVA value specified in the agreement (only for HT/EHT).**
> Mixing up these terms will cost you 2–3 marks per part.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:**
> 1. **Voltage Confusion:** Students write **"220 V / 440 V"** (old Indian standard) instead of the current **230 V / 415 V** (post-1987 Indian Standard). Always use the latter.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{3}$ Factor:** Three-phase power calculations **must** include $\sqrt{3}$ in both the power and the line-current formulas. Single-phase formulas have **no** $\sqrt{3}$.
> 3. **Power Factor Sign:** Always specify "**lagging**" or "**leading**" along with the PF value. A PF of 0.85 is meaningless without the qualifier.
> 4. **Earth Resistance Units:** State the value **with units** ($\Omega$) and specify whether it is for LT ($\leq 5 \, \Omega$) or HT ($\leq 1 \, \Omega$).
> 5. **Sanctioned Load vs. Contract Demand:** Use **Sanctioned Load (kW)** for LT and **Contract Demand (kVA)** for HT. Mixing them up shows incomplete understanding of the Supply Code.
> 6. **Forgetting the Diversity Factor:** In housing-complex problems, always apply the diversity factor to the connected load. The MD is always less than the connected load.
> 7. **Capacitor Bank kVAr Rating:** Do not confuse kVAr with kVA. The capacitor rating is in kVAr (reactive), not kVA (apparent).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Statutory Basis:** The Supply Code, 2014 is issued by **KSERC** under the **Electricity Act, 2003**.
- **Standard Voltages in Kerala:** **230 V (1-φ)**, **415 V (3-φ LT)**, **11 kV / 22 kV (HT)**, **66 kV / 110 kV / 220 kV (EHT)**, all at **50 Hz**.
- **Single-Phase Limit:** Up to **10 kW** sanctioned load.
- **Three-Phase LT Limit:** Above 10 kW, up to ~150 kVA.
- **HT Trigger:** Above ~150 kVA → **11 kV or 22 kV HT supply** is mandatory.
- **Contract Demand:** Specified only for **HT and EHT** consumers; recorded in the connection agreement.
- **Sanctioned Load:** The approved **kW** value; basis of tariff and security deposit.
- **Power Factor Standard:** $\geq 0.85$ lagging mandatory; penalty for non-compliance; APFC panel required.
- **Earthing:** **Two separate earth pits** with earth resistance $\leq 1 \, \Omega$ (HT) and $\leq 5 \, \Omega$ (LT).
- **RCCB / ELCB:** Mandatory for **all LT consumer installations**.
- **Metering:** **Whole-current meters** for LT up to ~100 A; **CT/PT-operated meters** for higher LT and all HT.
- **Connection Time Limit:** Licensee must provide connection within **one month** for already-electrified areas.
- **Disconnection Grounds:** Non-payment, meter tampering, unsafe installation, exceeding sanctioned load, PF violation, and harmonic violation.
- **Standards of Performance:** Licensee must pay **compensation** for failure to meet guaranteed standards (e.g., voltage variation, restoration time, meter-testing delay).
- **Harmonic Limit:** Voltage THD $\leq 5$ %; Current TDD $\leq 8$ % (typical HT consumer limit).
- **Safety Regulations:** All installations must comply with the **Central Electricity Authority (Measures relating to Safety and Electric Supply) Regulations, 2010**.
- **Designer's Implication:** Every item in the Supply Code has a **direct cost in the estimate** — earthing pits, RCCBs, APFC panels, meter boards, and EI clearances must all be quantified during the estimation stage.
- **Quick-Reference Rule of Thumb:**  
  - 1 kW at 1-φ 230 V → ~4.35 A.  
  - 1 kW at 3-φ 415 V → ~1.64 A.  
  - 1 kW at 3-φ 11 kV → ~0.062 A.  
  This trio of numbers is the fastest way to verify any current drawn in an exam calculation.
- **Conversion Cheat:** 1 HP (metric) = **0.746 kW**; 1 HP (Imperial) = **0.746 kW**; 1 kW = **1.34 HP**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IE Act 1910
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** IE Act 1910

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Indian Electricity Act, 1910 (Act No. IX of 1910)** is the **first comprehensive legislation in India** enacted by the British colonial Government of India to regulate the generation, transmission, supply, and use of electrical energy throughout the country. It came into force on **1 January 1911** and remained the primary statute governing the Indian electricity sector for over nine decades until it was finally repealed and replaced by the **Electricity Act, 2003 (Act No. 36 of 2003)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The IE Act 1910 is a statutory/legal foundation topic in Module 1 of PCEET602. Students must demonstrate the ability to recall key definitions, sections, licensing rules, and safety provisions. Examiner preference is high for **definitional accuracy** and **section-wise mapping** to practical design and estimation scenarios.

The Act applies to the **whole of India** and contains **40 sections** (originally 40, later amended) grouped into **7 chapters** along with a **Schedule** containing forms of licenses and notices.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Traffic Rules" for Electricity

Imagine an electrical network is like a **national highway system**. Just as motor vehicles cannot simply run anywhere without rules, speed limits, or licensing, electricity too needed a rulebook to:

- Determine **who** could generate and supply power (the "licensed driver").
- Define **how** wires could be laid across public roads.
- Set **safety standards** to protect citizens.
- Impose **penalties** for violations (similar to traffic fines).

Before 1910, electricity was in its infancy — used mainly for street lighting and a few industrial plants. As demand grew, the British government realised the need for a **centralised law** to prevent chaos, electrocutions, and monopolies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of the IE Act 1910 as the "Original Constitution of Indian Electricity."** It created the framework of licensing, safety, and regulation that the modern Electricity Act 2003 inherited and modernised.

## 1.3 Key Stakeholders and Bodies Defined Under the Act

| Term (As defined in Section 2) | Plain-English Meaning |
|-------------------------------|----------------------|
| **Appropriate Government** | The Central Government for inter-state / Union Territory matters; the State Government for intra-state supply. |
| **Electrical Inspector** | A government-appointed qualified engineer authorised to inspect, test, and certify electrical installations. |
| **Licensee** | A person or company holding a valid license to supply electrical energy in a specified area. |
| **Consumer** | Any person who is supplied with electrical energy by a licensee. |
| **Energy** | Electrical energy when used in the context of supply. |
| **Works** | All electrical lines, machinery, apparatus, and equipment used for generation, transmission, or supply. |
| **Street** | Any road, footpath, lane, or public way — including bridges and tunnels. |
| **Railway** | Any railway under the Indian Railways Act. |
| **Major Accident** | An accident causing loss of human life or serious injury — must be reported within **24 hours** under Section 22A. |

## 1.4 Historical and Legal Context (Background for the Examiner)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The IE Act 1910 was enacted in the **reign of King George V** during the Viceroyalty of Lord Hardinge. It received the Governor General's assent on **25 March 1910** and was published in the *Gazette of India* the following day.

**Chronological Evolution of Indian Electricity Law:**

$$\text{Electricity Act, 1910} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Indian Electricity Rules, 1956} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Electricity (Supply) Act, 1948} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Electricity Regulatory Commissions Act, 1998} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Electricity Act, 2003}$$

Despite its 2003 repeal, the IE Act 1910 is **still referenced** in KTU 2024 scheme syllabi because:
1. It establishes the **terminology** modern law continues to use.
2. It defines the **role of Electrical Inspectors** — a function still operational.
3. It provides a **comparative legal foundation** for understanding deregulation in the 2003 Act.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Act

The Act is organised into **Chapters and Sections**. For KTU 2024 Module 1, the following chapters are essential:

### Chapter I — Preliminary (Sections 1–2)
- **Section 1:** Title, extent, and commencement.
- **Section 2:** Definitions of all key terms (the most heavily tested section).

### Chapter II — Licensing of Electrical Supply (Sections 3–11)
This is the **licensing backbone** of the Act.
- **Section 3:** Appointment of Inspectors by the Appropriate Government.
- **Section 4:** License required for non-government/non-local-authority supply.
- **Section 5:** Revocation of licenses.
- **Section 6:** General duties of licensees.
- **Section 7:** Duty to supply on request.
- **Section 8:** Discontinuance of supply.
- **Section 9:** Power to enter premises for inspection.
- **Section 10:** Recovery of charges.
- **Section 11:** Provisions relating to non-licensee supply (e.g., generators, captive use).

### Chapter III — Works, Lines, and Supply Apparatus (Sections 12–19)
- **Section 12:** Power of licensee to place and maintain works on private property.
- **Section 13:** Power to break up streets, railways, tramways.
- **Section 14:** Notice required before commencing new works.
- **Section 15:** Power to lay cables in pipes, conduits, etc.
- **Section 16:** Use of existing structures (poles, buildings).
- **Section 17:** Compensation for damage.
- **Section 18:** Laying of supply lines near other lines.
- **Section 19:** Power of Telegraph authority to attach wires.

### Chapter IV — Safety Provisions (Sections 20–26)
- **Section 20:** Neutral conductor earthing.
- **Section 21:** Precautions against earth-leakage.
- **Section 22:** Safety regulations and protective equipment.
- **Section 22A:** Reporting of major accidents (within **24 hours**).
- **Section 23:** Power to Electrical Inspector to enter and inspect.
- **Section 24:** Power to test installations.
- **Section 25:** Defects to be remedied by owner/occupier.
- **Section 26:** Power to cut off supply in unsafe installations.

### Chapter V — Disputes, Arbitration, and Penalties (Sections 27–36)
- **Section 27:** Disputes between licensee and consumer.
- **Section 28:** Arbitration by Electrical Inspector.
- **Section 29:** Penalty for offences under Sections 21, 22, 24.
- **Section 30:** Penalty for non-compliance of safety rules.
- **Section 31:** Penalty for illegal supply.
- **Section 32:** Penalty for tampering with meters.
- **Section 33:** Penalty for interference with works.
- **Section 34:** Penalty for contravention of Section 11.
- **Section 35:** Cognizance of offences.
- **Section 36:** Trial of offences.

### Chapter VI — Miscellaneous (Sections 37–40)
- **Section 37:** Power of State Government to make rules.
- **Section 38:** Power of Local Government (now Central Government) to make rules.
- **Section 39:** Protection of licensees acting in good faith.
- **Section 40:** Repeals and savings.

### Chapter VII — Provisions Regarding Generating Companies (Section 40A and 40B)
Added later to address captive power and generating stations.
- **Section 40A:** Restrictions on generation.
- **Section 40B:** Rights of generating companies.

## 2.2 KTU Formula & Reference Sheet (High-Yield Quick Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> Since IE Act 1910 is a legal/regulatory topic, the "formula sheet" is a structured reference of **numerical thresholds, deadlines, and section-based obligations** that are commonly tested in 2-mark/3-mark KTU questions.

| Reference / Threshold | Value / Specification | Section Reference |
|----------------------|-----------------------|-------------------|
| **Maximum supply voltage to a consumer (DC, low voltage)** | **250 V DC** | Section 2(Definitions) |
| **Maximum low-tension AC voltage** | **250 V AC** | Section 2 |
| **Medium voltage upper limit** | **650 V** | Section 2 |
| **High voltage lower limit** | Above **650 V** | Section 2 |
| **Notice period for new works** | **1 month** minimum before commencement | Section 14 |
| **Reporting of major accidents** | Within **24 hours** | Section 22A |
| **Penalty maximum (Section 29, simple offence)** | Up to **Rs. 1,000** + daily fine Rs. 50 | Section 29 |
| **Penalty for meter tampering** | Up to **Rs. 500** fine, or 3 months imprisonment | Section 32 |
| **Appointing authority for Electrical Inspector** | State Government for intra-state; Central for inter-state | Section 3 |
| **License validity (default)** | As specified in license terms (typically 21–25 years) | Section 4 |
| **Inspection frequency of generating stations** | At least once a year by Electrical Inspector | Section 23 |
| **Period for licensee to remedy defects** | As specified in notice (usually 7–30 days) | Section 25 |

## 2.3 Detailed Theoretical Analysis of Key Sections

### Section 2 — Definitions (The Most Tested Section)

This section is the **alphabet of electricity law**. The KTU examiner regularly tests definitions such as:
- **"Appropriate Government"** — bifurcated responsibility between Centre and State.
- **"Electrical Inspector"** — must be a person holding a **degree in Electrical Engineering** (or equivalent) with prescribed experience; appointed by gazette notification.
- **"Licensee"** — any person licensed under Part II of the Act to supply energy. Holding a license is **mandatory** for any person (other than Government) supplying energy to others.
- **"Consumer"** — distinct from "consumer of electrical energy"; the term refers to the **end user** who pays for electricity.

### Section 6 — General Duties of Licensees

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **favourite 7-mark question topic** in KTU Module 1. Memorise the four key duties:

1. **Maintain supply** in accordance with license conditions.
2. **Ensure safety** of the public from electrical hazards.
3. **Provide connection** on reasonable request (subject to technical feasibility).
4. **Maintain a register of complaints** and address grievances.

A licensee who **wilfully** or **negligently** fails any of these duties is liable for penalties under Section 29.

### Section 11 — Non-licensee Supply (Captive/Restricted Use)

> [!NOTE]
> Section 11 allows supply of electrical energy **without a license** in the following cases:
> - **Generators** of less than 1 MW for the **generator's own use** (subject to safety conditions).
> - Supply to **employees, tenants, or co-owners** of the generating premises.
> - Supply for **charitable, agricultural, or industrial use** within a **500-metre radius** of the generation point, provided no line crosses a public street.

This is the **statutory origin of "captive power"** in Indian law — a concept expanded greatly in the Electricity Act 2003.

### Section 22A — Reporting of Accidents (KTU Favourite)

- Any **major accident** causing loss of life or serious injury must be reported to the **Electrical Inspector** within **24 hours**.
- A **preliminary report** must be sent within **48 hours**.
- The Inspector is empowered to **enter, inspect, photograph, and seize** the installation.
- A **final inquiry report** is published for public record.

### Section 28 — Arbitration

Disputes regarding:
- **Amount of compensation** for damage caused by works.
- **Recovery of expenses** for relocations.
- **Apportionment of joint costs** between licensees.

…are referred to the **Electrical Inspector**, who acts as the **arbitrator** under the **Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996** (as adopted by the relevant State).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

For an electrical system design and estimation professional, the IE Act 1910 (and its successor 2003 Act) governs:

- **Approval workflows** — designs must conform to safety standards enforced under this Act.
- **Right-of-way procedures** — for transmission lines crossing public or private land.
- **Inspection and certification** — before energising a new installation, the Electrical Inspector issues a fitness certificate.
- **Penalty exposure** — non-compliance can attract criminal prosecution and fine up to **Rs. 1 lakh per day** in the 2003 Act.
- **Statutory compliance in estimation** — every estimate must include provisions for **safety equipment, earthing, and statutory clearances** as mandated by the Act and the Indian Electricity Rules, 1956.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** A "design and estimate" that ignores statutory requirements is not a complete design. The IE Act 1910, although repealed, still forms the **legal vocabulary and operational framework** of Indian electricity regulation.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Section-Wise Mapping

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Section-to-Application Mapping

This matrix is designed for KTU Module 1 question preparation. Each row maps a section to its **practical application** in electrical system design.

| Section | Statutory Provision | Engineering Application | Penalty / Consequence |
|---------|--------------------|-------------------------|----------------------|
| **2** | Definitions of voltage classes (LV/ MV/ HV) | Determines the **design voltage class** of cables, switchgear, and clearances. | None — definitional. |
| **3** | Appointment of Electrical Inspector | Sets the **certifying authority** for any new installation. | Inspector's certificate mandatory before energising. |
| **4** | License for supply | Defines who can sell electricity — basis of **DISCOMs and licensees**. | Imprisonment up to **3 months** + fine for unlicensed supply. |
| **6** | Duties of licensees | Imposes **24×7 supply obligation** with safety duty. | Fine up to **Rs. 1,000** + Rs. 50/day. |
| **7** | Duty to supply on request | **Standard-of-service** obligation in distribution engineering. | Compensation if supply denied without reason. |
| **9** | Entry for inspection | Right of licensee to **enter premises for meter reading / inspection**. | None — but obstruction is punishable. |
| **11** | Non-licensee supply rules | Governs **captive power plants, DG sets, solar rooftop**. | Must be ≤ 500 m radius, no public street crossing. |
| **12** | Placing works on private property | **Easement and right-of-way** for distribution lines. | Compensation paid as per Section 17. |
| **13** | Breaking up streets, railways | **Permit requirement** for trenching roads for cable laying. | Restoration at licensee's cost. |
| **14** | Notice for new works (1 month) | **Statutory clearance workflow** for new projects. | Works must not begin prematurely. |
| **20** | Earthing of neutral | **Neutral earthing mandatory** — basis of TN/TT/IT systems. | Fine for non-compliance. |
| **21** | Earth-leakage protection | **ELCB/RCD requirement** in every consumer installation. | Fine up to Rs. 1,000. |
| **22** | Safety regulations | **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** for all line workers. | Mandatory. |
| **22A** | Reporting major accidents | **Incident reporting protocol** — 24-hour rule. | Failure = prosecution. |
| **23** | Power to inspect | **Annual inspection** of generating stations and substations. | Inspector's report is statutory. |
| **24** | Testing installations | **Pre-commissioning tests** — insulation, earth resistance, etc. | Failed test = no energisation. |
| **28** | Arbitration of disputes | **Dispute resolution** between licensee and consumer. | Award is binding. |
| **32** | Meter tampering | **Sealing of meters** — anti-tamper design. | Fine up to Rs. 500 + 3 months imprisonment. |

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Voltage Classification Logic

The IE Act 1910 (via Section 2 and the associated Rules) divides supply voltages into discrete bands. Although these are **definitions**, the logic of classification can be derived as follows:

### Step 1: Define the threshold of "extra-low voltage"

$$\text{Extra-Low Voltage (ELV)} \leq 50 \text{ V AC} \quad \text{or} \quad 120 \text{ V DC}$$

**Reasoning:** Below this threshold, electrocution risk is considered minimal in dry conditions. The choice of **50 V AC** comes from international standards (IEC 60449) adopted by India's IE Rules.

### Step 2: Define the upper bound of "low voltage"

$$\text{Low Voltage (LV)} = 51 \text{ V} \leq V \leq 250 \text{ V AC} \quad \text{or} \quad \leq 250 \text{ V DC}$$

**Reasoning:** The **250 V threshold** corresponds to the historical DC distribution standard of 110 V, 220 V, and 240 V DC systems. The Act adopted 250 V as the upper limit of LV, encompassing all standard domestic supplies in the early 20th century.

### Step 3: Define "medium voltage"

$$250 \text{ V} < V \leq 650 \text{ V AC}$$

**Reasoning:** Voltages above 250 V (e.g., **415 V three-phase**, **440 V industrial**) were considered "medium" because they required enhanced insulation but were still within the operational range of standard industrial switchgear.

### Step 4: Define "high voltage"

$$V > 650 \text{ V AC}$$

**Reasoning:** Above 650 V, the **creepage distance, clearance, and safety requirements** change drastically. All voltages exceeding 650 V (i.e., **11 kV, 22 kV, 33 kV, 66 kV, 110 kV, 220 kV, 400 kV, 765 kV** transmission) are classified as HV.

### Step 5: Consolidate

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Voltage Class} &= 
\begin{cases}
\text{ELV}, & V \leq 50 \text{ V AC} \\
\text{LV}, & 50 \text{ V} < V \leq 250 \text{ V AC} \\
\text{MV}, & 250 \text{ V} < V \leq 650 \text{ V AC} \\
\text{HV}, & V > 650 \text{ V AC} \\
\text{EHV}, & V \geq 220 \text{ kV (modern addition)}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}$$

**Why this matters in design:** The voltage class dictates **cable insulation thickness**, **creepage distance for insulators**, **safety clearances in substations**, and **authorisation levels** for personnel. A worker authorised to handle LV is not legally allowed to work on HV systems under the Act.

## 3.3 Numerical / Quantitative Application Example

**Example Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> A 22 kV transmission line passes through a residential area. A licensee plans to lay a new 11 kV feeder 3 metres away from the existing 22 kV line. Discuss the applicability of the IE Act 1910 sections, and determine:
> (a) The voltage class of each line.
> (b) The statutory notice period before starting work.
> (c) The clearance required.
> (d) The penalty if a worker is electrocuted due to non-compliance.

### Solution:

**(a) Voltage Class:**

$$\begin{aligned}
22 \text{ kV} &= 22{,}000 \text{ V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{High Voltage (HV)} \\
11 \text{ kV} &= 11{,}000 \text{ V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{High Voltage (HV)}
\end{aligned}$$

Both lines are **HV** under Section 2 of IE Act 1910.

**(b) Notice Period:**
Per Section 14, the licensee must serve a **written notice of 1 month** to all parties whose premises the line will cross, including the local authority. If the line crosses a **public street** or **railway**, additional approvals from the Public Works Department (PWD) and Railways are required under Section 13.

**(c) Clearance:**
For two parallel HV lines, the **Indian Electricity Rules 1956 (Rule 79)** specify:

$$d_{\text{min}} = k \cdot \sqrt{S} + 0.2 \text{ m}$$

where $S$ is the **sum of voltages in kV** of the two lines and $k$ is a constant (typically 0.3).

For 11 kV + 22 kV:

$$S = 11 + 22 = 33 \text{ kV}$$

$$d_{\text{min}} = 0.3 \cdot \sqrt{33} + 0.2 \approx 0.3 \cdot 5.74 + 0.2 \approx 1.92 \text{ m}$$

The proposed **3 m separation is acceptable**.

**(d) Penalty:**
If a worker is electrocuted due to non-compliance with safety regulations under Section 22:
- The licensee is liable under **Section 29** — fine up to **Rs. 1,000** + **Rs. 50 per day** of continued violation.
- The Electrical Inspector may invoke **Section 26** to **disconnect supply** until defects are remedied.
- Civil liability for compensation under the **Workmen's Compensation Act, 1923** may also apply.
- Under the **modern Electricity Act 2003 (Sections 53, 146)**, penalties are much higher — up to **Rs. 1 lakh per day** for safety violations.

## 3.4 Summary of Statutory Deadlines (Quick Reference for KTU)

| Event | Statutory Deadline | Section |
|-------|-------------------|---------|
| Notice for new works | 1 month prior | 14 |
| Major accident report | 24 hours | 22A |
| Preliminary accident report | 48 hours | 22A |
| Reply to defect notice | 7 days (default) | 25 |
| License revocation notice | 6 months | 5 |
| Arbitrator's award | 6 months | 28 |
| Annual inspection of generating stations | At least once per year | 23 |
| Period to remedy non-compliance | As specified in notice (7–30 days) | 25 |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2–3 Marks)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]
**Define the term "Licensee" as per Section 2 of the Indian Electricity Act, 1910. What is the statutory consequence of supplying electricity without a license?**

**Model Answer:**

A "Licensee" is defined under Section 2 of the IE Act 1910 as a **person or company** holding a valid license granted by the Appropriate Government authorising them to **transmit, distribute, or supply electrical energy** in a specified area for a defined period. The license is granted under Section 4 of the Act.

The statutory consequences of supplying electricity without a license are:
1. **Penalty under Section 31** of the IE Act 1910 — fine up to **Rs. 1,000** and/or imprisonment up to **3 months**.
2. **Punishment under Section 135 of the Electricity Act 2003** (the successor law) — imprisonment up to **3 years** and/or fine up to **Rs. 1 lakh**.
3. **Civil liability** — the consumer can sue for damages due to negligence.
4. **Revocation of all connected works** by the Electrical Inspector under Section 26.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse "Licensee" with "Consumer" or "Generating Company." The Licensee is **always the entity that supplies** energy to others. A person who generates only for self-use is **not** a licensee (per Section 11 exemption).

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]
**State the procedure for reporting a major electrical accident as per Section 22A of the IE Act, 1910. List the duties of the Electrical Inspector upon receiving such a report.**

**Model Answer:**

**Reporting Procedure (Section 22A):**

1. The occurrence of any **major accident** causing loss of human life or serious injury must be reported **forthwith** (i.e., without delay) to the **Electrical Inspector**.
2. The **preliminary report** must be submitted within **24 hours** of the accident.
3. A **detailed report** with root-cause analysis and remedial measures must follow within **7 days** (as per IE Rules 1956, Rule 44A).
4. The report must be sent in writing to the Inspector's office, with a copy to the District Magistrate.

**Duties of the Electrical Inspector:**

1. **Enter and inspect** the premises where the accident occurred (Section 23).
2. **Photograph and seize** evidence (Section 23).
3. **Conduct an inquiry** to determine the cause.
4. **Issue directions** to remedy defects under Section 25.
5. **Cut off supply** if the installation is unsafe (Section 26).
6. **Forward the inquiry report** to the State Government for further action.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students omit the **"24 hours"** and **"48 hours"** deadlines. Always quote the **exact statutory time limits** — this is a high-value marker for KTU evaluators.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Solutions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — [14 Marks, KTU ESE Pattern — Module 1 Choice]
**"Discuss the licensing framework under the Indian Electricity Act, 1910. Explain the procedure for grant, conditions, revocation, and statutory duties of a licensee, with reference to relevant sections."**

#### Part (a) — Licensing Framework and Grant of License (7 Marks)

**Solution Model:**

The licensing framework under the IE Act 1910 is laid out in **Chapter II (Sections 3 to 11)**. The following is the step-by-step analysis:

**1. Statutory Authority for Grant (Section 4):**
- A license to supply electrical energy can be granted by the **Appropriate Government**.
- For **intra-state** supply → **State Government** is the appropriate authority.
- For **inter-state** or **union territory** supply → **Central Government** is the appropriate authority.

**2. Eligibility and Application:**
- Any person, company, or local authority may apply to the Appropriate Government.
- The application must specify:
  - The **area of supply**.
  - The **nature of supply** (general or specific purpose).
  - The **technical capacity** and **financial soundness** of the applicant.
  - The **proposed voltage class** and **maximum demand**.

**3. Procedure for Grant:**
- The application is **referred to the Electrical Inspector** (Section 3) for technical evaluation.
- A **public notice** is issued inviting objections within **30 days** (Section 4(1)).
- If objections are received, a **public hearing** is conducted.
- The State Government, after considering the Inspector's report and objections, may grant the license **with or without conditions**.

**4. Statutory Conditions of License (Section 4 Sub-clause):**
- The license is granted for a **specific term** (typically **25 years**, renewable).
- The licensee must **furnish security** (Section 5) in the form of a **bank guarantee or cash deposit**.
- The licensee must **comply with all rules** made under the Act and the IE Rules 1956.
- The licensee must **supply to all applicants** in the area of supply on reasonable request (Section 7).

**5. Period and Renewal:**
- The license is valid for the term specified in the license document.
- Renewal is subject to satisfactory performance and is granted by the same authority.

---

#### Part (b) — Revocation, Duties, and Penalties (7 Marks)

**Solution Model:**

**1. Revocation of License (Section 5):**
- The Appropriate Government may revoke a license on grounds such as:
  - **Breach of license conditions**.
  - **Persistent failure to supply** in the licensed area.
  - **Public safety hazard** caused by the licensee's works.
  - **Insolvency or financial mismanagement**.
- A **6-month notice** is given before revocation.
- The licensee has the right to be **heard** before revocation.

**2. General Duties of Licensees (Section 6):**
1. **Maintain continuous supply** in accordance with license conditions, subject to permitted outages.
2. **Ensure public safety** by adhering to all statutory safety standards (Sections 20, 21, 22).
3. **Grant new connections** on reasonable request, subject to technical feasibility.
4. **Maintain accurate records** of consumers, supply, and equipment.
5. **Pay all statutory fees** to the Government.

**3. Penalty for Breach of Duties (Sections 29–34):**

| Breach | Section | Penalty |
|--------|---------|---------|
| Failure to comply with safety regulations (S21, S22) | 29 | Fine up to **Rs. 1,000** + Rs. 50/day |
| Unauthorised supply without license | 31 | Fine up to **Rs. 1,000** + 3 months imprisonment |
| Tampering with meters | 32 | Fine up to **Rs. 500** + 3 months imprisonment |
| Obstruction of Electrical Inspector | 33 | Fine up to **Rs. 200** |
| Failure to comply with Section 11 (non-licensee supply) | 34 | Fine up to **Rs. 500** |

**4. Modern Penalties (Electricity Act 2003):**
The successor law imposes much stricter penalties:
- Section 135: Theft of electricity — fine up to **Rs. 1 lakh** + imprisonment up to **3 years**.
- Section 138: Unauthorised use of electricity — fine up to **Rs. 50,000**.
- Section 146: General penalty — fine up to **Rs. 1 lakh per day** for continuing violations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question A:**
> 1. **Do not** discuss the Electricity Act 2003 unless specifically asked. The 2003 Act is in **Module 2** of KTU 2024. Stay focused on 1910 sections.
> 2. **Always quote section numbers** — examiners award marks for section-specific accuracy.
> 3. **Use a tabular format** for penalties — it is easier to read and gets full marks.

---

### QUESTION B — [14 Marks, KTU ESE Pattern — Module 1 Choice]
**"Explain the safety provisions of the Indian Electricity Act, 1910 with reference to earthing, accident reporting, and the powers of the Electrical Inspector. How do these provisions influence the design and estimation of an electrical installation?"**

#### Part (a) — Earthing, Earth-Leakage Protection, and Safety Regulations (7 Marks)

**Solution Model:**

The safety provisions of the IE Act 1910 are codified in **Chapter IV (Sections 20 to 26)**. These provisions form the **statutory backbone of safe electrical design**.

**1. Neutral Earthing (Section 20):**
- The **neutral conductor of every AC installation** must be **earthed** at the generating station or substation.
- The earthing must be done using a **dedicated earth electrode** with resistance **not exceeding 1 ohm** for high-voltage installations (per IE Rules 1956, Rule 61).
- The earth connection must be **mechanically robust** and protected from corrosion.
- **Design implication:** Every estimate must include **earthing materials** — earth electrodes (GI pipe, copper rod), earth-enhancing compound, inspection chambers, and maintenance provisions.

**2. Earth-Leakage Protection (Section 21):**
- All **low-voltage installations** must incorporate **earth-leakage protection devices** to disconnect supply in case of earth faults.
- The protective device must operate at a **leakage current not exceeding 30 mA** for domestic installations (per IE Rules 1956, Rule 73A).
- Common devices used: **ELCB (Earth Leakage Circuit Breaker)**, **RCD (Residual Current Device)**, and **RCCB (Residual Current Circuit Breaker)**.
- **Design implication:** Estimates must include **RCCBs at the consumer main board**, and a **continuity test** of the earth conductor is mandatory.

**3. Safety Regulations (Section 22):**
- All **personnel working on electrical installations** must be provided with **appropriate PPE** — insulating gloves, boots, helmets, line hose, earthing rods, etc.
- **Live-line work** is prohibited unless specifically authorised and performed under strict supervision.
- **High-voltage equipment** must be enclosed in a **fenced compound** with **interlocked gates**.
- **Warning signs** ("DANGER — HIGH VOLTAGE") must be displayed.

**4. Safety Procedures for Lines (Section 22A):**
- Any **major accident** causing loss of life or serious injury must be reported to the Electrical Inspector within **24 hours**.
- A preliminary report within **48 hours**, and detailed report within **7 days**.
- All accidents must be entered in a **statutory accident register** maintained at every installation.

---

#### Part (b) — Powers of the Electrical Inspector and Design Implications (7 Marks)

**Solution Model:**

**1. Powers of the Electrical Inspector (Sections 23–26):**

**(i) Power of Entry and Inspection (Section 23):**
- The Electrical Inspector may enter, inspect, and examine **any premises** where electrical works are in progress or in use.
- Inspection frequency: **at least once per year** for generating stations and HV substations.
- The Inspector may be accompanied by assistants or technical experts.
- The licensee must provide **full access and cooperation**.

**(ii) Power to Test (Section 24):**
- The Inspector may require the licensee or owner to **conduct tests** of the installation under his supervision.
- Common tests include:
  - **Insulation resistance test** (≥ 1 megohm for LV, ≥ 5 megohm for HV).
  - **Earth resistance test** (≤ 1 ohm for HV stations, ≤ 5 ohm for LV).
  - **Continuity test** of protective conductors.
  - **Dielectric voltage withstand test** for HV equipment.
- **Design implication:** Estimates must include provisions for **testing instruments** (megger, earth tester, HV test kit) and **labour for testing**.

**(iii) Power to Issue Defect Notices (Section 25):**
- If the Inspector finds any **defect or unsafe condition**, he issues a **written notice** to the owner/licensee specifying the **defect and the time limit** for rectification.
- The owner must comply within the specified period (typically 7–30 days).
- Failure to comply is a punishable offence.

**(iv) Power to Cut Off Supply (Section 26):**
- If the defect is **immediately hazardous** and not remedied, the Inspector may **order disconnection of supply**.
- Reconnection is allowed only after **defects are rectified and re-inspected**.

**2. How These Provisions Influence Design and Estimation:**

**(i) Statutory Approvals in Design:**
- Every new installation must be **approved by the Electrical Inspector** before commissioning.
- The design drawings, single-line diagrams, and protection scheme must be submitted for **statutory review**.

**(ii) Safety Provision Budgeting in Estimation:**
The estimate must allocate costs for:
- **Earthing system** (Section 20) — earth electrodes, GI/Cu flats, earth pits.
- **Earth-leakage protection** (Section 21) — RCCB, ELCB.
- **Safety equipment** (Section 22) — PPE, warning signs, fencing, interlocks.
- **Testing and commissioning** (Section 24) — instruments, labour, certificates.
- **Compliance documentation** (Sections 22A, 25) — accident registers, defect registers.

**(iii) Risk and Penalty Budgeting:**
- A non-compliance **contingency** of 2–5% of the project cost should be added to cover potential statutory fines and rectification costs.
- Example contingency calculation:

$$C_{\text{contingency}} = \text{Project Cost} \times 0.03$$

For a Rs. 1 crore project:

$$C_{\text{contingency}} = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.03 = 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

**3. Real-World Example — Substation Design:**

Consider a **11 kV / 433 V distribution substation** of 500 kVA capacity. The design must include:

- **Earthing** (Section 20): 4 earth pits with resistance ≤ 1 ohm each, interconnected by a 50×6 mm GI flat.
- **Earthing conductor sizing** (per IE Rules 1956, Rule 67):

$$A = \frac{I \cdot t}{K} \quad \text{(simplified adiabatic equation)}$$

For a 3-phase fault current $I = 25$ kA and fault clearing time $t = 1$ s, with $K = 80$ for steel:

$$A = \frac{25000 \cdot 1}{80} = 312.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

Use **50×6 mm GI flat (300 mm²) plus allowance** — close to the minimum required.

- **RCCB at LV side** (Section 21): 300 mA RCCB for substation auxiliary supply.
- **Statutory inspection** (Section 23): provision for **one annual inspection visit** with associated coordination costs.
- **Accident register** (Section 22A): printed register kept in substation control room.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question B:**
> 1. **Do not** skip the design implications — examiners want to see how law translates to **engineering practice**. A purely legal answer will score 7 out of 14 marks only.
> 2. **Quantify the design** — use numerical examples (voltages, fault currents, earth resistance) to demonstrate mastery.
> 3. **Connect to the course** — explicitly link each statutory provision to a **line item in the electrical estimate**. This shows "design + estimation" integration.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's General Valuation Warning (Read Carefully)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons for Loss of Marks in PCEET602 — IE Act 1910 Questions:**
> 1. **Quoting wrong section numbers** — Section 22A is for accident reporting; Section 22 is for safety regulations. Do not confuse them.
> 2. **Confusing the 1910 Act with the 2003 Act** — The 1910 Act is about licensing, safety, and basic rights. The 2003 Act is about deregulation, open access, and competition. Stay in your module.
> 3. **Omitting time limits** — Always quote the **24 hours** for accident reporting, **1 month** for new work notice, and **6 months** for license revocation.
> 4. **Forgetting the schedule forms** — The Act contains Form A (License) and Form B (Notice). Examiners occasionally test awareness of these.
> 5. **Not using Indian-context examples** — Use **Rs. (INR)**, Indian kV levels (11 kV, 22 kV, 110 kV, 220 kV), and Indian bodies (CEA, Electrical Inspector, DISCOMs).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this as your last-minute revision sheet before the KTU exam.**

### Key Sections to Memorise

- **Section 2:** Definitions (LV ≤ 250 V, MV ≤ 650 V, HV > 650 V).
- **Section 3:** Electrical Inspector appointed by Appropriate Government.
- **Section 4:** License required for supply (except Section 11 exemption).
- **Section 5:** License revocation — 6-month notice required.
- **Section 6:** General duties of licensees (4 key duties).
- **Section 7:** Duty to supply on request.
- **Section 11:** Non-licensee supply — captive, < 500 m radius, no public street.
- **Section 12:** Works on private property — with compensation.
- **Section 13:** Breaking up streets — permission required.
- **Section 14:** Notice of new works — 1 month prior.
- **Section 20:** Neutral earthing mandatory.
- **Section 21:** Earth-leakage protection mandatory.
- **Section 22:** Safety regulations and PPE.
- **Section 22A:** Accident reporting within **24 hours**.
- **Section 23:** Annual inspection of generating stations.
- **Section 24:** Testing power.
- **Section 26:** Cut off supply for unsafe installations.
- **Section 28:** Arbitration by Electrical Inspector.
- **Sections 29–34:** Penalties for various offences.
- **Section 40A:** Restrictions on generation.
- **Section 40B:** Rights of generating companies.

### Critical Numbers (Must Be Quoted Exactly)

- **250 V** = Low voltage upper limit.
- **650 V** = Medium voltage upper limit.
- **24 hours** = Accident reporting deadline.
- **1 month** = Notice period for new works.
- **6 months** = License revocation notice period.
- **30 days** = Public notice period for licensing objections.
- **30 mA** = Maximum leakage current for domestic RCCB.
- **1 ohm** = Maximum earth resistance for HV stations.

### Historical / Contextual Points

- Enacted in **1910** (Act IX of 1910).
- Came into force on **1 January 1911**.
- Replaced by **Electricity Act 2003** (Act 36 of 2003) on **10 June 2003**.
- Operational along with **Indian Electricity Rules 1956** until 2003.
- Vocabulary and roles still in use today.

### Practical Engineering Linkages

- **Earthing system design** ← Section 20.
- **RCCB/ELCB selection** ← Section 21.
- **PPE budgeting** ← Section 22.
- **Accident register maintenance** ← Section 22A.
- **Statutory testing** ← Section 24.
- **Defect rectification** ← Section 25.
- **Load-shedding / disconnections** ← Section 26.
- **Statutory contingency** in estimates ← Sections 29–34 penalties.

### One-Line Definitions for Quick Recall

- **"Appropriate Government"** = Centre for inter-state; State for intra-state.
- **"Electrical Inspector"** = A gazetted officer holding an electrical engineering degree, appointed to inspect and certify installations.
- **"Licensee"** = A person or company authorised by license to supply electrical energy.
- **"Consumer"** = Any person who is supplied with electrical energy by a licensee for own use.
- **"Works"** = All electrical lines, machinery, and apparatus used for generation, transmission, or supply.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When answering any KTU Module 1 question on the IE Act 1910, **always start with a one-sentence definition, then quote the section number, then explain the engineering implication.** This three-part structure guarantees full marks across all KTU cognitive levels (Remember, Understand, Apply).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IE Act 2003
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IE Act 2003

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Indian Electricity Act, 2003 (IE Act 2003)** is the omnibus legislation enacted by the Parliament of India on **26 March 2003** (notified on 10 June 2003) that consolidates the laws relating to **generation, transmission, distribution, trading, and use of electricity**. It repealed three prior Acts:
- The Indian Electricity Act, 1910
- The Electricity (Supply) Act, 1948
- The Electricity Regulatory Commissions Act, 1998

The Act contains **185 Sections** distributed across **13 Parts (Chapters)**, **5 Schedules**, and establishes a multi-tiered regulatory framework comprising the **Central Electricity Authority (CEA)**, **Central Electricity Regulatory Commission (CERC)**, **State Electricity Regulatory Commissions (SERCs)**, and the **Appellate Tribunal for Electricity (APTEL)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** From an *Electrical System Design and Estimation* perspective, IE Act 2003 is the **statutory backbone** that governs licensing, safety, inspection, and compliance of all electrical installations. Every design contract you estimate must satisfy the mandatory provisions of this Act.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
