> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Metering System # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Metering System**: A metering system is an integrated arrangement of measuring instruments, instrument transformers, protective devices, wiring, and enclosures used to **measure, record, and monitor** electrical quantities — primarily energy (kWh), apparent energy (kVAh), maximum demand (kVA/kW), and power factor — at the point of supply between the utility (KSEB / distribution licensee) and the consumer's installation, in accordance with the **Central Electricity Authority (CEA) Metering Regulations, IS 14697, IS 13779, and IS 13010**. In the context of **KTU PCEET602 — Electrical System Design and Estimation**, a metering system is studied under the broader umbrella of *low-tension (LT) and high-tension (HT) consumer connections*, where the *type, accuracy class, mounting, and rating* of meters and instrument transformers must be selected to suit the sanctioned load, voltage level, and tariff category. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Think of a metering system as the **"odometer + fuel gauge + speedometer of an electrical installation**. | Real-World Analogy | Electrical Equivalent | |---|---| | Odometer in a car | **kWh / kVAh energy meter** (records total energy consumed) | | Fuel gauge | **Voltmeter / Ammeter** (instantaneous state of system) | | Speedometer | **Maximum Demand Indicator (MDI)** (peak load drawn) | | Dashboard wiring | **Meter board, CTs, PTs, and connecting cables** | | Dashboard panel | **Metering cubicle / meter room** | Just as a car's dashboard tells the driver *how much fuel is being used, how fast, and how far*, an electrical metering system tells the utility and the consumer *how much energy is consumed, the peak demand drawn, the power quality maintained, and the tariff slab applicable*. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU Exam**: A metering system is NOT just "a meter." It is the **complete assembly** — meter + CT/PT + wiring + enclosure + sealing arrangement — that constitutes the legal point of billing between KSEB and the consumer. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Standard CT Secondary Current**: **5 A** (for metering) or **1 A** (for high-accuracy / long-distance metering) - **Standard PT Secondary Voltage**: **110 V / √3 = 63.5 V** (line-to-neutral) or **110 V** (line-to-line) - **Standard Accuracy Classes for Metering CTs**: **0.2, 0.5, 1.0** (per IS 2705) - **Standard Accuracy Classes for Energy Meters**: **Class 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0** (per IS 13779) - **Standard Frequency**: **50 Hz** (Indian power system) - **Standard Reference Voltage**: **240 V (LT single phase), 415 V (LT 3-phase), 11 kV / 22 kV / 33 kV (HT)** ## 1.4 Why Metering System is a Critical Topic in KTU PCEET602 The estimation and design of a metering system is the **only legally binding interface** between a consumer's installation and the utility. Errors here lead to: 1. **Billing disputes** (KSEB vs. consumer) 2. **Revenue loss** to the licensee 3. **Penalty under Section 126 of Electricity Act, 2003** for unauthorized use 4. **Failure of KTU project viva** (examiners specifically test CT/PT selection) > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip**: Always remember the **multiplication factor (MF)** of a meter. It is the most frequently asked concept in the metering chapter. MF = CT Ratio × PT Ratio. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Components of a Metering System (Logical Breakdown) A complete metering system, in the order of energy flow from source to billing, consists of: 1. **Current Transformers (CTs)** — step down high primary currents to safe, measurable secondary values. 2. **Potential Transformers (PTs)** — step down high primary voltages to safe, measurable secondary values. 3. **Energy Meter (kWh / kVAh)** — measures active or apparent energy. 4. **Maximum Demand Indicator (MDI)** — records the highest average load in a billing cycle (typically 15-min or 30-min window). 5. **Tri-Vector Meter (TVM)** — measures kWh, kVArh, and kVAh simultaneously; used for HT consumers. 6. **TOD (Time-of-Day) Meter** — records energy in different time slots (peak / off-peak / normal hours). 7. **Meter Board / Meter Cubicle / Meter Box** — protective enclosure, often with **sealing arrangement** to prevent tampering. 8. **Test Terminal Block (TTB)** — allows meter testing without disconnection. 9. **Sealing Screws & Seals** — utility-authorized lock to prevent meter bypass. 10. **Cabling between CT/PT secondary and meter** — typically 2.5 sq.mm copper, armored, in conduit. > [!NOTE] > **CEA Regulation 7(1)**: For HT consumers (≥11 kV connections), metering **must** be done using CTs and PTs of accuracy class **0.2** or better. For LT consumers, class **1.0** is acceptable. ## 2.2 Classification of Energy Meters ### 2.2.1 By Operating Principle | Type | Principle | Accuracy | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **Electromechanical (Ferraris)** | Aluminum disc rotates in magnetic field | Class 2.0 | Obsolete; used in old installations | | **Electronic (Static / Digital)** | ADCs sample voltage & current; μP computes power | Class 0.2 to 1.0 | All new installations (post-2000) | | **Hybrid** | Electronic with electromechanical register | Class 1.0 | Rare, legacy | ### 2.2.2 By Quantity Measured - **kWh meter** — Active energy (most common for domestic/LT) - **kVAh meter** — Apparent energy (used for HT industrial tariff in Kerala) - **kVArh meter** — Reactive energy (penalty for low PF) - **Maximum Demand (MD) meter** — kVA or kW peak - **TOD meter** — kWh with time-slot differentiation - **Tri-Vector Meter (TVM)** — kWh + kVArh + kVAh in one unit ### 2.2.3 By Connection Type - **Whole Current (WC) meter** — Direct connection, ≤ 60 A (LT single-phase, LT 3-phase small loads) - **CT-operated meter** — For loads > 60 A; requires external CTs - **CT-PT operated meter** — Mandatory for HT consumers (11 kV, 22 kV, 33 kV) ## 2.3 CT (Current Transformer) — Operational Theory A CT is a **step-down transformer** with a primary winding (often just one turn — the line conductor itself) and many secondary turns. The secondary current is proportional to the primary current by the inverse turns ratio. - **CT Ratio (N)**: e.g., 200/5 A → primary 200 A maps to secondary 5 A → ratio = 40 - **Burden**: VA rating of the secondary load (meter + wiring) - **Accuracy Limit Factor (ALF)**: Typically 5 to 10 for metering CTs - **Safety**: CT secondary must **NEVER** be open-circuited while primary is energized → induces dangerously high voltage. > [!WARNING] > **Critical Engineering Practice**: Always **short-circuit the CT secondary** before disconnecting any meter. An open CT secondary can develop voltages exceeding 1 kV, causing insulation failure and arcing. ## 2.4 PT (Potential Transformer) — Operational Theory A PT is a **step-down transformer** used for voltage measurement. For 11 kV primary, the standard secondary is 110/√3 V = 63.5 V (line-to-neutral). - **PT Ratio**: e.g., 11000/110 V → ratio = 100 - **Burden**: 50 VA to 200 VA typically - **Accuracy Class**: 0.2 / 0.5 for metering ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{CT Ratio} = \dfrac{I_{p,\text{primary}}}{I_{s,\text{secondary}}}$ | $I_p$ = primary, $I_s$ = secondary | A | CT selection | | 2 | $\text{PT Ratio} = \dfrac{V_{p,\text{primary}}}{V_{s,\text{secondary}}}$ | $V_p$ = primary, $V_s$ = secondary | V | PT selection | | 3 | $\text{Multiplication Factor (MF)} = \text{CT Ratio} \times \text{PT Ratio}$ | — | dimensionless | Convert meter reading to actual energy | | 4 | $\text{Actual Energy} = \text{Meter Reading} \times \text{MF}$ | — | kWh | Billing computation | | 5 | $I_{p,\text{primary}} = I_{s,\text{secondary}} \times \text{CT Ratio}$ | — | A | Reverse CT calculation | | 6 | $V_{p,\text{primary}} = V_{s,\text{secondary}} \times \text{PT Ratio}$ | — | V | Reverse PT calculation | | 7 | $S_{\text{max}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_{\text{full-load}}$ | $S$ = apparent power, $V_L$ = line voltage, $I$ = current | kVA | Sanctioned load / MD limit | | 8 | $I_{\text{full-load}} = \dfrac{S \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L}$ | — | A | CT primary sizing | | 9 | $\text{MD in kVA} = \dfrac{\text{MD in kW}}{\text{Power Factor (avg.)}}$ | — | kVA | HT billing (Kerala tariff uses kVA) | | 10 | $\text{Energy (kVAh)} = \text{Energy (kWh)} \times \dfrac{1}{\text{Power Factor}}$ | — | kVAh | LT to HT conversion for billing | | 11 | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{\text{kVA}_{\text{trafo}}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L} \times \dfrac{100}{\%Z}$ | $I_{sc}$ = short-circuit current, $\%Z$ = transformer impedance | A | CT dynamic stability check | | 12 | $\text{Meter Constant} = \dfrac{\text{No. of pulses}}{\text{kWh recorded}}$ | — | imp/kWh or imp/kVAh | Calibration / testing | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Mantra**: The Multiplication Factor (MF) is the **single most important formula** in the metering chapter. Memorize: $\text{MF} = \text{CT Ratio} \times \text{PT Ratio}$. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Revenue metering** in KSEB substations → ensures ₹ crores of revenue accuracy. - **A BT (Bulk Transmission) consumer** at 66 kV uses a TVM with 0.2-class CTs and PTs. - **Industrial HT consumers** in Kerala are billed on **kVA** basis (not kW), making **kVAh metering** with **TVMs** mandatory. - **TOD metering** allows utility to incentivize off-peak consumption → load flattening. - **Net metering** (for solar rooftop under KSEB scheme 2019) requires a **bidirectional TOD meter** with import-export registration. ## 2.7 Metering Scheme for Different Consumer Categories (KSEB Practice) | Consumer Category | Sanctioned Load | Voltage Level | Meter Type | CT/PT Required? | |---|---|---|---|---| | Domestic (LT-I) | ≤ 5 kW | 240 V, 1-phase | Whole-current kWh | No | | Commercial (LT-II) | ≤ 50 kW | 415 V, 3-phase | Whole-current kWh (≤ 60 A) | No | | Industrial LT (LT-IV) | 50–150 kW | 415 V, 3-phase | CT-operated kVAh | CT only | | Industrial HT (HT-I) | > 150 kW | 11 kV | CT-PT operated TVM | CT + PT | | HT Bulk | > 1 MVA | 22/33/66 kV | CT-PT operated TVM, 0.2 class | CT + PT | # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Multiplication Factor (MF) for a CT-PT Operated Meter A CT-PT operated meter measures the **secondary quantities** (5 A current, 63.5 V voltage). The meter register, however, is calibrated to display primary-side energy. The relationship is derived as follows: **Step 1**: Define CT ratio (also called CT transformation ratio, CTR): $$ \text{CTR} = \frac{I_{\text{primary}}}{I_{\text{secondary}}} $$ **Step 2**: Define PT ratio (also called PTR): $$ \text{PTR} = \frac{V_{\text{primary}}}{V_{\text{secondary}}} $$ **Step 3**: Real power on the primary side is: $$ P_{\text{primary}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{\text{primary}} \cdot I_{\text{primary}} \cdot \cos\phi $$ **Step 4**: Real power measured by the meter on the secondary side is: $$ P_{\text{secondary}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{\text{secondary}} \cdot I_{\text{secondary}} \cdot \cos\phi $$ **Step 5**: Take the ratio of primary power to secondary power (power factor is same on both sides since CT and PT are ideal): $$ \frac{P_{\text{primary}}}{P_{\text{secondary}}} = \frac{V_{\text{primary}} \cdot I_{\text{primary}}}{V_{\text{secondary}} \cdot I_{\text{secondary}}} $$ **Step 6**: Substitute the ratio definitions: $$ \frac{P_{\text{primary}}}{P_{\text{secondary}}} = \text{PTR} \times \text{CTR} $$ **Step 7**: For energy (power integrated over time), the same ratio holds: $$ \frac{E_{\text{primary}}}{E_{\text{secondary}}} = \text{PTR} \times \text{CTR} = \text{MF} $$ **Step 8**: Therefore, the actual energy consumed on the primary (billing) side is: $$ \boxed{E_{\text{primary}} = E_{\text{secondary}} \times \text{MF} = E_{\text{secondary}} \times \text{PTR} \times \text{CTR}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Note**: The above derivation assumes **ideal CT and PT** (zero losses, no ratio and phase error). In practice, **CT and PT errors are non-zero**, which is why accuracy classes (0.2, 0.5, 1.0) are specified to keep the composite error within limits. --- ## 3.2 Numerical Problem — Worked Example (Part B Style) **Problem**: An HT consumer is connected at **11 kV**, with a sanctioned load of **500 kVA**. The metering is done using: - CTs: **200/5 A** (single-ratio) - PTs: **11000/110 V** - Meter: 3-phase, 4-wire, CT-PT operated, kVAh register The meter reading for the month is **1,250 kVAh** (as displayed). **Find**: 1. The Multiplication Factor (MF). 2. The actual energy consumed in **kVAh** on the primary (11 kV) side. 3. The full-load current of the installation. 4. Verify whether the selected CT is correctly rated (should be ≥ 120% of full-load current per IS). --- ### Step 1: Compute the CT Ratio (CTR) $$ \text{CTR} = \frac{I_{\text{primary}}}{I_{\text{secondary}}} = \frac{200 \text{ A}}{5 \text{ A}} = 40 $$ ### Step 2: Compute the PT Ratio (PTR) $$ \text{PTR} = \frac{V_{\text{primary}}}{V_{\text{secondary}}} = \frac{11\,000 \text{ V}}{110 \text{ V}} = 100 $$ ### Step 3: Compute the Multiplication Factor (MF) $$ \text{MF} = \text{CTR} \times \text{PTR} = 40 \times 100 = 4000 $$ ### Step 4: Compute the Actual Energy in kVAh $$ E_{\text{actual}} = \text{Meter Reading} \times \text{MF} = 1250 \times 4000 = 5\,000\,000 \text{ kVAh} $$ Convert to **MVARh** or **lakh kVAh** as needed: $$ E_{\text{actual}} = 50.00 \text{ lakh kVAh} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ kVAh} $$ ### Step 5: Compute the Full-Load Current For a 3-phase balanced system: $$ I_{\text{full-load}} = \frac{S \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L} = \frac{500 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 11\,000} $$ Evaluate denominator first: $$ \sqrt{3} \times 11\,000 = 1.732 \times 11\,000 = 19\,052 \text{ V} $$ Then: $$ I_{\text{full-load}} = \frac{500\,000}{19\,052} = 26.24 \text{ A} $$ ### Step 6: Verify CT Rating as per IS 2705 CT must be rated at ≥ **120% of full-load current**: $$ I_{\text{CT,min}} = 1.2 \times 26.24 = 31.49 \text{ A} $$ Since the selected CT is **200/5 A** (primary current 200 A ≫ 31.49 A), the CT is **heavily oversized**. This is acceptable but uneconomical. A more optimal choice would be **50/5 A** (i.e., 10:1 ratio). This is a common design mistake flagged in KTU vivas. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example: Maximum Demand Calculation **Problem**: A 3-phase, 11 kV HT consumer has a sanctioned MD of **800 kVA** and recorded a **maximum demand** of **650 kVA** during the billing month. The energy consumption for the month is **2,00,000 kWh** and the average power factor is **0.85 lagging**. **Find**: 1. The average load factor. 2. The MD charges, if KSEB tariff penalizes any MD recorded above 90% of sanctioned MD. --- ### Step 1: Compute the Total Hours in the Billing Month $$ T = 30 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ h} = 720 \text{ h} $$ ### Step 2: Compute the Average Load in kVA $$ S_{\text{avg}} = \frac{E_{\text{kVAh}}}{T} = \frac{\text{Energy in kWh} / \text{pf}}{T} $$ First convert kWh to kVAh: $$ E_{\text{kVAh}} = \frac{E_{\text{kWh}}}{\text{pf}} = \frac{2\,00\,000}{0.85} = 2\,35\,294.12 \text{ kVAh} $$ Then compute average load: $$ S_{\text{avg}} = \frac{2\,35\,294.12}{720} = 326.80 \text{ kVA} $$ ### Step 3: Compute the Load Factor $$ \text{Load Factor} = \frac{S_{\text{avg}}}{S_{\text{MD}}} = \frac{326.80}{650} = 0.5027 \text{ or } 50.27\% $$ ### Step 4: Compute the 90% Threshold of Sanctioned MD $$ \text{Threshold} = 0.90 \times 800 = 720 \text{ kVA} $$ Since the recorded MD (650 kVA) is **less than** 720 kVA, **no MD penalty** is levied. ✅ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight**: The 90% MD rule is part of **KSERC (Kerala State Electricity Regulatory Commission) tariff order**. The billed MD is the **higher** of (a) 80% of contracted MD and (b) the actual recorded MD — minimum threshold. Above 90% of contracted MD, a **penalty rate** applies. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: CT Selection Criterion | Selection Parameter | Formula / Rule | Why It Matters | |---|---|---| | CT primary current | $\geq 1.2 \times I_{\text{full-load}}$ | Allows 20% overload margin (IS 2705) | | CT secondary | 5 A (standard) or 1 A (long runs) | Industry standard | | Accuracy class | 0.2 (revenue), 0.5 (general), 1.0 (backup) | Statutory compliance | | Burden | $\geq$ total VA of meter + cabling | Avoid CT saturation | | ALF (Accuracy Limit Factor) | 5 to 10 for metering | Survives fault transients | | Short-time current (Ith) | $\geq I_{sc}$ of downstream network | Mechanical withstanding | | Knee-point voltage (for protection) | $V_k \geq 2 \times I_{p,\text{max}} \times R_{\text{burden}}$ | Protection CTs (PS class) | ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Meter Selection for Consumer Categories | Consumer Class | Voltage | Load (kVA) | Meter | CT/PT | |---|---|---|---|---| | LT-I (Domestic) | 240 V, 1φ | ≤ 5 | WC, 1φ kWh, Class 1.0 | None | | LT-II (Commercial) | 415 V, 3φ | ≤ 50 | WC, 3φ kWh, Class 1.0 | None | | LT-IV (Industrial LT) | 415 V, 3φ | 50–150 | CT-op, 3φ kVAh, Class 0.5 | CT only | | LT-VII (General) | 415 V, 3φ | 50–150 | CT-op, 3φ kVAh, Class 0.5 | CT only | | HT-I (Industrial HT) | 11 kV | > 150 | CT-PT, 3φ TVM, Class 0.2 | CT + PT | | HT-II (Commercial HT) | 11 kV | > 150 | CT-PT, 3φ TVM, Class 0.2 | CT + PT | | HT-IV (Bulk) | 22/33 kV | > 1000 | CT-PT, 3φ TVM, Class 0.2, TOD | CT + PT | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a CT-PT Operated Metering System (HT Consumer) ```mermaid flowchart LR source1[11 kV 3-Phase Incoming Line] --> isolator1[Isolator / Breaker 11 kV] isolator1 --> ptUnit[Potential Transformer PT
11kV to 110 by sqrt 3 V
Class 0.2 Accuracy] isolator1 --> ctUnit[Current Transformers CT
3 Nos. one per phase
Class 0.2 Accuracy] ptUnit --> tvm1[Tri Vector Meter TVM
kWh plus kVArh plus kVAh
Class 0.2S] ctUnit --> tvm1 tvm1 --> mdi1[Maximum Demand Indicator MDI
15 minute integration window] tvm1 --> tod1[Time of Day TOD Register
6 Time Slots Peak and Off Peak] tvm1 --> ttb1[Test Terminal Block TTB
For Calibration Access] ttb1 --> seal1[Utility Sealing Arrangement
KSEB Authorized Seal] tvm1 --> dcu1[Data Collection Unit DCU
AMR Modem RS485] dcu1 --> cloud1[Cloud Server KSEB Billing
DLMS COSEM Protocol] ptUnit -.sealing lead.-> seal1 ctUnit -.sealing lead.-> seal1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram**: Power flows from the 11 kV line through the isolator. **CTs** sample current (in series with the line). **PTs** sample voltage (in parallel, line-to-ground). Both feed the **TVM**, which computes energy, demand, and TOD slots. The **TTB** allows KSEB to test the meter accuracy on-site without disconnecting the line. **Seals** prevent consumer tampering — a mandatory requirement under CEA Regulations 2006. --- ## 4.2 Decision Flowchart: Meter Selection for a Consumer ```mermaid flowchart TD start1[New Consumer Connection Request] --> load1{Sanctioned Load?} load1 --> lt1[Less than or equal to 5 kW] load1 --> lt2[5 to 50 kW 3 phase] load1 --> lt3[50 to 150 kW 3 phase] load1 --> ht1[Above 150 kW HT] lt1 --> met1[Whole Current 1 phase kWh
Class 1.0 Accuracy
Mounted in Meter Board] lt2 --> met2[Whole Current 3 phase kWh
Class 1.0 Accuracy
Mounted in Meter Cubicle] lt3 --> met3[CT Operated kVAh Meter
Class 0.5 Accuracy
External CTs Required] ht1 --> met4[CT PT Operated TVM
Class 0.2S Accuracy
TOD Register Mandatory] met1 --> seal1[Seal and Energize] met2 --> seal1 met3 --> seal1 met4 --> seal1 ``` ## 4.3 Logical Flow: Energy Calculation inside an Electronic Meter ```mermaid flowchart LR v1[Voltage Signals from PT Secondary] --> adc1[Analog to Digital Converter ADC
16 bit 4 kHz Sampling] i1[Current Signals from CT Secondary] --> shunt1[Burden Resistor Shunt] shunt1 --> adc1 adc1 --> dsp1[Digital Signal Processor DSP] dsp1 --> mul1[Multiplier V x I Instantaneous Power] mul1 --> int1[Integrator Time Average Power] int1 --> reg1[Energy Register kWh] int1 --> reg2[Reactive Energy Register kVArh] int1 --> reg3[Apparent Energy Register kVAh] int1 --> reg4[Maximum Demand Register
Sliding Window 15 min] reg1 --> disp1[LCD Display] reg2 --> disp1 reg3 --> disp1 reg4 --> disp1 ``` > [!TIP] > **For KTU Viva**: The examiner may ask *“Why is the ADC 16-bit and not 8-bit?”* The answer: 16-bit ADC gives **65,536 discrete steps** vs. 256 for 8-bit. For Class 0.2S accuracy over a 1000:1 dynamic range, 16-bit (or even 24-bit sigma-delta) is essential. --- ## 4.4 Block Diagram: LT Meter Board (Domestic / Small Commercial) ```mermaid flowchart TD inLine1[Service Cable from KSEB Pole] --> smcb1[Service MCB 32 A 1P or 3P] smcb1 --> earth1[Earth Connection IS 3043] smcb1 --> meter1[Whole Current Energy Meter
1 phase or 3 phase Class 1.0] meter1 --> mcb2[Distribution MCB] mcb2 --> load1a[Load 1 Lighting] mcb2 --> load2a[Load 2 Power Sockets] mcb2 --> load3a[Load 3 AC or Geyser] meter1 -.sealed.-> sealLT[Seal of Metering Authority] smcb1 -.sealed.-> sealLT ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Design Note (CEA Reg 9)**: The **service MCB**, the **meter**, and the **sealing arrangement** are all part of the *metering system*. The distribution MCB onwards is the *consumer's installation*. KSEB seals from service MCB to meter output; the consumer is responsible for everything after. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between a Current Transformer (CT) and a Potential Transformer (PT) with respect to their construction, secondary ratings, and typical applications in metering systems.** **Model Answer** (≈ 3 marks worth): | Feature | Current Transformer (CT) | Potential Transformer (PT) | |---|---|---| | **Connection in primary circuit** | **In series** with the line conductor | **In parallel** (line-to-ground) | | **Primary winding** | Often **single turn** (the line itself) | Many turns, HV-rated insulation | | **Secondary rating** | **5 A** (or 1 A) standard | **110 V / √3 = 63.5 V** (line-to-neutral) | | **Secondary loading** | Low (few VA burden) | Higher (50–200 VA) | | **Secondary open-circuit behavior** | **Dangerous** (high voltage, overheating) | **Safe** (induced EMF stays low) | | **Typical accuracy class (metering)** | 0.2 / 0.5 / 1.0 (IS 2705) | 0.2 / 0.5 (IS 3156) | | **Application** | Current measurement, protection, revenue metering | Voltage measurement, sync, metering | **Key One-liner for Exam**: *"CT is a series transformer with dangerous open-secondary; PT is a parallel transformer with safe open-secondary."* --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **Define "Multiplication Factor" (MF) of a CT-PT operated energy meter. Why is it necessary?** **Model Answer**: The **Multiplication Factor (MF)** of a CT-PT operated energy meter is the product of the CT ratio and the PT ratio: $$ \text{MF} = \text{CT Ratio} \times \text{PT Ratio} $$ **Why it is necessary**: 1. The meter measures the **secondary-side energy** (low voltage, low current), not the actual high-voltage energy consumed by the load. 2. To convert the **meter reading (secondary side)** into the **actual energy consumed on the primary side (billing side)**, we must multiply by MF. 3. Without applying MF, the billed energy will be **thousands of times lower** than the actual consumption — leading to **massive revenue loss** to the utility. 4. It is a statutory requirement under **CEA Metering Regulations 2006, Regulation 14**. **Example**: For 11 kV / 110 V PT and 200/5 A CT → MF = (11000/110) × (200/5) = 100 × 40 = **4000**. So a meter reading of 1000 kVAh corresponds to 1000 × 4000 = 40,00,000 kVAh = 4 million kVAh. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]** **a)** An HT industrial consumer is supplied at **11 kV, 3-phase, 50 Hz** with a sanctioned load of **1250 kVA**. The metering arrangement uses: - CTs: **400/5 A**, accuracy class **0.5**, 15 VA burden - PTs: **11000/110 V**, accuracy class **0.5**, 50 VA burden - 3-phase, 4-wire, **CT-PT operated Tri-Vector Meter** (kWh + kVArh + kVAh), class **0.2S** The meter's monthly reading shows: - kWh register: **2750** - kVArh register: **1820** - Maximum demand: **980 kVA** **Compute**: 1. The Multiplication Factor (MF). 2. The actual energy consumption in kWh, kVArh, and kVAh. 3. The average power factor of the installation. 4. Verify if the CT is correctly rated (IS 2705 rule: ≥ 1.2 × full-load current). **(7 marks)** **b)** With the aid of a block diagram, describe the complete metering scheme for an HT consumer, including TVM, MDI, TTB, sealing, and AMR. List the relevant **Indian Standards** governing each component. **(7 marks)** --- ### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A #### Part (a) — Numerical Solution **Step 1: Compute CT Ratio (CTR)** $$ \text{CTR} = \frac{400}{5} = 80 $$ **Step 2: Compute PT Ratio (PTR)** $$ \text{PTR} = \frac{11\,000}{110} = 100 $$ **Step 3: Compute Multiplication Factor (MF)** $$ \text{MF} = \text{CTR} \times \text{PTR} = 80 \times 100 = 8000 $$ **Step 4: Compute Actual Energy in kWh** $$ E_{\text{kWh}} = 2750 \times 8000 = 2.2 \times 10^7 \text{ kWh} = 22\,000\,000 \text{ kWh} = 220 \text{ lakh kWh} $$ **Step 5: Compute Actual Energy in kVArh** $$ E_{\text{kVArh}} = 1820 \times 8000 = 1.456 \times 10^7 \text{ kVArh} = 14\,560\,000 \text{ kVArh} $$ **Step 6: Compute kVAh (from definition: kVAh² = kWh² + kVArh²)** $$ E_{\text{kVAh}} = \sqrt{(2.2 \times 10^7)^2 + (1.456 \times 10^7)^2} $$ Square each term: $$ (2.2 \times 10^7)^2 = 4.84 \times 10^{14} $$ $$ (1.456 \times 10^7)^2 = 2.1199 \times 10^{14} $$ Add: $$ 4.84 \times 10^{14} + 2.1199 \times 10^{14} = 6.9599 \times 10^{14} $$ Take square root: $$ E_{\text{kVAh}} = \sqrt{6.9599 \times 10^{14}} = 2.638 \times 10^7 \text{ kVAh} = 26\,380\,000 \text{ kVAh} $$ **Step 7: Compute Average Power Factor** $$ \text{PF} = \frac{E_{\text{kWh}}}{E_{\text{kVAh}}} = \frac{2.2 \times 10^7}{2.638 \times 10^7} = 0.8339 \text{ (lagging)} $$ **Step 8: Verify CT Rating** Compute full-load current: $$ I_{\text{FL}} = \frac{S \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L} = \frac{1250 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 11\,000} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^6}{19\,052} = 65.61 \text{ A} $$ Required CT primary (per IS 2705): $$ I_{\text{CT, min}} = 1.2 \times 65.61 = 78.73 \text{ A} $$ Since selected CT is 400 A primary, which is **far greater** than the 78.73 A requirement. **The CT is oversized (unnecessary cost), but technically compliant**. An optimal selection would be **100/5 A** (CTR = 20). ✅ #### Part (b) — Block Diagram & Indian Standards **Block Diagram (described verbally for exam)**: ``` 11 kV Line → [Isolator] → [CTs (3 nos.)] ──┐ │ ├─→ [Test Terminal Block TTB] └──→ [PTs (3 nos.)] │ │ │ ↓ │ [Tri-Vector Meter TVM] │ ├── kWh Register │ ├── kVArh Register │ ├── kVAh Register │ └── MDI (15 min) │ │ │ ↓ │ [TOD Register (6 slots)] │ │ │ ↓ │ [AMR Modem / DCU] │ │ │ ↓ │ [KSEB Cloud Server] │ └──→ [Utility Sealing] ←┐ │ [Consumer Sealing] ←───┘ ``` **Indian Standards Applicable**: | Component | Governing IS | Title | |---|---|---| | CT | **IS 2705 (Parts 1–4)** | Current Transformers — specifications | | PT | **IS 3156 (Parts 1–4)** | Voltage Transformers — specifications | | Energy Meter (static) | **IS 13779** | AC Static Watt-hour Meters, Class 1.0 & 2.0 | | HT Tri-Vector Meter | **IS 14697** | AC Static Transformer Operated Watt-hour & VAR-hour Meters, Class 0.2S, 0.5S | | Electromechanical Meter | **IS 722** | AC Watthour meters (now superseded) | | Meter Board / Box | **IS 13010** | A.C. Watt-hour Meters, Class 0.5, 1, 2 — sealing arrangement | | Test Terminal Block | **IS 9249** | Test blocks for tariff meters | | Installation | **IS 732** / **IE Rules 1956** | Wiring installations, CEA Safety Regulations | --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **a)** Define **Maximum Demand (MD)**. Explain the **sliding window** method of MD measurement. A factory has the following half-hourly load readings (in kVA) for a 4-hour window: **500, 800, 1200, 950, 700, 1100, 1500, 1300**. If the MD integration period is **30 minutes**, compute the MD using the sliding window method. The sanctioned MD is 1000 kVA. Comment on penalty applicability (assume KSEB bills 90% of sanctioned MD as minimum and 100% as actual MD). **(7 marks)** **b)** Explain the concept of **TOD (Time-of-Day) metering** and its role in **load management**. List the standard time slots as per **KSERC tariff order** and explain how TOD benefits the consumer and the utility. **(7 marks)** --- ### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B #### Part (a) — Maximum Demand Computation **Definition**: Maximum Demand (MD) is the **highest value of the average load** (kW or kVA) drawn by a consumer over a specified time interval (typically 15 or 30 minutes) during a billing period. It determines the **demand charges** in the electricity bill. **Sliding Window Method**: The MD meter continuously records the average load over a fixed time window (e.g., 30 minutes). The window "slides" forward every minute (or every integration period), and the **maximum** of all the sliding averages is recorded as MD. **Numerical Computation** (assuming each reading is already a 30-min average for simplicity, since we only have 8 readings for 4 hours — 30 min per reading): | Reading # | Load (kVA) | |---|---| | 1 | 500 | | 2 | 800 | | 3 | 1200 | | 4 | 950 | | 5 | 700 | | 6 | 1100 | | 7 | 1500 | | 8 | 1300 | For 30-min integration with consecutive 30-min samples, MD is simply the **maximum reading**: $$ \text{MD} = \max\{500, 800, 1200, 950, 700, 1100, 1500, 1300\} = 1500 \text{ kVA} $$ **Penalty Analysis**: - 90% of sanctioned MD = 0.9 × 1000 = 900 kVA - Actual MD = 1500 kVA - Since 1500 kVA > 1000 kVA (sanctioned), the **excess MD = 500 kVA** is billed at a **penalty rate** (typically 1.5× to 2× the normal demand charge). - **Billed MD = max(900, 1500) = 1500 kVA** with penalty on the excess 500 kVA. #### Part (b) — TOD Metering **Definition**: TOD (Time-of-Day) metering is a tariff mechanism where **different rates are charged for electricity consumed in different time slots** of the day. It incentivizes consumers to shift their loads from **peak hours** to **off-peak hours**. **Standard Time Slots as per KSERC** (Kerala): | Slot | Time Window | Category | Typical Tariff | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 00:00 – 06:00 | **Night Off-Peak** | Lowest rate | | 2 | 06:00 – 09:00 | **Morning Normal** | Normal rate | | 3 | 09:00 – 12:00 | **Morning Peak** | Highest rate | | 4 | 12:00 – 18:00 | **Afternoon Normal** | Normal rate | | 5 | 18:00 – 22:00 | **Evening Peak** | Highest rate | | 6 | 22:00 – 24:00 | **Night Normal** | Normal rate | > [!NOTE] > **KSERC Tariff Order 2022** mandates TOD slots as above for HT consumers. For LT, TOD is optional but encouraged for new connections > 20 kW. **Benefits to Consumer**: - Shifts heavy loads (machinery, pumping, charging) to **off-peak** slots → **lower electricity bill**. - No installation cost — the TVM is already there. **Benefits to Utility (KSEB)**: - **Flattens the load curve** → reduces need for peaking power. - Avoids costly **power purchase** during peak hours. - Aligns with **solar generation curve** (afternoon generation → afternoon off-peak for solar consumers). **Net Metering Integration**: Under KSEB's rooftop solar scheme, the **import-export TOD register** is mandatory. Consumers are **paid** for energy exported to the grid (at TOD-weighted average rate) and **charged** for energy imported (at TOD-weighted rate). This double benefit makes TOD a **key tool for rooftop solar economics**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks**: > > 1. **Forgetting the PT ratio in MF** — Many students write MF = CT ratio only. For HT, **MF = CTR × PTR is mandatory**. Lose **2 marks** if forgotten. > 2. **Confusing kWh and kVAh** — Kerala's HT tariff is on **kVAh** (not kWh). If the question says "HT consumer," assume kVAh unless stated otherwise. Wrong assumption = **2–3 marks lost**. > 3. **Open CT secondary in diagrams** — In block diagrams, if a CT is shown without a **burden resistor / meter connected**, the examiner deducts marks for safety oversight. > 4. **Mixing IS numbers** — IS 13779 is for **direct-connected static meters**; IS 14697 is for **CT-PT operated TVMs**. Writing the wrong IS = **1 mark lost** per occurrence. > 5. **Not converting units** — Full-load current in A, MD in kVA, energy in kWh — students often mix up **MWh, lakh kWh, kVAh, kVArh**. Always write units explicitly. > 6. **Forgetting the 1.2× overload rule for CT selection** — A frequently tested item. IS 2705 specifies ≥ 120% of full-load current. No mention = **1 mark lost**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > 📌 **Metering System — Last-Minute KTU Revision** ### Core Concepts - [x] Metering system = **meter + CT/PT + TTB + sealing + wiring** (not just the meter). - [x] Three consumer classes: **LT (whole current), LT (CT-operated), HT (CT-PT operated)**. - [x] **Whole current meters** for loads ≤ 60 A; **CT-operated** for > 60 A LT; **CT-PT operated** for HT. ### Instrument Transformers - [x] **CT secondary = 5 A** (or 1 A for long runs). CT is **in series**. **Open secondary is dangerous** — always short before disconnecting meter. - [x] **PT secondary = 110 V / √3 = 63.5 V** (L-N). PT is **in parallel** (L-G or L-L). - [x] **CT accuracy class for revenue metering = 0.2** (per CEA Reg 7). - [x] **Meter accuracy class for HT = 0.2S** (per IS 14697). ### Critical Formulas - [x] $\text{MF} = \text{CTR} \times \text{PTR}$ - [x] $E_{\text{actual}} = E_{\text{meter}} \times \text{MF}$ - [x] $I_{\text{FL}} = \dfrac{S \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L}$ - [x] CT primary ≥ **1.2 × I_FL** (IS 2705) - [x] $\text{PF} = \dfrac{\text{kWh}}{\text{kVAh}}$ (from TVM readings) ### Indian Standards (MUST memorize) - [x] **IS 2705** → CT - [x] **IS 3156** → PT - [x] **IS 13779** → Static whole-current kWh meter - [x] **IS 14697** → CT-PT operated TVM (Class 0.2S, 0.5S) - [x] **IS 13010** → Sealing arrangement - [x] **IS 9249** → Test Terminal Block ### Kerala-Specific (KSERC) - [x] HT billing on **kVAh** basis (not kWh). - [x] MD penalty: **billed MD = max(80% of contracted, recorded MD)**, penalty on MD > 90% of contracted. - [x] **TOD** mandatory for HT-IV bulk consumers. - [x] **Net metering** uses bidirectional TOD meter for rooftop solar. ### Safety & Design Rules - [x] Always **short CT secondary** before meter removal. - [x] Always **fuse PT secondary** for short-circuit protection. - [x] **TTB** mandatory for revenue meters ≥ 11 kV. - [x] **Sealing** by KSEB up to meter output only. ### Common Exam Traps - [x] Don't mix **kWh, kVArh, kVAh** — use Pythagorean theorem: $\text{kVAh}^2 = \text{kWh}^2 + \text{kVArh}^2$. - [x] Don't forget to convert **A to kA, V to kV, W to kW** when plugging into formulas. - [x] For LT (415 V), **no PT** is needed — only CT. So MF = CT ratio only. - [x] For HT (≥ 11 kV), **both CT and PT** are needed. So MF = CTR × PTR.

topics.title: Basics of Star Rating and Labelling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basics of Star Rating and Labelling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Star Rating and Labelling** is a regulatory energy-efficiency benchmarking programme administered in India by the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** under the Ministry of Power, Government of India, established via the **Energy Conservation Act, 2001**. It mandates that designated electrical appliances and equipment display a standardised label indicating their **energy performance** relative to a benchmark, scaled from **1 star (least efficient)** to **5 stars (most efficient)**.

In the context of **Electrical System Design and Estimation**, the star rating framework allows design engineers to:
- Quantify long-term operating cost of electrical loads.
- Compare equipment options on a rational energy basis.
- Comply with the **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC)** and the **Eco Niwas Samhita (ENS)** for residential buildings.
- Estimate realistic life-cycle cost (LCC) for tender documents and detailed project reports (DPR).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are buying a car. Two cars of the same size can have **radically different fuel consumption**. A simple sticker on the windshield tells you "this car runs 15 km/litre" — this lets you compare and choose. **Star rating is exactly this sticker, but for electricity consumption.**

Think of electricity as **petrol measured in units (kWh)**. The star label tells you *how many kilometres (kilowatt-hours of cooling, lighting, or work)* the appliance will deliver *per litre of electricity it drinks*. A **5-star AC** is like a **hybrid car** — it travels further per unit of fuel. A **1-star AC** is like an **old diesel guzzler** — same comfort, but the electricity bill at the end of the month will make you cry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Analogy Mapping**
> | Real World | Electrical Equivalent |
> |---|---|
> | Fuel (Petrol/Diesel) | Electrical Energy (kWh) |
> | Mileage (km/litre) | EER / ISEER (output/input) |
> | Star rating sticker | BEE 5-Star Label |
> | Car showroom | Appliance dealer |

## 1.3 Governing Bodies & Statutory Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** — Statutory body under the Energy Conservation Act 2001. Headquarters: New Delhi. Sole authority for issuing and revising star rating schedules in India.

**Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** — Issues the corresponding **IS standards** (e.g., IS 1391 for ACs) that define testing methodology. BEE ratings are derived from BIS-defined test results.

**Star Rating Schedule** — A periodic revision document (e.g., Schedule 3 for Room Air Conditioners, Schedule 31 for LED Lamps) that defines:
- **ISEER bands** corresponding to 1 to 5 stars.
- Cut-off dates for each revision.
- List of appliances covered (mandatory vs. voluntary).

## 1.4 Mandatory vs. Voluntary Appliances

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Appliances (cannot be sold without label):** Room Air Conditioners (fixed-speed & inverter), Frost-Free Refrigerators, LED Lamps, Tubular Fluorescent Lamps, Distribution Transformers, Direct Cool Refrigerators, Colour TV, Washing Machine, Laptop, Computer (notebook), Microwave Oven, Ceiling Fans, TFL (Linear LED), Monobloc Pumps, Submersible Pumps, Air Compressors, Induction Motors, Agricultural Pumpsets.
>
> **Voluntary Appliances:** Many commercial equipment categories enter the voluntary phase first; mandatory status follows after market penetration of labelled products exceeds a threshold.

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold)

- **Star Rating Bands: 1 to 5** (integer scale).
- **ISEER Benchmark Cycle: T1, T2, T3** (different ambient temperature bins for AC testing per IS 1391).
- **Annual Energy Consumption (AEC): kWh/year** — the value printed on every BEE label.
- **Energy Efficiency Ratio (EER):** Cooling output (W) ÷ Electrical input (W), measured at one fixed operating condition.
- **Indian Seasonal Energy Efficiency Ratio (ISEER):** Weighted average of EERs measured at three different ambient temperatures (29 °C, 35 °C, 45 °C).
- **Cooling Seasonal Performance Factor (CSPF)** — alternate metric in the latest 2024 revision.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 How Star Rating is Determined — Step-by-Step Logic

The rating algorithm is fundamentally a **banded-normalisation** procedure. The manufacturer submits lab-tested performance numbers (e.g., ISEER for ACs). BEE then maps these values into discrete bands. The procedure is:

1. **Lab Testing:** Appliance is tested under **standard test conditions (STC)** as per the relevant IS standard in a BEE-accredited NABL laboratory.
2. **Computation of Metric:** The appropriate energy-efficiency metric (ISEER, EER, efficacy in lm/W, etc.) is calculated.
3. **Benchmark Threshold Definition:** BEE publishes a table — e.g., for split ACs (1.5 TR, ISEER):
   - **1 star:** ISEER $\geq$ 3.10, $<$ 3.30
   - **2 stars:** ISEER $\geq$ 3.30, $<$ 3.50
   - **3 stars:** ISEER $\geq$ 3.50, $<$ 3.70
   - **4 stars:** ISEER $\geq$ 3.70, $<$ 4.00
   - **5 stars:** ISEER $\geq$ 4.00
4. **Mapping:** The tested value is mapped to the matching band → that becomes the displayed star rating.
5. **Schedule Revision:** Every 1–2 years, BEE tightens the thresholds. A 5-star appliance of 2022 often becomes a 3-star appliance in 2024. This is called **"label stringency creep"** and pushes manufacturers to innovate.

## 2.2 Why Star Rating Exists — The Engineering Rationale

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core "Why"**
> Electricity is **invisible**, **post-paid**, and **intangible**. A consumer cannot *see* the waste. Stars convert this invisible waste into a **visible, comparable number**. From an **Electrical System Design** perspective, the rating determines the connected load factor, the diversity factor, the standby load, and the **transformer sizing**.

### Real-World Utility in Electrical Design

- **Load Estimation:** Knowing the ISEER of proposed ACs allows precise estimation of annual kWh → accurate **EB bill** forecast in the DPR.
- **Cable Sizing:** A 1-star 1.5 TR AC may draw ~1.8 kW; a 5-star one draws ~1.0 kW for the same cooling. This is a **44 % difference** in branch-circuit current.
- **Generator/UPS Sizing:** Reduced load → smaller DG set, smaller UPS, smaller battery bank → reduced capital cost.
- **Transformer Sizing:** Lower diversified load → smaller distribution transformer → lower no-load iron losses.
- **PFC (Power Factor Correction) Capacitor Sizing:** Lower kW connected load → smaller capacitor bank.
- **Earthing Conductor Sizing:** Lower fault levels downstream → smaller earth conductor cross-section in some cases.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorise this table for the KTU ESE.** Every numerical on "Star Rating" will be derived from these equations.

| Sl. | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $EER = \dfrac{Q_c}{P_{in}}$ | Energy Efficiency Ratio (fixed speed) | W/W or Btu/W·h |
| 2 | $ISEER = \dfrac{\sum (Q_{ci} \cdot h_i)}{\sum (P_{ini} \cdot h_i)}$ | Indian Seasonal EER (weighted) | Wh/Wh |
| 3 | $AEC = \dfrac{Q_{cool} \times H}{ISEER}$ | Annual Energy Consumption | kWh/year |
| 4 | $\eta_{eff} = \dfrac{\text{Useful output}}{\text{Electrical input}} \times 100$ | Generic efficiency percentage | % |
| 5 | $CSPF = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{E_{total}}$ | Cooling Seasonal Performance Factor | Wh/Wh |
| 6 | $L_{70} = \text{Lumen maintenance period}$ | LED lifetime till 70% output | hours |
| 7 | $\eta_{luminaire} = \dfrac{\Phi_{luminaire}}{\Phi_{lamp}}$ | Luminaire efficacy factor | dimensionless |
| 8 | $LPD = \dfrac{\sum \Phi}{A \times 1000}$ | Lighting Power Density (ECBC metric) | W/m² |
| 9 | $LCC = C_{cap} + \sum_{t=1}^{N} \dfrac{C_{op,t}}{(1+r)^t}$ | Life-Cycle Cost (capital + operating) | ₹ |
| 10 | $Payback = \dfrac{C_{5star} - C_{1star}}{\text{Annual saving}}$ | Star premium recovery period | years |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Anchor:** *ISEER is to EER what Seasonal AC is to Window AC.* Always **weight by hours of operation** at each temperature bin.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Weighted Averages

The **ISEER weighted average** uses bin hours $h_i$ as weights because in **real India**, the AC does not operate at a single ambient temperature. It operates at ~29 °C most of the night, ~35 °C during a typical afternoon, and ~45 °C during a heatwave. A simple arithmetic average of EERs would underestimate the energy. The **weighted average correctly captures the cumulative energy bill** over the year.

$$\text{Real Indian climate } \Rightarrow h_{29} = 600 \text{ h/yr}, \quad h_{35} = 1800 \text{ h/yr}, \quad h_{45} = 100 \text{ h/yr}$$

Total annual cooling load is therefore dominated by the **35 °C bin**, which receives the highest weight.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Annual Energy Consumption from ISEER

This is the single most important derivation in this module. The KTU board routinely tests this in **Part A (3 marks)** as well as embedded inside **Part B (14 marks)** questions.

**Given:**
- Cooling capacity of AC: $Q_c$ in Watts
- Annual operating hours: $H$ hours/year
- Indian Seasonal Energy Efficiency Ratio: ISEER (Wh/Wh)
- We need to find: Annual Energy Consumption $AEC$ in kWh/year

**Step 1 — Define instantaneous power input.**
The definition of ISEER gives us the ratio of useful cooling output (W) to electrical power consumed (W):

$$ISEER = \frac{Q_c \text{ (W of cooling)}}{P_{in} \text{ (W of electricity)}}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for instantaneous electrical power input.**

$$P_{in} = \frac{Q_c}{ISEER} \text{ [Watts]}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by annual operating hours to get annual electrical energy.**
Energy is power multiplied by time. Over one year:

$$E_{annual} = P_{in} \times H = \frac{Q_c \times H}{ISEER} \text{ [Watt-hours/year]}$$

**Step 4 — Convert Watt-hours to kiloWatt-hours (the unit on the BEE label).**
Since $1 \text{ kWh} = 1000 \text{ Wh}$:

$$AEC = \frac{Q_c \times H}{1000 \times ISEER} \text{ [kWh/year]}$$

**Step 5 — Final canonical form (this is what BEE prints on the label):**

$$\boxed{AEC = \frac{Q_c \times H}{1000 \times ISEER} \text{ kWh/year}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** Notice the **inverse proportionality** between AEC and ISEER. Doubling ISEER halves the electricity bill. This is why BEE aggressively tightens ISEER thresholds each schedule revision.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Life-Cycle Cost Comparison Between Star Ratings

**Problem Statement (KTU style):** *A 1.5 TR split AC has a cooling capacity of 5275 W. Annual operating hours = 1500 h. A 3-star model has ISEER = 3.50, costs ₹40 000. A 5-star model has ISEER = 4.50, costs ₹52 000. Electricity tariff = ₹7/unit. Compare life-cycle cost over 10 years. Find simple payback.*

### Step 1 — Compute AEC for the 3-star AC

$$AEC_{3} = \frac{5275 \times 1500}{1000 \times 3.50} = \frac{7\,912\,500}{3500} = 2260.71 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

### Step 2 — Compute AEC for the 5-star AC

$$AEC_{5} = \frac{5275 \times 1500}{1000 \times 4.50} = \frac{7\,912\,500}{4500} = 1758.33 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

### Step 3 — Annual energy saved

$$\Delta AEC = 2260.71 - 1758.33 = 502.38 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

### Step 4 — Annual monetary saving

$$\text{Saving} = 502.38 \times 7 = \text{₹}\,3516.66 \text{ per year}$$

### Step 5 — Star premium (extra capital cost)

$$\Delta C = 52\,000 - 40\,000 = \text{₹}\,12\,000$$

### Step 6 — Simple Payback Period

$$\text{Payback} = \frac{12\,000}{3516.66} = 3.41 \text{ years}$$

### Step 7 — Life-Cycle Cost (over 10 years, neglecting discount rate for simplicity)

$$LCC_{3} = 40\,000 + (2260.71 \times 7 \times 10) = 40\,000 + 158\,250 = \text{₹}\,198\,250$$

$$LCC_{5} = 52\,000 + (1758.33 \times 7 \times 10) = 52\,000 + 123\,083 = \text{₹}\,175\,083$$

### Step 8 — LCC advantage of 5-star over 10 years

$$\Delta LCC = 198\,250 - 175\,083 = \text{₹}\,23\,167 \text{ in favour of 5-star}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision rule for design engineers:** If simple payback $\leq$ remaining useful life / 2, choose the higher star rating. Here, payback 3.41 years vs. useful life 10 years → **definitely choose 5-star.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Lighting Power Density (LPD) from Star-Labelled LEDs

**Problem:** A room of $5 \text{ m} \times 6 \text{ m} = 30 \text{ m}^2$ requires an illuminance of **300 lux** at the working plane. You are choosing between:
- **Option A:** 6 nos. × 18 W LED panels, 90 lm/W → 1620 lm each.
- **Option B:** 6 nos. × 12 W LED panels, 120 lm/W → 1440 lm each.

Compute LPD in each case.

**Step 1 — Total luminous flux provided.**

$$\Phi_{A} = 6 \times 1620 = 9720 \text{ lm}$$

$$\Phi_{B} = 6 \times 1440 = 8640 \text{ lm}$$

**Step 2 — Verify against design requirement.**

$$E_{design} = \frac{\Phi \times UF \times MF}{A}$$

Assuming **Utilisation Factor (UF)** = 0.6, **Maintenance Factor (MF)** = 0.8, A = 30 m²:

$$E_{A} = \frac{9720 \times 0.6 \times 0.8}{30} = \frac{4665.6}{30} = 155.5 \text{ lux} \quad (\text{FAIL — below 300 lux})$$

$$E_{B} = \frac{8640 \times 0.6 \times 0.8}{30} = \frac{4147.2}{30} = 138.2 \text{ lux} \quad (\text{FAIL})$$

**Step 3 — Increase number of fixtures to meet 300 lux.**

For Option B at 138 lux with 6 fixtures → we need $N = 6 \times \frac{300}{138.2} = 13.02 \Rightarrow 14$ fixtures.

$$N_B = 14 \text{ fixtures} \Rightarrow \text{Total wattage} = 14 \times 12 = 168 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — LPD computation.**

$$LPD_B = \frac{168}{30} = 5.6 \text{ W/m}^2$$

ECBC 2017 limit for office = **9.0 W/m²** for 300 lux task → 5.6 W/m² **comfortably complies**.

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**
Choosing **higher-efficacy (higher star rated) LED fixtures** reduces fixture count and LPD, ensuring compliance with ECBC and reducing transformer-connected load.

---

| Star Rating | ISEER Range (Wh/Wh) | Indicative AEC (kWh/yr) | Approx. kW Input @ 35°C |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 BEE Star Labelling Programme — Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **end-to-end process flow** of the BEE Star Labelling Programme — from manufacturer lab-testing to end-consumer purchase. This is the kind of diagram that KTU examiners love as a 14-mark descriptive answer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Manufacturer] --> B[Manufactures Appliance]
    B --> C[Sample sent to NABL Accredited Lab]
    C --> D[Lab Testing as per IS Standard e.g. IS 1391]
    D --> E[Test Data: EER at T1 T2 T3]
    E --> F[Manufacturer computes ISEER weighted average]
    F --> G[Submit to BEE Online Portal]
    G --> H[BEE Validates and Assigns Star Band]
    H --> I[Approval for BEE Star Label]
    I --> J[Label printed on Product Carton]
    J --> K[Distributor ships to Dealer Network]
    K --> L[End Consumer reads label before purchase]
    L --> M{Compares ISEER and Annual kWh}
    M --> N[Purchase Decision based on Star Rating]
    N --> O[Lower annual electricity bill and ECBC compliance]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Answers:** Always narrate the flow in your own words. *Start with "The manufacturer submits a sample…"* and end with *"…resulting in informed consumer choice and national energy conservation."*

This flowchart shows **how an Electrical Design Engineer integrates star rating into the project design workflow**.

```mermaid

    Load --> Spec[Spect Appliance Specifications: Capacity Voltage Phase]
    Spec --> Choice{Select Star Rating Level}

    Choice -->|Balanced| Opt2[3 Star ECBC Compliant]

    Opt1 --> LCC1[Compute 10 yr LCC]

```

## 4.3 Energy Flow Block Diagram — From Grid to Appliance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Grid[EB Grid or DG Set] --> Meter[kWh Meter]
    Meter --> MCB[MCB and Distribution Board]
    MCB --> AC[Air Conditioner]
    MCB --> LED[LED Light]
    MCB --> FAN[Ceiling Fan]
    AC -->|Input kW| Cool[Cooling Output kW]
    LED -->|Input kW| Light[Lumen Output lm]
    FAN -->|Input kW| Air[Air Movement m cubed per min]
    Cool --> Label[Star Label kWh per year]
    Light --> Label
    Air --> Label
    Label --> Bill[Monthly EB Bill INR]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** Each appliance's **star rating is the bridge** between its **electrical input** (left side) and its **useful service output** (middle). The label quantifies the **input-to-output conversion efficiency**, allowing designers to optimise the *entire* electrical infrastructure — not just the appliance.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCEET602]**

**Define the following terms as used in the BEE Star Labelling Programme:**
**(a)** ISEER
**(b)** EER
**(c)** Star Rating

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Indian Seasonal Energy Efficiency Ratio (ISEER):** It is the **weighted average** of the EER (Energy Efficiency Ratio) of an air conditioner measured at **three different ambient temperatures** — T1 = 29 °C, T2 = 35 °C, and T3 = 45 °C — with the weights being the number of hours the appliance typically operates in each temperature bin over a year. It has units of **Wh/Wh** (or W/W).

**(b) Energy Efficiency Ratio (EER):** It is the ratio of the **cooling capacity output (in watts)** of an AC to the **electrical power input (in watts)** measured at a single, fixed, standard test condition (typically 35 °C ambient, 27 °C indoor WB). Higher EER = more efficient.

**(c) Star Rating:** A **visual comparative grading** assigned by BEE to an appliance based on its measured energy-efficiency metric (ISEER, efficacy, etc.), displayed as **1 to 5 stars** on the product label. **5 stars** indicate the highest efficiency and lowest annual energy consumption; **1 star** indicates the lowest efficiency among labelled products.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question below has sub-parts **(a) for 7 marks** and **(b) for 7 marks**. Solve both sub-parts. You may attempt **either Question A or Question B** as per the internal choice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the BEE Star Labelling Programme in India. Discuss its statutory background, governing body, and the procedure adopted to assign the star rating to a room air conditioner. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1.5 TR split air conditioner has a cooling capacity of 5275 W. Estimate the **annual energy consumption (AEC)** in kWh/year for **3-star** (ISEER = 3.50) and **5-star** (ISEER = 4.50) variants, assuming the AC operates for **1500 hours per year**. If the electricity tariff is ₹8 per kWh, compute the **annual savings** in INR. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

1. **Statutory Background:** The Star Labelling Programme was launched by the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** in 2006 under the provisions of the **Energy Conservation Act, 2001**.
2. **Governing Body:** BEE, under the **Ministry of Power**, Government of India. Lab testing follows **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)** specifications (e.g., IS 1391 Part 1 & 2 for ACs).
3. **Procedure for Assigning Star Rating to a Room AC:**
   - **Step 1:** Manufacturer produces the AC and sends a sample to a **NABL-accredited lab**.
   - **Step 2:** The lab measures the **cooling capacity (W)** and **electrical power input (W)** at three ambient temperatures: **T1 = 29 °C, T2 = 35 °C, T3 = 45 °C**, giving three EER values.
   - **Step 3:** Manufacturer computes **ISEER** = weighted average of the three EERs with weights being the bin hours (600, 1800, 100).
   - **Step 4:** The computed ISEER is compared against the **BEE Schedule** (e.g., Schedule 3 / Rev-23 for 2024).
   - **Step 5:** The matching band determines the star rating (1–5).
   - **Step 6:** BEE approves the label, and the manufacturer prints it on the product.
4. **Conclusion:** The system provides **transparent, comparable information** to consumers and incentivises manufacturers to innovate for higher efficiency.

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**Given:** $Q_c = 5275 \text{ W}, H = 1500 \text{ h/yr}, \text{ISEER}_{3} = 3.50, \text{ISEER}_{5} = 4.50, \text{Tariff} = \text{₹}8/\text{kWh}$

**Step 1 — AEC for 3-star:**

$$AEC_3 = \frac{Q_c \times H}{1000 \times \text{ISEER}_3} = \frac{5275 \times 1500}{1000 \times 3.50} = \frac{7\,912\,500}{3500} = 2260.71 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

**Step 2 — AEC for 5-star:**

$$AEC_5 = \frac{5275 \times 1500}{1000 \times 4.50} = \frac{7\,912\,500}{4500} = 1758.33 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

**Step 3 — Annual kWh saved:**

$$\Delta AEC = 2260.71 - 1758.33 = 502.38 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

**Step 4 — Annual monetary saving:**

$$\text{Saving} = 502.38 \times 8 = \text{₹}\,4019.04 \text{ per year}$$

**Final Answer:** 3-star AC consumes **2260.71 kWh/yr**, 5-star AC consumes **1758.33 kWh/yr**, saving **₹4019.04/year**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the **end-to-end workflow of the BEE Star Labelling Programme** from manufacturer testing to consumer purchase. Explain how the rating schedule is revised periodically. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A commercial office of **50 m × 20 m** is to be illuminated to **500 lux** at the working plane. Two LED options are available:
- **Option 1:** 36 W panel, efficacy 100 lm/W → 3600 lm
- **Option 2:** 24 W panel, efficacy 130 lm/W → 3120 lm

Compare the **number of fixtures, total connected load, and Lighting Power Density (LPD)** for both options. Assume **UF = 0.6, MF = 0.8**. State which option meets ECBC limits. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

1. **Workflow Diagram:** Refer to SECTION 4.1 of these notes — the Mermaid flowchart showing manufacturer → NABL lab → IS-standard testing → EER computation → ISEER → BEE portal submission → star band assignment → label printing → dealer → consumer.
2. **Description:** Manufacturer → Lab testing → ISEER calculation → BEE submission → Star band assignment → Labelled product to consumer.
3. **Periodic Revision of Schedule:**
   - BEE reviews data every **1–2 years**.
   - Technology improvement, market transformation, and **cost-effectiveness** are key drivers.
   - Revision *raises the ISEER threshold* for each star band — older 5-star models drop to 3-star.
   - Consumers benefit from **continuously improving efficiency**.
   - Example: Schedule for Room AC was Rev-22 in 2022 → Rev-23 in 2024.
4. **Conclusion:** Periodic tightening of the schedule creates a **virtuous cycle of efficiency improvement**.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**Given:** $A = 50 \times 20 = 1000 \text{ m}^2, E_{design} = 500 \text{ lux}, UF = 0.6, MF = 0.8$

**Step 1 — Total luminous flux required.**

$$\Phi_{req} = \frac{E \times A}{UF \times MF} = \frac{500 \times 1000}{0.6 \times 0.8} = \frac{500\,000}{0.48} = 1\,041\,666.67 \text{ lm}$$

**Step 2 — Number of fixtures (Option 1: 3600 lm each).**

$$N_1 = \left\lceil \frac{1\,041\,666.67}{3600} \right\rceil = \lceil 289.35 \rceil = 290 \text{ fixtures}$$

**Step 3 — Total connected load (Option 1).**

$$P_1 = 290 \times 36 = 10\,440 \text{ W} = 10.44 \text{ kW}$$

$$LPD_1 = \frac{10\,440}{1000} = 10.44 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Number of fixtures (Option 2: 3120 lm each).**

$$N_2 = \left\lceil \frac{1\,041\,666.67}{3120} \right\rceil = \lceil 333.87 \rceil = 334 \text{ fixtures}$$

**Step 5 — Total connected load (Option 2).**

$$P_2 = 334 \times 24 = 8016 \text{ W} = 8.016 \text{ kW}$$

$$LPD_2 = \frac{8016}{1000} = 8.016 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 6 — ECBC Compliance (Office, 500 lux).**
ECBC 2017 limit for office task lighting at 500 lux is approximately **12.0 W/m²**.

| Option | LPD (W/m²) | ECBC Compliant? |
|---|---|---|
| Option 1 (100 lm/W) | 10.44 | ✅ Yes (but higher load) |
| Option 2 (130 lm/W) | 8.016 | ✅ Yes (33 % less load) |

**Conclusion:** **Option 2 (130 lm/W, 5-star equivalent)** is preferred — fewer watts per lux, lower connected load, smaller cables, smaller transformer, lower electricity bill.
