# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define an ELCB and an RCCB. State two advantages of an RCCB over an ELCB.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**

**ELCB (Earth Leakage Circuit Breaker):** It is a voltage-operated protective device that monitors the potential difference between the consumer's earth electrode and an independent true-earth reference. When this potential exceeds ~50 V due to an earth fault, a trip coil is energised and the supply is disconnected.

**RCCB (Residual Current Circuit Breaker):** It is a current-operated protective device that uses a core-balance current transformer (CBCT) to detect any imbalance between the line and neutral currents. When the imbalance (residual current) exceeds a preset value $I_{\Delta n}$ (e.g., 30 mA), the device trips.

**Two advantages of RCCB over ELCB:**
1. The RCCB does not require an independent earth electrode — it works on the **current imbalance principle** and can be used in any earthing system (TN-S, TN-C-S, TT, IT).
2. The RCCB is more sensitive and faster — it can detect and clear faults at 30 mA in $\leq 40$ ms, whereas the ELCB was slower and required a measurable earth-potential rise to operate.

---

### **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain the working of the test button provided on an RCCB.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**

The test button is a manually operated, spring-loaded push button connected in series with a current-limiting resistor (typically $R_{test} \approx 1.5$–$6.8$ k$\Omega$ for a 230 V device). When pressed, it connects a small current from the line side to the neutral side **through a path that bypasses the toroidal core**.

This artificially creates an imbalance in the CBCT, simulating an earth-leakage fault of approximately:

$$
I_{test} = \frac{U_{phase}}{R_{test}} \approx \frac{230 \text{ V}}{6800 \ \Omega} \approx 33.8 \text{ mA}
$$

Since this current exceeds the typical $I_{\Delta n} = 30$ mA, the trip mechanism must operate, confirming that the RCCB is functionally healthy. The test button should be operated **monthly** as recommended by manufacturers to ensure continued reliability.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

#### **(a) With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of an RCCB. (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer:**

**Construction (4 marks):**

An RCCB consists of the following main components:

1. **Toroidal Core (CBCT):** A high-permeability ferrite ring core on which both the line and neutral conductors are wound as single-turn primary windings. A multi-turn secondary winding is wound on the same core.

2. **Secondary Trip Winding:** Connected to a sensitive polarized relay (electromechanical type) or an electronic trip circuit. Modern RCCBs use electronic amplification for higher sensitivity and immunity to nuisance tripping.

3. **Trip Mechanism:** A spring-loaded mechanical latch that holds the contacts in the closed position under normal conditions. When the trip relay is energised, it releases the latch, allowing the spring to snap the contacts open.

4. **Main Contacts:** Heavy-duty silver-alloy contacts that carry the full load current. They are designed to break the circuit under both normal and fault conditions.

5. **Arc Quenching Chamber:** A set of metal deion plates that split and cool the arc drawn between the separating contacts.

6. **Test Circuit:** A push button in series with a resistor connected between line and neutral, bypassing the toroid.

**Working (3 marks):**

Under healthy conditions, $\vec{I_L} + \vec{I_N} = 0$, so the net flux in the toroid is zero and the secondary winding has no induced EMF. When a leakage current $I_{\Delta}$ flows to earth (e.g., through a person), the vector sum becomes $\vec{I_L} + \vec{I_N} = \vec{I_{\Delta}} \neq 0$. The net flux induces an EMF in the secondary; if $I_{\Delta} \geq I_{\Delta n}$, the trip relay operates and the latch releases, opening the contacts in $\leq 40$ ms.

#### **(b) Compare ELCB and RCCB. Why is RCCB preferred in modern installations? (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer:**

| **S.No.** | **Parameter** | **ELCB (Voltage Operated)** | **RCCB (Current Operated)** |
|-----------|---------------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Operating quantity | Voltage between earth electrodes | Vector sum of L and N currents |
| 2 | Independent earth required | Yes | No |
| 3 | Sensitivity | Low (~50 V threshold) | High (6–500 mA range) |
| 4 | Trip time | ~100 ms (typical) | $\leq 30$ ms at $I_{\Delta n}$ |
| 5 | Suitability for TN systems | Not suitable | Suitable |
| 6 | Modern relevance | Phased out | Industry standard |
| 7 | Discrimination capability | Poor | Good (graded sensitivities) |
| 8 | Standards | Obsolete standards | IS 12640 / IEC 61008 |

**Why RCCB is preferred (2 marks):**
1. Higher sensitivity and faster disconnection save lives — directly addressing the 30 mA fibrillation threshold.
2. No need for an independent earth rod — works in all earthing systems.
3. Better discrimination through current-grading, essential in modern distribution design.
4. Conformance to international standards (IS 12640 / IEC 61008), enabling global product compatibility and tendering.

---

### **Question B:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

#### **(a) Explain different types of RCCBs based on their waveform sensitivity and give one application for each. (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer:**

**Type AC (AC-sensitive):** Trips on alternating residual currents only. Suitable for purely resistive or inductive loads (heaters, motors, lighting). Example: A 30 mA Type-AC RCCB at a residential lighting distribution board.

**Type A (Pulsating DC sensitive):** Trips on AC residual currents **and** pulsating DC residual currents (e.g., from single-phase rectifier loads). Required for circuits supplying washing machines, dishwashers, induction cooktops with electronic rectifiers. Example: 30 mA Type-A RCCB protecting a kitchen appliance circuit.

**Type B (Smooth DC sensitive):** Trips on AC, pulsating DC, **and** smooth DC residual currents. Required for circuits with three-phase rectifier loads, electric vehicle (EV) charging stations, photovoltaic (PV) inverters, variable-frequency drives (VFDs), and battery storage systems. Example: 30 mA Type-B RCCB for an EV charging bay.

**Type F (Frequency-converter resistant):** Enhanced resistance to high-frequency leakage currents from VFDs, while retaining Type-A characteristics. Used for heat pumps and similar variable-speed drives.

#### **(b) Discuss the IS/IEC standards applicable to RCCBs and the criteria for selecting an RCCB for a domestic installation. (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer:**

**Applicable Standards (3 marks):**
- **IS 12640 (Part 1): 2016** — Residual current-operated circuit-breakers without integral overcurrent protection (RCCBs) for household and similar uses. Equivalent to **IEC 61008-1**.
- **IS 12640 (Part 2): 2016** — RCCBs with integral overcurrent protection (RCBOs). Equivalent to **IEC 61009-1**.
- **IEC 62423** — Additional requirements for RCCBs with integral overcurrent protection, including Type B and Type F.
- **IEC 60364** — Wiring installations for buildings, particularly sections 415.1 (additional protection by RCDs) and 531 (RCD selection).
- **National Electrical Code (NEC) 2011** — Section on earth-fault protection.

**Selection Criteria for a Domestic Installation (4 marks):**
1. **Rated residual operating current $I_{\Delta n}$:** **30 mA** for socket-outlet circuits (personal protection); **100 mA** for lighting sub-circuits; **300 mA** at the main incomer (fire protection).
2. **Rated current $I_n$:** Must be greater than or equal to the design load current (commonly 25 A, 40 A, or 63 A for domestic).
3. **Number of poles:** 2-pole for single-phase, 4-pole for three-phase.
4. **Type:** Type AC for general lighting, Type A for kitchen circuits (rectifier loads).
5. **Breaking capacity:** Typically 6 kA or 10 kA, matching the upstream MCB/MCCB.
6. **Operating temperature:** $-5^{\circ}$C to $+40^{\circ}$C for tropical Indian installations.
7. **Voltage rating:** 240 V AC / 415 V AC.
8. **ISI mark / CE marking:** Mandatory for procurement.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in ESE:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing RCCB with MCB** — Many students describe the working of an MCB (bimetallic strip, solenoid) when asked about RCCB. Always remember: **MCB = overcurrent**, **RCCB = residual current imbalance**.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the test button description** — Examiners specifically award 2–3 marks for the test circuit. Don't skip it.
> 
> 3. **Wrong formula for residual current** — The correct vector relation is $\vec{I_L} + \vec{I_N} = \vec{I_{\Delta}}$, **NOT** $\vec{I_L} - \vec{I_N}$. This is because the two windings are wound in opposite directions to cancel magnetically; the algebraic sign convention follows the winding direction.
> 
> 4. **Not drawing the CBCT properly** — In diagrams, the toroidal core must clearly show both L and N conductors passing through it. A single-line through the core loses 2 marks.
> 
> 5. **Ignoring IS standard numbers** — Always quote **IS 12640 (Part 1 / Part 2)** and **IEC 61008 / IEC 61009**. Examiners reward standard citations.
> 
> 6. **Mixing up VO-ELCB and CO-ELCB** — Modern "ELCB" in catalogues usually refers to **Current-Operated ELCB (CO-ELCB)**, which is essentially an RCCB. Be careful with terminology.
> 
> 7. **Trip-time units** — Don't confuse **ms (milliseconds)** for RCCB trip time with **seconds** for MCB trip curves. RCCBs trip in **< 30 ms** at rated sensitivity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorise Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

- [x] **ELCB** = Earth Leakage Circuit Breaker — older term, often Voltage-Operated (VO-ELCB); requires an independent earth electrode; trips at ~50 V earth-potential rise.
- [x] **RCCB** = Residual Current Circuit Breaker — modern standard; operates on current imbalance principle using a CBCT.
- [x] **Core principle:** $\vec{I_L} + \vec{I_N} = \vec{I_{\Delta}}$ — healthy condition gives zero, fault gives residual.
- [x] **Sensitivity ratings:** **30 mA** for personal protection; **300 mA** for fire protection; **100 mA** for sub-circuits.
- [x] **Trip time:** $\leq 300$ ms at $I_{\Delta n}$; $\leq 40$ ms at $5 \times I_{\Delta n}$.
- [x] **Touch voltage limit:** $U_T \leq 50$ V AC.
- [x] **Standard:** **IS 12640 (Part 1 & 2)** / **IEC 61008 / IEC 61009**.
- [x] **Test button:** Bypasses the CBCT through a resistor to simulate fault; should be tested monthly.
- [x] **Types:** AC (resistive loads), A (rectifier loads), B (smooth DC, EV, PV), F (VFD-resistant).
- [x] **MCB vs RCCB:** MCB protects **cables/equipment**; RCCB protects **human life**.
- [x] **Vector sum sign convention:** Always $+$ sign because L and N windings on the CBCT are wound in opposing senses.
- [x] **Applications:** Domestic socket outlets, bathrooms, kitchens, outdoor circuits, hospital equipment rooms, industrial control panels.
- [x] **Discrimination:** Achieved by current-grading (e.g., 300 mA upstream + 30 mA downstream).
- [x] **Always pair an RCCB with an upstream MCB** — RCCB alone does not provide short-circuit or overload protection.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Isolator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Isolator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Isolator** (also called a **Disconnecting Switch** or **Disconnector**) is a mechanical switching device used in high-voltage (HV) and extra-high-voltage (EHV) electrical substations that provides a visible, reliable, and secure **galvanic isolation** of a circuit or equipment from the live power source. It is operated **only under no-load or negligible current conditions** (as per IEC 62271-102) to ensure absolute safety for maintenance personnel and downstream equipment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> An isolator is **NOT a load-breaking device**. It is strictly an **off-load switch** designed to be operated when the circuit current is virtually zero. Closing or opening it under load will result in catastrophic arcing and equipment failure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reference Standard:** IS 9921 (Part I to V) / IEC 62271-102 — *"High-voltage switchgear and control gear – Part 102: Alternating current disconnectors and earthing switches."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Master Lock of a Substation"

Imagine a high-voltage substation as a large industrial water-pumping station:
- The **Circuit Breaker (CB)** is the *valve* that controls water flow and can shut it off during a leak (fault).
- The **Isolator** is the *physical lock* on the main pipeline. Once the valve (CB) stops the water, a technician walks over, engages a heavy-duty padlock (the isolator), and visually confirms that water is **physically separated** from the section being repaired.

Without this "lock" (isolator), the technician can never be 100% certain the section is dead — residual current, back-feed, or human error could energize the line. The isolator provides the **visible air gap** (the physical separation you can see with your eyes) that guarantees safety.

### 1.3 Primary Functions of an Isolator

| Sl. No | Function | Engineering Purpose |
|:------:|:---------|:--------------------|
| 1 | **Visible Isolation** | Provides a physical air gap visible from a safe distance |
| 2 | **Maintenance Safety** | Allows safe human access to downstream equipment |
| 3 | **Bus Transfer** | Permits changeover between alternate power sources |
| 4 | **Earthing Coordination** | Works in tandem with earthing switches to safely discharge trapped charge |
| 5 | **Sectionalizing** | Isolates a faulty bus section or feeder without disturbing the rest |

### 1.4 Operating Conditions — The Golden Rule

$$\boxed{I_{operating} \approx 0 \text{ A (negligible current only)}}$$

An isolator is rated to carry **rated normal current continuously** and withstand **short-circuit currents for a specified duration (typically 1 s or 3 s)**, but it must **never be used to make or break load current** or **transformer magnetizing current** (except in very special low-MVA cases, generally below **~2 A** as per IS 9921).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Pre-isolation Step:** The associated **Circuit Breaker** is opened first, interrupting the load/fault current. This extinguishes the main arc in the CB.
2. **Mechanical Interlock Release:** A mechanical or electrical interlock between the CB and the isolator releases, allowing the isolator handle/operating mechanism to be unlocked.
3. **Contact Separation:** The moving contact of the isolator is slowly withdrawn from the fixed contact by a manual or motorized mechanism, creating an open air gap.
4. **Visible Confirmation:** The operator visually confirms the contact gap (typically minimum **1,150 mm** for 245 kV class as per IS / IEC clearance standards).
5. **Earthing Switch Closure (if required):** For extra safety, a downstream **Earth Switch** is closed, short-circuiting the isolated section to ground and discharging any trapped capacitive/inductive charge.
6. **Lock-out & Tag-out (LOTO):** The isolator is mechanically padlocked in the OPEN position, ensuring it cannot be accidentally re-closed during maintenance.

### 2.2 Essential Ratings of an Isolator (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Sl. No | Rating Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Example for 11 kV) | Unit |
|:------:|:-----------------|:------:|:---------------------------------:|:----:|
| 1 | Rated Voltage | $U_r$ | 12 (system voltage 11 kV) | kV |
| 2 | Rated Normal Current | $I_r$ | 630 / 1250 / 2000 / 2500 / 3150 | A |
| 3 | Rated Short-Time Withstand Current | $I_{k}$ | 25 / 31.5 / 40 / 50 | kA |
| 4 | Short-Circuit Duration | $t_k$ | 1 (standard) or 3 | s |
| 5 | Rated Peak Withstand Current | $I_p$ | $2.5 \times I_k$ | kA (peak) |
| 6 | Rated Frequency | $f$ | 50 | Hz |
| 7 | Power Frequency Withstand Voltage (1 min) | $U_d$ | 28 (rms) | kV |
| 8 | Lightning Impulse Withstand Voltage | $U_{p}$ | 75 (peak) | kV |
| 9 | Creepage Distance | — | 31 mm / kV (for heavily polluted) | mm |
| 10 | Mechanical Endurance Class | M1 / M2 | M2 ≥ 10,000 operations | cycles |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The relationship between short-time withstand current and peak withstand current is fixed by IEC:
> $$I_{p} = 2.5 \times I_{k}$$
> The factor **2.5** accounts for the peak-to-RMS ratio plus a small DC decay component, assuming standard $X/R$ ratio in the test circuit.

### 2.3 Classification of Isolators

**A. Based on Mounting / Construction**
- Single-Pole Isolator (operated individually per phase)
- Double-Pole / Two-Pole Isolator
- Three-Pole Gang-Operated Isolator (most common in distribution)

**B. Based on Contact Movement**
- **Single Break Isolator** — One break per phase; contact opens by rotation
- **Double Break Isolator** — Two breaks per phase; gives twice the gap for the same movement
- **Pantograph Isolator** — Vertical reach, used in EHV substations above 245 kV for bus-coupler
- **Centre-Break Isolator** — Contacts move apart horizontally from the centre

**C. Based on Operation Mechanism**
- Manually Operated (hand-lever or worm-gear operated)
- Motor Operated (for remote / SCADA integration)
- Pneumatic Operated (rare, special applications)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Isolators form the **backbone of substation switching schemes**, such as:
- **Single Bus Scheme** (1 Bus + 1 Isolator per feeder)
- **Double Bus Scheme** (2 Bus isolators per feeder for transfer flexibility)
- **1 ½ Circuit Breaker Scheme** (used in 400 kV / 765 kV EHV substations)
- **Main and Transfer Bus Scheme** (most common for 220 kV / 110 kV)

In **Electrical System Design & Estimation**, isolator costs typically contribute **8–12 %** of total substation switchgear cost. A 400 kV double-break motor-operated isolator with earthing switch may cost ₹ 8–15 lakhs per unit (as per recent utility tenders).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Peak Withstand Current from Short-Time Current

**Given:** The IEC formula relating peak withstand to short-time withstand.

**Step 1 — Start with the basic definition:**
The peak withstand current is the maximum instantaneous current the device can endure without mechanical damage.

**Step 2 — Apply the peak-to-RMS factor for a standard test circuit:**
The standard short-circuit current waveform follows:
$$i(t) = \sqrt{2} \, I_{k} \left[ e^{-(R/L) t} \cos(\omega t) - \cos(\omega t + \phi) \right]$$

**Step 3 — For standard $X/R$ ratio in the IEC test circuit (≈ 17 to 25), the peak factor is empirically fixed at 2.5:**
$$\text{Peak Factor} = 2.5$$

**Step 4 — Therefore:**
$$I_{p} = 2.5 \times I_{k} \quad \text{[kA peak]}$$

**Step 5 — Verification Example:**
For a 31.5 kA, 1-second isolator:
$$I_{p} = 2.5 \times 31.5 = 78.75 \text{ kA (peak)}$$
This matches the nameplate value given in utility specifications.

### 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Air Clearance for Isolator Open Position

**Given:** The minimum open-contact gap must withstand the rated lightning impulse voltage.

**Step 1 — Empirical formula for air gap vs. impulse voltage (as per IS 9921):**
$$d_{min} = k \times U_{p}^{0.85}$$

where $d_{min}$ is in mm, $U_{p}$ is in kV, and $k \approx 0.85$ for standard atmospheric conditions.

**Step 2 — Substituting for a 245 kV class isolator ($U_{p}$ = 1050 kV):**
$$d_{min} = 0.85 \times (1050)^{0.85}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the exponent:**
$$(1050)^{0.85} = e^{0.85 \times \ln(1050)} = e^{0.85 \times 6.957} = e^{5.913}$$

**Step 4 — Final numerical value:**
$$d_{min} = 0.85 \times 369.0 \approx 313.7 \text{ mm} \approx 315 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — With safety factor (1.5× for polluted / outdoor conditions):**
$$d_{designed} = 1.5 \times 315 = 472.5 \text{ mm} \approx 475 \text{ mm}$$

Hence a **double-break** design (which provides two gaps in series) can be more compact:
$$d_{single} \approx 475 \text{ mm} \quad \text{vs.} \quad d_{double-break} \approx 240 \text{ mm per gap} \times 2 \text{ gaps}$$

This is why **double-break isolators are preferred for EHV applications** where compactness is critical.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Isolator vs. Circuit Breaker vs. Load Break Switch

| Parameter | Isolator | Circuit Breaker (CB) | Load Break Switch (LBS) |
|:----------|:---------|:---------------------|:------------------------|
| **Breaking Capacity** | Zero (no-load only) | Very high (full short-circuit) | Moderate (load + small transformer magnetizing) |
| **Making Capacity** | Zero | Full short-circuit making | Limited |
| **Arc Quenching Media** | None (just air gap) | SF₆ / Vacuum / Oil / Air-blast | SF₆ / Air (limited arc control) |
| **Operating Speed** | Slow (2–10 s) | Very fast (3–5 cycles = 60–100 ms) | Medium (0.1–1 s) |
| **Interlock with CB** | Mandatory (mechanical & electrical) | Master interlock | Optional |
| **Cost (per unit, 11 kV)** | ₹ 0.3 – 0.6 L | ₹ 1.5 – 3.5 L | ₹ 0.8 – 1.5 L |
| **Visual Isolation** | Yes (mandatory) | No | Optional |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | High | Very High | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb for Estimation:** The total cost of isolators in a typical 110/11 kV substation = (Number of feeders × 2 isolators × unit cost) + (Bus coupler isolators) + (Earth switches). This is **separate** from circuit breaker cost.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Isolator with Interlock in a Substation Feeder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[BusBar 110kV] --> B[Isolator BusSide]
    B --> C[Current Transformer CT]
    C --> D[Circuit Breaker CB]
    D --> E[Isolator LineSide]
    E --> F[Earth Switch]
    F --> G[Outgoing Feeder Line]
    H[Mechanical Interlock] -.-> B
    H -.-> D
    H -.-> E
    I[SCADA Control] --> D
    I --> B
    I --> E
    I --> F
    J[Manual Lever] --> B
    J --> E
```

**Interpretation of the flow:**
- **A → G** is the power flow path during normal operation.
- **B** and **E** are the two isolators that flank the circuit breaker (a standard practice called the *CB-and-a-half* or *double-isolation* principle).
- **F** is the earth switch, which closes only after **E** is open and discharges the line to ground.
- **H** is the interlock — no isolator can be operated unless the **CB is OPEN** and **locked out**.

### 4.2 Schematic: Operating Mechanism of a Double-Break Isolator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operating Handle Lever] --> B[Worm Gear Reducer]
    B --> C[Rotating Insulator Post]
    C --> D[Moving Contact Arm Left]
    C --> E[Moving Contact Arm Right]
    D --> F[Fixed Contact Left]
    E --> G[Fixed Contact Right]
    F --> H[Open Air Gap 2 x d]
    G --> H
    I[Auxiliary Switch] --> C
    J[Position Indicator Open or Close] --> C
    K[Mechanical Latch Padlock] --> A
```

**Interpretation:**
- A single handle rotates the **insulator post** via a worm-gear.
- The two moving contact arms swing apart, creating **two air gaps in series**.
- An **auxiliary switch** signals position to SCADA; a **latch** allows padlocking in either OPEN or CLOSED state.

### 4.3 Decision Matrix for Isolator Selection (Design Engineer's Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Design Selection] --> B{Voltage Level}
    B -->|Up to 36 kV| C[Single Break OK]
    B -->|72.5 kV to 245 kV| D[Double Break Preferred]
    B -->|Above 245 kV| E[Pantograph or Centre Break]
    C --> F{Current Rating}
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F -->|Up to 1250 A| G[Manual Operation Possible]
    F -->|Above 1250 A| H[Motor Operation Recommended]
    G --> I{Earthing Required}
    H --> I
    I -->|Yes| J[Add Earth Switch]
    I -->|No| K[Standard Isolator]
    J --> L[Final Specification]
    K --> L
```

### 4.4 Constructional Cross-Section View (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Galvanized Steel Base Channel] --> B[Support Insulator Porcelain or Composite]
    B --> C[Rotating Insulator Post]
    C --> D[Moving Contact Blade with Silver Plating]
    D --> E[Fixed Tulip Contact with Silver Plating]
    E --> F[Terminal Connector for Conductor]
    F --> G[Arcing Horns Optional]
    A --> H[Earthing Connector for Base Frame]
    A --> I[Operating Mechanism Box at Ground Level]
    I --> J[Vertical Operating Rod with Insulating Coupling]
    J --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why silver-plated contacts?** Silver has the lowest contact resistance among practical metals (~1.59 μΩ·cm) and resists oxidation, ensuring low temperature rise even at 3,150 A continuous current. Copper is used for current-carrying parts; silver is only the contact face (typically 5–10 μm thick).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model QP]**
**Define an isolator. Why is it not operated under loaded conditions?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **isolator** is a mechanical switching device that provides a *visible open gap* in a high-voltage circuit for the safety of maintenance personnel. It is rated to carry the full normal current continuously and withstand the peak short-circuit current for a specified duration, but it has **no arc-quenching mechanism**.

It is **not operated under load** because (1 mark each):
1. The slow contact separation would allow an uncontrolled arc to sustain and damage the contacts permanently.
2. It lacks any arc-quenching medium (no SF₆, vacuum, or oil).
3. It would violate the mandatory mechanical interlock with the upstream circuit breaker, endangering the operator and system.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**List any four essential ratings that must be specified while ordering an isolator for a 110 kV substation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four essential ratings are:
1. **Rated Voltage** (e.g., 123 kV for 110 kV system) — 1 mark
2. **Rated Normal Current** (e.g., 1250 A or 2000 A) — 1 mark
3. **Short-Time Withstand Current & Duration** (e.g., 40 kA for 1 s) — 0.5 mark
4. **Lightning Impulse Withstand Voltage** (e.g., 550 kVp for 110 kV) — 0.5 mark

*(A fifth valid rating, like the dynamic peak current or creepage distance, may be added for completeness.)*

---

### PART B — Detailed Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplemented Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **construction and working** of a **double-break isolator** with a neat block diagram. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare an **isolator** with a **circuit breaker** in terms of at least six parameters. State two applications where isolators are mandatory in substation design. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction & Working of Double-Break Isolator (7 marks):**

A double-break isolator provides **two air gaps in series per phase**, thereby doubling the isolation for the same physical movement of the contact.

**Components (3 marks):**
- **Base frame** (galvanized steel channel) mounted on the substation structure
- **Rotating post insulator** (porcelain or composite) — rotates about its vertical axis
- **Two fixed contacts** (tulip-type, silver-plated) mounted on separate support insulators
- **One moving contact arm** spanning between the two fixed contacts
- **Operating mechanism** at ground level — worm-gear driven handle or motor
- **Auxiliary switch** for position indication and SCADA interlock

**Working (4 marks):**
1. The operator turns the handle (or motor receives command).
2. The worm-gear reducer transmits rotation to the rotating post insulator.
3. The post rotates by **~90°**, swinging the moving contact arm out of the two fixed contacts.
4. This creates **two air gaps in series**, each typically **240–300 mm** at 110 kV.
5. The auxiliary switch opens its 'closed' contact and closes its 'open' contact, signalling the new state to SCADA.
6. A mechanical latch engages, allowing the operator to **padlock** the isolator in the open position.

**Block Diagram (already provided in SECTION 4.2 of these notes).**

---

**(b) Comparison & Applications (7 marks):**

The 6-parameter comparison is given in the **Analytical Matrix in Section 3.3** of these notes (5 marks).

**Two mandatory applications (2 marks):**

1. **Bus sectionalizing in 110 kV / 220 kV substations** — Isolators sectionalize the bus so that maintenance can be carried out on one section without a complete shutdown.

2. **Isolating a circuit breaker for maintenance** — Even after opening the CB, an isolator on each side of the CB ensures the breaker itself can be safely serviced. This is the *double-isolation principle* mandated by IS/IEC and CEA Safety Regulations 2010.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**(a)** Discuss the **various types of isolators** based on construction. Mention typical applications of each. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **245 kV class substation** requires an isolator. The rated short-time withstand current is **40 kA for 1 s**. Calculate the **peak withstand current** and determine the **minimum single-gap air clearance** required. Take the empirical constant $k = 0.85$ and the standard lightning impulse voltage $U_p = 1050$ kV. Use a safety factor of 1.5. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Types of Isolators (7 marks):**

1. **Single-Break Isolator (1.5 marks)** — One contact break per phase. Used up to 36 kV in indoor distribution substations. Simple, compact, low cost.

2. **Double-Break Isolator (1.5 marks)** — Two breaks per phase. Used in 72.5 kV to 245 kV outdoor substations. Provides greater isolation in a smaller footprint.

3. **Pantograph Isolator (2 marks)** — Scissor-type mechanism that raises and lowers a contact vertically onto the busbar. Used as a **bus-coupler** in 245 kV to 765 kV EHV substations; saves horizontal space.

4. **Centre-Break Isolator (2 marks)** — Contacts move horizontally apart from the centre of the phase. Used in 245 kV to 420 kV applications, especially where vertical clearance is constrained.

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Peak Withstand Current (2 marks):**
$$I_{p} = 2.5 \times I_{k} = 2.5 \times 40 = 100 \text{ kA (peak)}$$

**Step 2 — Empirical air gap (2 marks):**
$$d_{min} = k \times U_{p}^{0.85} = 0.85 \times (1050)^{0.85}$$
$$= 0.85 \times 369.0 = 313.7 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply safety factor (2 marks):**
$$d_{designed} = 1.5 \times 313.7 = 470.5 \text{ mm} \approx 475 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
For a single-break isolator, the contact gap must be **at least 475 mm**. For a double-break design, the gap per break is:
$$d_{per-break} = \frac{475}{2} = 237.5 \text{ mm} \approx 240 \text{ mm}$$

This is why **double-break** isolators are universally preferred for 245 kV applications.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing the rating of an isolator with that of a circuit breaker.** An isolator is **not rated for breaking capacity** in MVA. Writing "isolator breaking capacity = 2,500 MVA" is a common error → loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the interlock requirement.** In substation design questions, students often forget to mention the **mechanical & electrical interlock** between CB and isolator → loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up $I_p$ and $I_k$ symbols.** $I_p$ is the **peak** (instantaneous) value; $I_k$ is the **RMS** short-time value. The factor is **2.5**, not 1.414 (which is $\sqrt{2}$ for a pure sinusoid).
> 4. **Not stating the Standard Reference.** Forgetting to cite **IS 9921 / IEC 62271-102** → loses 0.5 mark in design-type questions.
> 5. **Neglecting the earthing switch in single-line diagrams.** Always show the **earth switch** downstream of the line isolator in your SLD — examiners specifically look for it.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ An **isolator is an OFF-LOAD switch** — it is operated only when the circuit current is zero, after the CB has opened.
- ✅ The primary function is to provide **visible isolation** for maintenance safety, not to break load current.
- ✅ Mandatory interlock exists between the **circuit breaker** and the isolator — operating the isolator without opening the CB is mechanically prevented.
- ✅ Key ratings: **Rated voltage, Normal current, Short-time current ($I_k$), Peak current ($I_p = 2.5 I_k$), Impulse voltage, Creepage distance**.
- ✅ **Single-break** for ≤ 36 kV, **Double-break** for 72.5–245 kV, **Pantograph / Centre-break** for > 245 kV.
- ✅ **Silver-plated copper contacts** ensure low contact resistance and high current-carrying capacity.
- ✅ **Earth switch** is a critical companion device — it is closed only after the isolator is open, to discharge trapped charge and provide a safe working ground.
- ✅ **Double-isolation principle**: In any CB bay, isolators are placed on **both sides** of the CB so the breaker itself can be safely maintained.
- ✅ **Cost contribution** of isolators in a substation is typically 8–12 % of total switchgear cost.
- ✅ **Standards to remember:** **IS 9921 (Parts I–V)** and **IEC 62271-102** for design, testing, and rating; **CEA Safety Regulations 2010** for operational mandates.
- ✅ In SLDs, always show: **Bus → Isolator → CT → CB → Isolator → Earth Switch → Line** (the *golden sequence*).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Requirements for Electrical Installations - Residential/Commercial/High Rise Building
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** General Requirements for Electrical Installations - Residential/Commercial/High Rise Building

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **General Requirements for Electrical Installations** refer to the standardized set of design, safety, wiring, earthing, protection, and operational specifications governed primarily by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** and the **Indian Electricity Rules, 1956 (with amendments)** that must be satisfied before any Low Voltage (LV) electrical installation in Residential, Commercial, or High-Rise Building premises is energized and commissioned.

The governing code of practice in India is **IS 732:1989 (Reaffirmed 2014) – Code of Practice for Electrical Wiring Installations (System Voltage not exceeding 650 V)** along with the **National Electrical Code (NEC) 2011** and **National Building Code (NBC) 2016 – Part 8 (Building Services)**.

The term "General Requirements" encompasses the following nine cardinal pillars of an installation:

| # | Pillar | Governing Standard |
|---|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Safety of human life & property** | Indian Electricity Rules, IS 732 |
| 2 | **Wiring system selection & execution** | IS 732, IS 4648 |
| 3 | **Earthing & equipotential bonding** | IS 3043 |
| 4 | **Protection (OC, SC, EF, EL)** | IS 732, IS 8828 |
| 5 | **Accessibility & maintainability** | NBC 2016 |
| 6 | **Adequacy for present & future load** | IS 4648 |
| 7 | **Voltage drop limitations (≤ 4 % of nominal)** | IS 732 |
| 8 | **Sub-circuit segregation & load balancing** | IS 4648, IS 732 |
| 9 | **Documentation & inspection** | IE Rules 1956 |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: The "Nervous System of a Building"**
> Just as the human nervous system transmits signals safely from the brain (heart) to the limbs, the electrical installation in a building carries power from the utility supply (source) to every appliance (load). A general requirement is analogous to **medical protocols**:
> - **Insulation** is like the *myelin sheath* around nerves (prevents leakage / shock).
> - **Earthing** is the *earth return path*, similar to how a human body discharges static to the ground.
> - **MCB / ELCB** are the *reflex arcs* that instantly disconnect a faulty circuit.
> - **Voltage drop limits** are like *blood pressure limits* — too high and equipment gets damaged; too low and the device starves.
> - **Diversity factor** is similar to how not every muscle in your body works at 100 % simultaneously.

In a **Residential** building, the system is simple (one distribution board). In a **Commercial** building, the load mix (lighting, AC, lifts, computers) is heterogeneous. In a **High-Rise** building (height ≥ **15 m** as per NBC 2016 or ≥ **24 m** as per some state Fire Acts), the requirements include *fire-pump circuits, emergency lighting, staircase pressurization, vertical riser sizing, and lightning protection* — making the system complex and mission-critical.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - Standard residential supply voltage in India: **230 V (Single Phase, 2-wire)** or **400 V (Three Phase, 4-wire)**, **50 Hz** AC.
> - Maximum permissible voltage drop in an installation: **4 % of declared voltage** (3 % for sub-circuit + 1 % for main circuit) as per IS 732.
> - Standard height of light switch: **1.2 m to 1.5 m** from finished floor level (FFL).
> - Standard height of socket outlets: **0.3 m (general)**, **1.2 m (kitchen / washbasin)**, **1.0 m (work-table)**.
> - Minimum number of sub-circuits in a residence: 1 per **800 W to 1000 W** of total connected load.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Requirements

A safe and code-compliant electrical installation must satisfy the following logical flow:

1. **Step 1 — Assessment of Load**
   - Calculate the total connected load by listing every appliance with its wattage.
   - Apply **diversity factor** (DF) to determine *maximum demand*.
   - Formula: $\text{Maximum Demand} = \text{Connected Load} \times \text{Diversity Factor}$

2. **Step 2 — Selection of Supply System**
   - Single phase 230 V if maximum demand < **5 kW** (residential).
   - Three phase 400 V if load > **5 kW** or load mix contains heavy 3-ϕ equipment (lifts, AHUs, central AC).

3. **Step 3 — Sizing of Service Connection**
   - Current: $I = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L \times \cos\phi}$ (3-ϕ) or $I = \dfrac{P}{V_{ph} \times \cos\phi}$ (1-ϕ).
   - Apply derating factors for ambient temperature, grouping, and method of installation.

4. **Step 4 — Design of Sub-Circuits**
   - Segregate **lighting**, **power (socket-outlet)**, and **fixed equipment** sub-circuits.
   - Each sub-circuit protected by its own MCB at the Distribution Board (DB).
   - Maximum load per sub-circuit: **16 A** (≈ **3000 W** at 230 V) for ring/ radial.

5. **Step 5 — Conductor (Cable) Sizing**
   - Based on *current carrying capacity* AND *voltage drop* — whichever gives a *larger* conductor cross-section.
   - **Voltage drop formula** (single phase): $V_d = \dfrac{2 \times I \times L \times \rho \times \cos\phi}{A}$ (when using length L of run).
   - **Voltage drop formula** (three phase): $V_d = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \times I \times L \times \rho \times \cos\phi}{A}$.

6. **Step 6 — Selection of Protective Devices**
   - **MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker)** — overload + short-circuit.
   - **RCCB / RCBO (Residual Current Circuit Breaker)** — earth leakage (typically **30 mA** for personnel safety, **100 mA / 300 mA** for fire protection).
   - **MCCB** for higher ratings (> **100 A**).
   - Co-ordination study ensures discrimination between upstream and downstream breakers.

7. **Step 7 — Earthing & Equipotential Bonding**
   - **Pipe electrode** or **plate electrode** driven to a depth so that earth resistance $R_e \le 5\ \Omega$ (for residential/commercial) and $\le 1\ \Omega$ (for high-rise / sub-station).
   - Two separate earth pits: one for **system earth** (neutral), one for **equipment earth** (body).

8. **Step 8 — Inspection, Testing & Documentation**
   - Continuity, insulation resistance ($> 1\ \text{M}\Omega$ between phases/E/N/earth), earth resistance, polarity, and RCD trip-time checks are mandatory.

### 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Compliance with these general requirements:
> - **Reduces loss of life** (about **30 %** of fire accidents in India are electrical in origin — source: BPRA).
> - **Optimizes copper usage** (correct voltage drop ⇒ no over-sizing).
> - **Ensures insurance validity** for the building.
> - **Mandatory for occupancy certificate** from the municipal authority.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|--------|--------------|
| 1 | Power (3-ϕ) | $P = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_L \times \cos\phi$ | W, with $V_L$ line voltage |
| 2 | Power (1-ϕ) | $P = V_{ph} \times I_{ph} \times \cos\phi$ | W |
| 3 | Maximum Demand | $MD = \sum (\text{Load} \times \text{DF})$ | kW, DF depends on appliance |
| 4 | Current (3-ϕ) | $I_L = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi}$ | A |
| 5 | Current (1-ϕ) | $I_{ph} = \dfrac{P}{V_{ph} \cdot \cos\phi}$ | A |
| 6 | Voltage drop (1-ϕ) | $V_d = \dfrac{2 \cdot I \cdot L \cdot \rho \cdot \cos\phi}{A}$ | V |
| 7 | Voltage drop (3-ϕ) | $V_d = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot \rho \cdot \cos\phi}{A}$ | V |
| 8 | % Voltage drop | $\%V_d = \dfrac{V_d}{V_{rated}} \times 100$ | Should be $\le 4\ \%$ per IS 732 |
| 9 | Energy | $E = P \times t$ | kWh |
| 10 | Earth resistance | $R_e = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$ (pipe) | $\Omega$ |
| 11 | Diversity Factor (DF) | $DF = \dfrac{\text{Installed Load}}{\text{Max. Demand}}$ | Typically $0.5$ to $0.8$ |
| 12 | Number of sub-circuits | $N_{sub} = \left\lceil \dfrac{\text{Total Load}}{800\ \text{to}\ 1000\ \text{W}} \right\rceil$ | Always round-up |
| 13 | Floor area for sub-circuit | Each sub-circuit feeds $\le 100\ \text{m}^2$ floor area | IS 4648 guideline |
| 14 | Lightning protection radius | $R_{cone} = h \cdot (\text{protection level factor})$ | Laid down in IS/IEC 62305 |

### 2.4 Typical Diversity Factors (Recall Value for KTU)

| Load Type | Diversity Factor |
|-----------|-----------------|
| Lighting | 0.9 – 1.0 |
| Socket outlets (general) | 0.4 – 0.5 |
| Socket outlets (kitchen) | 0.6 – 0.7 |
| AC units (residential) | 0.7 – 0.8 |
| Lifts | 0.7 |
| Pumps (fire / domestic) | 0.6 – 1.0 |
| HVAC central plant | 0.7 – 0.9 |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Requirements Across Building Categories

| Requirement | Residential | Commercial | High-Rise Building |
|-------------|-------------|------------|-------------------|
| **Supply voltage** | 230 V 1-ϕ / 400 V 3-ϕ | 11 kV / 433 V HT-LT | 11 kV / 22 kV HT with multiple sub-stations |
| **Typical load density** | 50 – 80 W/m² | 100 – 200 W/m² | 150 – 300 W/m² (incl. HVAC, lifts) |
| **Sub-circuits** | 1 per 800 W | 1 per 1500 W (lighting), 1 per 3000 W (sockets) | Multiple risers, zone DBs per floor |
| **Earthing** | 2 pits ≤ 5 Ω | 4 – 6 pits ≤ 1 Ω | 8 – 20+ pits, ≤ 0.5 Ω, mesh + chemical |
| **Protection** | MCB + RCCB 30 mA | MCB + MCCB + RCCB 30/100 mA | ACB + MCCB + RCCB + AFDD + Surge |
| **Fire-pump circuit** | Optional | Required > 15 m | Mandatory, with dedicated feeder |
| **Emergency lighting** | Not required | Required in public areas | Required on all floors + staircases |
| **Lightning protection** | Not required usually | Required > 24 m | Mandatory > 24 m (NBC 2016) |
| **Standby power** | UPS only | DG set | DG + UPS + Emergency DG + solar |
| **Cable management** | Conduit / casing-capping | Cable tray / bus-duct | Vertical risers, bus-trunking, fire-rated |
| **Earthing type** | TN-S | TN-S or TN-C-S | TN-S with separate clean earth for IT |
| **Code of practice** | IS 732, IS 4648 | IS 732, NBC 2016 | NBC 2016, IS 732, IS 2309 / IEC 62305, NFPA 72 |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Derivation — Voltage Drop and Cable Sizing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample Problem:** A 230 V, 1-ϕ, 50 Hz sub-circuit feeds a load of **2 kW** at a power factor of **0.85 lagging** through a copper cable of run length **30 m**. Calculate the voltage drop in the cable if the cable cross-section is **4 mm²** and verify whether IS 732 limits are satisfied. Take resistivity of copper as $\rho = 0.0172\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{mm}^2/\text{m}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the full-load current of the load.**

$$I_{ph} \;=\; \frac{P}{V_{ph}\,\cos\phi} \;=\; \frac{2000}{230 \times 0.85} \;\text{A}$$

$$I_{ph} \;=\; \frac{2000}{195.5} \;\approx\; 10.23\ \text{A}$$

*Explanation:* In a single-phase AC circuit, real power equals the product of RMS voltage, RMS current, and power factor. We rearranged to find current.

**Step 2 — Express the conductor length seen by the current.**

For a single-phase two-wire system, current flows out through the phase conductor and returns through the neutral — therefore the *loop length* is twice the run length:

$$L_{loop} \;=\; 2 \times L_{run} \;=\; 2 \times 30 \;=\; 60\ \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the voltage drop formula for a single-phase circuit.**

The DC resistance of the conductor is:

$$R_{conductor} \;=\; \frac{\rho \cdot L_{loop}}{A} \;=\; \frac{0.0172 \times 60}{4}\ \Omega$$

$$R_{conductor} \;=\; \frac{1.032}{4} \;=\; 0.258\ \Omega$$

**Step 4 — Compute the actual voltage drop (resistive component is dominant for power cables < 25 mm²).**

$$V_d \;=\; I_{ph} \times R_{conductor} \times \cos\phi$$

$$V_d \;=\; 10.23 \times 0.258 \times 0.85 \;\text{V}$$

$$V_d \;\approx\; 2.243\ \text{V}$$

*Explanation:* The factor of $\cos\phi$ is applied because the resistive drop in-phase with the current and the reactive drop (out-of-phase) combine vectorially; for sub-circuit calculations under the *Approximate Method* of IS 732, the resistive drop is used directly (worst-case at unity PF) — but when the load PF is poor, the *$\cos\phi$ correction* is included.

**Step 5 — Express the voltage drop as a percentage of the nominal supply voltage.**

$$\%V_d \;=\; \frac{V_d}{V_{rated}} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{2.243}{230} \times 100$$

$$\%V_d \;\approx\; 0.975\ \%$$

**Step 6 — Compare with the IS 732 permissible limit.**

Permissible limit: **4 % of nominal = 4 % × 230 V = 9.2 V (3 % sub-circuit + 1 % main)**.

Observed: **0.975 % < 4 %** ⟹ **Compliant.** A 4 mm² copper cable is *over-specified*; a 2.5 mm² cable would also satisfy voltage drop. The current carrying capacity of 2.5 mm² PVC Cu is ~ 21 A, so it would suffice.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation — Maximum Demand of a Residential Dwelling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample Problem:** A 2-BHK residence has the following loads: 8 light points × 40 W, 4 fan points × 75 W, 1 TV socket 100 W, 1 fridge socket 200 W, 1 AC 1.5 TR (≈ 1800 W), 1 geyser 2000 W, 2 general sockets × 100 W. Compute (i) connected load, (ii) maximum demand using diversity factors.

**Step 1 — Sum the individual connected loads.**

$$P_{lights} = 8 \times 40 = 320\ \text{W}$$

$$P_{fans} = 4 \times 75 = 300\ \text{W}$$

$$P_{fixed\,appliances} = 1800 + 2000 + 200 = 4000\ \text{W}$$

$$P_{sockets} = 1 \times 100 + 1 \times 100 = 200\ \text{W (TV + general)}$$

$$P_{connected} = 320 + 300 + 4000 + 200 = 4820\ \text{W} \approx 4.82\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 2 — Apply diversity factor (DF).**

| Load | Power (W) | DF | Demand (W) |
|------|-----------|----|-----------|
| Lights | 320 | 0.9 | 288 |
| Fans | 300 | 0.8 | 240 |
| AC | 1800 | 0.8 | 1440 |
| Geyser | 2000 | 0.5 | 1000 |
| Fridge | 200 | 0.6 | 120 |
| General sockets | 200 | 0.4 | 80 |
| **Total** | **4820** | — | **3168** |

$$P_{max\,demand} \approx 3.17\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the design current.**

$$I = \frac{3170}{230 \times 0.85} \approx 16.22\ \text{A}$$

So the main MCB rating should be **20 A (standard next size up)** with a 6 mm² or 10 mm² main copper conductor.

**Step 4 — Number of sub-circuits required.**

$$N_{sub} = \left\lceil \frac{3170}{800} \right\rceil = \lceil 3.96 \rceil = 4\ \text{sub-circuits}$$

A practical split: (i) Lighting, (ii) Fans, (iii) General sockets (5 A 3-pin), (iv) Power sockets (15 A) for AC/Geyser.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1. List any three general requirements of electrical installations as per IS 732.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Adequate current-carrying capacity** — Every conductor shall be selected such that under normal conditions of use, it carries the design current without exceeding the permissible temperature limit for the type of insulation.
2. **Voltage drop limitation** — The total voltage drop in any sub-circuit or main circuit shall not exceed **4 % of the declared supply voltage** (3 % sub-circuit + 1 % main circuit).
3. **Provision for earthing** — All metal enclosures, conduits, switchgear, and equipment bodies shall be connected to a *low-impedance* earth electrode whose resistance does not exceed **5 Ω** for residential and **0.5 – 1 Ω** for high-rise / sub-station.

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> **Q2. What is the function of a Residual Current Circuit Breaker (RCCB) and at what rating is it normally used in residential premises?**

**Model Answer:**

The **RCCB** continuously senses the *algebraic sum of currents* flowing through the phase and neutral conductors of a circuit. Under normal conditions this sum is *zero* (Kirchhoff's current law). If a person touches a live conductor and current leaks to earth through the body, the sum becomes *non-zero* — the RCCB detects this *residual / earth-leakage current* and *trips the circuit* in under 40 ms.

For personnel safety in residential premises, the rated residual operating current is **30 mA** (high sensitivity). For fire protection in sub-distribution circuits, ratings of **100 mA or 300 mA** are used.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper Module 2]**
> **Q(A). (a)** Explain the general requirements to be satisfied for electrical installations in *residential buildings* as per **IS 732** and **IS 4648**. **(7 marks)**
> **Q(A). (b)** A single-storey residential building has 10 light points × 40 W, 4 fan points × 75 W, 2 × 15 A socket outlets (each 200 W), 1 × 5 A socket 100 W, and 1 AC of 1.5 TR (1800 W). Apply diversity and compute the **maximum demand** and the **size of the main MCB**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Q(A)(a)

The general requirements for a residential installation are tabulated below:

1. **Voltage**: 230 V single-phase 2-wire (50 Hz) for loads up to 5 kW; 400 V three-phase 4-wire for higher loads.
2. **Method of wiring**: Conduit (PVC concealed / GI surface) or casing-capping as per IS 732.
3. **Sub-circuit grouping**: Maximum of **800 W – 1000 W** per 5 A/6 A sub-circuit and **2000 W – 3000 W** per 15 A/16 A sub-circuit.
4. **Sub-circuit segregation**: Lighting, fans, and 5 A socket-outlet sub-circuits should be *separate* from 15 A power sub-circuits.
5. **Number of sub-circuits per residence**: A minimum of *two* for any one-room dwelling, *three* for a 2/3-BHK.
6. **Earthing**: Earth pit of $R_e \le 5\ \Omega$; two separate pits — one for body, one for neutral (TN-S).
7. **Switchboard height**: 1.2 m to 1.5 m; socket outlets 0.3 m above floor (general) / 1.2 m (kitchen).
8. **Main switch (ICDP/ICTP)**: Within 1.5 m of the meter board; *easily accessible* and *clearly labelled*.
9. **RCCB 30 mA**: Mandatory for all socket-outlet sub-circuits serving persons.
10. **Documentation**: Single-line diagram, layout, test report (continuity, IR, earth resistance) — to be furnished before energisation.

### Model Solution — Q(A)(b)

**Step 1 — Compute the connected load.**

| Load | Quantity × Power | Total (W) |
|------|------------------|----------|
| Lights | 10 × 40 | 400 |
| Fans | 4 × 75 | 300 |
| 15 A sockets | 2 × 200 | 400 |
| 5 A socket | 1 × 100 | 100 |
| AC | 1 × 1800 | 1800 |
| **Total** | — | **3000 W** |

**Step 2 — Apply diversity factors.**

| Load | Power (W) | DF | Demand (W) |
|------|----------|----|-----------|
| Lights | 400 | 0.9 | 360 |
| Fans | 300 | 0.8 | 240 |
| 15 A sockets | 400 | 0.5 | 200 |
| 5 A socket | 100 | 0.4 | 40 |
| AC | 1800 | 0.8 | 1440 |
| **Total** | **3000** | — | **2280 W** |

**Maximum Demand** = **2.28 kW** (≈ 2.3 kW).

**Step 3 — Compute the main current at 0.85 PF.**

$$I = \frac{2280}{230 \times 0.85} = \frac{2280}{195.5} \approx 11.66\ \text{A}$$

**Step 4 — Choose main MCB standard rating.**

Nearest standard MCB rating above 11.66 A is **16 A (single-pole + N / SPN)**. To allow future load growth of 30 %, a **20 A or 25 A SPN MCB** is recommended. Conductor sizing: 4 mm² copper (or 2.5 mm² with 16 A MCB), but 4 mm² is preferred for headroom.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q(B). (a)** With the help of a neat single-line diagram, explain the **electrical installation scheme for a high-rise building**, indicating all essential sub-systems (HT supply, transformer, DG, lifts, fire pump, earthing, lightning protection). **(7 marks)**
> **Q(B). (b)** Compare the **general requirements** of a *residential* and a *high-rise commercial* installation in a tabular form (minimum 8 points). **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Q(B)(a)

A high-rise building (height > 24 m) requires a **multi-source, multi-tier** electrical scheme:

1. **Source 1 — HT supply** (11 kV or 22 kV) from utility → **HT panel** with Vacuum Circuit Breaker (VCB) for protection and isolation.
2. **Step-down transformer** (e.g., 1000 kVA, 11 kV/433 V, Dyn11) housed in the **sub-station room** at the lowest basement.
3. **Main LT panel** (PCC — Power Control Centre) with **1600 A Air Circuit Breaker (ACB)** as the *main incoming device* and short-circuit, overload, and earth-fault releases.
4. **Sub-distribution panels** — segregated by function: lighting, power, HVAC, lifts, fire-fighting, DG.
5. **Vertical risers** — Bus-duct or armoured cable *risers* running through dedicated electrical shafts; **bus-duct** preferred for > 400 A load per phase.
6. **Floor Distribution Boards (FDBs)** — located in electrical shafts on each floor; FDBs further sub-divide lighting and power sub-circuits for that floor.
7. **DG set** (e.g., 500 kVA) with **AMF panel** — automatically starts on mains failure and supplies *essential load*: lifts, fire pump, common-area lighting, IT server room.
8. **UPS / Inverter** — for emergency lighting and IT loads, sized for 30 min back-up.
9. **Fire-pump starter** — dedicated feeder from the sub-station; not routed through FDB. Includes *Jockey pump*, *Main pump*, and *Booster pump* with auto-start logic from pressure switches and the fire alarm panel.
10. **Earthing** — *Earth-mesh* design using **50 × 6 mm GI strip** with multiple earth pits ($R_e \le 0.5\ \Omega$); separate *clean earth* for IT racks; *equipotential bonding* of all metallic services (water, gas, fire pipe).
11. **Lightning protection** — Faraday cage or single-mast air-termination as per IS/IEC 62305 — Class II or III; *down-conductors* taken via the building structure to dedicated earth pits.
12. **Power factor correction** — Automatic PFC panel with detuned harmonic filter (typically 7 %) at the LT bus.

*(The single-line diagram was drawn in SECTION 4.2 — refer to the Mermaid block above.)*

### Model Solution — Q(B)(b)

| # | Parameter | Residential | High-Rise Commercial |
|---|----------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Supply voltage | 230 V 1-ϕ or 400 V 3-ϕ | 11 kV HT, 433 V LT |
| 2 | Connected load | 2 – 10 kW | 500 kW – 5 MW |
| 3 | Sub-stations | Not required | Dedicated sub-station |
| 4 | DG / standby | Not required | Mandatory (essential load) |
| 5 | Earthing | 2 pits, ≤ 5 Ω | Earth mesh, ≤ 0.5 Ω |
| 6 | Protection | MCB + 30 mA RCCB | ACB + MCCB + RCCB + Surge |
| 7 | Fire-pump | Not required | Mandatory dedicated feeder |
| 8 | Lightning protection | Generally not needed | Mandatory above 24 m |
| 9 | Cabling | PVC conduit concealed | Bus-duct, fire-rated armoured |
| 10 | Energy monitoring | Sub-meter optional | Sub-metering + BMS integration |
| 11 | Compliance | IS 732, IS 4648 | IS 732, NBC 2016, IS 2309, IS 3043, NFPA 72 |
| 12 | AMC | Self-managed | Service contract with facility agency |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. *Mistake 1* — Students often *forget to apply the diversity factor* to compute the maximum demand, leading to a grossly oversized main switch.
> 2. *Mistake 2* — Conductor sizing based on **current carrying capacity alone** is *not sufficient*; IS 732 mandates *voltage drop check* as a co-condition.
> 3. *Mistake 3* — Students sometimes write the *single-phase voltage drop formula* for a three-phase circuit (and vice-versa). Check the line/phase value and the factor (2 vs. $\sqrt{3}$).
> 4. *Mistake 4* — For high-rise buildings, the *earth resistance* limit is often *quoted as 5 Ω* — this is wrong; the limit tightens to **0.5 – 1 Ω**.
> 5. *Mistake 5* — Mark loss occurs when students do *not mention the relevant BIS standard code number*. Always cite **IS 732**, **IS 4648**, **IS 3043**, **IS 2309**, **NBC 2016**.
> 6. *Mistake 6* — In the *residential* problem, students often include *all appliances at 100 %* in the demand calculation. Apply the diversity table provided above.
> 7. *Mistake 7* — Skip the *unit*. Always state *W, kW, V, A* explicitly to fetch the **½ mark** per KTU marking scheme.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist**
> - [ ] General requirements are governed by **IS 732, IS 4648, IS 3043, IS 2309, NBC 2016, Indian Electricity Rules 1956**.
> - [ ] Maximum voltage drop in a consumer installation = **4 % of declared voltage** (3 % sub + 1 % main).
> - [ ] Standard sub-circuit capacity: **5 A / 6 A** for lighting; **15 A / 16 A** for power.
> - [ ] Each sub-circuit feeds **≤ 800 W (5 A)** or **≤ 3000 W (15 A)** of connected load.
> - [ ] Number of sub-circuits in a residence: minimum **2 in 1 RK**, **3 in 2-3 BHK**, **4+ in villas**.
> - [ ] Switch height: **1.2 – 1.5 m**; socket height: **0.3 m (general), 1.0 – 1.2 m (kitchen)**.
> - [ ] RCCB **30 mA** for personnel safety; **100 / 300 mA** for fire/equipment protection.
> - [ ] Earth resistance: **≤ 5 Ω** residential; **≤ 0.5 Ω** high-rise mesh; **≤ 1 Ω** sub-station.
> - [ ] Two earth pits — *system* (neutral) and *equipment* (body), inter-linked only at one point in TN-S.
> - [ ] High-rise (> **24 m** as per NBC 2016) requires **fire pump, emergency lighting, staircase pressurization, lightning protection, dedicated sub-station, DG set, earthing mesh**.
> - [ ] Diversity factor (DF) is a multiplier applied to *connected load* to estimate *maximum demand*; never the reverse.
> - [ ] Voltage drop formula: 1-ϕ → multiply by **2 L**; 3-ϕ → multiply by **$\sqrt{3}$ L**.
> - [ ] Documentation: single-line diagram, layout, test sheets — **mandatory** before energising an installation.
> - [ ] IS 4648 = residential layout guide; IS 8884 = electric bell systems; IS 321 = emergency lighting.
> - [ ] Lightning protection levels (LPL I to IV) per IEC 62305: choose *Class II* for typical high-rise.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Method of Load Survey for Electrical Installation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Method of Load Survey for Electrical Installation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Load Survey** in electrical installation engineering is defined as the systematic and methodical process of identifying, recording, classifying, and quantifying all electrical loads (consumers) connected to an electrical network or installation, along with their operating characteristics, diversity of usage, time-of-use patterns, and demand profiles. It is the foundational engineering activity that precedes the design, estimation, and economic selection of any electrical distribution system — ranging from a domestic dwelling to a large industrial plant or a multi-storey commercial complex.

The procedure involves the enumeration of **connected loads**, the determination of **maximum demand**, the evaluation of **diversity factor** and **demand factor**, the construction of **load curves** (daily, weekly, seasonal), and the establishment of load patterns to enable accurate selection of transformers, switchgear, conductors, and protective devices as per **IS 732 (1989)** and **IE Rules 1956**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Load Survey is a *prerequisite* for the design of any LT/HT installation. Skipping this step results in oversized (loss of capital) or undersized (safety hazard) systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are planning a **wedding banquet for 500 guests** in a hotel. Before ordering food, drinks, plates, glasses, chairs, and AC capacity, you need to *survey* — how many guests will come, what dishes will be most consumed, peak eating times, etc.

A Load Survey is the **electrical equivalent of this pre-event survey**. Instead of food and chairs, we survey:
- How many lights, fans, AC units, motors, machines exist?
- Which ones run together (simultaneously)?
- For how long?
- When is the peak demand period?

> Just as a banquet planner over-orders or under-orders without a survey, an electrical engineer who skips the load survey ends up either **overspending** on an oversized system or **risking fires and breakdowns** on an undersized one.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are used in load survey (as per **IE Rules** and **IS 732**):

| Metric | Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Connected Load (CL) | kW / kVA | Sum of nameplate ratings of all loads |
| Maximum Demand (MD) | kW / kVA | Highest load drawn at any instant |
| Demand Factor (DF) | Ratio (≤1) | MD / CL |
| Diversity Factor (DiF) | Ratio (≥1) | Sum of individual MDs / Combined MD |
| Load Factor (LF) | Ratio (≤1) | Average Load / Peak Load |
| Average Load | kW | Energy consumed over time / total hours |
| Coincidence Factor | Ratio (≤1) | Reciprocal of Diversity Factor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Voltage Levels (India):**
> - **LT (Low Tension):** 415 V (3-phase), 240 V (1-phase) — **50 Hz**
> - **HT (High Tension):** 11 kV, 22 kV, 33 kV
> - **EHT (Extra High Tension):** 66 kV, 110 kV, 220 kV, 400 kV

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Objectives / Why Load Survey is Performed

The load survey is the **first deliverable** in any electrical design project. It is carried out for the following engineering and economic reasons:

1. **Determination of Maximum Demand (MD):** To size the transformer, bus-bars, and main switchgear.
2. **Estimation of Connected Load (CL):** To know the total installed capacity.
3. **Selection of Diversity and Demand Factors:** These vary with consumer class (residential, commercial, industrial, agricultural).
4. **Load Forecasting:** For future expansion — typically 10–20 years for distribution planning.
5. **Tariff / Billing Analysis:** KSEB (Kerala State Electricity Board) uses load data for sanctioned load and HT/EHT tariff structures.
6. **Voltage Drop and Power Loss Calculation:** Input for cable sizing.
7. **Earthing and Short-Circuit Calculation:** Fault levels depend on connected impedance and load.
8. **Energy Conservation Auditing:** Identifying wasteful loads for replacement.

### 2.2 Classification of Loads

Loads are classified based on functional use and load behaviour.

**A. Based on Function:**

| Category | Examples | Typical Power Range |
|---|---|---|
| Lighting Load | LED, fluorescent, CFL, halogen | 5 W – 500 W per point |
| Power Load | Fans, computers, printers, mixers | 50 W – 2 kW |
| Heating Load | Geysers, ovens, induction cooktops | 1 kW – 10 kW |
| Air-Conditioning | Split AC, window AC, central HVAC | 1.5 kW – 50 kW |
| Industrial Motors | Pumps, compressors, lathes, cranes | 0.5 kW – 500 kW |
| Welding Load | Arc/spot welding transformers | 5 kVA – 50 kVA |
| Elevator / Lift | Traction motors | 5 kW – 50 kW per lift |
| UPS / DG Backup | Battery banks, diesel generators | 5 kVA – 2000 kVA |

**B. Based on Duration of Operation:**

- **Continuous Loads:** Operate for 3 hours or more without interruption (HVAC, lifts, server rooms).
- **Intermittent Loads:** Operate in cycles (welding machines, cranes, lifts).
- **Standby / Emergency Loads:** Fire pumps, emergency lighting, hospital OT.

**C. Based on Load Profile (KSEB Classification):**

- **Domestic (Tariff LT-I)**
- **Commercial (LT-II, LT-IV)**
- **Industrial (LT-V, HT-I, HT-II, HT-III)**
- **Agricultural (LT-VI)**
- **Public Lighting (LT-VII)**
- **Traction (HT-IV)**

### 2.3 Steps in the Load Survey Method (KTU Board Pattern)

The load survey is executed in a strict sequential order. Missing any step will lead to **erroneous design** and **loss of marks** in KTU valuation.

1. **Step 1 — Drawing Review:** Collect architectural floor plans, single-line diagrams (SLD), and service drawings of the proposed installation.
2. **Step 2 — Site Visit (Physical Inspection):** Walk through the site, photograph each load point, record nameplate data using a clip-board/digital form.
3. **Step 3 — Load Enumeration:** Prepare a *Load Schedule Sheet* (Excel/printed form) listing:
   - Load ID, Location, Type of Load, Quantity, Wattage per unit, Hours/day, Diversity.
4. **Step 4 — Demand Calculation:** Apply demand factor for each load category and sum.
5. **Step 5 — Diversity Application:** For a group of consumers (e.g., 50 houses), apply diversity factor.
6. **Step 6 — Maximum Demand Determination:** Identify peak load time (typically 6 PM – 10 PM for residential; 10 AM – 4 PM for industrial).
7. **Step 7 — Load Curve Construction:** Plot load (kW) vs time (hours) for a typical day.
8. **Step 8 — Sanction Load Application:** Apply for sanctioned load with the distribution licensee (KSEB).
9. **Step 9 — Verification & Validation:** Cross-check calculated MD against energy bills of similar existing installations.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is HIGH-YIELD for KTU University Exams.** Memorize these formulas and their units. (Note: Absolute-value bars and parallel symbols are written using `\vert` to protect table syntax.)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Connected Load (CL) | $CL = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{i}$ | kW | Sum of all nameplate ratings |
| 2 | Demand Factor (DF) | $DF = \dfrac{MD}{CL}$ | ratio (≤1) | Per consumer |
| 3 | Maximum Demand (MD) | $MD = CL \times DF$ | kW | Sizing parameter |
| 4 | Diversity Factor (DiF) | $DiF = \dfrac{\sum MD_{individual}}{MD_{group}}$ | ratio (≥1) | Group of consumers |
| 5 | Coincidence Factor (CF) | $CF = \dfrac{1}{DiF}$ | ratio (≤1) | Reciprocal of diversity |
| 6 | Average Load | $Avg\ Load = \dfrac{Energy\ (kWh)}{24 \times 365}$ | kW | Time-averaged |
| 7 | Load Factor (LF) | $LF = \dfrac{Average\ Load}{Peak\ Load}$ | ratio (≤1) | Energy efficiency |
| 8 | Plant Capacity Factor | $PCF = \dfrac{Energy\ Generated\ (kWh)}{MD \times 8760}$ | ratio | Power station use |
| 9 | Utilisation Factor (UF) | $UF = \dfrac{MD}{Equipment\ Rated\ Capacity}$ | ratio (≤1) | Equipment use |
| 10 | Group Demand | $MD_{group} = \dfrac{\sum MD_{individual}}{DiF}$ | kW | After diversity |

**Typical Demand Factors (as per IE Rule / BIS recommendations):**

| Load Type | Demand Factor (DF) |
|---|---|
| Residential lighting | 0.6 – 0.8 |
| Commercial lighting | 0.7 – 0.9 |
| Industrial lighting | 0.8 – 1.0 |
| Domestic power (mixer/fan) | 0.4 – 0.6 |
| Industrial motors (group) | 0.5 – 0.7 |
| Welding sets | 0.3 – 0.5 |
| Lift / elevator | 0.5 – 0.6 (per lift) |
| HVAC / AC plant | 0.7 – 0.9 |
| Water pump | 0.6 – 0.8 |
| Group of residential consumers (50–100 houses) | DiF = 1.5 – 2.5 |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of Load Survey

- **For Transformers:** Correct kVA rating avoids core saturation and overheating.
- **For Cables:** Cross-section of LT cable depends on the current at peak demand, not connected load.
- **For Diesel Generators (DG Sets):** DG sizing = MD × 1.25 (overload margin).
- **For Energy Meters:** Sanctioned load determines CT ratio and meter class.
- **For PF Correction Capacitors:** kVAr sizing = kW × (tan φ₁ − tan φ₂).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: From Connected Load to Maximum Demand

The load survey process derives the **Maximum Demand (MD)** from the **Connected Load (CL)** through the systematic application of the **Demand Factor** and **Diversity Factor**.

**Step 1 — Connected Load (CL):**
The total installed capacity is the arithmetic sum of all nameplate ratings of every appliance, fitting, and machine.

$$CL = P_1 + P_2 + P_3 + \cdots + P_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$$

**Explanation:** $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$ are the wattages (in W or kW) of individual loads. This is the *theoretical maximum* if every load were ON simultaneously at full rating — which rarely happens.

**Step 2 — Maximum Demand of a Single Consumer (MD₁):**
Not all loads operate simultaneously nor at full load. The **Demand Factor (DF)** accounts for this.

$$MD_1 = CL \times DF$$

**Explanation:** DF is always ≤ 1. For example, a residence may have CL = 10 kW but MD = 4 kW, giving DF = 0.4.

**Step 3 — Group Maximum Demand (MDg):**
When multiple consumers (e.g., 100 houses) are connected, their individual peaks occur at *different times*. The **Diversity Factor (DiF)** reduces the group's combined peak.

$$MD_g = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} MD_{i}}{DiF}$$

where $N$ is the number of consumers.

**Step 4 — Relationship between DF and DiF:**

$$\begin{aligned}
MD_{group} &= \frac{\sum (CL_i \times DF_i)}{DiF} \\
CL_{total} &= \sum CL_i
\end{aligned}$$

Hence, the **overall demand factor** for the group is:

$$DF_{overall} = \frac{MD_{group}}{CL_{total}} = \frac{DF_{average}}{DiF}$$

**Step 5 — Load Factor (LF) from Load Curve:**

$$LF = \frac{\text{Average Load over period } T}{\text{Peak Load during period } T} = \frac{E_T}{MD \times T}$$

where $E_T$ is the total energy consumed in time $T$.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A residential colony has 80 houses. Each house has the following loads:
- 4 × 40 W fluorescent lamps
- 4 × 60 W fans
- 1 × 100 W TV
- 1 × 750 W kitchen mixer (used 15 min/day)
- 1 × 2 kW geyser (used 30 min/day in morning)

Assume the diversity factor for the colony = 2.0 and demand factor for each house = 0.6. Calculate:
(a) Connected Load per house.
(b) Maximum Demand per house.
(c) Maximum Demand of the entire colony.
(d) Energy consumption per house per day.

**Solution:**

**(a) Connected Load per house (CL_house):**

$$CL_{house} = (4 \times 40) + (4 \times 60) + (1 \times 100) + 750 + 2000$$

$$\begin{aligned}
CL_{house} &= 160 + 240 + 100 + 750 + 2000 \\
&= 3250\ W = 3.25\ kW
\end{aligned}$$

**(b) Maximum Demand per house (MD_house):**

$$MD_{house} = CL_{house} \times DF = 3.25 \times 0.6 = 1.95\ kW$$

**(c) Maximum Demand of Colony (MD_colony):**

Sum of individual MDs:

$$\sum MD_{i} = 80 \times 1.95 = 156\ kW$$

Applying diversity factor:

$$MD_{colony} = \frac{\sum MD_i}{DiF} = \frac{156}{2.0} = 78\ kW$$

**(d) Energy Consumption per House per Day (E):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{lamps} &= 4 \times 40 \times 5\ h = 800\ Wh \\
E_{fans} &= 4 \times 60 \times 10\ h = 2400\ Wh \\
E_{TV} &= 100 \times 6\ h = 600\ Wh \\
E_{mixer} &= 750 \times 0.25\ h = 187.5\ Wh \\
E_{geyser} &= 2000 \times 0.5\ h = 1000\ Wh
\end{aligned}$$

Total energy per day:

$$E_{total} = 800 + 2400 + 600 + 187.5 + 1000 = 4987.5\ Wh \approx 5\ kWh$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Method of Load Survey (Comparative Table)

| Aspect | Domestic (LT-I) | Commercial (LT-II) | Industrial (HT-I) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Connected Load (typical) | 2 – 10 kW/house | 50 – 500 kW | 500 kW – 20 MW |
| Demand Factor (DF) | 0.4 – 0.7 | 0.6 – 0.9 | 0.5 – 0.8 |
| Diversity Factor (DiF) | 1.5 – 2.5 | 1.2 – 1.8 | 1.05 – 1.3 |
| Peak Demand Time | 6 PM – 10 PM | 11 AM – 7 PM | 9 AM – 5 PM |
| Load Factor (LF) | 0.20 – 0.40 | 0.35 – 0.55 | 0.55 – 0.80 |
| Power Factor (avg) | 0.85 lag | 0.85 lag | 0.80 lag |
| Survey Method | Questionnaire + visit | Meter reading + audit | Continuous monitoring |
| Sanctioning Authority | KSEB Section Office | KSEB Division | KSEB Chief Engineer |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Load Survey Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Load Survey] --> B[Step 1: Review Drawings and Architectural Plans]
    B --> C[Step 2: Physical Site Inspection and Data Collection]
    C --> D[Step 3: Load Enumeration in Schedule Sheet]
    D --> E[Step 4: Classification of Loads]
    E --> F[Step 5: Apply Demand Factor to each Group]
    F --> G[Step 6: Apply Diversity Factor for Group]
    G --> H[Step 7: Compute Maximum Demand]
    H --> I[Step 8: Construct Load Curve]
    I --> J[Step 9: Apply for Sanctioned Load]
    J --> K[Step 10: Final Design and Estimation]
    K --> L[End]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Load Survey Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Electrical Load Survey] --> B[Connected Load]
    A --> C[Maximum Demand]
    A --> D[Diversity Factor]
    A --> E[Load Factor]
    A --> F[Demand Factor]
    B --> B1[Lighting Load]
    B --> B2[Power Load]
    B --> B3[Heating Load]
    B --> B4[AC and HVAC]
    B --> B5[Motors and Machines]
    C --> C1[Instantaneous Peak kW]
    C --> C2[15 minute Average kW]
    C --> C3[Hourly Average kW]
    F --> F1[Lighting DF 0.7 to 0.9]
    F --> F2[Motor DF 0.5 to 0.7]
    F --> F3[Welding DF 0.3 to 0.5]
    D --> D1[Group of Houses DiF 2.0]
    D --> D2[Group of Motors DiF 1.3]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block: Load Curve Construction (Typical Residential Day)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Typical Residential Daily Load Curve"
    x-axis [00, 02, 04, 06, 08, 10, 12, 14, 16, 18, 20, 22, 24]
    y-axis "Load in kW" 0 --> 10
    line [0.5, 0.4, 0.3, 1.0, 3.5, 4.0, 2.5, 2.0, 1.8, 6.0, 8.5, 5.0, 1.0]
```

**Interpretation of the Curve:**
- **00:00 – 05:00:** Refrigerator + night lamp (≈ 0.3 – 1.0 kW).
- **06:00 – 09:00:** Morning peak — geyser, lights, fans (≈ 3.5 – 4.0 kW).
- **10:00 – 17:00:** Mid-day low (most members at office).
- **18:00 – 22:00:** **Evening peak** — lights, fans, TV, AC, mixer (≈ 6.0 – 8.5 kW).
- **22:00 – 24:00:** Wind-down (≈ 1.0 kW).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **peak of the load curve** (here at 20:00) is the **Maximum Demand (MD)** used for transformer sizing.

### 4.4 Mermaid Block: Input-Output Flow of Load Survey

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN1[Building Floor Plan] --> PROC[Load Survey Engine]
    IN2[Appliance Inventory] --> PROC
    IN3[Usage Pattern Data] --> PROC
    IN4[Existing Energy Bills] --> PROC
    PROC --> OUT1[Load Schedule Sheet]
    PROC --> OUT2[Maximum Demand in kW]
    PROC --> OUT3[Load Curve Plot]
    PROC --> OUT4[Demand Factor and Diversity Factor]
    PROC --> OUT5[Sanction Load Application to KSEB]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]
Define the term **Demand Factor**. State its typical value range for a domestic consumer as per the IE Rules.

**Model Answer:**
Demand Factor (DF) is defined as the ratio of **Maximum Demand (MD)** of a consumer (or installation) to its **Connected Load (CL)**.

$$DF = \frac{MD}{CL}$$

The value of DF is always ≤ 1. For a **domestic consumer**, the typical value of demand factor is **0.4 to 0.7**, depending on the number and type of appliances, family size, and lifestyle.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]
Distinguish between **Connected Load** and **Maximum Demand** in the context of an electrical installation load survey.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Connected Load (CL) | Maximum Demand (MD) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Sum of nameplate ratings of all installed loads | Highest load actually drawn at any instant |
| Value | Always higher | Always ≤ CL |
| Determined by | Inspection of nameplates | Metering or load curve analysis |
| Use | Sanction application, wiring size | Transformer, DG, switchgear sizing |
| Units | kW / kVA | kW / kVA |

---

### Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**Q3 (a) — 7 Marks:** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]
A small industry has the following loads:
- 10 induction motors of 5 kW each
- 5 welding transformers of 8 kVA each (0.5 PF)
- 100 fluorescent lamps of 40 W each
- 10 fans of 75 W each
- 2 × 10 kW air-conditioning units

Assume demand factors: motors = 0.7, welding = 0.4, lighting = 0.8, fans = 0.6, AC = 0.9. Calculate the **Connected Load (kVA)** and **Maximum Demand (kVA)** of the industry assuming an overall power factor of 0.85 lag.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Connected Load Calculation:**

Motors: $10 \times 5 = 50\ kW$

Welding: $5 \times 8 = 40\ kVA$ (apparent power)

Lighting: $100 \times 40 = 4000\ W = 4\ kW$

Fans: $10 \times 75 = 750\ W = 0.75\ kW$

AC: $2 \times 10 = 20\ kW$

Total real CL: $50 + 4 + 0.75 + 20 = 74.75\ kW$

Total apparent CL (incl. welding at 0.5 PF → 8 × 0.5 = 4 kW):

$$CL_{apparent} = 74.75 + 4 = 78.75\ kW \approx 78.8\ kW$$

Converting to kVA: At PF 0.85:

$$CL_{kVA} = \frac{78.75}{0.85} \approx 92.6\ kVA$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Demand Calculation:**

Apply demand factors to each category:

$$\begin{aligned}
MD_{motors} &= 50 \times 0.7 = 35\ kW \\
MD_{welding} &= 4 \times 0.4 = 1.6\ kW \\
MD_{lighting} &= 4 \times 0.8 = 3.2\ kW \\
MD_{fans} &= 0.75 \times 0.6 = 0.45\ kW \\
MD_{AC} &= 20 \times 0.9 = 18\ kW
\end{aligned}$$

Total MD (real): $35 + 1.6 + 3.2 + 0.45 + 18 = 58.25\ kW$

Converting to kVA at 0.85 PF:

$$MD_{kVA} = \frac{58.25}{0.85} \approx 68.5\ kVA$$

**Answer:** $CL = 92.6\ kVA$, $MD = 68.5\ kVA$.

---

**Q3 (b) — 7 Marks:** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]
Explain the procedure of conducting a **load survey** for a residential colony of 200 houses. List the documents to be collected and the data to be recorded.

**Model Solution:**

**Procedure:**

1. **Stage 1 — Documentation Collection:**
   - Approved site plan from the local panchayat/municipality.
   - Floor plans of each house type.
   - Existing KSEB sanctioned load record.
   - Past 12 months' electricity bills (if existing).

2. **Stage 2 — Site Survey Form Design:**
   - House number, owner name, contact.
   - Type of house (1BHK, 2BHK, 3BHK, villa).
