### Real-World Utility

The DB and its protection devices form the **backbone of modern electrical safety** in:
- **Domestic installations:** House wiring (per IS 732) requires a DB with ELCB/RCCB at incomer and MCBs for each circuit.
- **Commercial buildings:** Floor-wise DBs with MCCBs for higher loads.
- **Industrial installations:** PCC (Power Control Centre), MCC (Motor Control Centre) using ACBs, MCCBs, and vacuum contactors.
- **Solar PV systems:** DC-rated MCBs, DC fuses, and SPDs for surge protection.
- **Hospitals & Data Centers:** IT-grade earthing, 30 mA RCDs, and isolated power systems (IPS).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: MCB Selection for a Domestic Load

**Problem Statement (KTU Typical):**
A residential house has the following loads:
- 8 × LED lamps of 9 W each
- 2 × Ceiling fans of 75 W each
- 1 × 1.5 kW geyser
- 2 × 15 A power sockets (general purpose)
- 1 × 2 TR air-conditioner (1.8 kW)

Design a Distribution Board and select appropriate MCBs for each circuit.

### Step 1: Calculate the Full Load Current of Each Circuit

**Circuit 1: Lighting Circuit (LED Lamps + Fans)**

$$P_1 = (8 \times 9) + (2 \times 75) = 72 + 150 = 222 \text{ W}$$

For single-phase, 230 V supply with $\cos\phi \approx 1.0$ for resistive loads:

$$I_1 = \frac{P_1}{V \cdot \cos\phi} = \frac{222}{230 \times 1.0} = 0.965 \text{ A}$$

**Circuit 2: Geyser Circuit**

$$P_2 = 1500 \text{ W}$$

$$I_2 = \frac{P_2}{V \cdot \cos\phi} = \frac{1500}{230 \times 1.0} = 6.52 \text{ A}$$

**Circuit 3: Power Socket Circuit (15A socket, but typically used at 70% capacity)**

$$P_3 = 2 \times 15 \text{ A} \times 230 \text{ V} \times 0.7 = 4830 \text{ W}$$

**Circuit 4: Air-Conditioner Circuit**

$$P_4 = 1800 \text{ W}$$

$$I_4 = \frac{P_4}{V \cdot \cos\phi} = \frac{1800}{230 \times 1.0} = 7.83 \text{ A}$$

### Step 2: Apply MCB Selection Rule

The KTU/IS 732 rule is: $I_{MCB} \geq 1.25 \times I_{load}$

**Circuit 1:** $I_{MCB,1} \geq 1.25 \times 0.965 = 1.21 \text{ A} \rightarrow$ Select **6 A MCB (Type B)**

**Circuit 2:** $I_{MCB,2} \geq 1.25 \times 6.52 = 8.15 \text{ A} \rightarrow$ Select **16 A MCB (Type B)** (because geysers are pure resistive)

**Circuit 3:** $I_{MCB,3} \geq 1.25 \times 10.5 = 13.13 \text{ A} \rightarrow$ Select **16 A MCB (Type C)** (because sockets may feed motors)

**Circuit 4:** $I_{MCB,4} \geq 1.25 \times 7.83 = 9.79 \text{ A} \rightarrow$ Select **16 A MCB (Type C)** (because AC has a starting compressor inrush)

### Step 3: Calculate Incomer Current

Sum of all individual circuit currents (with diversity applied):

$$I_{total} = I_1 + I_2 + I_3 + I_4 = 0.965 + 6.52 + 10.5 + 7.83 = 25.8 \text{ A}$$

Apply **80% loading rule**:

$$I_{incomer} \geq \frac{25.8}{0.8} = 32.3 \text{ A} \rightarrow \text{Select } \mathbf{40 \text{ A DP isolator + 30 mA RCCB}}$$

### Step 4: Final DB Schedule

| Way No. | Circuit Description | MCB Rating | MCB Type | Cable Size |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lighting + Fans | 6 A | B (SP) | 1.5 mm² Cu |
| 2 | Geyser | 16 A | B (SP) | 2.5 mm² Cu |
| 3 | Power Sockets (15A) | 16 A | C (SP) | 2.5 mm² Cu |
| 4 | Air Conditioner | 16 A | C (SP) | 2.5 mm² Cu |
| 5 | Spare | 16 A | C (SP) | 2.5 mm² Cu |
| 6 | Spare | 16 A | C (SP) | 2.5 mm² Cu |
| Incomer | 40 A DP Isolator + 30 mA RCCB | 40 A | — | 6 mm² Cu |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Number of Ways = 8-way SPN (Single Pole & Neutral) DB**, with 4 used and 2 reserved for future expansion. This is the standard **"2 spare" rule** in KTU design problems.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Selection Logic for Protection Devices

| Criterion | FUSE | MCB | MCCB | RCCB | ACB |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Current Range | 0.5 A – 1250 A | 0.5 A – 125 A | 100 A – 1600 A | 16 A – 100 A | 630 A – 6300 A |
| Breaking Capacity | 80 – 120 kA | 3 – 25 kA | 25 – 100 kA | 4.5 – 10 kA | 40 – 150 kA |
| Resetability | No (replace) | Yes (manual) | Yes (manual/auto) | Yes (manual) | Yes (manual/auto) |
| Trip Characteristic | Inverse time | Thermal + Magnetic | Adjustable Thermal/Magnetic | Earth leakage | Electronic Trip Unit |
| Overload Protection | Yes | Yes | Yes | **No** | Yes |
| Short-circuit Protection | Yes | Yes | Yes | **No** | Yes |
| Earth-fault Protection | Partial | Indirect | Indirect | **Yes (Primary)** | Indirect |
| Cost | Lowest | Low | Medium | Medium | Highest |
| Typical Use | Sub-circuits, backup | Domestic final circuits | Sub-DB incomers | Personal safety | HT-side, MDB incomers |

## 3.3 Derivation: Discrimination of RCCB Trip Current

**Problem:** A substation has an earth electrode resistance of $R_{earth} = 4 \Omega$. The phase-to-neutral voltage is $230 \text{ V}$. Determine the maximum permissible $I_{\Delta n}$ for the RCCB such that touch voltage remains below 50 V (safe limit per IS 3043).

**Step 1:** The maximum safe touch voltage is $U_{touch,max} = 50 \text{ V}$.

**Step 2:** The earth-fault current (which is the residual current detected by RCCB) is:

$$I_{earth} = \frac{U_{touch}}{R_{earth}} = \frac{50}{4} = 12.5 \text{ A} = 12500 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the KTU rule: $I_{\Delta n} \leq \dfrac{50}{R_{earth}}$ (in mA, with $R_{earth}$ in Ω):

$$I_{\Delta n} \leq \frac{50}{4} = 12.5 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 4:** Since standard RCCB ratings are 10, 30, 100, 300 mA, we select:

$$\boxed{I_{\Delta n} = 10 \text{ mA} \text{ RCCB}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the reason **30 mA RCCBs** are most common — they offer good personal protection and are tolerant of small leakage currents, avoiding nuisance tripping.

## 3.4 Derivation: Coordination of Cable, MCB, and Load (KEL-D Coordination)

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes this **three-tier coordination**:

**Rule 1:** $I_b \leq I_n \leq I_z$

where:
- $I_b$ = Design current of the circuit (actual load current)
- $I_n$ = Nominal current rating of the protective device (MCB)
- $I_z$ = Current-carrying capacity of the cable

**Rule 2:** $I_2 \leq 1.45 \times I_z$

where $I_2$ is the conventional non-fusing current of the protective device (typically $1.45 \times I_n$ for MCBs).

**Rule 3 (Discrimination):** The MCB must trip faster than the upstream device for the same fault current (i.e., upstream MCB rating $\geq 1.6 \times$ downstream MCB rating for total discrimination).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Distribution Boards

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Working Principle of MCB

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Current Flow] --> B{Is it overload or short circuit}
    B -->|Overload up to 10x| C[Thermal Trip Unit Bimetal Strip]
    B -->|Short circuit above 10x| D[Magnetic Trip Unit Solenoid]
    C --> E[Heat causes bimetal strip to bend]
    D --> F[High current creates magnetic force]
    E --> G[Trip Latch Released]
    F --> G[Trip Latch Released]
    G --> H[Contacts Open]
    H --> I[Circuit Isolated]
    I --> J[Manual Reset Required]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Working Principle of RCCB (Earth Fault Detection)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Line Conductor IL] --> B[Core CT]
    C[Neutral Conductor IN] --> B
    B --> D{Sum IL minus IN Zero}
    D -->|Yes healthy circuit| E[No trip]
    D -->|No leakage detected| F[Tripping Relay Activated]
    F --> G[Contacts Open in less than 30 ms]
    G --> H[Supply Disconnected]
    I[Earth Path Leakage Current] -.-> D
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A: 3-Mark Questions**

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a Distribution Board. List any four components of a DB.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Distribution Board (DB)** is an enclosure containing protective devices and busbars that distributes electrical power from the main supply to various sub-circuits, each protected by an individual MCB/MCCB.

**Four Components of a DB:**
1. **Incomer** (Isolator / MCCB) — main switching device
2. **Busbars** (3-phase + Neutral copper strips)
3. **Neutral Bar** — common neutral termination
4. **Earth Bar** — common earth termination
5. **DIN Rail** — mounting rail for MCBs
6. **Enclosure** — sheet steel or insulated box (IP-rated)

*(Any four to be credited)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between MCB and MCCB.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | MCB | MCCB |
|---|---|---|
| Current Rating | 0.5 A – 125 A | 100 A – 1600 A |
| Trip Unit | Fixed (thermal + magnetic) | Adjustable electronic trip unit |
| Breaking Capacity | 3 – 25 kA | 25 – 100 kA |
| Application | Domestic, sub-circuits | Industrial, MDB incomers |
| Mounting | DIN rail | Bolted / DIN rail (larger) |
| Cost | Low | Higher |

---

## **PART B: 14-Mark Questions**

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a Miniature Circuit Breaker (MCB) with neat diagrams. Discuss the different tripping characteristics (B, C, D curves). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An industrial load of 25 kW, 3-phase, 415 V, $\cos\phi = 0.85$ is supplied through a sub-DB. Design the DB incomer, outgoing MCCB, and cable size. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question A (a)** — 7 marks

**Construction of MCB:**

1. **Outer Casing** — Made of moulded insulating material (phenolic resin / thermoset plastic). Houses all internal parts.
2. **Contacts** — Fixed and moving contacts made of silver alloy for low contact resistance.
3. **Arc Chute** — A series of metal plates (arc splitters) that divide the arc into smaller segments for quick extinction.
4. **Bimetal Strip (Thermal Element)** — Two metals with different coefficients of thermal expansion, riveted together. On sustained overload, it bends and releases the trip latch.
5. **Solenoid / Electromagnetic Coil (Magnetic Element)** — Provides instantaneous trip on short circuit.
6. **Operating Handle** — External lever for ON/OFF and reset.
7. **Trip Latch Mechanism** — A spring-loaded mechanism that opens the contacts on receiving a signal from the thermal or magnetic element.

**Working of MCB:**

- **On Overload (1.13 to 2 × In):** The current heats the bimetal strip. As it bends, it releases the trip latch, opening the contacts. This is a **slow trip** (inverse-time characteristic).
- **On Short Circuit (above 5 to 10 × In):** The high current energizes the solenoid, which creates a strong magnetic field that pulls the armature, opening the contacts **instantaneously** (within 5 to 20 ms).
- The **arc** formed during contact opening is pushed by the magnetic field into the **arc chute**, where it is split, cooled, and extinguished.

**Tripping Characteristics:**

| Curve | Trip Range (× In) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **B Curve** | 3 to 5 × In | Purely resistive loads: lighting, heating |
| **C Curve** | 5 to 10 × In | Small inductive loads: fans, motors < 5 HP |
| **D Curve** | 10 to 20 × In | Highly inductive loads: welding sets, large motors, transformers |

---

### **Solution to Question A (b)** — 7 marks

**Given:**
- $P = 25 \text{ kW}$
- 3-phase, $V_L = 415 \text{ V}$
- $\cos\phi = 0.85$
- $\eta = 0.90$ (assumed motor efficiency)

**Step 1: Calculate Full Load Current**

$$I_{FLC} = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \eta} = \frac{25000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.85 \times 0.90}$$

$$I_{FLC} = \frac{25000}{549.42} = 45.5 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Select MCCB**

Apply 1.25 factor:

$$I_{MCCB} \geq 1.25 \times 45.5 = 56.9 \text{ A}$$

**Select 63 A, 3-Pole MCCB**, with adjustable thermal trip (50–63 A) and magnetic trip (5–10 × In).

**Step 3: Select Cable**

For 63 A MCCB, the cable current-carrying capacity (from IS 3961) should be ≥ 63 A. From standard 3-core copper PVC cable table (clipped direct, single circuit):
- 10 mm² Cu cable → 56 A
- **16 mm² Cu cable → 73 A** ✓ (suitable)

Alternatively, use **25 mm² Al cable → 84 A**.

**Step 4: Verify Short-Circuit Withstand**

Assuming $I_{sc} = 25 \text{ kA}$ at the DB, the MCCB breaking capacity $I_{cu} \geq 25 \text{ kA}$.

**Select: 63 A, 3P MCCB, 25 kA breaking capacity, with 30 mA RCCB at incomer for personal safety.**

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the working principle of an RCCB. Why is an RCCB always used in series with an MCB? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A commercial building has the following loads: 50 fluorescent lamps (40 W each), 20 ceiling fans (80 W each), 5 × 1.5 TR AC units (2.5 kW each), 2 × 5 kW lifts. Design a suitable Final Distribution Board with appropriate MCB ratings. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question B (a)** — 7 marks

**Working Principle of RCCB:**

A Residual Current Circuit Breaker (RCCB) operates on the principle of **Kirchhoff's Current Law** and the **Zero-Sequence Current Transformer (ZSCT)**.

**Construction:**
- A **toroidal (ring-shaped) core** of high-permeability magnetic material.
- The **phase (line)** and **neutral** conductors are wound through the core as a primary.
- A **secondary winding** is wound on the core, connected to a highly sensitive trip relay.

**Working in Healthy Condition:**

In normal operation, the current in the line conductor $I_L$ equals the current in the neutral conductor $I_N$, i.e.,

$$I_L - I_N = 0$$

The two currents produce **equal and opposite magnetic fluxes** in the toroidal core. These fluxes **cancel each other**, and the net flux in the core is zero. As a result, **no EMF is induced** in the secondary winding, and the trip relay remains inactive.

**Working during Earth Fault:**

When a person touches a live conductor or insulation fails, a small portion of the line current returns to earth through the human body or the fault path, **bypassing the neutral**. This is the **residual current** $I_{\Delta}$.

$$I_L - I_N = I_{\Delta} \neq 0$$

The imbalance creates a **net alternating flux** in the toroidal core, inducing an **EMF in the secondary winding**. This EMF energizes the **trip relay**, which opens the contacts within **30 milliseconds** (for 30 mA RCCB), isolating the supply.

**Why RCCB is used in series with MCB:**

- An RCCB **does NOT provide protection against overload or short circuit**. It only detects earth leakage.
- An MCB **does NOT provide earth-fault protection** for human safety.
- Therefore, the **combination of MCB + RCCB (or RCBO)** provides:
  - **MCB:** Overcurrent and short-circuit protection.
  - **RCCB:** Earth-leakage / personal protection.

---

### **Solution to Question B (b)** — 7 marks

**Given Loads:**

| Load | Quantity | Power per unit | Total Power |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fluorescent lamps (40 W) | 50 | 40 W | 2000 W |
| Ceiling fans (80 W) | 20 | 80 W | 1600 W |
| AC units (1.5 TR) | 5 | 2500 W | 12500 W |
| Lifts | 2 | 5000 W | 10000 W |
| **Total Connected Load** | | | **26,100 W ≈ 26.1 kW** |

**Step 1: Group into Sub-Circuits and Calculate Current**

**Circuit 1: Lighting Circuit (10 lamps per circuit)**

$$P_1 = 10 \times 40 = 400 \text{ W}, \quad I_1 = \frac{400}{230} = 1.74 \text{ A} \rightarrow \text{6A SP MCB (B-curve)}$$

**Circuit 2: Fan Circuit (5 fans per circuit)**

$$P_2 = 5 \times 80 = 400 \text{ W}, \quad I_2 = \frac{400}{230} = 1.74 \text{ A} \rightarrow \text{6A SP MCB (B-curve)}$$

**Circuit 3: AC Circuit (1 AC per circuit)**

$$P_3 = 2500 \text{ W}, \quad I_3 = \frac{2500}{230} = 10.87 \text{ A} \rightarrow \text{16A SP MCB (C-curve)}$$

**Circuit 4: Lift Circuit (3-phase)**

$$I_{lift} = \frac{5000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.8} = 8.7 \text{ A per phase} \rightarrow \text{16A TP MCB (C-curve)}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Incomer Current (with diversity)**

Apply diversity factor:
- Lighting: 80% → 4000 × 0.8 = 3200 W
- Fans: 70% → 1600 × 0.7 = 1120 W
- AC: 75% → 12500 × 0.75 = 9375 W
- Lifts: 100% → 10000 W (diversified 60% during simultaneous operation) → 6000 W

$$P_{demand} = 3200 + 1120 + 9375 + 6000 = 19695 \text{ W} \approx 19.7 \text{ kW}$$

For 3-phase, 415 V, $\cos\phi = 0.85$:

$$I_{incomer} = \frac{19700}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.85} = 32.2 \text{ A}$$

Apply 1.25 factor: $32.2 \times 1.25 = 40.3 \text{ A}$

**Select: 63 A, 3-Pole + Neutral MCCB (with 30 mA RCCB) as incomer.**

**Step 3: Final DB Design**

| Way | Description | MCB Type | Rating | Cable |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1–5 | 5 × Lighting | SP MCB (B) | 6 A | 1.5 mm² Cu |
| 6–9 | 4 × Fan | SP MCB (B) | 6 A | 1.5 mm² Cu |
| 10–14 | 5 × AC | SP MCB (C) | 16 A | 2.5 mm² Cu |
| 15 | Lift 1 | TP MCB (C) | 16 A | 4 mm² Cu |
| 16 | Lift 2 | TP MCB (C) | 16 A | 4 mm² Cu |
| 17–18 | Spare | SP MCB (C) | 16 A | 2.5 mm² Cu |
| **Incomer** | 63A MCCB + 30 mA RCCB | — | 63 A | 16 mm² Cu |

**Final DB: 18-way TPN (Triple Pole and Neutral) with 63 A MCCB + RCCB incomer**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing ELCB with RCCB:** The voltage-operated ELCB is **obsolete**; modern ELCB = RCCB. Always clarify.
> 2. **Forgetting the 1.25 multiplier:** KTU expects MCB ≥ 1.25 × load current. Skipping this is a **direct 2-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Using wrong MCB type:** Type B for AC is a mistake; AC compressors have inrush currents, so **Type C is mandatory**.
> 4. **Not providing spares:** KTU expects a minimum of **2 spare ways** in any DB design.
> 5. **Missing IP rating:** Always specify **IP 43 (indoor)** or **IP 55 (outdoor)** — this is a recurring 2-mark question.
> 6. **Not showing the working formula:** Even if the answer is correct, **without showing the FLC calculation**, marks are deducted.
> 7. **Mixing AC and DC ratings:** MCBs have **different ratings for AC and DC**; using a 230 V AC MCB on a 400 V DC solar string is a **dangerous design error**.

---

# 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔹 Quick-Reference Checklist

- [ ] **DB = Enclosure + Incomer + Busbars + Outgoing MCBs + Neutral Bar + Earth Bar**
- [ ] **MCB selection:** $I_{MCB} \geq 1.25 \times I_{load}$
- [ ] **Fuse selection (motor):** $I_{fuse} = 1.5 \times I_{FLC}$
- [ ] **Cable-MCB-Load coordination:** $I_b \leq I_n \leq I_z$
- [ ] **Type B MCB** → Lighting & heating; **Type C** → Motors & AC; **Type D** → Industrial/welding
- [ ] **RCCB 30 mA** for personal safety; **300 mA** for fire protection; **500 mA** for industrial
- [ ] **RCCB does NOT protect against overload** — always use in series with MCB
- [ ] **RCCB trip time** ≤ 30 ms at rated $I_{\Delta n}$
- [ ] **Standard DB IP rating:** IP 43 (indoor), IP 55 (outdoor)
- [ ] **80% Loading Rule** on busbars
- [ ] **Minimum 2 spare ways** in every DB
- [ ] **MCB breaking capacity** ($I_{cu}$) must exceed prospective short-circuit current ($I_{sc}$)
- [ ] **Earth-fault current formula:** $I_{earth} = U_{touch}/R_{earth}$
- [ ] **MCB operating time:** Thermal = seconds, Magnetic = milliseconds
- [ ] **ACB** for MDB incomers; **MCCB** for sub-DB; **MCB** for final DB; **HRC fuse** for backup

### 🔹 Key Standards to Memorize

| Standard | Topic |
|---|---|
| **IS 732** | Wiring installations |
| **IS 8623** | DB assemblies |
| **IS 8828 / IEC 60898** | MCBs (domestic) |
| **IS 13947 / IEC 60947** | MCCBs (industrial) |
| **IS 12640 / IEC 61008** | RCCBs |
| **IS 3043** | Earthing |
| **IS 3961** | Cable current ratings |

### 🔹 Common KTU Trick Questions

1. "An RCCB is used for overcurrent protection." → **FALSE** (RCCB is for earth leakage only)
2. "Type D MCB is used for domestic lighting." → **FALSE** (Type B is used for lighting)
3. "Busbar current rating should equal the total load current." → **FALSE** (apply 80% loading rule)
4. "Earthing is optional in domestic installations." → **FALSE** (mandatory as per IS 3043)
5. "MCB and MCCB are interchangeable." → **FALSE** (different current ranges and applications)

# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electrical Wiring Design** is the systematic, engineered process of planning, calculating, and specifying the physical arrangement of electrical conductors, protective devices, switching gear, and accessories that safely distribute electrical power from the supply authority's service head to the various load points (light points, fan points, socket outlets, and power outlets) within a premises, in strict compliance with the **Indian Electricity Rules (IER 1956)**, the **National Electrical Code (NEC)**, and the relevant **Bureau of Indian Standards (IS 732, IS 4648, IS 9537, IS 732)**.

The design process converts the architectural layout of a building into a safe, efficient, and code-compliant electrical distribution network, simultaneously producing a **Schedule of Materials (Estimation)** and a **Layout Drawing (Wiring Diagram)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Module 2 of PCEET602 explicitly requires students to understand the *sequential logic* of wiring design — from determining the sanctioned load to issuing the final estimate. The design steps form the **backbone** of all estimation problems in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **plumbing engineer** designing the water supply system for a large apartment. Before laying a single pipe, you must first:

1. **Count every tap** (lights, fans, sockets) — the *connected load*.
2. **Decide which taps can run simultaneously** (kitchen tap + bathroom tap, but not all taps at once) — the *diversity factor* and *maximum demand*.
3. **Choose the pipe material** (PVC, GI, copper) — the *type of wiring*.
4. **Pick the right pipe diameter** so that the kitchen tap doesn't get a trickle when the upstairs shower is on — the *conductor size for current and voltage drop*.
5. **Install a safety valve** that bursts if pressure goes too high — the *MCB/ELCB*.
6. **Ground the metal pipes** so static doesn't shock anyone — the *earthing system*.

Electrical wiring design is **literally the electrical twin of plumbing design**, except the "fluid" is electron flow, and the "pressure" is voltage.

## 1.3 Why These Steps Exist — The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Safety:** Incorrectly sized wires overheat → **fire hazard**. Proper design prevents this.
- **Functionality:** Undersized voltage drop → dim lights and sluggish motors.
- **Economy:** Oversized wires waste copper/money; correct sizing optimizes cost.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Inspector approves only code-compliant designs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Reference (Mandatory in KTU Answers):**
> - **IS 732 (1989)** — Code of Practice for Electrical Wiring Installations
> - **IS 4648 (1968)** — Guide for Electrical Wiring in Residential Buildings
> - **IS 9537** — Conduit specifications
> - **IS 3034** — Fire safety in wiring
> - **IE Rules 1956, Amendments 2010** — Statutory requirement

## 1.4 The Ten Sequential Design Steps — Master List

The complete design sequence (the "**KTU Golden Chain**") is:

1. **Step 1:** Ascertain the sanctioned load from the supply authority.
2. **Step 2:** Prepare the building layout plan with load positions.
3. **Step 3:** Calculate the *connected load* (sum of all individual loads).
4. **Step 4:** Apply the *diversity factor* to obtain the *maximum demand*.
5. **Step 5:** Determine the *full load current* of the installation.
6. **Step 6:** Decide the *number of sub-circuits* and their grouping.
7. **Step 7:** Select the *type of wiring system* (conduit, casing-cap, etc.).
8. **Step 8:** Calculate the *conductor (cable) size* based on current carrying capacity and *voltage drop*.
9. **Step 9:** Choose the *rating and type of protective device* (MCB, MCCB, ELCB/RCCB).
10. **Step 10:** Design the *earthing system*, prepare the *layout drawing*, and compile the *estimate/Schedule of Materials*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always present the solution in *this exact 10-step order* in your ESE answer script. Examiners look for this logical flow and award partial marks (often 1–2 marks) for sequence discipline even if numerical answers are slightly off.

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Explanation of Each Design Step

### **Step 1 — Ascertain the Sanctioned Load**

The sanctioned load is the *maximum connected load* approved by the electricity distribution company (e.g., KSEB in Kerala). It determines the size of the service connection and the energy meter. Sanctioned loads in Kerala typically range from **1 kW** (BPL domestic) to **150 kW** (commercial/industrial).

### **Step 2 — Prepare the Building Layout Plan**

A scaled architectural drawing is used to mark:
- Light points (L)
- Fan points (F)
- Wall socket outlets (5A/15A)
- Power sockets for AC, geyser, washing machine
- Distribution board (DB) location
- Main switch position

### **Step 3 — Calculate the Connected Load**

Each point load is assigned its rated wattage:

$$
P_{connected} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i
$$

**Standard Assumed Loads (as per IS 4648 / KSEB practice):**

| Point Type | Standard Assumed Load |
|---|---|
| Light point (incandescent) | 60 W |
| Light point (CFL/LED) | 9 W – 15 W |
| Ceiling fan | 60 W – 75 W |
| Exhaust fan | 60 W |
| 5A socket outlet | 100 W |
| 15A socket outlet | 1000 W |
| Power socket (AC/Geyser) | 1500 W – 2000 W |
| Bell point | 15 W |

### **Step 4 — Apply Diversity Factor**

$$
P_{maximum\ demand} = P_{connected} \times D_f
$$

Where $D_f$ is the **diversity factor** (always $\leq 1$).

**Standard Diversity Factors (KTU Table):**

| Installation Type | Diversity Factor |
|---|---|
| Residential (Domestic) | 0.6 – 0.7 |
| Commercial (Offices) | 0.7 – 0.8 |
| Industrial | 0.5 – 0.6 |
| Single Dwelling Unit | 0.7 – 0.8 |

### **Step 5 — Determine Full Load Current**

For **single-phase** (230 V) systems:

$$
I_{FL} = \frac{P_{md}}{V \times \cos\phi}
$$

For **three-phase** (415 V) systems:

$$
I_{FL} = \frac{P_{md}}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L \times \cos\phi}
$$

For purely **resistive loads** (lights, heaters), $\cos\phi = 1$. For mixed loads, typical $\cos\phi = 0.8$.

### **Step 6 — Sub-Circuit Division**

As per IS 732, a sub-circuit should **not exceed 10 points** and **not exceed 800 W** for lighting and **not exceed 3000 W** for power. Each sub-circuit is independently protected by an MCB.

### **Step 7 — Selection of Wiring System**

Common wiring systems used in Kerala:
- **PVC insulated copper conductor in PVC conduit (recessed/concealed)** — most common for residential.
- **PVC insulated copper conductor in surface conduit** — for industrial.
- **Casing and capping (wood/PVC)** — older installations.
- **Batten wiring (cleat wiring)** — temporary installations.
- **Lead sheathed wiring** — hazardous locations (obsolete).
- **Mineral Insulated Metal Sheathed (MICC)** — fire-prone areas.

### **Step 8 — Conductor Sizing**

Two criteria must be satisfied:

**(a) Current Carrying Capacity Criterion:**

The cable's rated capacity (from IS 3961 / manufacturer's chart) must exceed $I_{FL}$ with an appropriate **derating factor** for ambient temperature and grouping of cables.

**(b) Voltage Drop Criterion:**

Voltage drop from meter to the farthest load must not exceed **3% of the supply voltage** (or 4% for combined meter-to-main + main-to-load drop, as per IS 732).

### **Step 9 — Protective Device Selection**

- **MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker):** Rated current $I_{n} \geq I_{FL}$ but $I_{n} \leq$ cable current carrying capacity.
- **ELCB / RCCB:** Residual current rating — 30 mA for human protection, 300 mA for fire protection.
- **MCCB / ACB:** For higher ratings (>100 A).
- **Fuse:** Backup protection.

### **Step 10 — Earthing, Layout & Estimate**

- Earth electrode design (as per IS 3043).
- Single-line diagram preparation.
- Schedule of Materials with quantities, rates, and total cost.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Sl. No. | Formula / Rule | Notation | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{md} = P_{connected} \times D_f$ | $P_{md}$ = Maximum demand, $D_f$ = Diversity factor | W or kW | Step 4 — Demand calculation |
| 2 | $I_{FL(1\phi)} = \dfrac{P_{md}}{V \times \cos\phi}$ | $V$ = 230 V, $\cos\phi$ = power factor | Ampere | Step 5 — Single-phase current |
| 3 | $I_{FL(3\phi)} = \dfrac{P_{md}}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L \times \cos\phi}$ | $V_L$ = 415 V line voltage | Ampere | Step 5 — Three-phase current |
| 4 | $R = \dfrac{\rho \times L}{A}$ | $\rho$ = resistivity, $L$ = length, $A$ = area | Ω | Conductor DC resistance |
| 5 | $V_{drop(1\phi)} = \dfrac{2 \times I \times \rho \times L}{A}$ | Factor 2 for go + return | Volts | Single-phase voltage drop |
| 6 | $V_{drop(3\phi)} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \times I \times \rho \times L}{A}$ | $\sqrt{3}$ for 3-phase | Volts | Three-phase voltage drop |
| 7 | $\%V_{drop} = \dfrac{V_{drop}}{V_s} \times 100 \leq 3\%$ | $V_s$ = supply voltage | % | Code compliance check |
| 8 | $A = \dfrac{2 \times I \times \rho \times L \times 100}{V_s \times \%V_{drop}}$ | Rearranged form of (5) | mm² | Direct cable sizing |
| 9 | $I_{n(MCB)} \geq I_{FL}$ AND $I_{n} \leq I_{zz}$ | $I_{zz}$ = cable rating | A | MCB coordination |
| 10 | Earth resistance $R_e \leq 1\ \Omega$ (sub-station), $\leq 5\ \Omega$ (domestic) | Per IS 3043 | Ω | Earthing design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Resistivity Values to Memorize for KTU:**
> - Copper: $\rho = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot m$ at 20 °C
> - Aluminium: $\rho = 2.826 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot m$ at 20 °C
> - Standard value used in KTU problems: $\rho_{Cu} \approx 0.0172\ \Omega\cdot mm^2/m$ (engineering simplification)

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Electrical Consultants:** Use these steps daily to design buildings, factories, and hospitals.
- **KTU Placement Relevance:** Companies like L&T, Siemens, Schneider, and ABB test this topic in campus placements.
- **Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) Inspections:** Sanctioned load and design drawings are mandatory for new connections.

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Voltage Drop for Single-Phase AC Circuit

Starting from Ohm's Law for the loop resistance (go + return conductor):

$$
V_{drop} = I \times R_{loop}
$$

The loop resistance consists of *two* conductors (phase and neutral):

$$
R_{loop} = 2 \times R_{conductor} = 2 \times \frac{\rho \times L}{A}
$$

Substituting:

$$
V_{drop} = \frac{2 \times I \times \rho \times L}{A}
$$

For AC circuits with cable impedance $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2}$ (where $X$ is inductive reactance), the more accurate form is:

$$
V_{drop} = I \times (R \cos\phi + X \sin\phi) \times L
$$

In **KTU problems**, unless explicitly stated otherwise, the simplified resistive form is used:

$$
\boxed{V_{drop(1\phi)} = \frac{2 \times I \times \rho \times L}{A}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Voltage Drop for Three-Phase AC Circuit

For a balanced three-phase star or delta load, the line-to-neutral voltage drop is:

$$
V_{ph} = I_{ph} \times Z_{ph}
$$

The line-to-line voltage drop magnitude becomes:

$$
V_{drop(L-L)} = \sqrt{3} \times I_L \times Z_{ph}
$$

With pure resistance ($Z = R$):

$$
\boxed{V_{drop(3\phi)} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \times I \times \rho \times L}{A}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Required Conductor Cross-Section from Voltage Drop Limit

Rearranging the single-phase voltage drop formula for $A$:

$$
A = \frac{2 \times I \times \rho \times L}{V_{drop}}
$$

Expressing voltage drop as a percentage of supply voltage:

$$
V_{drop} = \frac{\%V_{drop}}{100} \times V_s
$$

Substituting:

$$
A = \frac{2 \times I \times \rho \times L \times 100}{\%V_{drop} \times V_s}
$$

**Numerator units check:**

$$
\frac{[A] \times [\Omega \cdot mm^2/m] \times [m]}{[\%V] \times [V]} = \frac{A \cdot \Omega \cdot mm^2 \cdot m}{m \cdot \%V \cdot V}
$$

Since $\Omega = V/A$, we get:

$$
\frac{A \cdot (V/A) \cdot mm^2}{\%V \cdot V} = \frac{mm^2 \cdot V \cdot 100}{\%V \cdot V} = mm^2
$$

Units verified. ✓

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard 14-Mark Pattern)

> **[PROBLEM]**
> A residential bungalow has the following loads:
> - 12 light points @ 60 W each
> - 6 ceiling fans @ 75 W each
> - 4 × 5A sockets @ 100 W each
> - 2 × 15A sockets @ 1000 W each
> - 1 AC point @ 2000 W
> - 1 geyser @ 2000 W
>
> The supply is single-phase 230 V, 50 Hz. The wiring is PVC insulated copper conductor in PVC conduit (recessed). The distance from the meter to the main DB is 8 m, and the longest sub-circuit run is 12 m. The ambient temperature is 40 °C. Assume a diversity factor of 0.6 and a power factor of 0.85 for the aggregate load.
>
> **Design the wiring system and determine the main cable size.**

### **SOLUTION (10 Steps, as expected by KTU Examiner):**

**Step 1: Sanctioned Load**

> The total load will be submitted to KSEB for sanction. From the calculation below, we will request **7 kW** sanction.

**Step 2: Layout Plan**

> A scaled plan shows 12 lights, 6 fans, 4 + 2 sockets, AC, and geyser distributed in 3 bedrooms, 1 hall, 1 kitchen, and 2 bathrooms.

**Step 3: Connected Load Calculation**

$$
P_{lights} = 12 \times 60 = 720\ W
$$

$$
P_{fans} = 6 \times 75 = 450\ W
$$

$$
P_{5A\ sockets} = 4 \times 100 = 400\ W
$$

$$
P_{15A\ sockets} = 2 \times 1000 = 2000\ W
$$

$$
P_{AC} = 1 \times 2000 = 2000\ W
$$

$$
P_{geyser} = 1 \times 2000 = 2000\ W
$$

$$
P_{connected} = 720 + 450 + 400 + 2000 + 2000 + 2000 = 7570\ W \approx 7.57\ kW
$$

**Step 4: Maximum Demand (Applying Diversity Factor $D_f = 0.6$)**

$$
P_{md} = 7570 \times 0.6 = 4542\ W \approx 4.54\ kW
$$

**Step 5: Full Load Current**

$$
I_{FL} = \frac{P_{md}}{V \times \cos\phi} = \frac{4542}{230 \times 0.85}
$$

Let us calculate the denominator first:

$$
230 \times 0.85 = 195.5
$$

Now divide:

$$
I_{FL} = \frac{4542}{195.5} \approx 23.23\ A
$$

**Step 6: Sub-Circuit Division**

> - **Sub-circuit 1:** Lights of Hall + 1 bedroom (5 points) = 300 W → 1.3 A
> - **Sub-circuit 2:** Lights of 2 remaining bedrooms + fans (8 points) = 930 W → 4.04 A
> - **Sub-circuit 3:** Kitchen 5A sockets + 15A socket (5 points) = 1400 W → 6.09 A
> - **Sub-circuit 4:** 15A socket + AC + Geyser (4 points) = 5000 W → 21.74 A
>
> Each sub-circuit will be protected by a separate MCB at the DB.

**Step 7: Type of Wiring**

> **PVC insulated copper conductor in PVC conduit (recessed)** — standard for residential buildings in Kerala. This is a 1.5 kV grade, Class 2 conductor.

**Step 8: Conductor Sizing**

**(a) Current Carrying Capacity Criterion for Main Cable:**

The main cable carries 23.23 A. From the **IS 3961 Part V current rating table** for 1.5 kV grade PVC copper cable (clipped/conduit), a **6 mm²** copper cable has a rated capacity of **32 A** (in single-phase two-core with earth). This satisfies the current criterion.

**Derating Check:** At 40 °C ambient, derating factor = 0.87. The derated capacity becomes:

$$
I_{derated} = 32 \times 0.87 = 27.84\ A
$$

Since $I_{derated} = 27.84\ A > I_{FL} = 23.23\ A$ → **6 mm² cable is safe on current basis.** ✓

**(b) Voltage Drop Criterion for Main Cable (Length L = 8 m):**

Using $\rho_{Cu} = 0.0172\ \Omega\cdot mm^2/m$:

$$
V_{drop} = \frac{2 \times I_{FL} \times \rho \times L}{A} = \frac{2 \times 23.23 \times 0.0172 \times 8}{6}
$$

Calculate the numerator step by step:

$$
2 \times 23.23 = 46.46
$$

$$
46.46 \times 0.0172 = 0.7991
$$

$$
0.7991 \times 8 = 6.393
$$

Now divide by 6:

$$
V_{drop} = \frac{6.393}{6} = 1.066\ V
$$

Calculate the percentage:

$$
\%V_{drop} = \frac{1.066}{230} \times 100 = 0.46\%
$$

Since $0.46\% < 3\%$ limit → **6 mm² cable is safe on voltage drop basis.** ✓

**Final Main Cable Size: 6 mm² Copper, 2-core PVC insulated, armoured (for underground service) or in conduit.**

**Step 9: Protective Device**

- **Main MCB:** Rating $I_n = 32\ A$ (next standard size above 23.23 A; C-curve).
- **RCCB/ELCB:** 30 mA, 40 A, 2-pole at the main board.
- **Sub-circuit MCBs:** 6 A for lighting circuits, 16 A for 5A socket circuits, 25 A for the 15A/AC/geyser circuit.
- **MCB coordination check:** $I_{n(MCB)} = 32\ A \leq I_{zz} = 32\ A$ ✓ (per IS 732).

**Step 10: Earthing, Layout & Estimate**

- **Earthing:** GI pipe electrode of 50 mm diameter, 3 m deep, with 50 mm charcoal and salt layer; earth resistance target $R_e \leq 5\ \Omega$.
- **Single-Line Diagram** (drawn in Section 4).
- **Schedule of Materials** (sample in Section 5).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer Summary for the Numerical:**
> - Connected Load = **7.57 kW**
> - Maximum Demand = **4.54 kW**
> - Full Load Current = **23.23 A**
> - Main Cable = **6 mm² Cu PVC 2-core**
> - Main MCB = **32 A, C-curve, with 30 mA RCCB**
> - Voltage Drop = **0.46 %** (well within 3 % limit)

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Wiring Systems

| Wiring System | Mechanical Protection | Aesthetic | Cost | Fire Safety | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| PVC Conduit (concealed) | Excellent | Best (hidden) | High | Excellent | Modern residential |
| PVC Conduit (surface) | Excellent | Poor | Medium | Excellent | Industrial sheds |
| Casing & Capping (wood) | Medium | Good | Medium | Poor | Old buildings (legacy) |
| Batten / Cleat | Poor | Poor | Low | Poor | Temporary |
| MICC (Mineral) | Excellent | Good | Very High | Best | Fire-prone zones |
| Lead Sheathed | Good | Good | High | Good | Damp / chemical areas |
| TRS (Tough Rubber Sheath) | Good | Good | Medium | Medium | Workshops |
| PVC Trough / Trunking | Good | Good | Medium | Good | Offices, commercial |

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Voltage Drop Validation Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compute Full Load Current I_FL] --> B[Assume Initial Cable Size from I_FL Table]
    B --> C[Look up Current Carrying Capacity I_zz from IS 3961]
    C --> D{I_FL is less than I_zz?}
    D -- No --> E[Increase Cable Size]
    E --> C
    D -- Yes --> F[Apply Derating Factors Temp Grouping]
    F --> G[Compute I_derated = I_zz times derating]
    G --> H{I_derated is greater than I_FL?}
    H -- No --> E
    H -- Yes --> I[Compute Voltage Drop V_d = 2 I L rho divided by A]
    I --> J[Compute percent V_d = V_d divided by V_s times 100]
    J --> K{percent V_d is less than 3 percent?}
    K -- No --> E
    K -- Yes --> L[Finalise Cable Size and Document]
    L --> M([Approved Cable Specification])
```

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**List the essential steps involved in the design of electrical wiring for a residential building.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The essential design steps are:

1. **Step 1 — Sanctioned Load:** Ascertain the sanctioned load from KSEB.
2. **Step 2 — Layout Plan:** Prepare a scaled building layout with all load points marked.
3. **Step 3 — Connected Load:** Calculate the connected load by summing all individual load wattages.
4. **Step 4 — Maximum Demand:** Apply the diversity factor ($D_f = 0.6$ to $0.7$ for residential) to get the maximum demand.
5. **Step 5 — Full Load Current:** Calculate $I_{FL} = P_{md} / (V \cos\phi)$.
6. **Step 6 — Sub-Circuits:** Divide the loads into sub-circuits (max 10 points per circuit).
7. **Step 7 — Wiring System:** Select the wiring system (PVC conduit, casing-capping, etc.).
8. **Step 8 — Cable Sizing:** Size the conductor by current capacity and voltage drop (≤ 3 %).
9. **Step 9 — Protection:** Choose MCB, RCCB, and fuse ratings.
10. **Step 10 — Earthing and Estimate:** Design the earthing, draw the wiring diagram, and prepare the schedule of materials.

*(1 mark for grouping into logical steps; 1 mark for correct sequence; 1 mark for mentioning voltage drop limit and protection.)* ✓

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the diversity factor? State its typical values for domestic and industrial installations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** The **diversity factor** is defined as the ratio of the *sum of individual maximum demands* of the various parts of a system to the *coincident maximum demand* of the whole system.

$$
D_f = \frac{\sum \text{Individual Maximum Demands}}{\text{Coincident Maximum Demand of the Whole System}}
$$

**Physical Meaning:** It represents the fact that not all loads operate simultaneously. The diversity factor is **always $\geq 1$**, and the *utilization factor* (its inverse) is $\leq 1$.

**Typical Values:**

| Installation Type | Diversity Factor |
|---|---|
| Domestic (Residential) | 0.6 – 0.7 |
| Commercial (Offices, Shops) | 0.7 – 0.8 |
| Industrial (Factories) | 0.5 – 0.6 |
| Single Dwelling Unit | 0.7 – 0.8 |

*(1 mark definition; 1 mark formula; 1 mark typical values.)* ✓

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A residential house has the following electrical loads:
- 8 light points × 60 W
- 4 ceiling fans × 75 W
- 2 × 5A socket outlets × 100 W
- 1 × 15A socket outlet × 1000 W

Assume the supply is single-phase 230 V, 50 Hz. The wiring is PVC insulated copper in PVC conduit. The distance from the energy meter to the main distribution board is 10 m. Take diversity factor = 0.7, power factor = 0.9, and resistivity of copper = $0.0172\ \Omega\cdot mm^2/m$. Design the wiring and determine the size of the main cable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the factors that influence the selection of the type of wiring system for a given installation. Compare PVC conduit wiring with casing-and-capping wiring on at least four points. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Sanctioned Load & Layout**

> The connected loads are listed and will be submitted to KSEB for sanction.

**Step 2: Connected Load Calculation**

$$
P_{lights} = 8 \times 60 = 480\ W
$$

$$
P_{fans} = 4 \times 75 = 300\ W
$$

$$
P_{5A\ sockets} = 2 \times 100 = 200\ W
$$

$$
P_{15A\ socket} = 1 \times 1000 = 1000\ W
$$

Summing all loads:

$$
P_{connected} = 480 + 300 + 200 + 1000 = 1980\ W = 1.98\ kW
$$

**Step 3: Maximum Demand (with $D_f = 0.7$)**

$$
P_{md} = 1980 \times 0.7 = 1386\ W = 1.386\ kW
$$

**Step 4: Full Load Current**

$$
I_{FL} = \frac{P_{md}}{V \times \cos\phi} = \frac{1386}{230 \times 0.9}
$$

Computing denominator:

$$
230 \times 0.9 = 207
$$

Therefore:

$$
I_{FL} = \frac{1386}{207} \approx 6.696\ A
$$

**Step 5: Sub-Circuit Division**

> - Sub-circuit 1: All 8 lights (8 points, within the limit of 10) → 480 W
> - Sub-circuit 2: All 4 fans + 2 × 5A sockets (6 points) → 500 W
> - Sub-circuit 3: 15A socket alone (1 point) → 1000 W

**Step 6: Wiring System**

> PVC insulated copper conductor in PVC conduit (recessed), 1.5 kV grade.

**Step 7: Main Cable Sizing — Current Capacity Criterion**

> $I_{FL} = 6.696$ A. From the IS 3961 table, a **2.5 mm²** copper cable has a rating of **18 A** in conduit, and a **1.5 mm²** cable has a rating of **14 A**. We can use 1.5 mm² on the current basis.

**Step 8: Main Cable Sizing — Voltage Drop Criterion**

Using:

$$
A = \frac{2 \times I_{FL} \times \rho \times L \times 100}{V_s \times \%V_{drop}}
$$

Assume a 3 % limit:

$$
A = \frac{2 \times 6.696 \times 0.0172 \times 10 \times 100}{230 \times 3}
$$

Computing the numerator step by step:

$$
2 \times 6.696 = 13.392
$$

$$
13.392 \times 0.0172 = 0.23034
$$

$$
0.23034 \times 10 = 2.3034
$$

$$
2.3034 \times 100 = 230.34
$$

Computing the denominator:

$$
230 \times 3 = 690
$$

Therefore:

$$
A = \frac{230.34}{690} = 0.3338\ mm^2
$$

The required area (0.33 mm²) is far smaller than the 1.5 mm² minimum from current rating. Hence, **the main cable size is 1.5 mm²** (next standard size based on current capacity). For safety margin and mechanical strength, **2.5 mm²** is recommended for the main run.

**Final Main Cable: 2.5 mm² Cu, 2-core PVC insulated.** ✓

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Factors Influencing the Selection of Wiring System:**

1. **Safety:** Protection against fire, electric shock, and mechanical damage.
2. **Cost of Installation:** Initial cost vs. lifecycle cost.
3. **Aesthetic Appearance:** Concealed vs. surface wiring for visual appeal.
4. **Type of Building:** Residential, commercial, industrial, or hazardous.
5. **Environmental Conditions:** Dampness, chemical vapors, temperature, dust.
6. **Mechanical Stress:** Possibility of physical impact on the wiring.
7. **Future Maintenance:** Ease of fault finding, additions, and modifications.
8. **Fire-Prone Areas:** Use of fire-resistant cables (MICC, XLPE).
9. **Durability & Life Expectancy:** Expected service life of the installation.
10. **Compliance with IS Standards and IE Rules.**

**Comparison Table — PVC Conduit vs. Casing and Capping:**

| S.No. | Parameter | PVC Conduit Wiring | Casing and Capping Wiring |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Aesthetic | Excellent (concealed, invisible) | Average (visible strips) |
| 2 | Mechanical Protection | Very High (rigid PVC tube) | Moderate (wood/PVC cap) |
| 3 | Cost | Higher (labour-intensive) | Lower (faster installation) |
| 4 | Fire Safety | Excellent (PVC is self-extinguishing) | Poor (wood is combustible) |
| 5 | Damp Resistance | Excellent (PVC is waterproof) | Poor (wood absorbs moisture) |
| 6 | Flexibility for Alteration | Difficult (concealed) | Easy (just open the cap) |
| 7 | Lifespan | 40+ years | 15–20 years (wood) |
| 8 | Inspection & Maintenance | Difficult | Easy |

*(1.5 marks for listing factors; 5.5 marks for detailed comparison with a 4+ point table.)* ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat single-line diagram, explain the design of a single-phase 230 V electrical wiring system for a small office having the following loads:
- 10 light points × 60 W
- 4 ceiling fans × 75 W
- 6 × 5A socket outlets × 100 W
- 1 AC unit × 2000 W

Assume diversity factor = 0.75, supply voltage = 230 V, power factor = 0.85. Calculate the maximum demand and full load current. Recommend the main cable size and protective device. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a *sub-circuit*? State the IS 732 rules for sub-circuit division. Why is it important to limit the number of points per sub-circuit? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Connected Load Calculation**

$$
P_{lights} = 10 \times 60 = 600\ W
$$

$$
P_{fans} = 4 \times 75 = 300\ W
$$

$$
P_{5A\ sockets} = 6 \times 100 = 600\ W
$$

$$
P_{AC} = 1 \times 2000 = 2000\ W
$$

Summing all loads:

$$
P_{connected} = 600 + 300 + 600 + 2000 = 3500\ W = 3.5\ kW
$$

**Step 2: Maximum Demand (with $D_f = 0.75$)**

$$
P_{md} = 3500 \times 0.75 = 2625\ W = 2.625\ kW
$$

**Step 3: Full Load Current**

$$
I_{FL} = \frac{P_{md}}{V \times \cos\phi} = \frac{2625}{230 \times 0.85}
$$

Denominator calculation:

$$
230 \times 0.85 = 195.5
$$

Therefore:

$$
I_{FL} = \frac{2625}{195.5} \approx 13.43\ A
$$

**Step 4: Main Cable Sizing**

- From current capacity: A 2.5 mm² Cu cable (18 A rating) is adequate.
- From voltage drop check (assume 8 m run, 3 % limit):

$$
V_{drop} = \frac{2 \times 13.43 \times 0.0172 \times 8}{2.5} = 1.48\ V
$$

$$
\%V_{drop} = \frac{1.48}{230} \times 100 = 0.64\% \quad <\ 3\% \quad \checkmark
$$

- **Recommended main cable: 2.5 mm² Cu, 2-core PVC insulated.**

**Step 5: Protective Device**

- **MCB:** 16 A, C-curve, single-pole + neutral (SPN).
- **RCCB:** 30 mA, 25 A, 2-pole at the main board.
- Sub-circuit MCBs: 6 A × 2 (lights), 6 A × 1 (fans), 16 A × 1 (sockets), 20 A × 1 (AC).

**Step 6: Single-Line Diagram (Description)**

> The single-line diagram shows: **KSEB meter** → **Double-pole switch (32 A)** → **RCCB (30 mA, 40 A)** → **Busbar in DB** → **Sub-circuit MCBs (5 of them)** → **Loads**. Earth conductor is connected from the main earth electrode to every metallic body.

**Step 7: Earthing**

> GI pipe earth electrode, $R_e \leq 5\ \Omega$.

*(1 mark connected load, 1 mark M.D., 1 mark FLC, 2 marks cable sizing, 1 mark protection, 1 mark single-line diagram description.)* ✓

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Definition of Sub-Circuit:**

A *sub-circuit* is a section of the electrical installation that originates from a final sub-circuit MCB and serves one or more load points. It is the *last segment* of the distribution system, with its own overcurrent protective device.

**IS 732 Rules for Sub-Circuit Division:**

1. **Number of Points:** No sub-circuit should have more than **10 points** (lights, fans, sockets combined).
2. **Maximum Load for Lighting Sub-Circuit:** Total connected load of a lighting sub-circuit must not exceed **800 W**.
3. **Maximum Load for Power Sub-Circuit:** Total connected load of a power sub-circuit must not exceed **3000 W**.
4. **Independent Sub-Circuits:** High-power appliances like AC units, geysers, and water heaters should be on **independent dedicated sub-circuits** with appropriately rated MCBs.
5. **Each Sub-Circuit Identified:** Every sub-circuit MCB in the DB must be clearly labeled.
6. **Earthing:** Every sub-circuit must have a continuous earth conductor.

**Why Limit the Number of Points per Sub-Circuit?**

- **Safety during Faults:** If a fault occurs, only a limited portion of the installation loses power, not the entire building.
- **Ease of Maintenance and Fault Finding:** Electricians can isolate and repair small sections.
- **Preventing Overload:** A 10-point limit naturally bounds the load on each MCB.
- **Selective Coordination:** A fault in one sub-circuit trips only its MCB; upstream MCBs remain unaffected.
- **Voltage Drop Control:** Smaller, branched sub-circuits keep the run length manageable, keeping voltage drop below 3 %.

*(1.5 marks definition; 3.5 marks IS 732 rules; 2 marks importance.)* ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Design Steps Questions:**
>
> 1. **Skipping the Sanctioned Load step** (Step 1): Examiners deduct 1 mark if you jump directly to load calculation.
> 2. **Forgetting the Diversity Factor:** Many students compute $I_{FL}$ from connected load directly, giving 60–70 % higher current → wrong cable size. *Always apply $D_f$.*
> 3. **Not Verifying Voltage Drop:** Sizing the cable only by current capacity is *incomplete*. You MUST check the 3 % voltage drop limit. Students lose 2 marks here.
> 4. **Wrong Unit for Resistivity:** Using $\rho = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}$ in SI units without unit conversion is a common error. Use $\rho = 0.0172\ \Omega\cdot mm^2/m$ for cable sizing in mm² and metres.
> 5. **MCB Sizing Logic:** Selecting MCB equal to $I_{FL}$ exactly is *wrong* — it should be the *next standard size above* $I_{FL}$ (e.g., 6.7 A → 10 A MCB, not 6 A).
> 6. **Neglecting the Power Factor:** Using $I = P/V$ instead of $I = P/(V\cos\phi)$ gives incorrect current.
> 7. **No Derating Factor:** Students ignore ambient temperature (40 °C Kerala conditions) and grouping derating.
> 8. **Single-Line Diagram Mistakes:** The busbar should be drawn as a single horizontal line, and the earth wire must be continuous, not broken.
> 9. **Forgetting to Convert kW to W:** A common calculation error when plugging into $I = P / V \cos\phi$.
> 10. **No Mention of IS Standards:** Examiners appreciate a one-line reference to IS 732 / IS 4648 in step 7 or step 9.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] The **10 design steps** must always be presented in logical order.
- [x] **Sanctioned Load** is the first and most critical step.
- [x] **Connected Load** = sum of all individual point loads (in W or kW).
- [x] **Maximum Demand** = Connected Load × **Diversity Factor** (≤ 1).
- [x] For **single phase**: $I = P_{md} / (230 \times \cos\phi)$.
- [x] For **three phase**: $I = P_{md} / (\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times \cos\phi)$.
- [x] **Standard diversity factor** for residential = 0.6 – 0.7; for industrial = 0.5 – 0.6.
- [x] **Sub-circuit limits** (IS 732): Max 10 points, ≤ 800 W lighting, ≤ 3000 W power.
- [x] **Cable sizing has TWO criteria** — current capacity AND voltage drop (≤ 3 %).
- [x] **Voltage drop formula** (1-phase): $V_d = 2IL\rho/A$.
- [x] **Voltage drop formula** (3-phase): $V_d = \sqrt{3}\,IL\rho/A$.
- [x] **Resistivity values:** Copper = $0.0172\ \Omega\cdot mm^2/m$; Aluminium = $0.0283\ \Omega\cdot mm^2/m$.
- [x] **MCB rating** = next standard size above $I_{FL}$.
- [x] **RCCB rating**: 30 mA for human protection, 300 mA for fire protection.
- [x] **Earth resistance**: ≤ 5 Ω for domestic, ≤ 1 Ω for sub-stations.
- [x] **Standard wiring systems** in Kerala: PVC conduit (recessed/surface) is most common.
- [x] **Earthing electrode** typically: 50 mm GI pipe, 3 m deep, with charcoal-salt packing.
- [x] **Final deliverables** of design: SLD, layout drawing, schedule of materials, cost estimate.
- [x] **Key standards** to quote: IS 732, IS 4648, IS 3043, IS 9537, NEC, IER 1956.
- [x] **Derating factors** apply for ambient temperature > 30 °C and cable grouping > 2.
- [x] **Mechanical strength** minimum cable size: 1.5 mm² Cu for power, 1.0 mm² for electronics.
- [x] **Colour code** (India): Phase = Red/Yellow/Blue; Neutral = Black; Earth = Green.
- [x] **Power factor** to assume for mixed load: 0.8 lagging (typical KTU value).
- [x] **Final check before submitting** the design: Voltage drop < 3 %, MCB coordination OK, earthing verified, SLD labelled, estimate ready.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material Estimation and Development of Single Line Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Material Estimation and Development of Single Line Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Single Line Diagram (SLD) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single Line Diagram (SLD)** is a simplified schematic representation of a three-phase (or polyphase) electrical power system in which all three phase conductors are represented by a **single line**, with standard symbolic notations used to depict the connected equipment, protection devices, and switching elements.

As per **IS 732 (1989)** and **IEC 60617**, an SLD shows:
- The **power flow path** from source (Grid/Generator) to load (Motor/Lighting)
- The **electrical interconnections** between major equipment
- The **protection scheme** (relays, breakers, fuses)
- The **metering and instrumentation** (CTs, PTs, kWh meters)
- The **bus configuration** and tap-off points

## 1.2 Material Estimation — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Material Estimation** is the systematic process of determining the **quantities, types, and specifications** of all electrical materials required for the execution of a given electrical installation project. It forms the technical basis for **Bill of Quantities (BOQ)**, **cost estimation**, and **procurement planning**.

The estimation must comply with:
- **IS 732** — Code of practice for electrical wiring installations
- **IE Rules 1956** — Indian Electricity Rules
- **NEC 2011** — National Electrical Code (India)
- **CPWD Specifications** — For government/public sector projects

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of an SLD like a Metro/Subway Map of an Electrical System.**
>
> Imagine you are looking at a city metro map. A red line shows one route from "Power Plant" to "Home". The SLD does the same thing with electricity. Three thick cables carrying 3-phase power are drawn as **just ONE line**, because electrically they are balanced and identical. Stations on the metro map = **transformers, breakers, panels**. Transfers = **bus couplers, isolators**. This makes the complex 3D wiring fit cleanly on a 2D page.
>
> For **Material Estimation**, think of it as the **grocery shopping list for an electrician**. Before building, the engineer must know exactly *how many* rolls of wire, *how many* MCBs, *how many* meters of conduit. Estimation is that careful "shopping list preparation" — measured, calculated, and padded with a small **wastage factor of 5% to 10%**.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Standard Supply Voltage (LT) | **415 V, 3-phase, 4-wire, 50 Hz** |
| Standard Supply Voltage (HT) | **11 kV / 22 kV / 33 kV / 66 kV / 110 kV** |
| Power Frequency | **50 Hz** |
| Standard Insulation Class | **Class A (105°C) / B / F / H** |
| Wastage Factor (Cables) | **5% to 10%** |
| Wastage Factor (Conduits) | **3% to 5%** |
| Standard Conduit Size (PVC) | **20 mm, 25 mm, 32 mm, 40 mm, 50 mm** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Standard Symbols Used in SLD (As per IS 2032 / IEC 60617)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Mastering these symbols is **mandatory** for KTU examinations. The examiner will test identification and use of these symbols in both Part A and Part B questions.

| Symbol | Component |
|---|---|
| Circle with "G" | Generator / Alternator |
| Two interlocking circles | Transformer (winding details inside) |
| Square with cross / X | Circuit Breaker (CB) |
| Single line with dot | Isolator / Disconnector |
| Triangle pointing up | Delta Connection (Δ) |
| Three vertical lines + curved top | Star Connection (Y) with Neutral |
| Rectangle with "M" | Motor (Induction/Synchronous) |
| Circle with arrow | Current Transformer (CT) |
| Two circles connected by line | Potential Transformer (PT) |
| Rectangular box with arrow | Relay (Numeric/Electromagnetic) |
| Hollow rectangle with diagonal | Bus Bar |
| Three lines (long-short-short) | Earth / Ground Connection |
| Inverted "T" with cross | Lightning Arrester (LA) |
| Square with vertical line | Power Capacitor |

## 2.2 Methodology for Material Estimation

The estimation process follows a **6-step systematic workflow**:

1. **Site Survey & Data Collection** — Measure actual route lengths, count points, identify load locations.
2. **Preparation of Layout Plan** — Develop wiring layout, lighting layout, and SLD on drawing sheets.
3. **Classification of Loads** — Lighting, Power (single-phase), Power (three-phase), HVAC, etc.
4. **Selection of Cable Sizes** — Based on current rating, voltage drop, and short-circuit withstand.
5. **Quantity Take-Off** — Calculate lengths of wires/cables, conduits, and number of accessories.
6. **Application of Wastage** — Add 5–10% wastage and prepare final BOQ.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_L \times \cos\phi$ | Three-phase Active Power | W (Watts) |
| 2 | $I = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L \times \cos\phi}$ | Full Load Current (3-phase) | A |
| 3 | $I = \dfrac{P}{V \times \cos\phi}$ | Full Load Current (1-phase) | A |
| 4 | $V_{drop} = \sqrt{3} \times I \times L \times (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$ | Voltage Drop (3-phase) | V |
| 5 | $A = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \times \rho \times I \times L \times 1000}{V_d \times V_L}$ | Cable Cross-Sectional Area | mm² |
| 6 | $L_{total} = L_{route} \times N_{runs} \times (1 + W_f)$ | Total Cable Length with Wastage | m |
| 7 | $N_{points} = N_{switches} + N_{sockets}$ | Total Wiring Points | Nos. |
| 8 | $L_{wire} = (H + D) \times W_{per point} \times N_{points}$ | Wire Length for Point Wiring | m |
| 9 | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{MVA}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L}$ | Short Circuit Current (HV side) | kA |
| 10 | $\%\text{V}_{drop} = \dfrac{V_{drop}}{V_{rated}} \times 100$ | Percentage Voltage Drop | % |

Where:
- $V_L$ = Line Voltage (V)
- $I_L$ = Line Current (A)
- $L$ = Length of cable (km)
- $\rho$ = Resistivity of copper = **0.0172 Ω·mm²/m**, aluminium = **0.0282 Ω·mm²/m**
- $W_f$ = Wastage factor (typically 0.05 to 0.10)
- $H$ = Average height of switchboard from floor (m)
- $D$ = Average horizontal distance from switchboard to load (m)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Application**: In a manufacturing plant, the SLD serves as the **master document** for the entire electrical distribution. Every protection setting, every cable schedule, every maintenance activity is referenced back to the SLD. Without it, troubleshooting becomes guesswork.
>
> **Estimation Application**: For a 100 kW solar plant or a 5000 sq.ft. commercial building, accurate material estimation can save **8–12% project cost** by preventing over-ordering (waste) and under-ordering (project delays).

## 2.5 Design Rules for SLD Preparation

- **Left to Right Power Flow**: Source on the left, load on the right.
- **Hierarchical Levels**: Grid → Transformer → HT Panel → Transformer → LT Panel → Distribution Boards → Loads.
- **Bus Representation**: Use thicker lines for bus bars (typically 2× stroke width).
- **Protection Hierarchy**: Show upstream and downstream protection devices clearly.
- **Standard Reference Designation**: Use tag numbers (e.g., T-1 for Transformer 1, B-1 for Breaker 1).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cable Cross-Sectional Area from Voltage Drop Constraint

### Problem Setup
A 3-phase, 415 V motor load draws current $I$ over a cable run of length $L$ (one-way). We need to find the **minimum cross-sectional area** $A$ of the cable conductor such that the voltage drop does not exceed $V_d$ (typically 3% of $V_L$ = 12.45 V for 415 V).

### Step 1: Express Voltage Drop in Terms of Resistance

For a 3-phase system, the voltage drop across the cable is:

$$V_{drop} = \sqrt{3} \times I \times R \times \cos\phi$$

(neglecting the reactance component for shorter LV runs, $X \sin\phi$ is small).

### Step 2: Express Resistance in Terms of Resistivity

By the fundamental definition of resistance:

$$R = \dfrac{\rho \times L}{A}$$

where $\rho$ is the resistivity in $\Omega \cdot \text{mm}^2 / \text{m}$, $L$ is in **meters**, and $A$ is in **mm²**.

### Step 3: Substitute and Rearrange

Substituting $R$ into the voltage drop equation:

$$V_{drop} = \sqrt{3} \times I \times \dfrac{\rho \times L}{A} \times \cos\phi$$

### Step 4: Solve for the Cross-Sectional Area

Cross-multiplying to isolate $A$:

$$A \times V_{drop} = \sqrt{3} \times I \times \rho \times L \times \cos\phi$$

$$A = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \times \rho \times I \times L \times \cos\phi}{V_{drop}}$$

To express $L$ in km (as is common in practical tables), we multiply numerator and denominator by **1000**:

$$A = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \times \rho \times I \times L_{(km)} \times 1000 \times \cos\phi}{V_{drop}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conversion Logic**: The factor **1000** appears because resistivity $\rho$ is defined per **meter**, but practical voltage drop tables use cable length in **km**. To convert km → m, we multiply by 1000.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example: Material Estimation for a Small Office

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement**: A small office has the following electrical points:
> - 12 Light Points (1 switch each)
> - 8 Fan Points (1 regulator + 1 switch each)
> - 6 5A Socket Outlets
> - 2 15A Socket Outlets (for AC units)
>
> Average height of switchboard $H = 1.5$ m, average horizontal distance $D = 4$ m. Standard 1.5 mm² copper wire used. Calculate the total length of phase wire and neutral wire.

### Step 1: Count the Total Number of Points

$$N_{points} = N_{lights} + N_{fans} + N_{5A} + N_{15A}$$

$$N_{points} = 12 + 8 + 6 + 2 = 28 \text{ points}$$

### Step 2: Compute Length per Point

$$L_{per\ point} = H + D = 1.5 + 4.0 = 5.5\ \text{m}$$

### Step 3: Compute Total Wire Length (Before Wastage)

The number of wires per point in a typical Indian office (switch + neutral + phase loop):
- Phase wire: 1 per point
- Neutral wire: 1 per point
- Switch wire (loop): 1 per point (for switch control)
- Earth wire: 1 per point

$$L_{raw} = 28 \times 5.5 = 154\ \text{m (per conductor)}$$

Total for 4 conductors (Phase, Neutral, Switch wire, Earth):

$$L_{total\ raw} = 154 \times 4 = 616\ \text{m}$$

### Step 4: Apply Wastage Factor (5%)

$$L_{final} = 616 \times (1 + 0.05)$$

$$L_{final} = 616 \times 1.05 = 646.8\ \text{m}$$

### Step 5: Round Up to Commercial Roll Size

Standard rolls are 90 m or 100 m. Order **7 rolls of 100 m each (700 m total)** to account for wastage during stripping and termination.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Estimation Quick-Reference

| Material | Unit | Estimation Rule of Thumb |
|---|---|---|
| **Cu Wire 1.5 mm²** | m | $(H + D) \times \text{points} \times 1.10$ |
| **Cu Wire 2.5 mm²** | m | $(H + D) \times \text{15A sockets} \times 1.10$ |
| **Cu Wire 4.0 mm²** | m | $(H + D) \times \text{AC points} \times 1.10$ |
| **PVC Conduit 20 mm** | m | Total cable route length × 1.05 |
| **PVC Conduit 25 mm** | m | Total cable route length × 1.05 |
| **Junction Boxes** | Nos. | 1 per 2–3 points + 1 per switchboard |
| **Switches 6A** | Nos. | Equal to number of light/fan points |
| **Socket 5A / 15A** | Nos. | As per design (per count) |
| **MCBs (SPN/TPN)** | Nos. | As per number of final circuits |
| **Earth Wire 14 SWG** | m | Equal to total phase wire length |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchical SLD Architecture

This block-level functional diagram represents the **power flow hierarchy** in a typical industrial/commercial SLD, mapping the interaction of components from the grid source to the final load.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Material Estimation Workflow

This flowchart illustrates the **step-by-step estimation process** from site survey to BOQ preparation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[STEP 1 SITE SURVEY]:::step
    B2[STEP 2 LOAD SCHEDULE]:::step
    B3[STEP 3 SLD PREPARATION]:::step
```

## 4.3 ASCII Schematic: Typical Domestic SLD

```
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  KSEB SUPPLY  ── 230V / 415V, 50 Hz, 3-PHASE, 4-WIRE     │
  └────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┘
                       │
              ┌────────▼────────┐
              │   MAIN SWITCH   │  (DP / TP / FP-ISOLATOR)
              │   WITH HRC FUSE │
              └────────┬────────┘
                       │
              ┌────────▼────────┐
              │   KWh METER     │  (Energy Meter)
              └────────┬────────┘
                       │
              ┌────────▼────────┐
              │  DISTRIBUTION   │  (MCB DB: SP + N / TP + N)
              │  BOARD (DB)     │
              └──┬─────┬─────┬──┘
                 │     │     │
       ┌─────────┘     │     └──────────┐
       │               │                │
  ┌────▼────┐    ┌─────▼─────┐    ┌─────▼─────┐
  │  6A MCB │    │  16A MCB  │    │  20A MCB  │
  │ (LIGHT) │    │ (SOCKET)  │    │   (AC)    │
  └────┬────┘    └─────┬─────┘    └─────┬─────┘
       │               │                │
   LIGHTS / FANS   5A/15A SOCKETS    AIR-CONDITIONER
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading Tip for KTU Exams**: The arrow convention in an SLD always flows **from source (left) to load (right)**. The vertical drop shows branching. Every level MUST have a **protective device** (MCB/MCCB) before the load.
