<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Single Line Diagram (SLD). List any four standard symbols used in SLD preparation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Single Line Diagram (SLD)** is a simplified schematic representation of a three-phase (or polyphase) electrical power system in which all three phase conductors are represented by a **single line**, with standard symbolic notations used to depict the connected equipment, protection devices, and switching elements, as per **IS 2032 / IEC 60617** standards. **(2 Marks)**

Four standard symbols used in SLD:
1. **Two interlocking circles** → Transformer
2. **Square with cross (X)** → Circuit Breaker
3. **Circle with "G"** → Generator
4. **Triangle** → Delta connection; **Y with neutral** → Star connection **(1 Mark)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by Bill of Quantities (BOQ)? Mention the typical wastage factors used for cables and conduits during material estimation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Bill of Quantities (BOQ)** is a comprehensive document listing **all materials, their quantities, specifications, and unit rates** required for the execution of an electrical installation project. It is the **commercial and technical foundation** for tendering, procurement, and project billing. **(2 Marks)**

Typical wastage factors:
- **Cables and Wires**: **5% to 10%** (accounts for cutting, termination, and jointing losses)
- **PVC Conduits**: **3% to 5%** (accounts for bending and cutting losses) **(1 Mark)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Model]`

**A small commercial building has the following loads. Prepare the Single Line Diagram and estimate the total cable, wire, and conduit quantities.**

**Load Details:**
- 20 Lighting Points (1 switch each, 1.5 mm² Cu wire)
- 10 Ceiling Fan Points (1 regulator + 1 switch, 1.5 mm² Cu wire)
- 8 No. 5A Socket Outlets (2.5 mm² Cu wire)
- 4 No. 15A Socket Outlets (2.5 mm² Cu wire)
- 2 AC Units (15A, 4 mm² Cu wire)

**Given:**
- Average height of switchboard $H = 1.5$ m
- Average horizontal distance $D = 5$ m
- Wastage factor = **8%**
- Standard conduit: 20 mm and 25 mm PVC

#### **Part (a) — SLD Development + Total Number of Points (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Draw the SLD**

```
   KSEB SUPPLY 415V, 3φ, 4W, 50 Hz
                │
        ┌───────▼────────┐
        │  MAIN ISOLATOR │  (TP, 100A)
        └───────┬────────┘
                │
        ┌───────▼────────┐
        │   KWh METER    │
        └───────┬────────┘
                │
        ┌───────▼────────┐
        │  MCB DB 12-WAY │  (with ELCB 30mA)
        └──┬─────┬─────┬──┘
           │     │     │
    ┌──────┘     │     └──────┐
    │            │            │
 6A MCB       16A MCB      25A MCB
(LIGHTS+FANS) (SOCKETS)   (AC UNITS)
    │            │            │
Lights/Fans   5A/15A Sockets  AC Units
    │            │            │
   EARTH       EARTH         EARTH
    └────────────┴────────────┘
                 │
            EARTHING PIT
```

**Step 2: Count the Total Points**

$$N_{points} = 20 + 10 + 8 + 4 + 2 = 44 \text{ points}$$

**Step 3: Identify Conductor Requirements**

| Conductor | Count |
|---|---|
| Phase wire | 44 |
| Neutral wire | 44 |
| Switch return wire | 30 (lights + fans) |
| Earth wire (14 SWG Cu) | 44 |

Total wire conductors for **1.5 mm² grade** = 44 × 3 (Phase, Neutral, Switch) = **132 wires**

---

#### **Part (b) — Material Quantity Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Length per Point**

$$L_{per\ point} = H + D = 1.5 + 5.0 = 6.5\ \text{m}$$

**Step 2: Raw Length of 1.5 mm² Cu Wire (for lights + fans = 30 points)**

$$L_{1.5mm^2} = 30 \times 6.5 \times 3 = 585\ \text{m (raw)}$$

Applying 8% wastage:

$$L_{1.5mm^2}^{final} = 585 \times 1.08 = 631.8\ \text{m}$$

**Step 3: Raw Length of 2.5 mm² Cu Wire (for sockets = 12 points)**

$$L_{2.5mm^2} = 12 \times 6.5 \times 2 = 156\ \text{m (raw)}$$

Applying 8% wastage:

$$L_{2.5mm^2}^{final} = 156 \times 1.08 = 168.48\ \text{m}$$

**Step 4: Raw Length of 4.0 mm² Cu Wire (for AC = 2 points)**

$$L_{4.0mm^2} = 2 \times 6.5 \times 3 = 39\ \text{m (raw)}$$

Applying 8% wastage:

$$L_{4.0mm^2}^{final} = 39 \times 1.08 = 42.12\ \text{m}$$

**Step 5: Total Conduit Length (20 mm + 25 mm)**

For 20 mm conduit (carrying 1.5 mm² wires):
$$L_{conduit}^{20mm} = 631.8\ \text{m}$$

For 25 mm conduit (carrying 2.5/4.0 mm² wires):
$$L_{conduit}^{25mm} = 168.48 + 42.12 = 210.6\ \text{m}$$

---

#### **Final BOQ Summary Table**

| S.No. | Material | Specification | Quantity | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cu Wire | 1.5 mm² | **632** | m |
| 2 | Cu Wire | 2.5 mm² | **169** | m |
| 3 | Cu Wire | 4.0 mm² | **43** | m |
| 4 | Cu Earth Wire | 14 SWG | 350 | m |
| 5 | PVC Conduit | 20 mm | **632** | m |
| 6 | PVC Conduit | 25 mm | **211** | m |
| 7 | Switches 6A | SP | 30 | Nos. |
| 8 | Fan Regulators | 100W | 10 | Nos. |
| 9 | 5A Sockets | 3-pin | 8 | Nos. |
| 10 | 15A Sockets | 3-pin | 4 | Nos. |
| 11 | MCB SP 6A | C-curve | 4 | Nos. |
| 12 | MCB SP 16A | C-curve | 2 | Nos. |
| 13 | MCB SP 25A | C-curve | 2 | Nos. |
| 14 | ELCB 30mA | 4-pole | 1 | No. |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Model]`

**Explain in detail the procedure for developing a Single Line Diagram for an industrial installation having an 11 kV HT supply, 500 kVA transformer, and LT distribution with motors and lighting load. List all standard symbols used.**

#### **Part (a) — Industrial SLD Procedure (7 Marks)**

**The SLD development for an industrial installation follows a 5-stage procedure:**

**Stage 1: Source Identification**
- Incoming supply: **11 kV, 3-phase, 50 Hz** from KSEB / utility grid
- Represented by an **incoming line arrow** with voltage and frequency annotation

**Stage 2: HT Side Equipment**
- **HT VCB (Vacuum Circuit Breaker)** — represented by a square with X
- **HT Isolator** — represented by a single line with a perpendicular break
- **CT (Current Transformer)** and **PT (Potential Transformer)** — for metering and protection
- **Lightning Arrester (LA)** — at the incoming point
- **HT Cable** — runs from HT panel to transformer primary

**Stage 3: Transformer Section**
- 500 kVA, **11 kV / 433 V**, Dyn11 vector group
- Represented by **two interlocking circles** with primary/secondary winding details inside

**Stage 4: LT Side Equipment**
- **LT Bus Bar** (415 V, 3-phase, 4-wire) — thicker line representation
- **ACB (Air Circuit Breaker)** as main breaker
- **Capacitor Bank** for power factor correction (typically 100 kVAr)
- **Outgoing feeders** to various loads

**Stage 5: Load Distribution**
- **Feeder 1**: 100 HP Induction Motor via starter
- **Feeder 2**: Lighting Distribution Board (LDB)
- **Feeder 3**: Power Distribution Board (PDB) for sockets
- **Feeder 4**: HVAC panel
- **Feeder 5**: Spare

#### **Part (b) — Standard Symbols Table (7 Marks)**

| Symbol | Component | Description |
|---|---|---|
| ⭕ (G) | Generator | Circle with letter G |
| ⊙⊙ | Transformer | Two interlocking circles |
| ⊠ | Circuit Breaker | Square with cross/X |
| ─●─ | Isolator | Single line with break and dot |
| △ | Delta Connection | Triangle |
| Y | Star Connection | Three lines meeting at a point |
| (M) | Motor | Circle with M |
| ⏚ | Earth | Three horizontal lines, decreasing |
| ↯ | Lightning Arrester | Inverted T with cross |
| ▭ | Bus Bar | Thick rectangle |
| ☐ | Relay | Square with identifier inside |
| ⊙ | CT | Circle with arrow |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Alert**
>
> Students commonly lose marks in the following ways:
> 1. **Forgetting the Neutral & Earth line** in the SLD — both are mandatory for a 3-phase, 4-wire system.
> 2. **Missing protection devices** — every outgoing feeder MUST have a breaker; failing to show this costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Wrong number of conductors in estimation** — remember Phase + Neutral + Switch wire + Earth = 4 conductors per switchboard point (not just 2).
> 4. **Not applying wastage factor** — raw length is incomplete; always multiply by (1 + Wf).
> 5. **Confusing 1-phase vs 3-phase formulas** — for 3-phase, use $\sqrt{3}$; for 1-phase, use just 1.
> 6. **Unit errors** — resistivity $\rho$ is in $\Omega \cdot mm^2 / m$, length in km, voltage in V. Mixing units is a common killer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and keep it as your last-minute revision sheet!**

- [ ] **SLD** stands for Single Line Diagram — a **1-line representation of 3-phase system** per **IS 2032 / IEC 60617**
- [ ] SLD always flows **left to right**: Source → Transformation → Distribution → Load
- [ ] Standard symbols: **Transformers = two interlocking circles**, **CB = square with X**, **Motor = circle with M**
- [ ] **Bus bar** in SLD is drawn with **thicker line** (typically 2× stroke width)
- [ ] **BOQ** = Bill of Quantities — basis of procurement and billing
- [ ] **Wastage factor**: Cables = **5–10%**, Conduits = **3–5%**
- [ ] **3-phase power formula**: $P = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_L \times \cos\phi$
- [ ] **Voltage drop formula**: $V_{drop} = \sqrt{3} \times I \times L \times (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$
- [ ] **Cable sizing formula**: $A = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \times \rho \times I \times L \times 1000}{V_d \times V_L}$
- [ ] **Resistivity** of Cu = **0.0172** $\Omega \cdot mm^2 / m$, Al = **0.0282** $\Omega \cdot mm^2 / m$
- [ ] **Wire length per point** = $(H + D)$ for switchboard wiring
- [ ] **Conductors per point** = Phase (1) + Neutral (1) + Switch wire (1) + Earth (1) = **4 wires**
- [ ] **Standard LT voltage** = **415 V, 3φ, 4W, 50 Hz** in India
- [ ] **Standard conduit sizes** = **20, 25, 32, 40, 50 mm**
- [ ] Always include **ELCB/RCD (30 mA)** for human safety protection
- [ ] **Percentage voltage drop limit** = **3% for lighting, 5% for motors** (as per IS 732)
- [ ] **PGD (Pipe Ground)** is shown with **3 decreasing horizontal lines** in SLD
- [ ] **Numeric relays** are the modern standard — they replace multiple electromechanical relays in one device
- [ ] SLD must show **CT ratios**, **PT ratios**, and **relay settings** in a tabular footnote
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical CAD (Optional)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrical CAD (Optional)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electrical CAD (ECAD)** — also referred to as **Electrical Computer-Aided Design** — is a specialized branch of computer-aided engineering that employs dedicated software platforms for the creation, modification, analysis, optimization, and documentation of **electrical schematic diagrams**, **wiring interconnection diagrams**, **control panel layouts**, **cable schedules**, and **bill of materials (BOM)** for power, control, and instrumentation systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** This is an **optional / supplementary topic** in Module 2. It is **not** part of the mandatory examination syllabus but is included to give students an industrial exposure to the digital tools used in modern electrical design offices. Questions from this section are typically **NOT** asked in the End Semester Examination (ESE), but may appear as **internal assignment / viva questions**.

### 1.2 Common ECAD Software Platforms

| Software | Developer | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|
| **AutoCAD Electrical** | Autodesk | General-purpose electrical schematic + panel layout |
| **EPLAN Electric P8** | EPLAN Software & Service | Large-scale industrial plant design |
| **SEE Electrical** | IGE+XAO (Schneider) | Industrial control panel schematics |
| **SolidWorks Electrical** | Dassault Systèmes | 3D electro-mechanical integration |
| **Promis-e** | Bentley Systems | Plant-level electrical design |
| **Electra E8** | Radica Software | Modular control panel design |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Digital Blueprinting Studio"**
>
> Imagine you are an architect designing a multi-storey building. Before computers existed, you would draw every wall, door, and pipe **by hand** on drafting tables. This was slow, error-prone, and very difficult to modify. Then came **AutoCAD**, which revolutionized architecture by digitizing drafting.
>
> **Electrical CAD works exactly the same way for electrical engineers.** Instead of drawing a motor starter circuit with a pencil on a paper, an engineer now drags a **pre-built contactor symbol** onto a digital canvas, connects wires automatically, and the software **auto-numbers** every wire, **auto-generates** the BOM, and **auto-checks** for errors. It is essentially the "**digital drafting table for the modern electrical design office**."

### 1.4 Why Electrical CAD is Different from Regular AutoCAD

This is a **critical conceptual distinction** that students often miss:

- **Regular AutoCAD (Mechanical CAD):** Designed for 2D/3D mechanical drawings. Has no concept of electrical components, wire numbers, or schematic logic.
- **Electrical CAD (ECAD):** Specifically engineered for **electrical engineering workflows**. It understands the **electrical connectivity model** — meaning the software "knows" that a wire from a contactor's terminal A1 must be **electrically continuous** with a push-button's terminal, even if the wire bends 5 times on the drawing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Reference:** The key industry benchmarks for ECAD software are:
> - **IEC 60617** — International standard for electrical symbols
> - **ANSI/IEEE 315 / 315A** — North American standard for electrical symbols
> - **NFPA 79** — Electrical standard for industrial machinery
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Core Features of Electrical CAD Software

Any professional ECAD platform provides the following **eight essential features**:

1. **Schematic Capture** — Drawing of electrical circuits using standardized symbols.
2. **Intelligent Symbol Library** — Pre-built IEC/ANSI symbols for contactors, relays, MCBs, MCCBs, timers, PLCs, etc.
3. **Automatic Wire Numbering** — Software auto-assigns unique wire numbers (e.g., `W1`, `W2`, `W3`) following predefined rules.
4. **Cross-Referencing** — Click on a contactor coil, and the software instantly shows you **wherever** its NO/NC contacts are drawn anywhere in the project.
5. **Component Tagging** — Automatic assignment of equipment tags (e.g., `K1`, `K2`, `Q1`, `F1`) per IEC/ANSI standards.
6. **Bill of Materials (BOM) Generation** — One-click extraction of every component, its quantity, part number, and description into a spreadsheet.
7. **Cable Schedule** — Auto-generated table listing every cable, its from/to location, conductor size, and length.
8. **Real-Time Error Checking** — Detects unconnected terminals, duplicate tags, and short circuits before fabrication.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Feature — Engineering Rationale

- **Schematic Capture** — *Why?* Because modern industrial panels can have **thousands of wires** drawn manually is impractical.
- **Auto Wire Numbering** — *Why?* Manual wire numbering in a 200-wire panel introduces **up to 15% transcription errors** (industry data). Automation reduces this to near zero.
- **Cross-Referencing** — *Why?* A relay coil `K1` might have **6 contacts** spread across 4 drawing pages. Manual tracking is impossible. ECAD links them in **milliseconds**.
- **BOM Generation** — *Why?* Procurement, costing, and panel fabrication all depend on an accurate **Bill of Materials**. Manual extraction is slow and error-prone.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since Electrical CAD is a **software/drafting topic**, the "formulas" are actually **numerical/quantitative parameters** that the design engineer configures inside the software. These are the values tested in viva/assignment:

| Parameter / Quantity | Standard Notation | Typical Industrial Value | Unit / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Minimum wire bend radius | $R_{min}$ | $4 \times d$ (where $d$ = wire diameter) | mm |
| Panel IP protection rating | $IP_{xx}$ | IP54 / IP65 for industrial | code (IEC 60529) |
| Minimum clearance between conductors | $d_{clear}$ | 10 mm (for 415 V industrial) | mm |
| Creepage distance | $d_{creep}$ | 25 mm (for 690 V, pollution degree 3) | mm |
| Number of wires per terminal | $n_{wire}$ | Maximum **2** (best practice) | count |
| Wire colour coding — Phase | R / Y / B | As per **IS 732** | colour code |
| Wire colour coding — Neutral | Black | As per **IS 732** | colour code |
| Wire colour coding — Earth | Green / Yellow | As per **IS 732** | colour code |
| Cable derating factor | $K_{derate}$ | 0.7 to 0.95 | dimensionless |
| Schematic sheet size (standard) | A0 / A1 / A2 / A3 / A4 | A1 most common for panels | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Memorize the **wire colour code (R-Y-B for phases, Black for Neutral, Green-Yellow for Earth)** — this is one of the most frequently tested viva questions from this optional topic.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Electrical CAD is the **backbone of modern electrical design offices** in the following industries:

- **Industrial Automation** — PLC panel design, MCC (Motor Control Centre) design
- **Power Distribution** — Switchgear, substation, and HT/LT panel design
- **Building Services** — Lighting, fire alarm, ELV, and BMS schematics
- **Automotive** — Vehicle wiring harness design (uses specialized tools like **Capital Harness** by Siemens)
- **Aerospace & Marine** — Aircraft/marine electrical system design with strict compliance to standards

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Fact:** A **single large industrial project** (e.g., a cement plant or refinery) can have **over 50,000 wires** and **8,000+ components** in its ECAD database. Without ECAD, such projects would take **years longer** and have **unacceptably high error rates**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Workflow & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Standard 10-Step Electrical CAD Design Workflow

This is the **exhaustive, professional-grade workflow** followed in any ECAD-based design office. Each step has a specific deliverable and tool action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important for KTU?** Even though this is an optional topic, viva examiners often ask: *"What is the workflow you follow when designing an electrical panel in ECAD?"* — knowing these 10 steps gives you full marks.

**Step 1 — Project Setup & Configuration**
- Define **project name**, **project number**, **drawing standard** (IEC / ANSI), **sheet size**, and **title block template**.
- Configure the **component database** (link to company's master parts library).
- Set up **wire numbering rules** (sequential, by function, by location, etc.).

**Step 2 — Load the Standardized Symbol Library**
- Load **IEC 60617** (European/international) or **ANSI/IEEE 315** (American) symbol libraries.
- Verify that symbols for contactors, MCBs, terminals, push-buttons, relays, and PLCs are available.

**Step 3 — Import Process & Instrumentation (P&ID) Data**
- In plant projects, the **P&ID** is the parent document. ECAD can **import tags** (e.g., motor tag `M-101`) from the P&ID to ensure consistency.

**Step 4 — Schematic Capture (Power Circuit)**
- Draw the **3-phase power circuit** (incomer → MCB → Contactor → Overload relay → Motor).
- Use **intelligent symbols** (not generic shapes) so the software understands electrical connectivity.

**Step 5 — Schematic Capture (Control Circuit)**
- Draw the **control circuit** (control transformer → push-buttons → contactor coil → auxiliary contacts → indicator lamps).
- The software **auto-creates wires** when you snap terminals together.

**Step 6 — Automatic Wire Numbering & Cross-Referencing**
- The engineer triggers a **"Renumber"** command.
- The software applies unique wire numbers following the configured rules.
- Cross-references (e.g., `/3` for page 3) are added next to contactor coil `K1` pointing to all its contacts.

**Step 7 — PLC I/O Design (If Applicable)**
- Add **PLC rack**, **DI/DO/AI/AO modules**.
- Map field devices to specific PLC I/O addresses (e.g., `I0.0`, `Q0.1`).
- ECAD auto-generates the **PLC I/O list**.

**Step 8 — Panel Layout Design**
- Switch from **schematic view** to **panel layout view**.
- Place physical components (MCBs, contactors, terminals, cable ducts) on the **back plate** of the panel.
- Apply **real-world dimensions** (e.g., MCB width = 17.5 mm per pole).

**Step 9 — Bill of Materials (BOM) & Cable Schedule Generation**
- Click **"Generate Reports"** → the software extracts:
  - **BOM** (component-wise list with quantities)
  - **Cable schedule** (cable tag, from, to, type, size, length)
  - **Terminal list** (terminal strip design)
  - **Wire list** (wire number, from, to, gauge, colour)

**Step 10 — Drawing Review, Plotting & Documentation Release**
- Run **electrical error check** (unconnected terminals, duplicate tags).
- Generate **PDF/DWG** drawings for **shop floor fabrication** and **client submission**.
- Issue drawing with revision number (e.g., `Rev 0`, `Rev 1`).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of ECAD vs Manual Drafting

| Engineering Parameter | Manual Drafting | Electrical CAD | Improvement Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Time to draw a 200-wire panel | $\approx 80$ hours | $\approx 12$ hours | $\approx 6.7\times$ faster |
| Wire numbering accuracy | $\approx 85\%$ | $\approx 99.9\%$ | $1.18\times$ |
| Modification time (single change) | $\approx 4$ hours | $\approx 15$ minutes | $16\times$ faster |
| BOM generation time | $\approx 6$ hours | $\approx 30$ seconds | $720\times$ faster |
| Cross-referencing accuracy | Manual & error-prone | Automatic & $100\%$ | qualitative leap |
| Drawing revision control | Manual annotations | Automated revision tracking | qualitative leap |
| Storage of completed project | Physical cupboards | Cloud/local server | qualitative leap |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Example: Wire Numbering Rule Application

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example Problem** (typical viva/assignment type)
>
> *"In an AutoCAD Electrical project, the engineer has set the wire numbering format as:*

$$
\text{Wire Tag} = \text{[Project Code]} - \text{[Page No] / [Sequence No]}
$$

*Given:*
- Project Code = `P-4501`
- Sheet No = `07`
- The software encounters 12 wires on this page in sequence
- *Determine the wire tag of the 9th wire on page 7.*"

**Solution (exhaustive step-by-step):**

**Step 1 — Identify the variables:**

$$\text{Project Code} = P\text{-}4501, \quad \text{Page No} = 07, \quad \text{Sequence No} = 9$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the wire tag formula:**

$$\text{Wire Tag} = \text{[Project Code]} - \text{[Page No] / [Sequence No]}$$

**Step 3 — Plug in the numerical values:**

$$\text{Wire Tag} = P\text{-}4501 - 07 / 9$$

**Step 4 — State the final answer with proper formatting:**

$$\boxed{\text{Wire Tag} = P\text{-}4501 \text{-} 07/9}$$

This tag uniquely identifies the 9th wire on page 7 of project P-4501 across the **entire multi-page project**, eliminating ambiguity in panel fabrication.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Example: Panel Layout Density Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
> *"A 1200 mm (height) × 800 mm (width) panel back-plate is to be fitted with 18 MCBs. Each MCB has a width of 17.5 mm (single-pole) and requires a minimum horizontal clearance of 10 mm on either side. Determine if all 18 MCBs fit on a single horizontal row."*

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate the effective width per MCB including clearances:**

$$w_{effective} = w_{MCB} + 2 \times c_{clear}$$

where $c_{clear}$ is the clearance on one side.

$$w_{effective} = 17.5 + 2 \times 10 = 37.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the total width required for 18 MCBs in one row:**

$$W_{total} = n \times w_{effective}$$

$$W_{total} = 18 \times 37.5 = 675 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with available panel width:**

$$W_{total} = 675 \text{ mm} \quad \text{vs.} \quad W_{available} = 800 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

Since $675 \text{ mm} < 800 \text{ mm}$, the 18 MCBs **fit comfortably on a single horizontal row**, with a remaining margin of:

$$W_{margin} = 800 - 675 = 125 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{\text{All 18 MCBs fit on a single row, with 125 mm margin remaining.}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: ECAD-Based Electrical Design Workflow

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the complete professional workflow from project kickoff to final documentation release, showing how the **8 core ECAD features** integrate into the design process.

```mermaid
    step10[Step 10: Drawing Review and Documentation Release]
    deliverable1[Deliverable: Power Schematic Drawing]

    deliverable5[Deliverable: Cable Schedule Excel]
    deliverable6[Deliverable: As Built PDF Package]
    step1 --> step2
    step2 --> step3
    step3 --> step4
    step4 --> step5
    step5 --> step6

    step7 --> step8
    step8 --> step9

```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Internal Architecture of an ECAD Software Platform

This diagram explains the **internal data model** of an ECAD system — i.e., how a "wire" on screen is actually a database object with multiple attributes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    userInput[User Action: Drag and Drop Symbol]
    symbolDB[(Symbol Library Database)]
    wireDB[(Wire Connectivity Database)]
    compDB[(Component Database)]

    userInput --> symbolDB
    symbolDB --> graphicsLayer

    compDB --> reportEngine

```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Comparison Architecture — Manual Drafting vs ECAD

This diagram contrasts the **linear, error-prone manual workflow** with the **iterative, automated ECAD workflow**, making the qualitative leap in productivity visually obvious.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Manual_Drafting_Workflow
        m1[Hand Draw Symbol] --> m2[Hand Write Wire Number]
        m2 --> m3[Manual Cross Reference Lookup]

    end
    subgraph ECAD_Workflow
        e1[Drag Intelligent Symbol] --> e2[Auto Wire Numbering]
```
