The CoU is therefore a dimensionless efficiency number, always lying between **0 and 1** (or expressed as a percentage between **0 % and 100 %**). It tells the lighting designer how efficiently the lumens *generated by the lamp* are *actually utilised* at the task surface.

> [!NOTE]
> In many international standards (CIBSE, IS 3646), CoU is also known as the **Utilization Factor (UF)**. For KTU 2024 scheme examination purposes, both terms are treated as synonymous.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Water Bucket Story"

Imagine you are filling a bucket (the **working plane**) using a hosepipe (the **luminaire emitting light**). 

- The **total water sprayed by the pipe** is the lumens emitted by the lamps.
- The **water that finally lands in the bucket** is the lumens reaching the working plane.
- Some water splashes outside, some evaporates (absorbed by dark walls, lost in space above ceiling, lost below the working plane).

The fraction of water that *actually fills the bucket* is exactly the **Coefficient of Utilisation**. A poorly designed room with dark walls and a very high roof is like a bucket placed far away from the hose — most of the water misses the bucket. Hence the **CoU is small**. A bright room with light-coloured walls and a properly proportioned height is like placing the bucket closer to the hose — most of the water lands inside. The **CoU is large**.

### 1.3 Light Loss Factor (LLF) — The Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Light Loss Factor (LLF)** is defined as the ratio of the illumination on the working plane **after a specified period of use** to the **initial illumination** provided when the installation was brand new.

Equivalently:

$$\text{Light Loss Factor (LLF)} = \frac{\text{Illumination at any later time}}{\text{Illumination when installation was new}}$$

In older British and Indian Standards, the same quantity is often called the **Maintenance Factor (MF)**. The KTU syllabus treats these as equivalent.

> [!NOTE]
> LLF is **always less than 1** (typically 0.5 to 0.8) because every lighting installation suffers depreciation with time — lamps dim, dust accumulates, and surfaces yellow.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The Dirty Window Story"

Think of a window that was crystal clear when newly installed. Over months:
- **Dust settles** on the glass (Luminaire Dirt Depreciation).
- **The glass itself** develops a slight haze (Lamp Lumen Depreciation as filament ages).
- Some panes may even **crack or be removed** (Lamp Burnout Factor).

The fraction of daylight still passing through this older, dirtier window compared to the day it was installed is the **Light Loss Factor**. The lighting designer must therefore **over-design** the initial installation so that even after this depreciation, the illumination remains above the required service value.

### 1.5 Why These Two Factors are the Heart of Lighting Design

In the standard lumen method:

$$\text{Number of Lamps / Luminaires} = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi \times n \times \text{CU} \times \text{LLF}}$$

Where:
- $E$ = Required illumination (lux)
- $A$ = Area of working plane (m²)
- $\Phi$ = Luminous flux per lamp (lumens)
- $n$ = Number of lamps per luminaire
- $\text{CU}$ = Coefficient of Utilisation
- $\text{LLF}$ = Light Loss Factor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus places **heavy weightage** on CU and LLF because a single mistake in either factor can shift the number of luminaires by **20 – 40 %**, directly affecting contract cost and energy bills.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Factors That Influence the Coefficient of Utilisation

The CU is determined by **four fundamental physical factors**. Mastering these four allows a designer to maximise CoU without spending extra money on more lamps.

#### (a) Luminous Intensity Distribution Curve of the Luminaire

The way in which the luminaire throws its light — upward, downward, sideways — decides what fraction reaches the working plane after multiple reflections.

| Luminaire Type | Light Distribution | Typical CU Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct** (≥ 90 % light downward) | Almost all light goes straight to the working plane | **0.55 – 0.75** |
| **Semi-direct** (60 – 90 % downward) | Mostly downward, some upward for ceiling reflection | **0.50 – 0.70** |
| **General Diffuser** (40 – 60 % each way) | Balanced up and down | **0.40 – 0.60** |
| **Semi-indirect** (60 – 90 % upward) | Light goes to ceiling first, then reflected | **0.35 – 0.55** |
| **Indirect** (≥ 90 % upward) | All light reflects from ceiling | **0.25 – 0.45** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Direct luminaires give the highest CU** because photons travel the shortest path to the task. Indirect luminaires look beautiful and reduce glare, but their CU is low because every lumen must bounce off the ceiling at least once, losing energy at each reflection.

#### (b) Mounting Height ($H_m$) and Room Proportions — The Room Index

The **Room Index (RI)** is the single most influential parameter for CU.

$$\boxed{\text{Room Index (RI)} = \frac{L \times W}{H_m \times (L + W)}}$$

Where:
- $L$ = Length of the room (metres)
- $W$ = Width of the room (metres)
- $H_m$ = Mounting height of the luminaire **above the working plane** (metres), i.e. $H_m = H - h_w$, where $H$ = height from working plane to ceiling, and $h_w$ = height of working plane above floor.

| Room Category | RI Value | Practical Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Very narrow / very tall | RI < 0.5 | Stairwell, lift shaft |
| Narrow / tall | 0.5 ≤ RI < 0.75 | Corridor |
| Average office | 0.75 ≤ RI < 1.5 | Typical classroom |
| Square / well-proportioned | 1.5 ≤ RI < 2.5 | Drawing hall |
| Very wide / shallow | RI ≥ 2.5 | Auditorium, open hall |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A high Room Index gives a high CU** because light has a relatively shorter distance to travel between ceiling and floor compared to the floor area it must cover.

Some American IES literature uses the **Room Cavity Ratio (RCR)** which is the reciprocal family of RI:

$$\text{RCR} = \frac{5 \times H_m \times (L + W)}{L \times W} = \frac{5}{\text{RI}}$$

For KTU 2024 problems, **RI is preferred**.

#### (c) Reflection Factors of Ceiling, Walls and Floor

Reflection factor ($\rho$) is the ratio of luminous flux reflected by a surface to the luminous flux incident upon it. Multi-bounce light only reaches the working plane if the ceiling and walls are good reflectors.

| Surface | Typical Reflection Factor ($\rho$) |
|---|---|
| White painted ceiling | **0.70 – 0.80** |
| Light-cream ceiling | **0.60 – 0.70** |
| Unpainted concrete ceiling | **0.30 – 0.40** |
| Light yellow walls | **0.50 – 0.60** |
| Medium grey walls | **0.20 – 0.30** |
| Dark wood floor | **0.10 – 0.20** |
| Light polished floor | **0.30 – 0.40** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Painting a dingy ceiling white can increase CU by 15 – 25 %** — often more cost-effective than buying extra luminaires.

#### (d) Shape and Proportions of the Room

- **Square vs long rectangular**: A perfectly square room maximises CU for a given area because the average wall reflection path is shortest.
- **L-shape / T-shape rooms**: Have a lower effective CU because corners receive less reflected light.
- **Obstructions** (pillars, beams, large machinery): These block light and effectively reduce CU by absorbing flux.

### 2.2 Factors That Influence the Light Loss Factor

LLF is the product of several individual depreciation factors:

$$\boxed{\text{LLF} = \text{LLD} \times \text{LDD} \times \text{BOF} \times \text{BF} \times \text{ATF}}$$

Where each factor ≤ 1.

#### (a) Lamp Lumen Depreciation (LLD)

As a lamp ages, the luminous flux it produces drops continuously.

- **Incandescent**: Drops fast — by end of life may be at 80 – 85 %.
- **Fluorescent (T8/T5)**: After 70 % of rated life, output may be 80 %.
- **LED**: Very slow depreciation, typically 70 – 80 % at end of life (L70 / L80 rating).
- **HID (HPMV / HPSV)**: Significant depreciation by 50 % of life.

| Lamp Type | Typical LLD after 70 % of life |
|---|---|
| Incandescent | **0.85** |
| Fluorescent | **0.80 – 0.85** |
| Mercury Vapour (HPMV) | **0.75 – 0.80** |
| Sodium Vapour (HPSV) | **0.85 – 0.90** |
| Metal Halide | **0.75 – 0.80** |
| LED | **0.90 – 0.95** |

#### (b) Luminaire Dirt Depreciation (LDD)

Dust accumulation on the luminaire reflector and diffuser reduces light transmission. **LDD depends on:**
1. **Ambient dirtiness** (clean office vs foundry).
2. **Luminaire design** (open / enclosed / sealed).
3. **Cleaning interval** (monthly / quarterly / never).
4. **Time elapsed since last cleaning**.

| Environment | LDD (Monthly Cleaning) |
|---|---|
| Clean office | **0.90 – 0.95** |
| Industrial (moderate dust) | **0.80 – 0.90** |
| Foundry / cement plant | **0.60 – 0.75** |

#### (c) Burnout Factor (BOF)

Some lamps may fail completely before group replacement is scheduled. With multiple lamps per luminaire, one dead lamp still leaves the luminaire operating but at reduced output.

$$\text{BOF} = \frac{\text{Number of lamps surviving}}{\text{Number originally installed}} \quad (\text{per luminaire})$$

For a 4-lamp luminaire with 1 expected burnout before re-lamping:
$$\text{BOF} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75$$

#### (d) Ballast Factor (BF)

Modern electronic ballasts deliver a slightly different lamp wattage than the rated value.

- **Conventional copper-iron ballast**: 0.95 – 1.00
- **Electronic ballast (HPF)**: 1.00 – 1.15
- **Low-loss ballast**: 1.00 – 1.05

#### (e) Ambient Temperature Factor (ATF)

Fluorescent and HID lamps have a strong temperature dependence. Lamp output peaks at around 25 – 30 °C ambient and falls off in hot factories or cold outdoor installations.

- Typical ATF range = **0.90 – 1.05**.

#### (f) Supply Voltage Factor

A 5 % over-voltage reduces lamp life by ~50 % and slightly raises output; under-voltage dims lamps. Designers usually assume rated voltage, so this factor = **1.00**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table consolidates every formula needed for KTU 2024 Module 2 numericals. Memorise this before attempting any exam question on lighting design.**

| **Formula / Quantity** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Units / Typical Range** |
|---|---|---|
| Coefficient of Utilisation | $\text{CU} = \dfrac{\Phi_{\text{working plane}}}{\Phi_{\text{lamp}}}$ | Dimensionless; **0.25 to 0.75** |
| Room Index | $\text{RI} = \dfrac{L \times W}{H_m \,(L + W)}$ | Dimensionless; **0.5 to 5.0** |
| Mounting Height | $H_m = H - h_w$ | Metres |
| Room Cavity Ratio | $\text{RCR} = \dfrac{5 \, H_m \,(L + W)}{L \times W} = \dfrac{5}{\text{RI}}$ | Dimensionless |
| Reflection Factor | $\rho = \dfrac{\Phi_{\text{reflected}}}{\Phi_{\text{incident}}}$ | **0 to 1** |
| Light Loss Factor | $\text{LLF} = \text{LLD} \times \text{LDD} \times \text{BOF} \times \text{BF} \times \text{ATF}$ | Dimensionless; **0.5 to 0.9** |
| Number of Lamps | $N = \dfrac{E \times A}{\Phi \times n \times \text{CU} \times \text{LLF}}$ | Whole number (round up) |
| Total Lumens Required | $\Phi_{\text{total req}} = \dfrac{E \times A}{\text{CU} \times \text{LLF}}$ | Lumens |
| Area of Working Plane | $A = L \times W$ | m² |
| Lamp Spacing to Height Ratio | $\text{SHR} = \dfrac{\text{Spacing}}{\text{Mounting Height}}$ | **1.0 to 1.5 typical** |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Energy audit and retrofit projects**: An auditor who finds an installation with dark walls and dirty luminaires can recommend a low-cost cleaning and re-painting programme — the improvement in CU and LLF can deliver 30 % energy savings without changing a single lamp.
- **Smart building design**: Modern BIM software (Revit, Dialux) uses these exact factors in their photometric engines to render 3-D lighting simulations.
- **Green building rating** (GRIHA, LEED): Mandatory calculation of CU and LLF is required to claim credit for lighting efficiency.
- **Industrial safety**: In hazardous areas, lower LLF means higher initial illumination must be designed — directly affecting safe escape-route visibility.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Working Plane Illumination Equation

Consider a room of area $A$ square metres. We require an **average illumination** $E$ (lux = lumens/m²) on the working plane.

**Total lumens needed on the working plane:**

$$\Phi_{\text{needed on WP}} = E \times A \quad \text{(lux)} \times \text{(m²)} = \text{lumens}$$

This lumens-on-working-plane must come from the lamps. However, only a fraction **CU** of the lamp lumens actually reaches the working plane. Therefore:

$$\Phi_{\text{lamp,total}} \times \text{CU} = \Phi_{\text{needed on WP}}$$

Re-arranging:

$$\Phi_{\text{lamp,total}} = \frac{E \times A}{\text{CU}}$$

If each lamp gives $\Phi$ lumens and each luminaire holds $n$ lamps, the number of luminaires required to deliver this flux **at the moment of installation** is:

$$N_{\text{initial}} = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi \times n \times \text{CU}}$$

Over time, the system suffers losses captured by LLF. So the **steady-state** requirement becomes:

$$N_{\text{final}} = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi \times n \times \text{CU} \times \text{LLF}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **standard lumen method formula** — the heart of KTU Module 2 numerical problems.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Example #1 — Room Index Computation

> **Question:** A classroom measures 12 m × 8 m with ceiling height 3.6 m. The working plane is 0.8 m above floor. Luminaires are surface-mounted at ceiling level. Determine the Room Index.

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry.**
Length $L = 12$ m, Width $W = 8$ m.

**Step 2 — Compute mounting height above working plane.**

$$H_m = H - h_w = 3.6 - 0.8 = 2.8 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the RI formula.**

$$\text{RI} = \frac{L \times W}{H_m \times (L + W)}$$

$$\text{RI} = \frac{12 \times 8}{2.8 \times (12 + 8)}$$

$$\text{RI} = \frac{96}{2.8 \times 20}$$

$$\text{RI} = \frac{96}{56} = 1.714$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**

Since $1.5 \le \text{RI} < 2.5$, this is a **well-proportioned square-type room**. The designer can expect a relatively **high CU** (typically 0.60 – 0.70 for direct luminaires with light-coloured ceiling).

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example #2 — Combined LLF Calculation

> **Question:** A factory uses 250 W HPSV lamps. From the manufacturer's data: LLD = 0.85, expected LDD = 0.80, BOF = 0.90 (single-lamp luminaires), BF = 1.00, ATF = 0.95. Compute the LLF.

**Step 1 — Write the LLF product formula.**

$$\text{LLF} = \text{LLD} \times \text{LDD} \times \text{BOF} \times \text{BF} \times \text{ATF}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values one by one.**

$$\text{LLF} = 0.85 \times 0.80$$

$$\text{LLF} = 0.680$$

**Step 3 — Continue the multiplication.**

$$\text{LLF} = 0.680 \times 0.90 = 0.6120$$

**Step 4 — Apply BF and ATF.**

$$\text{LLF} = 0.6120 \times 1.00 \times 0.95$$

$$\text{LLF} = 0.5814$$

**Step 5 — Express as percentage for clarity.**

$$\text{LLF} = 0.5814 \approx 58.1\,\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This means the installation will, after depreciation, deliver only about **58 %** of its initial light output. The designer must install **1 / 0.5814 ≈ 1.72 times** more lumens than a "perfect" no-loss scenario.

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Worked Example #3 — Full Sizing Calculation (KTU 2024 Pattern)

> **Question:** A workshop 20 m long × 10 m wide × 4.5 m high requires 200 lux on a working plane at 0.85 m above the floor. Two-lamp fluorescent luminaires are to be used, each lamp rated at 2800 lumens. From the photometric data sheet, CU = 0.55 and LLF = 0.70. Determine the number of luminaires and their layout.

**Step 1 — Compute the working plane area.**

$$A = L \times W = 20 \times 10 = 200 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute mounting height above working plane.**

$$H_m = 4.5 - 0.85 = 3.65 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the total lumens required on the working plane.**

$$E \times A = 200 \times 200 = 40{,}000 \text{ lumens}$$

**Step 4 — Apply CU and LLF to find total lamp lumens required.**

$$\Phi_{\text{total lamp}} = \frac{E \times A}{\text{CU} \times \text{LLF}} = \frac{40{,}000}{0.55 \times 0.70}$$

$$\Phi_{\text{total lamp}} = \frac{40{,}000}{0.385} = 103{,}896 \text{ lumens}$$

**Step 5 — Compute number of lamps.**

$$\text{Number of lamps} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{total lamp}}}{\Phi_{\text{per lamp}}} = \frac{103{,}896}{2800} = 37.1$$

**Step 6 — Convert to number of luminaires (2 lamps per luminaire).**

$$\text{Number of luminaires} = \frac{37.1}{2} = 18.55 \approx 19 \text{ luminaires}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Always **round up** to the next whole luminaire — never round down, because under-design violates the service illuminance.

**Step 7 — Layout of 19 luminaires in the 20 × 10 m room.**

Choose 4 rows × 5 columns = 20 luminaires (round to nearest grid). 

Row spacing = 20 / 4 = 5.0 m (or for 5 rows, 10 / 5 = 2.0 m — too close).

Better layout: **4 rows of 5 luminaires**, evenly spaced.

- Spacing along length = 20 / 5 = 4.0 m
- Spacing along width = 10 / 4 = 2.5 m

Spacing-to-Height ratio check:

$$\text{SHR}_{\text{across width}} = \frac{2.5}{3.65} = 0.68 \quad \text{(too low)}$$

$$\text{SHR}_{\text{along length}} = \frac{4.0}{3.65} = 1.10 \quad \text{(acceptable)}$$

Refine: Use **5 rows × 4 columns = 20 luminaires** (rotated grid):
- Spacing along length = 20 / 4 = 5.0 m → SHR = 5.0 / 3.65 = 1.37 ✓
- Spacing along width = 10 / 5 = 2.0 m → SHR = 2.0 / 3.65 = 0.55 (low)

> [!WARNING]
> When SHR falls below 1.0, **dark spots** appear between luminaires. Either reduce mounting height, increase luminaire rows, or switch to higher-output lamps.

### 3.5 Sensitivity Analysis Matrix — Effect of Each Factor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix below is a **typical KTU question type** — "Discuss how each factor affects the CU and LLF." Use this exact format in your answer.

| **Factor** | **Effect on CU** | **Effect on LLF** | **Mitigation Strategy** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Higher mounting height | **Decreases** (light spreads farther) | No direct effect | Use higher-output lamps; narrower beam luminaires |
| Light-coloured ceiling (high $\rho_c$) | **Increases** significantly | No direct effect | Paint ceilings white |
| Dirty luminaire | No direct effect | **Decreases** strongly | Periodic cleaning schedule |
| Old aged lamps | No direct effect | **Decreases** (LLD) | Group re-lamping at 70 – 80 % of rated life |
| Indirect luminaire | **Decreases** | No direct effect | Use only where aesthetics/glare demand |
| Square-shaped room | **Increases** | No direct effect | Avoid L-shaped or very long rooms |
| Higher room index | **Increases** | No direct effect | Reduce mounting height if possible |
| High ambient temperature | No direct effect | **Decreases** (ATF) | Use temperature-rated luminaires; ventilation |
| Electronic ballast | No direct effect | Slightly **increases** (BF > 1) | Replace copper-iron ballasts |
| Higher lamp lumen output | No direct effect | No effect | Use higher-wattage / high-efficiency lamps |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Map — Inter-relationships Between CU, LLF and Final Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Lamp Luminosity Output phi] --> B[Luminaire Distribution Curve]
    B --> C[Mounting Height H_m]
    C --> D[Room Index RI Calculation]
    D --> E[Ceiling rho_c]
    D --> F[Wall rho_w]
    D --> G[Floor rho_f]
    E --> H[Coefficient of Utilisation CU]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    B --> H
    H --> I[Standard Lumen Method Formula]
    J[Lamp Lumen Depreciation LLD] --> K[Light Loss Factor LLF]
    L[Luminaire Dirt Depreciation LDD] --> K
    M[Burnout Factor BOF] --> K
    N[Ballast Factor BF] --> K
    O[Ambient Temp Factor ATF] --> K
    K --> I
    I --> P[Number of Luminaires N]
    P --> Q[Final Lighting Layout]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Standard Lumen Method Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start Design]) --> S1[Step 1: Gather Room Dimensions L W H]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2: Determine Working Plane Height h_w]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Calculate Mounting Height H_m]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Compute Room Index RI]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Choose Luminaire Type]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Read CU from Photometric Table]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Estimate LLF Components]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Apply Standard Lumen Formula N = EA / phi CU LLF]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Round Up to Integer]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Layout Luminaires]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11: Verify Spacing Height Ratio]
    S11 --> Check{SHR between 1.0 and 1.5?}
    Check -->|Yes| End([Final Design Approved])
    Check -->|No| Adjust[Modify Layout or Lamp Choice]
    Adjust --> S10
```

### 4.3 Hierarchical Decomposition of LLF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LLF[Light Loss Factor LLF] --> LLD[Lamp Lumen Depreciation]
    LLF --> LDD[Luminaire Dirt Depreciation]
    LLF --> BOF[Burnout Factor]
    LLF --> BF[Ballast Factor]
    LLF --> ATF[Ambient Temperature Factor]
    LLD --> LLD1[Incandescent 0.85]
    LLD --> LLD2[Fluorescent 0.80]
    LLD --> LLD3[LED 0.90 to 0.95]
    LLD --> LLD4[HID 0.75 to 0.85]
    LDD --> LDD1[Clean Office 0.95]
    LDD --> LDD2[Industrial 0.85]
    LDD --> LDD3[Dirty Factory 0.65]
    BF --> BF1[Copper Iron 1.00]
    BF --> BF2[Electronic 1.05]
```

### 4.4 Reflectance Behaviour Diagram — Multiple Bounce Paths

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Lamp[Lamp emits Lumens phi] -->|Direct Path| WP[Working Plane]
    Lamp -->|Upward Emission| Ceil[Ceiling rho_c]
    Ceil -->|Reflected Down| WP
    Lamp -->|Sideways Emission| Wall[Wall rho_w]
    Wall -->|First Bounce| WP
    Wall -->|First Bounce| Ceil
    Ceil -->|Second Bounce| WP
    Wall -->|First Bounce| Floor[Floor rho_f]
    Floor -->|Reflected Up| WP
    WP -->|Absorbed| Abs[(Absorbed Lumens)]
    Ceil -->|Absorbed| Abs
    Wall -->|Absorbed| Abs
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Define Coefficient of Utilisation. List any four factors that affect its value.**

**Model Answer:**
Coefficient of Utilisation (CU) is the ratio of the total luminous flux reaching the working plane to the total luminous flux emitted by the lamps.

$$\text{CU} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{working plane}}}{\Phi_{\text{lamp}}}$$

The four factors affecting CU are:
1. **Luminous intensity distribution** of the luminaire (direct, indirect, etc.).
2. **Mounting height** and room proportions (captured by Room Index).
3. **Reflection factors** of ceiling, walls and floor.
4. **Shape of the room** and presence of obstructions.

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**What is Light Loss Factor? Mention any three individual factors that contribute to LLF.**

**Model Answer:**
Light Loss Factor (LLF) is the ratio of illumination at any given later time to the illumination at the time of installation. It accounts for all losses occurring between initial commissioning and routine operation.

$$\text{LLF} = \frac{E_{\text{after time } t}}{E_{\text{new}}}$$

Three contributing factors:
1. **Lamp Lumen Depreciation (LLD)** — reduction in lamp output due to ageing.
2. **Luminaire Dirt Depreciation (LDD)** — loss due to dust accumulation on the fitting.
3. **Burnout Factor (BOF)** — fraction of lamps surviving before group replacement.

*(Alternative valid answers: Ballast Factor, Ambient Temperature Factor.)*

---

### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Pattern 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the inter-relationship between **Room Index**, **mounting height** and **Coefficient of Utilisation**. A room 18 m × 9 m × 4.2 m (working plane at 0.8 m) is to be illuminated using direct-type fluorescent luminaires. Determine the Room Index and comment on the suitability of the room for high CU. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An office 15 m × 8 m × 3.6 m has a working plane at 0.85 m. The required illumination is 400 lux. Each luminaire carries 3 LED tubes, each rated at 3000 lumens. From the photometric table for RI = 1.50 and ceiling/wall reflectances 0.7 / 0.5, CU = 0.62. Assume LLF = 0.75. Determine the number of luminaires required. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to A(a):**

**Step 1 — Mounting height above working plane.**

$$H_m = H - h_w = 4.2 - 0.8 = 3.4 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Room Index.**

$$\text{RI} = \frac{L \times W}{H_m \times (L + W)} = \frac{18 \times 9}{3.4 \times (18 + 9)} = \frac{162}{3.4 \times 27} = \frac{162}{91.8} = 1.765$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.**

Since $1.5 \le \text{RI} = 1.765 < 2.5$, the room is **well-proportioned and square-type**, suitable for direct luminaires. A **high CU (typically 0.60 – 0.70)** is expected.

**Step 4 — Diagram (description).**

A neat sketch should show:
- The room rectangle with $L = 18$ m, $W = 9$ m.
- The ceiling plane and working plane separated by $H_m = 3.4$ m.
- Arrows showing direct, ceiling-reflected, and wall-reflected flux paths.
- Labels of reflection factors on each surface.

---

**Solution to A(b):**

**Step 1 — Area of working plane.**

$$A = L \times W = 15 \times 8 = 120 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Total lumens required on working plane.**

$$\Phi_{\text{WP needed}} = E \times A = 400 \times 120 = 48{,}000 \text{ lumens}$$

**Step 3 — Total lamp lumens required (with CU and LLF).**

$$\Phi_{\text{lamp}} = \frac{E \times A}{\text{CU} \times \text{LLF}} = \frac{48{,}000}{0.62 \times 0.75} = \frac{48{,}000}{0.465} = 103{,}226 \text{ lumens}$$

**Step 4 — Number of lamps (each LED tube = 3000 lumens).**

$$\text{Lamps} = \frac{103{,}226}{3000} = 34.41 \approx 35 \text{ lamps}$$

**Step 5 — Number of luminaires (3 tubes per luminaire).**

$$\text{Luminaires} = \frac{35}{3} = 11.67 \approx 12 \text{ luminaires}$$

**Step 6 — Layout.** 12 luminaires in a 4 × 3 grid: Spacing along length = 15/4 = 3.75 m; Spacing along width = 8/3 = 2.67 m. Acceptable for direct LED luminaires.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Pattern 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various factors contributing to the **Light Loss Factor (LLF)** with their typical values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A factory hall 30 m × 15 m × 6 m requires an illumination of 250 lux on a working plane 1 m above floor. 400 W metal halide lamps are used, each rated at 32,000 lumens. Luminaires carry one lamp each. The CU from the table for the appropriate RI and reflectances (ceiling 0.5, wall 0.3) is 0.58. The components of LLF are LLD = 0.80, LDD = 0.75, BOF = 1.00, BF = 1.05 and ATF = 0.95. Determine: (i) Total LLF; (ii) Number of luminaires; (iii) A suitable layout. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to B(a):**

**Light Loss Factor (LLF)** is the ratio of illumination after depreciation to the initial illumination. It is the product of individual factors:

**(i) Lamp Lumen Depreciation (LLD)** — age-related reduction of lamp output.
- Incandescent ≈ 0.85; Fluorescent ≈ 0.80; HPSV ≈ 0.88; Metal halide ≈ 0.78; LED ≈ 0.90 – 0.95.

**(ii) Luminaire Dirt Depreciation (LDD)** — dust on reflector/diffuser.
- Clean office ≈ 0.90 – 0.95; Industrial ≈ 0.80; Dirty factory ≈ 0.60 – 0.75.

**(iii) Burnout Factor (BOF)** — surviving lamp fraction.
- For single-lamp luminaires BOF = 1.00; for 4-lamp luminaire with 1 expected burnout BOF = 0.75.

**(iv) Ballast Factor (BF)** — actual vs rated lamp wattage.
- Copper-iron ≈ 0.95 – 1.00; Electronic ≈ 1.00 – 1.15.

**(v) Ambient Temperature Factor (ATF)** — temperature sensitivity.
- Typical ≈ 0.90 – 1.05; unity at 25 °C.

**(vi) Voltage Variation Factor** — usually unity at rated voltage.

The product of all these gives the LLF used in the lumen method.

---

**Solution to B(b):**

**(i) Total LLF.**

$$\text{LLF} = \text{LLD} \times \text{LDD} \times \text{BOF} \times \text{BF} \times \text{ATF}$$

$$\text{LLF} = 0.80 \times 0.75 \times 1.00 \times 1.05 \times 0.95$$

$$\text{LLF} = 0.80 \times 0.75 = 0.6000$$

$$\text{LLF} = 0.6000 \times 1.00 = 0.6000$$

$$\text{LLF} = 0.6000 \times 1.05 = 0.6300$$

$$\text{LLF} = 0.6300 \times 0.95 = 0.5985$$

$$\text{LLF} \approx 0.60$$

**(ii) Number of luminaires.**

Area: $A = 30 \times 15 = 450 \text{ m}^2$.

Total lumens needed on WP = $250 \times 450 = 112{,}500$ lumens.

Total lamp lumens = $\dfrac{112{,}500}{0.58 \times 0.60} = \dfrac{112{,}500}{0.348} = 323{,}276$ lumens.

Number of lamps (each 32,000 lm) = $\dfrac{323{,}276}{32{,}000} = 10.10$.

Round up to **11 luminaires** (each with 1 metal halide lamp).

**(iii) Suitable Layout.** With 11 luminaires, use a 6 × 2 grid (12 luminaires, rounding to nearest grid):
- Spacing along length = 30 / 6 = 5.0 m.
- Spacing along width = 15 / 2 = 7.5 m.
- Mounting height above WP = $6 - 1 = 5$ m.
- SHR along length = 5.0 / 5.0 = 1.00 ✓
- SHR across width = 7.5 / 5.0 = 1.50 (acceptable for high-bay industrial).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert RI conditions** — many students read CU from the wrong row of the photometric table, picking RI = 1.0 when actual RI = 2.0. This shifts CU by 10 – 20 % and inflates the luminaire count wrongly.
> 2. **Confusing CU with LLF** — CU is a *design* factor chosen from the photometric table; LLF is a *depreciation* factor computed from operating conditions. Mixing them up leads to nonsensical answers.
> 3. **Rounding down the number of luminaires** — KTU strict valuation: always round **up**. If your answer is 18.4, write 19. Writing 18 loses one mark.
> 4. **Forgetting to subtract working plane height** — mounting height is above WP, not above the floor. Using total ceiling height directly inflates RI wrongly.
> 5. **Ignoring SHR** — a numerically correct luminaire count but with bad SHR loses credit in the layout part of the question.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam**

- **CU is dimensionless**, between 0.25 and 0.75; pick it from the photometric table based on **Room Index** and **reflectances**.
- **Room Index formula** $\text{RI} = \dfrac{L \times W}{H_m (L + W)}$ — must use mounting height **above the working plane**, not above the floor.
- **Reflection factors** typically: ceiling 0.7, walls 0.5, floor 0.2 — for clean indoor spaces.
- **Direct luminaires → high CU; Indirect → low CU** but better glare control.
- **Light walls, ceiling, and short mounting heights → high CU.**
- **LLF = LLD × LDD × BOF × BF × ATF** — always multiply, never add.
- **LLD** depends on lamp type and age; LED has the highest LLD.
- **LDD** depends on ambient dirtiness and cleaning interval — dirty factories can have LDD as low as 0.60.
- **BOF** < 1 only for multi-lamp luminaires where some lamps may burn out.
- **BF** for electronic ballasts > 1 (around 1.05).
- **Standard Lumen Method formula**: $\; N = \dfrac{E \times A}{\Phi \times n \times \text{CU} \times \text{LLF}}\;$ — the most-tested equation in KTU Module 2.
- **Always round up** the number of luminaires to the next integer.
- **Spacing-to-Height Ratio (SHR)** should be between **1.0 and 1.5** for uniform illumination.
- **Total lumens on WP = E × A** is the most fundamental relationship — every KTU numerical starts from here.
- **Paint dark ceilings white** — single most cost-effective way to boost CU.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mastery Tip:** In the KTU 2024 exam, if a numerical question gives you photometric-table-style data, immediately calculate **RI** and look up **CU** — this single step fetches 3 – 4 marks by itself.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Estimation of Quantity of Material Required in Electrical Installation for Small Residential Building/Flat/Factory (Micro-Project)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design and Estimation of Quantity of Material Required in Electrical Installation for Small Residential Building/Flat/Factory (Micro-Project)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electrical Installation Estimation (Micro-Project)** is the systematic process of determining the **quantities**, **specifications**, and **costs** of all electrical materials, accessories, and labour required to execute the complete electrical wiring installation of a small residential building, flat, or factory. The estimation is performed from a sanctioned **Electrical Layout Plan (ELP)**, **Wiring Diagram**, and **Load Schedule**, and culminates in the preparation of a **Schedule of Quantities (SoQ)** and **Abstract of Cost (AoC)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCEET602 / Module 2):**
> "A micro-project in this module involves the complete design and estimation of quantities of material required for the electrical installation of a small residential building/flat/factory, including preparation of the wiring layout, load calculation, selection of wires, conduits, protective devices, and preparation of a detailed schedule of materials with cost."

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Electrical Shopping List"

Imagine you are building a small house. The civil engineer tells you: *"I need 5000 bricks, 200 bags of cement, and 50 tonnes of steel."* That is **estimation** — translating a drawing into quantities.

For the electrical side, the same principle applies. The architect gives you a floor plan; you, as an electrical estimator, must answer:

- How many **metres of wire** are needed?
- How many **metres of conduit pipe** must be laid in the ceiling/walls?
- How many **switches, sockets, holders, MCBs, and distribution boards** are required?
- What will all of this **cost**?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of Every Micro-Project:**
> 1. **Design** — Drawing the wiring layout, selecting the wiring system.
> 2. **Quantity Survey (QS)** — Counting every component and measuring every length.
> 3. **Costing** — Multiplying quantities by current market rates to arrive at total cost.

### 1.3 Scope of the Micro-Project

A typical KTU micro-project covers a **single-storey residential building** or **2 BHK flat** with the following:

- **1 BHK / 2 BHK layout** with 4–8 rooms.
- **Lighting points** (1 × 60 W lamp per room, average).
- **Fan points** (1 ceiling fan per room).
- **5 A socket outlets** (2 per room).
- **15 A socket outlets** (1 in kitchen for refrigerator/geyser).
- **Main Distribution Board (DB)** with MCB/ELCB protection.
- **Earthing** with GI pipe / copper plate.
- **Concealed PVC conduit** wiring in roof slab and walls.

> [!TIP]
> **Wiring System Used:** For all modern residential/flat micro-projects, the **Concealed PVC Conduit Wiring System** is mandatory as per KTU standard practice and IS 732 / IS 4648.

### 1.4 Key Standards and Codes

- **IS 732:1989** — Code of Practice for Electrical Wiring Installations.
- **IS 4648:1968** — Guide for Electrical Wiring in Residential Buildings.
- **IE Rules 1956** — Central Electricity Authority (CEA) Regulations.
- **NEC 2011 (National Electrical Code)** — BIS/SP 30.
- **CPWD Schedule of Rates (DSR)** — Reference for cost data.
- **KSEB / State Electricity Board** — Service connection rules.

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Standard fan point length** ≈ **6 m to 7 m** (from switch board to ceiling rose).
- **Standard light point length** ≈ **4 m to 5 m**.
- **Standard socket outlet point length** ≈ **3 m to 4 m**.
- **Conduit bend radius** = **Minimum 6 × outer diameter of conduit**.
- **Maximum number of wires in one conduit** = **4** (for easy drawing-in and derating).
- **Standard conduit sizes**: **20 mm PVC** (most common), **25 mm PVC** (for sub-main runs).
- **Earthing resistance** ≤ **1 Ω** (for IT systems) / **5 Ω** (for domestic).
- **Height of switchboard** = **1.5 m** from floor level.
- **Height of socket outlet** = **0.3 m** (general) / **1.5 m** (kitchen counter).
- **Height of distribution board** = **1.5 m to 2.0 m** from floor level.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Wiring Systems (Comparative Study)

| Wiring System | Application | Cost | Durability | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cleat Wiring** | Temporary / industrial sheds | Low | Low | Not for micro-project |
| **Casing-Capping** | Old residential | Medium | Medium | Obsolete |
| **Batten Wiring** | Godowns, low-cost houses | Low | Low | Not preferred |
| **Surface Conduit (PVC/MS)** | Factories, garages | Medium | High | Common in factories |
| **Concealed Conduit (PVC)** | Modern residential/flat | High | Very High | **Mandatory for micro-project** |

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Estimation Process

The estimation of electrical materials follows a **structured six-step methodology**:

**Step 1 — Study of Drawings**
- Read the **Architectural Floor Plan**.
- Read the **Electrical Single-Line Diagram (SLD)**.
- Identify the location of every electrical point.

**Step 2 — Preparation of Point Schedule**
- List every light point, fan point, socket outlet, and switch box with its rating (in Watts or Amperes).

**Step 3 — Length Measurement of Conduit and Wire**
- For each point, measure the conduit run from the **distribution board** to the **switch box**, and from the switch box to the **ceiling rose / socket**.
- Apply the **wastage factor** of 10–15 % to account for bends, joints, and wastage.

**Step 4 — Counting of Accessories**
- Count switches, sockets, ceiling roses, junction boxes, connector boxes, saddles, etc.

**Step 5 — Selection of Protective Devices**
- Choose the **Main Switch (MCB/Isolator)**, **SPN/TPN MCBs**, and **ELCB/RCCB** based on connected load.

**Step 6 — Preparation of Schedule of Quantities and Abstract of Cost**
- Tabulate all items with **quantity × unit rate = total cost**.
- Add **labour charges** (typically 40–60 % of material cost) and **contingencies** (5–10 %).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS — Memorise These for KTU ESE 2024**

| # | Formula / Rule | Expression | Unit / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Wire Length** | $L_{wire} = (L_{conduit} + 0.5 \text{ m at each end}) \times (1 + \text{Wastage Factor})$ | metres |
| 2 | **Conduit Length** | $L_{conduit} = L_{straight} + (N_{bend} \times 0.15 \text{ m})$ | metres |
| 3 | **Total Connected Load** | $P_{total} = \sum P_{point,i}$ | Watts |
| 4 | **Total Current per Phase** | $I = \dfrac{P_{total}}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L \times \cos\phi}$ | Amps (3-phase) |
| 5 | **Current for Single Phase** | $I = \dfrac{P}{V \times \cos\phi}$ | Amps (1-phase, $\cos\phi=0.8$ for lighting, 1.0 for resistive) |
| 6 | **Voltage Drop** | $\Delta V = \dfrac{2 \times I \times L \times \rho}{A}$ (1-phase) | Volts |
| 7 | **Permissible Voltage Drop** | $\Delta V \le 3\% \text{ of } V$ (lighting) / $5\%$ (power) | IS 732 rule |
| 8 | **MCB Rating Selection** | $I_{MCB} \ge 1.25 \times I_{full\text{-}load}$ | Amps |
| 9 | **Earthing Resistance** | $R \le 5\,\Omega$ (domestic) | Ohms |
| 10 | **Conduit Fill Factor** | $\dfrac{\sum A_{wire}}{A_{conduit,inner}} \le 40\%$ | IS 732 |
| 11 | **Earthing Electrode (GI Pipe)** | Length $\ge 2.5$ m, Diameter $\ge 40$ mm | metres |
| 12 | **Bend Radius** | $R_{bend} \ge 6 \times D_{conduit,outer}$ | mm |

### 2.4 Detailed Material Specification Table

| Material | Standard Specification | Use in Micro-Project |
|---|---|---|
| **PVC Insulated Copper Wire (FR-LS)** | 1.5 mm² / 2.5 mm² / 4 mm² / 6 mm² | Phase, Neutral, Earth |
| **PVC Conduit Pipe** | 20 mm / 25 mm (ISI marked) | Concealed wiring |
| **Modular Switch Box** | 4-module / 6-module / 8-module / 12-module | Switch boards |
| **Modular Switches** | 6 A / 16 A / 20 A | ON/OFF control |
| **Socket Outlets** | 5-pin 6 A / 5-pin 16 A / 6-pin 16 A | Plug points |
| **Ceiling Rose** | 6 A / 16 A | Lamp / fan connection |
| **Junction Boxes** | 1-way / 2-way / 3-way / 4-way | Loop-in connections |
| **MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker)** | SP / SPN / TP, 6 A to 32 A | Overload + short-circuit |
| **RCCB (Residual Current Circuit Breaker)** | 2-pole / 4-pole, 30 mA / 100 mA | Earth-leakage protection |
| **MCB Distribution Board** | 4-way / 8-way / 12-way SPN | Sub-DB |
| **Earthing (GI Pipe + Charcoal + Salt)** | 40 mm dia, 2.5 m length | Earth electrode |
| **Insulation Tape, PVC Saddle, Cement, Sand** | — | Misc. consumables |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Electrical Contractors** use this estimation to prepare **tenders and quotations** for residential projects.
- **PWD / CPWD Engineers** use it for **Government Building Electrification** schemes.
- **BIS Auditors** verify installations against the estimated quantities during inspection.
- **Smart Home Designers** extend this estimation methodology to add IoT sensors, smart switches, and conduit planning for Ethernet cables.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Comprehensive Case Study — 2 BHK Residential Flat (Concealed PVC Conduit)

**Given Data:**
- A 2 BHK flat with Drawing Room, 2 Bedrooms, Kitchen, 2 Bathrooms, 1 Balcony.
- **Wiring system**: Concealed PVC conduit.
- **Supply**: 1-phase 230 V, 50 Hz, 5 kW sanctioned load.
- **Lamp load**: 60 W per light point.
- **Fan load**: 75 W per fan.
- **Socket load (5 A)**: 100 W per socket (diversity applied).
- **Socket load (15 A)**: 1000 W per socket (geyser/refrigerator).

#### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Point Schedule Preparation

| Room | Light Pts | Fan Pts | 5 A Sockets | 15 A Sockets | Switch Boxes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Drawing Room | 2 | 1 | 2 | 0 | 1 (8-module) |
| Bedroom 1 | 1 | 1 | 2 | 0 | 1 (6-module) |
| Bedroom 2 | 1 | 1 | 2 | 0 | 1 (6-module) |
| Kitchen | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 (fridge) | 1 (6-module) |
| Bath 1 | 1 | 1 (exhaust) | 0 | 1 (geyser) | 1 (4-module) |
| Bath 2 | 1 | 1 (exhaust) | 0 | 0 | 1 (4-module) |
| Balcony | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 (4-module) |
| **TOTAL** | **8** | **5** | **7** | **2** | **7** |

#### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Total Connected Load Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{lights} &= 8 \times 60 \text{ W} = 480 \text{ W} \\
P_{fans} &= 5 \times 75 \text{ W} = 375 \text{ W} \\
P_{5A\_sockets} &= 7 \times 100 \text{ W} = 700 \text{ W} \\
P_{15A\_sockets} &= 2 \times 1000 \text{ W} = 2000 \text{ W} \\
P_{total} &= 480 + 375 + 700 + 2000 = 3555 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is well within the **5 kW sanctioned load**. Diversity factor applied = 0.7 for sockets gives an effective load of:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{eff} &= 480 + 375 + 2000 + (700 \times 0.7) \\
        &= 480 + 375 + 2000 + 490 \\
        &= 3345 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Main Cable Sizing

**Full load current** at unity power factor for sub-mains (single phase):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{FL} &= \frac{P_{eff}}{V \times \cos\phi} \\
       &= \frac{3345}{230 \times 1.0} \\
       &= 14.54 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply safety factor of 1.25:

$$
I_{design} = 1.25 \times 14.54 = 18.18 \text{ A}
$$

**Select 6 mm² copper PVC insulated cable** (current carrying capacity ≈ 32 A in concealed conduit).

#### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Conduit and Wire Length Calculation

**Logic:** The total length of the conduit run is estimated as the **horizontal distance from DB to switch box (P-to-P distance on plan)** + **vertical drop from switch box to ceiling rose (≈ 1.5 m)** + **wastage factor of 15 %** for bends and jointing.

**Analytical Approach (assumed room dimensions 4 m × 4 m, DB located in passage):**

| Point Type | No. of Pts | Avg. Length / Pt (m) | Total Length (m) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Light Point (switch to ceiling rose) | 8 | 1.5 | 12.0 |
| Fan Point (switch to ceiling rose) | 5 | 1.5 | 7.5 |
| 5 A Socket (DB loop) | 7 | 5.0 | 35.0 |
| 15 A Socket (DB loop) | 2 | 6.0 | 12.0 |
| Switch Box to DB (sub-main) | 7 | 6.0 | 42.0 |
| **Sub-Total (Horizontal + Vertical)** | | | **108.5** |
| **+ 15 % Wastage Factor** | | | **16.3** |
| **TOTAL CONDUIT LENGTH** | | | **≈ 125 m** |

#### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Wire Length Calculation (Phase + Neutral + Earth)

For a 1-phase 3-wire system (Phase + Neutral + Earth), the **multiplier is 3** for every conduit metre of point wiring (because 3 wires are drawn through each conduit section). For sub-mains, it is also 3 (P + N + E) for 1-phase.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{wire,total} &= 3 \times L_{conduit,total} + (0.5 \text{ m at each point} \times N_{points}) \\
              &= 3 \times 125 + (0.5 \times 22) \\
              &= 375 + 11 \\
              &= 386 \text{ m} \quad \text{(per wire size)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Different wire sizes are required for different circuits:**

| Wire Size | Use | Total Length Required |
|---|---|---|
| **1.5 mm²** | Light + Fan circuits (P + N + E) | $3 \times 22 \text{ pts} \times 1.5 \text{ m avg} \approx 110 \text{ m}$ |
| **2.5 mm²** | 5 A socket circuits (P + N + E) | $3 \times 7 \text{ pts} \times 5 \text{ m} \approx 105 \text{ m}$ |
| **4 mm²** | 15 A socket / AC point (P + N + E) | $3 \times 2 \text{ pts} \times 6 \text{ m} \approx 36 \text{ m}$ |
| **6 mm²** | Main sub-main (P + N + E) | $3 \times 12 \text{ m (DB to each room)} \approx 40 \text{ m}$ |

#### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Accessory Count

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{switches,6A} &= 8 \text{ (light)} + 5 \text{ (fan)} = 13 \\
N_{switches,16A} &= 2 \text{ (15 A sockets)} \\
N_{socket,5pin,6A} &= 7 \\
N_{socket,5pin,16A} &= 2 \\
N_{ceiling\_rose,6A} &= 8 \text{ (lamps)} \\
N_{fan\_hook} &= 5 \\
N_{junction\_boxes} &= 4 \text{ (one per room, 4-way)} \\
N_{switch\_boxes,modular} &= 7 \\
N_{MCB,SP,6A} &= 3 \text{ (light, fan, 5A-socket circuits)} \\
N_{MCB,SP,16A} &= 1 \text{ (15A socket circuit)} \\
N_{MCB,SP,32A} &= 1 \text{ (main isolator)} \\
N_{RCCB,2P,30mA,40A} &= 1 \\
N_{MCB\_DB,8-way,SPN} &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 3.1.7 Step 7 — Schedule of Quantities and Abstract of Cost

| # | Item | Spec | Qty | Unit Rate (₹) | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PVC Cu wire 1.5 mm² | FR-LS | 110 m | 18 | 1980 |
| 2 | PVC Cu wire 2.5 mm² | FR-LS | 105 m | 28 | 2940 |
| 3 | PVC Cu wire 4.0 mm² | FR-LS | 36 m | 45 | 1620 |
| 4 | PVC Cu wire 6.0 mm² | FR-LS | 40 m | 65 | 2600 |
| 5 | PVC Conduit 20 mm | ISI | 100 m | 14 | 1400 |
| 6 | PVC Conduit 25 mm | ISI | 25 m | 22 | 550 |
| 7 | Modular Switch Box 8-M | — | 1 | 180 | 180 |
| 8 | Modular Switch Box 6-M | — | 2 | 140 | 280 |
| 9 | Modular Switch Box 4-M | — | 4 | 110 | 440 |
| 10 | 6 A Modular Switch | — | 13 | 35 | 455 |
| 11 | 16 A Modular Switch | — | 2 | 55 | 110 |
| 12 | 5-pin 6 A Socket | — | 7 | 50 | 350 |
| 13 | 5-pin 16 A Socket | — | 2 | 90 | 180 |
| 14 | Ceiling Rose 6 A | — | 8 | 25 | 200 |
| 15 | Junction Box 4-way | — | 4 | 40 | 160 |
| 16 | MCB SP 6 A C-curve | — | 3 | 180 | 540 |
| 17 | MCB SP 16 A C-curve | — | 1 | 200 | 200 |
| 18 | MCB DP 32 A Isolator | — | 1 | 450 | 450 |
| 19 | RCCB 2P 40 A 30 mA | — | 1 | 1800 | 1800 |
| 20 | 8-way SPN MCB DB | — | 1 | 850 | 850 |
| 21 | GI Pipe 40 mm × 2.5 m (Earthing) | — | 1 | 350 | 350 |
| 22 | Charcoal + Salt + GI wire | — | Lot | 400 | 400 |
| 23 | Misc. (tape, saddles, cement) | — | Lot | 600 | 600 |
| | **MATERIAL TOTAL** | | | | **₹ 17,635** |
| 24 | Labour Charges (@ 50 %) | | | | ₹ 8,820 |
| | **GRAND TOTAL** | | | | **₹ 26,455** |

> [!TIP]
> **Approximate benchmark cost (2024 Kerala market)**: A 2 BHK concealed conduit electrification costs between **₹ 25,000 – ₹ 35,000** depending on brand of accessories (Anchor Roma / Legrand Myrius / Havells Coral).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]**
**Define the term "Schedule of Quantities (SoQ)" with respect to electrical installation estimation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Schedule of Quantities (SoQ)** is a detailed tabular document prepared during electrical estimation that lists every item of material required for an electrical installation project, along with its **specification, quantity, unit, and unit rate**. It serves as the primary document for **tendering, procurement, and billing** of electrical works. Each row of the SoQ contains the item description (e.g., "PVC insulated copper wire 2.5 mm² FR-LS"), the quantity (e.g., 105 m), the unit (e.g., metre), the rate per unit (₹ 28), and the total amount (₹ 2940). It is also called the **Bill of Quantities (BoQ)** in contractor terminology.

*(Keywords for full marks: definition + purpose + typical contents.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
**List any four materials required for concealed PVC conduit wiring in a residential building.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **PVC Insulated Copper Conductor Wires** of sizes 1.5 mm², 2.5 mm², 4 mm², and 6 mm² (FR-LS grade, ISI marked).
2. **PVC Conduit Pipes** of 20 mm and 25 mm outer diameter (ISI marked, rigid).
3. **Modular Switch Boxes and Modular Switches** (6 A and 16 A ratings).
4. **5-pin Socket Outlets** of 6 A and 16 A ratings.
5. **MCB Distribution Board (SPN)** with **MCBs** and **RCCB** for protection.
6. **GI Pipe Earth Electrode** (40 mm dia × 2.5 m long) with charcoal and salt backfill.

*(Any four correct materials with specifications carry full 3 marks.)*

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Module 2]**

A single-storey residential building has the following rooms with electrical points (concealed PVC conduit wiring, 1-phase 230 V supply):

| Room | Lights | Fans | 5 A Sockets | 15 A Sockets |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Verandah | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Drawing Room | 2 | 1 | 2 | 0 |
| Bedroom | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| Kitchen | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 (geyser) |
| Bathroom | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Store | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

**(a)** Calculate the **total connected load** and **full load current** (assume unity power factor). **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Prepare a **detailed schedule of quantities (SoQ)** for the above installation, showing all major materials. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Load Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Count total points:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{lights} &= 1 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 7 \\
N_{fans} &= 0 + 1 + 1 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 2 \\
N_{5A} &= 0 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 0 + 0 = 4 \\
N_{15A} &= 0 + 0 + 0 + 1 + 0 + 0 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total connected load** (Light = 60 W, Fan = 75 W, 5 A socket = 100 W, 15 A socket = 1000 W):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{lights} &= 7 \times 60 = 420 \text{ W} \\
P_{fans} &= 2 \times 75 = 150 \text{ W} \\
P_{5A} &= 4 \times 100 = 400 \text{ W} \\
P_{15A} &= 1 \times 1000 = 1000 \text{ W} \\
P_{total} &= 420 + 150 + 400 + 1000 = 1970 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Full load current** at unity power factor (single phase):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{FL} &= \frac{P_{total}}{V \times \cos\phi} \\
       &= \frac{1970}{230 \times 1.0} \\
       &= 8.57 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Design current (with safety factor 1.25):**

$$
I_{design} = 1.25 \times 8.57 = 10.71 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 5 — MCB selection:** Choose **16 A SP MCB** for sub-circuit and **32 A DP isolator** for main.

**Part (b) — Schedule of Quantities (7 marks):**

| 1 | PVC Cu Wire | 1.5 mm² FR-LS (Phase, Neutral, Earth for light + fan) | 75 m | m |
| 2 | PVC Cu Wire | 2.5 mm² FR-LS (for 5 A socket circuits) | 60 m | m |
| 3 | PVC Cu Wire | 4.0 mm² FR-LS (for 15 A socket / geyser) | 18 m | m |
| 4 | PVC Conduit Pipe | 20 mm dia, ISI | 80 m | m |
| 5 | PVC Conduit Pipe | 25 mm dia, ISI (for sub-main) | 15 m | m |
| 6 | Modular Switch Box 6-module | 6-M | 2 | nos |
| 7 | Modular Switch Box 4-module | 4-M | 4 | nos |
| 8 | Modular Switch 6 A | 6 A, 230 V | 9 | nos |
| 9 | Modular Switch 16 A | 16 A, 230 V | 1 | no |
| 10 | 5-pin Socket 6 A | 6 A | 4 | nos |

**Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024 Module 2, KTU Pattern]**

A small factory shed (30 m × 10 m) is to be electrified with **surface PVC conduit wiring** (industrial practice). The load details are:

- 12 Fluorescent tube lights (each 40 W) on the ceiling.
- 4 Ceiling fans (each 75 W).
- 6 Three-phase 5-pin 16 A socket outlets (each rated 1500 W) for machinery.
- 1 Three-phase 5-pin 32 A socket outlet (rated 5000 W) for the main machine.
- Supply: **3-phase 415 V, 50 Hz**.

**(a)** Calculate the **total connected load per phase** (balanced) and the **full load current**. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Estimate the **major quantities** of wire, conduit, MCBs, and switchgear required. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Load Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Total connected load:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{lights} &= 12 \times 40 = 480 \text{ W} \\
P_{fans} &= 4 \times 75 = 300 \text{ W} \\
P_{16A\text{-}sockets} &= 6 \times 1500 = 9000 \text{ W} \\
P_{32A\text{-}socket} &= 1 \times 5000 = 5000 \text{ W} \\
P_{total} &= 480 + 300 + 9000 + 5000 = 14780 \text{ W} = 14.78 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Load per phase (balanced 3-phase):**

$$
P_{phase} = \frac{14780}{3} = 4926.67 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3 — Full load line current (3-phase, $\cos\phi = 0.85$ for industrial):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{FL} &= \frac{P_{total}}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L \times \cos\phi} \\
       &= \frac{14780}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.85} \\
       &= \frac{14780}{611.04} \\
       &= 24.19 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Design current (× 1.25 safety factor):**

$$
I_{design} = 1.25 \times 24.19 = 30.24 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 5 — MCB selection:** Choose **40 A TP MCB** as main isolator, **16 A TP MCB** for socket sub-circuits, and **6 A SP MCB** for lighting.

**Part (b) — Quantity Estimation (7 marks):**

**Conduit length (surface wiring, 30 m × 10 m shed):**

- Perimeter = $2 \times (30 + 10) = 80$ m
- Length of main trunk conduit (along the length) = 30 m
- Length of branch conduit (6 cross-runs) = $6 \times 10 = 60$ m
- Drop to each fitting (12 lights + 4 fans + 7 sockets) = 23 × 1.5 m = 34.5 m
- **Sub-total = 80 + 30 + 60 + 34.5 = 204.5 m**
- **+ 10 % wastage = 20.45 m**
- **Total conduit length ≈ 225 m (use 25 mm PVC conduit)**

**Wire length** (3-phase 4-wire: R + Y + B + N + E = 5 wires per trunk):

- For 1-phase sub-circuits (light/fan): $5 \times 100 \text{ m} = 500 \text{ m of 1.5 mm²}$
- For 3-phase 16 A socket runs: $5 \times 60 \text{ m} = 300 \text{ m of 2.5 mm²}$
- For 3-phase 32 A socket run: $5 \times 25 \text{ m} = 125 \text{ m of 6 mm²}$

**Switchgear quantity:**

| Item | Qty |
|---|---|
| 40 A TP MCB (Main) | 1 |
| 32 A TP MCB (32 A socket) | 1 |
| 16 A TP MCB (16 A socket) | 2 |
| 6 A SP MCB (Lighting) | 3 |
| 6 A SP MCB (Fan) | 1 |
| 4P RCCB 63 A 30 mA | 1 |
| 12-way TPN MCB DB | 1 |

*(Full 7 marks for tabular format with specifications.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MARK-LOSS MISTAKES (Read Carefully!)**
>
> 1. **Forgot to include EARTH WIRE in length calculation** — Examiners specifically test whether you multiplied by **3 (P+N+E) or 5 (R+Y+B+N+E)**. A 2-mark penalty is common.
> 2. **Did not apply wastage factor (10–15 %)** — This is a **2-mark trap** in nearly every ESE paper.
> 3. **Wrong MCB selection** — Selecting **6 A MCB for a 14 A load** is a serious safety error. Always check $I_{MCB} \ge 1.25 \times I_{FL}$.
> 4. **Confusing SP / SPN / TP / TPN** — In 1-phase use SPN; in 3-phase use TPN. Mixing up loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgot to add labour charges and contingency** in the abstract of cost. This is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 6. **No earthing entry in SoQ** — Earthing is mandatory; missing it loses 1 mark.
> 7. **Unit mismatch** — Writing "5 metres" instead of "5 m" or "5 nos" can lose 0.5 mark per row.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 2 Micro-Project**

- **Definition:** Electrical estimation = translating wiring drawings into material quantities and cost.
- **Most common wiring system (residential):** Concealed PVC conduit.
- **Most common wiring system (factory):** Surface PVC conduit or GI conduit.
- **Standard wire sizes:** **1.5 mm²** (light/fan), **2.5 mm²** (5 A socket), **4 mm²** (15 A socket/AC), **6 mm²** (sub-main).
- **Standard conduit size:** **20 mm** for point wiring, **25 mm** for sub-mains.
- **Switch board height:** **1.5 m** from floor.
- **Socket height:** **0.3 m** (general), **1.5 m** (kitchen counter).
- **DB height:** **1.5–2.0 m** from floor.
- **Earthing resistance:** ≤ **5 Ω** (domestic), ≤ **1 Ω** (sensitive equipment).
- **Earthing electrode:** **GI pipe 40 mm × 2.5 m** with charcoal + salt backfill.
- **Wastage factor:** **10–15 %** for conduits and wires.
- **Safety factor for MCB:** **1.25 × Full Load Current**.
- **Voltage drop limit:** **3 %** for lighting, **5 %** for power.
- **Conduit fill factor:** ≤ **40 %** (as per IS 732).
- **Bend radius:** ≥ **6 × outer diameter** of conduit.
- **Number of wires per conduit:** Maximum **4** for easy maintenance.
- **MCB type for lighting:** **C-curve** (for resistive/inductive loads).
- **RCCB rating:** **30 mA** for personal protection, **100 mA** for fire protection.
- **Cost components:** Material + Labour (40–60 %) + Contingency (5–10 %) + GST (18 %).
- **Benchmark cost (2 BHK, 2024 Kerala):** ₹ **25,000 – ₹ 35,000**.
- **Codes to remember:** **IS 732, IS 4648, IS 3043, NEC 2011, IE Rules 1956**.
- **Document deliverables:** Floor plan + Single Line Diagram + Point Schedule + SoQ + Abstract of Cost + Technical Specification.

> [!NOTE]
> **END OF MODULE 2 MICRO-PROJECT NOTES**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) — PCEET602 — Electrical System Design and Estimation.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Indoor and Outdoor Substation - Selection of Transformer
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Indoor and Outdoor Substation - Selection of Transformer

# Indoor and Outdoor Substation – Selection of Transformer

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Indoor Substation – Formal Definition

An **Indoor Substation** is a type of electrical substation in which all the switching, controlling, current/voltage transforming equipment, bus-bars, isolators, circuit breakers, instrument transformers, protective relays, and the **transformer itself** are installed **inside a closed building/structure** made of brick masonry, RCC, or steel-frame construction. The equipment is protected from external atmospheric conditions such as rain, dust, sun, lightning, and pollution.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard):**
> "An indoor substation is one where the apparatus is installed within a closed building, the rating of the apparatus being up to 11 kV (sometimes up to 33 kV in special cases), and the transformer capacity generally not exceeding 1 MVA."

### 1.2 Outdoor Substation – Formal Definition

An **Outdoor Substation** is a substation in which the **transformer, switchgear, bus-bars, and other heavy electrical apparatus are installed in the open air**, supported on fabricated steel structures, concrete plinths, or gantries. The equipment is designed to withstand all weather conditions directly. Outdoor substations are economically preferred for voltages **above 11 kV** and transformer capacities **above 1 MVA**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Threshold (As per IS 1180 & IE Rules):**
> - **Up to 250 kVA & 11 kV** → Indoor preferred
> - **Above 250 kVA or above 11 kV** → Outdoor preferred
> - **Above 33 kV** → Outdoor mandatory

### 1.3 Selection of Transformer – Formal Definition

**Selection of Transformer** is the engineering process of determining the most appropriate transformer rating (kVA/MVA), voltage ratio, type (oil-immersed, dry-type, hermetically sealed, etc.), cooling method (ONAN, ONAF, OFAF), connection (Dyn11, Yyn0), impedance, and location for a given load requirement, ensuring **technical adequacy, economic feasibility, safety, and reliability** over its service life.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an electrical substation like a **kitchen in a restaurant**:
- The **transformer** is like the **main cooking vessel** (large, heavy, generates heat).
- The **circuit breakers & isolators** are like the **gas regulators and safety valves** (control flow).
- The **bus-bars** are the **pipes** carrying the prepared "food" (electricity) to tables (consumers).

Now imagine two scenarios:
1. **Indoor Kitchen (Indoor Substation)**: A small, climate-controlled kitchen where the chef works in comfort. Suited for small-scale cooking (<1 MVA) in clean, dry conditions. The walls protect everything.
2. **Outdoor Tandoor (Outdoor Substation)**: A large open-air setup with massive clay ovens, exposed to the elements but capable of handling huge volumes (>1 MVA) economically. Robust, weatherproof design is essential.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Mapping:**
> - Residential colony distribution transformer (100–250 kVA, 11 kV/0.4 kV) → **Indoor or Plinth-mounted outdoor**
> - Industrial HT consumer substation (500 kVA–2.5 MVA) → **Outdoor Plinth-mounted**
> - Power Grid 110/33/11 kV Substation → **Outdoor Switchyard (AIS/GIS)**

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Frequencies**: **50 Hz** (India)
- **Standard LV Voltage**: **415 V** (3-phase), **240 V** (1-phase)
- **Standard MV Voltages**: **11 kV, 22 kV, 33 kV**
- **Standard HV Voltages**: **66 kV, 110 kV, 132 kV, 220 kV, 400 kV, 765 kV**
- **Diversity Factor (DF)**: **1.1 – 1.5** (typical industrial)
- **Demand Factor (DFc)**: **0.5 – 0.8**
- **Power Factor (cos φ)**: **0.8 lagging (standard assumption)**
- **Ambient Temperature Reference**: **40°C** (IS 2026)
- **Altitude Correction**: Above **1000 m**, derating applies

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comparison: Indoor vs Outdoor Substation

| Parameter | Indoor Substation | Outdoor Substation |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Enclosed building | Open-air with steel structures |
| **Voltage Range** | Up to 11 kV (max 33 kV) | 11 kV and above |
| **Capacity** | Up to 1 MVA | Above 1 MVA |
| **Cost** | High (building cost) | Low (only structures) |
| **Space Required** | More (building footprint) | Less per kV |
| **Safety** | Higher (enclosed) | Lower (exposed) |
| **Maintenance** | Easier & cleaner | Difficult in bad weather |
| **Appearance** | Aesthetic (urban friendly) | Industrial look |
| **Fault Level** | Limited by enclosure | Higher, openly handled |
| **Cooling** | Forced/ventilated required | Natural air cooling |
| **Installation Time** | Longer (civil work) | Shorter |
| **Future Expansion** | Difficult | Easy |
| **Typical Use** | Urban, basements, commercial | Industrial, rural, grid substations |
| **Insulation Media** | Air/SF₆ (GIS) | Air/oil |
| **Switchgear Type** | VCB, SF₆ panels | Bulk oil/minimum oil OCBs |

## 2.2 Types of Outdoor Substations

1. **Pole-mounted Substation** – For distribution transformers up to 250 kVA, 11/0.4 kV
2. **Plinth-mounted Substation** – Transformer on RCC plinth with HT/LT panel nearby
3. **Conventional Outdoor Switchyard (AIS – Air Insulated Switchgear)** – Steel gantries, ACSR conductors
4. **Gas Insulated Switchgear (GIS) Substation** – SF₆ gas insulated modules (compact)
5. **Hybrid Substation** – Combination of AIS + GIS

## 2.3 Types of Indoor Substations

1. **Metal-enclosed Indoor Substation** – Sheet metal cubicles with VCB/SF₆
2. **Indoor Substation with Dry-type Transformer** – Cast Resin Dry Type (CRDT) transformer
3. **Packaged Substation (PSS)** – Pre-engineered compact unit
4. **Basement Substation** – Used in high-rise buildings
5. **Kiosk Type Substation** – Weatherproof enclosure for urban distribution

## 2.4 Selection of Transformer – Step-by-Step Methodology

### Step 1: Load Assessment
- List all electrical loads with their wattage and power factor.
- Categorize: Lighting, Power, HVAC, Motors, etc.

### Step 2: Apply Demand Factor (DFc)
$$P_{d} = P_{i} \times DF_{c}$$
Where $P_{i}$ is the installed load.

### Step 3: Apply Diversity Factor (D)
$$P_{max} = \frac{\sum P_{d}}{D}$$
For simultaneous maximum demand.

### Step 4: Add Future Expansion
- Typically 20%–25% margin is added.

### Step 5: Calculate kVA Rating
$$S = \frac{P_{max}}{\cos \phi}$$
Then select the next standard rating.

### Step 6: Choose Cooling Type
- ONAN, ONAF, OFAF, OFWF based on capacity.

### Step 7: Choose Voltage Ratio
- Based on grid supply (e.g., 11 kV/433 V, 33 kV/11 kV).

### Step 8: Choose Connection
- Dyn11 preferred for distribution.
- Yyn0 for generator transformers.

### Step 9: Choose Location
- Indoor if kVA < 1000 and voltage < 11 kV.
- Outdoor otherwise.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Standard Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | kVA from Load | $S = \frac{P}{\cos \phi}$ | kVA |
| 2 | Full Load Current (HV side) | $I_{HV} = \frac{S \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{HV}}$ | A |
| 3 | Full Load Current (LV side) | $I_{LV} = \frac{S \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{LV}}$ | A |
| 4 | Demand Factor | $DF_{c} = \frac{Max\ Demand}{Connected\ Load}$ | 0.5 – 0.8 |
| 5 | Diversity Factor | $D = \frac{\sum Individual\ Max\ Demand}{Maximum\ Demand\ on\ Station}$ | 1.1 – 1.5 |
| 6 | Utilization Factor | $UF = \frac{Max\ Demand}{Rated\ Capacity}$ | < 1 |
| 7 | Future Load | $S_{design} = S_{present} \times 1.25$ | 25% margin |
| 8 | Transformer Impedance | $Z\% = \frac{V_{sc}}{V_{rated}} \times 100$ | 4% – 12.5% |
| 9 | Short Circuit Current | $I_{sc} = \frac{I_{FL}}{Z_{pu}}$ | kA |
| 10 | Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{Output}{Output + Losses}$ | 95% – 99% |
| 11 | All-day Efficiency | $\eta_{ad} = \frac{kWh\ output\ in\ 24\ hr}{kWh\ output + kWh\ losses\ in\ 24\ hr}$ | – |
| 12 | Voltage Regulation | $VR = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{NL}} \times 100$ | % |
| 13 | Altitude Derating | Above 1000 m: 1% per 100 m | – |
| 14 | Ambient Temp Derating | Above 40°C: 1% per °C | – |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Transformer Ratings (kVA):** 16, 25, 63, 100, 160, 200, 250, 315, 400, 500, 630, 800, 1000, 1250, 1600, 2000, 2500, 3150, 5000, 10000...
> Always select the **next higher standard rating** above calculated load.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Utilities**: Transformer selection ensures uninterrupted power to millions.
- **Industries**: Critical for production continuity; wrong selection leads to downtime losses.
- **Renewable Energy**: Wind/Solar farm step-up transformers need special harmonic-withstand design.
- **Buildings**: High-rise substation selection is a statutory requirement under NBC 2016 and CEIG approval.
- **Smart Grids**: Modern selection now includes SCADA integration, online monitoring (DGA – Dissolved Gas Analysis).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix – Selection Decision Flow

| Stage | Parameter | Decision Criterion | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Load Magnitude | < 250 kVA | Indoor / Pole-mounted |
| 2 | Load Magnitude | 250 kVA – 1000 kVA | Plinth outdoor |
| 3 | Load Magnitude | > 1000 kVA | Outdoor switchyard |
| 4 | Voltage Class | ≤ 11 kV | Indoor / Plinth |
| 5 | Voltage Class | > 11 kV (22/33 kV) | Outdoor mandatory |
| 6 | Pollution Level | High (coastal/industrial) | Outdoor / Sealed tank |
| 7 | Fire Risk | High (indoor public) | Dry-type / Kiosk |
| 8 | Space Constraint | Urban (basement) | GIS / PSS |
| 9 | Future Growth | > 20% expected | Outdoor with spare bay |
| 10 | Altitude | > 1000 m | Derate + special insulation |

## 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Derivation 1: Transformer kVA Selection

**Problem:** A factory has the following loads:
- Lighting load: 50 kW (PF = 0.9 lag)
- Power load (motors): 200 kW (PF = 0.8 lag)
- HVAC load: 100 kW (PF = 0.85 lag)

Compute the transformer kVA rating assuming a **25% future expansion margin** and a **diversity factor of 1.2**.

### Solution (Step-by-Step):

**Step 1: Identify individual kVA contributions**

For each load:
$$S_i = \frac{P_i}{\cos \phi_i}$$

Lighting:
$$S_1 = \frac{50}{0.9} = 55.55\ kVA$$

Motors:
$$S_2 = \frac{200}{0.8} = 250.00\ kVA$$

HVAC:
$$S_3 = \frac{100}{0.85} = 117.65\ kVA$$

**Step 2: Total Connected Load (Installed kVA)**
$$S_{inst} = S_1 + S_2 + S_3$$
$$S_{inst} = 55.55 + 250.00 + 117.65$$
$$S_{inst} = 423.20\ kVA$$

**Step 3: Apply Diversity Factor**

When diversity factor is given, the actual maximum demand on the system is **less** than the sum of individual max demands:
$$S_{max} = \frac{S_{inst}}{D} = \frac{423.20}{1.2}$$
$$S_{max} = 352.67\ kVA$$

**Step 4: Apply Future Expansion Margin (25%)**
$$S_{design} = S_{max} \times 1.25$$
$$S_{design} = 352.67 \times 1.25 = 440.83\ kVA$$

**Step 5: Select Standard Transformer Rating**

The next higher standard rating is **500 kVA**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Selection:** A **500 kVA, 11 kV / 433 V, Dyn11, ONAN, Oil-immersed** transformer, mounted **outdoor plinth type** is recommended because the rating is > 250 kVA.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Numerical Derivation 2: Full-Load Current Calculation

For the 500 kVA, 11 kV / 433 V transformer selected above:

### HV Side Current (11,000 V line-to-line)

$$I_{HV} = \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{HV}}$$

Substituting:
$$I_{HV} = \frac{500 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 11000}$$
$$I_{HV} = \frac{500000}{1.732 \times 11000}$$
$$I_{HV} = \frac{500000}{19052.6}$$
$$I_{HV} = 26.24\ A$$

### LV Side Current (433 V line-to-line)

$$I_{LV} = \frac{500 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 433}$$
$$I_{LV} = \frac{500000}{1.732 \times 433}$$
$$I_{LV} = \frac{500000}{750.0}$$
$$I_{LV} = 666.67\ A$$

> [!NOTE]
> The LV side current is the basis for sizing bus-bars, LT panels, and the main circuit breaker (typically 800 A rating is selected for a 500 kVA transformer).

## 3.4 Exhaustive Numerical Derivation 3: Short Circuit Current

Assume transformer impedance = **5%** (typical for 500 kVA distribution transformer).

$$I_{sc} = \frac{I_{FL}}{Z_{pu}} = \frac{I_{FL}}{Z\%/100}$$

For LV side:
$$I_{sc,LV} = \frac{666.67}{0.05} = 13{,}333\ A = 13.33\ kA$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The downstream LV switchgear must have a **breaking capacity ≥ 25 kA** (next standard rating above 13.33 kA), and the bus-bars must be rated for **short-time withstand current** ≥ 25 kA for 1 second.

## 3.5 Exhaustive Numerical Derivation 4: Voltage Regulation

For a transformer delivering rated load at PF 0.8 lag:
$$VR = I \times (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi) \times \frac{100}{V}$$

Assuming **R = 0.005 pu, X = 0.04 pu** (typical):
$$VR = 1 \times (0.005 \times 0.8 + 0.04 \times 0.6) \times 100$$
$$VR = (0.004 + 0.024) \times 100 = 2.8\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> IS 1180 mandates **voltage regulation < 5%** at full load and unity-to-0.8 PF. The calculated 2.8% is well within limits.

## 3.6 Exhaustive Numerical Derivation 5: Efficiency at Half Load

At half load with $P_i$ (iron loss) = 1.2 kW and $P_{cu,FL}$ (full load copper loss) = 6.5 kW (for 500 kVA):

$$P_{cu,HL} = P_{cu,FL} \times \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2 = 6.5 \times 0.25 = 1.625\ kW$$

Output at half load, unity PF:
$$P_{out} = 500 \times 0.5 = 250\ kW$$

Total losses at half load:
$$P_{losses} = P_i + P_{cu,HL} = 1.2 + 1.625 = 2.825\ kW$$

Efficiency:
$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{losses}} \times 100 = \frac{250}{252.825} \times 100 = 98.88\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> Energy-efficient transformers (IS 1180 Level-2) have lower $P_i$ and $P_{cu}$. Always prefer star-rated energy-efficient units (BEE 5-star).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram – Indoor Substation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[HT INCOMING 11 kV CABLE] --> A2[HT PANEL WITH VCB]
    A2 --> A3[ISOLATOR / SF6 SWITCH]
    A3 --> A4[HT BUSBAR]
    A4 --> A5[OIL COOLED TRANSFORMER 500 kVA]
    A5 --> A6[LT BUSBAR]
    A6 --> A7[LT PANEL ACB]
    A7 --> A8[FEEDERS TO LOAD]
    A4 --> A9[CT PT METERING]
    A9 --> A10[RELAY PANEL PROTECTION]
    A2 --> A11[CONTROL ROOM]
    A11 --> A12[SCADA / DATA LOGGER]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram – Outdoor Substation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    B1[INCOMING 33 kV OVERHEAD LINE] --> B2[LINE GANTRIES]
    B2 --> B3[ISOLATOR OUTDOOR]
    B3 --> B4[CT PT COMBINATION]
    B4 --> B5[OUTDOOR CIRCUIT BREAKER]
    B5 --> B6[MAIN BUSBAR ACSR CONDUCTOR]
    B6 --> B7[TRANSFORMER PLINTH OUTDOOR]
    B7 --> B8[LV PANEL NEAR PLINTH]
    B8 --> B9[LOAD CENTRE]
    B6 --> B10[INCOMER PANEL]
    B10 --> B11[FEEDER BAYS]
    B5 --> B12[PROTECTION RELAY OUTDOOR]
    B12 --> B13[CONTROL CABLE TO RELAY ROOM]
```

## 4.3 Flowchart – Transformer Selection Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[START: IDENTIFY LOAD kW] --> S2[APPLY PF cos phi]
    S2 --> S3[CALCULATE kVA]
    S3 --> S4[APPLY DEMAND FACTOR]
    S4 --> S5[APPLY DIVERSITY FACTOR]
    S5 --> S6[ADD 25 PERCENT FUTURE MARGIN]
    S6 --> S7{CHOOSE NEXT HIGHER STANDARD RATING}
    S7 --> S8{kVA LESS THAN 250}
    S8 --> S9[INDOOR POLE MOUNTED]
    S8 --> S10{kVA LESS THAN 1000}
    S10 --> S11[OUTDOOR PLINTH MOUNTED]
    S10 --> S12[OUTDOOR SWITCHYARD AIS]
    S7 --> S13{VOLTAGE GREATER THAN 11 kV}
    S13 --> S14[OUTDOOR MANDATORY]
    S13 --> S15[INDOOR PERMITTED UP TO 11 kV]
    S9 --> S16[FINALIZE TRANSFORMER]
    S11 --> S16
    S12 --> S16
    S14 --> S16
    S15 --> S16
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram – Functional Interaction of Substation Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[POWER SOURCE GRID] --> P2[ISOLATING SWITCH]
    P2 --> P3[CIRCUIT BREAKER PROTECTION]
    P3 --> P4[INSTRUMENT TRANSFORMER CT PT]
    P4 --> P5[MAIN TRANSFORMER STEP DOWN]
    P5 --> P6[LV SWITCHBOARD]
    P6 --> P7[FEEDER CIRCUIT BREAKERS]
    P7 --> P8[INDUSTRIAL LOAD]
    P3 --> P9[RELAY PANEL]
    P9 --> P10[TRIP COIL]
    P4 --> P11[METERING ENERGY]
    P5 --> P12[COOLING SYSTEM ONAN ONAF]
    P12 --> P5
```

## 4.5 Comparative Block Diagram – Indoor vs Outdoor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INDOOR SUBSTATION] --> A1[ENCLOSED BRICK ROOM]
    A1 --> A2[VCB SWITCHGEAR]
    A1 --> A3[DRY TYPE OR OIL TANK]
    A1 --> A4[FORCED VENTILATION]
    A1 --> A5[FIRE SUPPRESSION]
    B[OUTDOOR SUBSTATION] --> B1[STEEL GANTRIES]
    B1 --> B2[OPEN BUSBAR ACSR]
    B1 --> B3[OIL COOLED TANK]
    B1 --> B4[NATURAL AIR COOLING]
    B1 --> B5[WEATHERPROOF ENCLOSURE]
    A1 --> C[OUTPUT 11 kV TO 415 V]
    B1 --> C
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Set 1]

**Define the term "Indoor Substation" and state two situations where it is preferred over an outdoor substation.**

**Model Answer:**
An indoor substation is a substation in which all electrical apparatus, including the transformer, switchgear, bus-bars, and protective devices, are installed inside a closed building.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two situations where indoor is preferred:**
> 1. **Urban/Densely Populated Areas** – Where space is limited, pollution is high, and aesthetic considerations demand enclosed installation.
> 2. **Fire-Risk Sensitive Premises** – Such as high-rise buildings, hospitals, malls where dry-type or SF₆ insulated indoor transformers enhance safety and reduce fire hazards.
> 3. (Bonus) **Coastal/Corrosive Atmosphere** – Enclosure protects equipment from salt-laden air.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper Set 2]

**List any four factors that govern the selection of a transformer for a substation.**

**Model Answer:**
The four key factors are:
1. **kVA Rating** – Based on connected load, demand factor, diversity factor, and future expansion.
2. **Voltage Ratio** – Primary and secondary voltage levels (e.g., 11 kV / 0.433 kV).
3. **Type of Cooling** – ONAN, ONAF, OFAF, etc., depending on capacity.
4. **Connection (Vector Group)** – Dyn11, Yyn0, etc., based on load nature and harmonic profile.
5. (Bonus) **Impedance** – Determines fault level and voltage regulation.
6. (Bonus) **Location (Indoor/Outdoor)** – Based on capacity and voltage.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – With Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Compare Indoor and Outdoor substations in detail under the following heads: (i) Definition, (ii) Voltage Range, (iii) Space Requirement, (iv) Cost, (v) Safety, (vi) Typical Applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 11 kV / 433 V transformer is rated 1000 kVA. Calculate:
(i) Full-load current on HV and LV sides.
(ii) Percentage voltage regulation at full load 0.8 PF lag, given R = 0.006 pu, X = 0.045 pu.
(iii) The short-circuit current on the LV side. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

| Parameter | Indoor | Outdoor |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Equipment inside building | Equipment installed in open air |
| **Voltage Range** | Up to 11 kV (max 33 kV) | 11 kV and above |
| **Space** | More (building footprint) | Less per kV |
| **Cost** | Higher (building + civil) | Lower (only gantries) |
| **Safety** | Higher (enclosed) | Lower (exposed) |
| **Application** | Urban, basements, high-rise | Industrial, rural, grid |

#### Model Solution (b):

**(i) Full-Load Current:**

HV side ($V_{HV} = 11{,}000$ V):
$$I_{HV} = \frac{1000 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 11000} = \frac{1000000}{19052.6} = 52.49\ A$$

LV side ($V_{LV} = 433$ V):
$$I_{LV} = \frac{1000 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 433} = \frac{1000000}{750.0} = 1333.33\ A$$

**(ii) Voltage Regulation:**
$$VR\% = I_{pu} \times (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi) \times 100$$
$$\cos\phi = 0.8, \sin\phi = 0.6$$
$$VR = 1 \times (0.006 \times 0.8 + 0.045 \times 0.6) \times 100$$
$$VR = (0.0048 + 0.027) \times 100 = 3.18\%$$

**(iii) Short-Circuit Current on LV Side:**
$$Z_{pu} = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2} = \sqrt{0.006^2 + 0.045^2} = \sqrt{0.000036 + 0.002025}$$
$$Z_{pu} = \sqrt{0.002061} = 0.0454\ pu$$
$$I_{sc,LV} = \frac{1333.33}{0.0454} = 29{,}368\ A \approx 29.37\ kA$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The downstream LV switchgear must be rated for at least **35 kA breaking capacity** (next standard).

### Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch/block diagram, describe the layout of a typical outdoor 33/11 kV substation and list its main components. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A small-scale industry has the following loads:
- 30 kW Lighting at 0.9 PF
- 150 kW Motors at 0.8 PF
- 60 kW Heaters at unity PF
Assuming a demand factor of 0.8, a diversity factor of 1.2, and a future expansion margin of 25%, determine the kVA rating of the distribution transformer. Also decide whether it should be installed indoor or outdoor. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Layout of 33/11 kV Outdoor Substation:**

A typical outdoor 33/11 kV substation consists of:
1. **Incoming Line Gantry** – For 33 kV overhead line take-off.
2. **Outgoing Gantry for 33 kV Lines** – For power evacuation.
3. **Outdoor Isolators and Earth Switches** – For isolation.
4. **Current Transformers (CT) and Potential Transformers (PT)** – For metering and protection.
5. **33 kV Circuit Breaker** – Outdoor OCB.
6. **33 kV Bus Bars** – ACSR conductors.
7. **Power Transformer 33/11 kV** – Plinth-mounted, oil-immersed.
8. **11 kV Side Equipment** – CT, PT, Isolators, Breaker.
9. **11 kV Outgoing Feeder Bay** – Multiple feeder gantries.
10. **LT Switchboard** – Near plinth.
