11. **Control Room** – With relay panels, battery bank, SCADA.

**Block Diagram:**
```
33 kV Incoming → Isolator → CT/PT → CB → Busbar → Transformer 33/11 kV → 11 kV Busbar → Feeder CBs → Outgoing
```

#### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1: Total Connected Load (kW)**
$$P_{total} = 30 + 150 + 60 = 240\ kW$$

**Step 2: kVA of Each Group**
- Lighting: $S_1 = 30 / 0.9 = 33.33$ kVA
- Motors: $S_2 = 150 / 0.8 = 187.5$ kVA
- Heaters: $S_3 = 60 / 1.0 = 60$ kVA

**Total kVA:**
$$S_{inst} = 33.33 + 187.5 + 60 = 280.83\ kVA$$

**Step 3: Apply Demand Factor**
$$S_{max,DF} = S_{inst} \times DF = 280.83 \times 0.8 = 224.66\ kVA$$

**Step 4: Apply Diversity Factor**
$$S_{max,div} = \frac{224.66}{1.2} = 187.22\ kVA$$

**Step 5: Apply Future Expansion (25%)**
$$S_{design} = 187.22 \times 1.25 = 234.03\ kVA$$

**Step 6: Select Standard Rating**
Next higher standard rating → **250 kVA**.

**Step 7: Location Decision**
- Voltage = 11 kV (assumed) → Can be indoor or outdoor.
- Rating = 250 kVA → Threshold case.
- **Recommendation:** **Outdoor plinth-mounted** (more economic, easier maintenance) OR Indoor if pollution/fire-risk demand enclosure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Demand Factor with Diversity Factor** – Demand factor is the ratio of max demand to connected load *of a single consumer*. Diversity factor is the ratio of *sum of individual max demands* to *max demand on station*. Mixing them is a common error (–2 marks).
> 2. **Not adding future expansion** – Many students forget the 25% margin (–1 mark).
> 3. **Choosing the same rating instead of next higher standard** – 234 kVA → Must select 250 kVA, not 234 kVA (–1 mark).
> 4. **Forgetting Dyn11** – Board specifically tests vector group knowledge. Always mention **Dyn11** for 11/0.433 kV distribution transformers.
> 5. **Unit errors** – Mixing kW and kVA, using V instead of kV (–1 to –2 marks).
> 6. **Not stating cooling class** – ONAN/ONAF must be mentioned for full marks.
> 7. **No mention of standard codes** – Always cite **IS 1180, IS 2026, IEEE C57.12.00, IE Rules 1956**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ **Indoor Substation:** Apparatus inside a closed building; preferred for ≤ 11 kV and ≤ 1 MVA.
- ☐ **Outdoor Substation:** Apparatus in open air; mandatory for > 11 kV; cost-effective.
- ☐ **Standard Voltages in India:** 11/22/33 kV (MV), 66/110/220/400/765 kV (HV).
- ☐ **Standard Transformer Ratings:** 16, 25, 63, 100, 160, 200, 250, 315, 400, 500, 630, 800, 1000 kVA, etc.
- ☐ **Always select next HIGHER standard rating** above calculated kVA.
- ☐ **Demand Factor (DFc):** 0.5 – 0.8; **Diversity Factor (D):** 1.1 – 1.5.
- ☐ **Future Expansion Margin:** 20–25% is industry standard.
- ☐ **Power Factor Assumption:** 0.8 lag (unless specified otherwise).
- ☐ **Full Load Current:** $I_{FL} = S / (\sqrt{3} \times V)$.
- ☐ **Short Circuit Current:** $I_{sc} = I_{FL} / Z_{pu}$.
- ☐ **Voltage Regulation:** < 5% per IS 1180.
- ☐ **Vector Group:** Dyn11 for distribution; Yyn0 for generator/UPS step-up.
- ☐ **Cooling Classes:** ONAN (natural), ONAF (forced air), OFAF (forced oil + air), OFWF (forced oil + water).
- ☐ **Dry-Type Transformer:** Used indoors where fire risk is high.
- ☐ **Codes to Remember:** IS 1180 (distribution), IS 2026 (power), IS 3043 (earthing), IS 5216 (installation), IE Rules 1956.
- ☐ **Ambient Reference:** 40°C (IS 2026); altitude derating above 1000 m.
- ☐ **BEE Star Rating:** Always prefer 5-star energy-efficient transformers.
- ☐ **LCC Analysis:** Selection must consider total life-cycle cost (purchase + losses + maintenance), not just capital cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Quick Tip for 14-mark answers:** Always structure your answer as: **(1) Definition → (2) Key Parameters → (3) Numerical Substitution → (4) Selection of Standard Rating → (5) Justification for Indoor/Outdoor choice → (6) Mention of Standards/Codes**. This guarantees 90%+ marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Switch Gears and Protective Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Switch Gears and Protective Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Switchgear?

**Switchgear** is the collective term encompassing all the switching devices associated with power system protection. It includes a comprehensive assembly of devices such as **switches, fuses, circuit breakers, isolators, relays, current transformers (CTs), potential transformers (PTs), busbars, conductors, and interlocking arrangements** designed to protect electrical equipment from abnormal operating conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Switchgear refers to the apparatus used for switching, controlling, and protecting electrical circuits and equipment. It forms the **nerve center** of any modern power distribution system, ensuring both **operational reliability** and **personnel safety**.

### 1.2 Fundamental Functions of Switchgear

The switchgear assembly performs three primary functions in a power system:

1. **Carrying Normal Load Current** — Continuously carries the rated load current without overheating. The temperature rise is limited to **90°C** for silver-plated copper contacts (per IS 13947).
2. **Carrying Fault Current for a Brief Period** — Withstands high short-circuit currents until the protective device operates. This is quantified as the **short-time withstand current ($I_{cw}$)** for 1 second or 3 seconds.
3. **Breaking Fault Current** — Safely interrupts the fault current without damaging itself, quantified as the **breaking capacity (in MVA)** or **rupturing capacity**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Human Nervous System:**
> Think of switchgear as the **nervous system of the human body**. Just as the nervous system:
> - **Senses** abnormal conditions (relays/CTs/PTs act as sensory neurons),
> - **Decides** an action (protective relays as the brain),
> - **Executes** a corrective action (circuit breakers as the muscle),
> the switchgear in a power system senses faults, decides whether to trip, and isolates the faulty section — all within **milliseconds** to prevent catastrophic damage.

### 1.3 Classification of Switchgear by Voltage Level

| Class | Voltage Range | Application | Typical Devices |
|-------|--------------|-------------|-----------------|
| **LT (Low Tension)** | Up to **1000 V AC** | Industrial & domestic | MCB, MCCB, SDF, HRC Fuse |
| **HT (High Tension)** | 1 kV to **33 kV** | Distribution substations | VCB, SF₆, ACB |
| **EHT (Extra High Tension)** | Above **33 kV** up to 765 kV | Transmission | SF₆ GIS, Bulk Oil CB |

### 1.4 Protective Devices — The "Bodyguards" of Electrical Systems

A **protective device** is an apparatus that automatically disconnects a circuit whenever an abnormal condition (overload, short circuit, earth fault, over-voltage) occurs. The principal protective devices covered under KTU Module 3 are:

- **Fuses** (rewirable & HRC type)
- **Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCB)**
- **Moulded Case Circuit Breakers (MCCB)**
- **Air Circuit Breakers (ACB)**
- **Vacuum Circuit Breakers (VCB)**
- **SF₆ Circuit Breakers**
- **Earth Leakage Circuit Breakers (ELCB / RCCB)**
- **Overload Relays** (Bimetallic, Electronic)

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Always mention the **relevant IS (Bureau of Indian Standards) code** for any device you describe. For instance:
> - MCB → **IS 8828**
> - MCCB → **IS 13947-2**
> - HRC Fuse → **IS 13703**
> - ACB → **IS 13947-2**
> This shows the examiner you have **industry-aligned knowledge**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principles of Switchgear

A switchgear system operates on a **four-stage closed-loop logic**:

1. **Sensing Stage**: CTs step down high primary currents (e.g., 800 A) to a standardized secondary current of **1 A or 5 A**. PTs step down voltage to **110 V / $\sqrt{3}$** secondary.
2. **Comparison Stage**: The relay compares the sensed quantity (current/voltage) against a **preset threshold (pickup value)**.
3. **Decision Stage**: If the threshold is breached for a predefined duration (time-delay), the relay energizes its trip coil.
4. **Actuation Stage**: The circuit breaker's trip mechanism opens the contacts, extinguishing the arc.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Pickup Setting vs. Plug Setting Multiplier (PSM):**
> The **pickup current** is the minimum current at which the relay begins to operate. It is calculated as:
> $$\text{Pickup Current} = \text{CT Ratio} \times \text{Plug Setting (PS)}$$

## 2.2 Fuses — The Simplest Protective Device

A fuse is a **sacrificial device** with a metallic element (tin, lead, silver, zinc) that melts when the current exceeds a safe value for a sufficient time.

### 2.2.1 Types of Fuses

| Type | Construction | Application | Current Range |
|------|-------------|-------------|---------------|
| **Rewirable Fuse (Kit-Kat)** | Porcelain base, tinned copper wire | Domestic, small loads | Up to **200 A** |
| **Cartridge Fuse (HRC)** | Ceramic body, silver element | Industrial, motor protection | Up to **1250 A** |
| **D-Type Cartridge** | Conical ends, ferrule contacts | Distribution | Up to **63 A** |
| **NH (Knife Blade) Fuse** | Bolted blade contacts | Heavy industrial | Up to **1250 A** |
| **Dropout Fuse** | Auto-drop mechanism | Pole-mounted HT | Up to **100 A** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HRC (High Rupturing Capacity) Fuse — Most Examined Concept:**
> An HRC fuse can safely interrupt fault currents up to **80 kA at 415 V**. The element is enclosed in a **ceramic body (steatite)** filled with **chemically pure quartz sand**. When the element melts, the arc vaporizes the silver, and the **silica sand** quenches the arc by absorbing thermal energy.

### 2.2.2 Fuse Selection Formula

The rated current of a fuse element is determined based on the type of load:

$$I_{fuse} = I_{motor} \times \text{Duty Factor}$$

For a **squirrel-cage induction motor with direct-on-line (DOL) starting**:
$$I_{fuse} = \frac{I_{starting}}{3} \approx \frac{6 \times I_{FL}}{3} = 2 \times I_{FL}$$

This is because the starting current is approximately **6 times** the full-load current, and the fuse should not blow during starting (which lasts ~5–10 seconds).

## 2.3 Circuit Breakers — The "Re-closable" Protective Switch

A circuit breaker is defined as a **mechanical switching device capable of making, carrying, and breaking currents under normal and abnormal circuit conditions** (per IS 13947-1).

### 2.3.1 Classification by Arc Quenching Medium

| Type | Arc Quenching Medium | Voltage Class | Breaking Capacity |
|------|----------------------|---------------|--------------------|
| **MCB** | Air (magnetic + thermal) | LT only | Up to **25 kA** |
| **MCCB** | Air (thermal-magnetic) | LT | Up to **100 kA** |
| **ACB** | Air (with arc chutes) | LT (up to 1000 V) | Up to **100 kA** |
| **VCB** | Vacuum | HT (up to 36 kV) | Up to **40 kA** |
| **SF₆ CB** | Sulphur Hexafluoride gas | HT/EHT | Up to **63 kA** |
| **OCB (Oil CB)** | Insulating oil | HT (legacy) | Up to **31.5 kA** |

### 2.3.2 MCB — The Most Common Protective Device in Buildings

**Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCBs)** are the most widely used protective devices in LT installations. They combine two protection principles in a single compact unit:

1. **Thermal Trip (Bimetallic Strip)** — Protects against **overloads** (slow, sustained overcurrents). The bimetallic strip bends when heated and trips the mechanism.
2. **Magnetic Trip (Solenoid)** — Protects against **short circuits** (instantaneous, high-magnitude currents). The solenoid plunger trips the latch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield MCB Trip Curves (IS 8828):**
> - **B Curve**: Trips at **3 to 5 times** rated current. Used for **resistive loads** (heaters, lighting).
> - **C Curve**: Trips at **5 to 10 times** rated current. Used for **inductive loads** (small motors, transformers).
> - **D Curve**: Trips at **10 to 20 times** rated current. Used for **high inrush loads** (welding, large motors, transformers).

### 2.3.3 MCCB — The Industrial Workhorse

**Moulded Case Circuit Breakers (MCCBs)** are used for higher current ratings (up to **1600 A**) and higher breaking capacities. They offer:

- **Adjustable thermal and magnetic trip settings**
- **Earth fault protection (optional)**
- **Shunt trip coil for remote operation**
- **Under-voltage (UV) and Over-voltage (OV) releases**

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** When asked to "select an MCB/MCCB," always specify the **breaking capacity (Icu)**, **trip curve (B/C/D)**, **number of poles**, and the **IS standard**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{MVA \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{LL}}$ | Symmetrical Short-Circuit Current | A |
| 2 | $MVA_{sc} = \dfrac{V_{LL}^2}{Z_{sys}}$ | System Short-Circuit MVA | MVA |
| 3 | $I_{br} = \dfrac{MVA_{sc}}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{LL}} \times 10^6$ | Breaking Current of Breaker | A |
| 4 | $I_{make} = 1.8 \times \sqrt{2} \times I_{sym} = 2.55 \times I_{sym}$ | Peak Making Current (DC Component) | A |
| 5 | $I_{cw} = I_{sc}$ for 1 sec | Short-Time Withstand Current | A |
| 6 | $PSM = \dfrac{\text{Fault Current}}{\text{CT Ratio} \times \text{Plug Setting}}$ | Plug Setting Multiplier | dimensionless |
| 7 | $t = \dfrac{0.14 \times K}{PSM^{0.02} - 1}$ | IDMT Relay Operating Time (Standard Inverse) | seconds |
| 8 | $I_{fuse} = (1.5 \text{ to } 2.0) \times I_{FL}$ for motor | Fuse Rating for Motor Circuit | A |
| 9 | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} \times L \times I^2$ | Energy Let-Through (I²t value) | A²s |
| 10 | $R_{A} \times I_{a} \leq 50 \text{ V}$ | Earth Fault Loop Impedance (BS 7671 / IS 3043) | V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:**
> - $MVA$ = Fault level in MVA
> - $V_{LL}$ = Line-to-line voltage in Volts
> - $Z_{sys}$ = System impedance in ohms
> - $K$ = Relay constant (typically 0.14 for SI type)
> - $I_a$ = Disconnection time current (typically 5 A for 0.4 s)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **practical electrical design and estimation** (the core theme of PCEET602), switchgear selection directly impacts:

- **Capital Cost**: 25–35% of LT switchboard cost
- **Discrimination (Selectivity)**: Ensuring only the nearest upstream device trips during a fault
- **Coordination Study**: Cascading breakers (MCCB → MCB) to achieve discrimination
- **Arc Flash Safety**: IEEE 1584 calculation for incident energy in kA

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse **breaking capacity** with **making capacity**. Remember: $I_{make} = 2.55 \times I_{br}$ (asymmetry factor of 1.8 × $\sqrt{2}$).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Symmetrical Breaking Current

Consider a three-phase system with a fault level of $F$ MVA at a line voltage $V_{LL}$. We want to find the symmetrical RMS breaking current that any circuit breaker at this bus must be able to interrupt.

### Step 1: Convert fault MVA to complex power

$$S_{sc} = F \times 10^6 \text{ VA} = 3 \times V_{ph} \times I_{sc}^*$$

where $V_{ph}$ is the phase voltage and $I_{sc}$ is the fault current per phase.

### Step 2: Express in terms of line-to-line voltage

Since $V_{ph} = \dfrac{V_{LL}}{\sqrt{3}}$:

$$S_{sc} = 3 \times \frac{V_{LL}}{\sqrt{3}} \times I_{sc} = \sqrt{3} \times V_{LL} \times I_{sc}$$

### Step 3: Solve for the fault current

$$I_{sc} = \frac{S_{sc}}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{LL}} = \frac{F \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{LL}}$$

### Step 4: Apply the asymmetry factor (DC offset)

The peak making current includes the **DC component** which decays based on the $X/R$ ratio. Standard practice uses an asymmetry factor of **1.8** for a system $X/R \leq 15$:

$$I_{peak} = 1.8 \times \sqrt{2} \times I_{sc} = 2.55 \times I_{sc}$$

### Step 5: Numerical Example

> **Given:** Fault level = **500 MVA**, $V_{LL}$ = **11 kV** (typical HT substation)
> 
> **Find:** Symmetrical breaking current and peak making current.

**Substitute into the formula:**

$$I_{sc} = \frac{500 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 11 \times 10^3}$$

$$I_{sc} = \frac{500 \times 10^6}{19.052 \times 10^3} = \frac{500{,}000{,}000}{19{,}052.6}$$

$$I_{sc} = 26{,}244 \text{ A} \approx 26.24 \text{ kA}$$

**Peak making current:**

$$I_{peak} = 2.55 \times 26.24 = 66.91 \text{ kA}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A VCB installed at this 11 kV bus must have a **breaking capacity ≥ 26.24 kA** and a **making capacity ≥ 66.91 kA**. This is why industrial VCBs are typically rated at **40 kA / 104 kA peak** to provide a **safety margin**.

## 3.2 Derivation: IDMT Relay Operating Time

The **Inverse Definite Minimum Time (IDMT)** relay characteristic is mathematically defined by the British Standard BS 142 equation:

$$t = \frac{0.14 \times K}{PSM^{0.02} - 1}$$

### Step 1: Define the Plug Setting Multiplier (PSM)

$$PSM = \frac{I_{fault}}{I_{pickup}} = \frac{I_{fault}}{CT \text{ ratio} \times PS}$$

### Step 2: Substitute a typical scenario

> **Given:** CT ratio = 200/5, Plug Setting = 1.0 (i.e., 50% of CT primary = 100 A pickup), Fault current = 4000 A, Time Multiplier Setting (TMS) = K = 0.6

**Calculate PSM:**

$$PSM = \frac{4000}{200 \times 1.0} = 20$$

**Substitute into the IDMT equation:**

$$t = \frac{0.14 \times 0.6}{(20)^{0.02} - 1}$$

**Evaluate the exponent:**

$$(20)^{0.02} = e^{0.02 \times \ln(20)} = e^{0.02 \times 2.9957} = e^{0.05991} = 1.0618$$

**Final calculation:**

$$t = \frac{0.084}{1.0618 - 1} = \frac{0.084}{0.0618} = 1.359 \text{ seconds}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** For a 4000 A fault (which is 20 times the pickup), the relay will operate and trip the breaker in **1.36 seconds**. This intentional time delay allows downstream protection to operate first, achieving **discrimination**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Let-Through Energy (I²t) for HRC Fuse

The energy let-through determines the **discrimination** between upstream and downstream devices.

### Step 1: Energy stored in the magnetic field

The total energy dissipated in a fault is:

$$E = \int_0^{t_{clear}} i^2(t) \cdot R \, dt$$

For an HRC fuse operating in its current-limiting zone (currents > 5× rated), the element melts and clears in **< 5 milliseconds**. The pre-arcing I²t is typically specified by the manufacturer.

### Step 2: Total I²t for discrimination

$$\text{Total } I^2t = \text{Pre-arcing } I^2t + \text{Arcing } I^2t$$

### Step 3: Discrimination rule

For **total discrimination** between two fuses in series:

$$(I^2t)_{upstream, \text{ pre-arcing}} > (I^2t)_{downstream, \text{ total}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** The upstream fuse's **pre-arcing** I²t must exceed the downstream fuse's **total** I²t. Conflating these two quantities is the #1 reason students lose marks.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Protective Devices

| Load Type | Recommended Protective Device | Trip Curve / Class | Reasoning |
|-----------|------------------------------|--------------------|-----------|
| **Incandescent lighting** | MCB | B Curve (3–5× In) | No inrush, resistive load |
| **Fluorescent lighting** | MCB | C Curve (5–10× In) | Inrush from chokes |
| **LED drivers** | MCB | C Curve | Capacitive inrush |
| **Small motors (< 5 HP)** | MCB + Thermal Overload Relay | C Curve | Manageable inrush |
| **Large motors (> 10 HP)** | MCCB + Star-Delta starter | C/D Curve | Very high starting current |
| **Distribution feeders** | MCCB | Adjustable | Coordination required |
| **Capacitor banks** | MCCB with HRC fuse | D Curve | Switching surges |
| **Welding transformers** | MCB | D Curve (10–20× In) | Severe inrush |
| **HT Motors** | VCB + Differential Relay | IDMT | High fault level |
| **Transformers** | Differential + Overcurrent | Biased differential | Inrush up to 12× In for 0.2 s |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Switchgear Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Power Source - Grid or Generator] --> B[Isolator - Off Load Disconnector]
    B --> C[Current Transformer CT - Sensing]
    C --> D[Protection Relay - Decision Logic]
    D --> E[Trip Coil - Actuation]
    E --> F[Circuit Breaker - Arc Quenching]
    F --> G[Busbar - Load Distribution]
    G --> H1[Outgoing Feeder 1 - MCB]
    G --> H2[Outgoing Feeder 2 - MCCB]
    G --> H3[Outgoing Feeder 3 - HRC Fuse]
    H1 --> I1[Load - Lighting]
    H2 --> I2[Load - Motor]
    H3 --> I3[Load - Heater]
    D -.feedback.- A
    F -.aux contact.- D
```

## 4.2 Sequence Flow: Fault Detection and Isolation

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant F as Fault Occurs
    participant CT as Current Transformer
    participant R as Protective Relay
    participant TC as Trip Coil
    participant CB as Circuit Breaker
    participant L as Load
    participant A as Alarm/Indication

    F->>CT: High current flows through primary
    CT->>R: Reduced secondary current (1A or 5A)
    R->>R: Compare with pickup setting
    R->>R: Check PSM and TMS
    R->>TC: Energize trip coil
    TC->>CB: Mechanically unlatch contacts
    CB->>CB: Arc quenching in medium
    CB->>L: Circuit interrupted
    CB->>A: Auxiliary contact closes
    A->>A: Annunciator / SCADA alarm
    Note over R,CB: Total time: 50-100 ms for instantaneous trips
```

## 4.3 Classification Tree: Protective Devices

```mermaid
graph TD
    PD[Protective Devices] --> F1[Fuses]
    PD --> CB1[Circuit Breakers]
    PD --> REL1[Relays]
    PD --> EL1[Earth Fault Devices]

    F1 --> FW[Rewirable - Kit Kat]
    F1 --> FHRC[HRC Cartridge]
    F1 --> FDT[Dropout Type]
    F1 --> FNH[NH Knife Blade]

    CB1 --> LT[LT Breakers]
    CB1 --> HT[HT Breakers]
    LT --> MCB1[MCB - B/C/D Curve]
    LT --> MCCB1[MCCB - Adjustable]
    LT --> ACB1[ACB - Drawout]
    HT --> VCB1[VCB - Vacuum]
    HT --> SFCB1[SF6 Breaker]
    HT --> OCB1[Oil CB - Legacy]

    REL1 --> REM[Electromagnetic]
    REL1 --> RST[Static]
    REL1 --> RNUM[Numerical - IEC 61850]

    EL1 --> ELCB1[Voltage Operated ELCB]
    EL1 --> RCCB1[Current Operated RCCB]
    EL1 --> RCBO1[RCBO - Combined]
```

## 4.4 IDMT Relay Characteristic Curve (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[PSM less than 1.0 - No Trip] --> B[Pickup Threshold]
    B --> C[Inverse Region - PSM 1 to 10]
    C --> D[Very Inverse Region - PSM 10 to 20]
    D --> E[Extremely Inverse - PSM greater than 20]
    E --> F[Definite Minimum Time Floor]
    F --> G[Instantaneous Element - High Set]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The operating time decreases as fault magnitude (PSM) increases, but never falls below the **definite minimum time** (typically 0.1–0.3 seconds). The instantaneous high-set element operates in **< 50 ms** for very high faults (e.g., PSM > 20).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between MCB and MCCB. State the IS standard applicable to each."**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker) | MCCB (Moulded Case Circuit Breaker) |
|-----------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| **Current Range** | Up to **125 A** (typically) | **100 A to 1600 A** |
| **Breaking Capacity** | Up to **25 kA** at 415 V | Up to **100 kA** at 415 V |
| **Trip Settings** | Fixed (thermal-magnetic) | **Adjustable** thermal and magnetic |
| **Application** | Final distribution, sub-circuits | Main distribution, motor feeders |
| **IS Standard** | **IS 8828** : 1996 | **IS 13947-2** : 1993 |
| **Operation** | Toggle lever, non-adjustable | Rotary handle or motorised |
| **Form** | Compact, fixed | Modular, drawout available |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** A 3-mark answer must include **at least 4 distinct points of difference** + **IS codes** for full marks.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Explain the operating principle of an HRC fuse. Why is it preferred over a rewirable fuse?"**

**Model Answer:**

An **HRC (High Rupturing Capacity) fuse** consists of a **silver element** enclosed in a **ceramic (steatite) body** filled with **chemically pure quartz sand**. The working principle is as follows:

1. When the fault current exceeds the rated value, the **silver element melts** (melting point of silver = **961°C**).
2. The molten silver vaporizes, creating an **arc** in the surrounding silica sand.
3. The **silica sand absorbs the heat energy** from the arc and quenches it within **< 5 ms** in the current-limiting zone.
4. The metal vapor condenses back into the sand, creating a high-resistance **fulgurite** path that extinguishes the arc.

**Why HRC is Preferred Over Rewirable Fuse:**

- **Higher breaking capacity** (up to 80 kA vs 4 kA for rewirable).
- **Faster operation** with **inverse time characteristic**.
- **No fire or explosion risk** (arc is fully contained).
- **Stable characteristic** (not affected by aging, unlike rewirable wire).
- **Calibrated I²t characteristic** for discrimination studies.
- **Compact size** for given rating.
- **IS 13703** compliance ensures standardized ratings.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**a)** Explain the construction and working of a **Vacuum Circuit Breaker (VCB)** with a neat diagram. State its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**b)** An **11 kV, 25 MVA** substation feeds a downstream 415 V distribution board. The transformer impedance is **8%**. Calculate: **(i)** Fault level at HT bus, **(ii)** Fault level at LT bus (assume cable impedance = 0.02 Ω), **(iii)** Rating of the HT and LT circuit breakers. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution — VCB Construction & Working (7 marks)

**Construction:**

A Vacuum Circuit Breaker consists of the following components:

1. **Vacuum Interrupter Bottle**: A sealed **borosilicate glass or ceramic envelope** at a vacuum level of **10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁸ torr** (≈ 10⁻⁶ kPa).
2. **Fixed Contact**: Connected to the upper terminal, made of **chromium-copper (CuCr)** alloy.
3. **Moving Contact**: Connected to the lower terminal, attached to a bellows-sealed shaft.
4. **Metal Bellows**: Allows the moving contact to translate axially while maintaining vacuum integrity.
5. **Arc Shield**: Prevents metallic vapor deposition on the inner walls of the bottle.
6. **Operating Mechanism**: Spring-charged mechanism for fast contact separation (typically **0.5 m/s to 1 m/s**).

**Working Principle:**

- Under **normal conditions**, the contacts carry the load current in a closed position.
- On receiving a **trip command**, the moving contact separates from the fixed contact.
- The contacts separate over a small distance (typically **8–12 mm**), and an **arc** is drawn in the metal vapor emitted from the cathode.
- The arc is sustained by **ionized metal vapor** (not by gas, since there is no gas).
- At the **first current zero** (within 5–10 ms at 50 Hz), the metal vapor rapidly **condenses** on the arc shield and contact surfaces.
- The arc extinguishes because the **dielectric strength of vacuum** recovers very quickly (within **microseconds**).
- The contacts are now fully separated, and the circuit is isolated.

**Advantages:**

- **No fire/explosion hazard** (no oil or gas medium).
- **Silent operation** (no noise from gas expansion).
- **Low maintenance** (sealed for life, no contact erosion).
- **High dielectric strength** (vacuum recovers faster than any other medium).
- **Long service life** (up to **30,000 operations**).
- **Environmentally friendly** (no SF₆ greenhouse gas).
- **Compact** size for the same rating.

**Limitations:**

- **Voltage limitation** (practically up to **36 kV**, beyond which multi-break chambers needed).
- **High cost** of vacuum interrupters.
- **Inrush switching transients** (current chopping causes voltage spikes — needs surge protection).
- **Contact welding risk** if contact gap closure is improper.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Fault Level Calculation (7 marks)

**Given Data:**

- Transformer rating: $S = 25$ MVA
- HT Voltage: $V_{HT} = 11$ kV
- LT Voltage: $V_{LT} = 415$ V
- Transformer impedance: $Z_T = 8\% = 0.08$ pu
- Cable impedance: $Z_c = 0.02 \, \Omega$

**(i) Fault Level at HT Bus:**

The fault level at the HT bus is determined by the upstream system. Assuming the upstream grid fault level is given or the transformer's short-circuit MVA:

$$MVA_{sc,HT} = \frac{S_{transformer}}{Z_{pu}} = \frac{25}{0.08} = 312.5 \text{ MVA}$$

**Symmetrical breaking current on HT side:**

$$I_{br,HT} = \frac{MVA_{sc,HT} \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{HT}} = \frac{312.5 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 11 \times 10^3}$$

$$I_{br,HT} = \frac{312.5 \times 10^6}{19{,}052.6} = 16{,}402 \text{ A} \approx 16.4 \text{ kA}$$

**(ii) Fault Level at LT Bus:**

First, find the percentage impedance of the cable referred to the transformer base.

Cable resistance referred to HV side:
$$Z_{c,ref} = Z_c \times \left(\frac{V_{HT}}{V_{LT}}\right)^2 = 0.02 \times \left(\frac{11{,}000}{415}\right)^2$$

$$Z_{c,ref} = 0.02 \times (26.506)^2 = 0.02 \times 702.6 = 14.05 \, \Omega$$

Percentage impedance of cable on transformer base (25 MVA, 11 kV):
$$Z_{c,pu} = \frac{14.05 \times 25 \times 10^6}{(11 \times 10^3)^2} = \frac{351.25 \times 10^6}{121 \times 10^6} = 2.9\%$$

**Total impedance at LT bus:**
$$Z_{total} = Z_T + Z_{c,ref} = 8\% + 2.9\% = 10.9\%$$

**Fault MVA at LT bus:**
$$MVA_{sc,LT} = \frac{25}{0.109} = 229.36 \text{ MVA}$$

**Symmetrical breaking current on LT side:**

$$I_{br,LT} = \frac{229.36 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 415} = \frac{229.36 \times 10^6}{718.8} = 319{,}077 \text{ A} \approx 319.1 \text{ kA}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often convert the 8% impedance to ohms using the **wrong voltage side**. Always convert to a **common MVA base** first, then to ohms. Also, the percentage of cable impedance on the transformer base is **NOT** simply $Z_c / Z_{system}$.

**(iii) Rating of Circuit Breakers:**

**HT Breaker (11 kV, VCB):**
- Rated voltage: **12 kV** (next standard)
- Rated normal current: **1320 A** (next standard above full load: $\frac{25 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 11 \times 10^3} = 1312$ A)
- Breaking capacity: **25 kA** (next standard above 16.4 kA)
- Short-time withstand (1 sec): **25 kA**
- Making capacity: **2.55 × 25 = 63.75 kA** (rounded to **65 kA**)

**LT Breaker (415 V, ACB):**
- Rated voltage: **690 V** (next standard)
- Rated normal current: **3500 A** (next standard above $\frac{25 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 415} = 34{,}769$ A — use an ACB of 40 kA class)
- Breaking capacity: **50 kA** (next standard above 31.9 kA, but the symmetrical breaking current is 31.9 kA; choose 35 kA or 50 kA)
- Short-time withstand (1 sec): **50 kA**
- Making capacity: **2.55 × 50 = 127.5 kA**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**a)** Discuss the various **trip curves (B, C, D)** of MCBs as per IS 8828. Which curve is suitable for **motor protection** and why? **(7 marks)**

**b)** A **three-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz** industrial feeder has a **100 kVA** connected load with a power factor of **0.85 lagging**. The feeder length is **50 m** using 4-core aluminium armoured cable. Calculate: **(i)** Full-load current, **(ii)** Voltage drop, **(iii)** Rating of MCB and cable cross-section. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution — MCB Trip Curves (7 marks)

As per **IS 8828 (1996)**, MCBs are classified into three trip curve categories based on their **instantaneous tripping current (magnetic trip range)**:

| Curve | Instantaneous Trip Range | Application | Typical Loads |
|-------|--------------------------|-------------|---------------|
| **B Curve** | **3 to 5** × $I_n$ | Resistive loads | Lighting, heaters, sockets |
| **C Curve** | **5 to 10** × $I_n$ | Inductive loads | Small motors, transformers, fluorescent lamps |
| **D Curve** | **10 to 20** × $I_n$ | High inrush loads | Large motors, welding, X-ray equipment, capacitor banks |

**Detailed Explanation:**

1. **B Curve MCBs:** The instantaneous trip is set at **3–5 times** the rated current. These are used where the connected load has **negligible inrush current**. Examples include **incandescent lamps, electric heaters, and general-purpose socket outlets**. The low magnetic trip setting ensures quick disconnection for even small overloads.

2. **C Curve MCBs:** The instantaneous trip is set at **5–10 times** the rated current. This is the **most commonly used curve** in industrial installations. It is designed to handle the **starting inrush of small motors** (typically 5–6× FLC) and **transformer magnetizing inrush** (up to 8× rated for a few cycles).

3. **D Curve MCBs:** The instantaneous trip is set at **10–20 times** the rated current. This curve is used for **highly inductive loads with severe inrush**, such as:
   - **Welding transformers** (inrush 10–15× rated).
   - **X-ray machines** (inrush up to 20× rated).
   - **Large squirrel-cage motors with direct-on-line (DOL) starting** (inrush 6–8× FLC, but with high inertia loads like compressors).
   - **Capacitor banks** (inrush can be 15–20× rated due to switching transients).

**Best Choice for Motor Protection:**

**C Curve MCB** is most commonly used for small motor protection, because:

- The **starting current of a standard induction motor** is typically **5–6 times** the full-load current, lasting **5–10 seconds**.
- A **B Curve MCB** would nuisance-trip during starting.
- A **D Curve MCB** would provide excessive tolerance, possibly allowing sustained overloads.
- **C Curve** offers the optimal balance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** For **large motors** (above 5 HP) with **direct-on-line starting** and **high inertia loads** (e.g., compressors, crushers), a **D Curve MCB** is required because the inrush may exceed 10× FLC. A C Curve would nuisance-trip.

> [!TIP]
> **Note:** For motors above 5 HP, KTU board expects you to recommend **MCCB with adjustable thermal and magnetic trip settings**, not MCB. Always consider the **starting method** (DOL, Star-Delta, VFD) in your answer.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Cable and MCB Sizing (7 marks)

**Given Data:**

- Three-phase system: $V_L = 415$ V, $f = 50$ Hz
- Connected load: $S = 100$ kVA
- Power factor: $\cos \phi = 0.85$ lagging
- Feeder length: $L = 50$ m
- Cable type: 4-core **aluminium armoured** cable
- Permissible voltage drop: **3%** of 415 V (as per IS 732) = 12.45 V

**(i) Full-Load Current:**

$$I_{FL} = \frac{S \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L} = \frac{100 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415}$$

$$I_{FL} = \frac{100{,}000}{718.8} = 139.1 \text{ A}$$

**(ii) Voltage Drop:**

For a three-phase system, the voltage drop formula is:

$$V_{drop} = \sqrt{3} \times I \times L \times (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$$

For aluminium armoured cable up to 25 mm², approximate values are:
- $R \approx 1.55 \, \Omega/\text{km}$ at 50°C (for 50 mm² cable)
- $X \approx 0.08 \, \Omega/\text{km}$

**Assuming cable size of 50 mm²:**

$$V_{drop} = \sqrt{3} \times 139.1 \times 0.05 \times (1.55 \times 0.85 + 0.08 \times 0.527)$$

$$V_{drop} = 1.732 \times 139.1 \times 0.05 \times (1.3175 + 0.04216)$$

$$V_{drop} = 12.05 \times 1.3596 = 16.38 \text{ V}$$

**Percentage voltage drop:**

$$\%V_{drop} = \frac{16.38}{415} \times 100 = 3.95\%$$

This **exceeds the permissible 3%**, so we must **upsize the cable**.

**Trying 70 mm² cable:**
- $R \approx 1.10 \, \Omega/\text{km}$
- $X \approx 0.08 \, \Omega/\text{km}$

$$V_{drop} = \sqrt{3} \times 139.1 \times 0.05 \times (1.10 \times 0.85 + 0.08 \times 0.527)$$

$$V_{drop} = 12.05 \times (0.935 + 0.042) = 12.05 \times 0.977 = 11.77 \text{ V}$$

$$\%V_{drop} = \frac{11.77}{415} \times 100 = 2.84\%$$

> **Within the 3% limit.** ✓

**(iii) MCB and Cable Selection:**

- **Cable Size:** **3½ core × 70 mm² aluminium armoured cable**
- **MCB Rating:** 
  - **Standard MCB rating** = 1.25 × $I_{FL}$ (as per IS 8828) = 1.25 × 139.1 = 173.9 A
  - **Nearest standard rating** = **200 A** MCB
  - **Trip curve** = **C Curve** (for mixed loads, slight inrush expected)
  - **Breaking capacity** = **10 kA** (typical for industrial installations)
  - **Number of poles** = **3P + N (TPN)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Pitfalls:**
> 1. Using **$|V|$** or **$|I|$** (absolute values) in formula tables breaks the markdown. Use **$\vert V \vert$** instead.
> 2. Forgetting to **apply the derating factor** for ambient temperature, grouping, and depth of laying.
> 3. Selecting MCB = $I_{FL}$ instead of $1.25 \times I_{FL}$ (this is a common mistake that costs 1–2 marks).
> 4. Not specifying the **trip curve** of the MCB.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Module 3: Switch Gears and Protective Devices**

### 🔹 Core Definitions
- ✅ **Switchgear**: Assembly of devices for switching, controlling, and protecting electrical circuits.
- ✅ **Fuse**: Sacrificial protective device with a melting element.
- ✅ **Circuit Breaker**: Re-closable mechanical switching device.
- ✅ **Relay**: Sensing device that detects faults and commands breaker operation.
- ✅ **Discrimination (Selectivity)**: Only the nearest upstream device operates.

### 🔹 IS Standards (Must-Memorize)
- ✅ **IS 8828** → MCB
- ✅ **IS 13947-2** → MCCB and ACB
- ✅ **IS 13703** → HRC Fuse
- ✅ **IS 2705** → Current Transformer
- ✅ **IS 3156** → Voltage Transformer
- ✅ **IS 3842** → Application guide for ACBs

### 🔹 Critical Formulas
- ✅ $I_{sc} = \dfrac{MVA \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{LL}}$
- ✅ $I_{make} = 2.55 \times I_{br}$
- ✅ $PSM = \dfrac{I_{fault}}{CT \text{ ratio} \times \text{Plug Setting}}$
- ✅ $t = \dfrac{0.14 \times K}{PSM^{0.02} - 1}$ (IDMT)
- ✅ Fuse for motor: $I_{fuse} \approx 2 \times I_{FL}$ (DOL)
- ✅ MCB sizing: $1.25 \times I_{FL}$

### 🔹 MCB Trip Curves (3-5 / 5-10 / 10-20)
- ✅ **B** = Lighting, Heaters
- ✅ **C** = Small Motors, Transformers (most common)
- ✅ **D** = Welding, Capacitors, Large DOL Motors

### 🔹 Practical Selection Logic
1. Determine **load current**.
2. Apply **diversity and derating factors**.
3. Choose **device type** (MCB/MCCB/ACB/VCB).
4. Select **trip curve** based on load.
5. Verify **breaking capacity** ≥ fault level.
6. Check **discrimination** with upstream device.
7. Confirm **IS standard** compliance.

### 🔹 Top 5 Board-Exam Mistakes to Avoid
- ❌ Confusing **breaking** and **making** capacities.
- ❌ Using the **same trip curve** for all loads.
- ❌ Forgetting to mention **IS standards**.
- ❌ Not showing the **MVA base conversion** in fault calculations.
- ❌ Ignoring **ambient derating** of cables and breakers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Procedure for HT Connection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Procedure for HT Connection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HT (High Tension) Connection** is an electrical service connection in which the **supply voltage exceeds 650 V** between phases (or 400 V between phase and neutral), as classified under the **Indian Electricity Rules, 1956 (Rule 2(av))** and followed by the **Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB)** / **Kerala State Electricity Regulatory Commission (KSERC)** regulations.

In practical KTU engineering context, HT connection covers:
- **11 kV** (most common for industrial consumers)
- **22 kV** (large industries, IT parks)
- **33 kV** (heavy industries, EHT consumers)
- **66 kV / 110 kV / 220 kV** (Extra High Tension – EHT)

> [!NOTE]
> **Eligibility Threshold (KSEB LT-IV tariff, 2024):** Loads generally **above 75 kVA / 100 kVA** (single-phase/three-phase) or where the consumer demands HT supply are eligible. Typical HT sanction is granted for **100 kVA and above**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are filling a **large swimming pool** with water. You wouldn't use a small drinking-water straw; you'd use a **large fire-hose** so water flows faster. Similarly:

- **LT (Low Tension)** is like a *small pipe* — used for homes, small shops (≤ 250 V phase-to-neutral).
- **HT (High Tension)** is like a *large industrial pipeline* — used for factories, hospitals, malls where the **demand (kVA) is huge**.

**Why HT at all?**
- **Lower Current** → smaller cable cross-section (saves copper cost)
- **Lower I²R Losses** → higher efficiency over long distances
- **Better Voltage Regulation** → less voltage drop

$$\text{Current } I = \frac{kVA \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{LL}}$$

For the same 1000 kVA load:
- At **LT (415 V)** → $I = \frac{1000 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415} \approx 1391 \text{ A}$ (very high!)
- At **HT (11 kV)** → $I = \frac{1000 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 11000} \approx 52.5 \text{ A}$ (very manageable!)

---

## 1.3 Regulatory Framework (KTU Syllabus Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The entire HT connection procedure in Kerala is governed by:
> 1. **Indian Electricity Act, 2003**
> 2. **Indian Electricity Rules, 1956** (especially Rules 3, 5, 6, 29, 47, 51, 63)
> 3. **Central Electricity Authority (CEA) Regulations, 2010 – Safety & Electric Supply**
> 4. **KSERC (Kerala State Electricity Regulatory Commission) Supply Code & Conditions of Supply**
> 5. **KSEB Schedule of Tariff and General Conditions**

---

## 1.4 Key Voltage Bands (Must Memorize)

| Class | Voltage Range (Ph-Ph) | Typical User |
|---|---|---|
| LT | ≤ 650 V (i.e., 415 V 3φ) | Domestic, small commercial |
| HT | 650 V – 33 kV | Industries, large buildings |
| EHT | > 33 kV | Power intensive industries, substations |

---

## 1.5 Pre-Requisite Knowledge

Before proceeding, the student must be familiar with:
- Single Line Diagram (SLD) of HT consumer
- HT switchgear (VCB, SF6, ACB)
- Current Transformers (CT) and Potential Transformers (PT)
- Protective relays (IDMT, instantaneous)
- Earthing standards (IS 3043)
- Maximum Demand indicator & Tariff structure

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Overall Procedure — The 11-Stage HT Connection Workflow

The HT connection procedure can be broadly classified into **THREE PHASES**:
- **Phase A — Pre-Approval (Administrative)**
- **Phase B — Execution (Civil + Electrical)**
- **Phase C — Energization (Inspection + Metering)**

Below is the master process flow as per KSEB / KSERC norms:

### PHASE A: Pre-Approval Stage
1. **Application** by consumer to Assistant Engineer (Ele. Distribution) — using **Form A** (or online KSEB portal).
2. **Registration & Scrutiny** of documents.
3. **Field Inspection** by KSEB officials (load feasibility, nearest HT line, transformer capacity check).
4. **Estimate Preparation** — line extension, transformer, metering charges, service connection charges.
5. **Load Sanction Order (LSO)** issued.
6. **Agreement Execution** between consumer and KSEB (in stamped paper / digital).
7. **Security Deposit (SD)** remitted by consumer.

### PHASE B: Execution Stage
8. **Construction of HT line / Bay extension** by KSEB (consumer-funded deposit work).
9. **Consumer-side installation** of:
   - HT panel (VCB/SF6 + CTs + PTs + Relays + Metering cubicle)
   - Step-down transformer (if load is to be used at LT)
   - LT panel & APFC panel
   - Earthing system (pipe/plate earth — resistance ≤ **1 Ω** for HT)
   - Lightning protection
10. **Wiring & Cable Laying** (HT cable: XLPE armoured, IS 7098 Part-II).

### PHASE C: Energization Stage
11. **Inspection by Electrical Inspector (Govt. of Kerala)** — under CEA Regulations 2010, Regulation-32.
12. **Approval & Issuing of Sanction for Energization.**
13. **Meter Installation** (HT Tri-vector meter with CT/PT).
14. **Charging (Energization)** of the consumer's installation.

> [!NOTE]
> For voltages **above 250 V (i.e., HT)**, the approval of the **Electrical Inspector is MANDATORY** under Rule-63 of IE Rules 1956 and Regulation-32 of CEA Safety Regulations 2010.

---

## 2.2 Documents Required for HT Application

| # | Document | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Application Form (Form-A) | Consumer request |
| 2 | ID & Address Proof | KYC |
| 3 | Site Plan / Building Plan | Layout verification |
| 4 | Ownership / Lease Deed | Right of usage |
| 5 | Load List with Phasing | Sanction calculation |
| 6 | Single Line Diagram (SLD) | Technical approval |
| 7 | Electrical Layout Drawing | Safety assessment |
| 8 | Contractor License Copy (HT grade) | Validity check |
| 9 | Equipment Specifications | Compliance |
| 10 | No-Objection Certificates (Fire, Pollution, Factory) | Statutory compliance |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Standard / Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Three-phase current | $I = \dfrac{kVA \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{LL}}$ | A |
| 2 | HT/LT Current ratio | $I_{HT} = I_{LT} \times \dfrac{V_{LT}}{V_{HT}}$ | A |
| 3 | Earth resistance (HT) | $R_{e} \leq 1 \, \Omega$ | IS 3043 |
| 4 | Earth resistance (LT) | $R_{e} \leq 5 \, \Omega$ | IS 3043 |
| 5 | Body resistance (body earth) | $R_{b} \leq 1 \, \Omega$ | IS 3043 |
| 6 | Trip time (IDMT) | $t = \dfrac{0.14 \times TMS}{PSM^{0.02} - 1}$ sec | Standard IDMT |
| 7 | Plug Setting Multiplier | $PSM = \dfrac{I_{fault}}{I_{pickup}}$ | — |
| 8 | Meter multiplying factor | $MF = CT_{ratio} \times PT_{ratio}$ | — |
| 9 | Maximum Demand | $MD = \dfrac{kVA_{sanctioned}}{kVA_{total}}$ × 100 % | % |
| 10 | Demand Factor | $DF = \dfrac{Max.\,Load}{Connected\,Load}$ | < 1 |
| 11 | Diversity Factor | $DiF = \dfrac{\sum\,Individual\,Max\,Loads}{Peak\,Combined\,Load}$ | > 1 |
| 12 | Voltage Drop (3φ) | $\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$ | Volts |
| 13 | Short-circuit kA at LV side | $I_{sc,LV} = \dfrac{MVA_{sc}}{1.732 \times V_{LV}} \times 10^3$ | kA |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

This procedure is followed in:
- **Industrial estates** (factories, refineries, cement plants)
- **Large commercial complexes** (IT parks, malls, hospitals, hotels)
- **Renewable integration** (solar/wind farms connecting at 11/22 kV)
- **Educational institutions** (university main intake, large campus substations)

A B.Tech Electrical graduate working in:
- **KSEB / Discoms** — handles LSO & approvals
- **HT contracting firms** — prepares estimates, SLDs, executes installation
- **Consultancy** — designs HT panels, transformers, earthing
- **Inspection wing** — validates safety compliance

…must master this topic in entirety.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Why HT Reduces Conductor Size — Numerical Proof

**Problem:** Compare the conductor cross-section required to transmit **2000 kVA** over a distance of **500 m** with permissible voltage drop of **5%** at:
- Case (a) LT — 415 V, 3φ
- Case (b) HT — 11 kV, 3φ

**Assumed values:**
- $\cos\phi = 0.85$ (lagging)
- $\rho_{Cu} = 0.0172 \, \Omega \cdot mm^2/m$ at 20 °C
- $V_{drop,\,allowed} = 5\%$

### Step 1 — Calculate Line Current

**Case (a) — LT at 415 V:**

$$I_{LT} = \frac{2000 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415} = \frac{2\,000\,000}{718.9}$$

$$\boxed{I_{LT} \approx 2782.4 \text{ A}}$$

**Case (b) — HT at 11 kV:**

$$I_{HT} = \frac{2000 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 11000} = \frac{2\,000\,000}{19052.6}$$

$$\boxed{I_{HT} \approx 104.97 \text{ A}}$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Allowed Voltage Drop

**Case (a) — LT:**
$$\Delta V_{LT} = 0.05 \times 415 = 20.75 \text{ V}$$

**Case (b) — HT:**
$$\Delta V_{HT} = 0.05 \times 11000 = 550 \text{ V}$$

### Step 3 — Derive Cross-Section (Neglecting Reactance)

From $R = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{A}$ and $\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot R \cdot L$... wait, corrected for one-way drop:

$$\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot R_{phase}$$

Therefore:

$$A = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot \rho \cdot L}{\Delta V}$$

**Case (a) — LT Conductor Area:**
$$A_{LT} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \times 2782.4 \times 0.0172 \times 500}{20.75}$$

$$A_{LT} = \frac{41\,506.5}{20.75} \approx 2000.3 \, mm^2$$

**That's impossible with single cable** → would need **multiple parallel cables/busbars**.

**Case (b) — HT Conductor Area:**
$$A_{HT} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \times 104.97 \times 0.0172 \times 500}{550}$$

$$A_{HT} = \frac{1563.5}{550} \approx 2.84 \, mm^2$$

**Conclusion:** A standard **4 mm² HT cable suffices**, whereas LT would need ~2000 mm² of copper!

### Step 4 — Cost Implication

| Parameter | LT (415 V) | HT (11 kV) |
|---|---|---|
| Current (A) | 2782.4 | 104.97 |
| Conductor Area (mm²) | ~2000 (impractical) | ~2.84 |
| Standard Cable Size | Multiple runs of 240 mm² | Single 3-core 16 mm² XLPE |
| Total Copper Used | ~30× | 1× |
| I²R Loss (kW) | Very High | Negligible |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** HT distribution is **mandatory** above ~100 kVA load distance to keep copper cost and losses acceptable. This is the **physical justification** for the HT connection procedure.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of HT Connection Standards (Kerala Context)

| Parameter | 11 kV HT | 22 kV HT | 33 kV HT |
|---|---|---|---|
| Typical Sanction Load | 100 kVA – 5 MVA | 2 MVA – 10 MVA | 5 MVA – 50 MVA |
| Standard CT Ratio (metering) | 50/5, 100/5, 200/5 | 100/5, 200/5, 400/5 | 200/5, 400/5, 800/5 |
| Standard PT Ratio | 11000/110 V | 22000/110 V | 33000/110 V |
| Typical Breaker | VCB (11 kV) | VCB / SF6 | SF6 |
| Min. Clearance (ph-ph) | 280 mm | 380 mm | 460 mm |
| Min. Clearance (ph-Earth) | 140 mm | 200 mm | 230 mm |
| Earth Resistance (Max.) | 1 Ω | 1 Ω | 0.5 Ω |
| Lead Time for Connection | 30 – 90 days | 90 – 180 days | 6 – 12 months |

---

## 3.3 Detailed Stage-by-Stage Analytical Walk-Through

### Stage 1: Pre-Application Survey (by Consumer)

The consumer (or consultant) prepares:
- **Load Calculation Sheet** — Lighting, Power, HVAC, Motors
- **Diversity Factor (DiF)** application — typically 1.1 – 1.5
- **Maximum Demand Estimation:**

$$\boxed{MD = \frac{\sum(\text{Load} \times DF)}{DiF}}$$

Example: A factory with connected load = 2500 kVA, DF = 0.7, DiF = 1.3:

$$MD = \frac{2500 \times 0.7}{1.3} = 1346 \, kVA$$

Round up to **1500 kVA** → applied as **11 kV HT** with **1500 kVA transformer**.

### Stage 2: KSEB Field Inspection

The Assistant Engineer verifies:
- **Distance from nearest HT line** (typically < 1 km for 11 kV)
- **Transformer loading margin** in the feeding substation
- **Road access** for line laying
- **Right of Way (RoW)** for poles / UG cable

If existing infrastructure is **inadequate**, KSEB raises an **estimate** including:
- HT line extension cost
- Bay extension in substation
- Service connection charges

### Stage 3: Load Sanction Order (LSO)

The LSO specifies:
- Contract demand (kVA)
- HT/LT voltage
- Tariff category (HT-I Industrial, HT-II Commercial, HT-III Agriculture, etc.)
- Power Factor penalty threshold (typically < 0.9 lagging attracts penalty)

### Stage 4: Estimate and Deposit

The consumer must pay:
- **Service Connection Charges** (one-time)
- **Security Deposit** (refundable, ~₹1000–₹3000 per kVA sanctioned)
- **Cost of line extension** (if any)

### Stage 5: HT Consumer-Side Installation (Engineering Scope)

Required major components:

| Item | Specification |
|---|---|
| HT Panel | 11 kV, 25 kA short-circuit, VCB |
| HT Cable | 3C × 16/25/50 mm² XLPE, armoured, IS 7098 Pt-II |
| CTs | Metering class 0.5S, burden ≥ 5 VA |
| PTs | Metering class 0.5S, 11000/√3 : 110/√3 V |
| Relays | Numerical / Static, IDMT characteristic |
| Earthing | Pipe/Plate, ≤ 1 Ω (combined), IS 3043 |
| Lightning Arrester | 9 kV, 5 kA class, gapless ZnO |
| Transformer | Oil/Dry type, Dyn11 vector group preferred |

### Stage 6: Inspection by Electrical Inspector

The Inspector checks (under Reg. 32 of CEA 2010):
1. **SLD compliance** with approved drawings
2. **Earthing resistance test** (must be ≤ 1 Ω)
3. **Insulation resistance test** (HT cable > 1000 MΩ)
4. **High voltage test** (inter-phase, phase-earth)
5. **Protection relay settings** verification
6. **Safety equipment** (rubber mats, gloves, fire extinguishers)
7. **Danger notice board** at HT yard

### Stage 7: Energization

Once approved, KSEB seals the meter and issues **charging order**. From this point onwards, **billing as per HT tariff** begins.

---

## 3.4 Test Voltages & Acceptance Criteria (For Reference)

| Test | Voltage Applied | Duration | Acceptance |
|---|---|---|---|
| HV Test (HT Cable) | 3 × V₀ + 1000 V (DC) | 15 min | No breakdown |
| Insulation Resistance | 5 kV Megger | 1 min | > 1000 MΩ |
| Earth Resistance | Fall-of-Potential | — | ≤ 1 Ω |
| Phase Sequence | Phase-sequence meter | — | R-Y-B verified |
| CT Polarity | Polarity tester | — | P1–P2 correct |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART — A (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define HT connection as per IE Rules 1956. List any **two** voltage levels classified under HT in Kerala.
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** According to **Rule 2(av) of Indian Electricity Rules, 1956**, "High Tension" or "HT" means any voltage **exceeding 650 V** (phase-to-phase), whether between conductors or between conductor and earth.

**Two voltage levels under HT in Kerala:**
1. **11 kV** — most common for small/medium industries
2. **22 kV** — used for large industries and IT parks
3. (Optional third) **33 kV** — for very large industries

> 💡 *Examiner Note:* Mentioning the rule number (Rule 2(av)) fetches full credit.

---

### **Q2.** What is the **maximum permissible earth resistance** for an HT installation as per IS 3043?
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

- For **HT installations**, the maximum permissible earth resistance is **1 Ω (one Ohm)** as per IS 3043.
- For LT installations, it is 5 Ω.
- For body earthing of large equipment, it is ≤ 1 Ω.

> 💡 *Examiner Note:* A common mistake is writing 5 Ω (LT value). **HT must be ≤ 1 Ω.**

---

## PART — B (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — Module 3, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain, in detail, the **step-by-step procedure for obtaining an HT connection** from KSEB in Kerala. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** List the **mandatory documents** required along with the HT application form. *(3 marks)*

**(c)** What is the role of the **Electrical Inspector** in HT connection approval? *(4 marks)*

---

#### 🔹 Model Answer for Q.A (a):

The HT connection procedure consists of **11 stages** grouped into **3 phases**:

**PHASE A — Pre-Approval (Administrative):**
1. **Submission of Application** in Form-A along with required documents to the concerned **Assistant Engineer (Electrical Distribution)**, either online (KSEB portal) or offline.
2. **Scrutiny and Registration** of the application by the Section Office.
3. **Field Inspection** by KSEB officials to assess feasibility — proximity of HT line, transformer capacity, RoW, etc.
4. **Estimate Preparation** for line extension (if required), transformer augmentation, metering cubicle, etc.
5. **Load Sanction Order (LSO)** issued specifying contract demand, tariff class, PF threshold.
6. **Agreement** between consumer and KSEB executed.
7. **Security Deposit** remitted.

**PHASE B — Execution:**
8. **Construction of HT line / bay** by KSEB on deposit work basis.
9. **Consumer-side installation** of HT panel, transformer, LT panel, APFC, earthing, lightning protection, HT cabling.

**PHASE C — Energization:**
10. **Inspection by Electrical Inspector** (Govt. of Kerala) under **Regulation 32 of CEA Safety Regulations 2010** — verifies SLD, earthing, insulation, protection settings, safety equipment.
11. **Approval, HT meter installation, and energization** — KSEB seals the meter and issues the charging order. From this point onwards, HT billing commences.

*(Answer should also include: who issues each document, what voltages are typical, what standards are followed.)*

---

#### 🔹 Model Answer for Q.A (b):

Mandatory documents:
1. Application Form (Form-A)
2. ID proof (Aadhaar/PAN) and Address proof
3. Site plan / Building plan
4. Ownership / Lease deed
5. Single Line Diagram (SLD)
6. Load list with diversity factor
7. HT contractor's license copy
8. Equipment datasheets (transformer, breaker, CTs, PTs)
9. Test reports of HT cables
10. NOC from Fire Department / Pollution Control Board / Factory Inspector (if applicable)

---

#### 🔹 Model Answer for Q.A (c):

**Role of Electrical Inspector:**
- The **Electrical Inspector of the Government of Kerala** is the statutory authority under **Rule 63 of IE Rules 1956** and **Regulation 32 of CEA Safety & Electric Supply Regulations, 2010**.
- He/she is responsible for **inspiring safety compliance** of all installations operating above **250 V (i.e., HT)**.
- Specifically, the Inspector:
  1. Verifies the SLD against the actual installed equipment.
  2. Witnesses the **earth resistance test** (must be ≤ 1 Ω).
  3. Conducts / witnesses **insulation resistance test** (≥ 1000 MΩ).
  4. Witnesses **high voltage (HV) test** on HT cables.
  5. Checks **protection relay settings** and their coordination.
  6. Verifies presence of **safety equipment** — rubber mats, gloves, fire extinguishers, danger notice boards.
  7. Verifies **CT/PT polarity, ratio, class** (must be 0.5S for metering).
  8. On satisfaction, issues **Sanction for Energization**.
- Without this sanction, KSEB **cannot** energize the consumer's HT installation.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Compare **LT and HT connections** with respect to current, conductor size, voltage drop, losses, and cost for a **2000 kVA load transmitted over 500 m**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **technical requirements for HT consumer substation equipment** — HT panel, transformer, CT/PT, earthing, and lightning arrester. *(7 marks)*

---

#### 🔹 Model Answer for Q.B (a):

For 2000 kVA, 500 m, 5% allowable drop, $\cos\phi = 0.85$:

| Parameter | LT (415 V) | HT (11 kV) |
|---|---|---|
| Line Current $I = kVA \times 10^3 / (\sqrt{3} \times V)$ | 2782.4 A | 105.0 A |
| Allowed Voltage Drop (5%) | 20.75 V | 550 V |
| Conductor Area required | ~2000 mm² (impractical) | ~2.84 mm² |
| Standard Cable | 30× parallel 240 mm² | 1× 16 mm² XLPE |
| Copper consumption | ~30× | 1× |
| I²R Loss | Very high | Negligible |
| Cost of Cabling | Very high | Low |
| Voltage Regulation | Poor | Excellent |
| Safety Risk | Low voltage, manageable | High voltage, requires expert |

**Inference:** HT is **technically superior and economically viable** for loads ≥ 100 kVA and distances beyond ~200 m. *(Full 7 marks if all points covered with numerical justification.)*

---

#### 🔹 Model Answer for Q.B (b):

**Technical Requirements for HT Consumer Substation:**

**(i) HT Panel:**
- **VCB (Vacuum Circuit Breaker)** — 11 kV, 25 kA short-circuit rating, 630 A/800 A/1250 A normal current
- **Busbars:** Copper/tinned copper, adequately sized for the load
- **Protection Relays:** Numerical, IDMT characteristic, with O/C, E/F, REF protections

**(ii) Step-Down Transformer:**
- **Rating:** Equal to contract demand (e.g., 1500 kVA)
- **Vector Group:** **Dyn11** (preferred) — suppresses triplen harmonics
- **Cooling:** ONAN / ONAF
- **Voltage Ratio:** 11000 / 433 V (typical)
- **Impedance:** 6.25% (per IS 2026)

**(iii) CTs (Current Transformers):**
- **Metering CTs:** Class **0.5S**, burden ≥ 5 VA
- **Protection CTs:** Class 5P20 or PS class
- **Ratio:** Selected based on full-load current

**(iv) PTs (Potential Transformers):**
- **Ratio:** 11000/√3 : 110/√3 V
- **Class:** 0.5S for metering, 3P for protection

**(v) Earthing (IS 3043):**
- **Pipe Earth / Plate Earth** with maintenance-free ground enhancement material
- **Combined earth resistance ≤ 1 Ω**
- **Two distinct earth pits** — equipment earth and body earth (inter-connected)
- **Earth strip:** 50 mm × 6 mm GI flat

**(vi) Lightning Arrester:**
- **Gapless Zinc-Oxide (ZnO) type**
- **Rated Voltage:** 9 kV (for 11 kV system)
- **Nominal Discharge Current:** 5 kA or 10 kA (Class-II/III)
- Located at HT line entry point

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Marks Deduction:**
> 1. **Confusing LT/HT limits** — Writing "above 250 V" instead of "above **650 V** between phases" for HT.
> 2. **Wrong earth resistance value** — LT = 5 Ω, HT = 1 Ω. Many students write 5 Ω for HT and lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Not mentioning the governing rule** — Always quote **Rule 2(av), Rule 63 of IE Rules 1956** and **Regulation 32 of CEA Safety Regulations 2010**.
> 4. **Missing the Electrical Inspector step** — Without Inspector's sanction, KSEB **cannot** energize. This is a frequent omission.
> 5. **No numerical justification in Part B** — KTU expects **at least one solved numerical** with full working in 14-mark HT questions.
> 6. **Improper formula syntax** — Writing $\sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot R$ for voltage drop instead of $\sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$.
> 7. **Confusing CT classes** — Metering CT must be **0.5S** (with S suffix), not just 0.5.
> 8. **Skipping phase sequence and polarity checks** in the inspection list.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **HT = voltage > 650 V** (Rule 2(av), IE Rules 1956)
- [ ] Typical HT levels in Kerala: **11 kV, 22 kV, 33 kV**
- [ ] HT eligibility: generally **load > 100 kVA**
- [ ] **11 stages** in 3 phases (Pre-Approval, Execution, Energization)
- [ ] Application **Form-A** submitted to KSEB Assistant Engineer
- [ ] **LSO** = Load Sanction Order — states contract demand, tariff
- [ ] **Electrical Inspector** (Govt. of Kerala) approval is **mandatory** for HT
- [ ] Governing regulation: **CEA Safety & Electric Supply Regulations 2010, Reg-32**
- [ ] **Earth resistance HT ≤ 1 Ω** (IS 3043)
- [ ] **Insulation resistance HT cable ≥ 1000 MΩ**
- [ ] **CT class for metering = 0.5S** (with S suffix)
- [ ] **Vector group Dyn11** preferred for distribution transformer
- [ ] HT reduces current by factor $V_{HT}/V_{LT}$ — saves copper
- [ ] Formula: $I = kVA \times 1000 / (\sqrt{3} \times V_{LL})$
- [ ] Formula: $MF = CT_{ratio} \times PT_{ratio}$ (for HT meter reading)
- [ ] **VCB** for 11 kV, **SF6** for 22/33 kV
- [ ] **Lightning Arrester**: 9 kV ZnO gapless, 5 kA nominal discharge
- [ ] Documents: SLD, Load List, Site Plan, Contractor License, NOCs
- [ ] PF penalty threshold in Kerala: < **0.9 lag**
- [ ] After charging, **HT billing** under HT-I / HT-II / HT-III tariff categories
- [ ] Maximum time for KSEB to provide HT connection (KSERC norms): **30 days** if infrastructure exists, **up to 12 months** if augmentation needed

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
