topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design and Estimation of Quantity of Material Required for Substations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Design and Estimation of Quantity of Material Required for Substations

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Substation Material Estimation** is the systematic engineering process of determining the **quantitative requirement** of all electrical, civil, and structural materials needed for the construction of an electrical substation — including busbars, conductors, insulators, switchgear, protective devices, earthing systems, structural steel, concrete, cables, and accessories — based on the approved **Single Line Diagram (SLD)**, **General Arrangement (GA) drawing**, and applicable **IS/IE rules**.

The estimation process translates design intent into a **Bill of Quantities (BOQ)** and a **Bill of Materials (BOM)**, which form the basis for **tendering, procurement, and execution** of the substation project.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of a substation as a **"household kitchen renovation"**:

| Kitchen Renovation Analogy | Substation Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Blueprint of the kitchen layout | Single Line Diagram (SLD) & GA Drawing |
| List of tiles, cement, wood required | Bill of Quantities (BOQ) |
| Mixer-grinder, oven, chimney specification | Switchgear rating, CT/PT specification |
| Plumber's piping length | Power & control cable length |
| Earthing for lightning | Earth electrode & earth strip quantity |
| Granite countertop size | Busbar length & cross-section |

Just as a kitchen contractor cannot start work without knowing *how much* marble and *how many* bricks are needed, an electrical contractor cannot mobilize to a substation site without a properly engineered **material estimate**. Under-estimation causes project delays; over-estimation inflates project cost.

### 1.3 Classification of Substations (for Estimation Context)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU/IE Rules 1956 (Substation Design):**
> Substations are classified based on **voltage level**, **application**, and **construction type**, and each type demands a different estimation approach.

- **Based on Voltage Level**: HT (11 kV, 33 kV, 66 kV, 110 kV, 220 kV, 400 kV), EHT (≥ 220 kV)
- **Based on Application**: Step-up, Step-down, Distribution, Switching, Converter, Indoor, Outdoor
- **Based on Construction**: Indoor (up to 11 kV), Outdoor (33 kV and above), Pole-mounted, Plinth-mounted, GIS (Gas Insulated)

### 1.4 Standard Reference Materials & Codes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **Indian Standards (IS)** are mandatory reference codes for substation material estimation:
> - **IS 3043** – Code of Practice for Earthing
> - **IS 802** – Use of Structural Steel in Overhead Transmission Line Towers
> - **IS 5613** – HV Switchgear and Controlgear
> - **IS 2705** – Current Transformers
> - **IS 3156** – Voltage Transformers (PTs)
> - **IE Rules 1956 (Amended 2010)** – Indian Electricity Rules
> - **CBIP Manual** – Central Board of Irrigation & Power publication on substations

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Substation (Estimation Perspective)

The estimation engineer classifies substation materials into the following **Six Major Categories**:

### Category 1: Electrical Equipment (Major Items)
- Power Transformer
- Circuit Breakers (CB) — SF₆, Vacuum, Oil
- Isolators / Disconnectors
- Current Transformers (CT)
- Potential Transformers (PT) / Voltage Transformers (VT)
- Lightning Arresters (LA)
- Busbar (Aluminum / Copper)
- Neutral Grounding Resistor (NGR) / Earthing Transformer
- Power Capacitors (if reactive compensation)

### Category 2: Conductors & Cables
- ACSR / AAC busbars (overhead conductors)
- Power cables (HT XLPE, LT PVC)
- Control cables (1.5 mm², 2.5 mm² copper conductor)
- DC cables, Battery cables
- Earth conductors / strips

### Category 3: Structural & Civil Materials
- Structural steel (ISMB, ISA sections for gantries)
- Reinforcement steel (TMT bars)
- Cement, sand, aggregate (concrete)
- Cable trench materials
- Foundation bolts, nut-bolts

### Category 4: Earthing Materials
- Earth electrodes (GI pipe / copper bonded rod / chemical earthing)
- Earth strip (GI flat 50×6 mm, 25×6 mm)
- Earth pit construction materials (charcoal, salt, brick)
- Earth resistance improvement compound (Bentonite / Marconite)

### Category 5: Protection & Control
- Relays (Numerical / Static)
- Metering (kWh meter, Ammeter, Voltmeter, MFM)
- Indicating lamps, control switches (TNC / SMC)
- Annunciator panels
- Battery bank & battery charger

### Category 6: Miscellaneous
- Lighting fixtures, sockets
- Fire-fighting equipment
- Cable glands, lugs, terminations kits
- Concrete marker, danger boards

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Workflow for Estimation

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** Questions on estimation typically present a Single Line Diagram + some dimensions and ask you to compute quantities. Follow this 6-step approach:

1. **Step 1 — Study the SLD**: Identify voltage level, transformer rating, number of incoming/outgoing bays, bus configuration (single/double bus, breaker-and-a-half, ring main).
2. **Step 2 — Reference GA / Layout Drawing**: Get physical dimensions — busbar run length, switchgear spacing, transformer plinth size, cable trench length.
3. **Step 3 — List of Materials**: Prepare item-wise list from SLD → e.g., 1 no. transformer, 4 nos. CT, 1 no. CB, etc.
4. **Step 4 — Quantity Takeoff (QTO)**: For linear items (cables, busbars, earth strip) compute L = Σ(length × nos.); for volume items (concrete) compute V = L × B × H; for weight items (steel) compute W = Σ(unit weight × length).
5. **Step 5 — Add Wastage & Contingency**: Usually **5–10 %** on cables/conductors, **2.5 %** on structural steel (as per CPWD DSR norms).
6. **Step 6 — Cost Estimation**: Multiply each quantity by its current schedule of rates (SSR / DSR) to obtain total cost.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Estimation Core Equations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These formulas are **frequently tested** in KTU University Examinations. Memorize and practice each.

### A. Weight of Conductors & Busbars

$$W_{\text{conductor}} = \rho_{m} \times L \times A$$

where:
- $\rho_{m}$ = material density ($\text{Al} = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\text{Cu} = 8960 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\text{Steel} = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$)
- $L$ = total length in metres
- $A$ = cross-sectional area in $\text{m}^2$

**Equivalent for standard ACSR conductors:**

$$W = L \times w_{\text{per metre}}$$

where $w_{\text{per metre}}$ is taken from conductor tables (e.g., **Weasel ACSR = 0.193 kg/m**, **Rabbit ACSR = 0.435 kg/m**).

### B. Volume of Concrete (Foundation / Cable Trench / Plinth)

$$V_{\text{concrete}} = L \times B \times H \quad (\text{m}^3)$$

> For trapezoidal footing: $V = H \times \left[ B_{1} \times B_{2} + \dfrac{(B_{1} + B_{2}) \times L_{1}}{2} \right]$

### C. Earth Work Excavation Volume

$$V_{\text{excav}} = L_{\text{pit}} \times B_{\text{pit}} \times D_{\text{pit}} \quad (\text{m}^3)$$

For a row of earthing pits:

$$V_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{pits}} \times (L \times B \times D)$$

### D. Cable Length Calculation (with sag & termination allowance)

$$L_{\text{cable}} = \sqrt{(L_{\text{run}})^2 + (H_{\text{rise}})^2} + L_{\text{term}} + L_{\text{loop}}$$

where $L_{\text{term}}$ = 1.5–2 m per end (termination allowance), $L_{\text{loop}}$ = spare loop (typically 1–2 m).

### E. Weight of Structural Steel

$$W_{\text{steel}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( w_{i} \times L_{i} \right)$$

where $w_{i}$ = unit weight per metre for the $i$-th section (from **IS Handbook on Steel Sections**).

| Section Type | Unit Weight Range (kg/m) |
|---|---|
| ISA 50×50×6 mm | 4.50 |
| ISA 75×75×8 mm | 9.10 |
| ISMB 150 | 14.90 |
| ISMB 200 | 24.20 |
| ISMB 250 | 37.30 |
| MS Flat 50×6 mm | 2.36 |
| MS Round 16 mm | 1.58 |
| MS Channel 100×50 | 9.56 |

### F. Earth Resistance of Pipe Electrode (IS 3043 Formula)

For verification during estimation of earthing:

$$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right] \quad (\Omega)$$

where $\rho$ = soil resistivity (Ω·m), $L$ = length of electrode (m), $d$ = diameter (m).

> **Acceptable limit:** ≤ **1 Ω** for HV substations, ≤ **5 Ω** for LV installations.

### G. Quantity Summary Table (Standard Format)

| Sl. | Material Description | Unit | Quantity | Rate (₹) | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power Transformer 11/0.433 kV, 500 kVA | No. | 1 | — | — |
| 2 | Vacuum CB 11 kV, 630 A | No. | 4 | — | — |
| 3 | ACSR Weasel Conductor | m | 120 | — | — |
| 4 | GI Flat 50×6 mm (Earthing) | m | 250 | — | — |
| 5 | Concrete M25 (Foundation) | m³ | 8.5 | — | — |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Solved Analytical Problem — Quantity Estimation for an 11 kV / 0.433 kV Outdoor Substation

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Problem Pattern:** A standard 11 kV substation with 1 incoming + 3 outgoing feeders, 500 kVA transformer. Estimator must compute the major material quantities from given dimensions.

### Given Data (Typical KTU-Style Problem)

A 11 kV / 0.433 kV outdoor distribution substation has the following layout:

- **Busbar run length (HT side):** 24 m
- **Busbar run length (LT side):** 18 m
- **Number of incoming feeders:** 1
- **Number of outgoing feeders:** 3
- **Transformer plinth size:** 2.0 m × 2.0 m × 0.5 m (L × B × H)
- **CB plinth per breaker:** 1.0 m × 1.0 m × 0.4 m
- **Earth pit quantity:** 6 nos., each pit size 0.5 m × 0.5 m × 2.5 m
- **Earth strip (GI flat 50×6 mm) total run including risers:** 220 m
- **Control cable length per bay:** 30 m
- **Use of ACSR Weasel conductor (cross-section 19.54 mm², weight = 0.193 kg/m)**

**Required:** Estimate the quantity of:
1. HT busbar conductor (kg)
2. LT busbar conductor (kg) — use Aluminum flat 100×10 mm
3. Concrete for transformer plinth (m³)
4. Concrete for all CB plinths (m³)
5. Earth pit excavation (m³)
6. Earthing strip (kg)
7. Control cable (m)

---

### Solution 1 — HT Busbar Conductor (ACSR Weasel)

$$L_{\text{HT}} = 24 \text{ m}$$

$$W_{\text{HT}} = L_{\text{HT}} \times w_{\text{per m}} = 24 \times 0.193$$

$$\boxed{W_{\text{HT}} = 4.632 \text{ kg} \approx 4.65 \text{ kg}}$$

> Add 5 % wastage allowance (KTU/CPWD practice): $4.65 \times 1.05 = 4.88$ kg.

---

### Solution 2 — LT Busbar Conductor (Aluminum Flat 100×10 mm)

Cross-sectional area:
$$A = 100 \text{ mm} \times 10 \text{ mm} = 1000 \text{ mm}^2 = 1000 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 = 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

Density of aluminum:
$$\rho_{\text{Al}} = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

Total length:
$$L_{\text{LT}} = 18 \text{ m}$$

Weight:
$$W_{\text{LT}} = \rho_{\text{Al}} \times L_{\text{LT}} \times A$$

$$W_{\text{LT}} = 2700 \times 18 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{W_{\text{LT}} = 48.6 \text{ kg}}$$

> With 5 % wastage: $48.6 \times 1.05 = 51.03$ kg.

---

### Solution 3 — Concrete for Transformer Plinth

$$V_{\text{Tx plinth}} = L \times B \times H = 2.0 \times 2.0 \times 0.5$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{Tx plinth}} = 2.0 \text{ m}^3}$$

---

### Solution 4 — Concrete for All Circuit Breaker Plinths

Number of CBs required:
$$N_{\text{CB}} = 1 \text{ (incoming)} + 3 \text{ (outgoing)} = 4 \text{ nos.}$$

Volume per CB plinth:
$$V_{\text{CB}} = 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.4 = 0.4 \text{ m}^3$$

Total volume:
$$V_{\text{total CB}} = N_{\text{CB}} \times V_{\text{CB}} = 4 \times 0.4$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{total CB}} = 1.6 \text{ m}^3}$$

---

### Solution 5 — Earth Pit Excavation

Volume per pit:
$$V_{\text{pit}} = 0.5 \times 0.5 \times 2.5 = 0.625 \text{ m}^3$$

Total excavation:
$$V_{\text{total pits}} = 6 \times 0.625$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{total pits}} = 3.75 \text{ m}^3}$$

> For backfilling with alternate layers of charcoal & salt (ratio 1:1), volume of charcoal = salt = $3.75 / 2 = 1.875$ m³.

---

### Solution 6 — Earthing Strip (GI Flat 50×6 mm)

Cross-section: $50 \times 6 = 300 \text{ mm}^2 = 3 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$

Density of steel (GI): $\rho_{\text{st}} = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$

Length: $L = 220$ m

$$W_{\text{E strip}} = 7850 \times 220 \times 3 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$W_{\text{E strip}} = 518.1 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{W_{\text{E strip}} \approx 518.10 \text{ kg}}$$

---

### Solution 7 — Control Cable Length

Number of bays = 4 (1 incoming + 3 outgoing)

$$L_{\text{control}} = N_{\text{bays}} \times 30 = 4 \times 30 = 120 \text{ m}$$

> Add 10 % for looping at panel and termination: $120 \times 1.10 = 132$ m.

$$\boxed{L_{\text{control}} = 132 \text{ m}}$$

---

## 3.2 Consolidated Quantity Summary Table (Model Answer)

| Sl. No. | Item Description | Unit | Quantity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ACSR Weasel HT Conductor (with 5% wastage) | kg | 4.88 |
| 2 | Aluminum Flat 100×10 mm LT Busbar | kg | 51.03 |
| 3 | M25 Concrete for Transformer Plinth | m³ | 2.00 |
| 4 | M25 Concrete for CB Plinths (4 nos.) | m³ | 1.60 |
| 5 | Earth Pit Excavation (6 pits) | m³ | 3.75 |
| 6 | Charcoal + Salt for Earth Pits | m³ | 1.875 |
| 7 | GI Flat 50×6 mm Earth Strip | kg | 518.10 |
| 8 | Control Cable (with 10% loop) | m | 132.00 |
| 9 | Structural Steel (gantry) — additional | kg | As per GA |
| 10 | Reinforcement Steel (TMT 12 mm @ 80 kg/m³) | kg | 288.00 |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping SLD Elements to Quantities

| SLD Element (Visual) | Engineering Material | Estimation Unit | Formula / Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transformer | Power Transformer | No. | Direct count × rating |
| Circuit Breaker | VCB / SF₆ CB | No. | Count from SLD |
| CT / PT | Instrument Transformer | No. | 3 per CB bay (typically) |
| Isolator | Double-break isolator | No. | Count from SLD |
| Busbar | ACSR / Al Flat | kg | $\rho \cdot L \cdot A$ |
| Insulator | Porcelain / Composite | No. | Insulators per support |
| Lightning Arrester | ZnO LA | No. | 3 per transformer + line entry |
| Earth Strip | GI Flat | kg | $\rho_{\text{st}} \cdot L \cdot A$ |
| Control Cable | 1.5/2.5 mm² Cu | m | $L_{\text{run}} + \text{loop}$ |
| Foundation | Concrete (M25) | m³ | $L \times B \times H$ |
| Gantry | Structural Steel | kg | $\Sigma w_i L_i$ |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Substation Material Estimation — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **logical workflow** of the estimation process, from input drawings to final Bill of Quantities. This is the **KTU-recommended** architectural representation when physical drawings are not feasible in the answer sheet.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input Design Documents] --> A1[Single Line Diagram SLD]
    A0 --> A2[General Arrangement GA Drawing]
    A0 --> A3[Specifications and IS Standards]
    
    A1 --> B1[Identify Equipment List]
    A2 --> B2[Extract Physical Dimensions]
    A3 --> B3[Define Material Grades and Ratings]
    
    B1 --> C1[Major Equipment Count]
    B2 --> C2[Linear Quantity Takeoff]
    B2 --> C3[Volumetric Quantity Takeoff]
    B2 --> C4[Weight Quantity Takeoff]
    
    C1 --> D1[Bill of Equipment BOE]
    C2 --> D2[Busbar and Cable Length]
    C3 --> D3[Concrete Volume and Excavation]
    C4 --> D4[Steel Weight and Conductor Weight]
    
    D1 --> E1[Consolidated Bill of Materials BOM]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    
    E1 --> F1[Add Wastage 5 to 10 percent]
    F1 --> F2[Apply Schedule of Rates SSR]
    F2 --> F3[Final Abstract of Cost]
    
    F3 --> G1[Administrative Approval]
    G1 --> G2[Tendering and Award]
    G2 --> G3[Site Execution and Material Reconciliation]
```

## 4.2 Single Line Diagram (Textual Representation)

```
   11 kV INCOMING LINE
         │
    ─────┴─────  (Dropper / Tension String)
         │
      ╔══╧══╗
      ║  LA ║  ← Lightning Arrester
      ╚═════╝
         │
      ┌──┴──┐
      │ ISO │  ← Isolator (with EB)
      └──┬──┘
         │
      ┌──┴──┐
      │  CT │  ← Current Transformer (3 nos.)
      └──┬──┘
         │
      ┌──┴──┐
      │  CB │  ← Vacuum Circuit Breaker
      └──┬──┘
         │
      ┌──┴──┐
      │ ISO │  ← Downstream Isolator
      └──┬──┘
         │
    ────┴────  Busbar (ACSR Weasel)
   ┌────┼────┐
   │    │    │
  TX   OUT1 OUT2 OUT3   ← 1 Transformer + 3 Feeders
```

## 4.3 Typical Outdoor Substation GA Layout (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SITE["OUTDOOR 11 kV SUBSTATION LAYOUT"]
        A1[Incoming Tower with LA and Isolator] --- B1[HT Busbar Gantry]
        B1 --- C1[CB Yard with 4 Circuit Breakers]
        C1 --- D1[Power Transformer 500 kVA]
        D1 --- E1[LT Distribution Panel]
        E1 --- F1[Outgoing Feeders 1 to 3]
        G1[Control Room Building] --- C1
        H1[Earthing Grid GI 50x6 mm] --- A1
        H1 --- B1
        H1 --- C1
        H1 --- D1
        H1 --- E1
    end
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*List the major categories of materials required for the construction of an outdoor 11 kV substation. Briefly explain the role of any three of them.*

**Model Answer:**
The major categories of substation materials are:
1. **Electrical equipment** — Transformer, CB, CT, PT, LA, Isolators
2. **Conductors and cables** — HT busbar (ACSR), LT busbar (Al flat), power cables, control cables
3. **Structural and civil materials** — Structural steel (ISMB/ISA), concrete (M25), reinforcement steel
4. **Earthing materials** — GI pipe electrodes, GI flat earth strip, charcoal and salt
5. **Protection and control** — Numerical relays, meters, annunciator, battery
6. **Miscellaneous** — Lighting, fire-fighting, cable terminations, glands

**Three roles explained:**
- **Power Transformer:** Steps down 11 kV to 0.433 kV for distribution; rating typically 250 kVA to 2.5 MVA.
- **Lightning Arrester:** Diverts atmospheric surge currents safely to earth, protecting insulation.
- **GI Earth Strip:** Provides low-impedance equipotential bonding across the entire switchyard, ensuring operator safety and system earthing as per **IS 3043**.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*Define Bill of Quantities (BOQ). What is its role in substation estimation?*

**Model Answer:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BOQ Definition (KTU Standard):** A **Bill of Quantities (BOQ)** is a tabulated document listing every item of work, its quantity, unit, and rate, prepared from design drawings and used for **tendering, contracting, and execution**.

**Role in Substation Estimation:**
1. Forms the basis for inviting tenders from contractors.
2. Enables **transparent comparison** of contractor quotations.
3. Provides a **measurement record** for contractor billing during execution.
4. Serves as a **control document** for material procurement.
5. Facilitates **value engineering** and cost optimization.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Students frequently **forget to add wastage/contingency** (5–10 % on cables/conductors) — this costs **2–3 marks**.
> - Marks are also lost for **mixing units** (e.g., computing weight in kg but volume in cm³ without conversion).
> - Always show **formula substitution** explicitly — partial credit is awarded for correct method even if arithmetic slips.
> - **Do not skip the SLD interpretation** — 2–3 marks are reserved for correctly identifying equipment from the SLD.

---

### Question B — Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – July 2023 | Model Paper Pattern]**

A 11 kV / 0.433 kV outdoor distribution substation has the following specification:

- 1 no. Power Transformer, 11/0.433 kV, 500 kVA, Oil-immersed
- 1 no. 11 kV incoming line with isolator + CB + CT set
- 3 nos. 11 kV outgoing feeders (each with CB + CT set + isolator)
- HT busbar run length: 30 m of ACSR Weasel conductor
- LT busbar run length: 20 m of Aluminum flat 80×8 mm
- 4 nos. CB plinths, each 1.2 m × 1.0 m × 0.4 m (L × B × H)
- Transformer plinth: 2.5 m × 2.0 m × 0.5 m
- 6 nos. earth pits, each 0.6 m × 0.6 m × 2.8 m
- Earth strip GI flat 50×6 mm — total run = 250 m
- Control cable per bay: 35 m

**(a) Estimate the quantity of busbar conductors (HT and LT) in kg, with 5 % wastage allowance.** **[7 Marks]**

**(b) Estimate the quantity of concrete (m³) and earth strip (kg), with 5 % wastage on steel.** **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B Choice A

**Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1: HT Busbar — ACSR Weasel**

From standard tables: weight of ACSR Weasel = **0.193 kg/m**

$$L_{\text{HT}} = 30 \text{ m}$$

$$W_{\text{HT raw}} = 30 \times 0.193 = 5.79 \text{ kg}$$

With 5 % wastage:

$$W_{\text{HT}} = 5.79 \times 1.05$$

$$\boxed{W_{\text{HT}} = 6.08 \text{ kg}}$$

**Step 2: LT Busbar — Aluminum Flat 80×8 mm**

Cross-section:
$$A = 80 \times 8 = 640 \text{ mm}^2 = 640 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$

Density of Al:
$$\rho_{\text{Al}} = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

Length:
$$L_{\text{LT}} = 20 \text{ m}$$

$$W_{\text{LT raw}} = 2700 \times 20 \times 640 \times 10^{-6} = 34.56 \text{ kg}$$

With 5 % wastage:

$$W_{\text{LT}} = 34.56 \times 1.05 = 36.288$$

$$\boxed{W_{\text{LT}} \approx 36.29 \text{ kg}}$$

**Step 3: Total busbar quantity**

$$W_{\text{total}} = 6.08 + 36.29$$

$$\boxed{W_{\text{total busbar}} \approx 42.37 \text{ kg}}$$

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1: Concrete for Transformer Plinth**

$$V_{\text{Tx plinth}} = 2.5 \times 2.0 \times 0.5 = 2.5 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Concrete for CB Plinths**

$$V_{\text{per CB}} = 1.2 \times 1.0 \times 0.4 = 0.48 \text{ m}^3$$

$$V_{\text{CB total}} = 4 \times 0.48 = 1.92 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3: Total Concrete**

$$V_{\text{total concrete}} = 2.5 + 1.92$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{total concrete}} = 4.42 \text{ m}^3}$$

**Step 4: Earth Pit Excavation**

$$V_{\text{per pit}} = 0.6 \times 0.6 \times 2.8 = 1.008 \text{ m}^3$$

$$V_{\text{pits total}} = 6 \times 1.008 = 6.048 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5: Earth Strip (GI Flat 50×6 mm)**

Cross-section:
$$A = 50 \times 6 = 300 \text{ mm}^2 = 3 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

Density of steel:
$$\rho_{\text{st}} = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

Length:
$$L_{\text{strip}} = 250 \text{ m}$$

$$W_{\text{strip raw}} = 7850 \times 250 \times 3 \times 10^{-4} = 588.75 \text{ kg}$$

With 5 % wastage:

$$W_{\text{strip}} = 588.75 \times 1.05 = 618.19$$

$$\boxed{W_{\text{earth strip}} \approx 618.19 \text{ kg}}$$

**Step 6: Control Cable (Additional)**

$$L_{\text{control}} = 4 \times 35 \times 1.10 = 154 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Pattern Used by KTU Examiner:**
> - Formula identification: **1 mark**
> - Correct substitution: **2 marks**
> - Final numeric answer: **2 marks**
> - Wastage/contingency step: **1 mark**
> - Unit & clear presentation: **1 mark**

---

### Question B — Choice B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a) Explain the step-by-step methodology for preparing a Bill of Quantities (BOQ) for a 33 kV outdoor substation. List any six major items with their typical units of measurement.** **[7 Marks]**

**(b) The single line diagram of a 33 kV substation shows 1 incoming bay and 2 outgoing bays. Each bay has 1 CB, 1 isolator, 3 CTs, 1 PT and 1 LA. The HT busbar uses ACSR Panther (weight = 0.407 kg/m) of length 45 m. Calculate the total number of major electrical items and the weight of HT busbar (with 8 % wastage).** **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B Choice B

**Solution to Part (a):**

**Methodology for BOQ Preparation:**

1. **Study the Single Line Diagram (SLD)** — identify all electrical equipment.
2. **Refer to the General Arrangement (GA) drawing** — extract physical dimensions.
3. **Refer to Specifications** — confirm ratings, makes, standards.
4. **List each item of work** with a unique serial number.
5. **Compute quantities** using takeoff methods (length, volume, weight).
6. **Add wastage allowance** (5–10 %).
7. **Apply Schedule of Rates (DSR/SSR)** — multiply by rate.
8. **Summarize as Abstract of Cost**.

**Six Major Items and Units:**

| Sl. | Item | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power Transformer | No. (with rating kVA/MVA) |
| 2 | Circuit Breaker | No. |
| 3 | ACSR Conductor | m / kg |
| 4 | Concrete (M25) | m³ |
| 5 | Structural Steel | kg |
| 6 | Earthing Strip | kg / m |

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1: Total number of bays**

$$N_{\text{bays}} = 1 \text{ (incoming)} + 2 \text{ (outgoing)} = 3 \text{ bays}$$

**Step 2: Quantity of each electrical item per bay**

| Item | Per Bay | Total (×3 bays) |
|---|---|---|
| Circuit Breaker | 1 | 3 |
| Isolator | 1 | 3 |
| Current Transformer (CT) | 3 | 9 |
| Potential Transformer (PT) | 1 | 3 |
| Lightning Arrester (LA) | 1 | 3 |

**Step 3: HT Busbar Weight**

Given:
$$w_{\text{Panther}} = 0.407 \text{ kg/m}, \quad L = 45 \text{ m}$$

$$W_{\text{raw}} = 45 \times 0.407 = 18.315 \text{ kg}$$

With 8 % wastage:

$$W_{\text{busbar}} = 18.315 \times 1.08 = 19.78$$

$$\boxed{W_{\text{HT busbar}} \approx 19.78 \text{ kg}}$$

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Block — Memorize Before Exam:**

- [ ] Substation estimation is a **design-to-procurement translation** process.
- [ ] Always begin by interpreting the **SLD and GA drawing** before any calculation.
- [ ] Standard density values to memorize:
    - **Aluminum = 2700 kg/m³**
    - **Copper = 8960 kg/m³**
    - **Steel / GI = 7850 kg/m³**
- [ ] **Common busbar weights (ACSR):**
    - Weasel = **0.193 kg/m**
    - Rabbit = **0.435 kg/m**
    - Panther = **0.407 kg/m**
    - Dog = **0.652 kg/m**
- [ ] **Wastage allowance (CPWD/IS standard):**
    - Conductors/cables = **5 %**
    - Structural steel = **2.5 to 5 %**
    - Concrete = **5 %**
- [ ] **Concrete grade:** Use **M25** for substation foundations (1:1:2 ratio).
- [ ] **Earth electrode standard sizes:** GI pipe **40 mm NB × 2.5 m** or **50 mm NB × 3 m**.
- [ ] **Earth strip standard:** GI flat **50×6 mm** (main grid) and **25×6 mm** (equipment earthing).
- [ ] **Earth pit:** Always 2 pits per equipment with **charcoal + salt** backfilling in alternate layers.
- [ ] **CB plinth typical size:** 1.0 m × 1.0 m × 0.4 m (M25 concrete).
- [ ] **Transformer plinth:** Always larger than transformer footprint; provision for oil soak pit (110 % oil volume).
- [ ] **Safety margin for cable:** Add 1.5–2 m per end for **termination + looping**.
- [ ] **Earth resistance target:** ≤ **1 Ω** for HV substations (IS 3043).
- [ ] **Lightning arrester count:** 3 per transformer + 3 per line entry.
- [ ] **Insulator counting:** Tension string (suspension) = 2 per support; Tension disc = as per voltage level.
- [ ] **Final costing** uses current **Schedule of Rates (SSR)** — multiply each BOQ quantity by its unit rate.
- [ ] **Report writing:** Always close with an **Abstract of Cost** summary for management approval.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Strategy:** If the question provides an SLD, always reproduce it schematically in your answer script — KTU examiners award **2 easy marks** for clear visual interpretation before the calculation begins.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pre-Commissioning Tests for Transformers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pre-Commissioning Tests for Transformers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Pre-commissioning tests for transformers** are a standardized sequence of electrical, mechanical, and chemical checks performed on a power transformer **after installation but before energizing it onto the live grid**. These tests verify that the transformer has not been damaged during transportation, storage, or erection, and that it conforms to the design specifications, statutory safety regulations, and IS/IEC standards (e.g., **IS 2026**, **IS 1180**, **IEC 60076**).

The tests are broadly classified into:

- **Routine Tests** – Performed on every unit.
- **Type Tests** – Performed on a prototype design.
- **Special Tests** – Performed as per customer agreement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU Board Examiner pattern, students must remember that **pre-commissioning tests are a contractual obligation** between the manufacturer, erector, and the utility/consumer. A "pass certificate" is mandatory before charging the transformer.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have just purchased a brand-new **car** from a showroom. Even though it was tested at the factory, before you drive it on the highway, you (or the dealer) will:

- Check the **tyre pressure** (insulation resistance).
- Check the **brake fluid level** (transformer oil dielectric strength).
- Check the **headlight alignment and horn** (polarity, phase sequence).
- Take a **short test drive in the parking lot** (no-load test).
- Verify the **gear ratios work** (turns ratio test).

In exactly the same way, a transformer cannot simply be switched on after being transported for hundreds of kilometers on a truck. The oil sloshes, the windings vibrate, the bushings may crack, and moisture can seep in. **Pre-commissioning tests are the "parking lot test drive"** of the transformer.

> [!NOTE]
> A transformer failure in service can cost ₹20–50 lakhs and lead to industrial downtime running into crores. Hence, a few hours spent on pre-commissioning tests saves catastrophic losses later.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics (Important Constants)

The following standard values must be memorized for the KTU examination:

- **Acceptable Insulation Resistance (IR) value:** Generally, the rule of thumb is **1 MΩ per kV of operating voltage** plus a margin of **1000 MΩ** at 1-minute reading.
- **Minimum Breakdown Voltage (BDV) of new transformer oil:** **30 kV (rms) minimum** for oil up to **170 kV class**, per **IS 335**.
- **Maximum allowable Earth Resistance:** **1 Ω** for large power stations, **2 Ω** for substations, and **5 Ω** for small distribution transformers.
- **Allowable deviation in Turns Ratio:** **± 0.5%** of the declared ratio (or **± 1/10th of the impedance percentage**, whichever is lower, per IS 2026).

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed List of Pre-Commissioning Tests

The following table presents a structured, high-yield overview of the tests, their purpose, and the principle used.

| **Sl. No.** | **Test Name** | **Purpose / Why is it done?** | **Standard / Reference** |
|:-----------:|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Visual & Mechanical Inspection** | Check for physical damage, oil leaks, proper tank earthing, bushing cracks, and tightness of bolted joints. | IS 2026 (Part 1) |
| 2 | **Insulation Resistance (IR) Test (Megger Test)** | To measure the resistance of insulation between windings and between winding-to-earth. Detects moisture ingress. | IS 2026 (Part 1) |
| 3 | **Dielectric Absorption Index (DAI) Test** | A 10-minute vs 1-minute IR comparison to assess dryness of insulation. | IEEE 43 / IS 2026 |
| 4 | **Winding Resistance (Ohmic) Test** | To check for loose connections, broken strands, and short-circuited turns using a Kelvin Bridge / Ohmmeter. | IS 2026 (Part 1) |
| 5 | **Turns Ratio (TTR) Test** | To verify the voltage ratio of primary to secondary matches the nameplate rating. | IS 2026 (Part 1) |
| 6 | **Vector Group & Polarity Test** | To confirm the phase displacement (Dy11, Yd1, etc.) and polarity matches the design. | IS 2026 (Part 1) |
| 7 | **Phase Sequence Test** | To confirm correct phase sequence (R-Y-B) on the LV side matches the HV side. | IS 2026 (Part 1) |
| 8 | **Magnetizing Current & No-Load Loss Test** | To check core quality, identify inter-winding shorts, and verify no-load performance. | IS 2026 (Part 1) |
| 9 | **Short-Circuit (Impedance) Test** | To measure percentage impedance and load loss. | IS 2026 (Part 1) |
| 10 | **Breakdown Voltage (BDV) of Oil** | To confirm dielectric strength of insulating oil. | IS 335 |
| 11 | **Earth Resistance Measurement** | To ensure the tank, neutral, and body earthing are within safe limits. | IS 3043 |
| 12 | **High Voltage (HV) / Pressure Test** | To verify insulation withstands over-voltages. | IS 2026 (Part 3) |
| 13 | **Temperature Rise Test** | Type test. Checks that oil and winding temperature rise is within limits. | IS 2026 (Part 2) |
| 14 | **Neutral Current & Zero Sequence Test** | To check neutral displacement, single-phase fault behaviour. | IS 2026 (Part 1) |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU examination, students often confuse *routine* with *type* tests. **Routine tests** are mandatory on every unit, while **type tests** are done on a new design or sample.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table lists the **must-know formulas** for solving pre-commissioning test problems.

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Standard / Acceptable Value** |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum Insulation Resistance | $\text{IR}_{\min} \;=\; \frac{V_{LL} \;\text{(in kV)}}{\text{kVA rating}} \times C$ (where C is a constant) | $\geq 200 \text{ M}\Omega$ for 11 kV class (general rule) |
| Practical IR Rule (Index) | $\text{IR}_{\min} \;=\; 1 \text{ M}\Omega \text{ per kV of } V_{LL} \;+\; 1000 \text{ M}\Omega$ | Empirical rule |
| Dielectric Absorption Index | $\text{DAI} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{IR at 10 min}}{\text{IR at 1 min}}$ | $\geq 1.3$ (good); $\geq 1.6$ (excellent) |
| Polarization Index (PI) | $\text{PI} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{IR at 10 min}}{\text{IR at 1 min}}$ | $\geq 2.0$ (Class A); $\geq 1.5$ (Class B) |
| Turns Ratio (Transformer) | $R \;=\; \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} \;=\; \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$ | Deviation $\leq \pm 0.5\%$ |
| Percentage Impedance | $\%Z \;=\; \dfrac{V_{sc}}{V_{rated}} \times 100$ | As per nameplate $\pm 10\%$ tolerance |
| Earth Resistance (Parallel Electrodes) | $R_{eq} \;=\; \dfrac{R_1 \cdot R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ (for two electrodes) | $\leq 1 \text{ }\Omega$ (major substations) |
| Oil BDV Required | Direct measurement using **test cell** | $\geq 30 \text{ kV rms}$ for new oil |
| Three-Phase Three-Winding Turns Ratio | $\dfrac{V_{HV}}{V_{LV}} \;=\; \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | $\pm 0.5\%$ of declared |
| Winding Resistance Temperature Correction | $R_2 \;=\; R_1 \cdot \dfrac{235 + T_2}{235 + T_1}$ | For copper windings |
| Voltage Divider Rule (Tapping) | $V_{tap} \;=\; V_{full} \cdot \left(1 \pm \dfrac{\% \text{ tap}}{100}\right)$ | For $\pm 5\%$ or $\pm 2.5\%$ taps |
| Energy Stored in Winding Inductance | $E \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2} L I^2$ (used in impulse tests) | Joules |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In all derivations and numerical problems, **standard temperature** for winding resistance correction is taken as **75°C** for oil-immersed transformers.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility in the Real World

1. **Utility Companies (KSEB, MSEB, etc.):** Mandatory before charging transformers into service. The kVA-wise test sheet must be signed by the engineer-in-charge.
2. **Industrial Plants (Steel, Cement, Petrochemical):** Prevents unscheduled shutdowns — a single transformer failure can halt production worth ₹1 crore per day.
3. **EHV Substations (400 kV / 765 kV):** Tests like **partial discharge**, **impulse voltage**, and **dielectric dissipation factor (tan δ)** are critical because of high dielectric stress.
4. **Renewable Energy (Solar/Wind Inverters):** Step-up transformers connecting solar farms to grids must be tested for harmonics and DC bias.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Insulation Resistance Rule — Empirical Derivation

The "**1 MΩ per kV + 1000 MΩ**" rule is empirical but widely accepted. Let us **derive** the underlying logic and the relevant correction factors.

### 3.1.1 Ohmic Model of Winding Insulation

Consider the equivalent circuit of a transformer winding as a **parallel combination** of the pure resistance $R_i$ (insulation) and the geometric capacitance $C_g$.

$$I_{total} \;=\; I_R + j I_C \;=\; \frac{V}{R_i} + j \omega C_g V$$

A DC Megger test effectively measures the DC component only:

$$I_{DC} \;=\; \frac{V_{test}}{R_i}$$

Therefore, the insulation resistance is given by:

$$R_i \;=\; \frac{V_{test}}{I_{DC}}$$

### 3.1.2 Temperature Correction of IR

Insulation resistance is **highly temperature dependent**. It approximately halves for every 10°C rise. The correction formula is:

$$R_{T_2} \;=\; R_{T_1} \cdot 10^{\,k \cdot (T_1 - T_2)/10}$$

For Class A insulation, $k \approx 0.5$. For Class B, $k \approx 0.4$.

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU Board Exam Style):** A 500 kVA, 11 kV / 433 V transformer is tested with a 1000 V Megger. The 1-minute reading is **1500 MΩ** and the 10-minute reading is **4500 MΩ**. The oil temperature during the test is **40°C**. Calculate:
1. The Polarization Index (PI).
2. The corrected IR at standard reference temperature of 75°C.

**Step 1 — Compute PI:**

$$\text{PI} \;=\; \frac{\text{IR}_{10\,\text{min}}}{\text{IR}_{1\,\text{min}}} \;=\; \frac{4500}{1500} \;=\; 3.0$$

Since PI $\geq 2.0$, the winding is in **excellent condition**.

**Step 2 — Temperature Correction:**

Using $R_{75} = R_{40} \cdot 10^{0.5 \cdot (40 - 75)/10}$:

$$R_{75} \;=\; 4500 \cdot 10^{\,0.5 \cdot (-3.5)} \;=\; 4500 \cdot 10^{-1.75}$$

$$R_{75} \;=\; \frac{4500}{56.23} \;\approx\; 80.0 \text{ M}\Omega$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> While the corrected value looks low, it is **acceptable** for this kVA class. The IR rule of thumb $1 \text{ M}\Omega/\text{kV} + 1000$ gives $11 + 1000 = 1011 \text{ M}\Omega$, which is far higher. Always refer to manufacturer's acceptance values.

## 3.2 Turns Ratio (TTR) — Analytical Derivation

The turns ratio is derived directly from the **transformer EMF equation**.

### 3.2.1 EMF Equation

For a sinusoidal flux $\phi = \phi_m \sin(\omega t)$:

$$E \;=\; N \cdot \frac{d\phi}{dt} \;=\; N \cdot \omega \cdot \phi_m \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$

The RMS value is:

$$E_{rms} \;=\; 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N \cdot \phi_m$$

### 3.2.2 Turns Ratio

For two windings on the same core (same $\phi_m$ and $f$):

$$\frac{E_1}{E_2} \;=\; \frac{N_1}{N_2} \;=\; \frac{V_1}{V_2} \;=\; R$$

### 3.2.3 Tolerance and Deviation Calculation

**Worked Example:** A 1000 kVA, 11 kV / 433 V transformer has a nameplate ratio of 25.40. A TTR test at tap-3 (–2.5%) gives 24.80. Calculate the **percentage deviation**.

$$\text{Expected ratio} \;=\; 25.40 \times \left(1 - \frac{2.5}{100}\right) \;=\; 25.40 \times 0.975 \;=\; 24.77$$

$$\text{Deviation} \;=\; \frac{|24.80 - 24.77|}{24.77} \times 100 \;=\; 0.12\%$$

Since deviation is **0.12% < 0.5%**, the transformer is within the IS 2026 acceptance limit.

## 3.3 Earth Resistance Derivation

The **fall of potential method** is used. A current $I$ is passed between the **earth electrode under test (E)** and an **auxiliary current electrode (C)**. The voltage drop $V$ is measured between E and a **potential probe (P)**.

$$R_{earth} \;=\; \frac{V_{EP}}{I_{EC}}$$

For a **plate electrode** of area $A$ buried at depth $d$ in soil of resistivity $\rho$:

$$R_{plate} \;=\; \frac{\rho}{4} \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{A}}$$

For a **rod electrode** of length $L$ and radius $a$:

$$R_{rod} \;=\; \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \ln\left(\frac{4L}{a}\right)$$

For **parallel electrodes**:

$$R_{total} \;=\; \frac{R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3}{R_1 R_2 + R_2 R_3 + R_3 R_1}$$

## 3.4 Magnetizing Current — Phasor Analysis

The magnetizing current is the small current drawn by the transformer primary when the secondary is open. It has two components:

$$I_m \;=\; I_c + j I_\mu$$

Where $I_c$ = core loss component (in phase with $V$) and $I_\mu$ = magnetizing component (90° lagging).

The **percentage magnetizing current** is typically **0.5% to 2%** of full load current. An unusually high magnetizing current indicates **inter-turn shorts** or **core damage**.

$$I_m \,(\%) \;=\; \frac{I_{m, \text{ measured}}}{I_{FL}} \times 100$$

## 3.5 Short-Circuit Test — Impedance Calculation

In the SC test, the LV winding is shorted and the HV winding is supplied with reduced voltage such that full-load current flows.

The applied voltage is $V_{sc}$. The percentage impedance is:

$$\%Z \;=\; \frac{V_{sc}}{V_{rated,HV}} \times 100$$

The **equivalent resistance** referred to HV side is:

$$R_{eq,HV} \;=\; \frac{P_{sc}}{I_{FL}^2}$$

The **leakage reactance**:

$$X_{eq} \;=\; \sqrt{Z_{eq}^2 - R_{eq}^2}$$

Where $Z_{eq} = V_{sc} / I_{FL}$.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Board Examiner Common Trap: Students often confuse the **impedance voltage** (the test voltage) with the **rated voltage**. Always divide by **rated HV voltage**, not by the test voltage!

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Architecture — Pre-Commissioning Test Sequence

The following **Mermaid flowchart** depicts the **logical sequence** in which a transformer undergoes pre-commissioning checks on site. It is essential for KTU students to understand the *order of execution* (e.g., you cannot perform an HV test before IR and BDV tests).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Transformer Arrives at Site] --> B[Visual and Mechanical Inspection]
    B --> C[Oil Sampling and BDV Test]
    B --> D[Earthing Continuity Check]
    B --> E[Insulation Resistance Megger Test]
    E --> F[Polarization Index Test 10 min]
    C --> G{All Preliminary OK?}
    D --> G
    F --> G
    G -->|No| H[Rectify Issue and Retest]
    H --> G
    G -->|Yes| I[Winding Resistance Test]
    I --> J[Turns Ratio TTR Test]
    J --> K[Vector Group and Polarity Test]
    K --> L[Phase Sequence Test]
    L --> M[Magnetizing Current and No Load Test]
    M --> N[Short Circuit Impedance Test]
    N --> O[High Voltage Withstand Test]
    O --> P{All Tests Passed?}
    P -->|No| H
    P -->|Yes| Q[Issue Commissioning Certificate]
    Q --> R[Energize Transformer]
```

## 4.2 Megger Test Connection Diagram

This **Mermaid block diagram** shows the standard connections for an IR test between **HV-Earth**, **LV-Earth**, and **HV-LV** (also called the **double pressure test**).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[1000V to 5000V DC Megger] --> T1[Terminal L]
    M --> T2[Terminal E]
    T1 --> HV[HV Winding Bus]
    T1 --> LV[LV Winding Bus]
    T2 --> G[Earth Tank and Neutral]
    HV -.->|Step 1 HV to E| G
    LV -.->|Step 2 LV to E| G
    HV -.->|Step 3 HV to LV| LV
```

> [!NOTE]
> For a **11 kV / 433 V** transformer, the test voltages are typically:
> - HV-E: **1000 V DC** Megger
> - LV-E: **500 V DC** Megger
> - HV-LV: **1000 V DC** Megger

## 4.3 Fall of Potential Method for Earth Resistance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Transformer Body to be Earthed] --> P[Potential Probe P 50 percent distance]
    X --> C[Current Electrode C 100 percent distance]
    Tester[Earth Resistance Tester] --- X
    Tester --- P
    Tester --- C
```

**Note:** For accurate measurement, the auxiliary electrodes **P** and **C** are placed **50% and 100%** of the diagonal length of the sub-station from the body under test. The **61.8% rule** (also called the **Wenner 4-point method**) is used for sub-station earthing design.

## 4.4 Short-Circuit Test Wiring Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC[3-Phase Variac or Reduced Voltage Source] --> AMM[Ammeter A]
    AMM --> V1[Voltmeter V1]
    V1 --> HVH[HV Winding of Transformer]
    HVH --> LVH[LV Winding of Transformer]
    LVH --> SHORT[Short Circuit Link]
    SHORT --> LVH
    W1[Wattmeter W1] --- SRC
    W1 --- HVH
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that the ammeter is on the **HV side** because the current is higher there. The voltmeter measures the **short-circuit voltage** $V_{sc}$, and the wattmeter gives **load loss** $P_{sc}$ (which equals $I^2 R$ losses plus stray losses).

## 4.5 Vector Group Test — Phasor Diagram Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HV Side
        HVA[HV-A] --> HVB[HV-B]
        HVB --> HVC[HV-C]
    end
    subgraph LV Side
        LVA[LV-a] --> LVB[LV-b]
        LVB --> LVC[LV-c]
    end
    HVA -.->|Link 1| LVA
    HVB -.->|Link 2| LVB
    HVC -.->|Link 3| LVC
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **vector group test** is conducted by **shorting the same-phase terminals** (e.g., A–a, B–b, C–c) and then applying a 3-phase reduced voltage on the HV side. The **phase displacement** between HV and LV is measured using a voltmeter on the open terminals. For example, for a **Dyn11** transformer, the displacement is **+30°**, meaning LV leads HV by 30°.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Set B]**
**Define Insulation Resistance Test. What is its purpose in transformer pre-commissioning?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Insulation Resistance (IR) test**, popularly called the **Megger test**, is a pre-commissioning test performed using a **hand-cranked or electronic DC high-voltage Megger** (typically 500 V, 1000 V, or 2500 V DC) to measure the resistance of the transformer's insulation between windings and between each winding and earth.

**Purpose (2 Marks):**
1. To detect moisture ingress in the windings or oil, which reduces dielectric strength.
2. To identify any insulation degradation, contamination, or short circuits that may have occurred during transport/storage.
3. To confirm that the insulation is fit to withstand the operational voltage stress.

The **minimum acceptable IR value** is given by the empirical rule:

$$\text{IR}_{\min} \;=\; 1 \, \text{M}\Omega \text{ per kV} \;+\; 1000 \, \text{M}\Omega$$

(Acceptable answer should include: instrument, terminals tested, and the rule of thumb.)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**What is the Breakdown Voltage (BDV) test of transformer oil? State the standard value for new oil.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Breakdown Voltage (BDV) test** of transformer oil is performed to determine the **dielectric strength** of the insulating oil. A sample of oil is placed in a **standard test cell** containing two spherical or disc-shaped electrodes (typically 2.5 mm gap), and a continuously rising **AC voltage (at 50 Hz)** is applied until the oil breaks down (arcs across the gap). The procedure is repeated **6 times** at 1-minute intervals, and the **average of the 6 readings** is taken as the BDV of the oil.

**Standard Values (as per IS 335):**
- For **new oil**: minimum **30 kV (rms)**.
- For **oil in service**: minimum **20 kV (rms)** for voltages up to 170 kV class.
- For voltages above 170 kV class: minimum **40 kV (rms)**.

(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for procedure, 1 mark for standard value.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **pre-commissioning tests** to be carried out on a **1000 kVA, 11 kV / 433 V distribution transformer** before charging it into service. Discuss the **instrumentation, test procedures, acceptable values, and statutory standards** for each test. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A 500 kVA, 11 kV / 433 V transformer has the following pre-commissioning test results at 40°C oil temperature:
   - 1-minute IR (HV-E): **1200 MΩ**
   - 10-minute IR (HV-E): **3000 MΩ**
   - TTR measured: **25.20** (nameplate ratio at tap-1: +5% is **26.67**)

Calculate the **Polarization Index (PI)** and the **percentage TTR deviation**. Comment on the acceptability of the transformer for charging. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Answer for Question A (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Visual and Mechanical Inspection (1 Mark):**
- Check for oil leaks, tightness of bolts, condition of bushings, presence of silica gel (blue dry), oil level in conservator, proper tank earthing, and condition of radiator fins.

**2. Insulation Resistance Test — Megger Test (1 Mark):**
- Use a **1000 V DC Megger** for LV side and **2500 V DC Megger** for HV side.
- Test **HV-Earth**, **LV-Earth**, and **HV-LV**.
- Compare with the empirical rule $\text{IR}_{\min} = 1 \text{ M}\Omega/\text{kV} + 1000 \text{ M}\Omega$.
- For 11 kV, minimum IR = 11 + 1000 = **1011 MΩ**.

**3. Oil BDV Test (1 Mark):**
- Take an oil sample from the bottom drain valve using a clean dry glass jar.
- Test in a BDV kit, 6 readings, average ≥ **30 kV** for new oil (IS 335).

**4. Turns Ratio Test (TTR) (1 Mark):**
- Use a **TTR meter** at all taps.
- Compare with nameplate ratio; tolerance **± 0.5%** as per IS 2026.

**5. Vector Group and Polarity (0.5 Marks):**
- Verify Dyn11 marking matches actual phasor displacement.

**6. Phase Sequence (0.5 Marks):**
- Confirm R-Y-B on LV matches HV using a phase sequence meter.

**7. Magnetizing Current and No-Load Loss (1 Mark):**
- Apply rated LV voltage with HV open.
- Measure no-load current and wattage; compare with nameplate.

**8. Short-Circuit (Impedance) Test (1 Mark):**
- Short LV, apply reduced HV voltage to circulate full-load current.
- Measure $V_{sc}$, $P_{sc}$; compute %Z. Acceptable deviation **± 10%** of nameplate.

**Conclusion:** All these are **routine tests** as per IS 2026 Part 1, and the transformer can be charged only if **all tests pass**.

---

#### Model Answer for Question A (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Calculate Polarization Index (PI) (2 Marks):**

$$\text{PI} \;=\; \frac{\text{IR}_{10\,\text{min}}}{\text{IR}_{1\,\text{min}}} \;=\; \frac{3000 \text{ M}\Omega}{1200 \text{ M}\Omega} \;=\; 2.5$$

**Comment:** Since PI $= 2.5 \geq 2.0$, the winding insulation is in **excellent condition** and free from moisture.

**Step 2 — Calculate Expected Ratio at +5% Tap (2 Marks):**

The nameplate ratio at principal tap is:

$$R_{\text{base}} \;=\; \frac{11000}{433} \;=\; 25.40$$

At +5% tap, the HV voltage is reduced by 5%, so the expected ratio is:

$$R_{\text{expected}} \;=\; R_{\text{base}} \times \left(1 - \frac{5}{100}\right) \;=\; 25.40 \times 0.95 \;=\; 24.13$$

*Note:* The nameplate value of 26.67 is inconsistent with standard ratios. Re-checking using actual tap convention: at +5% tap, ratio $= 25.40 \times 0.95 = 24.13$. The student's TTR measured 25.20, so:

**Step 3 — Calculate % Deviation (2 Marks):**

$$\% \text{ Deviation} \;=\; \frac{|25.20 - 24.13|}{24.13} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{1.07}{24.13} \times 100 \;\approx\; 4.43\%$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 Mark):**

Since the deviation **4.43% > 0.5%** (IS 2026 limit), the transformer **does NOT pass** the TTR test. It indicates a possible **winding short** or **incorrect tap connection**. The unit should be **rejected for charging** until the defect is rectified.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Forgetting to convert kV to V or vice-versa. Always show units in the answer.
> 2. **Tap Convention Confusion:** +5% tap means the HV is REDUCED (since fewer turns are used to compensate for low system voltage). The ratio therefore *decreases*, not increases.
> 3. **Forgetting the 0.5% tolerance:** Many students calculate the deviation but do not compare it with the IS 2026 acceptance limit. Always end with a **Pass/Fail comment**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, explain the **Fall of Potential Method** for measuring **earth resistance** of a transformer body. State the standard acceptable values of earth resistance for (i) large power stations, (ii) substations, and (iii) small distribution transformers. (7 Marks)

**(b)** During the **BDV test of transformer oil**, the following 6 readings (in kV) were taken at 1-minute intervals:
**28, 32, 30, 34, 31, 35**.
Determine the **average BDV** of the oil sample. State whether the oil is **acceptable for use in a 11 kV transformer** as per **IS 335**. What remedial action is required if the BDV is below standard? (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Answer for Question B (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Diagram (3 Marks):**
The test setup consists of three electrodes:
- **E** (Earth Electrode under test — connected to transformer body).
- **P** (Potential Probe — placed at 50% of the diagonal distance from E).
- **C** (Current Electrode — placed at 100% of the diagonal distance from E).

A known current $I$ is passed between E and C using a **Megger-type Earth Tester** or **Clamp-on Earth Resistance Tester**. The voltage $V$ is measured between E and P.

$$R_{earth} \;=\; \frac{V_{EP}}{I_{EC}}$$

**2. Standard Acceptable Values (3 Marks):**
- **Large Power Stations (400/220 kV):** $\leq 0.5 \, \Omega$
- **EHV Substations (110 kV and above):** $\leq 1.0 \, \Omega$
- **Major Substations (33/11 kV):** $\leq 2.0 \, \Omega$
- **Small Distribution Transformers (LT side):** $\leq 5.0 \, \Omega$

**3. Precautions (1 Mark):**
- The auxiliary electrodes should not be near any metallic structure.
- The test should be repeated at multiple directions to avoid errors due to soil heterogeneity.
- The reading is most accurate when the **62% distance** (sometimes used in the **61.8% method**) is maintained.

---

#### Model Answer for Question B (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Average BDV Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$\text{Average BDV} \;=\; \frac{28 + 32 + 30 + 34 + 31 + 35}{6} \;=\; \frac{190}{6} \;=\; 31.67 \text{ kV}$$

**Step 2 — Comparison with IS 335 (2 Marks):**

- For **new oil** in a 11 kV transformer, the IS 335 standard is **30 kV minimum**.
- The average BDV of 31.67 kV > 30 kV.
- **Therefore, the oil is acceptable** for charging the 11 kV transformer.

**Step 3 — Acceptance Range (1 Mark):**
- For oil in **service** (after some months/years), the minimum permissible BDV is **20 kV** for voltages up to 170 kV class.

**Step 4 — Remedial Action if Below Standard (2 Marks):**

If the BDV is below the standard value, the following actions are recommended:

1. **Filtration** — Pass the oil through a **filter press** with absorbent material (Fuller's earth) to remove moisture and impurities.
2. **Dehydration** — Use a **vacuum dehydrator** to remove dissolved water.
3. **Centrifuging** — Separate water and heavy contaminants by centrifugal force.
4. **Reconditioning** — If moisture and acidity are both high, pass oil through a **reconditioning plant**.
5. **Replacement** — If oil is severely degraded, replace with fresh, BDV-tested oil.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Wrong Formula for Average:** Some students take the median or skip the lowest reading. Always take the **arithmetic mean of all 6 readings**.
> 2. **Confusion between New Oil and In-Service Oil Standards:** IS 335 specifies **30 kV (new)** and **20 kV (in service)** — using the wrong value in the comparison will lose marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to give a Pass/Fail conclusion** — always state the final verdict explicitly.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

The following is a high-density summary of the most important concepts to revise in the final hour before the KTU examination.

- ✅ Pre-commissioning tests are **mandatory before energizing** a transformer, as per IS 2026 and IS 1180.
- ✅ **IR Rule of Thumb:** $\text{IR}_{\min} = 1 \text{ M}\Omega/\text{kV} + 1000 \text{ M}\Omega$.
- ✅ **Polarization Index (PI):** $\text{PI} = \text{IR}_{10} / \text{IR}_{1} \geq 2.0$ → winding is **dry and healthy**.
- ✅ **Dielectric Absorption Index (DAI):** $\text{DAI} = \text{IR}_{10} / \text{IR}_{1} \geq 1.3$ → minimum acceptable.
- ✅ **Oil BDV Standard (IS 335):** ≥ 30 kV (new), ≥ 20 kV (in service) for ≤ 170 kV class.
- ✅ **Turns Ratio Tolerance:** ± 0.5% of declared nameplate ratio (IS 2026).
- ✅ **% Impedance Tolerance:** ± 10% of declared nameplate value.
- ✅ **Earth Resistance:** ≤ 0.5 Ω (HV), ≤ 1 Ω (substation), ≤ 5 Ω (LT).
- ✅ **Megger Voltages:** 500 V for LV, 1000 V for 11 kV HV, 2500 V for 33 kV HV, 5000 V for 66 kV and above.
- ✅ **Winding Resistance Test:** Use a **Kelvin Double Bridge** or **digital micro-ohmmeter**; correct to **75°C** for copper.
- ✅ **Vector Group Test:** Connect **A-a, B-b, C-c** together, apply reduced 3-phase HV; measure voltage on **A-c, B-a, C-b** open terminals.
- ✅ **Phase Sequence Test:** R-Y-B on LV must match R-Y-B on HV; tested using a **rotating-disc phase sequence meter**.
- ✅ **Magnetizing Current:** Typically 0.5–2% of full-load current; **higher** values indicate inter-turn shorts.
- ✅ **HV Withstand Test Voltage:** $V_{test} = (2 \times V_{LL} + 1) \text{ kV}$ for routine test (1 minute).
- ✅ **Routine Tests Performed:** IR, TTR, Vector group, Polarity, Phase sequence, Winding resistance, No-load, SC, BDV, Earth resistance.
- ✅ **Type Tests:** Temperature rise, Impulse voltage, Short-circuit withstand — performed on one unit per design.
- ✅ **Kelvin Bridge formula:** $R_x = \frac{P}{Q} \cdot R$ (where P, Q are ratio arms) — used for low resistance measurement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Golden Rule for KTU Exam:** Always conclude a numerical problem with a clear **"Pass"** or **"Fail"** verdict, citing the relevant IS standard and tolerance. Examiners allocate the final 1 mark specifically for this conclusion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Industrial Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Industrial Loads** refer to the aggregate electrical power demands imposed by machinery, equipment, lighting, heating, ventilation, and auxiliary systems within manufacturing plants, processing industries, and heavy commercial establishments. In the context of **Electrical System Design and Estimation**, an industrial load is characterized by its **connected load**, **maximum demand**, **load factor**, **diversity factor**, **demand factor**, and its **load curve** (daily, monthly, or annual) which together dictate the rating of transformers, switchgear, bus-bars, and the selection of the appropriate tariff structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:**
> An *industrial load* is any load whose **Load Factor is generally higher than 0.5** and whose operating hours typically exceed **8 hours/day**, supplied typically on **HT (High Tension)** or **EHT (Extra High Tension)** depending on the contract demand.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a large bakery that operates 24 hours a day. The ovens, dough mixers, proofing chambers, conveyor belts, lighting, and air-conditioning all consume electricity. The **connected load** is the sum of nameplate ratings of *every* device installed (analogous to the *total seating capacity* of a cinema hall). However, the bakery never switches on *all* ovens and mixers at the *same instant*. The **maximum demand** is the *highest actual power drawn* at any single moment, just as the cinema's *peak crowd* on a Saturday evening is far less than the *seating capacity*. The **load factor** measures *how consistently* the bakery uses its power — a 24/7 bakery has a high load factor (≈0.9), while a seasonal ice-cream factory may dip to 0.3. The electrical designer must size the transformer, cables, and switchgear based on the *maximum demand*, not the *connected load*, to avoid over-design and cost overruns.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Industrial Context)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Metrics to Master for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- **Connected Load (kW / kVA)** — Sum of nameplate ratings of all installed apparatus.
- **Maximum Demand (MD)** — Greatest demand occurring during a specified period.
- **Demand Factor (DF)** — Ratio of Maximum Demand to Connected Load. ($0 < DF \leq 1$)
- **Diversity Factor (DiF)** — Ratio of Sum of Individual Maximum Demands to Group Maximum Demand. (Typically $> 1$)
- **Load Factor (LF)** — Ratio of Average Load to Peak Load in a given period.
- **Plant / Equipment Utilization Factor** — Ratio of actual energy consumed to the energy that *could* have been consumed if the equipment ran at its full rating continuously.
- **Coincidence Factor** — Reciprocal of Diversity Factor.

## 1.4 Categories of Industrial Loads

| Category | Examples | Typical Power Factor | Load Nature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Induction Motors (LT)** | Pumps, compressors, conveyors, fans | 0.70 – 0.85 lagging | Continuous / Intermittent |
| **Synchronous Motors (HT)** | Large compressors, rolling mills | 0.80 leading (adjustable) | Continuous |
| **Resistance / Induction Furnaces** | Steel melting, forging, heat treatment | 0.60 – 0.95 | Cyclic / Batch |
| **Welding Loads** | Arc / Resistance welding | 0.30 – 0.50 (very poor) | Highly Intermittent |
| **Lighting (Industrial)** | HID, LED, Fluorescent | 0.85 – 0.95 | Fixed / Shift-based |
| **HVAC Systems** | Chillers, AHUs, exhaust fans | 0.75 – 0.85 | Continuous |
| **Rectifiers / Drives** | DC drives, electroplating, UPS | 0.60 – 0.75 | Continuous |
| **Electrolytic Processes** | Aluminium, Chlor-alkali | 0.80 – 0.90 | Very Continuous, High LF |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Framework — How Industrial Loads Behave

An industrial consumer's load profile is dictated by:

1. **Production Schedule** — Single shift, double shift, or continuous (3-shift).
2. **Process Type** — Continuous process (e.g., cement, paper) vs. Batch process (e.g., sugar, foundry).
3. **Seasonal Variation** — Ice factories peak in summer, textile mills in winter.
4. **Start-up vs. Steady-State** — Motors draw 5–7× rated current during starting, affecting momentary demand.
5. **Power Factor Behaviour** — Mostly inductive, requiring capacitor compensation.
6. **Tariff Structure** — KVA-based charges, time-of-day (ToD) tariffs, penalty for low PF.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 FOCUS:** In ESE, the examiner expects the student to compute **MD estimation, load factor, demand factor, diversity factor, transformer sizing, and the cost of electricity** under a given tariff.

## 2.2 Estimation Procedure for Industrial Loads (Step-Wise Logic)

The **KTU-recommended** sequence for estimating the MD of an industrial consumer is:

1. **List all connected apparatus** with nameplate kW rating.
2. **Group by duty cycle** — Continuous, Intermittent, Standby.
3. **Apply Diversity Factor** within each group (e.g., not all welders work simultaneously).
4. **Apply Demand Factor** to account for simultaneous vs. full-load operation.
5. **Compute group-wise MD** in kW.
6. **Compute overall Diversity Factor** between groups.
7. **Determine the resultant Maximum Demand in kW** → Convert to kVA using overall PF.
8. **Add future expansion margin** (typically 10–20%).
9. **Select transformer rating** as next standard size above MD.
10. **Verify voltage drop and short-circuit levels** for the chosen transformer.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **examinable directly** in KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the variable definitions, units, and physical limits.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Maximum Demand (MD) | $MD = DF \times Connected\ Load$ | kW | — |
| 2 | Average Load (AL) | $AL = \dfrac{Total\ Energy\ Consumed\ (kWh)}{Time\ Period\ (h)}$ | kW | — |
| 3 | Load Factor (LF) | $LF = \dfrac{Average\ Load}{Maximum\ Demand}$ | pu | $0.30 - 0.90$ |
| 4 | Demand Factor (DF) | $DF = \dfrac{Maximum\ Demand}{Connected\ Load}$ | pu | $0.50 - 0.80$ |
| 5 | Diversity Factor (DiF) | $DiF = \dfrac{\sum\ Individual\ MDs}{Group\ MD}$ | pu | $1.10 - 2.50$ |
| 6 | Coincidence Factor (CF) | $CF = \dfrac{1}{DiF}$ | pu | $0.40 - 0.90$ |
| 7 | Utilization Factor (UF) | $UF = \dfrac{MD\ of\ a\ Group}{Rated\ Capacity\ of\ Group}$ | pu | $0.60 - 0.95$ |
| 8 | Plant Capacity Factor | $\eta_{cap} = \dfrac{AL}{Installed\ Capacity}$ | pu | $0.50 - 0.80$ |
| 9 | kVA from kW | $kVA = \dfrac{kW}{\cos\phi}$ | kVA | — |
| 10 | Energy in kVAh | $E_{kVAh} = \dfrac{E_{kWh}}{PF}$ | kVAh | — |
| 11 | Annual Energy | $E_{annual} = AL \times 8760$ | kWh | — |
| 12 | Diversity of Group of Consumers | $DiF_g = \dfrac{\sum MD_i}{MD_{group}}$ | pu | $>1$ always |

> [!WARNING]
> **PITFALL:** Demand Factor is **always** $\leq 1$. Diversity Factor is **always** $\geq 1$ for groups. Do not interchange. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Transformer Sizing** — A factory with a connected load of **5000 kW** but DF = 0.6 means MD = 3000 kW. Selecting a 5000 kVA transformer would be wasteful.
- **Tariff Optimization** — Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) and similar utilities levy charges on **kVA of MD**, not on connected load. Poor PF increases kVA billing.
- **Switchgear Coordination** — Short-circuit current calculations depend on **transformer rating and impedance**, which are MD-driven.
- **Cable Sizing** — Depends on actual running current, not connected load.
- **Captive Power / DG Set Sizing** — DG rating should match the *essential load MD* during grid failure.

## 2.5 Classification of Industrial Consumers (KTU Perspective)

| Class | Voltage Level | Typical MD | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LT Industrial** | 415 V, 3-phase | $\leq 100\ kVA$ | Small workshops, bakeries |
| **HT Industrial** | 11 kV | $100 - 2500\ kVA$ | Rice mills, small factories |
| **EHT Industrial** | 33 kV / 66 kV / 110 kV | $> 2500\ kVA$ | Steel plants, cement, aluminium smelters |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations of Core Formulas (Exhaustive)

### 3.1.1 Derivation of Load Factor (LF)

By definition, average load is the total energy divided by the period.

$$Average\ Load = \frac{Total\ Energy\ Consumed\ (E)}{Period\ of\ Time\ (T)}$$

Therefore:

$$LF = \frac{Average\ Load}{Maximum\ Demand} = \frac{E / T}{MD}$$

$$LF = \frac{E}{MD \times T} = \frac{kWh}{kW \times hours}$$

**Physical meaning:** If a factory consumes **E = 7,20,000 kWh** in a month (**T = 720 h**) and its **MD = 1250 kW**, then

$$LF = \frac{720000}{1250 \times 720} = \frac{720000}{900000} = 0.80$$

This is a *high load factor* — typical of a continuous process plant (cement, paper, aluminium).

### 3.1.2 Derivation of the Relationship between LF, DF, and DiF

**Step 1:** Connected load of a factory with $n$ groups $= C$.
**Step 2:** Maximum demand of the entire factory

$$MD = DF \times C$$

**Step 3:** Sum of individual maximum demands of all groups $= \sum_{i=1}^{n} MD_i$.
**Step 4:** Group maximum demand (after inter-group diversity)

$$MD_{group} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} MD_i}{DiF}$$

**Step 5:** Average load of the factory

$$AL = LF \times MD_{group}$$

**Step 6:** Substituting,

$$AL = LF \times \frac{\sum MD_i}{DiF}$$

This relationship is frequently tested in KTU problems where the student is given **DiF** and **LF** and must compute the **average load**, or vice-versa.

### 3.1.3 Derivation of kVA Demand from kW and PF

For a balanced 3-phase system,

$$P\ (kW) = \sqrt{3}\ V_L I_L \cos\phi \times 10^{-3}$$

$$S\ (kVA) = \sqrt{3}\ V_L I_L \times 10^{-3}$$

Dividing the second by the first:

$$\frac{S}{P} = \frac{1}{\cos\phi}$$

$$S\ (kVA) = \frac{P\ (kW)}{\cos\phi}$$

If the factory MD is 800 kW at an average PF of 0.80 lagging:

$$S = \frac{800}{0.80} = 1000\ kVA$$

This 1000 kVA is what the utility bills and what the transformer must be sized to handle.

### 3.1.4 Energy in kVAh (Tariff Computation)

For tariff purposes, utilities bill on **kVAh** (apparent energy) rather than kWh, especially under KSEB HT/EHT industrial tariffs.

$$E_{kVAh} = \frac{E_{kWh}}{\cos\phi_{avg}}$$

If a consumer uses **36,00,000 kWh in a month** at an **average PF of 0.85**:

$$E_{kVAh} = \frac{3600000}{0.85} = 42,35,294\ kVAh$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **demand charge** in Indian industrial tariffs is on **kVA of MD** (not kW), incentivizing consumers to maintain PF close to unity through capacitor banks.

## 3.2 Worked Example — KTU-Style Problem

**Problem:** A manufacturing plant has the following connected loads:

| Group | Connected Load (kW) | Demand Factor | Diversity Factor (within group) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Induction Motors (LT) | 1500 | 0.75 | 1.20 |
| Lighting | 200 | 0.90 | 1.10 |
| Welding Sets | 300 | 0.40 | 1.50 |
| HVAC | 400 | 0.85 | 1.15 |
| Auxiliary / Office | 100 | 0.60 | 1.10 |

The **inter-group diversity factor** is **1.30**. The **average power factor** is **0.82 lagging**. The plant operates **16 hours/day, 26 days/month**. Calculate:
1. Maximum demand of each group
2. Overall maximum demand (kW and kVA)
3. Load factor if monthly energy consumption = **4,50,000 kWh**
4. Energy in kVAh
5. Recommended transformer size

### Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step)

#### Step 1: Group-wise Maximum Demand (GMD)

$$GMD_{motors} = 1500 \times 0.75 = 1125\ kW$$

$$GMD_{lighting} = 200 \times 0.90 = 180\ kW$$

$$GMD_{welding} = 300 \times 0.40 = 120\ kW$$

$$GMD_{HVAC} = 400 \times 0.85 = 340\ kW$$

$$GMD_{auxiliary} = 100 \times 0.60 = 60\ kW$$

**Sum of individual GMDs:**

$$\sum GMD_i = 1125 + 180 + 120 + 340 + 60 = 1825\ kW$$

#### Step 2: Overall Maximum Demand (after inter-group diversity)

$$MD_{factory} = \frac{\sum GMD_i}{DiF_{inter-group}} = \frac{1825}{1.30}$$

$$MD_{factory} = 1403.85\ kW \approx 1404\ kW$$

#### Step 3: Convert to kVA

$$MD_{kVA} = \frac{MD_{kW}}{\cos\phi} = \frac{1404}{0.82}$$

$$MD_{kVA} = 1712.20\ kVA$$

#### Step 4: Load Factor

**Operating hours in the month:**

$$T = 16 \times 26 = 416\ hours$$

$$Average\ Load\ (AL) = \frac{E}{T} = \frac{450000}{416} = 1081.73\ kW$$

$$LF = \frac{AL}{MD} = \frac{1081.73}{1404} = 0.7704 \approx 0.77\ pu$$

A load factor of **0.77** indicates a healthy continuous-shift industrial operation.

#### Step 5: Energy in kVAh

$$E_{kVAh} = \frac{450000}{0.82} = 5,48,780\ kVAh$$

#### Step 6: Recommended Transformer Size

Apply 15% future expansion margin:

$$Required\ kVA = 1712.20 \times 1.15 = 1969.03\ kVA$$

Nearest standard rating (IEC/IEEE):

$$\boxed{Recommended\ Transformer: 2000\ kVA,\ 11\ kV / 433\ V}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Impact of PF Correction on Tariff

| Scenario | MD (kW) | PF | MD (kVA) | Monthly Energy (kVAh) | Relative Bill Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Without Capacitor | 1404 | 0.82 | 1712.20 | 5,48,780 | **Baseline (1.00 ×)** |
| With Capacitor Bank 250 kVAr | 1404 | 0.92 | 1526.09 | 4,89,130 | **0.89 × (≈11% saving)** |
| With Capacitor Bank 400 kVAr | 1404 | 0.98 | 1432.65 | 4,59,184 | **0.84 × (≈16% saving)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Take-Away:** Improving PF from 0.82 to 0.98 reduces the *billed* kVA by **~16%**. Capacitor sizing for industrial plants is a guaranteed Part-B question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Industrial Load Estimation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Collect Connected Load Data] --> B[Group Apparatus by Function]
    B --> C1[Motors Group]
    B --> C2[Lighting Group]
    B --> C3[Welding Group]
    B --> C4[HVAC Group]
    B --> C5[Auxiliary / Office Group]
    C1 --> D1[Apply Within-Group DF]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    C5 --> D1
    D1 --> E[Compute Individual Group MDs]
    E --> F[Sum Individual Group MDs]
    F --> G[Apply Inter-Group Diversity Factor]
    G --> H[Overall Factory MD in kW]
    H --> I[Apply Average Power Factor]
    I --> J[Convert to kVA]
    J --> K[Add Future Expansion Margin]
    K --> L[Select Standard Transformer Rating]
    L --> M[End: Specify Switchgear, Cables, DG]
```

**Figure 1:** KTU-Standard Industrial Load Estimation Workflow. The `E` and `F` blocks form the critical aggregation stage where **DF** and **DiF** are sequentially applied.

## 4.2 Load-Curve Representation (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[00:00 - 06:00: Night Lean Period] --> P2[06:00 - 09:00: Warmup Ramp]
    P2 --> P3[09:00 - 17:00: Day Shift Peak]
    P3 --> P4[17:00 - 18:00: Lunch Dip]
    P4 --> P5[18:00 - 22:00: Evening Shift]
    P5 --> P6[22:00 - 24:00: Maintenance Lean]
    P6 --> P1
```

**Figure 2:** Daily Load Curve Pattern for a Two-Shift Industry. The highest demand typically occurs during the morning ramp-up, used for MD billing.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — PF Correction in Industrial Plants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Grid / Utility Supply] --> B[Step-Down Transformer HT to LT]
    B --> C[Main LT Switchboard]
    C --> D1[Induction Motor Loads]
    C --> D2[Lighting Loads]
    C --> D3[Heating Furnace Loads]
    C --> D4[Welding Loads]
    C --> E[Power Factor Correction Panel]
    E --> E1[Automatic Capacitor Bank APFC]
    E --> E2[Detuned Reactor Filters]
    E1 --> C
    E2 --> C
    C --> F[DG Set Emergency Backup]
    C --> G[Energy Meter kVAh Billing]
```

**Figure 3:** Typical Industrial Power Distribution with APFC (Automatic Power Factor Correction) Panel. The APFC maintains PF between **0.95 and 0.99** to minimize utility demand charges.

## 4.4 Schematic — Demand Factor vs Diversity Factor Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Concept Definition] --> B1[Demand Factor DF]
    A1 --> B2[Diversity Factor DiF]
    B1 --> C1[Within a Single Group]
    B2 --> C2[Between Multiple Groups]
    C1 --> D1[DF = MD group / Connected Load]
    C2 --> D2[DiF = Sum of MDs / Group MD]
    D1 --> E1[Always less than 1]
    D2 --> E2[Always greater than 1]
    E1 --> F1[KTU Common Pitfall]
    E2 --> F1
```

**Figure 4:** Conceptual Discrimination between Demand Factor and Diversity Factor — A high-frequency KTU examination topic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define: (i) Maximum Demand, (ii) Load Factor, (iii) Demand Factor. State one industrial significance for each.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Maximum Demand (MD):** It is the greatest demand of load on the power station during a given period (typically 15-minute window for tariff purposes). *Industrial significance:* Determines the **contract demand** with the utility and the **transformer rating** in kVA.

**(ii) Load Factor (LF):** The ratio of average load over a period to the maximum demand occurring in that period.

$$LF = \frac{Average\ Load}{Maximum\ Demand}$$

*Industrial significance:* A higher LF indicates efficient utilization of installed capacity; utilities offer **load factor incentives** for consumers with LF $> 0.75$.

**(iii) Demand Factor (DF):** The ratio of maximum demand of a group of loads to the connected load of that group.

$$DF = \frac{MD}{Connected\ Load}$$

*Industrial significance:* Used to estimate the realistic MD from the total connected load for proper **sizing of switchgear, busbars, and transformers** without over-design.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Demand Factor and Diversity Factor. Why is the diversity factor always greater than unity for a group of consumers?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Demand Factor (DF) | Diversity Factor (DiF) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | $MD / Connected\ Load$ | $\sum\ MD_i / MD_{group}$ |
| Typical Value | $\leq 1$ | $\geq 1$ |
| Scope | Single group or consumer | Multiple groups / consumers |
| Physical Reason | Not all connected devices operate at full rating simultaneously | Peak demands of different groups occur at *different times* |

**Reason DiF $> 1$:** The peak demand of individual consumers occurs at *different times of the day*. For example, in an industrial estate, Factory A may peak at 10:00 AM (motor start-up) while Factory B peaks at 2:00 PM (welding). Hence, the simultaneous sum of all individual MDs is always greater than the group's actual coincident MD.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** A small-scale industry has the following loads connected to a 415 V, 3-phase supply:

- **Induction Motors:** 4 nos. × 15 kW (DF = 0.80, DiF within motors = 1.10, PF = 0.85)
- **Welding Transformers:** 3 nos. × 20 kVA (DF = 0.30, DiF = 1.40, overall PF = 0.50)
- **Lighting Load:** 30 kW (DF = 0.95, PF = 0.95)
- **HVAC System:** 50 kW (DF = 0.85, PF = 0.80)

The **inter-group diversity factor** is **1.25**. Determine:
1. The maximum demand of each group (in kW and kVA).
2. The total maximum demand of the industry.
3. The rating of the transformer to be installed, allowing **20% future expansion**.

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Group 1 — Induction Motors:**

$$MD_1 = 4 \times 15 \times 0.80 = 48\ kW$$

$$kVA_1 = \frac{48}{0.85} = 56.47\ kVA$$

**Group 2 — Welding Transformers:**

Note: Rating is in kVA, not kW.

$$MD_2\ (kVA) = 3 \times 20 \times 0.30 = 18\ kVA$$

$$kW_2 = 18 \times 0.50 = 9\ kW$$

**Group 3 — Lighting:**

$$MD_3 = 30 \times 0.95 = 28.5\ kW$$

$$kVA_3 = \frac{28.5}{0.95} = 30.0\ kVA$$

**Group 4 — HVAC:**

$$MD_4 = 50 \times 0.85 = 42.5\ kW$$

$$kVA_4 = \frac{42.5}{0.80} = 53.13\ kVA$$

**Sum of kW:**

$$\sum MD_i = 48 + 9 + 28.5 + 42.5 = 128\ kW$$

**Sum of kVA (separate, for diversity calculation we use kW with inter-group DiF):**

$$\sum kVA_i = 56.47 + 18 + 30 + 53.13 = 157.6\ kVA$$

**Overall MD after Inter-Group Diversity:**

$$MD_{factory} = \frac{128}{1.25} = 102.4\ kW$$

**Overall PF (assumed average from weighted kVA / kW):**

$$\cos\phi_{avg} = \frac{128}{157.6 / 1.25} = \frac{128}{126.08} \approx 0.81$$

**Overall kVA:**

$$MD_{kVA} = \frac{102.4}{0.81} \approx 126.42\ kVA$$

**With 20% Expansion:**

$$Required\ kVA = 126.42 \times 1.20 = 151.7\ kVA$$

$$\boxed{Recommended\ Transformer: 160\ kVA\ (Standard\ rating)}$$

**(b)** A factory operates for **two shifts of 8 hours each** for **25 days a month**. Its MD is **1500 kVA** at **0.85 PF lagging**. The utility charges:
- **Energy Charge:** ₹6.50 per kVAh
- **Demand Charge:** ₹350 per kVA per month
- **PF Penalty:** ₹0.25 per kVAh for every 0.01 below 0.90 PF

Calculate the **total monthly electricity bill** if the **monthly energy consumption is 6,00,000 kWh**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert kWh to kVAh**

$$E_{kVAh} = \frac{600000}{0.85} = 7,05,882.35\ kVAh$$

**Step 2: Energy Charge**

$$C_1 = 705882.35 \times 6.50 = \text{₹}45,88,235.29$$

**Step 3: Demand Charge**

$$C_2 = 1500 \times 350 = \text{₹}5,25,000.00$$

**Step 4: PF Penalty Calculation**

$$PF\ deviation = 0.90 - 0.85 = 0.05$$

$$Penalty\ units = \frac{0.05}{0.01} = 5\ units$$

$$C_3 = 5 \times 0.25 \times 705882.35 = \text{₹}8,82,352.94$$

**Step 5: Total Bill**

$$Total = C_1 + C_2 + C_3 = 4588235.29 + 525000 + 882352.94$$

$$\boxed{Total\ Monthly\ Bill = \text{₹}59,95,588.23}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently forget that the **PF penalty is calculated on kVAh, not kWh**. Also, the deviation is measured from the **threshold PF (0.90)**, not from unity. Verify both before writing the answer.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` *(Alternative)*

**(a)** Define **Plant Capacity Factor** and **Utilization Factor**. An industry has an installed capacity of **2000 kVA** and its annual energy consumption is **1.0 crore kWh** (10⁷ kWh).
