The maximum demand recorded is **1600 kVA** at **0.8 PF lagging**. Calculate the **Load Factor** and **Plant Capacity Factor**.

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions:**

- **Plant Capacity Factor:** The ratio of the average load on the plant to the installed capacity of the plant.
- **Utilization Factor:** The ratio of the maximum demand on the plant to the rated capacity of the plant.

**Given Data:**

- Installed capacity $C = 2000\ kVA$
- MD $= 1600\ kVA$
- PF $= 0.8$ lagging
- $E = 1.0 \times 10^7\ kWh$ annually
- $T = 8760\ hours/year$

**Step 1: Average Load (kW)**

$$AL = \frac{E}{T} = \frac{10^7}{8760} = 1141.55\ kW$$

**Step 2: Convert MD to kW**

$$MD_{kW} = 1600 \times 0.8 = 1280\ kW$$

**Step 3: Load Factor**

$$LF = \frac{AL}{MD_{kW}} = \frac{1141.55}{1280} = 0.892 \approx 0.89\ pu$$

**Step 4: Plant Capacity Factor**

$$CF = \frac{AL\ (kW)}{Installed\ Capacity\ (kVA) \times 1} = \frac{1141.55}{2000} = 0.571\ pu$$

(For PF correction, the more accurate formula uses installed kW, but with single PF we approximate.)

**Step 5: Utilization Factor**

$$UF = \frac{MD_{kVA}}{Installed\ kVA} = \frac{1600}{2000} = 0.80\ pu$$

$$\boxed{LF = 0.89,\ CF = 0.57,\ UF = 0.80}$$

**(b)** Discuss the **role of capacitor banks in industrial power factor improvement**. A 3-phase, 400 V motor draws **100 A at 0.7 PF lagging**. Calculate the rating of the capacitor bank (in kVAr) required to improve the PF to **0.95 lagging**. What is the new line current after compensation?

**Model Solution:**

**Discussion (Key Points — 4 marks):**

1. Reduces kVA demand, hence **demand charges** drop.
2. Reduces **I²R losses** in cables and transformers.
3. Improves **voltage regulation** of the system.
4. Releases **transformer capacity** for additional load.
5. Avoids **PF penalty** imposed by utilities (e.g., KSEB, BESCOM).
6. **Detuned filters** must be used with capacitors to avoid harmonic resonance (VFD-driven plants).

**Calculations (3 marks):**

**Step 1: Real Power (kW)**

$$P = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_L \times \cos\phi \times 10^{-3}$$

$$P = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 100 \times 0.7 \times 10^{-3} = 48.50\ kW$$

**Step 2: Initial Reactive Power**

$$\phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.7) = 45.57°$$

$$Q_1 = P \times \tan\phi_1 = 48.50 \times \tan(45.57°) = 48.50 \times 1.020 = 49.49\ kVAr$$

**Step 3: Final Reactive Power at 0.95 PF**

$$\phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.95) = 18.19°$$

$$Q_2 = P \times \tan\phi_2 = 48.50 \times \tan(18.19°) = 48.50 \times 0.3287 = 15.94\ kVAr$$

**Step 4: Capacitor Rating**

$$Q_C = Q_1 - Q_2 = 49.49 - 15.94 = 33.55\ kVAr$$

**Step 5: New Line Current**

$$I_{new} = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L \times \cos\phi_2} = \frac{48500}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 0.95}$$

$$I_{new} = \frac{48500}{658.18} = 73.69\ A$$

$$\boxed{Capacitor\ Bank = 33.55\ kVAr,\ New\ Line\ Current = 73.69\ A}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students apply the wrong tangent of the angle or interchange **Q₁ and Q₂**. Always compute **Q₁ (initial) − Q₂ (final)**. Also, the capacitor does **not** reduce kW, only kVAr.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Industrial Loads**

- [x] **MD** drives transformer and switchgear sizing, not connected load.
- [x] **DF $\leq 1$**; **DiF $\geq 1$**; **CF = 1 / DiF**.
- [x] **LF = Average Load / MD** — high LF means efficient plant utilization.
- [x] **Industrial Tariff** is on **kVA** (apparent), not kW (real).
- [x] **PF Improvement** is mandatory in industries (target PF: **0.95 – 0.99**).
- [x] **Capacitor bank sizing:** $Q_C = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$.
- [x] **Standard transformer sizes** (IEC): 100, 160, 250, 400, 500, 630, 1000, 1250, 1600, 2000, 2500 kVA.
- [x] **HT/EHT industrial** consumers pay demand charges + energy charges + PF penalty.
- [x] **Diversity Factor** between groups is **always greater than 1** for a real consumer mix.
- [x] **Utilization Factor** compares MD with *installed capacity*; **Capacity Factor** compares *average load* with installed capacity.
- [x] **Future expansion margin** in design: typically **10–25%** of MD.
- [x] **Hours per year:** $8760 = 365 \times 24$.
- [x] For 3-phase: $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi \times 10^{-3}\ kW$.
- [x] Welding sets have **very low PF (0.3–0.5)** — apply heavy derating.
- [x] **Continuous process industries** (cement, aluminium, paper) have LF $> 0.80$.
- [x] **Batch process industries** (foundry, sugar) have LF between 0.50–0.70.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Starters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Selection of Starters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Starter (Motor Starter):** An electrical switching and control device, typically a contactor-based assembly, used to safely apply a reduced or controlled voltage to an electric motor during the starting period and then transition the motor to full-voltage (or rated) running conditions once it has accelerated to a safe operational speed.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCEET602), the **Selection of Starters** is the engineering process of choosing an appropriate reduced-voltage or starting method for **three-phase induction motors** (both squirrel-cage and slip-ring/ wound rotor types) such that:

1. The **starting current** drawn from the supply is restricted within utility limits (typically **2 to 6 times** the full-load current).
2. The **starting torque** is sufficient to overcome the load's breakaway torque and accelerate the rotor to rated speed.
3. The mechanical and thermal stresses on the motor windings, shaft, coupling, and driven load are minimized.
4. The starter complies with the relevant **IS/IEC standards** (e.g., **IS 13947**, **IEC 60947-4-1**) and Indian Electricity Rules.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Heavy Truck on an Incline**
> Imagine a fully loaded 20-tonne truck parked on a steep hill. If you suddenly engage the clutch in 5th gear (an aggressive start), the engine will stall, the clutch plate will burn out, and the truck will jerk violently. Instead, the driver uses **1st gear** (low gear ratio) to build momentum gradually, then shifts up. 
>
> An **electrical starter** is exactly this "low gear" for a motor. A motor at standstill looks like a short-circuited transformer secondary with very low rotor resistance, so it can draw **5 to 8 times** its rated current. A starter steps in to "soft-launch" the motor, limiting this inrush, and then hands over control once the rotor is up to speed.

## 1.3 Why Starters Are Mandatory in Design Estimation

> [!WARNING]
> **Consequence of Skipping a Starter (DOL on Large Motors):**
> - Voltage dip in the **distribution feeder** → flicker of lights, malfunction of sensitive equipment.
> - Tripping of the **incoming circuit breaker** or upstream protective device.
> - Mechanical shock → broken shaft keys, damaged couplings, premature bearing failure.
> - **IS 3043 / IE Rule 51** compliance failure during inspection by Electrical Inspector.

In **Electrical System Design and Estimation**, selecting the correct starter is essential because the **short-circuit current contribution** and **starting VA** of motors directly affect the rating of:
- The **transformer** (its impedance and kVA rating),
- The **cable** size (voltage-drop check during starting),
- The **alternator / DG set** (motor starting capability curve, only relevant in PCEET602 indirectly).

## 1.4 Classification of Starters (As per KTU Syllabus)

| Class | Type | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|
| **Full-Voltage** | Direct-On-Line (DOL) | Small motors (≤ 5 kW as per IE Rule) |
| **Reduced-Voltage** | Star-Delta | Medium motors, no-load / light-load start |
| **Reduced-Voltage** | Auto-Transformer | Heavy-load start, tap variability needed |
| **Reduced-Voltage** | Primary Reactor (Reactor Starter) | Large motors, industrial networks |
| **Rotor-Side** | Rotor Resistance Starter | Slip-ring / wound-rotor motors only |
| **Solid-State** | Soft Starter (Thyristor-based) | Modern plants, soft-stop needed |
| **Inverter-based** | VFD / Variable Frequency Drive | Premium efficiency, speed control required |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Generalized

A starter operates on one of three physical principles:

1. **Voltage Reduction at Stator Terminals** → Stator flux $\phi \propto V$ is reduced; rotor current is reduced; starting current is reduced.
2. **Rotor Circuit Impedance Insertion** → Effective rotor resistance is increased, improving starting torque while limiting current.
3. **Frequency & Voltage Modulation** → Electronic control of $V/f$ to maintain flux and limit current (Soft Starters, VFDs).

## 2.2 Direct-On-Line (DOL) Starter — The Baseline

- **Construction:** A contactor (main), an overload relay (OLR), and start/stop push-buttons.
- **Working:** On START, the contactor closes and applies **100%** line voltage to the motor terminals.
- **Equivalent Circuit at Starting:** Rotor is at standstill, slip $s = 1$. The motor draws the **locked-rotor current**.
- **Standard Starting Current:** $\mathbf{I_{st} \approx 6 \text{ to } 8 \times I_{fl}}$ for standard efficiency induction motors.
- **Standard Starting Torque:** $\mathbf{T_{st} \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2.5 \times T_{fl}}$.
- **IE Rule Reference:** As per Indian Electricity Rules, motors up to **5 kW** (or per local DISCOM norms) may be started DOL.

## 2.3 Star-Delta Starter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating Sequence:**
> 1. Initially, motor windings are connected in **STAR (Y)** → phase voltage $V_{ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$.
> 2. After a preset time delay (typically **5–10 s**), a timer-cum-contactor arrangement shifts the connection to **DELTA (Δ)** → full line voltage across each winding.

**Why this reduces current and torque:**

In star connection, the per-phase voltage is reduced by a factor of $\sqrt{3}$, and additionally the line current in star equals the phase current (not $\sqrt{3}$ times the phase current as in delta). The combined effect reduces the **line current** drawn from the supply to **one-third** of the DOL starting current.

## 2.4 Auto-Transformer Starter

- **Construction:** A 3-phase auto-transformer with **taps** (typically **50%, 60%, 65%, 80%**).
- **Working:** Motor is first fed through the auto-transformer at a reduced voltage equal to the **tap value × line voltage**. Once near full speed, the auto-transformer is bypassed and the motor receives full voltage.
- **Advantage over Star-Delta:** Tap is **selectable** → starting current AND starting torque can both be tuned for the specific load.

## 2.5 Rotor Resistance Starter (Slip-Ring Motor)

- Only applicable to **wound-rotor (slip-ring) induction motors**.
- External resistances are inserted into the rotor circuit via slip rings and brushes.
- As the motor accelerates, the resistances are **progressively cut out** in steps until all are removed and the rotor is short-circuited.
- Provides **high starting torque (up to 2.5× full-load torque)** with **low starting current**.

## 2.6 Soft Starter (Electronic)

- Uses **back-to-back thyristors (SCRs)** in each phase, fired by phase-angle control.
- Ramps up voltage over an adjustable time (typically **2–60 s**).
- Often includes **soft-stop** (controlled deceleration).
- No moving high-current parts → long life, low maintenance.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula bank** for this topic. All derivations are given in SECTION 3.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Notes / Standard Multiplier |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DOL Starting Current | $I_{st(DOL)} = K_1 \cdot I_{fl}$ | $K_1 = 6$ to $8$ |
| 2 | DOL Starting Torque | $T_{st(DOL)} = K_2 \cdot T_{fl}$ | $K_2 = 1.5$ to $2.5$ |
| 3 | Star-Delta Line Starting Current | $I_{st(Y-\Delta)} = \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot I_{st(DOL)}$ | Universal factor of 1/3 |
| 4 | Star-Delta Starting Torque | $T_{st(Y-\Delta)} = \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot T_{st(DOL)}$ | Same factor of 1/3 |
| 5 | Auto-Trans. Starting Current (from mains) | $I_{mains} = x^{2} \cdot I_{st(DOL)}$ | $x$ = tap fraction (e.g., 0.6) |
| 6 | Auto-Trans. Starting Torque | $T_{st(AT)} = x^{2} \cdot T_{st(DOL)}$ | Same $x^{2}$ factor |
| 7 | Voltage Drop during Starting | $\Delta V\,[\%] = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}\cdot I_{st}\cdot Z_{cable}}{V_{L}} \times 100$ | Must be $< 10\%$ (typically $< 5\%$) |
| 8 | Transformer kVA Required | $S_{tx}[kVA] = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}\cdot V_{L}\cdot I_{st}}{1000} \times \text{simultaneity}$ | For largest motor starting |
| 9 | Rotor Resistance for max starting torque | $r_{2}' = \dfrac{r_{2}}{s_{mT}}$ | At slip of max torque $s_{mT}$ |
| 10 | Full-Load Current (3-φ) | $I_{fl} = \dfrac{P[kW] \times 1000}{\sqrt{3}\cdot V_{L}\cdot \eta \cdot \cos\phi}$ | Standard motor formula |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** Both **Star-Delta** and **Auto-Transformer** starters reduce the starting torque by the **square of the voltage reduction factor**, while they reduce the starting current by the **square** as well (for auto-trans) or by a linear **1/3** factor (for star-delta, due to the line-to-phase current relationship). This is a key conceptual difference students confuse in exams.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **industrial electrical design**:
- **Compressors, conveyors, and pumps** (low starting torque) → Star-Delta is the most economical.
- **Crushers, mills, mixers** (high breakaway torque) → Auto-Transformer or Rotor Resistance.
- **HVAC fans, escalators** (smooth start/stop essential) → Soft Starter.
- **Process lines with variable speed** (pumps for flow control) → VFD.

The selection directly affects the **capital cost** of the switchgear panel and the **running efficiency** of the plant. KTU examiners frequently test this trade-off.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Star-Delta Starting Current Reduction

**Given:** A 3-phase induction motor of rated line voltage $V_L$, full-load current $I_{fl}$, and DOL starting current $I_{st(DOL)}$.

**Step 1 — DOL Case (Reference):**

In direct-on-line starting, the line current drawn from the supply equals the per-phase current of the motor windings (assumed delta-connected internally for a standard induction motor designed for DOL operation):

$$I_{st(DOL)} = I_{ph(\Delta)} \quad \text{[at standstill]}$$

**Step 2 — Star-Delta Initial State (Y-Connection of Stator Windings):**

The same motor windings, when externally reconfigured in STAR, now receive a per-phase voltage:

$$V_{ph(Y)} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$$

The per-phase current at standstill is proportional to voltage (assuming rotor impedance unchanged at $s=1$):

$$I_{ph(Y)} = \dfrac{I_{ph(\Delta)}}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 3 — Line Current in Star Connection:**

A fundamental property of a balanced star connection is that the **line current equals the phase current**:

$$I_{L(Y)} = I_{ph(Y)} = \dfrac{I_{ph(\Delta)}}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting $I_{ph(\Delta)} = I_{st(DOL)}$:**

$$I_{L(Y)} = \dfrac{I_{st(DOL)}}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Factor 1/3:**

We must square both numerator and denominator to express the **power/current squared ratio** used in the standard result. The equivalent per-phase impedance at standstill is $Z = V_{ph(\Delta)} / I_{ph(\Delta)}$. In star, the per-phase voltage is reduced to $V_L/\sqrt{3}$, so the line current is $V_L/(\sqrt{3} \cdot Z)$. When expressed in terms of the DOL line current at line voltage $V_L$ (where $I_{st(DOL)} = V_L/Z$), the ratio becomes:

$$I_{L(Y)} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \dfrac{V_L/\sqrt{3}}{Z} = \dfrac{V_L}{3 \cdot Z} = \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot I_{st(DOL)}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,I_{st(Y-\Delta)} = \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot I_{st(DOL)}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Starting Torque Derivation:**

Induction motor torque is proportional to the square of the supply voltage (with rotor and slip held constant):

$$T \propto V_{ph}^{2}$$

Therefore, the torque ratio is:

$$\dfrac{T_{st(Y)}}{T_{st(\Delta)}} = \left(\dfrac{V_{ph(Y)}}{V_{ph(\Delta)}}\right)^{2} = \left(\dfrac{V_L/\sqrt{3}}{V_L}\right)^{2} = \dfrac{1}{3}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_{st(Y-\Delta)} = \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot T_{st(DOL)}\,}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Auto-Transformer Starter

**Given:** Auto-transformer with tap ratio $x$ (where $x \cdot V_L$ is the voltage applied to the motor), and DOL starting current $I_{st(DOL)}$.

**Step 1 — Current in Motor Winding at Tap $x$:**

The motor sees a reduced voltage $V_m = x \cdot V_L$. Since rotor impedance is unchanged at standstill:

$$I_{motor(at\,tap\,x)} = x \cdot I_{st(DOL)}$$

**Step 2 — Current Drawn from Mains (Power Balance):**

The auto-transformer is (ideally) lossless. Therefore, **input VA = output VA**:

$$V_L \cdot I_{mains} = V_m \cdot I_{motor} = (x \cdot V_L) \cdot (x \cdot I_{st(DOL)})$$

$$I_{mains} = x^{2} \cdot I_{st(DOL)}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,I_{st(AT,\,mains)} = x^{2} \cdot I_{st(DOL)}\,}$$

**Step 3 — Starting Torque:**

Since torque is proportional to the square of the voltage applied to the motor:

$$T_{st(AT)} = x^{2} \cdot T_{st(DOL)}$$

## 3.3 Numerical Verification — Worked Example

**Problem:** A 15 kW, 400 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz induction motor has a full-load current of 28 A, DOL starting current of $6 \times I_{fl}$, and DOL starting torque of $2 \times T_{fl}$. Calculate starting current and starting torque for:
(a) DOL starter
(b) Star-Delta starter
(c) Auto-Transformer starter at 65% tap

**Solution:**

**(a) DOL Starter:**

$$I_{st(DOL)} = 6 \times 28 = 168\,A$$

$$T_{st(DOL)} = 2 \times T_{fl}$$

**(b) Star-Delta Starter:**

$$I_{st(Y-\Delta)} = \dfrac{1}{3} \times 168 = 56\,A$$

$$T_{st(Y-\Delta)} = \dfrac{1}{3} \times T_{st(DOL)} = 0.667 \times T_{fl}$$

**(c) Auto-Transformer at 65% Tap ($x = 0.65$):**

$$I_{mains(AT)} = (0.65)^{2} \times 168 = 0.4225 \times 168 = 70.98\,A$$

$$T_{st(AT)} = (0.65)^{2} \times 2 \times T_{fl} = 0.845 \times T_{fl}$$

**Analytical Comparison Matrix:**

| Starter | Mains Current | Current Saved | Starting Torque | Torque Available |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| DOL | 168 A | 0% (Baseline) | 2.0 × FLT | Highest |
| Star-Delta | 56 A | 67% reduction | 0.67 × FLT | Lowest |
| Auto-Trans. 65% | 70.98 A | 57.8% reduction | 0.845 × FLT | Intermediate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Trick:** Note that the auto-transformer at 65% gives **higher starting torque (0.845 vs 0.67)** than star-delta, but with **slightly higher current (70.98 A vs 56 A)**. This is why auto-transformer is preferred for **high-inertia loads** like crushers and centrifuges, where star-delta would stall the motor.

## 3.4 Derivation: Rotor Resistance Starter — Maximum Starting Torque Condition

**Given:** Wound-rotor motor with rotor resistance per phase $r_2$ and standstill rotor reactance $x_2$.

**Step 1 — Condition for Maximum Torque (Thevenin Equivalent):**

The slip at which maximum torque occurs is:

$$s_{mT} = \dfrac{r_2'}{R_{th}}$$

where $r_2'$ is the **total rotor circuit resistance referred to stator** and $R_{th}$ is the Thevenin equivalent resistance seen from the rotor side.

**Step 2 — Maximum Torque Magnitude:**

The maximum torque is **independent of rotor resistance** and equals:

$$T_{max} = \dfrac{1.5}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_{th}^{2}}{R_{th} + R_{th}} = \dfrac{1.5 \cdot V_{th}^{2}}{2 \cdot \omega_s \cdot R_{th}} = \dfrac{k \cdot V_{L}^{2}}{R_{th}}$$

**Step 3 — Designing Rotor Resistance for High Starting Torque:**

By inserting external rotor resistance $R_{ext}$ such that the new total $r_2' = r_2 + R_{ext}$ makes $s_{mT} \geq 1$, the motor can produce **maximum torque right from standstill**:

$$\boxed{\,r_2 + R_{ext} \geq R_{th} \implies s_{mT} \geq 1 \implies T_{start} = T_{max}\,}$$

This is the unique advantage of slip-ring motors: **high starting torque with low starting current**, ideal for **cranes, hoists, elevators, and heavy mills**.

## 3.5 Decision Matrix for Starter Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix is the **ready-reckoner for KTU ESE questions**. It is the most-tested topic in Module 3.

| Motor Rating (kW) | Motor Type | Load Type | Recommended Starter | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **≤ 5 kW** | Squirrel-Cage | Any | **DOL** | IE Rule permits, simplest, cheapest |
| **5 to 15 kW** | Squirrel-Cage | Light (Pump, Fan) | **Star-Delta** | Low cost, sufficient torque |
| **15 to 75 kW** | Squirrel-Cage | Heavy (Compressor) | **Auto-Transformer** | Higher starting torque, tunable |
| **> 75 kW** | Squirrel-Cage | Heavy | **Auto-Transformer / Reactor** | Avoid supply voltage dip |
| **> 75 kW** | Squirrel-Cage | Smooth start-stop | **Soft Starter (Solid-State)** | Modern, low maintenance |
| **> 75 kW** | Squirrel-Cage | Variable speed needed | **VFD** | Speed + torque control |
| **Any (slip-ring)** | Wound-Rotor | High inertia (Crane) | **Rotor Resistance Starter** | High torque, low current |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Starter Selection Logic Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Motor Selection Required] --> B{Motor Type?}
    B -- Squirrel Cage --> C{Motor Rating kW}
    B -- Slip Ring / Wound --> SR1[Use Rotor Resistance Starter]
    
    C -- less 5kW --> D1[DOL Starter]
    C -- 5 to 15kW --> D2{Load Type}
    C -- 15 to 75kW --> D3{Load Type}
    C -- greater 75kW --> D4{Speed Control Needed}
    
    D2 -- Light Fan Pump --> E1[Star Delta Starter]
    D2 -- Heavy Compressor --> E2[Auto Transformer Starter]
    
    D3 -- Light --> F1[Star Delta]
    D3 -- Heavy --> F2[Auto Transformer or Reactor]
    
    D4 -- No --> G1[Soft Starter Electronic]
    D4 -- Yes --> G2[VFD Variable Frequency Drive]
    
    D1 --> H1[Add Overload Relay OLR]
    E1 --> H1
    E2 --> H1
    F1 --> H1
    F2 --> H1
    G1 --> H1
    G2 --> H1
    SR1 --> H1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Power Flow Architecture of an Auto-Transformer Starter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M3[3 Phase Mains 415V] --> CB1[Main Circuit Breaker MCCB]
    CB1 --> K1[Contactor K1 Input]
    K1 --> AT[Auto Transformer Taps 50 60 65 80 percent]
    AT --> K2[Contactor K2 Reduced Voltage Mode]
    K2 --> MOTOR[3 Phase Induction Motor Stator]
    
    M3 --> K3[Bypass Contactor K3 Full Voltage Mode]
    K3 --> MOTOR
    
    TIMER[Electronic Timer Relay] -- Triggers transition after 5 to 10 s --> K2
    TIMER -- De energizes K2 and energizes K3 --> K3
    
    OLR[Overload Relay] -- Trips on sustained overcurrent --> K1
    OLR --> K2
    OLR --> K3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Star-Delta Starter Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Push Button Pressed] --> K1C[Main Contactor K1 Closes]
    K1C --> K2C[Star Contactor K2 Closes]
    K2C --> STATE1[Motor Windings Connected in STAR]
    STATE1 --> RUN1[Motor Runs at reduced V by factor 1.41]
    RUN1 --> TIMER[Timer Counts up to 5 to 10 s]
    TIMER --> K2O[Star Contactor K2 Opens]
    K2O --> ARC[Arc Suppression Chute Prevents Short]
    ARC --> K3C[Delta Contactor K3 Closes]
    K3C --> STATE2[Motor Windings Reconnected in DELTA]
    STATE2 --> RUN2[Motor Runs at Full Line Voltage]
    
    STOP[Stop Push Button] --> K1O[All Contactors Open]
    K1O --> STOPSTATE[Motor Disconnected from Supply]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Soft Starter Operation (V/f Profile)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[Time 0 s] -- Voltage ramp start --> T2[Time 2 s]
    T2 -- Voltage reaches 40 percent --> T5[Time 5 s]
    T5 -- Voltage reaches 80 percent --> T10[Time 10 s]
    T10 -- Voltage reaches 100 percent --> T12[Time 12 s Bypass Contactor Closes]
    T12 --> END[Soft Starter Bypassed Thyristors Idle]
    
    T0 -.- M1[Thyristor Firing Angle 150 degrees]
    T2 -.- M2[Firing Angle 100 degrees]
    T5 -.- M3[Firing Angle 60 degrees]
    T10 -.- M4[Firing Angle 15 degrees]
    T12 -.- M5[Thyristors Fully ON]
```

## 4.5 Comparison Block Diagram — Starter Type Trade-offs

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRADEOFF [Starter Selection Trade Off Cube]
        direction LR
        X[Cost Axis Low to High]
        Y[Torque Axis Low to High]
        Z[Current Limitation Axis Low to High]
    end
    
    TRADEOFF --> DOL[DOL: Low Cost, High Torque, No Current Limitation]
    TRADEOFF --> SD[Star Delta: Low Cost, Low Torque, Good Current Limitation]
    TRADEOFF --> AT[Auto Transformer: Medium Cost, High Torque, Best Current Limitation]
    TRADEOFF --> SS[Soft Starter: High Cost, Medium Torque, Programmable]
    TRADEOFF --> VFD[VFD: Highest Cost, Full Control, Premium Efficiency]
    TRADEOFF --> ROT[Rot Res: Medium Cost, Highest Torque, Requires Slip Ring Motor]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagrammatic Reading Hint:** In KTU board exams, drawing **any one** functional block diagram correctly (showing the contactor logic, timer, and OLR) fetches **3 to 4 marks** even in a 14-mark question. Always include the **OLR** symbol — it is a favourite evaluation point.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a starter. Why is a starter required for a 3-phase induction motor?**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

A starter is an electrical control device used to limit the **starting current** and provide **suitable starting torque** to a motor during the acceleration period. A starter is required because, at standstill (slip $s = 1$), the rotor of an induction motor behaves like a short-circuited secondary of a transformer and draws **5 to 8 times** the full-load current. Direct switching on such a high current causes:
- Severe **voltage drop** in the supply mains, affecting other connected loads.
- **Overheating** of motor windings and mechanical stress on the shaft.
- **Tripping** of protective devices.
Hence, a starter is necessary to ensure safe and reliable motor operation. **(3 marks — definition: 1, explanation: 1, consequences: 1)**

---

### **Q2. Compare Star-Delta and Auto-Transformer starters in terms of starting current and starting torque reduction.**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample]**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

| Parameter | Star-Delta | Auto-Transformer (tap $x$) |
|---|---|---|
| Starting current (from mains) | $\dfrac{1}{3} \cdot I_{st(DOL)}$ | $x^{2} \cdot I_{st(DOL)}$ |
| Starting torque | $\dfrac{1}{3} \cdot T_{st(DOL)}$ | $x^{2} \cdot T_{st(DOL)}$ |

In Star-Delta, both quantities are reduced by a **fixed factor of 1/3**. In Auto-Transformer, the reduction is **adjustable** via the tap setting $x$ (commonly 0.5, 0.6, 0.65, 0.8). For the same current reduction, Auto-Transformer delivers **higher starting torque** than Star-Delta. **(3 marks — table 2, explanation 1)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse the two star-delta factors.** Some students write $I_{st(Y)} = I_{st(\Delta)} / \sqrt{3}$ instead of the correct $1/3$ for line current. This costs **2 full marks**.
> 2. **Always specify the reference point** — is the current in the motor winding or in the supply mains? Auto-transformer mains current vs motor current is a classic trap.
> 3. **Show units** in the final numerical answer. Marks are deducted for "168" without "A".
> 4. **Draw the contactor logic** for full marks on descriptive questions — examiners reward diagrams.
> 5. **Mention IE Rule** reference wherever motor rating is discussed (e.g., "≤ 5 kW per IE Rule 51").

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat functional diagram the working of an **Auto-Transformer starter** used for starting a 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor. Mention its advantages over the Star-Delta starter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **22 kW, 400 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz** squirrel-cage induction motor has a full-load current of **42 A**, a full-load speed of **1440 rpm**, DOL starting current of **6 × FL current**, and DOL starting torque of **1.8 × FL torque**. Calculate the starting current and starting torque when started using:
- (i) **Star-Delta starter**
- (ii) **Auto-Transformer starter at 70% tap**

Comment on the suitability of each for a **centrifugal pump load**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Auto-Transformer Starter — Description (7 marks):**

**Construction:** A 3-phase auto-transformer with multiple **taps** (typically 50%, 60%, 65%, 80% of line voltage), three main contactors (one for input, one for reduced-voltage output, one for bypass), an **electronic timer relay**, an **overload relay (OLR)**, and start/stop push buttons.

**Working Principle (Step-by-step):**
1. On pressing the **START** button, contactor **K1** (input) and contactor **K2** (reduced-voltage) close. The motor receives a reduced voltage of $x \cdot V_L$ (where $x$ is the selected tap fraction).
2. The motor accelerates at reduced voltage, drawing only $x^2 \cdot I_{st(DOL)}$ from the mains.
3. The **timer relay** starts counting (preset to 5–10 s, the time for the motor to reach ~80% of full speed).
4. After the preset time, **K2 opens** (with arc suppression to prevent short with K3) and contactor **K3** (bypass) closes. The motor now receives **full line voltage** $V_L$.
5. The OLR continuously monitors current and trips the contactors in case of sustained overload.

**Advantages over Star-Delta (3 marks allocated here):**
- **Adjustable** starting voltage via tap selection (Star-Delta is fixed at 1/√3).
- For the same current reduction, the auto-transformer delivers **higher starting torque** (since $T \propto V^2$ and motor sees the same $x \cdot V_L$ in both cases, but the reduction factor in star-delta is fixed at 1/√3, whereas the auto-transformer can be set to 0.8, 0.65, etc., with better torque at higher taps).
- **Higher power factor** during starting.
- Suitable for **high-inertia loads** like compressors, mills, and conveyors where star-delta would stall.

**Functional Diagram:** Already drawn in SECTION 4.2 of these notes. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Rated power: $P = 22\,kW$
- Rated voltage: $V_L = 400\,V$
- Full-load current: $I_{fl} = 42\,A$
- Full-load speed: $N_{fl} = 1440\,rpm$
- DOL starting current: $I_{st(DOL)} = 6 \times I_{fl}$
- DOL starting torque: $T_{st(DOL)} = 1.8 \times T_{fl}$

**Step 1 — Compute DOL starting current:**

$$I_{st(DOL)} = 6 \times 42 = 252\,A$$

**Step 2 — Compute DOL starting torque:**

$$T_{st(DOL)} = 1.8 \times T_{fl}$$

**Step 3 — Compute full-load torque:**

$$T_{fl} = \dfrac{P}{2\pi N_{fl}/60} = \dfrac{22000}{2\pi \times 1440/60} = \dfrac{22000}{150.80} = 145.89\,N\cdot m$$

Therefore, $T_{st(DOL)} = 1.8 \times 145.89 = 262.6\,N\cdot m$.

**(i) Star-Delta Starter:**

$$I_{st(Y-\Delta)} = \dfrac{1}{3} \times I_{st(DOL)} = \dfrac{1}{3} \times 252 = 84\,A$$

$$T_{st(Y-\Delta)} = \dfrac{1}{3} \times T_{st(DOL)} = \dfrac{1}{3} \times 262.6 = 87.5\,N\cdot m$$

**(ii) Auto-Transformer at 70% Tap ($x = 0.70$):**

$$I_{mains(AT)} = x^{2} \times I_{st(DOL)} = (0.70)^{2} \times 252 = 0.49 \times 252 = 123.48\,A$$

$$T_{st(AT)} = x^{2} \times T_{st(DOL)} = (0.70)^{2} \times 262.6 = 0.49 \times 262.6 = 128.7\,N\cdot m$$

**Step 4 — Suitability for Centrifugal Pump:**

A centrifugal pump is a **low starting-torque load** (torque varies as the square of speed, $T \propto N^2$). At standstill, it requires only **~20–30% of full-load torque** to break away.

- **Star-Delta** delivers $87.5\,N\cdot m$ of starting torque, which is **60% of full-load torque (87.5 / 145.89 = 0.60)** — well above the pump's 20–30% requirement. ✅ **Suitable and economical**.
- **Auto-Transformer at 70%** delivers $128.7\,N\cdot m$ (88% of FLT) — also suitable, but more expensive and unnecessary for this light load. ❌ **Over-specified**.

**Conclusion:** **Star-Delta starter is the optimal choice** for this centrifugal pump due to its low starting torque demand and the cost-effectiveness of the star-delta configuration.

**Final Answer Summary (for KTU answer sheet):**
| Starter | Starting Current | Starting Torque | Pump Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|
| DOL | 252 A | 262.6 N·m | Excessive current, not preferred |
| Star-Delta | **84 A** | **87.5 N·m** | **Suitable and economical** |
| Auto-Trans 70% | 123.48 A | 128.7 N·m | Suitable but over-specified |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a **Direct-On-Line (DOL) starter**. State **two advantages** and **two limitations** of the DOL starter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **7.5 kW, 400 V, 3-phase** induction motor draws a DOL starting current of **35 A** and a starting torque of **22 N·m**. The full-load current and torque are **14 A** and **40 N·m** respectively. Design and recommend a suitable starter for this motor, with full calculations justifying your choice. Mention the Indian Electricity Rule reference. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**(a) DOL Starter — Description (7 marks):**

**Construction:** A DOL starter consists of:
- A **3-pole contactor** (main switching device) with electromagnetic coil.
- An **Overload Relay (OLR)** — bimetal or electronic type — for sustained overcurrent protection.
- **Start (NO)** and **Stop (NC)** push buttons.
- **Auxiliary contacts** for indication and interlocking.

**Working:**
1. When the START button is pressed, the contactor coil is energized through the STOP-NC and START-NO contacts.
2. The contactor main poles close, applying **full 3-phase line voltage** (415 V) to the motor terminals.
3. The auxiliary contact (NO) closes in parallel with START button to provide **seal-in / holding** contact.
4. The motor accelerates from standstill to full speed, drawing a high inrush current (typically 6–8 × FL) for a few cycles, which then decays to full-load current.
5. The OLR continuously monitors the current. If it exceeds the set value (e.g., 1.1–1.2 × FL) for a preset time, the bimetal strip bends and trips the contactor.
6. Pressing STOP de-energizes the coil and opens the contactor.

**Two Advantages (2 marks):**
- **Simple, robust, and cheapest** starter.
- **Highest starting torque** — full voltage applied.
- Compact size, easy maintenance.

**Two Limitations (2 marks):**
- **Very high starting current** (5–8 × FL), causing voltage dip in the distribution network.
- **Not suitable for large motors** (> 5 kW as per IE Rule 51).
- Severe mechanical stress on the driven load.

**Neat Sketch:** Refer to standard textbook (control circuit with START/STOP push-buttons, contactor coil, OLR, and seal-in auxiliary contact). Marks for the diagram: **2 marks**.

**(b) Starter Design Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Motor rating: $P = 7.5\,kW$
- Voltage: $V_L = 400\,V$ (3-phase)
- DOL starting current: $I_{st(DOL)} = 35\,A$
- DOL starting torque: $T_{st(DOL)} = 22\,N\cdot m$
- Full-load current: $I_{fl} = 14\,A$
- Full-load torque: $T_{fl} = 40\,N\cdot m$

**Step 1 — Compute DOL starting current as a multiple of FL current:**

$$K_{I(DOL)} = \dfrac{I_{st(DOL)}}{I_{fl}} = \dfrac{35}{14} = 2.5$$

**Step 2 — Compute DOL starting torque as a multiple of FL torque:**

$$K_{T(DOL)} = \dfrac{T_{st(DOL)}}{T_{fl}} = \dfrac{22}{40} = 0.55$$

**Step 3 — Comment on DOL Suitability:**

The motor is 7.5 kW, which **exceeds the 5 kW limit** specified by the **Indian Electricity Rule (Rule 51 / 76 depending on revision)** for direct-on-line starting. Additionally, the starting current ratio $2.5 \times I_{fl}$ is acceptable but on the higher side. A reduced-voltage starter is **recommended**.

**Step 4 — Star-Delta Starter Analysis:**

$$I_{st(Y-\Delta)} = \dfrac{1}{3} \times 35 = 11.67\,A$$

$$T_{st(Y-\Delta)} = \dfrac{1}{3} \times 22 = 7.33\,N\cdot m$$

$$T_{st(Y-\Delta)} \text{ as a fraction of } T_{fl} = \dfrac{7.33}{40} = 0.183 \approx 18.3\%$$

**Step 5 — Auto-Transformer at 80% Tap ($x = 0.80$):**

$$I_{mains(AT)} = (0.80)^{2} \times 35 = 0.64 \times 35 = 22.4\,A$$

$$T_{st(AT)} = (0.80)^{2} \times 22 = 0.64 \times 22 = 14.08\,N\cdot m$$

$$T_{st(AT)} \text{ as a fraction of } T_{fl} = \dfrac{14.08}{40} = 0.352 \approx 35.2\%$$

**Step 6 — Auto-Transformer at 65% Tap ($x = 0.65$):**

$$I_{mains(AT)} = (0.65)^{2} \times 35 = 0.4225 \times 35 = 14.79\,A$$

$$T_{st(AT)} = (0.65)^{2} \times 22 = 0.4225 \times 22 = 9.295\,N\cdot m$$

$$T_{st(AT)} \text{ as a fraction of } T_{fl} = \dfrac{9.295}{40} = 0.232 \approx 23.2\%$$

**Step 7 — Load Assumption and Final Selection:**

Assuming a typical industrial load (e.g., a small compressor or a centrifugal pump) that requires a starting torque of **20–30% of full-load torque**:

| Starter | Starting Current | Starting Torque (% of FLT) | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| DOL | 35 A (250% of FL) | 55% | Exceeds IE Rule 51 limit |
| Star-Delta | 11.67 A | 18.3% | Marginal, may stall under load |
| Auto-Trans 80% | 22.4 A | 35.2% | **Recommended** — adequate torque |
| Auto-Trans 65% | 14.79 A | 23.2% | Also acceptable |

**Conclusion:** For a **7.5 kW** motor requiring a starting torque of 20–30% of full-load torque, an **Auto-Transformer starter at 65% or 80% tap** is the optimal choice. If the load is very light (e.g., a fan with no breakaway torque), a **Star-Delta starter** is the most economical alternative.

**Indian Electricity Rule Reference:** As per **IE Rule 51 (and the related CEA Safety Regulations)**, motors of rating **above 5 kW** must be provided with a **reduced-voltage starter** to limit the starting disturbance on the supply network. **DOL starting is restricted to motors ≤ 5 kW**.

**Final Design Recommendation:** **Auto-Transformer starter at 80% tap** for the most robust operation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this checklist in the last 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Definition:** A starter limits starting current and provides controlled starting torque.
- ✅ **DOL current** = $6$ to $8 \times I_{fl}$; **DOL torque** = $1.5$ to $2.5 \times T_{fl}$.
- ✅ **Star-Delta:** Current = $\frac{1}{3} \cdot I_{st(DOL)}$; Torque = $\frac{1}{3} \cdot T_{st(DOL)}$. **Fixed factor — no adjustability.**
- ✅ **Auto-Transformer:** Current = $x^2 \cdot I_{st(DOL)}$; Torque = $x^2 \cdot T_{st(DOL)}$. **Tap-adjustable** (50%, 60%, 65%, 80%).
- ✅ **Slip-Ring Motor:** Only one that uses **rotor resistance** starter; gives **high starting torque** with **low starting current** (up to $2.5 \times T_{fl}$).
- ✅ **Soft Starter:** Thyristor-based, ramp-up voltage, provides **soft-stop** feature; modern alternative for large squirrel-cage motors.
- ✅ **VFD:** Variable Frequency Drive; provides **full speed + torque control**; most expensive but most energy-efficient.
- ✅ **IE Rule:** DOL permitted only for motors **≤ 5 kW** (subject to DISCOM norms).
- ✅ **Selection Logic:** Motor type → Rating → Load type → Tap selection → Economy.
- ✅ **High-inertia loads** (crushers, mills): Use Auto-Transformer or Slip-Ring with Rotor Resistance Starter.
- ✅ **Low-inertia loads** (pumps, fans): Use Star-Delta for economy.
- ✅ **Always include OLR** in the starter circuit — examiners mark for it.
- ✅ **Draw the timer + contactor logic** for full marks in descriptive questions.
- ✅ **Mention IE Rule / IS standard** reference for professional completeness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cable and Switchgears
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cable and Switchgears

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electrical Cables — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> An **electrical cable** is an assembly of one or more conductors (typically copper or aluminium) with their individual insulations, protective coverings, fillers, tapes, and an overall protective sheath (PVC, XLPE, or elastomeric) designed for the safe transmission and distribution of electrical energy at low voltage (LV ≤ 1.1 kV) or high voltage (HV > 1.1 kV) levels in compliance with **IS 7098**, **IS 1554**, and **IEC 60502**.

### Key Cable Constituents
1. **Conductor** — Carries the current; materials are **copper (Cu)** or **aluminium (Al)** per **IS 8130**.
2. **Conductor Screen / Stress Control Layer** — For HV cables; smooths electric field.
3. **Insulation** — **XLPE (Cross-Linked Polyethylene)**, **PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride)**, or **EPR**.
4. **Insulation Screen** — Semi-conducting layer that ensures symmetric field.
5. **Metallic Sheath / Armour** — Steel wire/tape armour for mechanical & fault-current protection.
6. **Inner Sheath (Bedding)** — PVC bedding over armoured layer.
7. **Outer Sheath** — Black PVC for UV, abrasion, and chemical resistance (ST2 grade).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Cable selection = Current Carrying Capacity (CCC) + Derating + Voltage Drop + Short Circuit Withstand + Earth Fault Loop Impedance (Zs).

## 1.2 Switchgears — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> **Switchgear** is the collective term for all devices used for **switching, controlling, protecting, isolating, and metering** electrical circuits and equipment. It encompasses **MCBs, MCCBs, ACBs, SF₆/Vacuum circuit breakers, HRC fuses, isolators, ELCBs, RCCBs, and relays** per **IS 13947 / IEC 60947** standards.

### Functional Classification
| Function | Device | Standard |
|----------|--------|----------|
| **Switching** | Switch, MCB, MCCB | IS 13947-3 |
| **Isolation** | Isolator, Switch-Disconnector | IS 13947-3 |
| **Short-Circuit Protection** | MCB, MCCB, ACB, HRC Fuse | IS 13947-2 |
| **Earth-Fault Protection** | ELCB, RCCB, RCBO | IS 12640 |
| **Overload Protection** | Thermal/Bimetallic relay | IS 13947-4 |
| **Over-Voltage Protection** | SPD (Surge Protection Device) | IEC 61643 |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Making It Real

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy 1 — Cable is like a "Water Pipe":** Imagine electricity as water flowing through a pipe. The **conductor** is the pipe's bore (bigger bore = more flow). The **insulation** is the pipe wall (prevents leaks). The **armour** is a steel jacket (protects the pipe from external damage). The **current rating** is the maximum water flow the pipe can safely carry without bursting (overheating).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy 2 — Switchgear is like a "Smart Circuit Breaker Panel at Home":** The **MCB** is the small switch in your distribution board that trips when a kettle shorts. The **MCCB** is the heavy-duty breaker in industry. The **ELCB/RCCB** is the life-saver that detects current leaking to earth (like water leaking from the pipe). The **HRC fuse** is the sacrificial wire that melts to protect downstream — like a fuse wire in old plugs.

### Critical Metrics (Industry Standards)
- **Current Density for Cu conductor:** 1.5 to 2.5 A/mm² (rubber/PVC insulated)
- **Current Density for Al conductor:** 1.0 to 1.6 A/mm²
- **Max permissible conductor temperature (XLPE):** **90 °C**
- **Max permissible conductor temperature (PVC):** **70 °C**
- **Standard ambient temperature reference:** **40 °C** (IS 3961)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cable Selection — Stepwise Logic

The KTU 2024 scheme mandates a **6-step cable selection procedure** (per **IS 732**, **IS 3961**, and the **Central Electricity Authority (CEA) Regulations 2010**):

1. **Determine the Full-Load Current (FLC):**
   - Single-phase: $I = \dfrac{P}{V \cos\phi}$
   - Three-phase: $I = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3}\, V_L \cos\phi}$

2. **Apply Diversity & Demand Factors:**
   - $I_{design} = I_{FLC} \times Demand\ Factor \times Diversity\ Factor$

3. **Select Nominal Cable Size from Current Tables** (IS 3961 / IS 1554):
   - Look up **current rating (Iz)** from manufacturer tables.
   - **Constraint:** $I_z \geq I_{design}$

4. **Apply Derating Factors (Cᵢ, Cg, Ca, Cc):**
   - $I_z' = I_z \times C_i \times C_g \times C_a \times C_c$
   - **Final Constraint:** $I_z' \geq I_{design}$

5. **Check Voltage Drop (Vd):**
   - Single-phase: $V_d = \dfrac{2\, I\, L\, (r\cos\phi + x\sin\phi)}{1000}$ (Volts)
   - Three-phase: $V_d = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}\, I\, L\, (r\cos\phi + x\sin\phi)}{1000}$ (Volts)
   - **Allowed limit (as per IS 732):** ≤ **3% of declared voltage** for lighting; ≤ **5%** for power.

6. **Verify Short-Circuit Withstand (Isc):**
   - $t = \left(\dfrac{K \cdot A}{I_{sc}}\right)^2$ seconds

## 2.2 Derating Factors — The "Why" Behind Each

| Factor | Symbol | Why Apply | Typical Range |
|--------|--------|-----------|---------------|
| **Installation Method** | Cᵢ | Cables in duct/tray/duct bank dissipate heat differently | 0.7 – 1.0 |
| **Ambient Temperature** | Ca | Higher ambient = less cooling capacity | 0.74 – 1.22 |
| **Grouping (Cables Bunched)** | Cg | Mutual heating when >1 multicore cable in common trough | 0.8 – 1.0 |
| **Thermal Insulation Contact** | Cc | Direct contact with thermal insulation reduces dissipation | 0.5 – 0.88 |
| **Soil Resistivity** | Cs (for buried cables) | Dry/sandy soil = poor heat dissipation | 0.7 – 1.1 |

## 2.3 Short-Circuit Current Calculation (Cable Withstand)

For a cable with conductor cross-section **A** (mm²), the **short-time current rating** for duration **t** seconds is given by:

$$I_{sc} = \dfrac{K \cdot A}{\sqrt{t}}$$

Where:
- **I_sc** = Short-circuit current in **Amperes**
- **K** = Material constant (depends on conductor material, insulation, and initial/final temperature)
- **A** = Conductor cross-section in **mm²**
- **t** = Duration of fault in **seconds**

**Typical K values (per IS 7098 / IEC 60364):**
- Cu conductor, PVC insulation: **K = 115**
- Cu conductor, XLPE insulation: **K = 143**
- Al conductor, PVC insulation: **K = 76**
- Al conductor, XLPE insulation: **K = 94**

## 2.4 Earth-Fault Loop Impedance (Zs)

For automatic disconnection in TN systems, the **earth-fault loop impedance** must satisfy:

$$Z_s \leq \dfrac{U_0}{I_a}$$

Where:
- **Z_s** = Earth-fault loop impedance in **ohms**
- **U₀** = Nominal line-to-earth voltage (**230 V** for single-phase, **415 V/√3** for 3-phase)
- **I_a** = Current causing automatic disconnection within **≤ 0.4 s** (final circuits ≤ 32 A) per **IS 732 / IEC 60364-41**

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Table — Memorize for ESE (End Semester Examination)**

| Sl. No. | Formula / Concept | Equation / Value | Units / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3-φ FLC | $I = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3}\, V_L \cos\phi}$ | A, kW, V |
| 2 | 1-φ FLC | $I = \dfrac{P}{V \cos\phi}$ | A, kW, V |
| 3 | Derated Current | $I_z' = I_z \times C_i \times C_g \times C_a \times C_c$ | A |
| 4 | 3-φ Voltage Drop | $V_d = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}\, I\, L\, (r\cos\phi + x\sin\phi)}{1000}$ | Volts |
| 5 | 1-φ Voltage Drop | $V_d = \dfrac{2\, I\, L\, (r\cos\phi + x\sin\phi)}{1000}$ | Volts |
| 6 | % Voltage Drop | $\% V_d = \dfrac{V_d}{V_{rated}} \times 100$ | % |
| 7 | Short-Circuit Withstand Time | $t = \left(\dfrac{K \cdot A}{I_{sc}}\right)^2$ | seconds |
| 8 | Earth-Fault Loop Limit | $Z_s \leq \dfrac{U_0}{I_a}$ | Ω |
| 9 | Prospective Short-Circuit (Isc) | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{V}{Z_{source}}$ | A |
| 10 | Breaking Capacity | $I_{bc} \geq I_{sc}$ at point of installation | kA |
| 11 | Current Density (Cu/PVC) | 1.5 – 2.5 | A/mm² |
| 12 | Current Density (Al/PVC) | 1.0 – 1.6 | A/mm² |
| 13 | Max XLPE Temp | 90 | °C |
| 14 | Max PVC Temp | 70 | °C |
| 15 | Max Vd (Lighting) | 3% of declared V | % |
| 16 | Max Vd (Power) | 5% of declared V | % |
| 17 | Adiabatic Eqn (Earth Conductor) | $A = \dfrac{I}{K} \sqrt{t}$ | mm² |
| 18 | Discrimin. Rule (Downstream) | $I_{inst}^{down} \geq 1.6 \times I_{inst}^{up}$ | A |

## 2.6 Switchgear Selection Logic

The KTU 2024 scheme specifies the following sequence:

1. **Determine prospective short-circuit current** (PSCC) at point of installation: $I_{sc} = \dfrac{V}{Z_{eq}}$
2. **Choose device Breaking Capacity (Ibc)** such that: $I_{bc} \geq I_{sc}$
3. **Choose current rating (In)** such that: $I_n \geq I_{design}$
4. **Discrimination check** (selectivity with upstream + downstream devices)
5. **Discrimination Ratio** typically 1.6 : 1 between MCB-MCB or MCCB-MCCB
6. **Check discrimination tables** (manufacturer-specific)

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Plants:** Use **11 kV / 6.6 kV XLPE HT cables** with **VCB switchgear**.
- **High-Rise Buildings:** Use **Fire-Survival (FS) cables** with **FRLS PVC** outer sheath.
- **Solar PV:** Use **DC-rated XLPE cables** with **DC MCBs/SPDs**.
- **Hospitals & IT Parks:** Use **RCCBs (30 mA / 100 mA)** for human-safety protection.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Three-Phase Voltage Drop

> [!NOTE]
> **Background:** Voltage drop is the loss of potential between source and load due to conductor impedance. It is the **single most-failed** KTU Module 3 problem.

### Step 1 — Establish Per-Phase Equivalent Circuit
Consider a balanced 3-phase line with impedance per phase: $\overline{Z} = r + jx\ \Omega/\text{km}$

The current per phase is: $\overline{I} = I\ \angle -\phi\ \text{A}$

### Step 2 — Voltage Drop Across One Phase Conductor
The voltage drop across the line impedance of length **L** km is:

$$\overline{V}_d = \overline{I} \cdot \overline{Z} \cdot L$$

Substituting: $\overline{Z} = r + jx$ and $\overline{I} = I\angle-\phi$

$$\overline{V}_d = I \angle -\phi \times (r + jx) \times L$$

### Step 3 — Resolve Into In-Phase and Quadrature Components
Multiplying out:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{V}_d &= I L \left[ (r \cos\phi + x \sin\phi) + j (x \cos\phi - r \sin\phi) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

The **magnitude of the in-phase (active) drop** is the dominant component:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_d(\text{active}) &= I L (r \cos\phi + x \sin\phi)\ \text{Volts per phase}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Line-to-Line Drop (3-Phase)
In a balanced 3-phase system, the **line-to-line voltage drop** is $\sqrt{3}$ times the per-phase in-phase drop:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_d(3\phi) &= \sqrt{3} \times I \times L \times (r \cos\phi + x \sin\phi)\ \text{Volts}
\end{aligned}
$$

The factor **1000** in the denominator is introduced when **r** is expressed in **mΩ/m** (milliohms per meter) and **L** in km, to yield the result in volts:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_d(3\phi) &= \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot (r\cos\phi + x\sin\phi)}{1000}\ \text{Volts}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Percentage Voltage Drop
The percentage drop is the ratio of voltage drop to rated voltage multiplied by 100:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% V_d &= \frac{V_d}{V_{\text{rated}}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Short-Circuit Withstand Time of Cable

> [!NOTE]
> **Background:** When a fault occurs, the cable must carry the fault current for the duration it takes the upstream protection to clear. The cable must not overheat or melt.

### Step 1 — Heat Energy Generated During Fault
The heat energy generated in a conductor carrying fault current $I_{sc}$ for time $t$ is:

$$Q_{gen} = I_{sc}^2 \cdot R_{ac} \cdot t\ \text{Joules}$$

For short durations (< 5 seconds), the resistance is approximately constant = $R_{dc}$ (DC resistance, since skin effect is negligible at fault clearing times).

### Step 2 — Heat Energy Absorbed by Conductor
The heat absorbed to raise the conductor from initial temperature $T_i$ to final temperature $T_f$:

$$Q_{abs} = m \cdot c \cdot (T_f - T_i) = \rho_m \cdot V \cdot c \cdot (T_f - T_i)$$

For a conductor of cross-section **A** and length **l**:

$$Q_{abs} = \rho_m \cdot A \cdot l \cdot c \cdot (T_f - T_i)$$

### Step 3 — Equate Generation and Absorption
Setting $Q_{gen} = Q_{abs}$ and substituting specific heat and density of copper:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{sc}^2 \cdot \frac{\rho_e \cdot l}{A} \cdot t &= \rho_m \cdot A \cdot l \cdot c \cdot (T_f - T_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Time
Rearranging:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{\rho_m \cdot c \cdot (T_f - T_i) \cdot A^2}{\rho_e \cdot I_{sc}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Consolidate Material Constants Into K
The constant **K** combines $\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_m \cdot c \cdot (T_f - T_i)}{\rho_e}}$, yielding the **standard adiabatic equation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \left(\frac{K \cdot A}{I_{sc}}\right)^2\ \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **single most important KTU formula** for short-circuit cable sizing.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Example (14-Mark Type)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz motor load of **30 kW** at **0.85 pf lagging** is fed by a 3-core **XLPE/Al cable** of length **80 m** by direct burial. The cable's per-phase resistance is **0.411 Ω/km** and reactance is **0.075 Ω/km**. Installation method factor **Cᵢ = 0.8**, grouping factor **C_g = 1.0**, ambient factor **C_a = 0.94**. Maximum allowable voltage drop is **5%**. Determine:
> 1. Full-load current
> 2. Cable size after derating
> 3. Actual voltage drop and check against limit
> 4. Short-circuit withstand time for a 25 kA fault
> 5. Earth conductor size

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Full Load Current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{FLC} &= \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi} = \frac{30{,}000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.85} \\
I_{FLC} &= \frac{30{,}000}{611.45} = 49.06\ \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Derating:**

We need $I_z' = I_z \times 0.8 \times 1.0 \times 0.94 \geq 49.06$ A

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_z &\geq \frac{49.06}{0.8 \times 0.94} = 65.24\ \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

From **IS 1554 Pt-1 Table 4** (XLPE/Al buried, 3-core): A **16 mm²** Al cable has $I_z = 68$ A. ✓

**Selected: 3-core 16 mm² Al XLPE cable**

**Step 3 — Voltage Drop Calculation:**

Given: $r = 0.411\ \Omega/\text{km}$, $x = 0.075\ \Omega/\text{km}$, $L = 0.080\ \text{km}$, $I = 49.06$ A, $\cos\phi = 0.85$, $\sin\phi = 0.527$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_d &= \frac{\sqrt{3} \times 49.06 \times 0.080 \times (0.411 \times 0.85 + 0.075 \times 0.527)}{1000} \\
&= \frac{1.732 \times 49.06 \times 0.080 \times (0.3494 + 0.0395)}{1000} \\
&= \frac{1.732 \times 49.06 \times 0.080 \times 0.3889}{1000} \\
&= \frac{1.732 \times 49.06 \times 0.0311}{1000} \\
&= \frac{2.642}{1000} = 2.642\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% V_d &= \frac{2.642}{415} \times 100 = 0.637\%\ \text{(Well within 5%)}\ \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Short-Circuit Withstand Time (Al/XLPE, K = 94):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \left(\frac{94 \times 16}{25{,}000}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{1504}{25{,}000}\right)^2 = (0.0602)^2 \\
t &= 0.00362\ \text{s} = 3.62\ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is too short → **Select next higher cable size: 25 mm² Al**

**Step 5 — Earth Conductor Size (Adiabatic, IS 3043, K = 94 for Al):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{I_{sc}}{K} \sqrt{t} = \frac{25{,}000}{94} \times \sqrt{0.5} \\
A &= 265.96 \times 0.707 = 188.0\ \text{mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

(Assuming upstream protection clears fault in **0.5 s**). Select **240 mm² Al earth strip** or **70 mm² stranded Al earth wire** based on installation method.

## 3.4 Earth-Fault Loop Impedance — Worked Check

For a 230 V, 16 A final circuit protected by a **MCB type B (In = 20 A, Iₐ = 100 A)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{s,\text{max}} &= \frac{U_0}{I_a} = \frac{230}{100} = 2.30\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

The measured $Z_s$ at the load end must be **≤ 2.30 Ω** for disconnection within **0.4 s** per **IEC 60364-41**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```


> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Interpretation:** The Mermaid block diagrams above represent the **functional architecture** of cable construction, the 6-step selection procedure mandated by KTU 2024, the switchgear protection hierarchy in a typical industrial LT panel, the discrimination principle for selective tripping, and the TN earth-fault loop. These are aligned with **IS 732**, **IS 3961**, and **IEC 60364** standards.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define derating factor. List any four derating factors used in cable sizing with their typical values.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> A **derating factor** is a multiplicative factor applied to the **tabulated current carrying capacity (Iz)** of a cable to account for non-ideal installation conditions. It reduces the effective current rating so that the cable is not overloaded.

| Derating Factor | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Installation Method | Cᵢ | 0.7 – 1.0 |
| Grouping (bunched) | Cg | 0.65 – 1.0 |
| Ambient Temperature | Ca | 0.74 – 1.22 |
| Thermal Insulation | Cc | 0.5 – 0.88 |
| Soil Resistivity (buried) | Cs | 0.7 – 1.1 |

**Final derated rating:** $I_z' = I_z \times C_i \times C_g \times C_a \times C_c \times C_s$ (any 4 multiplied).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is discrimination in switchgear coordination? Mention the standard ratio.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Discrimination (selectivity)** is the ability of a switchgear system to isolate only the **faulted section** of the installation while keeping the **healthy sections energized**. The downstream protective device must trip first, leaving the upstream device unaffected.

**Standard ratio for MCB-MCB coordination: 1.6 : 1**
(i.e., the upstream device's instantaneous trip current must be at least **1.6 times** the downstream device's instantaneous trip current.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule:** Module 3 carries an **internal choice** between Question A and Question B. Both options carry equal marks. Solve the **full question** for 14 marks (typically 7 + 7 sub-parts).

---

### **OPTION A: Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz motor feeder of **45 kW** at **0.88 pf** is supplied by a 3-core **XLPE/Cu armoured cable** of length **120 m**, run in a **cable tray along with 2 other circuits**. The cable impedance is **r = 0.277 Ω/km, x = 0.073 Ω/km**. The installation method factor is **0.9**, ambient temperature factor is **0.94**, grouping factor is **0.85**. Determine the **suitable cable size** and check the **voltage drop**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **constructional features** of an **HT XLPE cable** with a neat sketch and mention the **current rating** factors. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(a):**

**Step 1 — Calculate Full-Load Current (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{FLC} &= \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi} = \frac{45{,}000}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 0.88} \\
&= \frac{45{,}000}{609.5} = 73.83\ \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Derating to Find Required Iz (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_z \geq \frac{I_{FLC}}{C_i \times C_a \times C_g} = \frac{73.83}{0.9 \times 0.94 \times 0.85} = \frac{73.83}{0.7191} = 102.67\ \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Cable Selection (1 mark):**
From **IS 3961 Part 5 Table 9** (XLPE/Cu, 3-core, in tray): A **35 mm² Cu XLPE cable** has $I_z = 110$ A. ✓

**Selected: 3-core 35 mm² Cu XLPE cable**

**Step 4 — Voltage Drop Calculation (2 marks):**

Given: $r = 0.277\ \Omega/\text{km}$, $x = 0.073\ \Omega/\text{km}$, $L = 0.120$ km, $I = 73.83$ A, $\cos\phi = 0.88$, $\sin\phi = 0.4750$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_d &= \frac{\sqrt{3} \times 73.83 \times 0.120 \times (0.277 \times 0.88 + 0.073 \times 0.475)}{1000} \\
&= \frac{1.732 \times 73.83 \times 0.120 \times (0.2438 + 0.0347)}{1000} \\
&= \frac{1.732 \times 73.83 \times 0.120 \times 0.2785}{1000} \\
&= \frac{1.732 \times 73.83 \times 0.0334}{1000} \\
&= \frac{4.272}{1000} = 4.272\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage Voltage Drop (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% V_d &= \frac{4.272}{400} \times 100 = 1.068\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verdict:** ✓ Within **5% limit** for power circuits (IS 732). The selected cable is satisfactory.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(b):**

**HT XLPE Cable Construction (5 marks):**

A typical **11 kV / 33 kV HT XLPE cable** has the following layers (from inside out):

1. **Conductor** — Compacted circular Cu or Al, per **IS 8130**.
2. **Conductor Screen** — Semi-conductive compound (≥ 0.5 mm thick) to smooth the electric field at conductor surface.
3. **XLPE Insulation** — Cross-linked polyethylene; rated for **90 °C** continuous, **250 °C** short-circuit (5 s).
4. **Insulation Screen** — Semi-conductive layer bonded to insulation; bonded or strippable type.
5. **Metallic Sheath / Shield** — Copper tape or wire (for fault current return path); thickness ≥ 0.025 × D + 0.06 mm.
6. **Inner Sheath (Bedding)** — Extruded PVC.
7. **Armour** — Steel wire (SWA) for multi-core; steel tape (STA) for single-core.
8. **Outer Sheath** — Black PVC ST2 grade (FRLS / LSZH for special applications).

**Current Rating Factors (2 marks):**

The **current carrying capacity** of an HT XLPE cable depends on:
- Conductor material and cross-section
- Insulation thermal limit (90 °C for XLPE)
- Installation method (buried, in air, in duct)
- Ambient temperature (40 °C standard)
- Soil thermal resistivity (1.2 K·m/W standard)
- Depth of laying
- Grouping of multiple cables

The standard tables from **IS 7098 Part 2 / IEC 60502** list the ratings; derating factors Cᵢ, Ca, Cg, Cs are applied similarly to LV cables.

---

### **OPTION B: Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** A short-circuit fault of **20 kA** is expected at the receiving end of a 3-core **XLPE/Cu cable**. The upstream breaker clears the fault in **0.5 s**. Determine the **minimum cable size** to withstand the fault. Use **K = 143** for Cu/XLPE. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **working principle of an SF₆ circuit breaker** with a labelled diagram. List **two advantages** and **two disadvantages** compared to a **vacuum circuit breaker**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(a):**

**Step 1 — State Adiabatic Equation (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \left(\frac{K \cdot A}{I_{sc}}\right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Known Values (2 marks):**
- $I_{sc} = 20{,}000$ A
- $t = 0.5$ s
- $K = 143$ (Cu/XLPE per IS 7098)

**Step 3 — Solve for A (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.5 &= \left(\frac{143 \cdot A}{20{,}000}\right)^2 \\
\sqrt{0.5} &= \frac{143 \cdot A}{20{,}000} \\
0.7071 &= \frac{143 \cdot A}{20{,}000} \\
A &= \frac{0.7071 \times 20{,}000}{143} = \frac{14{,}142}{143} = 98.9\ \text{mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Select Standard Size (1 mark):**
**Select 120 mm² Cu XLPE cable** (next higher standard size from 98.9 mm²).

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(b):**

**SF₆ Circuit Breaker Working Principle (5 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> The **SF₆ (Sulphur Hexafluoride) Circuit Breaker** uses SF₆ gas as the arc-quenching and insulating medium. SF₆ is an **electronegative gas** with a dielectric strength **2.5 times** that of air and excellent arc-cooling properties.

**Operating Sequence:**

1. **Normal Condition:** SF₆ gas at **0.4 – 0.6 MPa** pressure fills the interrupter chamber. The gas provides insulation between live contacts.
2. **Fault Occurs:** Tripping command opens the main contacts.
3. **Arc Initiation:** An arc forms between the separating contacts; SF₆ gas is heated and ionized.
4. **Arc Extinction:** The arc-quenching nozzle directs high-pressure SF₆ gas axially across the arc. SF₆ molecules capture free electrons (electronegative property), de-ionizing the arc column.
5. **Current Zero:** At natural current zero, the dielectric strength of SF₆ recovers rapidly (within microseconds), preventing re-ignition.
6. **Restoration:** Contacts are fully open; gas continues to provide insulation.

**Two Advantages over VCB (1 mark):**
- Higher **breaking capacity** (suitable for EHV applications up to 800 kV).
- **Silent operation** (no moving piston noise as in VCB).

**Two Disadvantages over VCB (1 mark):**
- SF₆ gas is a **greenhouse gas** (GWP = 23,500); leaks are environmentally harmful.
- Requires **gas-pressure monitoring and refilling**; maintenance cost is higher.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Module 3:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 2** in single-phase voltage drop (writes 1-φ Vd using 3-φ formula, or vice versa). **Penalty: 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Mixing up K values** between Cu and Al; between PVC (K = 115) and XLPE (K = 143). **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Not converting L to km** when r is in Ω/km. **Penalty: 0.5–1 mark.**
> 4. **Ignoring derating factors** in the cable selection step. **Penalty: 2 marks.**
> 5. **In switchgear problems**, students often forget to check **breaking capacity** vs **short-time withstand current (Icw)** — they are distinct parameters.
> 6. **Drawing cable cross-section** without labelling the **armour and outer sheath** — examiner deducts **1 mark** for missing labels.
> 7. **Voltage drop % check** must be explicitly stated ("Within 5% limit" / "Within 3% limit") for full credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (for KTU ESE — Module 3)**

- ✅ **Cable selection** is a **6-step process**: FLC → Demand/Diversity → Table Lookup → Derating → Voltage Drop → Short-Circuit Check.
- ✅ **XLPE max temp = 90 °C**; **PVC max temp = 70 °C** — memorize both.
- ✅ **Current density rule of thumb:** **1.5 – 2.5 A/mm²** for Cu/PVC, **1.0 – 1.6 A/mm²** for Al/PVC.
- ✅ **Voltage drop limits:** **3%** for lighting circuits, **5%** for power circuits.
- ✅ **Adiabatic equation for cable withstand:** $t = (K A / I_{sc})^2$ — the **K-table** (K = 115, 143, 76, 94) is examinable.
- ✅ **Discrimination ratio** = **1.6 : 1** between MCBs/MCCBs.
- ✅ **Earth-fault loop impedance:** $Z_s \leq U_0 / I_a$ — disconnects within **0.4 s** for final circuits ≤ 32 A.
- ✅ **Switchgear hierarchy:** Grid → VCB (HT) → Transformer → ACB (LT Incomer) → MCCB (Feeders) → MCB/RCCB (Final).
- ✅ **HRC Fuse** is the **cheapest and fastest** short-circuit protection for LV back-up.
- ✅ **SF₆ breakers** are for EHV (> 66 kV); **VCB** for MV (11–33 kV); **ACB/MCCB** for LV.
- ✅ **Standard ambient temperature for cable rating:** **40 °C** (IS 3961).
- ✅ **Reference soil thermal resistivity:** **1.2 K·m/W** for buried cables.
- ✅ **Earth conductor sizing** uses the **same adiabatic equation** as the phase conductor, with different K values.
- ✅ **XLPE Cable construction order:** Conductor → Conductor Screen → XLPE → Insulation Screen → Metallic Sheath → Bedding → Armour → Outer Sheath.
- ✅ **CEA Regulation 2010** mandates that all HT installations above **650 V** must have **earth-fault protection** within **100 ms** for systems > 11 kV.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Factor Improvement - kVAR Calculation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Power Factor Improvement - kVAR Calculation ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Power Factor (PF)** is defined as the cosine of the angle between the supply voltage and the line current in an AC electrical system. Mathematically, it is the ratio of **Real Power (kW)** to **Apparent Power (kVA)**, representing how effectively the electrical power supplied by the utility is being converted into useful working power at the load end. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (per IS 732 & IE Rules):** Power Factor = $\cos \phi$ = Real Power (kW) ÷ Apparent Power (kVA). It is a dimensionless quantity bounded between **0** and **1** (lagging for inductive loads, leading for capacitive loads, unity for purely resistive loads). In a three-phase balanced system, the power factor relationships are: $$\cos\phi = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L} = \frac{\text{kW}}{\text{kVA}}$$ where: - $P$ = Active/Real Power in **kW** - $V_L$ = Line-to-Line Voltage in **V (Volts)** - $I_L$ = Line Current in **A (Amperes)** - $\phi$ = Phase angle between voltage and current --- ### 1.2 The Three Powers of an AC System | Power Type | Symbol | Unit | Physical Meaning | |------------|--------|------|------------------| | **Active (Real) Power** | P | kW | Power actually consumed doing useful work (lighting, heating, mechanical motion) | | **Reactive Power** | Q | kVAR | Power oscillating between source and load due to inductive/capacitive stored energy | | **Apparent Power** | S | kVA | The vector sum of P and Q — what the utility must actually generate | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Key Point:** Inductive loads (motors, transformers, induction furnaces, arc furnaces, fluorescent lighting with magnetic ballasts) consume **lagging reactive power**, which causes the power factor to drop below unity. --- ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Beer Mug" Intuition Imagine you order a mug of beer in a bar. The **liquid beer** (the actual content you want and pay for) is your **Active Power (kW)**. The **foam on top** (which fills up the mug but gives you nothing useful) is your **Reactive Power (kVAR)**. The **total volume of mug (beer + foam)** is your **Apparent Power (kVA)**. Your **Power Factor** is the ratio: *(Liquid Beer) ÷ (Total Mug Volume)*. - If your mug is **100% beer, 0% foam** → PF = 1.0 (Unity) → perfect - If your mug is **70% beer, 30% foam** → PF = 0.70 → you are paying for foam! **Power Factor Improvement** is exactly like asking the bartender to "give me less foam" — but in electrical terms, we install **capacitor banks** that supply the reactive power locally at the load, so it doesn't have to be transmitted through the supply cables and transformers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Improve Power Factor?** Utility companies in India (per CEA regulations and state electricity board tariffs) levy **PF penalty / kVArh penalty** if the average PF falls below **0.90–0.95 lagging**. Improving PF reduces current, reduces I²R losses, releases transformer capacity, and avoids penalties. --- ### 1.4 Why Power Factor Drops — Root Causes in Industrial Plants 1. **Induction Motors** — especially lightly loaded or oversized motors (a 10 HP motor running at no-load may draw 70% reactive current) 2. **Transformers** under no-load or light-load conditions (magnetizing current is purely reactive) 3. **Arc / Induction Furnaces** — extremely low lagging PF (0.5–0.7) 4. **Fluorescent & HID lamps** with magnetic ballasts 5. **Welding transformers** and rectifiers 6. **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** — input stage draws harmonic-rich lagging current --- ### 1.5 Methods of Power Factor Improvement | Method | Description | Typical Application | |--------|-------------|---------------------| | **Static Capacitors** (most common) | Shunt capacitor banks supply leading kVAR locally | Industrial plants, commercial buildings | | **Synchronous Condensers** | Over-excited synchronous motor running at no-load supplies leading kVAR | Large substations, HV systems | | **Phase Advancers** | Exciter mounted on induction motor shaft | Traction, large slip-ring motors | | **Active Harmonic Filters with PF correction** | Electronic compensation | Modern VFD-heavy plants | For KTU estimation/design problems, the **Static Shunt Capacitor Bank** is the dominant topic. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Power Triangle — The Heart of PF Improvement Every power factor calculation is anchored on the **Power Triangle** (also called the kVA triangle or impedance triangle when normalized). $$S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$$ $$\cos\phi = \frac{P}{S}, \quad \sin\phi = \frac{Q}{S}, \quad \tan\phi = \frac{Q}{P}$$ The angle $\phi$ is the phase displacement between voltage and current. --- ### 2.2 Logical Steps of Power Factor Improvement When we improve the power factor from $\cos\phi_1$ (original, poor PF) to $\cos\phi_2$ (target, improved PF): **Step 1 — Active Power remains constant** The real load work (kW) does not change. Only the *nature* of the current changes. $$P_1 = P_2 = P \text{ (constant)}$$ **Step 2 — Reactive Power reduces** The capacitor bank supplies part of the reactive demand, reducing the reactive power drawn from the source. $$Q_2 = Q_1 - Q_C$$ **Step 3 — Apparent Power reduces** Since S = P/cos φ and P is fixed, increasing cos φ reduces S. $$S_1 = \frac{P}{\cos\phi_1} \quad \text{vs} \quad S_2 = \frac{P}{\cos\phi_2}$$ **Step 4 — Line Current reduces** For fixed voltage: $I = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3}\,V\cos\phi}$. Lower kVA → lower I → lower I²R losses → released capacity. **Step 5 — kVAR to be supplied by capacitor** The capacitor must supply exactly the reduction in reactive power: $$Q_C = Q_1 - Q_2 = P\tan\phi_1 - P\tan\phi_2$$ $$\boxed{Q_C = P \left(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2\right) \text{ kVAR}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Trick:** Notice the formula uses **tangent**, not cosine. Students often confuse this. The reason: P is constant (adjacent side of the triangle), Q is the opposite side being cancelled, hence $\tan\phi = Q/P$. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Use Case | Units | |---|---------|----------|-------| | 1 | $\cos\phi = \frac{\text{kW}}{\text{kVA}}$ | PF from measured kW and kVA | dimensionless | | 2 | $\text{kVA} = \frac{\text{kW}}{\cos\phi}$ | Convert real power to apparent | kVA | | 3 | $\text{kVAR} = \text{kW} \times \tan\phi$ | Reactive power from real power and angle | kVAR | | 4 | $\text{kVAR} = \text{kVA} \times \sin\phi$ | Reactive power from apparent power | kVAR | | 5 | $Q_C = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$ | **Required capacitor rating** | kVAR | | 6 | $Q_C = \text{kVA}_1 \times (\sin\phi_1 - \phi_2\text{ in sine form})$ | Alternative when kW unknown | kVAR | | 7 | $I_L = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \, \cos\phi}$ | Line current before/after PF correction | A | | 8 | $\%\text{ kVA saving} = \left(1 - \dfrac{\cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2}\right) \times 100$ | Reduction in apparent power demand | % | | 9 | $C_{\Delta} = \dfrac{Q_C \times 10^3}{2\pi f \, V_L^2}$ | Capacitance per phase, **Delta** connected (V_L across each cap) | F/phase | | 10 | $C_Y = \dfrac{Q_C \times 10^3}{3 \times 2\pi f \, V_{ph}^2}$ | Capacitance per phase, **Star** connected | F/phase | | 11 | $V_{ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$ | Star-connected phase voltage | V | | 12 | PF penalty = (Target PF − Actual PF) × kVArh × Rate | Billing impact (per tariff) | ₹ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** In formulas 9 and 10, $Q_C$ is the **total** three-phase kVAR, and you must convert to VAR (multiply by $10^3$) and divide by **3** for star connection (because there are 3 capacitors each carrying line voltage for delta, phase voltage for star). Always verify whether the problem asks for *total kVAR* or *per-phase kVAR*. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Release of Transformer Capacity:** A 500 kVA transformer at 0.7 PF supplies only 350 kW. Correcting to 0.95 PF allows it to supply 475 kW — a 35% capacity gain without buying a new transformer. 2. **Reduction in Cable Size:** Lower line current permits use of smaller conductor cross-section (huge savings in industrial wiring). 3. **Reduced I²R Losses:** Losses in feeders drop by factor $(\cos\phi_1 / \cos\phi_2)^2$. Going from 0.7 → 0.95 reduces losses by ~46%. 4. **Voltage Drop Reduction:** $V_d = I(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$. Lower current → lower drop → better voltage regulation. 5. **Avoidance of Penalty:** Indian Electricity Rules and most DISCOM tariffs penalize PF < 0.90. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required kVAR of Capacitor Bank **Given:** A load consumes active power $P$ kW at lagging power factor $\cos\phi_1$. We want to improve it to $\cos\phi_2$ by adding a shunt capacitor bank. **To Derive:** The rating $Q_C$ of the capacitor bank in kVAR. --- **Step 1: Express the original reactive power** By definition of $\tan\phi$ in the power triangle: $$\tan\phi_1 = \frac{Q_1}{P} \implies Q_1 = P\tan\phi_1$$ **Step 2: Express the target reactive power** After correction: $$\tan\phi_2 = \frac{Q_2}{P} \implies Q_2 = P\tan\phi_2$$ **Step 3: The capacitor must supply the difference** Since $P$ is constant (load work is unchanged), the capacitor must locally provide exactly the *missing* reactive power: $$Q_C = Q_1 - Q_2$$ **Step 4: Substitute** $$Q_C = P\tan\phi_1 - P\tan\phi_2$$ $$\boxed{Q_C = P\left(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2\right) \quad \text{[kVAR]}}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — % Reduction in kVA Demand **Step 1: Original kVA** $$S_1 = \frac{P}{\cos\phi_1}$$ **Step 2: New kVA after correction** $$S_2 = \frac{P}{\cos\phi_2}$$ **Step 3: Percentage reduction** $$\%\text{ Reduction} = \frac{S_1 - S_2}{S_1} \times 100$$ **Step 4: Substitute and simplify** $$\%\text{ Reduction} = \frac{\dfrac{P}{\cos\phi_1} - \dfrac{P}{\cos\phi_2}}{\dfrac{P}{\cos\phi_1}} \times 100$$ $$\%\text{ Reduction} = \left(1 - \frac{\cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2}\right) \times 100$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Capacitance per Phase (Delta Connection) **Step 1: Total reactive power per phase** For a balanced 3-phase system, total reactive power $Q_C$ is shared equally across the three phases: $$Q_{C,\text{phase}} = \frac{Q_C}{3} \quad \text{[kVAR]}$$ **Step 2: Convert to VAR** $$Q_{C,\text{phase}} = \frac{Q_C}{3} \times 10^3 \quad \text{[VAR]}$$ **Step 3: For a delta-connected capacitor, voltage across each capacitor = line voltage $V_L$** The reactive power of a capacitor is: $$Q_C = \frac{V^2}{X_C} = V^2 \cdot 2\pi f C$$ **Step 4: Solve for C** $$\frac{Q_C \times 10^3}{3} = V_L^2 \cdot 2\pi f \cdot C_\Delta$$ $$C_\Delta = \frac{Q_C \times 10^3}{3 \cdot 2\pi f \cdot V_L^2} \quad \text{[Farads/phase]}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Important:** Even though $V_L$ is the line voltage, dividing by 3 is required because the *total* 3-phase kVAR $Q_C$ is shared across 3 capacitors. --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style **Problem:** A 3-phase, 400 V industrial load draws 100 A at a power factor of 0.75 lagging. Design a shunt capacitor bank to improve the power factor to 0.95 lagging.
