Find: (a) The rating of the capacitor bank in kVAR (b) The new line current (c) The percentage reduction in kVA (d) The capacitance per phase if connected in delta (assume $f$ = 50 Hz) --- **Solution:** **Step 1: Compute original kW** $$P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi_1$$ $$P = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 100 \times 0.75$$ $$P = 51{,}961.5 \text{ W} \approx 51.96 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 2: Compute the angles** $$\phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.75) = 41.41^\circ \implies \tan\phi_1 = \tan(41.41^\circ) = 0.8819$$ $$\phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.95) = 18.19^\circ \implies \tan\phi_2 = \tan(18.19^\circ) = 0.3287$$ **Step 3: Required capacitor rating** $$Q_C = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$$ $$Q_C = 51.96 \times (0.8819 - 0.3287)$$ $$Q_C = 51.96 \times 0.5532$$ $$\boxed{Q_C = 28.74 \text{ kVAR}}$$ **Step 4: New line current** $$I_2 = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi_2} = \frac{51.96 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 0.95}$$ $$I_2 = \frac{51961.5}{658.18} = 78.95 \text{ A}$$ $$\boxed{I_2 \approx 79 \text{ A (reduced from 100 A)}}$$ **Step 5: Percentage reduction in kVA** $$\%\text{ kVA saving} = \left(1 - \frac{\cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2}\right) \times 100$$ $$= \left(1 - \frac{0.75}{0.95}\right) \times 100$$ $$= (1 - 0.7895) \times 100 = 21.05\%$$ **Step 6: Capacitance per phase (delta connection)** $$C_\Delta = \frac{Q_C \times 10^3}{3 \cdot 2\pi f \cdot V_L^2}$$ $$C_\Delta = \frac{28.74 \times 10^3}{3 \times 2\pi \times 50 \times 400^2}$$ $$C_\Delta = \frac{28740}{3 \times 314.159 \times 160000}$$ $$C_\Delta = \frac{28740}{150{,}796{,}447}$$ $$\boxed{C_\Delta \approx 190.6 \text{ μF/phase}}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Shortcut:** Students often compute $C$ as $\frac{Q_C \times 10^3}{2\pi f V^2}$ *without* dividing by 3 — this is a very common error costing 2 marks. Always remember: **Total 3-phase kVAR ÷ 3 = per-phase kVAR**. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flowchart Logic | Condition | Action | |-----------|--------| | kW given + PF given | Use $Q_C = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$ | | kVA given + PF given | Use $Q_C = \text{kVA}_1 \times (\sin\phi_1 - \sin\phi_2)$ | | Line current + PF given | First compute kW, then proceed as above | | Star connection demanded | Use $V_{ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$ in capacitance formula | | Delta connection demanded | Use $V_L$ directly, divide total kVAR by 3 | | Need I²R loss reduction | Compute $(\cos\phi_1 / \cos\phi_2)^2$ percentage | | Capacitor kVAR rating to be split | Standard sizes: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 50 kVAR — round up and use switching stages | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Power Factor Correction System The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete signal/power flow architecture of an industrial PF correction installation — from load sensing to capacitor switching. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Three Phase AC Supply
11 kV or 415 V Bus] --> B[Main HT/LT Breaker
with CT and PT] B --> C[Distribution Bus
Three Phase 4 Wire] C --> D[Industrial Load Bank
Motors, Transformers, Furnaces] C --> E[APFC Control Unit
Microprocessor Based] E --> F1[Capacitor Stage 1
5 kVAR] E --> F2[Capacitor Stage 2
10 kVAR] E --> F3[Capacitor Stage 3
20 kVAR] E --> F4[Capacitor Stage 4
50 kVAR] E --> F5[Capacitor Stage 5
100 kVAR] E --> G[Contactor Bank
One per stage] G --> H[Shunt Capacitor Bank
Total Qc kVAR] H --> C E --> I[Display Panel
PF, kVAr, kWh] E --> J[Alarm Output
Under/Over PF] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The APFC (Automatic Power Factor Controller) monitors the bus PF via PT/CT inputs. When the bus PF drops below the target setpoint (e.g., 0.95), it switches in capacitor stages. When PF rises above 1.0 (leading), it switches out stages to prevent over-correction and resonance. --- ### 4.2 Power Triangle Transformation Diagram (Before vs After PF Correction) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BEFORE["BEFORE PF Correction - PF 0.75 lagging"] B1[kW P Constant
Real Power] --> B2[Power Triangle] B3[kVAR Q1
Large Reactive Power] --> B2 B2 --> B4[kVA S1
Large Apparent Power] B5[Phi1 angle 41.41 deg] --> B2 end subgraph CAPACITOR["SHUNT CAPACITOR BANK"] C1[kVAr Qc
Local Supply] --> CAPNODE[Compensation] end subgraph AFTER["AFTER PF Correction - PF 0.95 lagging"] CAPNODE --> A2 A1[kW P Same
Unchanged] --> A2[Power Triangle] A3[kVAR Q2 Reduced
Remaining Demand] --> A2 A2 --> A4[kVA S2 Reduced
Smaller Apparent] A5[Phi2 angle 18.19 deg] --> A2 end ``` **Interpretation:** Notice that **P (horizontal axis) is identical** before and after correction. The capacitor does **not** supply any real power — only reactive (kVAR). The kVAR component shrinks, so the kVA (hypotenuse) shrinks, and the angle $\phi$ becomes smaller. --- ### 4.3 Capacitor Connection Topologies (Block Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Three Phase Bus
R Y B] --> B{Connection Type} B -->|Delta Connection| C1[Cap between R-Y] B -->|Delta Connection| C2[Cap between Y-B] B -->|Delta Connection| C3[Cap between B-R] B -->|Star Connection| D1[Cap between R-N] B -->|Star Connection| D2[Cap between Y-N] B -->|Star Connection| D3[Cap between B-N] C1 & C2 & C3 --> E[Total 3-Phase kVAR
V across each cap = VL] D1 & D2 & D3 --> F[Total 3-Phase kVAR
V across each cap = VL divided by sqrt 3] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Design Rule (KTU):** Delta connection is preferred for LV PF correction because each capacitor handles only line voltage and the per-phase capacitance required is smaller. Star connection is preferred at HV levels (11 kV / 33 kV) because the capacitor voltage rating is $\sqrt{3}$ times lower. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition) #### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define power factor. Why is power factor improvement necessary in industrial installations?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Power factor is the cosine of the angle between supply voltage and line current in an AC circuit. It equals the ratio of real power (kW) to apparent power (kVA). $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P}{S}$. **Necessity (1 mark):** Power factor improvement is necessary to (i) reduce line current and I²R losses, (ii) release transformer and feeder capacity, (iii) improve voltage regulation, and (iv) avoid penalty levied by electricity boards when PF falls below 0.90–0.95. --- #### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **A factory load is 500 kW at 0.7 lagging power factor. Find the kVAR rating of the capacitor bank required to improve the PF to 0.9 lagging.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Given: $P = 500$ kW, $\cos\phi_1 = 0.7$, $\cos\phi_2 = 0.9$ $$\tan\phi_1 = \tan(\cos^{-1}0.7) = 1.020$$ $$\tan\phi_2 = \tan(\cos^{-1}0.9) = 0.484$$ $$Q_C = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2) = 500 \times (1.020 - 0.484)$$ $$Q_C = 500 \times 0.536 = \boxed{268 \text{ kVAR}}$$ --- ### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024] **(a)** A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz induction motor load draws 80 kW at 0.78 lagging power factor. Calculate the kVAR rating of the shunt capacitor bank to improve the PF to 0.95 lagging. Also find the new line current and percentage reduction in kVA demand. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the working of an Automatic Power Factor Controller (APFC) used in industrial capacitor banks. List any four advantages of power factor improvement. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Question A(a) (7 marks):** **Step 1: Identify given data** $P = 80$ kW, $V_L = 400$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $\cos\phi_1 = 0.78$, $\cos\phi_2 = 0.95$ **Step 2: Find angles** $$\phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.78) = 38.79^\circ, \quad \tan\phi_1 = 0.8023$$ $$\phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.95) = 18.19^\circ, \quad \tan\phi_2 = 0.3287$$ **Step 3: Required kVAR of capacitor bank** (2 marks) $$Q_C = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2) = 80 \times (0.8023 - 0.3287)$$ $$Q_C = 80 \times 0.4736 = \boxed{37.89 \text{ kVAR}}$$ **Step 4: New line current** (3 marks) $$I_1 = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} V_L \cos\phi_1} = \frac{80 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 0.78} = \frac{80000}{540.42} = 148.05 \text{ A}$$ $$I_2 = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} V_L \cos\phi_2} = \frac{80000}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 0.95} = \frac{80000}{658.18} = 121.55 \text{ A}$$ $$\boxed{I_2 \approx 121.55 \text{ A}}$$ **Step 5: Percentage reduction in kVA** (2 marks) $$\%\text{ Reduction} = \left(1 - \frac{\cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{0.78}{0.95}\right) \times 100$$ $$= (1 - 0.8211) \times 100 = 17.89\%$$ $$\boxed{\text{Reduction} \approx 17.89\%}$$ --- #### **Model Solution — Question A(b) (7 marks):** **Working of APFC (4 marks):** An **Automatic Power Factor Controller (APFC)** is a microprocessor-based relay that continuously monitors the power factor of the bus through current transformers (CT) and potential transformers (PT). The operating principle is: 1. The CT senses line current and PT senses bus voltage; the controller computes the instantaneous PF using $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3} V I}$. 2. If the measured PF is **below** the target setpoint (e.g., 0.95 lag), the APFC switches **in** capacitor stages sequentially through contactors until PF returns to target. 3. If PF goes **leading** (over-correction), the controller switches **out** stages to prevent leading PF and possible resonance. 4. The switching uses a time delay (typically 30–120 seconds) to avoid hunting (rapid in-out switching due to momentary load changes). **Block Diagram:** [Refer to Section 4.1 above] **Advantages of PF Improvement (3 marks — any four):** 1. **Reduction in line current** → smaller cable size and lower copper cost 2. **Reduced I²R losses** → improved system efficiency 3. **Released transformer capacity** → avoids transformer upgradation 4. **Improved voltage regulation** → reduced voltage drop in feeders 5. **Avoidance of PF penalty** → reduced electricity bill 6. **Reduced kVA demand charges** → lower maximum demand tariff --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024] **(a)** A 3-phase, 11 kV industrial consumer has a maximum demand of 800 kVA at an average power factor of 0.72 lagging. The supply authority offers a tariff incentive to improve the power factor to 0.95 lagging. Calculate: (i) The kVAR rating of the capacitor bank required. (ii) The new kVA demand after correction. (iii) The annual saving in maximum demand charges if the demand charge is ₹350 per kVA per month. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare star and delta connection of capacitor banks for 3-phase PF correction. In which situation is each preferred? Mention any two applications of synchronous condensers. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Question B(a) (7 marks):** **Step 1: Given data** $S_1 = 800$ kVA, $\cos\phi_1 = 0.72$, $\cos\phi_2 = 0.95$, Tariff = ₹350/kVA/month **Step 2: Compute kW (real power remains constant)** $$P = S_1 \cos\phi_1 = 800 \times 0.72 = 576 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3: Compute angles** $$\phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.72) = 43.95^\circ, \quad \tan\phi_1 = 0.9638$$ $$\phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.95) = 18.19^\circ, \quad \tan\phi_2 = 0.3287$$ **Step 4: Capacitor bank kVAR** (3 marks) $$Q_C = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2) = 576 \times (0.9638 - 0.3287)$$ $$Q_C = 576 \times 0.6351 = \boxed{365.82 \text{ kVAR}}$$ **Step 5: New kVA demand** (2 marks) $$S_2 = \frac{P}{\cos\phi_2} = \frac{576}{0.95} = 606.32 \text{ kVA}$$ $$\boxed{S_2 \approx 606.32 \text{ kVA}}$$ **Step 6: Monthly and annual savings** (2 marks) $$\text{kVA reduction} = 800 - 606.32 = 193.68 \text{ kVA}$$ $$\text{Monthly saving} = 193.68 \times 350 = \text{₹}67{,}788$$ $$\text{Annual saving} = 67{,}788 \times 12 = \boxed{\text{₹}8{,}13{,}456}$$ --- #### **Model Solution — Question B(b) (7 marks):** **Comparison Table (5 marks):** | Parameter | Star Connection | Delta Connection | |-----------|----------------|------------------| | Voltage across each capacitor | $V_L / \sqrt{3}$ | $V_L$ | | Current through each capacitor | $I_L / \sqrt{3}$ | $I_L / \sqrt{3}$ | | Total kVAR for same $C$ | $C V_L^2 / 3$ | $3 C V_L^2$ — i.e., **3× more kVAR** for same C | | Required capacitance per phase | Larger (3×) | Smaller (1×) | | Preferred application | **HV systems (11 kV, 33 kV)** — reduces capacitor voltage rating | **LV systems (400 V, 415 V)** — preferred for industrial LT plants | | Neutral availability | Yes (4-wire system possible) | No | **When to prefer each (1 mark):** - **Star:** when capacitor voltage rating is limited and HV operation is needed. - **Delta:** when LV PF correction is needed and maximum kVAR is required from a given capacitance. **Synchronous Condenser Applications (1 mark — any two):** 1. Large substations and EHV grids where static capacitor banks cannot meet dynamic reactive requirements. 2. Industries with rapidly fluctuating loads (arc furnaces, rolling mills) needing **continuous** and **smooth** reactive power control. 3. Long EHV transmission lines for voltage regulation and reactive support. --- ### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes costing 2–3 marks each in KTU Valuation:** > 1. **Using `cos` instead of `tan` in $Q_C = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$** — Read the formula carefully; it is *tangent*, not cosine. > 2. **Forgetting to divide by 3** when computing per-phase capacitance from total 3-phase kVAR. > 3. **Using $V_L$ instead of $V_{ph} = V_L/\sqrt{3}$** for star-connected capacitors. > 4. **Mixing up kVA reduction with kW reduction** — real power is *never* reduced by PF correction. > 5. **Not specifying units** (kVAR vs VAR) — KTU valuation penalizes missing units. > 6. **Rounding errors from $\cos^{-1}$** — use exact trig values or tables; do not round early. --- ### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Power Factor Improvement / kVAR Calculation** > > ✅ **PF = cos φ = kW / kVA** (always between 0 and 1) > > ✅ **Power Triangle:** $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$; P constant, Q reduced by capacitor > > ✅ **Master Formula:** $Q_C = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$ kVAR > > ✅ **Alternative Formula:** $Q_C = \text{kVA}_1 \times (\sin\phi_1 - \sin\phi_2)$ kVAR > > ✅ **% kVA saving:** $(1 - \cos\phi_1/\cos\phi_2) \times 100$ > > ✅ **% I²R loss reduction:** $(1 - (\cos\phi_1/\cos\phi_2)^2) \times 100$ > > ✅ **New line current:** $I_2 = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3} V_L \cos\phi_2}$ > > ✅ **Delta capacitance per phase:** $C_\Delta = \dfrac{Q_C \times 10^3}{3 \cdot 2\pi f V_L^2}$ > > ✅ **Star capacitance per phase:** $C_Y = \dfrac{Q_C \times 10^3}{3 \cdot 2\pi f (V_L/\sqrt{3})^2}$ > > ✅ **Standard capacitor sizes:** 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 50, 75, 100 kVAR — round up > > ✅ **PF Target per Indian tariff:** ≥ 0.90 to 0.95 lagging (DISCOM-specific) > > ✅ **Penalty clause:** Penalty per kVArh when actual PF < target PF > > ✅ **Methods of PF improvement:** Static capacitors (most common), synchronous condensers, phase advancers, active filters > > ✅ **Capacitor supplies leading kVAR; load consumes lagging kVAR — net effect is cancellation** > > ✅ **Always mention:** delta vs star connection, voltage rating, frequency, switching stages

topics.title: Correction Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Correction Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Definition of Correction Methods

In the context of **Electrical System Design and Estimation**, **Correction Methods** refer to the systematic application of mathematical **derating (or uprating) factors** to the *base current carrying capacity* of electrical conductors, cables, and equipment so that the design remains safe, reliable, and compliant with **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS / IS)** under *actual site installation conditions* that differ from the standard reference conditions assumed by the cable manufacturer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCEET602 – Module 3):**
> *"Correction factors are multipliers applied to the tabulated current rating of cables to account for deviations from standard reference conditions such as ambient temperature, depth of laying, thermal resistivity of soil, grouping of circuits, and method of installation."*

In simpler words — when a manufacturer tests a cable in a *laboratory*, they do so under **standard conditions** (usually **30 °C ambient air temperature** for IS 3961 Part II or **40 °C** for motors as per IS 325). But on an actual **KTU project site** in Kerala, conditions are rarely standard. Therefore, we must *correct* the rating before sizing.

## 1.2 Standard Reference Conditions (As per IS / IEC)

The reference conditions assumed when we open any cable current-rating table are:

| Parameter | Standard Reference Value | Governing Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Ambient Air Temperature | **30 °C** | IS 3961 (Part II) |
| Ground Temperature | **30 °C** | IS 3961 (Part II) |
| Thermal Resistivity of Soil | **150 °C·cm/W** | IS 3961 (Part II) |
| Depth of Laying (UG cable) | **0.5 m to 1.0 m** | IS 1255 |
| Installation Method | Single cable in free air | IS 3961 |
| Frequency | **50 Hz** | Indian Grid |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The T-Shirt Size Problem"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — Plain English Analogy**
> Imagine buying a cotton T-shirt labelled *"Fits perfectly in 25 °C room"*. On a hot Kerala summer afternoon (38 °C with 90 % humidity), the same shirt feels tighter, uncomfortable, and your body overheats. The cotton fibre cannot dissipate your body heat fast enough.
>
> A cable behaves similarly: under hotter conditions, its insulation cannot dissipate the I²R heat efficiently, so its safe current-carrying limit **drops**. The "correction factor" is simply a *shrinkage multiplier* telling us how much smaller the allowed current must become to keep the cable "comfortable and safe" in the real installation environment.

## 1.4 Types of Corrections Covered in KTU Module 3

The five correction methods typically tested in KTU university examinations are:

1. **Correction for Ambient / Ground Temperature** ($K_1$)
2. **Correction for Depth of Laying** ($K_2$) — underground cables
3. **Correction for Thermal Resistivity of Soil** ($K_3$) — underground cables
4. **Correction for Grouping of Cables / Circuits** ($K_4$)
5. **Correction for Method of Installation** ($K_5$) — open, conduit, tray, etc.

## 1.5 Master Equation

$$
I_{\text{derated}} \;=\; I_{\text{tab}} \;\times\; K_1 \;\times\; K_2 \;\times\; K_3 \;\times\; K_4 \;\times\; K_5
$$

where $I_{\text{tab}}$ is the *tabulated* current from the cable chart.

> [!TIP]
> A correction factor **< 1 means derating** (reduce current), and **> 1 means uprating** (rare, but seen in cold climates or grouped cables spread far apart).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Physical Reasoning — Why Are Corrections Necessary?

A cable's insulation (PVC, XLPE, EPR) has a maximum **continuous operating temperature**:

| Insulation Type | Max Conductor Temp |
|---|---|
| PVC | **70 °C** |
| XLPE | **90 °C** |
| EPR | **90 °C** |
| Mineral (MICC) | **95 °C – 250 °C** |

The steady-state heat-balance equation for a buried cable is:

$$
I^2 R \;+\; W_d \;=\; (T_c - T_a) \cdot \left[\, R_{\text{th,air}} \;+\; R_{\text{th,soil}} \,\right]^{-1}
$$

- $W_d$ = dielectric loss (negligible for LV cables)
- $T_c$ = max conductor temperature
- $T_a$ = ambient or ground temperature
- $R_{\text{th}}$ = thermal resistance of surrounding medium

If $(T_c - T_a)$ **shrinks** (hotter ambient) or $R_{\text{th}}$ **rises** (dry sandy soil, deeper burial, multiple cables huddled together), then $I$ must drop to keep the equation balanced. Hence the **derating factor**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### Core Master Formula

$$
\boxed{\, I_{\text{design}} \;=\; \dfrac{I_{\text{load}}}{\eta \cdot \cos\phi \cdot K_s \cdot K_1 \cdot K_2 \cdot K_3 \cdot K_4} \,}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $I_{\text{load}}$ | Actual load current | A |
| $\eta$ | Efficiency of load | — |
| $\cos\phi$ | Power factor | — |
| $K_s$ | Safety factor (1.25 – 1.5) | — |
| $K_1$ | Temperature correction | — |
| $K_2$ | Depth-of-laying correction | — |
| $K_3$ | Soil thermal-resistivity correction | — |
| $K_4$ | Grouping correction | — |

### Correction Factor Reference Table (Most-Asked in KTU)

**Table A — Temperature Correction Factor $K_1$ (PVC, XLPE — 70 / 90 °C)**

| Ambient / Ground Temp (°C) | 20 | 25 | 30 | 35 | 40 | 45 | 50 | 55 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| PVC (70 °C) | 1.32 | 1.25 | 1.00 | 0.91 | 0.82 | 0.71 | 0.58 | 0.41 |
| XLPE (90 °C) | 1.15 | 1.10 | 1.05 | 1.00 | 0.96 | 0.91 | 0.87 | 0.82 |

**Table B — Depth-of-Laying Correction $K_2$ (for cables directly buried)**

| Depth (m) | Single-core | 3-core (up to 16 mm²) | 3-core (>25 mm²) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.50 | 1.00 | 1.00 | 1.00 |
| 0.75 | 0.97 | 0.98 | 0.99 |
| 1.00 | 0.95 | 0.96 | 0.97 |
| 1.25 | 0.93 | 0.94 | 0.96 |
| 1.50 | 0.91 | 0.92 | 0.94 |

**Table C — Soil Thermal Resistivity Correction $K_3$**

| Soil Thermal Resistivity $g$ (°C·cm/W) | 100 | 120 | 150 | 200 | 250 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $K_3$ (direct buried) | 1.10 | 1.05 | 1.00 | 0.92 | 0.86 |

**Table D — Grouping Correction $K_4$ (touching trefoil / flat formation)**

| No. of Circuits | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 8 | 9 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $K_4$ | 0.80 | 0.70 | 0.65 | 0.60 | 0.57 | 0.52 | 0.50 |

## 2.3 Boundary Conditions & When to Apply Each

- $K_1$: Apply **always** — both for air-laid and buried cables.
- $K_2$: Apply **only** when cable is **directly buried** in ground.
- $K_3$: Apply **only** when soil resistivity ≠ **150 °C·cm/W** (the standard).
- $K_4$: Apply when **≥ 2 circuits** share a common conduit, tray, or trench with **no spacing**.
- $K_5$: Apply when installation method differs from "single cable in free air".

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students multiply correction factors *and* safety factor together *twice*. Safety factor $K_s$ is separate from environmental derating. **Never confuse $K_s$ with $K_1$–$K_4$.**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Correction methods are the silent backbone of every **KSEB (Kerala State Electricity Board) service connection estimate**, every **KTDA industrial feeder design**, and every **IS-standard LT panel schedule**. Without these factors, an incorrectly sized cable in a coastal Kerala cement plant (ambient 45 °C, soil resistivity 200) would overheat within weeks of commissioning — leading to insulation failure, earth faults, and fire hazards.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Temperature Correction Factor

**Starting Point — Steady-State Heat Dissipation:**

For a cable in a thermally stable environment, heat generated = heat dissipated.

$$
I^2 R \;=\; \dfrac{(T_c - T_a)}{R_{\text{th}}}
$$

**Step 1 — Define Standard Current:**

At reference temperature $T_{a,\text{ref}}$ (30 °C) and reference thermal resistance $R_{\text{th,ref}}$:

$$
I_{\text{tab}}^2 R \;=\; \dfrac{T_c - T_{a,\text{ref}}}{R_{\text{th,ref}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Define Actual Current:**

At actual ambient $T_a$ and same thermal resistance (we're isolating temperature effect):

$$
I_{\text{derated}}^2 R \;=\; \dfrac{T_c - T_a}{R_{\text{th,ref}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Divide (2) by (1):**

$$
\dfrac{I_{\text{derated}}^2}{I_{\text{tab}}^2} \;=\; \dfrac{T_c - T_a}{T_c - T_{a,\text{ref}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Correction Factor $K_1$:**

$$
\boxed{\, K_1 \;=\; \dfrac{I_{\text{derated}}}{I_{\text{tab}}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{T_c - T_a}{T_c - T_{a,\text{ref}}}} \,}
$$

This is the *exact* theoretical form. IS tabulated values are simplified rounded versions of the same physics.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 (KTU Standard 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz induction motor draws **80 A** full-load current with efficiency **0.88** and power factor **0.85 lagging**. The motor is to be supplied by an XLPE-insulated, 4-core aluminium-armoured cable laid on a perforated cable tray along with **3 other identical circuits**, in an ambient temperature of **45 °C**. The depth of laying is **not applicable** (cable is in air). Standard conditions are 30 °C. Find the minimum cable size from the table below.

| Cable Size (mm²) | Tabulated Current in Free Air (A) |
|---|---|
| 25 | 84 |
| 35 | 100 |
| 50 | 120 |
| 70 | 150 |

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate Load Current Requirement:**

$$
I_{\text{load}} \;=\; \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V \cdot \eta \cdot \cos\phi}
$$

But here the FLC is *given directly* = 80 A.

**Step 2 — Apply Safety Factor $K_s$:**

$$
I_{\text{design,base}} \;=\; 80 \times 1.25 \;=\; 100 \; \text{A}
$$

**Step 3 — Read Correction Factors from Tables:**

- $K_1$ (Temperature, XLPE at 45 °C, standard 30 °C) → from Table A → **0.91**
- $K_2$ → not applicable (cable in air) → **1.00**
- $K_3$ → not applicable (cable in air) → **1.00**
- $K_4$ (4 circuits in tray) → from Table D → **0.65**

**Step 4 — Required Derated Cable Current Capacity:**

$$
I_{\text{required,derated}} \;=\; \dfrac{I_{\text{design,base}}}{K_1 \cdot K_4}
$$

$$
I_{\text{required,derated}} \;=\; \dfrac{100}{0.91 \times 0.65} \;=\; \dfrac{100}{0.5915} \;=\; 169.05 \; \text{A}
$$

**Step 5 — Select Cable Size:**

From the table, the smallest cable whose derated rating ≥ 169.05 A:
- 50 mm² → 120 A ❌
- 70 mm² → 150 A ❌
- 95 mm² (interpolated, ≈ 180 A) ✅

**Answer: Use 95 mm² XLPE cable** (or jump to next standard size if 95 mm² not listed).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Underground Cable with All Corrections

**Problem:** A 3-core, 11 kV, 70 mm² XLPE cable has a tabulated current rating of **220 A** under standard conditions. It is to be directly buried in soil at a depth of **1.0 m**, the soil thermal resistivity is **200 °C·cm/W**, ambient soil temperature is **35 °C**, and the trench carries **3 such cables side-by-side**. Find the derated current.

**Step 1 — Apply $K_1$ (Temperature 35 °C, XLPE 90 °C):**

From Table A → $K_1 = 1.00$

**Step 2 — Apply $K_2$ (Depth 1.0 m, 3-core > 25 mm²):**

From Table B → $K_2 = 0.97$

**Step 3 — Apply $K_3$ (Soil resistivity 200):**

From Table C → $K_3 = 0.92$

**Step 4 — Apply $K_4$ (Grouping 3 cables):**

From Table D → $K_4 = 0.70$

**Step 5 — Final Derated Current:**

$$
I_{\text{derated}} \;=\; 220 \times 1.00 \times 0.97 \times 0.92 \times 0.70
$$

$$
I_{\text{derated}} \;=\; 220 \times 0.6246 \;=\; 137.42 \; \text{A}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A cable rated 220 A in lab can only deliver **137.42 A** on-site — a **37 % devaluation** purely due to installation conditions. This is why correction methods are non-negotiable in any KTU electrical estimation problem.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Map

| If the cable is … | Apply these corrections |
|---|---|
| Laid in **free air** (overhead / on tray) | $K_1, K_4$ |
| Laid in **conduit / trunking** | $K_1, K_4, K_5$ |
| **Directly buried** in ground | $K_1, K_2, K_3, K_4$ |
| Inside a **building / switch-room** | $K_1, K_5$ |
| **Grouped in trench** + deeply buried | $K_1, K_2, K_3, K_4$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Cable Sizing with Correction Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Load Requirement] --> B[Determine Load Current I_load]
    B --> C[Apply Safety Factor Ks]
    C --> D[Identify Installation Type]
    D --> D1{Laid in Air?}
    D1 -- Yes --> E1[Read Tabulated Current I_tab]
    D1 -- No --> E2[Directly Buried in Ground]
    E2 --> E1
    E1 --> F[Lookup Correction Factors K1 to K5]
    F --> F1[K1: Ambient Temperature]
    F --> F2[K2: Depth of Laying]
    F --> F3[K3: Soil Thermal Resistivity]
    F --> F4[K4: Grouping of Cables]
    F --> F5[K5: Method of Installation]
    F1 --> G[Compute Required Derated Capacity]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    G --> G1[Formula: I_req = I_design_base divided by K1 x K2 x K3 x K4]
    G1 --> H[Compare with Tabulated Values]
    H --> H1{I_tab_derated >= I_req?}
    H1 -- Yes --> I[Select Smallest Standard Cable Size]
    H1 -- No --> J[Move to Next Higher Standard Size]
    J --> H
    I --> K[Verify Voltage Drop and Short Circuit Withstand]
    K --> L[Finalize Cable Specification]
    L --> M[End]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Heat Balance Behind Correction Factors

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Conductor I2R Loss] --> C[Heat Balance Equation]
    B[Dielectric Loss Wd] --> C
    D[Insulation R_th_insulation] --> C
    E[Sheath and Armour R_th_metal] --> C
    F[Surrounding Medium R_th_air or R_th_soil] --> C
    G[Ambient Temperature T_a] --> C
    C --> H[Conductor Temperature T_c]
    H --> I{Within Insulation Limit?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Safe Operation]
    I -- No --> K[Derating Required]
    K --> L[Apply K1 K2 K3 K4 K5]
    L --> M[Reduced I_design]
    M --> A
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Tree — Hierarchy of Correction Factors

```mermaid
graph TD
    CF[Correction Factors in Cable Sizing] --> CF1[K1 Temperature]
    CF --> CF2[K2 Depth of Laying]
    CF --> CF3[K3 Soil Thermal Resistivity]
    CF --> CF4[K4 Grouping]
    CF --> CF5[K5 Installation Method]
    CF1 --> CF1a[Air Temperature for Air Laid Cables]
    CF1 --> CF1b[Ground Temperature for Buried Cables]
    CF2 --> CF2a[Direct Buried Cables Only]
    CF3 --> CF3a[Moist Clay 100]
    CF3 --> CF3b[Sandy Dry Soil 250]
    CF4 --> CF4a[Circuits in Conduit]
    CF4 --> CF4b[Cables in Tray or Trench]
    CF5 --> CF5a[Surface Clipped]
    CF5 --> CF5b[Embedded in Wall]
    CF5 --> CF5c[In Duct or Pipe]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Typical KTU Exam Question Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[KTU Exam Question on Correction] --> Q1[Given: Load, Voltage, PF, Efficiency]
    Q --> Q2[Given: Ambient Temp, Depth, Soil, Grouping]
    Q1 --> S[Step 1 Compute Load Current]
    Q2 --> T[Step 2 Pick Correction Factors from Tables]
    S --> U[Step 3 Apply Formula]
    T --> U
    U --> V[Step 4 Select Cable Size]
    V --> W[Step 5 State Final Answer with Justification]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

*Define correction factor in the context of cable sizing. List any **four** correction factors used while estimating the current carrying capacity of an underground cable.*

**Model Answer:**

A **correction factor** is a numerical multiplier applied to the *tabulated current rating* of a cable to account for the deviation between **actual site installation conditions** and the **standard reference conditions** assumed in the manufacturer's catalogue.

The four correction factors used for underground cables are:

1. **$K_1$ — Ambient / Ground Temperature Correction**
2. **$K_2$ — Depth of Laying Correction**
3. **$K_3$ — Thermal Resistivity of Soil Correction**
4. **$K_4$ — Grouping of Cables Correction**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

*A PVC-insulated cable rated 100 A at a standard ambient of 30 °C is to be installed where the ambient is 45 °C. Compute the temperature correction factor and the new derated current.*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify Temperature Difference:**

$$
\Delta T \;=\; 45 - 30 \;=\; 15 \;^{\circ}\text{C above standard}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Standard IS Correction:**

From Table A (PVC 70 °C at 45 °C) → $K_1 = 0.71$

**Step 3 — Compute Derated Current:**

$$
I_{\text{derated}} \;=\; 100 \times 0.71 \;=\; 71 \; \text{A}
$$

**Answer:** $K_1 = 0.71$, new current rating = **71 A**.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the various correction factors that must be applied while estimating the size of an underground power cable. For each factor, state the **physical reason** for applying it.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 3-phase, 11 kV feeder is to be supplied through a 3-core, 120 mm² XLPE armoured cable. The load current is **150 A**, depth of laying is **1.0 m**, ambient soil temperature is **40 °C**, thermal resistivity of soil is **200 °C·cm/W**, and the trench carries **3 cables** touching each other. The tabulated current rating of the cable at standard conditions is **285 A**. Determine the actual current rating. Justify whether the selected cable is adequate. Standard conditions: 30 °C, depth 0.5 m, soil resistivity 150 °C·cm/W.

**Model Solution (a):**

**Factor 1 — $K_1$ Temperature Correction:**
- **Physical reason:** A hotter ambient raises the baseline temperature of the insulation, leaving less thermal headroom for $I^2R$ heating. To keep conductor temperature below the **insulation limit** (90 °C for XLPE), the current must drop.
- **Formula:** $K_1 = \sqrt{\dfrac{T_c - T_a}{T_c - T_{a,\text{ref}}}}$

**Factor 2 — $K_2$ Depth of Laying:**
- **Physical reason:** Deeper burial increases the **thermal path length** to the surface. Heat dissipation becomes harder, so current must drop.
- **Cables at 1.5 m depth typically see $K_2 \approx 0.91$ to 0.94.**

**Factor 3 — $K_3$ Soil Thermal Resistivity:**
- **Physical reason:** Dry sandy soil has high thermal resistivity (≈ 250 °C·cm/W) and dissipates heat poorly, while moist clay has low resistivity (≈ 100 °C·cm/W) and dissipates heat efficiently.
- **Direct-buried cables in dry soil need up to 14 % derating.**

**Factor 4 — $K_4$ Grouping:**
- **Physical reason:** When multiple cables run in the same trench, each cable heats its neighbours. The cumulative temperature rise forces all cables to derate.
- **3 cables in trefoil → $K_4 = 0.70$ (30 % derating).**

**Factor 5 — $K_5$ Installation Method:**
- **Physical reason:** A clipped cable dissipates heat better than one embedded in thermal insulation. Method change alters the effective thermal resistance.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Read Correction Factors:**

- $K_1$ (XLPE, 40 °C, standard 30 °C) → from Table A → **0.96**
- $K_2$ (3-core, 120 mm², 1.0 m depth) → from Table B → **0.97**
- $K_3$ (soil resistivity 200) → from Table C → **0.92**
- $K_4$ (3 cables grouped) → from Table D → **0.70**

**Step 2 — Compute Derated Rating:**

$$
I_{\text{derated}} \;=\; I_{\text{tab}} \times K_1 \times K_2 \times K_3 \times K_4
$$

$$
I_{\text{derated}} \;=\; 285 \times 0.96 \times 0.97 \times 0.92 \times 0.70
$$

$$
I_{\text{derated}} \;=\; 285 \times 0.5996 \;=\; 170.89 \; \text{A}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Safety Factor (if required, $K_s = 1.25$):**

$$
I_{\text{design,min}} \;=\; 150 \times 1.25 \;=\; 187.5 \; \text{A}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison and Decision:**

Since $I_{\text{derated}} = 170.89 \; \text{A} < 187.5 \; \text{A}$, the **120 mm² cable is NOT adequate**.

**Step 5 — Recommended Action:**

Choose the next standard size, **150 mm² or 185 mm²**, and re-verify until the derated current ≥ required design current.

**Answer: The 120 mm² XLPE cable is inadequate; select 185 mm².**

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches, explain the procedure for **derating cables laid in groups on a cable tray**. How does the grouping correction factor $K_4$ vary with (i) number of circuits, (ii) method of laying (trefoil vs flat-spaced), and (iii) spacing between cables?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 3-phase, 415 V, 7.5 kW induction motor ($I_{\text{FLC}} = 14 \; \text{A}, \; \eta = 0.88, \; \cos\phi = 0.85$) is to be fed by an armoured aluminium cable through a **GI conduit along with 2 other circuits** in an industrial environment where the ambient temperature is **48 °C**. Standard conditions: 30 °C. Recommend a suitable cable size from the table below.

| Size (mm²) | Tabulated Current (A) |
|---|---|
| 2.5 | 18 |
| 4 | 24 |
| 6 | 32 |
| 10 | 43 |

**Model Solution (a):**

When multiple circuits share a common cable tray or conduit, each circuit heats its neighbours. The grouping correction factor $K_4$ is read from IS 3961 tables.

**Variation of $K_4$:**

| Parameter | Effect |
|---|---|
| **Number of circuits ↑** | $K_4$ ↓ (more derating) |
| **Trefoil (touching)** vs **Flat-spaced** | Flat-spaced allows better cooling → higher $K_4$ |
| **Horizontal spacing** between cables ↑ | $K_4$ approaches 1.0 (less derating) |

**Sketch (described in words):**

```
Touching Trefoil (Poor):       Flat-spaced (Better):
   C1                             C1          C2
  / \                                       (spacing)
 C — C                                    
   C2                             C3          C4
   K4 ≈ 0.70                      K4 ≈ 0.85
```

General rules:
- **2 circuits touching** → $K_4 = 0.80$
- **3 circuits touching** → $K_4 = 0.70$
- **3 circuits spaced one diameter apart** → $K_4 = 0.85$

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Required Load Current:**

Given $I_{\text{FLC}} = 14 \; \text{A}$.

**Step 2 — Apply Safety Factor:**

$$
I_{\text{design,base}} \;=\; 14 \times 1.25 \;=\; 17.5 \; \text{A}
$$

**Step 3 — Correction Factors:**

- $K_1$ (PVC/XLPE, 48 °C): PVC 70 °C at 45 °C → 0.71, at 50 °C → 0.58. Interpolate for 48 °C:
$$
K_1 \approx 0.71 - \dfrac{3}{5}(0.71 - 0.58) = 0.71 - 0.078 = 0.632
$$
Using XLPE instead (more realistic for armoured) → $K_1 \approx 0.85$ (interpolated between 0.87 at 50 °C and 0.91 at 45 °C). Let us adopt $K_1 = 0.85$.
- $K_4$ (3 circuits in conduit, touching) → **0.70**
- $K_2, K_3, K_5$ → not applicable → **1.00**

**Step 4 — Required Derated Current from Cable:**

$$
I_{\text{cable,required}} \;=\; \dfrac{17.5}{0.85 \times 0.70} \;=\; \dfrac{17.5}{0.595} \;=\; 29.41 \; \text{A}
$$

**Step 5 — Select Cable Size:**

From the table:
- 2.5 mm² → 18 A ❌
- 4 mm² → 24 A ❌
- 6 mm² → 32 A ✅ (≥ 29.41 A)

**Answer: Use 6 mm² aluminium armoured cable.**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Correction Method Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Safety Factor $K_s$:** Many students jump straight to applying $K_1, K_2, K_3, K_4$ on the tabulated current without first multiplying load current by **1.25**. Always state $K_s$ explicitly.
>
> 2. **Adding instead of multiplying correction factors:** A common panic-mistake is writing $I = I_{\text{tab}} - (K_1 + K_2 + K_3 + K_4)$. Correction factors are **multiplicative**, not additive.
>
> 3. **Confusing PVC with XLPE tables:** At 45 °C ambient, PVC's $K_1 = 0.71$ but XLPE's $K_1 = 0.91$. Marking depends on the insulation specified — **read the question twice**.
>
> 4. **Applying $K_2$ and $K_3$ to cables in air:** These are **only for directly buried cables**. A 1-mark penalty per wrong factor application is common in KTU valuation.
>
> 5. **Not stating units and justifying the final cable selection:** Just writing "95 mm²" loses half a mark; write "95 mm² because its derated rating of 180 A ≥ required 169.05 A".
>
> 6. **Missing the "next higher standard size" clause:** If your derated current falls between two standard sizes, you **must move up**, never down.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (print this before exam)**

- [ ] **Master Formula:** $I_{\text{design}} = \dfrac{I_{\text{load}} \cdot K_s}{K_1 K_2 K_3 K_4 K_5}$ — write this on the first page of your answer booklet.
- [ ] **Standard Reference:** Always 30 °C ambient, 150 °C·cm/W soil, 0.5 m depth, single cable in air.
- [ ] **$K_1$** = Temperature. Always applicable. PVC derates faster than XLPE.
- [ ] **$K_2$** = Depth of laying. **Only for buried cables.**
- [ ] **$K_3$** = Soil thermal resistivity. **Only for buried cables.** Dry soil = more derating.
- [ ] **$K_4$** = Grouping. Multi-cable tray/conduit → derating. More cables = more derating.
- [ ] **$K_5$** = Installation method. Embedded/conduit derate more than surface-clipped.
- [ ] **Safety Factor $K_s$** = **1.25** for motors, **1.5** for feeders (some standards).
- [ ] **Always multiply correction factors**, never add.
- [ ] **Always move to next higher standard cable size** if the exact match is unavailable.
- [ ] **Cite the IS Standard** in every answer — IS 3961 (Part II), IS 1255 for UG cables.
- [ ] **Verify voltage drop (< 3 % for motors, < 5 % for feeders)** after sizing — a separate KTU Module 4 topic but examiners love combining the two.
- [ ] **For a typical Kerala site** (ambient 33–40 °C, soil 150–200 °C·cm/W, depth 1.0 m, grouped), expect **30–40 % total derating**.
- [ ] **Remember the 70/90 mnemonic:** PVC max **70 °C**, XLPE max **90 °C**.

**Suggested Memory Acronym — "K-T-D-S-G":**
**K**-temperature, **T**-depth, **D**-soil thermal resistivity, **S**-grouping, **G**-installation method. Apply only those relevant to your installation type.

---

*End of Notes — Correction Methods, PCEET602 Module 3.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of MSB & SSB including Motor Control Centre (MCC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of MSB & SSB including Motor Control Centre (MCC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Main Switch Board (MSB)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
The **Main Switch Board (MSB)** is the principal low-voltage (LV) power distribution assembly that receives electrical power directly from the transformer (or main incoming supply) and distributes it to downstream sub-circuits, Sub-Switch Boards (SSBs), and Motor Control Centres (MCCs). It typically operates at **415 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz** and houses the main incoming circuit breaker, busbars, outgoing feeders, protective devices (ACBs/MCCBs), and metering instruments as per **IS 8623 / IEC 61439** standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Focus:** The MSB is the *nerve centre* of any industrial/building electrical installation. Students must know its components, form of construction, IP rating, and short-circuit withstand capacity.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Grand Central Station"
Think of an MSB exactly like the **Grand Central Station** of a metropolitan city. The high-voltage power (11 kV/22 kV) enters the city through transformer stations and is *stepped down* to 415 V. This 415 V power arrives at the MSB, which acts like the Grand Central Station. From here, "tracks" (feeders/cables) carry power to smaller stations (SSBs) — like suburban junctions. Each SSB then feeds local "neighbourhoods" (final distribution boards, lighting, sockets). Without the MSB, no train (load) could be dispatched, controlled, or protected.

---

## 1.2 Sub-Switch Board (SSB)

**Formal Definition:**
A **Sub-Switch Board (SSB)** is an intermediate LV distribution panel fed from the MSB, used to further distribute power to localised load groups such as lighting distribution boards, small power distribution boards, or specific floor/zone loads. It typically contains **MCCBs / MCBs as outgoing devices** and operates at **415 V / 240 V**.

> [!NOTE]
> An SSB is essentially a *miniature MSB* — it does not have a transformer incomer but receives its supply from the upstream MSB.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "District Post Office"
If the MSB is the Grand Central Station (handles trunk power), the SSB is the **District Post Office** in your locality. Mail (electrical power) is dispatched from the main hub, comes to the district office, and from there is sorted and delivered to individual houses (loads). It also serves as a *localized protection point* — a fault in one house doesn't crash the whole city's mail system.

---

## 1.3 Motor Control Centre (MCC)

**Formal Definition:**
A **Motor Control Centre (MCC)** is a specialized LV switchboard assembly that consolidates the motor starters, protective devices, and control components for multiple motors in one centralized enclosure. It is designed as per **IS 13947 / IEC 60947** and operates at **415 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz**. Each starter section is a *withdrawable/fixed unit* and includes the **contactor, overload relay, short-circuit protection (HRC fuse / MCB), and indicator lamps**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** MCCs are *motor-dedicated* switchboards — they are not generic power distribution boards. Each outgoing unit is designed for a specific motor's starting method (DOL, Star-Delta, Soft-Starter, VFD).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Fleet Garage"
Picture a **bus depot** with 20 buses (motors). Instead of each bus driver building his own garage, the depot has *one large centralized facility* with parking bays (cubicles), each equipped with a key (contactor), a fuel-cutoff safety (overload relay), a service door (isolator), and a dashboard (ammeter/indicators). When a bus (motor) is to be deployed, the bay is activated; when it returns, the bay is locked. The MCC is exactly this — a *centralized, modular garage* for all motors in a plant.

---

## 1.4 Standard Reference Framework

| Standard | Full Title | Application |
|----------|-----------|-------------|
| **IS 8623 / IEC 61439-1** | Low-voltage switchgear assemblies | General design rules for MSB, SSB, MCC |
| **IS 13947 / IEC 60947** | Low-voltage switchgear & controlgear | Contactors, starters, MCBs, MCCBs |
| **IS 13935** | Busbar arrangements | Sizing & current density of busbars |
| **IS 2705** | Current Transformers | Metering CTs in panels |
| **IE Rules 1956 (Amended)** | Central Electricity Authority Rules | Statutory earthing, clearance |

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board Data)

- **System Voltage (LV):** **415 V ± 10%**, 3-Phase, 4-Wire
- **Frequency:** **50 Hz ± 5%**
- **Standard Fault Level (LV side):** **25 kA / 35 kA / 50 kA** (1 sec)
- **Ambient Reference Temperature for Busbar:** **40°C**
- **Busbar Current Density (Copper):** **1.0 to 1.6 A/mm²**
- **Busbar Current Density (Aluminium):** **0.8 to 1.2 A/mm²**
- **Standard Busbar Cross-Sections (A):** 100, 200, 400, 630, 800, 1000, 1250, 1600, 2000, 2500, 3200, 4000
- **Minimum IP Rating (Indoor Panel):** **IP 42 / IP 54**
- **Minimum IP Rating (Outdoor Panel):** **IP 55 / IP 65**
- **Standard Form of Construction:** **Form 2 / Form 3b / Form 4a / Form 4b**
- **Operating Temperature Limit (Busbar):** **85°C (PVC) / 105°C (XLPE)**
- **Standard Earthing Resistance:** **≤ 1 Ω** (for large installations)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Philosophy of MSB

The design of an MSB follows a **hierarchical, top-down methodology** as mandated by **IS 8623 / IEC 61439-1**. The design flow is:

1. **Load Assessment** → Sum total connected load + diversity factor
2. **Determine Rated Current of Incomer** → $I_{rated} = \dfrac{P_{total}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi}$
3. **Short-Circuit Calculation** → $I_{sc} = \dfrac{MVA_{sc}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L}$
4. **Select Busbar Size** → based on current density & short-circuit withstand
5. **Select Incomer Breaker** → ACB (for > 800 A) / MCCB (for < 800 A)
6. **Select Outgoing Feeders** → MCCBs rated to load
7. **Form of Segregation** → Form 2 / 3b / 4a / 4b
8. **IP & IK Rating** → as per installation environment

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the hierarchy matters:** Each step constrains the next. Under-rated busbars (Step 4) cannot be saved by an oversized breaker (Step 5). The KTU board expects students to follow this *sequential logic* in design problems.

---

## 2.2 Design Philosophy of SSB

An SSB is a *simplified* MSB. Its design logic:

1. **Input Supply:** From MSB feeder (already protected upstream)
2. **Rating of Incomer:** MCB / MCCB sized to sum of outgoing loads × diversity factor
3. **Outgoing Devices:** MCBs (single/three-pole) for lighting, small power, socket circuits
4. **Metering:** kWh meter + indicator lamps + selector switch
5. **Earthing:** Two earth strips (main + spare) connected to earth bus
6. **Form of Construction:** Typically **Form 2** or **Form 3a**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** In design questions, SSBs almost always have a *maximum height* of **1.8 m** and *maximum width* of **1.2 m** to suit floor-mounting in corridors. Single-front (F-F) panels are used when rear access is restricted.

---

## 2.3 Design Philosophy of MCC

The MCC is the *most standardized* of the three. Its design revolves around the **motor feeder module**, which is a single bay/unit housing:

- **Isolator / MCCB** (short-circuit protection)
- **Contactor** (switching ON/OFF)
- **Overload Relay** (sustained overcurrent protection)
- **Start/Stop Pushbuttons + Indications**
- **Ammeter + Selector Switch** (optional)

**Design Steps:**
1. List all motors with ratings (kW / HP), starting method, duty cycle
2. Calculate Full-Load Current (FLC) of each motor: $I_{FLC} = \dfrac{P \cdot 1000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \eta \cdot \cos\phi}$
3. Apply derating factors for grouping (per IS 3961)
4. Select starter type (DOL ≤ 5 HP, Star-Delta 5–25 HP, Soft-Starter > 25 HP)
5. Size isolator, contactor, OLR per **IS 13947**
6. Determine cubicle count (typically 6–12 sections per panel)
7. Ensure common busbar, common earth, common cable alley

---

## 2.4 Components of an MSB (Detailed)

| Component | Function | Typical Specification |
|-----------|----------|----------------------|
| **Incomer ACB/MCCB** | Main isolation & short-circuit protection | 800–4000 A, 50 kA Icu |
| **Busbars (R, Y, B, N, E)** | Current carrying & distribution | Copper/Al, electro-tinned |
| **Current Transformers (CTs)** | Metering & protection | 100/5, 200/5, etc. |
| **Voltage Transformers (VTs)** | Metering & protection | 415/√3 / 110/√3 V |
| **Protective Relays** | Overcurrent, earth fault, differential | Numerical type |
| **Indicating Meters** | Ammeter, Voltmeter, kW, kWh, PF, Hz | Digital/Analogue |
| **Outgoing MCCBs/MCBs** | Branch protection | 63–800 A |
| **Cable Termination Compartments** | Incoming/outgoing cable glanding | IP 54 minimum |
| **Earthing Bus** | Equipotential bonding | Copper 50×6 mm |
| **Bus-Section Coupler (BSC)** | Tie-line between two MSBs (when dual source) | ACB + mechanical interlock |

---

## 2.5 Busbar Sizing — The Core Calculation

Busbar sizing uses **current density** for thermal design and **electromagnetic force** for mechanical design.

### (a) Thermal Design (Current Carrying Capacity)

The cross-sectional area of the busbar:

$$A_{bus} = \dfrac{I_{rated}}{J}$$

where $J$ is the permissible current density:
- **Copper busbar (indoor, painted):** $J = 1.0 \text{ to } 1.6 \text{ A/mm}^2$
- **Aluminium busbar (indoor, painted):** $J = 0.8 \text{ to } 1.2 \text{ A/mm}^2$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention (Board Exam):** Use **J = 1.2 A/mm² for copper** and **J = 0.8 A/mm² for aluminium** unless specified otherwise. These are the *most commonly accepted* values in KTU model solutions.

### (b) Short-Circuit Withstand (Thermal)

The minimum cross-section for short-circuit thermal endurance (1 sec duration):

$$A_{sc} = \dfrac{I_{sc}}{\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{K^2}{t}\right) \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{\theta_f + \beta}{\theta_i + \beta}\right)}}$$

A simplified empirical form (used in board exams):

$$A_{sc} = \dfrac{I_{sc}}{C}$$

where:
- $C$ = material constant: **Copper = 159 A/mm²·s⁻¹ᐟ²**, **Aluminium = 91 A/mm²·s⁻¹ᐟ²**
- $I_{sc}$ = RMS short-circuit current in A
- $t$ = duration (typically 1 sec)

### (c) Electromagnetic Force on Parallel Busbars

When a fault current flows through two parallel busbars, the force per metre length:

$$F = \dfrac{2 \cdot I_{sc}^2 \cdot 10^{-7}}{d}$$

where $d$ = centre-to-centre distance (m), $I_{sc}$ = peak short-circuit current (A), $F$ is in N/m.

---

## 2.6 Voltage Drop in MSB/SSB Feeders

$$\%V_d = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)}{V_L \cdot 10}$$

**Permissible Limits (IS Standards):**
- For **motors:** ≤ **10%** at motor terminals during starting
- For **lighting:** ≤ **3%** at farthest lamp
- For **general power:** ≤ **5%**

---

## 2.7 Derating Factors for Grouped Cables (IS 3961)

When multiple circuits are bunched inside an MCC trough, derating is applied:

| No. of Circuits in Trough | Derating Factor |
|---------------------------|-----------------|
| 2 | 0.80 |
| 3 | 0.70 |
| 4 | 0.65 |
| 6 | 0.57 |
| 9 or more | 0.50 |

---

## 2.8 Forms of Segregation (IS 8623 — KTU Critical)

| Form | Description | Typical Use |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Form 1** | No internal separation | Non-essential services |
| **Form 2a** | Separation of busbars from functional units | Small commercial |
| **Form 2b** | Same as 2a + separation of busbars from external terminations | Small SSBs |
| **Form 3a** | Separation of busbars + separation of functional units from each other (no separation between units and terminations) | Industrial SSBs |
| **Form 3b** | Same as 3a + separation between functional units and their terminations | Industrial SSBs/MCCs |
| **Form 4a** | Separation of busbars + functional units + terminations, **no separation between adjacent units** | MSB |
| **Form 4b** | Full segregation including between adjacent functional units | Critical MSB, hospitals |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Students often confuse Form 3a with Form 3b, and Form 4a with Form 4b. Remember: **3a vs 3b = presence of terminal separation; 4a vs 4b = separation between *adjacent units themselves***. This is a guaranteed 1–2 mark question every year.

---

## 2.9 Motor FLC Reference Table (415 V, 3-φ, 50 Hz, Efficiency 0.85, PF 0.85)

| Motor kW | Approx. FLC (A) | Recommended Starter |
|----------|-----------------|---------------------|
| 0.37 | 1.0 | DOL |
| 0.75 | 1.8 | DOL |
| 1.5 | 3.4 | DOL |
| 2.2 | 5.0 | DOL |
| 3.7 | 8.0 | DOL |
| 5.5 | 11 | Star-Delta |
| 7.5 | 15 | Star-Delta |
| 11 | 22 | Star-Delta |
| 15 | 29 | Star-Delta |
| 18.5 | 35 | Star-Delta |
| 22 | 41 | Star-Delta |
| 30 | 56 | Soft-Starter |
| 37 | 68 | Soft-Starter |
| 45 | 83 | Soft-Starter |
| 55 | 100 | Soft-Starter |
| 75 | 135 | Soft-Starter |
| 90 | 160 | VFD |
| 110 | 200 | VFD |
| 132 | 240 | VFD |
| 160 | 285 | VFD |

> Use the **exact formula** $I_{FLC} = \dfrac{P \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L \times \eta \times \cos\phi}$ in board exam; the table is only for verification.

---

## 2.10 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Comprehensive)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Full-Load Current (3-φ motor) | $I_{FLC} = \dfrac{P \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \eta \cdot \cos\phi}$ | A |
| 2 | Busbar cross-section (thermal) | $A_{bus} = \dfrac{I_{rated}}{J}$ | mm² |
| 3 | Busbar cross-section (SC withstand) | $A_{sc} = \dfrac{I_{sc}}{C}$ | mm² |
| 4 | Short-circuit current (LV side) | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{MVA_{sc}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L}$ | A |
| 5 | Short-circuit current (MVA method) | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{kVA_{sc}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \times 10^3}$ | kA |
| 6 | Electromagnetic force on busbar | $F = \dfrac{2 \cdot I_{p}^2 \times 10^{-7}}{d}$ | N/m |
| 7 | Peak short-circuit current | $I_p = 2.55 \cdot I_{sc(rms)}$ | A |
| 8 | Percentage voltage drop | $\%V_d = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)}{V_L \times 10}$ | % |
| 9 | Minimum cable size | $A_{cable} = \dfrac{I_{FLC}}{K \cdot D_f}$ | mm² |
| 10 | Total connected load (MSB) | $P_{total} = \sum P_i \cdot D_f$ | kW |

Where:
- $J$ = current density
- $C$ = 159 (Cu) or 91 (Al)
- $K$ = cable current rating factor
- $D_f$ = derating factor (grouping, ambient, soil)
- $d$ = busbar centre-to-centre distance (m)
- $I_p$ = peak (asymmetric) short-circuit current

---

## 2.11 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Plants** (cement, steel, automotive): MSB at the transformer secondary; multiple SSBs for workshop zones; large MCC for motor control of conveyors, mills, pumps.
- **High-Rise Buildings:** MSB at the basement, multiple SSBs per floor cluster, dedicated MCC for HVAC & lift motors.
- **Data Centres:** MSB with **Form 4b segregation**, dual-bus, ATS, and paralleling with generator bus.
- **Hospitals:** Critical MSB with **99.999%** reliability (Tier IV) — Form 4b, IP 54, redundant incomers.
- **Renewable Plants (Solar/Wind):** MCCs for tracker motors, azimuth drives, and SCADA-controlled actuators.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: MSB Incomer Current from Connected Load

> **Problem Statement:** A 3-phase, 415 V industrial MSB feeds the following loads:
> - 3 × 75 kW motors (η = 0.88, cos φ = 0.85)
> - 2 × 30 kW motors (η = 0.87, cos φ = 0.82)
> - 1 × 50 kW welding set (η = 0.90, cos φ = 0.60, intermittent duty)
> - General lighting: 25 kW (cos φ = 0.95)
>
> Determine the **MSB incomer current rating** assuming **diversity factor 0.85** for motors and 1.0 for lighting.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Compute Full-Load Current (FLC) of each motor.

For a 3-phase motor:

$$I_{FLC} = \dfrac{P \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \eta \cdot \cos\phi}$$

**For 75 kW motor (individual):**

$$I_{75} = \dfrac{75 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.88 \times 0.85}$$

$$I_{75} = \dfrac{75000}{1.732 \times 415 \times 0.748}$$

$$I_{75} = \dfrac{75000}{537.8} = 139.46 \text{ A}$$

> Note: We use $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$ and $0.88 \times 0.85 = 0.748$.

**For 30 kW motor (individual):**

$$I_{30} = \dfrac{30 \times 1000}{1.732 \times 415 \times 0.87 \times 0.82}$$

$$I_{30} = \dfrac{30000}{1.732 \times 415 \times 0.7134}$$

$$I_{30} = \dfrac{30000}{512.5} = 58.54 \text{ A}$$

**For 50 kW welding set (intermittent, consider as 0.5 duty cycle):**

$$I_{weld} = \dfrac{50 \times 1000}{1.732 \times 415 \times 0.90 \times 0.60} \times 0.5$$

$$I_{weld} = \dfrac{50000}{388.0} \times 0.5$$

$$I_{weld} = 128.86 \times 0.5 = 64.43 \text{ A}$$

**For 25 kW lighting load:**

$$I_{light} = \dfrac{25 \times 1000}{1.732 \times 415 \times 0.95}$$

$$I_{light} = \dfrac{25000}{683.0} = 36.60 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2:** Total individual FLCs.

$$I_{total} = (3 \times 139.46) + (2 \times 58.54) + 64.43 + 36.60$$

$$I_{total} = 418.38 + 117.08 + 64.43 + 36.60$$

$$I_{total} = 636.49 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3:** Apply diversity factor 0.85 for motors and 0.5 for welding (already done), lighting at 1.0:

$$I_{MSB} = (3 \times 139.46 + 2 \times 58.54) \times 0.85 + 64.43 + 36.60 \times 1.0$$

$$I_{MSB} = (535.46) \times 0.85 + 64.43 + 36.60$$

$$I_{MSB} = 455.14 + 64.43 + 36.60$$

$$I_{MSB} = 556.17 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4:** Standardize to next available ACB/MCCB frame size: **630 A**.

> The MSB incomer is selected as **630 A, 50 kA ACB**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Busbar Cross-Section for Thermal + Short-Circuit Withstand

> **Problem Statement:** For the above MSB (incomer rating 630 A, fault level 35 kA for 1 sec at 415 V), size the **main busbar** using:
> - Copper material, current density J = 1.2 A/mm²
> - Material constant C = 159 (Cu)
> - Verify that thermal + SC both are satisfied.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Thermal (continuous current) sizing.

$$A_{bus,th} = \dfrac{I_{rated}}{J} = \dfrac{630}{1.2} = 525 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2:** Short-circuit thermal sizing.

$$A_{bus,sc} = \dfrac{I_{sc}}{C} = \dfrac{35{,}000}{159}$$

$$A_{bus,sc} = 220.13 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3:** Final busbar area = maximum of the two.

$$A_{bus} = \max(525, 220.13) = 525 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4:** Standardize to a physical busbar.

A typical 525 mm² copper busbar is realized as:
- **1 strip** of $100 \times 6$ mm = 600 mm² (over-rated, used commonly)
- **1 strip** of $80 \times 6.5$ mm = 520 mm² (close fit)

Selecting: **1 × 100 × 6 mm copper busbar per phase = 600 mm²**

> **Design Check:** $600 \text{ mm}^2 \geq 525 \text{ mm}^2$ ✓ (thermal OK)
> $600 \text{ mm}^2 \geq 220.13 \text{ mm}^2$ ✓ (SC OK)

**Step 5:** Verify ampacity of 100×6 mm Cu busbar.

From IS 13935 tables, a 100×6 mm Cu busbar (painted, indoor) carries **650 A** continuously. Since 650 A > 630 A, design is acceptable.

> **Final Selection:** **1 strip of 100 × 6 mm Cu busbar per phase**, plus a neutral busbar (half the phase size) = **50 × 6 mm**, plus an earth busbar = **50 × 6 mm Cu**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Electromagnetic Force on Busbars (Mechanical Design)

> **Problem Statement:** Two parallel 100 × 6 mm copper busbars, centre-to-centre distance = 75 mm, are subjected to a peak fault current $I_p = 90$ kA. Calculate the **mechanical force** per metre and verify that supports can be spaced to handle this.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute peak (asymmetric) short-circuit current.

$$I_p = 2.55 \times I_{sc,rms} = 2.55 \times 35{,}000 = 89{,}250 \text{ A} \approx 90 \text{ kA}$$

(As given)

**Step 2:** Compute force per metre length.

$$F = \dfrac{2 \cdot I_p^2 \cdot 10^{-7}}{d}$$

$$F = \dfrac{2 \times (90{,}000)^2 \times 10^{-7}}{0.075}$$

$$F = \dfrac{2 \times 8.1 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-7}}{0.075}$$

$$F = \dfrac{1620}{0.075}$$

$$F = 21{,}600 \text{ N/m}$$

> This is a massive force — equivalent to **~2.2 tonnes per metre** of busbar.

**Step 3:** Convert to bending stress on the busbar.

If supports are spaced at $L = 0.6$ m (typical), the bending moment:

$$M = \dfrac{F \cdot L^2}{8} = \dfrac{21600 \times 0.36}{8} = 972 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4:** Bending stress in Cu busbar.

Section modulus of a 100 × 6 mm bar (bending about its long axis):

$$Z = \dfrac{b \cdot d^2}{6} = \dfrac{6 \times 100^2}{6} = 10{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$\sigma = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{972 \times 10^3}{10{,}000} = 97.2 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 5:** Compare with Cu permissible stress (~120 N/mm²).

Since $97.2 \text{ N/mm}^2 < 120 \text{ N/mm}^2$ ✓, **the support spacing of 600 mm is acceptable.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Mechanical design of busbars is often overlooked by students. The board expects you to *check the force, bending moment, and stress*, and then either accept the support spacing or reduce it. This is a high-value 7-mark derivation in Part B.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: MCC Feeder Module for 30 kW, 3-Phase Motor

> **Problem Statement:** Design a single feeder module of an MCC for a 30 kW, 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz, squirrel-cage induction motor (η = 0.88, cos φ = 0.85). Use:
> - Star-Delta starter
> - HRC fuses for short-circuit protection
> - Overload relay in the delta winding
> - Cable from MCC to motor: 25 m, 3-core Cu, PVC insulated, run on perforated cable tray (grouped with 4 circuits).

### Solution

**Step 1:** FLC of motor.

$$I_{FLC} = \dfrac{30 \times 1000}{1.732 \times 415 \times 0.88 \times 0.85}$$

$$I_{FLC} = \dfrac{30{,}000}{537.8}$$

$$I_{FLC} = 55.78 \text{ A} \approx 56 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2:** Star-Delta line current during starting (≈ 1/√3 of FLC).

$$I_{start,Y} = \dfrac{56}{\sqrt{3}} = 32.33 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3:** Contactor selection.

Per IS 13947, contactor rating ≥ FLC:

- **Main (Line) Contactor (K1):** 63 A, AC-3 duty
- **Star Contactor (K2):** 32 A, AC-3 duty
- **Delta Contactor (K3):** 63 A, AC-3 duty

**Step 4:** HRC fuse selection.

HRC fuse rating = 1.5 to 2 × FLC for motor circuits:

$$I_{fuse} = 1.5 \times 56 = 84 \text{ A} \rightarrow \text{Next standard: 100 A HRC}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Why not use MCB?** For motors, **HRC fuses are preferred** because they can withstand high inrush currents (6–8 × FLC) during DOL starting, while MCBs would nuisance-trip. The board expects this reasoning in 2024 scheme.

**Step 5:** Overload relay setting.

OLR setting = 0.58 × FLC (because in star-delta, the OLR is in the delta winding which carries phase current = line current / √3):

$$I_{OLR} = 0.58 \times 56 = 32.5 \text{ A} \rightarrow \text{Set at 32 A on relay dial}$$

**Step 6:** Cable sizing.

**Apply derating:** Grouping of 4 circuits → $D_g = 0.65$; ambient 40°C → $D_a = 0.87$; PVC → $K_{PVC} = 1.0$ (base).

Overall derating factor:

$$D_f = 0.65 \times 0.87 = 0.5655$$

Required cable current rating:

$$I_{cable,req} = \dfrac{I_{FLC}}{D_f} = \dfrac{56}{0.5655} = 99.0 \text{ A}$$

From IS 3961 cable rating tables, **10 mm² Cu 3-core PVC cable** = 60 A (un-grouped). With derating:

$$I_{cable,derated} = 60 \times 0.5655 = 33.9 \text{ A} \quad (\text{Not sufficient!})$$

Try **16 mm² Cu** = 76 A ungrouped:

$$I_{cable,derated} = 76 \times 0.5655 = 43.0 \text{ A} \quad (\text{Still not sufficient!})$$

Try **25 mm² Cu** = 99 A ungrouped:

$$I_{cable,derated} = 99 \times 0.5655 = 56.0 \text{ A} \approx I_{FLC} \quad (\text{Marginal OK})$$

For safety margin, select **35 mm² Cu, 3-core, PVC armoured cable**.

**Step 7:** Voltage drop check.

For 35 mm² Cu, $R \approx 0.524$ Ω/km, $X \approx 0.07$ Ω/km, $L = 25$ m = 0.025 km:

$$\%V_d = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \times 56 \times 0.025 \times (0.524 \times 0.85 + 0.07 \times 0.527)}{415 \times 10}$$

$$\%V_d = \dfrac{1.732 \times 56 \times 0.025 \times (0.4454 + 0.0369)}{4150}$$

$$\%V_d = \dfrac{2.42 \times 0.4823}{4150}$$

$$\%V_d = \dfrac{1.167}{4150} \times 100 = 0.028\%$$

This is exceptionally low, well within the 3% permissible limit.

> **Final MCC Feeder Module:** Contactor (K1=63 A, K2=32 A, K3=63 A), HRC fuse 100 A, OLR set 32 A, Cable 35 mm² Cu 3-core PVC armoured.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Design Decision Tree (KTU 2024)

| Design Parameter | If < 100 A | If 100–800 A | If > 800 A |
|------------------|-----------|-------------|-----------|
| **Incomer device** | MCCB | MCCB / ACB | ACB (with draw-out) |
| **Busbar material** | Aluminium | Copper | Copper (tinned) |
| **Busbar size** | 25×6 mm Al | 50×6 to 100×6 mm Cu | 2 × 100×10 mm Cu (double run) |
| **Form of segregation** | Form 2 | Form 3b | Form 4a / 4b |
| **IP rating (indoor)** | IP 42 | IP 52 | IP 54 |
| **Cable entry** | Bottom | Top + Bottom | Top (through cable cell) |
| **Panel height** | 1.5 m | 1.8 m | 2.2 m (with plinth) |
| **Protection relay** | Thermal OLR | Static OLR | Numerical multi-function |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a Main Switch Board (MSB). List any four components housed inside an MSB.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Main Switch Board (MSB)** is the principal low-voltage (415 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz) power distribution assembly of an electrical installation that receives power directly from the secondary of the distribution transformer and distributes it to downstream Sub-Switch Boards (SSBs), Motor Control Centres (MCCs), and large loads. It is designed, tested, and constructed as per **IS 8623 / IEC 61439-1** and **IE Rules 1956**.

**Four essential components of an MSB:**
1. **Incomer ACB/MCCB** — for isolation and short-circuit/overload protection of the entire board.
2. **Main busbars (R, Y, B, N, E)** — made of copper or aluminium, electro-tinned, for current distribution.
3. **Current Transformers (CTs) and Metering** — for ammeter, kWh meter, and protection relays.
4. **Outgoing MCCBs / Switch Fuse Units** — for protection of downstream feeders to SSBs, MCCs, and direct loads.

> **(1 mark definition + 0.5 mark each component = 3 marks total)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Differentiate between an SSB and an MCC. (Any 3 points)**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Parameter | Sub-Switch Board (SSB) | Motor Control Centre (MCC) |
|---|-----------|------------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **Function** | Further distribution of power to localized lighting/socket/small-power loads. | Centralized control & protection of multiple motors. |
| 2 | **Typical Outgoing Devices** | MCBs (single/triple pole) for final sub-circuits. | Contactors + OLR + starters (DOL, Star-Delta, VFD) per motor. |
| 3 | **Form of Construction** | Form 2 / Form 3a — simpler segregation. | Form 3b / Form 4a — higher segregation for safety during motor fault. |
| 4 | **Metering** | kWh meter + RYB indication. | Per-module ammeter + selector switch + status lamps. |
| 5 | **Number of Feeders** | 6 to 18 outgoing ways typical. | 6 to 12 cubicles per panel, each dedicated to a motor. |

> **(1.5 marks table + 0.5 mark each for additional key point, max 3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [Module 3 Internal Choice Option 1]

**(a) [7 Marks]** An industrial MSB at 415 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz, is fed by a 1500 kVA transformer with 6% impedance. The MSB feeds the following loads:
- 2 × 90 kW, 3-φ motors (η = 0.90, cos φ = 0.86)
- 4 × 55 kW, 3-φ motors (η = 0.88, cos φ = 0.85)
- 1 × 75 kW welding transformer (η = 0.92, cos φ = 0.55, intermittent 50% duty)
- General lighting: 30 kW (cos φ = 0.95)

**Calculate (i) the full-load current of each load group, (ii) the MSB incomer rating assuming a diversity factor of 0.8 for motors, 0.5 for welding, and 1.0 for lighting.**

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the above MSB, design the **main copper busbar** considering:
- Current density = 1.2 A/mm²
- Material constant C = 159 A/mm²·sec⁻¹ᐟ²
- Fault level = 35 kA (RMS) for 1 sec

**Verify that the busbar satisfies both the thermal and short-circuit withstand criteria. Recommend a standard busbar size.**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- $V_L = 415$ V
- System: 3-phase, 50 Hz
- 2 × 90 kW motors
- 4 × 55 kW motors
- 1 × 75 kW welding transformer (intermittent)
- 30 kW lighting

**Step 1: FLC of 90 kW motor**

$$I_{90} = \dfrac{90 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.90 \times 0.86}$$

$$I_{90} = \dfrac{90{,}000}{1.732 \times 415 \times 0.774}$$

$$I_{90} = \dfrac{90{,}000}{556.2}$$

$$I_{90} = 161.81 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: FLC of 55 kW motor**

$$I_{55} = \dfrac{55 \times 1000}{1.732 \times 415 \times 0.88 \times 0.85}$$

$$I_{55} = \dfrac{55{,}000}{1.732 \times 415 \times 0.748}$$

$$I_{55} = \dfrac{55{,}000}{537.8}$$

$$I_{55} = 102.27 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: FLC of 75 kW welding set (intermittent 50%)**

$$I_{weld} = \dfrac{75 \times 1000}{1.732 \times 415 \times 0.92 \times 0.55} \times 0.5$$

$$I_{weld} = \dfrac{75{,}000}{1.732 \times 415 \times 0.506} \times 0.5$$

$$I_{weld} = \dfrac{75{,}000}{363.86} \times 0.5$$

$$I_{weld} = 206.14 \times 0.5$$

$$I_{weld} = 103.07 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4: FLC of 30 kW lighting load**

$$I_{light} = \dfrac{30 \times 1000}{1.732 \times 415 \times 0.95}$$

$$I_{light} = \dfrac{30{,}000}{683.0}$$

$$I_{light} = 43.92 \text{ A}$$

**Step 5: Total individual currents (before diversity)**

$$I_{total} = (2 \times 161.81) + (4 \times 102.27) + 103.07 + 43.92$$

$$I_{total} = 323.62 + 409.08 + 103.07 + 43.92$$

$$I_{total} = 879.69 \text{ A}$$

**Step 6: Apply diversity factors**

$$I_{MSB} = (323.62 + 409.08) \times 0.8 + 103.07 \times 0.5 + 43.92 \times 1.0$$

$$I_{MSB} = 732.70 \times 0.8 + 51.54 + 43.92$$

$$I_{MSB} = 586.16 + 51.54 + 43.92$$

$$\boxed{I_{MSB} = 681.62 \text{ A} \approx 682 \text{ A}}$$

**Step 7: Standardize to next standard ACB frame**

$$\boxed{\text{MSB Incomer Rating: } \textbf{800 A, 50 kA ACB}}$$

> **[Marks distribution]: 1.5 (FLC 90 kW) + 1.5 (FLC 55 kW) + 1.5 (FLC welding) + 1.0 (FLC lighting) + 1.5 (MSB current with diversity) = 7 marks**

---

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Thermal (continuous) cross-section**

$$A_{th} = \dfrac{I_{rated}}{J} = \dfrac{800}{1.2} = 666.67 \text{ mm}^2$$

(Using 800 A as incomer rating — though current demand is 682 A, we design for the breaker rating.)

**Step 2: Short-circuit cross-section**

$$A_{sc} = \dfrac{I_{sc}}{C} = \dfrac{35{,}000}{159} = 220.13 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Governing value**

$$A_{bus} = \max(666.67, 220.13) = 666.67 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4: Standard physical realization**

A single 80 × 10 mm Cu strip = 800 mm² (over-rated by 20%, acceptable).

Or, two strips of **50 × 8 mm Cu** = 2 × 400 = 800 mm² (more compact, better current distribution).

**Recommended: 2 × 50 × 8 mm Cu busbar per phase = 800 mm²**

> **(Decision logic:** For currents > 600 A, single strips > 100 mm wide become mechanically cumbersome. Multiple parallel strips are preferred for better heat dissipation and easier installation.)

**Step 5: Verification**

- **Thermal:** 2 × 50 × 8 mm Cu carries approximately **860 A** (from IS 13935) > 800 A ✓
- **Short-circuit:** 800 mm² ≫ 220.13 mm² ✓
- **Phase-to-phase clearance:** ≥ 25 mm (per IS 13935)
- **Neutral:** Half of phase = 50 × 8 mm = 400 mm²
- **Earth:** 50 × 6 mm Cu

> **[Marks distribution]: 2 (thermal) + 2 (SC) + 2 (selection + verification) + 1 (drawing/inference) = 7 marks**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Module 3 Internal Choice Option 2]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat single-line diagram, describe the **internal architecture of a typical MCC** designed to control four 3-phase induction motors of ratings: **5.5 kW (DOL), 15 kW (Star-Delta), 30 kW (Soft-Starter), and 55 kW (VFD)**. Indicate the protective device, starter, and cable rating for each feeder.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the 55 kW, 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz motor controlled by a VFD in the above MCC:
- η = 0.92, cos φ = 0.87
- Cable length from MCC to motor = 30 m
- 4-core Cu, XLPE cable, run in cable tray grouped with 3 other circuits
- Ambient temperature = 45°C
- **Calculate (i) the FLC, (ii) the size of HRC fuse, (iii) the cable size, and (iv) verify the voltage drop (must be ≤ 3%).**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]**

A typical MCC consists of **one incoming section** and **four feeder sections (cubicles)**, each dedicated to one motor. The single-line diagram of the internal architecture:

> **(See Section 4.3 Mermaid block in Section 4 for the visual representation; the textual description follows.)**

**MCC Architecture Description:**

**Section 1 — Incomer:**
- 1250 A ACB, 50 kA Icu
- Multifunction meter (V, A, kW, kWh, PF, Hz)
- CTs 1250/5 A, VT 415/√3 / 110/√3 V
- Common copper busbar: 2 × 80 × 8 mm = 1280 mm², R-Y-B-N-E

**Section 2 — Feeder for 5.5 kW Motor (DOL Starter):**
- MCCB 32 A, 35 kA
- Contactor K1: 16 A AC-3 duty
- OLR: 4–6 A adjustable, set at 5.5 A
- HRC fuse: 25 A (back-up)
- 3-core Cu, 4 mm² PVC armoured cable
- Indications: Run (Green), Trip (Yellow)

**Section 3 — Feeder for 15 kW Motor (Star-Delta Starter):**
- MCCB 63 A, 35 kA
- Main contactor K1: 32 A; Star K2: 20 A; Delta K3: 32 A
- OLR set at 17 A (in delta winding, current = FLC/√3)
- HRC fuse: 50 A
- Star-Delta timer: 0–30 sec
- 3-core Cu, 10 mm² PVC armoured cable

**Section 4 — Feeder for 30 kW Motor (Soft-Starter):**
- MCCB 100 A, 35 kA
- Soft-Starter: 63 A rating
- Bypass contactor: 63 A
- OLR: 33 A
- HRC fuse: 80 A
- 3-core Cu, 16 mm² XLPE armoured cable

**Section 5 — Feeder for 55 kW Motor (VFD):**
- MCCB 160 A, 35 kA
- VFD: 110 A rating (110% continuous, 150% for 60 sec)
- Line reactor: 110 A
- OLR: integrated in VFD
- HRC fuse: 200 A
- 3-core Cu, 35 mm² XLPE armoured cable with shielded variant
- EMC filter

**Common Features:**
- Earth bus: 50 × 6 mm Cu running full length
- Cable alley: top entry, 200 mm wide
- Form 4a segregation
- IP 54 enclosure, RAL 7035 paint

> **[Marks distribution]: 1.5 (single-line description) + 4 × 1.0 (each feeder) + 1.5 (common features) = 7 marks**

---

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Given:** $P = 55$ kW, $V_L = 415$ V, $\eta = 0.92$, $\cos\phi = 0.87$, $L = 30$ m, ambient 45°C, grouped with 3 other circuits.

**Step 1: FLC of motor**

$$I_{FLC} = \dfrac{55 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.92 \times 0.87}$$

$$I_{FLC} = \dfrac{55{,}000}{1.732 \times 415 \times 0.8004}$$

$$I_{FLC} = \dfrac{55{,}000}{575.0}$$

$$\boxed{I_{FLC} = 95.65 \text{ A} \approx 96 \text{ A}}$$

**Step 2: HRC fuse size**

For motor feeder with VFD, HRC fuse rating:

$$I_{fuse} = 1.5 \times I_{FLC} = 1.5 \times 96 = 144 \text{ A}$$

Next standard: **160 A HRC fuse, type gG**

**Step 3: Cable sizing**

**Derating factors:**
- Grouping (4 circuits in tray): $D_g = 0.65$
- Ambient 45°C: $D_a = 0.87$ (for XLPE, 90°C rated)
- Thermal insulation: $D_i = 1.0$ (cable on tray, not buried)

**Overall derating:**

$$D_f = 0.65 \times 0.87 \times 1.0 = 0.5655$$

**Required cable current capacity:**

$$I_{req} = \dfrac{96}{0.5655} = 169.76 \text{ A}$$

From IS 3961 / XLPE cable current rating tables:

| Cable size (Cu 4-core XLPE) | Current rating (A) | Derated (× 0.5655) |
|----------------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 25 mm² | 127 | 71.8 ✗ |
| 35 mm² | 152 | 85.9 ✗ |
| **50 mm²** | **185** | **104.6 ✓** |
| 70 mm² | 228 | 129.0 ✓ (over-rated) |

**Selected cable: 50 mm², 4-core Cu, XLPE armoured**

**Step 4: Voltage drop verification**

For 50 mm² Cu XLPE cable:
- $R = 0.387$ Ω/km
- $X = 0.069$ Ω/km
- $L = 0.030$ km
- $I = 96$ A
- $\cos\phi = 0.87$, $\sin\phi = 0.493$

$$\%V_d = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \times 96 \times 0.030 \times (0.387 \times 0.87 + 0.069 \times 0.493)}{415 \times 10}$$

$$\%V_d = \dfrac{1.732 \times 96 \times 0.030 \times (0.3367 + 0.0340)}{4150}$$

$$\%V_d = \dfrac{4.989 \times 0.3707}{4150}$$

$$\%V_d = \dfrac{1.850}{4150} \times 100 = 0.045\%$$

**This is well within the 3% limit. ✓**

> **[Marks distribution]: 1.5 (FLC) + 1.5 (fuse) + 2.0 (cable sizing) + 2.0 (voltage drop) = 7 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Diversity factor application:** Students often apply diversity to *all* loads uniformly. *Pitfall:* Lighting, sockets, and emergency loads have **DF = 1.0** in most cases; only motors allow DF < 1. **Loss of 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Star-Delta OLR setting:** OLR must be set at **FLC / √3** (because it is in the delta phase winding), not at FLC. *Pitfall:* Setting at FLC will cause nuisance trips. **Loss of 1 mark.**
> 3. **Cable derating:** Skipping derating for grouping or ambient temperature is a guaranteed **2-mark loss**. Always list all derating factors explicitly.
> 4. **Form of segregation confusion:** Conflating Form 3a with Form 3b or Form 4a with Form 4b is the single most common error. **Loss of 1 mark.**
> 5. **HRC fuse for VFD-fed motors:** Many students assume MCB is acceptable. *Pitfall:* MCBs nuisance-trip due to VFD switching transients; **HRC fuses are mandatory** upstream of the VFD.
> 6. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to multiply kW by 1000 in the FLC formula is the #1 silly mistake — **−1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3**

### 🔑 Key Definitions
- **MSB:** Principal LV distribution board, fed directly from transformer secondary.
- **SSB:** Intermediate LV board, fed from MSB, for localized distribution.
- **MCC:** Motor-dedicated LV board with contactors, OLRs, and starters per module.

### 🔑 Governing Standards
- **IS 8623 / IEC 61439-1** — General rules for LV switchgear assemblies.
- **IS 13947 / IEC 60947** — Contactors, starters, MCBs, MCCBs.
- **IS 13935** — Busbar sizing & arrangements.
- **IS 3961** — Cable derating for grouping & ambient conditions.
- **IE Rules 1956** — Statutory clearance, earthing, accessibility.

### 🔑 Critical Formulas (Top 5)
1. $I_{FLC} = \dfrac{P \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \eta \cdot \cos\phi}$
2. $A_{bus,th} = \dfrac{I_{rated}}{J}$
3. $A_{bus,sc} = \dfrac{I_{sc}}{C}$ (C = 159 Cu, 91 Al)
4. $I_p = 2.55 \cdot I_{sc,rms}$
5. $F = \dfrac{2 \cdot I_p^2 \cdot 10^{-7}}{d}$

### 🔑 Design Decision Rules
- **ACB** for incomers > 800 A; **MCCB** < 800 A.
- **DOL starter** ≤ 5 HP (3.7 kW); **Star-Delta** 5–25 HP; **Soft-Starter/VFD** > 25 HP.
- **Current density:** Cu = 1.2 A/mm²; Al = 0.8 A/mm².
- **Form 4b** for critical installations (hospitals, data centres).
- **Voltage drop limits:** Lighting ≤ 3%, Motors ≤ 10% (during start), Power ≤ 5%.

### 🔑 Mandatory Components Checklist
