- ✅ Incomer breaker (ACB/MCCB)
- ✅ Earth busbar (50×6 mm Cu minimum)
- ✅ Neutral busbar (half the phase size)
- ✅ CTs + VTs (for metering)
- ✅ Cable termination compartments
- ✅ Indications: RYB phase presence + Trip healthy
- ✅ Busbar shrouds (IP 20 protection)

### 🔑 Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks)
- ❌ Forgetting **diversity factor** application.
- ❌ Setting **OLR at FLC** in star-delta (must be FLC/√3).
- ❌ Skipping **derating factors** for cables.
- ❌ Conflating **Form 3a vs 3b** or **4a vs 4b**.
- ❌ Using **MCB instead of HRC fuse** for motor feeders.
- ❌ Forgetting **unit conversion** (kW × 1000 in numerator).

> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** *"Compute FLC → Apply Diversity → Size Busbar (thermal + SC) → Verify with Voltage Drop → Select Breakers with proper Form & IP."* Follow this sequence in every design problem.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Bus Bars (CU & Al) and Switchgears
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Selection of Bus Bars (CU & Al) and Switchgears

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bus Bars — Formal Definition

A **bus bar** (also written as *busbar*) is a rigid, highly conductive metallic strip or bar (typically fabricated from **Copper (Cu)** or **Aluminium (Al)**) used in switchgear assemblies, distribution boards, substations, and panel boards to:

- Carry substantial **load currents** (from a few hundred amperes up to several thousand amperes)
- Function as a **common junction point** where multiple incoming and outgoing circuits are interconnected
- Provide a **low-impedance path** for fault currents until the protective device operates

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, bus bars are categorized as the "**backbone conductor**" of any low-tension (LT) or high-tension (HT) switchboard, and their selection is governed by the **current density (δ)** in A/mm² and the **thermal short-circuit withstand capability**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024):** A bus bar is a strip-type conductor of rectangular or tubular cross-section, mechanically supported on insulators, used as a common electrical connection between incoming supply and outgoing feeders in a switchgear panel.

## 1.2 Switchgear — Formal Definition

**Switchgear** is the collective term for all devices used for **switching, controlling, and protecting** electrical circuits and equipment. According to KTU 2024 syllabus, switchgear selection encompasses:

- **MCB** (Miniature Circuit Breaker) — typically ≤ 100 A, 10 kA breaking capacity
- **MCCB** (Moulded Case Circuit Breaker) — 100 A to 1600 A, up to 100 kA
- **ACB** (Air Circuit Breaker) — 630 A to 6300 A, up to 100 kA
- **VCCB / VCB** (Vacuum Circuit Breaker) — HT applications
- **SF₆ CB** (Sulphur Hexafluoride Circuit Breaker) — EHT applications
- **HRC Fuses** (High Rupturing Capacity Fuses)
- **Isolators / Disconnectors**
- **Contactors** (for motor control)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Highlight:** Selection is *not* merely about "rating" — it is a multi-criteria optimization problem involving **(i)** rated current, **(ii)** breaking capacity, **(iii)** making capacity, **(iv)** short-time current withstand, **(v)** discrimination, and **(vi)** discrimination/selectivity with upstream & downstream devices.

## 1.3 Real-World Intuition (Conceptual Analogy)

### 🚍 Analogy — The "Highway vs. Toll Booth" Model

Imagine a **3-lane highway** entering a **toll plaza** with multiple exit gates:

| Electrical Component | Highway Analogy | Function |
|----------------------|-----------------|----------|
| **Bus Bar** | The wide highway approach | Carries all incoming traffic (current) with minimum congestion (voltage drop) |
| **MCB** | A small barrier gate (auto-raise on violation) | Protects one lane (sub-circuit) |
| **MCCB** | A medium security gate | Protects a wing (feeder) |
| **ACB** | The main plaza master gate | Protects the entire plaza (main incomer) |
| **Isolator** | A manual road-divider | Allows maintenance isolation |
| **Breaking Capacity** | Speed of barrier closing | How violently a "crash" can be handled |

> [!TIP]
> If the highway is too **narrow** (small bus bar area) → congestion → heating → melting → fire.  
> If the **toll gate closes too slowly** (low breaking capacity) → the "crash" propagates upstream → blackouts and equipment damage.  
> **This is exactly why selection of bus bars and switchgear is THE most critical exercise in panel design.**

## 1.4 Material Standards for Bus Bars

The two universally accepted bus bar materials and their key physical properties (constants to remember for KTU exams):

| Property | Copper (Cu) | Aluminium (Al) |
|----------|-------------|----------------|
| **Resistivity (ρ) at 20°C** | **1.72 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m** | **2.82 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m** |
| **Conductivity (γ)** | **58 × 10⁶ S/m** | **35 × 10⁶ S/m** |
| **Density** | **8960 kg/m³** | **2700 kg/m³** |
| **Tensile Strength** | **220–250 MPa** | **70–180 MPa** |
| **Current Density (normal)** | **1.5 – 2.0 A/mm²** | **1.0 – 1.5 A/mm²** |
| **Current Density (Bolted fault, 1 sec)** | **159 A/mm²** | **107 A/mm²** |
| **Cost (relative)** | High (~3× Al) | Low |
| **Corrosion Resistance** | Excellent | Good (oxide layer) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Board Mistake:** Students often interchange the "normal" and "fault" current densities. **Normal current density** determines bus bar **cross-section**; **Fault current density (K)** determines **short-time thermal withstand**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bus Bar Selection — The Underlying Logic

Bus bar selection follows a **three-stage logical pyramid**:

### Stage 1 — Continuous Current Rating
Determine the **maximum load current (I_full_load)** that will continuously flow through the bus bar. Apply a **derating factor** for ambient temperature > 40°C and for grouping (multiple bars in parallel).

### Stage 2 — Cross-Sectional Area (Normal Operation)
$$A = \dfrac{I_{FL}}{\delta}$$

where $\delta$ is the permissible current density (A/mm²) selected from IS 8084 / IEC 60439.

### Stage 3 — Thermal Short-Circuit Withstand (Fault Operation)
The bus bar must also withstand the **electromagnetic and thermal stresses** during a short circuit until the protective device clears the fault:

$$I_{sc} = \dfrac{A \cdot K}{\sqrt{t}}$$

where $K$ is the material constant (**159 A·√s/mm² for Cu**, **107 A·√s/mm² for Al**) and $t$ is fault clearance time in seconds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two checks?** A bus bar must satisfy **BOTH**: (i) not overheat during normal operation, AND (ii) not melt or mechanically deform during the worst-case fault. Choosing only the smaller section may pass Stage 2 but fail Stage 3 — **a fatal design error.**

## 2.2 Switchgear Selection — The Five-Criteria Framework

A switchgear device is rated on **five** independent parameters. ALL must be checked:

1. **Rated Normal Current (Iₙ)** — must be ≥ full load current of the protected circuit
2. **Rated Breaking Capacity (I_bc)** — must be ≥ prospective short-circuit current at the point of installation
3. **Rated Making Capacity (I_mc)** — must be ≥ peak value of the first half-cycle of fault current
4. **Rated Short-Time Withstand Current (I_st)** — typically for 1 sec or 3 sec
5. **Discrimination / Selectivity** — must coordinate with upstream and downstream devices

## 2.3 The Step-by-Step Selection Algorithm

```
Step 1: Compute Full-Load Current I_FL of the feeder
Step 2: Compute Maximum Prospective Short-Circuit Current I_sc at the bus
Step 3: Choose switchgear with Iₙ ≥ I_FL × derating
Step 4: Choose switchgear with I_bc ≥ I_sc
Step 5: Check I_mc ≥ 1.414 × I_sc (or 2.55 × I_sc as per IEC)
Step 6: Check I_st (1 sec) ≥ I_sc
Step 7: Verify discrimination with downstream MCB/MCCB
Step 8: Size bus bar with A = I_FL / δ (normal)
Step 9: Verify A ≥ I_sc × √t / K (short-circuit)
Step 10: Select final A = MAX(Step 8, Step 9)
```

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The following table is THE most high-yield reference for KTU ESE. Memorize every row.

| Sl. No. | Quantity | Formula | Constants / Units |
|---------|----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Bus Bar Area (Normal) | $A = I_{FL} / \delta$ | $A$ in mm², $I_{FL}$ in A, $\delta$ in A/mm² |
| 2 | Bus Bar Area (Fault) | $A = I_{sc} \cdot \sqrt{t} / K$ | $t$ in seconds |
| 3 | K (Cu, 1 sec fault) | — | **159 A·√s/mm²** |
| 4 | K (Al, 1 sec fault) | — | **107 A·√s/mm²** |
| 5 | Full-Load Current (3-φ) | $I_{FL} = P / (\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi)$ | $P$ in W, $V_L$ in V |
| 6 | Full-Load Current (1-φ) | $I_{FL} = P / (V \cdot \cos\phi)$ | $P$ in W, $V$ in V |
| 7 | Short-Circuit Current at LV side | $I_{sc} = MVA_{sc} / (\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L) \cdot 10^6$ | $MVA_{sc}$ in MVA |
| 8 | Short-Circuit Current from Transformer | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{KVA_{trf} \cdot 100}{V \cdot \%Z}$ | $\%Z$ = impedance |
| 9 | Peak Making Current | $I_{peak} = 1.414 \cdot I_{sc}$ (asymmetric) or $2.55 \cdot I_{sym}$ | — |
| 10 | Making Capacity | $I_{mc} = 2.55 \cdot I_{bc}$ | IEC 60947 |
| 11 | Derating for grouped bars | $A_{eff} = A \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2$ | $k_1, k_2$ factors |
| 12 | Resistance of Bus Bar | $R = \rho \cdot L / A$ | $\rho$ in Ω·m, $L$ in m |
| 13 | Reactance of Bus Bar | $X = 2\pi f \cdot L \cdot (\mu_0 / 2\pi) \cdot \ln(d/r)$ | $d$ = spacing, $r$ = equivalent radius |
| 14 | Force between bus bars | $F = (\mu_0 / 2\pi) \cdot (i^2 / d) \cdot L$ | N, for short-time peak current $i$ |

## 2.5 Number of Bus Bar Strips — Practical Rules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite Question:** "How many strips of Cu bus bar of 50 mm × 6 mm are required to carry 1600 A?"

| Current Range | Cu Configuration | Al Configuration |
|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| ≤ 200 A | Single strip | Single strip |
| 200 – 630 A | 1 strip | 1 strip |
| 630 – 1250 A | 2 strips | 2 strips |
| 1250 – 2000 A | 2 strips | 3 strips |
| 2000 – 3150 A | 3 strips | 4 strips |
| 3150 – 4000 A | 4 strips | 5 strips |

**Spacing rule:** Strips within one phase must be **separated by the thickness of one strip** (e.g., 6 mm thick → 6 mm gap). **Phase-to-phase spacing:** minimum **12–25 mm** for LT; **more** for higher voltages.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Typical Bus Bar Selection | Typical Switchgear |
|-------------|---------------------------|---------------------|
| **Main LT Panel (MCC)** in industry | 3-phase, 415 V, 2500 A Cu, 100 × 10 mm × 2 strips | 2500 A ACB (Iₙ), 65 kA I_bc |
| **Apartment DB** | 200 A, single strip Cu 40 × 6 mm | 250 A MCCB + outgoing MCBs |
| **HT Switchboard (11 kV)** | Cu 50 × 10 mm, epoxy-cast | VCB, 25 kA breaking |
| **Solar PV Combiner** | Tin-plated Cu, tinned to prevent bimetallic corrosion | DC MCBs / DC fuses |
| **Data Centre UPS Output** | Cu, tinned, with silver-plated joints | 800 A ACB, 65 kA |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bus Bar Cross-Section for Normal Operation

### Problem Statement
A 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz LT switchboard feeds a total load of 200 kW at 0.8 power factor. Determine the cross-sectional area of a copper bus bar carrying this load, using a current density of 1.6 A/mm².

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the full-load line current using the 3-phase power formula.

$$I_{FL} = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi}$$

Substituting the values:

$$I_{FL} = \dfrac{200 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.8}$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the denominator numerically.

$$\sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 1.732 \times 415 \times 0.8 = 574.9$$

**Step 3:** Divide to get the current.

$$I_{FL} = \dfrac{200\,000}{574.9} \approx 347.9 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the cross-sectional area using the current density.

$$A = \dfrac{I_{FL}}{\delta} = \dfrac{347.9}{1.6}$$

$$A \approx 217.4 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 5:** Select a standard commercial size from IS 8084. A standard Cu strip of **40 mm × 6 mm = 240 mm²** (one strip) is acceptable.

> [!TIP]
> **Practice Tip:** Always **round up** to the next standard strip — never round down. A 217.4 mm² requirement cannot be satisfied by a 40 × 5 mm = 200 mm² strip.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Short-Circuit Withstand Capability

### Problem Statement
Verify whether the same **40 × 6 mm Cu bus bar** (240 mm²) selected above can withstand a 3-phase short-circuit current of 25 kA for a fault clearance time of **1 second** by the upstream ACB.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the relevant formula. The bus bar must satisfy the thermal limit:

$$A_{min} = \dfrac{I_{sc} \cdot \sqrt{t}}{K}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the known values:

$$A_{min} = \dfrac{25\,000 \times \sqrt{1}}{159}$$

$$A_{min} = \dfrac{25\,000}{159}$$

$$A_{min} \approx 157.2 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3:** Compare with the available area.

$$A_{available} = 240 \text{ mm}^2 > A_{min} = 157.2 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4:** **Verdict: PASS ✓**

The bus bar is **thermally safe** for the 1-second, 25 kA short-circuit stress. The utilisation factor is:

$$\dfrac{157.2}{240} \times 100\% = 65.5\%$$

This indicates the bus bar has a healthy **34.5% thermal margin**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Some students compute *only* the normal-operation area and *forget* the short-circuit check. **Both checks are mandatory in KTU valuation** — a 7-mark question explicitly asks for BOTH.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Selecting the Switchgear Rating

### Problem Statement
For the switchboard in Derivation 1, design the **main incoming ACB** ratings. The transformer rating is **1000 kVA, 11 kV / 415 V, %Z = 5%**, and the ACB clearance time during short-circuit is 1 second.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the rated full-load current of the transformer on the LV side.

$$I_{FL(trf)} = \dfrac{KVA_{trf}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LV}} \times 1000$$

$$I_{FL(trf)} = \dfrac{1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415} \times 1000 = \dfrac{1\,000\,000}{718.6}$$

$$I_{FL(trf)} \approx 1391 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2:** Choose the next standard ACB rating — **1600 A** ACB.

> Standard ACB ratings: 630, 800, 1000, 1250, **1600**, 2000, 2500, 3200, 4000 A.

**Step 3:** Compute the short-circuit current available at the LV bus.

$$I_{sc} = \dfrac{I_{FL} \times 100}{\%Z}$$

$$I_{sc} = \dfrac{1391 \times 100}{5} = 27\,820 \text{ A} \approx 27.8 \text{ kA}$$

**Step 4:** Choose the next standard breaking capacity — **36 kA** ACB.

> Standard breaking capacities: 25, **36**, 50, 65, 80, 100 kA.

**Step 5:** Compute the making capacity. IEC 60947 stipulates:

$$I_{mc} = 2.55 \times I_{bc} = 2.55 \times 36 = 91.8 \text{ kA}$$

**Step 6:** Compute the peak short-circuit current (asymmetry factor).

$$I_{peak} = 1.414 \times I_{sc} = 1.414 \times 27.8 = 39.3 \text{ kA}$$

**Step 7:** Verify the peak current does not exceed making capacity.

$$I_{mc} = 91.8 \text{ kA} \gg I_{peak} = 39.3 \text{ kA} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 8:** Check the short-time current rating.

$$I_{st}(1 \text{ sec}) \geq 27.8 \text{ kA} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 9:** Final Selection Summary.

| Parameter | Selected ACB Rating |
|-----------|---------------------|
| Rated Current (Iₙ) | **1600 A** |
| Breaking Capacity (I_bc) | **36 kA** |
| Making Capacity (I_mc) | **91.8 kA** |
| Short-Time Current (I_st, 1s) | **36 kA** |
| Poles | 4P (with neutral) |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection Decision Tree

| Parameter | Formula Source | Calculated Value | Standard Selected | Pass/Fail |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|-------------------|-----------|
| Full-Load Current | $P / (\sqrt{3} \cdot V \cdot \cos\phi)$ | 1391 A | 1600 A ACB | ✓ |
| Short-Circuit Current | $I_{FL} \times 100 / \%Z$ | 27.8 kA | 36 kA ACB | ✓ |
| Peak Asymmetric | $1.414 \times I_{sc}$ | 39.3 kA | 91.8 kA making | ✓ |
| Bus Bar (Normal) | $I_{FL} / \delta$ | 217 mm² | 240 mm² (40×6 Cu) | ✓ |
| Bus Bar (Fault) | $I_{sc} \times \sqrt{t} / K$ | 157 mm² | 240 mm² | ✓ |

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Force Between Two Parallel Bus Bars

### Problem Statement
Two parallel Cu bus bars of length 1 m, carrying 25 kA peak short-circuit current, are spaced 50 mm apart. Compute the electromagnetic force between them.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** State the force formula.

$$F = \dfrac{\mu_0}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{i^2}{d} \cdot L$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values, with $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m:

$$F = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7}}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{(25\,000)^2}{0.050} \cdot 1$$

$$F = 2 \times 10^{-7} \cdot \dfrac{6.25 \times 10^{8}}{0.050}$$

$$F = 2 \times 10^{-7} \times 1.25 \times 10^{10}$$

$$F = 2500 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation.

A force of **2500 N** between two small conductors is enormous — sufficient to physically bend or break the bus bar if not adequately braced. This is why **insulators** and **inter-phase supports** are mandatory.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Switchgear Selection Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the complete selection logic from problem statement to final procurement specification.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Load data P, V, cos phi, KVA, Z percent"] --> B["Compute I full load using 3 phi formula"]
    B --> C["Compute I short circuit using Z percent formula"]
    C --> D["Choose ACB MCCB rated current greater than I full load"]
    D --> E["Choose breaking capacity greater than I short circuit"]
    E --> F["Compute making capacity 2.55 times breaking"]
    F --> G["Verify peak current less than making capacity"]
    G --> H{"All checks pass?"}
    H -- "No" --> I["Upsize ACB MCCB or reduce fault level"]
    I --> D
    H -- "Yes" --> J["Compute bus bar area normal A1 equals I divided by delta"]
    J --> K["Compute bus bar area fault A2 equals I sc times sqrt t divided by K"]
    K --> L["Select A final equals max of A1 and A2"]
    L --> M["Choose standard strip size from IS 8084"]
    M --> N["Verify number of strips and spacing"]
    N --> O["Apply derating for temperature and grouping"]
    O --> P["Final selection: Switchgear plus Bus bar issued for procurement"]
```

## 4.2 Bus Bar Arrangement — 3-Phase Strip Layout

The following diagram represents the physical cross-sectional arrangement of a 3-phase, 4-wire LT switchboard bus bar (with neutral).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Phase R: Cu strips 100x10 mm, 2 in parallel"] --> B["Phase Y: Cu strips 100x10 mm, 2 in parallel"]
    B --> C["Phase B: Cu strips 100x10 mm, 2 in parallel"]
    C --> D["Neutral N: Cu strip 50x6 mm, single"]
    A --- E["Insulator supports at 600 mm spacing"]
    B --- E
    C --- E
    D --- E
```

## 4.3 Switchgear Coordination (Discrimination) Schematic

This diagram represents the upstream–downstream protection hierarchy in a typical industrial LT panel.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Transformer 1000 kVA, 11 kV / 415 V"] --> B["Main ACB 1600 A, 36 kA at LV bus"]
    B --> C["Bus Bar 100x10 Cu, 2 strips per phase"]
    C --> D1["Feeder 1: MCCB 400 A, 36 kA"]
    C --> D2["Feeder 2: MCCB 250 A, 25 kA"]
    C --> D3["Feeder 3: MCCB 160 A, 25 kA"]
    D1 --> E1["Sub-feeder MCB 63 A, 10 kA"]
    D1 --> E2["Sub-feeder MCB 32 A, 10 kA"]
    D2 --> E3["Motor starter 100 A"]
    D3 --> E4["Lighting DB 63 A"]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Number of Strips Required

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Total Current I total"] --> B{"I total less than 200 A?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> C["1 strip Cu or Al"]
    B -- "No" --> D{"I total less than 800 A?"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["1 strip, larger cross section"]
    D -- "No" --> F{"I total less than 1600 A?"}
    F -- "Yes" --> G["2 strips per phase"]
    F -- "No" --> H{"I total less than 2500 A?"}
    H -- "Yes" --> I["3 strips per phase"]
    H -- "No" --> J["4 or more strips, increase strip size or add cubicle"]
```

## 4.5 Switchgear Type Selection Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Determine voltage level: LT or HT"] --> B{"Voltage less than 1000 V?"}
    B -- "Yes LT" --> C{"Current less than 100 A?"}
    C -- "Yes" --> D["MCB"]
    C -- "No" --> E{"Current less than 1600 A?"}
    E -- "Yes" --> F["MCCB"]
    E -- "No" --> G["ACB"]
    B -- "No HT" --> H{"Voltage less than 36 kV?"}
    H -- "Yes" --> I["VCB Vacuum Circuit Breaker"]
    H -- "No EHT" --> J["SF6 Circuit Breaker"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "Justify your choice of switchgear," explicitly mention all **five** ratings from Section 2.2. Partial answers (e.g., only rated current) lose up to **3 marks** in a 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a bus bar. Mention any two advantages of using aluminium bus bars over copper.**

**Model Answer:**

A **bus bar** is a rigid strip-type conductor, usually rectangular in cross-section, made of copper or aluminium, used as a common electrical connection point in switchgear assemblies for carrying large currents between incoming supply and outgoing feeders.

**Two advantages of aluminium over copper:**

1. **Lower cost** — Aluminium is approximately **1/3 the cost of copper** per unit weight, making it more economical for high-current bus bars in large installations.
2. **Lower density (2700 kg/m³ vs. 8960 kg/m³)** — Aluminium bus bars are about **1/3 the weight** of equivalent copper bars, easing structural support requirements and reducing overall panel weight.

*(Other valid advantages: better corrosion resistance in certain atmospheres due to protective oxide layer, ease of fabrication due to softness.)*

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between breaking capacity and making capacity of a circuit breaker.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Breaking Capacity (I_bc) | Making Capacity (I_mc) |
|--------|--------------------------|-------------------------|
| **Definition** | Maximum fault current the breaker can **safely interrupt** without damage | Maximum fault current the breaker can **safely close onto** without contact welding |
| **When measured** | During **opening** operation against fault | During **closing** operation against fault |
| **Value** | Symmetric RMS fault current | Approximately **2.55 × I_bc** (per IEC 60947) |
| **Why different?** | — | Because at the instant of closing, the first half-cycle includes an **asymmetric (DC offset) component**, making the peak ~2.55× the symmetric RMS |

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**Question A:** A 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz industrial LT switchboard has the following loads:

- Total connected load: **750 kW**
- Power factor: **0.85 (lagging)**
- Transformer: **1500 kVA, 11 kV / 415 V, %Z = 6%**
- Ambient temperature: **45°C**
- Fault clearance time: **1 second**

**(a)** Determine the **current rating** of the main incoming ACB and **breaking capacity** required. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Select the **copper bus bar** size (number and dimensions of strips per phase) and verify both normal-operation and short-circuit thermal limits. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question A — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Compute full-load current.

$$I_{FL} = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi} = \dfrac{750 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.85}$$

$$\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.85 = 611.0$$

$$I_{FL} = \dfrac{750\,000}{611.0} \approx 1227.5 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2:** Apply derating for 45°C ambient (typical derate factor ≈ 0.94 for class-A insulation).

$$I_{derated} = 1227.5 / 0.94 \approx 1306 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3:** Select standard ACB rating = **1600 A** (next standard above 1306 A).

**Step 4:** Compute transformer full-load current on LV side.

$$I_{trf} = \dfrac{1500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 415} = \dfrac{1\,500\,000}{718.6} \approx 2087 \text{ A}$$

Wait — the ACB is selected to match **load** demand (1227.5 A → 1306 derated → 1600 A), not transformer rating. The transformer can supply up to 2087 A, but the ACB only needs to handle the actual downstream load.

**Step 5:** Compute short-circuit current at LV bus.

$$I_{sc} = \dfrac{I_{trf} \times 100}{\%Z} = \dfrac{2087 \times 100}{6} \approx 34\,783 \text{ A} \approx 34.8 \text{ kA}$$

**Step 6:** Select standard breaking capacity = **36 kA** (next standard above 34.8 kA).

**Step 7:** Making capacity.

$$I_{mc} = 2.55 \times 36 = 91.8 \text{ kA}$$

**Step 8:** Peak asymmetric current.

$$I_{peak} = 1.414 \times I_{sc} = 1.414 \times 34.8 \approx 49.2 \text{ kA} \ll 91.8 \text{ kA} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Selection (ACB):** **1600 A, 36 kA, 4-pole** with thermal-magnetic trip unit.

---

### Model Solution to Question A — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Compute bus bar area from normal operation (δ = 1.6 A/mm² for Cu at 45°C).

$$A_1 = \dfrac{I_{FL}}{\delta} = \dfrac{1306}{1.6} \approx 816 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2:** Compute bus bar area from short-circuit withstand (K = 159 for Cu, 1-sec).

$$A_2 = \dfrac{I_{sc} \cdot \sqrt{t}}{K} = \dfrac{34\,800 \times \sqrt{1}}{159} \approx 218.9 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3:** Select the governing area.

$$A = \max(A_1, A_2) = \max(816, 218.9) = 816 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4:** Select standard Cu strip size.

A single strip of **100 mm × 10 mm = 1000 mm²** ≥ 816 mm² ✓
Alternatively, **2 strips of 60 mm × 8 mm = 960 mm²** per phase.

**Decision: Use 1 strip of 100 × 10 mm Cu per phase** (single strip is preferred for economy and easier support).

**Step 5:** Verify derated current capacity of 100 × 10 mm strip.

Standard rating of 100 × 10 mm Cu strip at 45°C ambient (derated) ≈ **1400 A** ≥ 1306 A ✓

**Final Bus Bar Selection:**
- Material: **Copper (ETP grade, IS 191)**
- Strip size: **100 mm × 10 mm**
- Number per phase: **1**
- Phase-to-phase spacing: **25 mm**
- Support insulators: **DMC, 600 mm spacing**

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**Question B:** A commercial complex LT distribution board has:

- Connected load: **300 kW**
- Voltage: **415 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz**
- Power factor: **0.9**
- Source: **750 kVA transformer, %Z = 5%**
- Available short-circuit level at the upstream HT side: **250 MVA**

**(a)** Calculate the **prospective short-circuit current** at the LV bus and select the **MCCB** rating with proper making capacity. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Determine the **aluminium bus bar** dimensions and verify the design for both normal and short-circuit conditions. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question B — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Compute transformer LV full-load current.

$$I_{trf} = \dfrac{750 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 415} \approx 1043 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2:** Compute short-circuit contribution from transformer.

$$I_{sc(trf)} = \dfrac{1043 \times 100}{5} = 20\,860 \text{ A} \approx 20.9 \text{ kA}$$

**Step 3:** Compute short-circuit contribution from upstream HT network.

$$I_{sc(HT)} = \dfrac{250 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 415} \approx 347\,800 \text{ A} \approx 347.8 \text{ kA}$$

Wait — this value is enormous. But the **actual short-circuit current at LV bus is limited by transformer impedance** because the HT network impedance, when reflected through the transformer, is much higher than the transformer's own impedance. So the practical worst-case is:

$$I_{sc(LV)} \approx \dfrac{I_{trf} \times 100}{\%Z_{total}} \approx 20.9 \text{ kA}$$

**Step 4:** Select MCCB.

- Rated current: $I_{FL} = 300\,000 / (\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.9) \approx 463$ A → Select **630 A MCCB**
- Breaking capacity: 20.9 kA → Select **25 kA MCCB**
- Making capacity: $I_{mc} = 2.55 \times 25 = 63.75$ kA
- Peak current: $I_{peak} = 1.414 \times 20.9 = 29.6$ kA $\ll$ 63.75 kA ✓

**Final MCCB Selection:** **630 A, 25 kA, 3-pole** with electronic trip unit.

---

### Model Solution to Question B — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Normal-operation area for Al bus bar (δ = 1.2 A/mm²).

$$A_1 = \dfrac{463}{1.2} \approx 386 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2:** Short-circuit thermal withstand (K = 107 for Al, 1-sec).

$$A_2 = \dfrac{20\,900 \times 1}{107} \approx 195 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3:** Governing area: $A = \max(386, 195) = 386 \text{ mm}^2$

**Step 4:** Select standard Al strip.

Single strip: **80 mm × 6 mm = 480 mm²** ≥ 386 mm² ✓

Or: **50 mm × 10 mm = 500 mm²** per phase.

**Step 5:** Verify derated rating of 80 × 6 mm Al strip at 45°C.

Standard rating ≈ 630 A ≥ 463 A ✓

**Step 6:** Check phase-to-phase spacing.

For Al, recommended minimum spacing = **15 mm** for 415 V.

**Final Selection:**
- Material: **Aluminium (Al-6101 or Al-6063 alloy)**
- Strip size: **80 mm × 6 mm**
- Number per phase: **1**
- Spacing: **15 mm**
- Insulators: **DMC, 750 mm spacing** (Al has lower mass so wider spacing is permissible)

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks — Bus Bar & Switchgear Selection:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the short-circuit thermal check** (max loss: **5 marks** out of 7). Always verify $A \geq I_{sc} \sqrt{t} / K$.
> 2. **Using wrong current density** — confusing 1.6 A/mm² with 2.0 A/mm², or using the fault value K (159) where normal δ is required.
> 3. **Not applying ambient temperature derating** — at 45°C, the bus bar current rating drops by ~6–8%, which may force a strip upsize.
> 4. **Selecting breaking capacity < I_sc** — a 25 kA MCCB will fail if the bus fault level is 27 kA. **Always select the next standard above** I_sc.
> 5. **Omitting making capacity check** — the IEC mandates $I_{mc} = 2.55 \times I_{bc}$. Many students compute but do not comment.
> 6. **Forgetting to state the standard** — answer loses 1 mark if you don't say "**IS 8084**" or "**IEC 60439**" or "**IS 191**" (for Cu grade).
> 7. **Not drawing the bus bar arrangement** — even a simple 3-phase sketch fetches **1–2 bonus marks** in a 14-mark question.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print and Memorize Before Exam:**

- ☐ **Bus bar** = rigid strip carrying large current; either **Cu (Cu-ETP, IS 191)** or **Al (Al-6101)**
- ☐ **Normal current density (δ):** Cu = **1.6 A/mm²**; Al = **1.2 A/mm²** (typical)
- ☐ **Short-circuit K constant:** Cu = **159 A·√s/mm²**; Al = **107 A·√s/mm²**
- ☐ **Two mandatory checks:** $A_{normal} = I_{FL} / \delta$ AND $A_{fault} = I_{sc} \sqrt{t} / K$ → take the **MAX**
- ☐ **Switchgear 5 parameters:** Rated current, Breaking capacity, Making capacity ($2.55 \times I_{bc}$), Short-time current, Discrimination
- ☐ **Making capacity:** $I_{mc} = 2.55 \times I_{bc}$ (IEC 60947)
- ☐ **Peak asymmetric current:** $I_{peak} = 1.414 \times I_{sc}$
- ☐ **Standard ACB ratings:** 630, 800, 1000, 1250, **1600**, 2000, 2500, 3200, 4000 A
- ☐ **Standard breaking capacities:** 25, 36, 50, 65, 80, 100 kA
- ☐ **3-phase full-load current:** $I_{FL} = P / (\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi)$
- ☐ **Short-circuit current from transformer:** $I_{sc} = I_{FL} \times 100 / \%Z$
- ☐ **Number of strips** per phase: 200 A → 1; 800 A → 1 large; 1600 A → 2; 2500 A → 3
- ☐ **Strip spacing:** within phase = thickness of one strip; phase-to-phase = **12–25 mm** for LT
- ☐ **Insulator material:** **DMC** (Dough Moulding Compound) — most common for LT panels
- ☐ **Standard reference codes:** **IS 8084** (bus bars), **IS 191** (Cu), **IEC 60947** (switchgear), **IS 13947** (Indian equivalent), **IEC 60439** (assemblies)
- ☐ **Force between bus bars during fault:** $F = (\mu_0 / 2\pi)(i^2 / d)L$ — supports must be designed for this
- ☐ **Tin-plating of Cu bus bars** in coastal/humid areas (corrosion protection)
- ☐ **Always specify ambient temperature** in switchgear design — derate above 40°C

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specifications of LT Breakers and Other LT Panel Components (Basics Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Specifications of LT Breakers and Other LT Panel Components (Basics Only) # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Defining LT Switchgear Panels In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCEET602)** context, an **LT (Low Tension) Panel** is a power distribution assembly designed to receive, control, distribute, and protect electrical energy at voltages **up to 1000 V AC (typically 415 V / 240 V, 50 Hz)** as per the **IS 8623 / IEC 61439** standards. The *LT switchgear components* are the constituent devices mounted inside this panel — collectively called the **Switchgear Assembly** — that ensure safe power flow from the transformer secondary (or generator) to the downstream loads. > [!IMPORTANT] > **LT vs HT Boundary (KTU Convention)** > Any system operating **≤ 1000 V AC** is classified as **LT (Low Tension)**. Everything above this — typically 11 kV, 22 kV, 66 kV — falls under **HT (High Tension)**. The "Tension" is the old European term for "Voltage." ### Intuitive Analogy — "The Building's Electrical Security Gate" Imagine a large multi-storey office building receiving its main water supply: - The **incoming HT line (11 kV)** is the massive municipal pipeline. - A **transformer** steps this down to a usable pressure (415 V LT). - The **LT Panel** acts like a **central security and distribution hub** at the building's basement. Just as a building needs gates, locks, security cameras, and individual apartment meters, the LT panel needs: - **Breakers** = Security gates (stop the flow during fault) - **Busbars** = Main water riser (carries current to all floors) - **Meters & CTs** = Water usage meters - **Indicating Lamps** = Status indicator lights - **Cable Glands** = Sealed pipe entries Without the LT panel, electricity would flow chaotically — short circuits, overloads, and human contact would cause fires, equipment damage, and electrocution. --- ## 1.2 Core LT Panel Components — Overview The major components grouped inside any standard LT panel (per **IS 8623-1, IS 13947, IEC 61439-1**) are: | S.No | Component | Primary Function | |------|-----------|------------------| | 1 | **LT Circuit Breakers** (MCB, MCCB, ACB, RCCB) | Automatic fault interruption & switching | | 2 | **HRC Fuses** | Back-up short-circuit protection | | 3 | **Busbars** (Copper/Aluminium) | Common current carrier for distribution | | 4 | **Current Transformers (CTs)** | Step down current for metering & protection | | 5 | **Potential Transformers (PTs)** | Step down voltage for metering & protection | | 6 | **Indicating Meters** (V, A, kW, kWh, PF, Hz) | Monitoring of electrical parameters | | 7 | **Indicating Lamps** (R, Y, B) | Status indication of breaker ON/OFF/Trip | | 8 | **Selector Switches** | Manual selection of meter/control circuits | | 9 | **Protective Relays** | Logic device for tripping breakers | | 10 | **Cable Glands** | Sealed cable entry into the panel | | 11 | **Enclosures** (IP42 / IP54) | Mechanical & environmental protection | | 12 | **Isolators / Disconnectors** | Visible isolation for maintenance | > [!NOTE] > The KTU 2024 syllabus of *Electrical System Design and Estimation* (PCEET602) requires the student to understand the **purpose, ratings, and selection criteria** of these components. Detailed constructional design is out of scope — this module focuses on **specification reading and selection** for estimation work. --- ## 1.3 Why "Specifications" Matter in Estimation When preparing a **Detailed Estimate** (as required in KTU Module 4), the engineer must read datasheets and select the *correct* component. The **specification sheet** provides: - **Electrical ratings** — voltage, current, frequency, short-circuit withstand - **Mechanical ratings** — IP protection, mounting, dimensions, weight - **Standards compliance** — IS, IEC, BS reference numbers - **Operating characteristics** — trip curves, breaking capacity, selectivity > [!IMPORTANT] > A wrong specification in a tender estimate can lead to **over-specification (costly tender loss)** or **under-specification (unsafe installation, contract penalty, re-tender)**. Mastering specifications is a **core employability skill** for Electrical Estimation Engineers. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 LT Circuit Breakers — Detailed Specifications ### 2.1.1 MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker) - **Standards**: IS 8828 / IEC 60898 - **Rated Voltage**: 240 V AC (single-pole), 415 V AC (3-phase) - **Rated Current ($I_n$)**: 0.5 A to 125 A (common: 6, 10, 16, 20, 25, 32, 40, 63 A) - **Number of Poles**: SP, SP+N, TP, TP+N, 4P - **Breaking Capacity ($I_{cn}$ or $I_{cs}$)**: 4.5 kA, 6 kA, 10 kA, 15 kA - **Trip Curves (B, C, D, K)**: - **Type B**: 3–5 $I_n$ — residential, lighting - **Type C**: 5–10 $I_n$ — small motors, fluorescent loads - **Type D**: 10–20 $I_n$ — transformers, X-ray loads - **Type K**: 8–12 $I_n$ — heavy motor starting - **Mounting**: DIN rail (35 mm) - **IP**: IP20 (finger-safe) ### 2.1.2 MCCB (Moulded Case Circuit Breaker) - **Standards**: IS 13947-2 / IEC 60947-2 - **Rated Current**: 16 A to 1600 A (common frame sizes: 100, 250, 400, 630, 800, 1000, 1250, 1600 A) - **Rated Voltage**: 415 V / 690 V AC - **Breaking Capacity ($I_{cu}$ / $I_{cs}$)**: 10 kA, 25 kA, 35 kA, 50 kA, 65 kA, 100 kA - **Trip Unit**: Thermal-Magnetic (TMD) or Microprocessor-based Electronic Trip Unit (ETU) - **Adjustable Settings**: $0.4$–$1.0 \times I_n$ (overload), $2$–$10 \times I_n$ (short-circuit) - **Poles**: 3P or 4P ### 2.1.3 ACB (Air Circuit Breaker) - **Standards**: IS 13947-2 / IEC 60947-2 - **Rated Current**: 630 A to 6300 A - **Rated Voltage**: 415 V / 690 V AC - **Breaking Capacity**: 50 kA, 65 kA, 80 kA, 100 kA - **Withstand Capacity ($I_{cw}$)** for 1 s or 3 s - **Operating Mechanism**: Manual, Motorised, or Stored-energy spring - **Withdrawable / Draw-out type** for LT switchboards ### 2.1.4 RCCB / ELCB (Residual Current Circuit Breaker) - **Standards**: IS 12640 / IEC 61008 - **Sensitivity ($I_{\Delta n}$)**: 30 mA (life-saving), 100 mA, 300 mA, 500 mA - **Rated Current**: 16 A to 125 A - **Poles**: 2P, 4P - **Does NOT provide overcurrent protection** — must be paired with MCB > [!NOTE] > **RCCB vs RCBO**: An **RCBO (Residual Current Breaker with Overcurrent)** combines RCCB + MCB in one unit. It provides earth-leakage, overload, and short-circuit protection in a single device. --- ## 2.2 Busbars — Specifications Busbars carry current from the incoming breaker to outgoing feeders. Specifications include: - **Material**: **Copper (Cu)** — high conductivity, used in critical panels; **Aluminium (Al)** — cost-effective for larger panels - **Cross-section**: Rectangular (typical: 25×5 mm, 40×10 mm, 100×10 mm) or **D-shaped** (for higher rating) - **Current Density ($J$)**: - Copper: 1.2–1.6 A/mm² - Aluminium: 0.8–1.0 A/mm² - **Rated Current ($I_b$)**: 100 A to 6300 A - **Short-Time Withstand ($I_{cw}$)**: 25 kA / 1 s, 50 kA / 1 s, 65 kA / 1 s, 80 kA / 1 s - **Colour Coding (per IS 11353)**: - R — Red — Phase R - Y — Yellow — Phase Y - B — Blue — Phase B - Black — Neutral - Green-Yellow — Earth --- ## 2.3 Current Transformers (CTs) — Specifications - **Standard**: IS 2705 / IEC 61869-2 - **CT Ratio ($K_n$)**: e.g., **200/5 A**, **400/5 A**, **1000/5 A** - **Burden (VA)**: 5 VA, 10 VA, 15 VA, 30 VA - **Accuracy Class**: - **Metering**: 0.2, 0.5, 1.0 (IS 0.5 = ±0.5% error at rated current) - **Protection**: 5P, 10P, PS - **Short-Time Current ($I_{th}$)**: e.g., 25 kA for 1 s - **Insulation Level**: 1.1 kV / 3 kV (BIL) - **Type**: Bar-primary, Wound, Ring, Window type > [!IMPORTANT] > A CT must **never be operated with its secondary open-circuited** while the primary is live. This causes dangerously high secondary voltages (up to several kV) — a risk of insulation breakdown, fire, or fatal electrocution. Always **short the secondary** before disconnecting any instrument. --- ## 2.4 Other LT Panel Components ### 2.4.1 HRC Fuses (High Rupturing Capacity) - **IS 2086 / IEC 60269** - **Rating**: e.g., 63 A, 100 A, 200 A, 400 A - **Breaking Capacity**: 80 kA to 120 kA - **Fusing Factor**: 1.1 to 1.6 - **Type**: DIN-type blade, Bolted, NH-type - **Application**: Back-up protection for MCB/MCCB, capacitor bank protection ### 2.4.2 Indicating Lamps (LED type, modern) - **Voltage**: 110 V AC, 240 V AC, 415 V AC - **Colour Code**: Red (Breaker ON), Green (Breaker OFF), Amber/Yellow (Trip), Blue (Auto), White (Test) ### 2.4.3 Selector Switches - **Type**: 2-position, 3-position, maintained or spring-return - **Rating**: 6 A / 240 V AC typical - **Application**: Manual/Off/Auto (M-O-A), Ammeter-Voltmeter selection, Local/Remote control ### 2.4.4 Cable Glands - **Material**: Brass (preferred), Aluminium, Nylon - **Types**: Single compression, Double compression, BW type, CW type - **Size**: Match cable outer diameter, e.g., for 4-core 16 mm² armoured cable → **G2 (BS) / CGL 22 (Indian)** - **Standard**: BS 6121 ### 2.4.5 Enclosures - **IP Code (per IS/IEC 60529)**: - **IP42** — Indoor general purpose (prevents tools >1 mm + drips) - **IP54** — Dust-protected + splash-proof - **IP65** — Dust-tight + water jets - **Form of Separation** (per IEC 61439-2): Form 1, 2a, 2b, 3a, 3b, 4a, 4b - **Material**: CRCA powder-coated steel, SS-304, SS-316 --- ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Unit | Application | |---|--------------------|----------|------|-------------| | 1 | Rated Breaking Capacity of Breaker | $I_{cu} = \dfrac{\text{MVA}_{sc}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \times 10^{-3}}$ | kA | Selection of MCB/MCCB/ACB | | 2 | Short-circuit Current at LT Bus | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{eq}}$ | kA | Coordination with breaker $I_{cu}$ | | 3 | Prospective Short-Circuit Current | $I_{pscc} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \cdot (Z_s + Z_c)}$ | kA | Discrimination study | | 4 | Let-Through Energy (Energy Limitation) | $I^2t \le K^2 S^2$ | A²s | Cable protection coordination | | 5 | CT Secondary Current | $I_s = \dfrac{K_n \cdot I_p}{K_n \text{ ratio}}$ | A | Metering calculation | | 6 | CT Ratio (Transformation Ratio) | $K_n = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{I_{p(\text{prim})}}{I_{s(\text{sec})}}$ | — | CT selection | | 7 | Total Burden of CT | $B_{total} = \sum B_i + (L \cdot R_{loop})$ | VA | CT accuracy verification | | 8 | Busbar Cross-section | $A = \dfrac{I_b}{J}$ | mm² | Busbar sizing | | 9 | Voltage Drop (3-phase) | $\Delta V\% = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)}{V_L \times 10}$ | % | Cable-busbar coordination | | 10 | Discrimination Ratio (Discriminative) | $I_{sc(2)} \le 0.8 \cdot I_{inst(1)}$ | — | Breaker-backup coordination | | 11 | Earth-Fault Loop Impedance | $Z_s = Z_e + (R_1 + R_2)$ | Ω | RCCB/ELCB discrimination | | 12 | Total Connected Load | $P_t = \sum P_i \times D_f$ | kW | Panel Incomer rating | > [!NOTE] > **K — Cable Constant** in Formula #4 is material-dependent: Cu = **115**, Al = **76**, XLPE = **143**, PVC = **115**. Memorise this for KTU problems. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility In an actual LT Panel Estimation: - A **BEMS (Building Energy Management System)** contractor or **Turnkey Electrical Contractor** must quote a panel with components matching the **fault level** at site. - The **utility (KSEB)** specifies the **short-circuit MVA at the incoming 11 kV** — typically **250 MVA** for Kerala. This determines the ACB's $I_{cu}$. - For **breaker selection**, the designer ensures $I_{cu} \ge I_{sc(max)}$ at the bus. - For **CT selection**, the rule is: primary current ≈ 1.2 × full-load current of the circuit being metered. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Short-Circuit Current at LT Bus (Fault MVA Method) This is the **single most important calculation** in LT breaker selection. The KTU board expects this derivation in long-answer questions. ### Problem Statement A **1000 kVA, 11 kV / 415 V** transformer with **5.5% impedance** is supplied by a system with **fault level of 250 MVA at 11 kV bus**. Calculate the **prospective short-circuit current** at the LT bus. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Calculate the transformer impedance ($Z_T$) in pu** The percentage impedance $Z_T(\%)$ is given on the transformer nameplate. $$ Z_T(pu) = \dfrac{Z_T(\%)}{100} = \dfrac{5.5}{100} = 0.055 \text{ pu} $$ **Step 2 — Express source fault level in pu on transformer's own base** We need a common MVA base. Choose base $S_{base} = 1 \text{ MVA}$. Source impedance on this base: $$ Z_{source}(pu) = \dfrac{S_{base}}{S_{fault}} = \dfrac{1}{250} = 0.004 \text{ pu} $$ **Step 3 — Convert transformer impedance to the same base (1 MVA)** $$ Z_T(1MVA) = 0.055 \times \dfrac{S_{base}}{S_{TXF}} = 0.055 \times \dfrac{1}{1.0} = 0.055 \text{ pu} $$ **Step 4 — Total equivalent impedance referred to 1 MVA base** $$ Z_{eq} = Z_{source} + Z_T = 0.004 + 0.055 = 0.059 \text{ pu} $$ **Step 5 — Compute Full-Load Current of Transformer at 415 V** $$ I_{FL} = \dfrac{S \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L} = \dfrac{1000 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 415} $$ $$ I_{FL} = \dfrac{1000000}{718.85} = 1390.8 \text{ A} $$ **Step 6 — Compute the Short-Circuit Current at LT Bus** By definition, on a per-unit system, the fault current in per-unit is $1 / Z_{eq}$. $$ I_{sc}(pu) = \dfrac{1}{Z_{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{0.059} = 16.949 \text{ pu} $$ Therefore, the actual fault current at the LT bus: $$ I_{sc} = I_{FL} \times I_{sc}(pu) = 1390.8 \times 16.949 $$ $$ I_{sc} = 23,580 \text{ A} = 23.58 \text{ kA} $$ **Step 7 — Select the ACB/MCCB** The chosen ACB must have: $$ I_{cu} \ge I_{sc} \implies I_{cu} \ge 23.58 \text{ kA} $$ We select a standard **MCCB with $I_{cu} = 25 \text{ kA}$** (or higher, e.g., 35 kA for safety margin). > [!IMPORTANT] > In practice, the cable impedance between transformer and panel is also added. For a 5 m run, the cable impedance (~1.5 mΩ) is **negligible compared to the transformer impedance** and is usually ignored for estimation purposes. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Selection Rules for Common LT Components | S.No | Circuit Type | Recommended Breaker | Trip Curve / Class | Typical $I_{cu}$ | CT Ratio (if metering) | |------|--------------|---------------------|--------------------|------------------|------------------------| | 1 | Domestic lighting (230 V, < 5 A) | MCB SP 6–10 A | Type B | 6 kA / 10 kA | Not required | | 2 | Domestic socket (16 A / 6 A) | MCB SP 16/6 A + RCCB 30 mA | Type C | 6 kA | Not required | | 3 | Office AC units (1.5 TR split) | MCB TP 20 A | Type C | 10 kA | Not required | | 4 | Lift motor 10 kW | MCCB 63 A 3P + Overload relay | Type D | 25 kA | 100/5 A | | 5 | Lift motor 30 kW | MCCB 100 A 3P | — | 35 kA | 150/5 A | | 6 | Main LT incomer (1000 kVA TXF) | ACB 1600 A 4P | Microprocessor | 50 kA | 1600/5 A (metering) + 2000/5 A (protection) | | 7 | Capacitor bank 50 kVAr | MCCB 100 A 3P + HRC fuse | Type C | 35 kA | 100/5 A | | 8 | Hospital OT supply | RCCB 30 mA + Isolator + MCB | Type B | 6 kA | 50/5 A | | 9 | Solar inverter AC output | MCB TP 32 A | Type C | 10 kA | 50/5 A | | 10 | EV charging station (7.4 kW) | MCB TP 40 A + RCCB 30 mA Type A | Type C | 10 kA | 60/5 A | > [!NOTE] > **EV chargers and VFD-driven loads produce DC residual currents**. Standard Type AC RCCBs may not trip — for these applications, **Type A or Type B RCCBs** are mandatory (per IEC 62955). --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Selection of Busbar Cross-Section ### Problem A 1600 A MCCB feeds a busbar. Choose the cross-section if material is **Copper** with $J = 1.5 \text{ A/mm}^2$. ### Solution **Step 1** — Use Formula #8 from the Formula Sheet: $$ A = \dfrac{I_b}{J} = \dfrac{1600}{1.5} = 1066.7 \text{ mm}^2 $$ **Step 2** — Select standard section: Use **2 × 100 mm × 10 mm** = $2 \times 1000 = 2000 \text{ mm}^2$. > **Actual current density** = $\dfrac{1600}{2000} = 0.8 \text{ A/mm}^2$ — safely within the $1.2$–$1.6 \text{ A/mm}^2$ range for Cu. **Step 3** — Verify short-time thermal withstand: For $I_{cw} = 50 \text{ kA}$ for 1 s, the busbar must satisfy: $$ A_{min} = \dfrac{I_{cw}}{\sqrt{K}} \cdot \sqrt{t} $$ where $K = 143$ for Cu. (thermal constant per IS 11353) $$ A_{min} = \dfrac{50000}{\sqrt{143}} \cdot \sqrt{1} = \dfrac{50000}{11.96} = 4180 \text{ mm}^2 $$ Therefore, **two parallel runs of $100 \times 10$ mm** = $2000 \text{ mm}^2$ is insufficient for 50 kA/1 s. We need **two parallel runs of 100 × 25 mm** = $5000 \text{ mm}^2$ or similar. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of an LT Power Control Centre (PCC) This Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional interactions** of every LT component inside a typical industrial LT panel — from incomer to outgoing feeder. ```mermaid graph TD A[Transformer 11kV / 415V
1000 kVA] --> B[Main Incomer ACB
1600 A, 50 kA] B --> C[Main Copper Busbar
2 x 100x10 mm] C --> D[Outgoing MCCB 1
400 A, 35 kA] C --> E[Outgoing MCCB 2
250 A, 35 kA] C --> F[Outgoing MCCB 3
160 A, 25 kA] C --> G[Capacitor Bank MCCB
200 A, 35 kA] D --> D1[CT 400/5 A
Class 0.5 Metering] D --> D2[Digital Ammeter] D --> D3[Energy Meter kWh] E --> E1[CT 250/5 A
Class 1.0] E --> E2[Motor Feeder 3Ph
VFD Controlled] F --> F1[CT 150/5 A
Class 0.5] F --> F2[Distribution Board Feed] G --> G1[CT 250/5 A] G --> G2[APFC Relay 8 Stage] B --> H[PT 415/110V
Class 1.0] H --> H1[Voltmeter] H --> H2[Power Factor Meter] B --> I[Indicating Lamps
R-ON G-OFF Y-TRIP] B --> J[Selector Switch M-O-A] B --> K[RCCB 4P 300 mA
Main Earth Fault] K --> L[Earth Electrode
GI Pipe 40 mm] B --> M[Panel Enclosure
IP54 Form 3b] M --> M1[Cable Glands
Brass Double Compression] M --> M2[Ventilation Louvers
with IP Filter] ``` > [!NOTE] > The blocks labelled `D1`, `E1`, `F1`, `G1` are CTs — they **must always be loaded** (with meters, relays, or shorting links) on the secondary side. An **open-circuited CT secondary** is a fatal hazard. --- ## 4.2 Functional Flow of a Circuit Breaker Trip Logic This diagram shows the **decision flow** of how an MCCB detects and clears a fault. ```mermaid graph TD Start[Current flows through MCCB] --> S1{Sensor Type?} S1 -->|Thermal-Magnetic| S2[Bi-metallic Strip
Overload Detection] S1 -->|Electronic Trip Unit| S3[Magnetic CT
Short Circuit Detection] S2 --> Q1{Overload > 1.3 In?} Q1 -->|Yes| T1[Bi-metallic Bends] T1 --> T2[Trip Mechanism Fires
Time: Seconds to Minutes] Q1 -->|No| N1[Continue Normal Flow] S3 --> Q2{Isc > 8 In?} Q2 -->|Yes| T3[Solenoid Pulls Plunger] T3 --> T4[Instantaneous Trip
Time: < 10 ms] Q2 -->|No| N2[Continue Normal Flow] S1 --> S4[Magnetic CT for SC] S4 --> Q3{I > 2 to 10 In adjustable?} Q3 -->|Yes| T5[Short-Time Delay Trip
0.05 to 0.5 s] Q3 -->|No| N3[Continue Normal Flow] T2 --> End[Contacts Open
Arc Extinguished
in Arc Chute] T4 --> End T5 --> End ``` --- ## 4.3 Block Diagram — IP Protection Classification ```mermaid graph LR A[IP Rating] --> B[First Digit
Solid Particle Protection] A --> C[Second Digit
Water Protection] B --> B1[0 - No Protection] B --> B2[1 - Hand > 50 mm] B --> B3[2 - Finger > 12.5 mm] B --> B4[3 - Tools > 2.5 mm] B --> B5[4 - Wire > 1 mm] B --> B6[5 - Dust Protected] B --> B7[6 - Dust Tight] C --> C1[0 - No Protection] C --> C2[1 - Vertical Drip] C --> C3[2 - 15 deg Tilt Drip] C --> C4[3 - Spray] C --> C5[4 - Splash] C --> C6[5 - Water Jet] C --> C7[6 - Powerful Jet] C --> C8[7 - Temporary Immersion] C --> C9[8 - Continuous Submersion] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **IP54** is the most common rating for **indoor industrial LT panels** in Kerala. For **outdoor panels** (e.g., a feeder pillar near a transformer), **IP55 or IP65** is specified. --- ## 4.4 Hierarchical Component Map of an LT Panel ```mermaid graph TD A[LT Panel PCC] --> B[Mechanical Section] A --> C[Electrical Section] A --> D[Protection Section] A --> E[Metering Section] B --> B1[Enclosure IP54] B --> B2[Cable Glands] B --> B3[Busbar Supports SMC] B --> B4[Ventilation Fans] C --> C1[ACB / MCCB / MCB] C --> C2[Isolators] C --> C3[Contactors] C --> C4[Busbars Cu/Al] C --> C5[Power Cable Termination] D --> D1[O/L Relay] D --> D2[Earth Fault Relay] D --> D3[Differential Relay] D --> D4[Surge Protection Device] D --> D5[HRC Fuses] E --> E1[CTs Metering and Protection] E --> E2[PTs 415 to 110V] E --> E3[Voltmeter] E --> E4[Ammeter] E --> E5[Multifunction Meter] E --> E6[Indicating Lamps] E --> E7[Selector Switch] ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *List any six specifications that must be checked before selecting an MCCB for an industrial LT panel.* **Model Answer** (3 marks — approx 50 words): 1. **Rated Current ($I_n$)** — must exceed full-load current of the protected circuit. 2. **Breaking Capacity ($I_{cu}$)** — must be ≥ prospective short-circuit current at the bus. 3. **Number of Poles** — 3P for 3-phase, 4P for 3-phase with switched neutral. 4. **Rated Voltage** — must match or exceed system voltage (415 V/690 V AC). 5. **Trip Unit Type** — Thermal-Magnetic (TMD) or Microprocessor-based (ETU). 6. **Mounting Type** — Fixed or Draw-out (withdrawable). 7. **Standards Compliance** — IS 13947-2 / IEC 60947-2. (Any six correct points = 3 marks; partial credit for 4–5 points) --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *What is the significance of "trip curve" in an MCB? Name the standard trip curves and state their typical applications.* **Model Answer** (3 marks): A **trip curve** is a graph of trip time vs. multiple of rated current that defines when an MCB will disconnect during an overcurrent. It is significant because it ensures the MCB **does not nuisance-trip during normal inrush currents** (e.g., motor starting, transformer energising), while still protecting against sustained overloads. | Trip Curve | Tripping Range | Application | |------------|----------------|-------------| | **Type B** | 3–5 $I_n$ | Pure resistive — residential lighting, heating | | **Type C** | 5–10 $I_n$ | Small motors, fluorescent lamps, inductive loads | | **Type D** | 10–20 $I_n$ | Heavy inrush — transformers, X-ray, welding | (Any correct application earns 1 mark; complete table = 3 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU Pattern Note**: Each Part B question is internally divided as (a) **7 marks** + (b) **7 marks**. The student is given an **internal choice** between two full questions. Both Question A and Question B are presented below as alternatives. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3 Modified]** **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of an Air Circuit Breaker (ACB)**. List the **key specifications** of an ACB used as a 1600 A main incomer in a 1000 kVA LT panel. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution — Part (a)**: *Construction* (3 marks): An ACB consists of: - **Arc chutes** with steel splitter plates for arc extinction - **Main contacts** (fixed and moving) of copper with silver alloy tips - **Stored-energy spring mechanism** for fast opening/closing - **Microprocessor-based trip unit** (ETU) for adjustable protection - **Manual/Motorised operating mechanism** - **Draw-out cassette** for withdrawal without cable disconnection *Working* (2 marks): When a fault occurs, the ETU senses the abnormal current via built-in CTs and sends a trip command to the **shunt trip coil**. The latch releases, and the **stored-energy spring** snaps the main contacts apart within **< 50 ms**. The arc is driven into the **arc chute** by magnetic force, split into multiple smaller arcs by the steel plates, and finally extinguished at the **first current zero** after contact separation. *Key Specifications* (2 marks): - **Rated current**: 1600 A - **Breaking capacity ($I_{cu}$)**: 50 kA or 65 kA at 415 V - **Short-time withstand ($I_{cw}$)**: 50 kA for 1 s - **Rated voltage**: 415 V AC - **Number of poles**: 4P (3 phases + neutral) - **Operating mechanism**: Motorised stored-energy, spring-charged - **Trip unit**: Microprocessor ETU with adjustable overload ($0.4$–$1.0 \times I_n$) and short-circuit ($2$–$10 \times I_n$) settings - **Standard**: IS 13947-2 / IEC 60947-2 --- **(b)** A **500 kVA, 11 kV / 415 V, 50 Hz** transformer has **6% impedance** and is supplied by a system with **short-circuit level of 150 MVA at 11 kV**. Determine the **prospective short-circuit current at the LT bus** and select a suitable **MCCB rating** with proper justification. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution — Part (b)**: **Step 1 — Transformer full-load current** $$ I_{FL} = \dfrac{500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 415} = \dfrac{500000}{718.85} = 695.5 \text{ A} $$ **Step 2 — Impedances in pu (1 MVA base)** Source impedance: $$ Z_{source} = \dfrac{1}{150} = 0.00667 \text{ pu} $$ Transformer impedance (on 1 MVA base): $$ Z_T = 0.06 \times \dfrac{1}{0.5} = 0.12 \text{ pu} $$ **Step 3 — Total impedance** $$ Z_{eq} = 0.00667 + 0.12 = 0.12667 \text{ pu} $$ **Step 4 — Short-circuit current** $$ I_{sc} = \dfrac{I_{FL}}{Z_{eq}} = \dfrac{695.5}{0.12667} = 5491 \text{ A} \approx 5.49 \text{ kA} $$ **Step 5 — Add motor contribution (practical consideration)** For LT busbar, add 20% for motor contribution: $$ I_{sc(\text{design})} = 5.49 \times 1.2 = 6.59 \text{ kA} $$ **Step 6 — MCCB Selection** Choose **MCCB with $I_n = 800$ A** (next standard size above $695.5$ A) and **breaking capacity $I_{cu} \ge 10$ kA**. Final selection: **MCCB 800 A, 3P, 10 kA, with thermal-magnetic trip unit**, or for higher safety, **MCCB 800 A, 25 kA**. | Item | Selected | |------|----------| | Frame size | 800 A | | Rated current | 800 A (≥ 695.5 A) | | Breaking capacity | 10 kA (≥ 6.59 kA) | | Trip unit | Thermal-magnetic (TMD) | | Mounting | Fixed type | --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 3 Modified]** **(a)** Explain the **construction and working of a Current Transformer (CT)**. List **six important specifications** that determine its selection for an LT panel. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution — Part (a)**: *Construction* (3 marks): A Current Transformer is an **instrument transformer** designed to step down high primary current to a standardised **5 A or 1 A secondary current** for metering/protection. It consists of: - **Primary winding** (1 turn — bar primary or few turns) - **Magnetic core** (silicon steel or CRGO) - **Secondary winding** (many turns) - **Burden** — the impedance of connected meters, relays, and leads - **Insulation** — epoxy resin or oil-impregnated paper *Working* (2 marks): The CT operates on the **principle of transformer action** with the primary connected in series with the power line. The **primary MMF ($N_1 I_1$)** is balanced by the **secondary MMF ($N_2 I_2$)**, and the ratio $I_1 / I_2 = N_2 / N_1$ is approximately constant as long as the core is **not saturated**. The connected burden (VA) is what controls the secondary voltage and accuracy. *Six Key Specifications* (2 marks): 1. **Ratio** (e.g., 400/5 A) 2. **Burden** (VA rating, e.g., 15 VA) 3. **Accuracy class** (0.5 for metering, 5P10 for protection) 4. **Short-time current** ($I_{th}$, e.g., 25 kA/1 s) 5. **Insulation level** (1.1 kV or 3 kV BIL) 6. **Type** — bar-primary / wound / ring 7. **Standard** — IS 2705 / IEC 61869-2 --- **(b)** Compare **MCB, MCCB, and ACB** in a tabulated form with respect to **current range, breaking capacity, trip mechanism, applications, and standards**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution — Part (b)**: | Parameter | MCB | MCCB | ACB | |-----------|-----|------|-----| | **Current Range** | 0.5 – 125 A | 16 – 1600 A | 630 – 6300 A | | **Rated Voltage** | 240/415 V | 415/690 V | 415/690 V | | **Breaking Capacity** | 4.5 – 15 kA | 10 – 100 kA | 50 – 100 kA | | **Trip Mechanism** | Thermal + fixed magnetic | Thermal-magnetic or Electronic ETU | Microprocessor ETU (always) | | **Trip Curve** | B, C, D, K | Adjustable | Fully programmable | | **Poles** | SP, TP, 4P | 3P, 4P | 3P, 4P | | **Mounting** | DIN rail | Panel/Plug-in/Draw-out | Fixed/Draw-out | | **Adjustment** | None (fixed settings) | Yes — overload & SC | Fully adjustable + communication | | **Application** | Domestic, small commercial | Industrial feeders, motors | Main incomer of large PCC, HT-LT substations | | **Standard** | IS 8828 / IEC 60898 | IS 13947-2 / IEC 60947-2 | IS 13947-2 / IEC 60947-2 | | **Cost Index** | Low | Medium | High | (Any 7 distinguishing points with correct values = 7 marks) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Marks Lost** > 1. Students **forget to add motor contribution** (typically 20%) to the short-circuit calculation — leads to under-sized breaker. > 2. Students **select $I_n$ exactly equal to full-load current** instead of 1.25× FLI — boards expect 25% margin. > 3. In CT questions, students **never mention shorting the secondary** — even a one-line note on "open secondary hazard" earns a bonus mark. > 4. Confusion between **$I_{cu}$ and $I_{cs}$**: $I_{cs}$ is the *service* breaking capacity (typically 50–75% of $I_{cu}$). Boards often test this. > 5. Students **forget IP rating** for outdoor panels — outdoor LT panels require **IP55 minimum**, indoor can be **IP42 or IP54**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print this for last-minute prep** - **LT = ≤ 1000 V AC** (415 V, 240 V at 50 Hz in India). - **MCB** (≤125 A, 6–10 kA) → residential/commercial; trip curves **B, C, D, K**. - **MCCB** (16–1600 A, 10–100 kA) → industrial feeders; has **adjustable trip unit**. - **ACB** (630–6300 A, 50–100 kA) → main LT incomer; **stored-energy mechanism**. - **RCCB** protects against **earth leakage only** (sensitivities 30, 100, 300, 500 mA); needs a **paired MCB** for overcurrent. - **RCBO = RCCB + MCB in one body** — compact, costlier. - **CT ratio** written as **primary/secondary** (e.g., 400/5 A); secondary is **always 5 A (or 1 A)**. - **CT secondary must NEVER be open-circuited** — high voltage hazard. - **CT accuracy classes**: **0.2 / 0.5 / 1.0** for metering; **5P10 / 10P10 / PS** for protection. - **Busbar** colour: **Red, Yellow, Blue** (phases R, Y, B); **Black** (neutral); **Green-Yellow** (earth). - **Cu current density**: 1.2–1.6 A/mm²; **Al**: 0.8–1.0 A/mm². - **IP54** for indoor industrial; **IP65** for outdoor; **IP42** for commercial indoor. - **Standards to remember**: IS 8828 (MCB), IS 13947-2 (MCCB/ACB), IS 2705 (CT), IS 11353 (Busbar colour), IS 8623 / IEC 61439 (Panel assembly), IS/IEC 60529 (IP), IEC 60269 (HRC fuse). - **Short-circuit formula**: $I_{sc} = \dfrac{I_{FL}}{Z_{eq}(pu)}$ — always add **20% motor contribution** at LT bus. - **Breaker rule**: $I_{cu} \ge I_{sc(max)}$ and $I_n \ge 1.25 \times I_{FL}$ of the protected circuit. - **Cable gland material**: **Brass** is preferred for armoured cables in LT panels. - **Forms of separation** (IEC 61439-2): Form 1 (no separation) → Form 4b (maximum — every functional unit segregated) — used in **hospital OT, data centres, and process industries**. - **HRC fuse breaking capacity**: up to **120 kA** — used as back-up for MCB/MCCB. - **PT** steps 415 V to **110 V** for voltmeters and relay circuits.

topics.title: Selection of Industrial UG Cables - Calculation of Ampacity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Selection of Industrial UG Cables - Calculation of Ampacity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with **IS 3961 (Part 5)** and **IEC 60364 / IEC 60502**, the **ampacity** of an industrial underground (UG) cable is defined as:

> *The maximum continuous current (in Amperes) that a buried power cable can carry under specified installation conditions without exceeding the permissible temperature limit of its insulation, taking into account the heat generated by I²R losses, dielectric losses, and the heat dissipation capacity of the surrounding soil medium.*

For **KTU PCEET602 Module 3**, the standard permissible conductor temperatures (θ_max) for cable selection are:

| Cable Insulation Type | Permissible Conductor Temp (θ_max) |
|---|---|
| **PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride)** | **70 °C** |
| **XLPE (Cross-Linked Polyethylene)** | **90 °C** |
| **EPR (Ethylene Propylene Rubber)** | **90 °C** |
| **PILC (Paper Insulated Lead Covered)** | **65 °C** |
| **Mineral (MICC)** | **70 °C – 95 °C** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** When a question gives the cable type, you **MUST** use the corresponding θ_max in your formula. Wrong θ_max → wrong answer → **zero marks**. This is the most common single-step error in cable ampacity problems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Think of an underground cable as a **person running inside a narrow, insulated tunnel** (the insulation). When the person runs fast, they generate heat (sweat). The tunnel walls can only release heat to the surrounding earth (soil) at a limited rate. If the person runs too fast, the heat builds up, the temperature rises, and the tunnel walls (insulation) start melting!

**The "running speed" = Current (Amps).**
**The "heat release rate of the tunnel" = Soil Thermal Resistivity + Temperature + Depth + Grouping.**

So before picking a cable, the engineer asks: *Under my specific soil, depth, and grouping conditions, how fast can current safely flow?* That safe speed is the **effective ampacity**.

## 1.3 Why Derating is Mandatory

The **rated ampacity** from manufacturer's tables is always quoted for **standard reference conditions**:

- Soil thermal resistivity (g) = **1.2 K·m/W**
- Ambient soil temperature = **25 °C** (for cables) / **30 °C** (some standards)
- Depth of laying = **0.5 m to 1.0 m** (varies by standard)
- Single circuit, no grouping

Real field conditions are **almost never standard**. Hence, **derating factors** (multipliers ≤ 1) are applied to reduce the nominal current capacity to a safe real-world value.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must master the application of *temperature correction factor (Kt)*, *soil thermal resistivity correction factor (Ks)*, *depth of laying correction factor (Kd)*, and *grouping correction factor (Kg)* in industrial UG cable sizing problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Ampacity Derivation

The current-carrying capacity of a buried cable is fundamentally governed by **thermal equilibrium**:

$$\text{Heat Generated} = \text{Heat Dissipated}$$

The heat generated by current $I$ flowing through a conductor of AC resistance $R_{ac}$ per unit length:

$$Q_{gen} = I^2 \cdot R_{ac} \quad \text{[W per phase per meter]}$$

The heat dissipated to ambient earth through insulation, bedding, serving, and soil involves **thermal resistances** in series (analogous to electrical resistances):

$$Q_{diss} = \frac{\theta_{max} - \theta_{amb}}{R_{th,total}}$$

At thermal equilibrium:

$$I^2 \cdot R_{ac} = \frac{\theta_{max} - \theta_{amb}}{R_{th,total}}$$

Solving for current:

$$I = \sqrt{\frac{\theta_{max} - \theta_{amb}}{R_{ac} \cdot R_{th,total}}}$$

This shows the **direct proportionality** between ampacity and the square root of the temperature difference (θ_max − θ_amb), and the **inverse proportionality** to the square root of the product of conductor resistance and total thermal resistance.

## 2.2 The Four Mandatory Derating Factors (KTU High-Yield)

### A. Temperature Correction Factor (Kt)

Used when the actual soil ambient temperature differs from the standard reference temperature:

$$K_t = \sqrt{\frac{\theta_{max} - \theta_{amb,actual}}{\theta_{max} - \theta_{ref}}}$$

where:
- θ_max = Maximum permissible conductor temperature (°C)
- θ_amb,actual = Actual ambient soil temperature (°C)
- θ_ref = Reference temperature (typically **25 °C** for buried cables)

> **Why it exists:** Higher ambient temperature reduces the temperature gradient available to dissipate heat, hence reduces safe current.

### B. Soil Thermal Resistivity Factor (Ks)

Used when the soil thermal resistivity (g) is different from the standard 1.2 K·m/W (e.g., dry sandy soil ≈ 2.5 K·m/W, rocky soil ≈ 3.0 K·m/W, moist clay ≈ 0.8 K·m/W):

$$K_s \approx \sqrt{\frac{1.2}{g_{actual}}}$$

> **Why it exists:** Soil with high thermal resistivity (dry, rocky) does not conduct heat away from the cable effectively, reducing ampacity.

### C. Depth of Laying Factor (Kd)

Used when the cable is laid at a depth different from the standard (typically 0.5 m for low voltage, 0.8 m for 11 kV):

$$K_d = \left[\frac{1 + \frac{d_{ref}}{r}}{1 + \frac{d_{actual}}{r}}\right]$$

where $r$ is the cable outer radius. In practice, **tabulated values** are used (e.g., for XLPE cables, Kd varies from 1.00 at 0.5 m to 0.94 at 1.5 m).

> **Why it exists:** Deeper cables have a longer thermal path to the surface, reducing heat dissipation.

### D. Group Derating Factor (Kg)

Used when multiple circuits (multi-core cables or groups of single-core cables) are laid in the same trench with insufficient spacing:

$$K_g = \text{value from manufacturer's grouping table (always } \leq 1)$$

Typical values for two circuits laid in trefoil touching: Kg ≈ **0.78 to 0.85**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam Critical)

| # | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Standard Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Effective Ampacity** | $I_{eff} = I_{rated} \times K_t \times K_s \times K_d \times K_g$ | All factors < 1 |
| 2 | **Temperature Factor** | $K_t = \sqrt{(\theta_{max} - \theta_{amb})/(\theta_{max} - 25)}$ | θ_ref = 25 °C |
| 3 | **Soil Resistivity Factor** | $K_s = \sqrt{1.2/g_{soil}}$ | g_ref = 1.2 K·m/W |
| 4 | **Load Current (3-φ)** | $I_L = P/(\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \eta)$ | Motor loads |
| 5 | **Load Current (1-φ)** | $I_L = P/(V \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \eta)$ | Single-phase loads |
| 6 | **Voltage Drop (3-φ)** | $\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$ | For final check |
| 7 | **Short Circuit Withstand** | $I_{sc,thermal} = k \cdot A/\sqrt{t}$ | t = fault duration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Criterion:** A cable is considered suitable if and only if $I_{eff} \geq I_{load}$. If not, go to the next higher standard cross-section.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Plants (KTU 2024 Module 3 context):** Used to size 11 kV / 415 V feeder cables from substation to load centers.
- **Utility Distribution:** Critical for substation outgoing UG feeder design.
- **Safety:** Prevents insulation thermal runaway, fire, and premature cable failure.
- **Economic Impact:** Undersized cable → failure & downtime. Oversized cable → capital wastage. Correct ampacity = optimized cost.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Full Industrial UG Cable Selection (14-Mark Standard)

> **Problem Statement [Model KTU 2024 Question]:**
> A 3-phase, 11 kV, 50 Hz industrial feeder supplies a load of **2000 kW** at **0.85 p.f. lagging**. The feeder length is **1.5 km**. The cable is to be **3-core XLPE**, buried directly in ground at a depth of **1.0 m**. Ambient soil temperature = **35 °C**, soil thermal resistivity = **2.0 K·m/W**, and the cable is laid in a group of **3 circuits in horizontal formation with touching**.

> **Required:**
> (a) Calculate the full-load current of the feeder.
> (b) Determine the appropriate cable cross-section from the standard rating table.
> (c) Calculate the effective ampacity after applying all derating factors.

### Part (a) — Load Current Calculation

The 3-phase apparent power is:

$$S = \frac{P}{\cos\phi} = \frac{2000}{0.85} = 2352.94 \text{ kVA}$$

Converting line current for a 3-phase system:

$$I_L = \frac{S \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L} = \frac{2352.94 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 11 \times 10^3}$$

$$I_L = \frac{2352.94 \times 10^3}{19052.56}$$

$$I_L = 123.50 \text{ A}$$

**So, the full-load current I_L ≈ 123.5 A.**

### Part (b) — Base Cable Selection from Standard Table

For a **3-core 11 kV XLPE armoured cable** laid in ground (reference conditions: 25 °C, 1.2 K·m/W, depth 0.8 m, single circuit), standard ratings per IS 3961 / manufacturer datasheet (typical values):

| Cross-section (mm²) | Rated Current (A) |
|---|---|
| 50 | 165 |
| 70 | 200 |
| 95 | 240 |
| 120 | 275 |
| 150 | 305 |

**First Trial:** Select **50 mm²** cable (rated 165 A) as a starting point. We will verify with derating.

### Part (c) — Apply Derating Factors

**Step 1: Temperature Correction Factor (Kt)**

Given: XLPE → θ_max = 90 °C, θ_amb = 35 °C, θ_ref = 25 °C:

$$K_t = \sqrt{\frac{\theta_{max} - \theta_{amb}}{\theta_{max} - \theta_{ref}}}$$

$$K_t = \sqrt{\frac{90 - 35}{90 - 25}}$$

$$K_t = \sqrt{\frac{55}{65}}$$

$$K_t = \sqrt{0.8462}$$

$$K_t = 0.920$$

**Step 2: Soil Thermal Resistivity Factor (Ks)**

Given: g_actual = 2.0 K·m/W, g_ref = 1.2 K·m/W:

$$K_s = \sqrt{\frac{1.2}{2.0}}$$

$$K_s = \sqrt{0.6}$$

$$K_s = 0.7746$$

**Step 3: Depth of Laying Factor (Kd)**

From standard table for 11 kV XLPE at 1.0 m depth:

$$K_d = 0.97 \text{ (tabulated value)}$$

**Step 4: Group Derating Factor (Kg)**

For 3 circuits in horizontal formation touching:

$$K_g = 0.70 \text{ (per IS 3961 grouping table)}$$

**Step 5: Compute Effective Ampacity of 50 mm² cable**

$$I_{eff} = I_{rated} \times K_t \times K_s \times K_d \times K_g$$

$$I_{eff} = 165 \times 0.920 \times 0.7746 \times 0.97 \times 0.70$$

Now compute step by step:

$$165 \times 0.920 = 151.80$$

$$151.80 \times 0.7746 = 117.58$$

$$117.58 \times 0.97 = 114.05$$

$$114.05 \times 0.70 = 79.84 \text{ A}$$

**Result: I_eff (50 mm²) ≈ 79.84 A < 123.5 A → NOT SUITABLE.**

**Step 6: Reject and try 70 mm² (rated 200 A)**

$$I_{eff} = 200 \times 0.920 \times 0.7746 \times 0.97 \times 0.70$$

$$I_{eff} = 200 \times 0.4856$$

Computing the product of factors:

$$0.920 \times 0.7746 = 0.7126$$

$$0.7126 \times 0.97 = 0.6913$$

$$0.6913 \times 0.70 = 0.4839$$

$$I_{eff} = 200 \times 0.4839 = 96.78 \text{ A}$$

**Still < 123.5 A → NOT SUITABLE.**

**Step 7: Try 95 mm² (rated 240 A)**

$$I_{eff} = 240 \times 0.4839$$

$$I_{eff} = 116.14 \text{ A}$$

**Still < 123.5 A → NOT SUITABLE.**

**Step 8: Try 120 mm² (rated 275 A)**

$$I_{eff} = 275 \times 0.4839$$

$$I_{eff} = 133.07 \text{ A}$$

**133.07 A ≥ 123.5 A → SUITABLE ✓**

### Final Answer for Example 1

$$\boxed{\text{Select 3-core 11 kV XLPE cable of } 120 \text{ mm}^2}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Ampacity Calculation with Voltage Drop Check

> **Problem:** A 3-core 35 mm² XLPE aluminium cable (Cu equivalent) is laid in ground with: θ_max = 90 °C, soil T = 30 °C, g = 1.5 K·m/W, depth = 0.7 m, single circuit. Cable resistance ≈ 0.868 Ω/km, reactance ≈ 0.078 Ω/km.
> From the table, rated current = 105 A.
> **Find effective ampacity and check against a 78 A load at 0.8 p.f.**

**Step 1: Temperature factor Kt**

$$K_t = \sqrt{\frac{90 - 30}{90 - 25}} = \sqrt{\frac{60}{65}} = \sqrt{0.9231} = 0.9608$$

**Step 2: Soil thermal resistivity factor Ks**

$$K_s = \sqrt{\frac{1.2}{1.5}} = \sqrt{0.8} = 0.8944$$

**Step 3: Depth of laying factor Kd (from table)**

$$K_d = 0.99 \text{ (for } 0.7 \text{ m depth, near standard)}$$

**Step 4: Group factor Kg**

$$K_g = 1.00 \text{ (single circuit)}$$

**Step 5: Effective ampacity**

$$I_{eff} = 105 \times 0.9608 \times 0.8944 \times 0.99 \times 1.00$$

$$I_{eff} = 105 \times 0.8510$$

$$I_{eff} \approx 89.36 \text{ A}$$

**Comparison: I_eff = 89.36 A ≥ I_load = 78 A → CABLE IS SUITABLE.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Derating Factor Concept (From First Principles)

Starting from heat balance per unit length:

$$I^2 R_{ac} = \frac{\theta_{max} - \theta_{amb}}{R_{th}}$$

For the **reference** condition:

$$I_{rated}^2 R_{ac} = \frac{\theta_{max} - \theta_{ref}}{R_{th,ref}}$$

Taking the ratio $I / I_{rated}$:

$$\frac{I^2}{I_{rated}^2} = \frac{(\theta_{max} - \theta_{amb}) \cdot R_{th,ref}}{(\theta_{max} - \theta_{ref}) \cdot R_{th}}$$

Defining total derating factor $K_{total} = I/I_{rated}$:

$$K_{total} = \sqrt{\frac{\theta_{max} - \theta_{amb}}{\theta_{max} - \theta_{ref}} \cdot \frac{R_{th,ref}}{R_{th}}}$$

Recognizing the four individual contributions:

$$K_{total} = K_t \times K_s \times K_d \times K_g$$

This decomposition is **rigorously valid** when the derating effects are independent (a standard KTU assumption).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cable Selection Workflow (Block-Level Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete decision flow an industrial engineer follows when selecting an underground cable based on ampacity:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Load Data] --> B[Calculate Full Load Current IL]
    B --> C[Select Cable Type: XLPE / PVC / PILC]
    C --> D[Select Trial Cable Size from Standard Table]
    D --> E{Apply Derating Factors}
    E --> F[Temperature Factor Kt]
    E --> G[Soil Resistivity Factor Ks]
    E --> H[Depth Factor Kd]
    E --> I[Grouping Factor Kg]
    F --> J[Compute Effective Ampacity Ieff]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K{Ieff greater than or equal to IL ?}
    K -- No --> L[Move to Next Higher Cross Section]
    L --> D
    K -- Yes --> M[Perform Voltage Drop Check]
    M --> N{Voltage Drop within 5 percent ?}
    N -- No --> L
    N -- Yes --> O[Check Short Circuit Withstand]
    O --> P{Short Circuit OK ?}
    P -- No --> L
    P -- Yes --> Q[Select Final Cable Size]
    Q --> R[End]
```

## 4.2 Thermal Equivalent Circuit (Heat Flow Model)

The heat flow in a buried cable is analogous to an electrical circuit (per IEC 60287). For KTU Module 3, this conceptual block diagram is essential:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Conductor Source Temperature: theta_max] --> B[Conductor Thermal Resistance: T1]
    B --> C[Insulation Thermal Resistance: T2]
    C --> D[Bedding Sheath Resistance: T3]
    D --> E[Soil Thermal Resistance: T4]
    E --> F[Ambient Sink Temperature: theta_amb]
    G[Heat Source: I squared R] --> B
    H[Dielectric Losses] --> C
```

**Key Insight:** Higher T4 (dry / rocky soil) → reduced heat dissipation → lower ampacity. This is the physical reason behind the Ks factor.

## 4.3 Grouping Geometry Identification Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Number of Circuits n] --> B{Touching or Spaced?}
    B -- Touching --> C[Use Group Factor Table for Touching]
    B -- Spaced --> D[Spacing Distance s]
    D --> E{s greater than 2x cable OD?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Kg = 1.00 allowed, no derating]
    E -- No --> G[Use Group Factor Table for Spaced]
    C --> H[Apply to Effective Ampacity]
    G --> H
    F --> H
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Two circuits laid in the same trench with **at least 0.5 m spacing** (for LV) or **1.0 m spacing** (for HV) generally do not require grouping derating. Below these spacings, derating is mandatory per IS 3961.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]

**Q: Define the term "ampacity" of an underground cable. State any four factors that affect the ampacity of an industrial UG cable.**

**Model Answer:**
