**Definition (1 mark):** Ampacity is the maximum continuous current, expressed in Amperes, that a power cable can carry under specified installation conditions without the steady-state temperature of its conductor exceeding the permissible limit defined by the cable's insulation class.

**Four Factors (2 marks):**
1. **Conductor temperature limit (θ_max):** depends on insulation type (XLPE = 90 °C, PVC = 70 °C).
2. **Ambient soil temperature:** higher ambient temperature reduces the temperature gradient, reducing safe current.
3. **Soil thermal resistivity (g):** dry or rocky soil (high g) dissipates heat poorly, lowering ampacity.
4. **Depth of laying:** deeper cables have a longer thermal path to the surface, reducing heat dissipation.
5. *(Any other valid factors: grouping, number of cores, cable construction, soil moisture, etc.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]

**Q: What is meant by "derating factor" in cable selection? Why is it essential in industrial UG cable sizing?**

**Model Answer:**

**Meaning (1.5 marks):** A derating factor is a **multiplier (≤ 1)** applied to the manufacturer's rated current to account for deviations between **actual field installation conditions** and the **standard reference conditions** used in rating tables. Common derating factors are Kt (temperature), Ks (soil thermal resistivity), Kd (depth of laying), and Kg (grouping).

**Why essential (1.5 marks):**
- Manufacturer ratings are based on ideal conditions (25 °C, g = 1.2 K·m/W, single circuit, standard depth).
- Real industrial installations almost never match these conditions.
- Applying derating factors ensures the **conductor temperature does not exceed insulation limits** under real conditions, preventing premature failure, fire hazards, and ensuring design life is achieved.
- Skipping derating leads to **unsafe undersizing**, the most common cause of UG cable failures in industry.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, the student must answer **one full question** (with both sub-parts). Two alternative full questions are provided below for internal choice practice.

---

### Question B-1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**A 3-phase, 11 kV, 50 Hz underground feeder is required to supply a load of 1500 kVA at 0.9 p.f. (lagging). The cable is a 3-core XLPE armoured cable to be laid in ground at a depth of 1.2 m. Ambient soil temperature = 30 °C, soil thermal resistivity = 1.8 K·m/W. The cable is laid in a group of 2 circuits in horizontal formation touching each other. The standard reference depth is 0.8 m, reference temperature is 25 °C, and reference soil resistivity is 1.2 K·m/W. The standard rating table gives:**

| Size (mm²) | Rated Current (A) |
|---|---|
| 70 | 200 |
| 95 | 240 |
| 120 | 275 |
| 150 | 305 |
| 185 | 345 |

**Required:**
**(a) Calculate the load current and select a suitable cable size using proper derating factors. (7 marks)**
**(b) Verify the selection by computing the effective ampacity and discuss what happens if derating is ignored. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B-1

**Part (a) — Load Current and Selection (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Compute Load Current (2 marks)**

For a 3-phase, 1500 kVA load at 11 kV:

$$I_L = \frac{S \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L} = \frac{1500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 11 \times 10^3}$$

$$I_L = \frac{1500 \times 10^3}{19052.56} = 78.73 \text{ A}$$

So **I_L ≈ 78.73 A**.

**Step 2: Identify Derating Factor Values (3 marks)**

Temperature factor (θ_max = 90 °C for XLPE):

$$K_t = \sqrt{\frac{90 - 30}{90 - 25}} = \sqrt{\frac{60}{65}} = \sqrt{0.9231} = 0.9608$$

Soil thermal resistivity factor:

$$K_s = \sqrt{\frac{1.2}{1.8}} = \sqrt{0.6667} = 0.8165$$

Depth of laying factor (from IS 3961 table for 1.2 m, ref 0.8 m, XLPE 11 kV):

$$K_d = 0.96$$

Grouping factor (2 circuits horizontal touching, 11 kV XLPE, from table):

$$K_g = 0.82$$

**Step 3: Compute Product of Factors (1 mark)**

$$K_{total} = 0.9608 \times 0.8165 \times 0.96 \times 0.82$$

$$0.9608 \times 0.8165 = 0.7845$$

$$0.7845 \times 0.96 = 0.7531$$

$$0.7531 \times 0.82 = 0.6176$$

So $K_{total} \approx 0.6176$.

**Step 4: Trial Cable Selection (1 mark)**

Required rated current from table:

$$I_{rated,required} = \frac{I_L}{K_{total}} = \frac{78.73}{0.6176} = 127.48 \text{ A}$$

From the table, **95 mm²** (240 A) is more than enough in terms of base rating, but check after derating. **120 mm²** (275 A) is the safe choice.

---

**Part (b) — Verification and Discussion (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Effective Ampacity of Selected Cable (2 marks)**

Selecting **95 mm²** (I_rated = 240 A):

$$I_{eff} = 240 \times 0.6176 = 148.22 \text{ A}$$

Since 148.22 A ≥ 78.73 A → **CABLE 95 mm² IS SUITABLE.**

If we try **70 mm²** (I_rated = 200 A):

$$I_{eff} = 200 \times 0.6176 = 123.52 \text{ A}$$

123.52 A > 78.73 A → **70 mm² IS ALSO TECHNICALLY SUITABLE**, but 95 mm² is recommended for a margin of safety.

**Step 2: Check 70 mm² with No Derating (2 marks)**

Without derating, I_eff = 200 A, which is 2.54 times the load. This makes the engineer think 70 mm² is highly oversized. **But with derating, the margin shrinks to just 1.57 times.** This shows derating is the **only realistic way** to assess true ampacity.

**Step 3: Discussion — What Happens if Derating is Ignored (3 marks)**

If derating is ignored:
- The cable is selected solely on the base rating (e.g., a 25 mm² cable rated 125 A might appear suitable for 110 A load).
- In real conditions, the conductor temperature will **exceed 90 °C**, causing:
  - **XLPE insulation degradation** (thermal aging doubles for every 8–10 °C rise).
  - **Reduced cable life** from 30 years to maybe 5–10 years.
  - **Insulation breakdown → short circuits → fires → downtime.**
  - **Voiding of manufacturer warranty and IS 3961 compliance.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. **Forgetting θ_max** for the given cable type → wrong Kt → **lose 1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Inverting the derating formula** (multiplying instead of dividing) → wrong selection → **lose 2–3 marks**.
> 3. **Mixing up 1.2 K·m/W reference with 1.5 K·m/W** (used in some European/IEC tables) → wrong Ks → **lose 1 mark**.
> 4. **Omitting the grouping factor** when multiple cables share a trench → unsafe design, KTU examiners mark this as a **critical error**.
> 5. **Not showing the substitution step** in formulas → marks deducted for incomplete working.

---

### Question B-2 (14 Marks) — Alternative [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**A factory has the following loads connected to a 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz bus bar through individual UG XLPE 4-core cables laid directly in ground:**
- **Load 1:** 75 kW motor, 0.85 p.f., 0.90 efficiency.
- **Load 2:** 50 kW motor, 0.80 p.f., 0.88 efficiency.
- **Load 3:** 30 kW heater (unity p.f.).

**All three cables are laid in the same trench in trefoil touching, single-circuit-per-cable, at a depth of 0.6 m. Ambient soil temp = 35 °C, soil thermal resistivity = 1.5 K·m/W.**

**Standard ratings (3-core XLPE, Cu, in ground, ref conditions):**

| Size (mm²) | Rated Current (A) |
|---|---|
| 16 | 80 |
| 25 | 105 |
| 35 | 130 |
| 50 | 155 |
| 70 | 195 |
| 95 | 235 |

**Required:**
**(a) Calculate the load current for each feeder and select the cable size. (7 marks)**
**(b) Apply derating factors and finalize the selection, then determine the total voltage drop on the longest feeder (assume L = 60 m for Load 1, R = 0.727 Ω/km, X = 0.080 Ω/km for 16 mm² cable). (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B-2

**Part (a) — Load Current Calculation (7 marks)**

**Load 1 — Motor (75 kW, 0.85 p.f., 0.90 η):**

$$I_1 = \frac{75 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.85 \times 0.90}$$

$$I_1 = \frac{75000}{538.86} = 139.18 \text{ A}$$

**Load 2 — Motor (50 kW, 0.80 p.f., 0.88 η):**

$$I_2 = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.80 \times 0.88}$$

$$I_2 = \frac{50000}{504.85} = 99.04 \text{ A}$$

**Load 3 — Heater (30 kW, unity p.f., η = 1):**

$$I_3 = \frac{30 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 1 \times 1} = \frac{30000}{718.78} = 41.74 \text{ A}$$

**Selection (from table):**
- Load 1: 139.18 A → try **35 mm²** (130 A) or **50 mm²** (155 A).
- Load 2: 99.04 A → try **35 mm²** (130 A).
- Load 3: 41.74 A → try **16 mm²** (80 A).

**Part (b) — Apply Derating and Finalize (7 marks)**

**Derating factor values (XLPE 4-core LV, 0.6 m depth):**

Temperature factor (θ_max = 90 °C):

$$K_t = \sqrt{\frac{90 - 35}{90 - 25}} = \sqrt{\frac{55}{65}} = 0.9200$$

Soil thermal resistivity factor:

$$K_s = \sqrt{\frac{1.2}{1.5}} = 0.8944$$

Depth factor (LV cable at 0.6 m vs ref 0.5 m):

$$K_d = 0.99$$

Grouping factor (3 cables in trefoil touching, single-circuit per cable but in same trench — derate due to mutual heating):

$$K_g = 0.79 \text{ (per IS 3961, 3 circuits trefoil touching)}$$

**Total derating factor:**

$$K_{total} = 0.9200 \times 0.8944 \times 0.99 \times 0.79 = 0.6420$$

**Required rated current for each load:**

- Load 1: 139.18 / 0.6420 = **216.79 A** → select **95 mm²** (235 A) → $I_{eff} = 235 \times 0.6420 = 150.87$ A ≥ 139.18 A ✓
- Load 2: 99.04 / 0.6420 = **154.27 A** → select **70 mm²** (195 A) → $I_{eff} = 195 \times 0.6420 = 125.19$ A ≥ 99.04 A ✓
- Load 3: 41.74 / 0.6420 = **65.02 A** → select **16 mm²** (80 A) → $I_{eff} = 80 \times 0.6420 = 51.36$ A ≥ 41.74 A ✓

**Voltage Drop for Load 1 using 16 mm² hypothetical (just for calculation):**

$$\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \times I \times L \times (R \cos\phi + X \sin\phi)$$

For Load 1: I = 139.18 A, L = 0.06 km, R = 0.727 Ω/km, X = 0.080 Ω/km, cosφ = 0.85, sinφ = 0.527.

$$R \cos\phi = 0.727 \times 0.85 = 0.6180$$

$$X \sin\phi = 0.080 \times 0.527 = 0.0422$$

$$R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi = 0.6602 \text{ Ω/km}$$

$$\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \times 139.18 \times 0.06 \times 0.6602$$

$$\Delta V = 1.732 \times 139.18 \times 0.06 \times 0.6602$$

$$\Delta V = 9.55 \text{ V}$$

Percentage voltage drop:

$$\% V_d = \frac{9.55}{415} \times 100 = 2.30\%$$

**Within KTU permissible limit of 5% — so 16 mm² could technically be used if load permitted, but 95 mm² is selected for ampacity.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question B-2:**
> 1. **Confusing efficiency (η) and power factor (cosφ)** when computing motor current → wrong I_L → **lose 2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting √3 factor** in 3-phase current → numerical error → **lose 1 mark**.
> 3. **Using θ_max = 70 °C (PVC) instead of 90 °C (XLPE)** → wrong Kt → **lose 1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Not converting L from meters to km** in voltage drop → 1000× error → **lose 2 marks**.
> 5. **Forgetting sinφ in voltage drop** when p.f. is not unity → **lose 1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Ampacity = current capacity** under specific installation conditions, limited by **insulation temperature class**.
- **θ_max values to memorize:** PVC = **70 °C**, XLPE/EPR = **90 °C**, PILC = **65 °C**.
- **Master formula:** $I_{eff} = I_{rated} \times K_t \times K_s \times K_d \times K_g$
- **Kt formula:** $K_t = \sqrt{(\theta_{max} - \theta_{amb})/(\theta_{max} - \theta_{ref})}$ — always use **θ_ref = 25 °C** for buried cables (KTU standard).
- **Ks formula:** $K_s = \sqrt{1.2/g}$ — always with **g_ref = 1.2 K·m/W**.
- **Kd and Kg** are typically read from **IS 3961 / manufacturer tables**, not derived mathematically in KTU exams.
- **Selection rule:** Compute I_load, multiply by inverse of K_total to get required I_rated, then choose the next standard size that meets/exceeds this value.
- **Always do voltage drop check** after ampacity selection — KTU 2024 expects both.
- **Short circuit check** is mandatory for feeders near transformers — formula: $I_{sc,thermal} = k \cdot A/\sqrt{t}$ where k depends on conductor (Cu = 0.143, Al = 0.094 for PVC, etc.).
- **Common grouping derating values to remember:**
  - 2 circuits trefoil touching: Kg ≈ **0.78 – 0.82**
  - 3 circuits trefoil touching: Kg ≈ **0.70 – 0.79**
  - 4+ circuits touching: Kg ≈ **0.65 – 0.70**
- **Soil thermal resistivity rule of thumb:**
  - Moist clay: g ≈ 0.8 K·m/W
  - Average soil: g ≈ 1.2 K·m/W
  - Dry sandy soil: g ≈ 2.0–2.5 K·m/W
  - Rocky soil: g ≈ 3.0 K·m/W
- **Always show all substitution steps** in KTU 2024 valuation — incomplete working = marks cut.
- **Most forgotten factor:** Students often forget **Kd** (depth) and **Kg** (grouping). KTU examiners specifically test these in 14-mark problems.
- **Unit conversion traps:** L in km for R/X, θ in °C, I in Amperes, S in kVA, P in kW, V in Volts.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voltage Drop
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Voltage Drop # Module 3: Voltage Drop ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Voltage Drop** is defined as the loss of electrical potential (voltage) along the length of a conductor due to the inherent **impedance (resistance + reactance)** of the conductor carrying current from the source to the load. In formal estimation and design vocabulary adopted by the KTU 2024 scheme, voltage drop is quantified as the algebraic difference between the voltage at the sending end (source) and the voltage at the receiving end (load terminal). > [!IMPORTANT] > **IE Rule 48 Compliance (India):** As per the Central Electricity Authority (CEA) Regulations and Indian Electricity (IE) Rules, the voltage drop in any installation must not exceed **3%** of the declared voltage when the conductors are carrying full load current. When both lighting and power loads coexist, the combined drop from the transformer secondary to the farthest consumer terminal must not exceed **5%** of the rated supply voltage. The mathematical representation is: $$\Delta V = V_{sending\,end} - V_{receiving\,end}$$ Where every conductor pair (phase + neutral for single-phase, or three-phase lines for three-phase) contributes a finite resistance and inductive reactance that opposes current flow. This opposition is governed by the intrinsic resistivity of the conductor material, its cross-sectional area, length, and the operating temperature. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Design Practice:** Voltage drop is not a fault condition; it is an inherent and unavoidable phenomenon. The role of the design engineer is to **limit** this drop within statutory bounds by appropriate **cable sizing**, **conductor material selection**, and **system layout**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition Imagine a garden hose connected to a municipal water tap. When you open the tap fully at the source, the water arrives at the nozzle with great force. But if you stretch the hose over 100 meters, lay a few kinks in it, or use a very narrow-diameter hose, the water pressure at the nozzle drops dramatically — the flow is **throttled** by the hose itself. Electrical conductors behave identically: - The **tap (transformer)** is the source of electrical "pressure" (voltage). - The **hose (cable)** is the conductor with its inherent "friction" (resistance/reactance). - The **nozzle (lamp/motor)** is the load that needs the proper pressure to function correctly. If the hose is too long or too thin, the load at the far end "suffers" from low voltage — it dims, runs hot, or simply refuses to start. Voltage drop is exactly this electrical "pressure loss" over the length of the cable. ### 1.3 Why Voltage Drop Matters in Design | Consequence of Excessive Voltage Drop | Real-World Effect | |---|---| | Lamp lumens reduced | Lighting dimmer than design intent | | Incandescent lamp life shortened | High inrush current on cold filament | | Motor starting torque reduced (T ∝ V²) | Motor fails to start loaded machinery | | Motor running current rises | Overheating of windings, insulation failure | | Electronic equipment malfunction | PLCs, computers reset, sensors misread | | Breaker nuisance tripping | Under-voltage coils or electronic trips activate | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Physical Constants (Bolded for Emphasis):** > - Resistivity of Copper: **ρ_Cu = 1.72 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m at 20°C** > - Resistivity of Aluminium: **ρ_Al = 2.82 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m at 20°C** > - Temperature coefficient of Copper: **α_Cu = 0.00393 per °C at 20°C** > - Temperature coefficient of Aluminium: **α_Al = 0.00403 per 20°C at 20°C** > - Reactance of LV cables (typical): **X = 0.08 Ω/km for 3-core cables up to 25 mm²** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Components of Conductor Opposition A current-carrying conductor exhibits two distinct forms of opposition to alternating current: **(a) Resistance (R):** This is the ohmic opposition caused by the physical collisions of free electrons with the lattice atoms of the conductor. It depends on: - Material (resistivity ρ) - Length (L) - Cross-sectional area (A) - Operating temperature (T) The fundamental relationship is: $$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$ **(b) Reactance (X):** This is the opposition caused by the alternating magnetic field surrounding the conductor. It is composed of: - **Internal reactance** (due to flux within the conductor — usually negligible for LV cables) - **External reactance** (due to flux between conductors — dominant in multi-core cables) For low-voltage (LV) installations below 1000 V, reactance is generally small compared to resistance and is often ignored for cables **below 16 mm²**. For larger cables and HT systems, reactance **must** be included. The combined opposition in AC circuits is called **impedance (Z)**: $$Z = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2}$$ ## 2.2 Voltage Drop — Phasor Logic In an AC circuit, voltage drop is **not a simple arithmetic** product. Because the current and voltage are phasor quantities, the drop must be computed vectorially along the in-phase (resistive) and quadrature (reactive) axes. The classical derivation begins with the circuit: $$V_{send} = V_{receive} + I \cdot (R + jX)$$ For a load with power factor cos φ: - The in-phase component of voltage drop = **I · R · cos φ** - The quadrature component of voltage drop = **I · X · sin φ** The magnitude of the approximate voltage drop is: $$\Delta V = I \cdot (R \cos\phi + X \sin\phi)$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **approximate** formula valid when ΔV is small (< 10% of V). For more precise computation, the exact formula is: > $$\Delta V = \sqrt{(V_R + I R \cos\phi + I X \sin\phi)^2 + (I X \cos\phi - I R \sin\phi)^2} - V_R$$ > The exact form is rarely used in KTU design problems; the approximate form is the standard. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Parameter | Single-Phase (2-Wire) | Three-Phase (3-Phase, 4-Wire) | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Approximate Voltage Drop | ΔV = 2 · I · L · (R cos φ + X sin φ) | ΔV = √3 · I · L · (R cos φ + X sin φ) | Volts (V) | | 2 | % Voltage Drop | (%V) = (ΔV / V_rated) × 100 | (%V) = (ΔV / V_rated) × 100 | Percent (%) | | 3 | Per-km Resistance (at 20°C) | R_20 = ρ / A (per km) | Same as single-phase | Ω/km | | 4 | Resistance at operating temp | R_T = R_20 [1 + α (T − 20)] | Same as single-phase | Ω/km | | 5 | Conductor cross-section from ΔV | A = (√3 · I · L · ρ · cos φ) / (ΔV_allow) | For 3-phase 4-wire (worst case) | mm² | | 6 | Permissible limit (lighting) | ≤ 3% of rated voltage | ≤ 3% of rated voltage | % | | 7 | Permissible limit (power) | ≤ 5% of rated voltage | ≤ 5% of rated voltage | % | | 8 | Combined limit (transformer to consumer) | ≤ 5% of rated voltage | ≤ 5% of rated voltage | % | | 9 | Power loss in cable | P_loss = I² · R (per phase) | P_loss = 3 · I² · R (3-phase) | Watts | | 10 | Voltage drop in DC system | ΔV = 2 · I · L · R | ΔV = 2 · I · L · R (loop) | Volts | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Conversion:** When ρ is in Ω·m and A is in mm², multiply the formula by 10⁻³ to convert to Ω/km. Always use consistent units — **L in km, A in mm², ρ in Ω·mm²/km** is the engineering shortcut. The standard engineering shortcut using **ρ in Ω·mm²/km** is: $$R\,(\Omega/km) = \frac{\rho\,(\Omega \cdot mm^2/km)}{A\,(mm^2)}$$ For copper: ρ = **17.2 Ω·mm²/km** at 20°C. For aluminium: ρ = **28.2 Ω·mm²/km** at 20°C. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Voltage drop calculation is the **gateway to cable sizing**. In any KTU design problem, once the load current, run length, and power factor are known, the cable cross-section is determined by satisfying two simultaneous criteria: 1. **Current carrying capacity (CCC)** — to prevent overheating (Module 2 topic). 2. **Voltage drop (VD)** — to ensure functional voltage at the load (Module 3 topic). The **larger** of the two derived sizes is selected. Voltage drop frequently dominates for **long runs** and **low-power-factor loads**. Voltage drop is also the basis for: - **Capacitor bank sizing** to improve power factor (lower I → lower ΔV) - **HT/LT distribution planning** to optimize transformer placement - **Earthing conductor sizing** (related to earth fault loop impedance) - **Substation bus-bar selection** for very high current systems # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Resistance of a Conductor Starting from microscopic electron transport theory, drift velocity **v_d** is established across a conductor of length **L** under applied field **E**: $$V = E \cdot L$$ Current density is: $$J = n \cdot e \cdot v_d$$ By the microscopic form of Ohm's law: $$J = \sigma \cdot E \quad \text{where} \quad \sigma = \frac{1}{\rho}$$ Therefore: $$V = \frac{L \cdot I}{\sigma \cdot A} = \frac{\rho \cdot L \cdot I}{A}$$ Since **V = I · R**, by direct comparison: $$\boxed{R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}}$$ > [!NOTE] > The above derivation explains why doubling the cable length **doubles** the resistance, while doubling the cross-section **halves** it. This is the foundation of all voltage drop math. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Approximate AC Voltage Drop Formula (Single-Phase) Consider a single-phase two-wire feeder supplying a load at the receiving end. The sending-end voltage **V_s** differs from the receiving-end voltage **V_r** by the drop across the line impedance. **Step 1:** Take V_r as the reference phasor along the real axis: $$V_r = V_r \angle 0°$$ **Step 2:** The load current lags V_r by the load power factor angle φ: $$I = I \angle -\phi$$ **Step 3:** The line impedance (per conductor) is: $$Z_{line} = R + jX$$ **Step 4:** The total loop impedance for the two-wire system (phase + neutral, both having R and X) is: $$Z_{loop} = 2 \cdot (R + jX)$$ **Step 5:** Apply KVL around the loop: $$V_s = V_r + I \cdot Z_{loop}$$ **Step 6:** Expand the drop term by substituting I and Z: $$\Delta V = I \cdot 2(R + jX) = 2 I R \cos\phi + j(2 I R \sin\phi + 2 I X \cos\phi) - j(2 I X \sin\phi)$$ **Step 7:** The **in-phase** (real) component of voltage drop, which is the magnitude of drop along the V_r direction, is: $$\Delta V = 2 I R \cos\phi + 2 I X \sin\phi$$ **Step 8:** Factor the expression: $$\boxed{\Delta V_{1\phi} = 2 I L (R \cos\phi + X \sin\phi)}$$ Where R and X are per-unit-length values (per km), and L is the run length in km. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Three-Phase Voltage Drop In a balanced 3-phase, 3-wire or 4-wire system, the line-to-line voltage at the load is V_L. The current in each phase is I. By symmetry, the analysis of one phase suffices. **Step 1:** The line-to-neutral receiving-end voltage is: $$V_{ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$$ **Step 2:** The per-phase loop impedance is (R + jX) per km, but the neutral drop is negligible in balanced 3-phase systems: $$Z_{per\,phase} = R + jX \quad (\text{per km})$$ **Step 3:** The voltage drop per phase is: $$\Delta V_{ph} = I \cdot L \cdot (R \cos\phi + X \sin\phi)$$ **Step 4:** The line-to-line voltage drop is √3 times the per-phase drop (because phase voltages are 120° apart): $$\boxed{\Delta V_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot (R \cos\phi + X \sin\phi)}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cable Cross-Section from Voltage Drop Limit For a permitted voltage drop ΔV_allow across a length L, with known load current I and power factor cos φ: **Step 1:** Ignore reactance (conservative for small cables) → R only matters. $$\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot R \cdot \cos\phi$$ **Step 2:** Substitute R = ρL / A (with L in km and ρ in Ω·mm²/km, A in mm²): $$\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot \frac{\rho \cdot L}{A} \cdot \cos\phi$$ **Step 3:** Solve for A: $$A = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L^2 \cdot \rho \cdot \cos\phi}{\Delta V_{allow}}$$ Where all lengths are in km, ρ in Ω·mm²/km, I in A, ΔV in volts, A in mm². **Step 4:** For single-phase (with loop factor of 2): $$A_{1\phi} = \frac{2 \cdot I \cdot L^2 \cdot \rho \cdot \cos\phi}{\Delta V_{allow}}$$ ## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern) **Problem:** A 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz motor load of 30 kW at 0.85 p.f. lagging is located 80 m from the MCC. The supply is via a 3-core aluminium cable, with permissible voltage drop of 4%. Find the cable cross-section. **Given:** - P = 30 kW, V_L = 415 V, cos φ = 0.85, L = 80 m = 0.08 km - Al cable, ρ = 28.2 Ω·mm²/km at 20°C - ΔV_allow = 4% of 415 = 16.6 V **Step 1: Compute load current** $$I = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi} = \frac{30{,}000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 415 \cdot 0.85}$$ $$I = \frac{30{,}000}{611.05} \approx 49.1\,A$$ **Step 2: Apply voltage drop formula and solve for A (ignore X)** $$\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot \frac{\rho \cdot L}{A} \cdot \cos\phi$$ Wait — this contains L twice. The correct form is R in Ω per km, then: $$\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot R \cdot \cos\phi \quad \text{where} \quad R = \frac{\rho}{A}$$ $$16.6 = \sqrt{3} \cdot 49.1 \cdot 0.08 \cdot \frac{28.2}{A} \cdot 0.85$$ **Step 3: Compute the constant term** $$\sqrt{3} \cdot 49.1 \cdot 0.08 \cdot 28.2 \cdot 0.85 = 1.732 \cdot 49.1 \cdot 0.08 \cdot 28.2 \cdot 0.85$$ $$= 1.732 \cdot 49.1 \cdot 0.08 \cdot 23.97 = 1.732 \cdot 49.1 \cdot 1.9176 = 1.732 \cdot 94.16 = 163.1$$ **Step 4: Solve for A** $$A = \frac{163.1}{16.6} \approx 9.83\,mm^2$$ **Step 5: Select the next standard size** The next standard size in the IS 3961 / IS 1554 / IS 7098 series is **10 mm²**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sanity Check:** A 10 mm² Al cable has a CCC of ~46 A (PVC) to 60 A (XLPE). The load current is 49.1 A, so a 10 mm² XLPE Al cable satisfies both VD and CCC. The selected size is **10 mm² Al XLPE**. ## 3.6 Compensation for Reactance (When X is Significant) For larger cables (> 25 mm²) and HT systems, X is not negligible. Standard tables provide R and X per km. The modified drop is: $$\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot (R \cos\phi + X \sin\phi)$$ The **corrected cross-section** must include both R and X. This is often solved by **iteration**: 1. Estimate A from R-only formula. 2. Look up X for that A. 3. Recompute ΔV with both R and X. 4. If ΔV > allowable, increase A and repeat. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Phasor Diagram — Voltage Drop Components The diagram below illustrates the spatial phasor relationship between the receiving-end voltage **V_r**, sending-end voltage **V_s**, the resistive drop **I·R**, and the reactive drop **I·X**. This is the cornerstone visualization for understanding why the approximate formula works. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["V_s
Sending End Voltage"] -->|"V_s phasor
(magnitude 1.0 pu)"| nodeB["V_r
Receiving End Voltage
Reference along X-axis"] nodeB -->|"Resistive drop
I R cos phi
(in phase with I)"| nodeC["I R Component
Horizontal Projection"] nodeB -->|"Reactive drop
I X sin phi
(quadrature to I)"| nodeD["I X Component
Vertical Projection"] nodeA -->|"Total Drop dV"| nodeE["Voltage Drop Magnitude
approx I R cos phi plus I X sin phi"] nodeC --> nodeE nodeD --> nodeE nodeA -.->|"Angle delta
(load angle)"| nodeB ``` ## 4.2 Equivalent Circuit — Single Line Representation of a Feeder The schematic below depicts the lumped-parameter equivalent of a single-phase or three-phase feeder, which is the model used in all voltage drop derivations. ```mermaid flowchart LR src["Source
V_sending_end"] -->|"Cable Length L km"| blockR["R per km
Resistance Component
rho L by A"] blockR --> blockX["X per km
Reactance Component
typically 0.08 ohm per km"] blockX --> load["Load Terminal
V_receiving_end
Connected to Load"] load -.->|"Return path
Neutral or 2nd phase"| src style src fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#ff8c00 style load fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#4682b4 style blockR fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#daa520 style blockX fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#daa520 ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Voltage Drop Design Workflow The flowchart below maps the **engineering decision process** followed in a real KTU design project, from the moment a load specification is received to the moment the cable size is finalized. ```mermaid flowchart TD startA["Step 1
Receive Load Specification
kW, p.f., V, run length L"] --> stepB["Step 2
Compute Load Current I
I equals P by root 3 V cos phi"] stepB --> stepC["Step 3
Set Permissible dV
3 percent lighting or 5 percent power"] stepC --> stepD["Step 4
Compute Required A
using VD formula ignoring X"] stepD --> stepE["Step 5
Apply Current Carrying Capacity
Check CCC for selected A"] stepE --> decisionA{"A from VD
vs A from CCC
which is larger?"} decisionA -- "VD larger" --> stepF["Step 6a
Select A from VD"] decisionA -- "CCC larger" --> stepG["Step 6b
Select A from CCC"] decisionA -- "Both equal" --> stepH["Step 6c
Either value is fine"] stepF --> stepI["Step 7
Round up to next standard size
from IS 3961 Table"] stepG --> stepI stepH --> stepI stepI --> stepJ["Step 8
Final Verification
Recompute actual dV and
confirm less than allowable"] stepJ --> stopEnd["Step 9
Document and Release Cable Schedule"] style startA fill:#ffebcd,stroke:#ff8c00 style stepI fill:#98fb98,stroke:#006400 style stopEnd fill:#87cefa,stroke:#00008b style decisionA fill:#fffacd,stroke:#b8860b ``` ## 4.4 Distribution Hierarchy — Permissible Voltage Drop Allocation The figure below visualizes how the **5% statutory total** voltage drop is **typically allocated** across the LT distribution network from the secondary of the distribution transformer to the farthest consumer terminal. ```mermaid flowchart LR tx["11 kV / 415 V
Distribution Transformer
Secondary Bus"] -->|"Drop 1 percent
service line"| sl["Service Main Cable
from TX to Building Main"] sl -->|"Drop 1.5 percent
submain"| sm["Submain Cable
Floor Distribution Board"] sm -->|"Drop 1.5 percent
final circuit"| fc["Final Circuit
to Load Terminal"] fc --> load["Lamp / Motor / Socket
Receiving End Voltage"] tx -.->|"Total drop 4 percent
from TX to consumer"| load style tx fill:#ffd700,stroke:#b8860b style sl fill:#ffa07a,stroke:#8b0000 style sm fill:#ffa07a,stroke:#8b0000 style fc fill:#ffa07a,stroke:#8b0000 style load fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Heuristic (KTU Standard Practice):** Although IE Rules permit up to 5% total drop, modern KTU-design and CBIP manuals recommend allocating only **2% to 3% to the final sub-circuits**, so that even if a submain adds 1.5% and a service line adds 1%, the total stays at **4% to 5%** with margin for future load growth. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define voltage drop. State the permissible limit as per IE Rules. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** Voltage drop is the reduction in electrical potential between the sending end and the receiving end of a conductor carrying current, due to the impedance of the conductor itself. $$\Delta V = V_{sending} - V_{receiving}$$ **Permissible Limits (as per IE Rules & IS 732):** - The drop in any installation, measured from the consumer's terminals to any point in the installation, must **not exceed 3%** of the declared voltage when the conductors are carrying full-load current. - For a combined lighting and power load, the total drop from the transformer secondary to the farthest consumer must not exceed **5%**. **[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark limits]** --- ### Question 2: Why is voltage drop more critical in low-power-factor installations? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** For a given active power P, the line current I is inversely proportional to the power factor: $$I = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V \cdot \cos\phi}$$ When cos φ decreases, I increases, and since voltage drop is directly proportional to I: $$\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot (R \cos\phi + X \sin\phi)$$ The drop rises on two fronts: 1. **Larger I** due to smaller cos φ. 2. **Larger sin φ term** multiplying the reactance. **Conclusion:** A 0.7 p.f. load draws **20% more current** than a 0.85 p.f. load for the same kW, and incurs proportionally greater cable voltage drop. Hence improving the power factor via capacitor banks is a direct remedy for excessive voltage drop. **[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 mark formula + 1 mark explanation + 1 mark conclusion]** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` A single-phase 230 V, 50 Hz, 5 kW air-conditioner load is to be fed from a distribution board located 35 m away. The power factor of the load is 0.85 lagging. The supply is to be provided using a 2-core copper cable. Permissible voltage drop is 4% of the rated voltage. #### (a) Calculate the load current and the required cable cross-section neglecting reactance. Assume copper conductor at 20°C with ρ = 17.2 Ω·mm²/km. (7 Marks) **Step 1: Compute the load current** For a single-phase load: $$I = \frac{P}{V \cdot \cos\phi} = \frac{5{,}000}{230 \cdot 0.85}$$ $$I = \frac{5{,}000}{195.5} = 25.58\,A$$ **Step 2: Determine the permissible voltage drop** $$\Delta V_{allow} = 4\% \cdot 230 = 9.2\,V$$ **Step 3: Apply the single-phase voltage drop formula (loop)** $$\Delta V = 2 \cdot I \cdot L \cdot R \cdot \cos\phi \quad \text{where} \quad R = \frac{\rho}{A}$$ Substituting: $$9.2 = 2 \cdot 25.58 \cdot 0.035 \cdot \frac{17.2}{A} \cdot 0.85$$ **Step 4: Compute the constant term** $$2 \cdot 25.58 \cdot 0.035 \cdot 17.2 \cdot 0.85 = 1.7906 \cdot 14.62 = 26.18$$ **Step 5: Solve for A** $$A = \frac{26.18}{9.2} = 2.85\,mm^2$$ **Step 6: Select the next standard cable size** From the standard IS 3961 series, the next size above 2.85 mm² is **4 mm²**. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall 1:** Students often forget the **factor of 2** for single-phase systems (the loop is phase + neutral). This causes a doubled cross-section calculation error and forfeits 2 marks immediately. #### (b) Verify the CCC adequacy, and suggest whether derating for ambient temperature or grouping is required. (7 Marks) **Step 1: Look up CCC of 4 mm² Cu cable (PVC insulated, single-core, 2-core)** From IS 3961 / IS 1554: - **Cu 4 mm², PVC, 2-core, installed in conduit: CCC = 27 A** (at 30°C ambient) **Step 2: Compare with load current** Load current = 25.58 A < 27 A → **Adequate from CCC perspective.** **Step 3: Check ambient temperature derating** If the cable runs in a **trunking at 45°C ambient**, the derating factor (from IS 3961 Table 4) is approximately **0.87**. $$CCC_{derated} = 27 \cdot 0.87 = 23.49\,A$$ **Step 4: Check adequacy after derating** 23.49 A < 25.58 A → **NOT ADEQUATE.** **Step 5: Action — select the next size up** The next standard size is **6 mm² Cu cable**, with CCC = 35 A (PVC, 2-core). After derating: $$CCC_{derated} = 35 \cdot 0.87 = 30.45\,A \geq 25.58\,A \quad \checkmark$$ **Conclusion:** Select **6 mm² Cu, 2-core, PVC cable** to satisfy both voltage drop and CCC criteria. **Step 6: Recompute actual voltage drop for 6 mm² cable** $$\Delta V = 2 \cdot 25.58 \cdot 0.035 \cdot \frac{17.2}{6} \cdot 0.85$$ $$\Delta V = 1.7906 \cdot \frac{14.62}{6} = 1.7906 \cdot 2.437 = 4.36\,V$$ $$\% \Delta V = \frac{4.36}{230} \cdot 100 = 1.9\% \quad \checkmark \text{(well within 4\%)}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall 2:** Students often **stop at Step 6(a)** and never verify the CCC. Voltage drop is only one of the two criteria. The other is current carrying capacity, and **derating factors** (grouping, ambient, soil thermal resistivity) are frequently missed. Losing 3 to 4 marks here is common. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` A 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz industrial load of 50 kW at 0.80 p.f. lagging is to be supplied from a substation 120 m away using a 3.5-core aluminium cable. Permissible voltage drop is 4%. #### (a) Compute the cable cross-section assuming cable reactance is 0.08 Ω/km and verify it against the standard size. (7 Marks) **Step 1: Load current** $$I = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V \cdot \cos\phi} = \frac{50{,}000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 415 \cdot 0.80}$$ $$I = \frac{50{,}000}{574.7} = 86.99\,A$$ **Step 2: Permissible voltage drop** $$\Delta V_{allow} = 4\% \cdot 415 = 16.6\,V$$ **Step 3: First-pass estimate ignoring X** $$\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot \frac{\rho}{A} \cdot \cos\phi$$ $$16.6 = \sqrt{3} \cdot 86.99 \cdot 0.12 \cdot \frac{28.2}{A} \cdot 0.80$$ Constant term: $$\sqrt{3} \cdot 86.99 \cdot 0.12 \cdot 28.2 \cdot 0.80 = 1.732 \cdot 86.99 \cdot 0.12 \cdot 22.56$$ $$= 1.732 \cdot 86.99 \cdot 2.7072 = 1.732 \cdot 235.50 = 408.0$$ **Step 4: Solve for A** $$A = \frac{408.0}{16.6} = 24.58\,mm^2$$ **Step 5: Select next standard size — 25 mm² Al** > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall 3:** Students sometimes confuse **ρ for Al in Ω·m (2.82 × 10⁻⁸)** with **Ω·mm²/km (28.2)**. Using the wrong ρ produces a cross-section error of 10× and is an instant 4-mark deduction. #### (b) Recompute the actual voltage drop including reactance, and recommend corrective action if needed. (7 Marks) **Step 1: Look up R and X for 25 mm² Al XLPE cable** From standard cable tables: - R = 1.44 Ω/km (at 90°C conductor temperature) - X = 0.08 Ω/km **Step 2: Apply the full voltage drop formula** $$\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot (R \cos\phi + X \sin\phi)$$ Compute sin φ: $$\sin\phi = \sqrt{1 - 0.80^2} = 0.60$$ **Step 3: Substitute values** $$\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \cdot 86.99 \cdot 0.12 \cdot (1.44 \cdot 0.80 + 0.08 \cdot 0.60)$$ $$= 1.732 \cdot 86.99 \cdot 0.12 \cdot (1.152 + 0.048)$$ $$= 1.732 \cdot 86.99 \cdot 0.12 \cdot 1.20$$ $$= 1.732 \cdot 86.99 \cdot 0.144 = 1.732 \cdot 12.527 = 21.70\,V$$ **Step 4: Compute percentage drop** $$\% \Delta V = \frac{21.70}{415} \cdot 100 = 5.23\%$$ **Step 5: Compare with allowable** 5.23% > 4% allowed → **NOT ADEQUATE.** **Step 6: Corrective action options** **Option A: Increase cable size to 35 mm² Al** - R = 1.04 Ω/km, X = 0.08 Ω/km - ΔV = √3 · 86.99 · 0.12 · (1.04·0.80 + 0.08·0.60) = 18.49 V → 4.46% (still slightly over) **Option B: Increase to 50 mm² Al** - R = 0.73 Ω/km - ΔV = √3 · 86.99 · 0.12 · (0.73·0.80 + 0.08·0.60) = 14.60 V → 3.52% (within limit) ✓ **Option C: Improve power factor to 0.95 by adding capacitors** - I_new = 50,000 / (√3 · 415 · 0.95) = 73.2 A - sin φ_new = 0.312 - ΔV = √3 · 73.2 · 0.12 · (1.44·0.95 + 0.08·0.312) = 19.32 V → 4.66% (still over for 25 mm²) **Option D: Use copper instead of aluminium** For **25 mm² Cu cable:** R = 0.727 Ω/km, X = 0.08 Ω/km ΔV = √3 · 86.99 · 0.12 · (0.727·0.80 + 0.08·0.60) = 15.19 V → 3.66% ✓ **Recommended Action:** **50 mm² Al XLPE cable** (most economical) or **25 mm² Cu XLPE cable** (compact, higher cost) — both satisfy the 4% voltage drop limit. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall 4:** When the answer comes out **above the permissible limit**, students often increase cable size by **one standard step** (e.g., 25 → 35) and stop. They fail to **recompute** and verify that the next size actually brings the drop below 4%. Always iterate until satisfied. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for Voltage Drop (KTU Module 3):** - **Definition:** ΔV = V_send − V_receive; always positive in magnitude. - **Statutory Limit:** 3% lighting / 5% power / 5% total (TX to consumer) as per **IS 732** and **IE Rule 48**. - **Single-Phase Loop Formula:** ΔV = 2·I·L·(R cos φ + X sin φ) — the factor **2** is for the phase + neutral return loop. - **Three-Phase Formula:** ΔV = √3·I·L·(R cos φ + X sin φ) — no factor of 2 (no neutral current in balanced 3-phase). - **Approximate vs Exact:** The approximate formula is universally used in KTU; the exact phasor formula is only for high-precision HT design. - **R = ρL/A:** This is the **single most important** formula. Memorize it and the unit conversion **ρ [Ω·mm²/km] = ρ [Ω·m] × 10⁶**. - **Standard ρ values (at 20°C):** Cu = **17.2** Ω·mm²/km, Al = **28.2** Ω·mm²/km. - **Temperature correction:** R_T = R_20 [1 + α(T − 20)]; for XLPE 90°C operation, multiply R_20 by ≈ 1.28 for Cu. - **Voltage drop is NOT a heating criterion** — that is the CCC. A cable can be CCC-OK and VD-FAIL, or VD-OK and CCC-FAIL. Always check both. - **Larger cable sizes are required** when: 1. The run is long. 2. The load current is high. 3. The power factor is low. 4. The conductor material is aluminium (vs copper). - **Design ordering principle:** Compute A from VD → compute A from CCC → **select the larger** of the two and round up to the next standard size. - **Remedies for excessive VD:** 1. Increase cable cross-section. 2. Improve power factor using capacitor banks. 3. Use copper instead of aluminium. 4. Reduce the run length (relocate MCC, substation). 5. Use a higher distribution voltage (step-up transformer). - **Always iterate:** For cables > 25 mm² or HT systems, the **reactance cannot be ignored**. First ignore X, then **verify** with both R and X, then **adjust** if necessary. - **Motor starting consideration:** During direct-on-line starting, the motor draws 6× to 8× full-load current. The voltage drop during starting must be checked to ensure **contactors do not drop out** (typically > 80% of rated voltage at the motor terminals). - **Document units rigorously:** L in km, ρ in Ω·mm²/km, A in mm², I in A, ΔV in V. One unit slip = whole problem wrong. - **Memorize the standard cable sizes:** **1.5, 2.5, 4, 6, 10, 16, 25, 35, 50, 70, 95, 120, 150, 185, 240, 300, 400, 500, 630 mm²** — these are from **IS 3961** and **IS 1554** (PVC) / **IS 7098** (XLPE) for both Cu and Al. - **Exam Tip (KTU):** In Part B problems, the 14 marks are split **7 + 7**. Part (a) is typically the direct calculation; part (b) is the verification / corrective action. Address both. Skipping part (b) = losing 7 marks minimum.

topics.title: Short Circuit Withstand Capacity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Short Circuit Withstand Capacity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔌 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Short Circuit Withstand Capacity (SCWC)** is the maximum prospective short-circuit current (both peak and short-time/RMS value) that an electrical equipment, conductor, or switching device can carry for a specified duration **without sustaining damage** — either permanent (thermal) or mechanical (dynamic/electrodynamic) — that would render it unfit for further service.

In the KTU 2024 scheme terminology, the short circuit withstand capacity is expressed using **two interrelated ratings**:

- **Peak Withstand Current (`ip` or `I_dynamic`):** The instantaneous peak value of the first major loop of the short-circuit current that the equipment can withstand **mechanically** without deformation, contact separation, or welding.
- **Short-Time Withstand Current (`Icw`):** The RMS value of the short-circuit current that the equipment can carry for a defined period (typically **1 second**, sometimes 3 s) **thermally** without exceeding permitted temperature limits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:** *"Short Circuit Withstand Capacity is the ability of an electrical apparatus to carry, for a specified short time and under prescribed conditions, a current equal to the prospective short-circuit current, without abnormal heating, mechanical stress, or loss of continuity."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Dam Analogy

Imagine a **concrete dam** built across a river.

- The **river's normal flow** = the *rated load current* of the equipment.
- A **flash flood** downstream = a *short circuit* — an enormous, sudden surge of current.
- The **dam wall's strength** = the *short circuit withstand capacity*.
- If the dam wall is **dynamically weak**, the first huge wave (the *peak current*) shatters it. → This represents *peak/electrodynamic failure*.
- If the dam wall can survive the first impact but the **constant water pressure over hours** erodes it, it collapses *thermally*. → This represents *thermal/I²t failure*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Withstand" and not "Carry"?** Because a healthy protection system will trip the breaker in milliseconds. The equipment only has to *tolerate* the fault long enough for the protective device to clear it — it never has to *sustain* the fault indefinitely.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Peak factor (3-phase fault) | `κ` | **1.8 (typical)** | `ip = κ √2 Isc` |
| Peak factor (theoretical max) | `κ` | **2.0** | When R/X → 0 |
| Conductor heating constant — Copper | `K_cu` | **142 A·s^½/mm²** | Used in I²t cable sizing |
| Conductor heating constant — Aluminium | `K_al` | **94 A·s^½/mm²** | Used in I²t cable sizing |
| Standard SC duration | `t` | **1 s** | Default `Icw` rating time |
| Voltage factor (IEC 60909) | `c` | 1.05 / 1.1 | For `Ipk = c · Un / (√3 · Zk)` |
| Network frequency | `f` | **50 Hz** | For Indian/KTU context |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a Short Circuit Current

When a three-phase short circuit occurs, the fault current is not a clean sine wave. It consists of two superimposed components:

1. **Symmetrical AC Component (`is`):** A sinusoidal waveform whose magnitude decays exponentially from initial value `I″k` to steady-state value `Ik` due to the synchronous generators' sub-transient, transient, and synchronous reactances.
2. **DC (Asymmetrical) Component (`idc`):** An exponentially decaying DC offset that depends on the instant the fault occurs relative to the voltage zero crossing. Its decay constant is the `X/R` ratio of the fault loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the DC offset matters in KTU board exams:** The *peak current* `ip` is governed almost entirely by the DC component. The *breaking current* `Ib` (the current the breaker must interrupt at contact separation time) is also affected by the residual DC component, hence the introduction of the **asymmetry factor μ (mu)**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

### Step 1 — Determine the Symmetrical RMS Initial Short Circuit Current

This is the **first-cycle RMS value** of the AC component, assuming the fault occurs at the instant producing the *maximum* DC offset.

The fundamental single-line expression used throughout KTU Module 3 is:

$$I_{sc} = \frac{U}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{sc}}$$

where `U` is the line-to-line voltage, and `Zsc` is the total short-circuit impedance (Rs + jXs) of the network up to the fault point.

### Step 2 — Compute the Peak (Make) Current

The instantaneous peak current is the sum of the AC peak and the DC offset at `t = 0`:

$$i_p = \sqrt{2} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot \left(1 + e^{-\pi \cdot R / X}\right)$$

For convenience, this is simplified using the **peak factor κ**:

$$i_p = \kappa \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot I_{sc}$$

For systems with low R/X (typical transformers), `κ ≈ 1.8`. For purely inductive networks (R → 0), `κ → 2`.

### Step 3 — Compute the Breaking Current

The breaker contacts separate after a finite time delay (typically 20 ms to 100 ms). By then, the DC component has partially decayed. The breaking current is:

$$I_b = \mu \cdot I_{sc}$$

where `μ` is the **asymmetry factor**, a function of breaker minimum tripping time `t_min` and the `R/X` ratio of the fault loop.

### Step 4 — Thermal Withstand (I²t Criterion)

The heat generated in a conductor during the short circuit is governed by **Joule's Law**:

$$Q_{fault} = I^2 \cdot R \cdot t$$

To limit the conductor temperature rise to a safe value, the cross-section `A` of the conductor must satisfy:

$$I^2 \cdot t \le K^2 \cdot A^2$$

Rearranged for the *minimum cable cross-section*:

$$A_{min} = \frac{I_{sc} \cdot \sqrt{t}}{K}$$

### Step 5 — Dynamic (Mechanical) Withstand

The electrodynamic force between current-carrying conductors varies as the **square of the instantaneous current** and reverses direction twice per cycle. Busbars and contacts must therefore withstand:

$$F = \frac{\mu_0}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{i_p^2 \cdot L}{d}$$

where `L` is the length of the parallel conductors and `d` is the spacing. This force is *transient* (peaks within the first half-cycle) and drives the **peak withstand rating**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Condition / Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Symmetrical short circuit current | $I_{sc} = U / (\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{sc})$ | `U` in V, `Z` in Ω | Initial RMS AC component |
| 2 | Peak short circuit current | $i_p = \kappa \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot I_{sc}$ | κ between 1.0 and 2.0 | For busbar/dynamic design |
| 3 | Peak factor (general) | $\kappa = 1.02 + 0.98 \cdot e^{-3R/X}$ | IEC standard | For 3-phase faults |
| 4 | Breaking current | $I_b = \mu \cdot I_{sc}$ | μ from tables (0–1) | Circuit breaker selection |
| 5 | Thermal energy limit | $I^2 \cdot t \le K^2 \cdot A^2$ | t in seconds | Cable sizing |
| 6 | Min. cable CSA | $A = I_{sc} \cdot \sqrt{t} / K$ | K = 142 (Cu) or 94 (Al) | Cable short circuit test |
| 7 | Source SC MVA | $MVA_{sc} = \sqrt{3} \cdot U \cdot I_{sc} / 10^6$ | MVA | Utility fault level |
| 8 | % Impedance method | $I_{sc} = I_{fl} \cdot 100 / (\%Z)$ | Transformer-fed | Quick approximations |
| 9 | Force on busbars | $F = (\mu_0 / 2\pi) \cdot i_p^2 \cdot L / d$ | Newtons | Mechanical bracing |
| 10 | Asymmetry decay | $i_{dc}(t) = \sqrt{2} I_{sc} \cdot e^{-t / (X / \omega R)}$ | Function of t | Breaker trip coordination |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU boards, the value of **κ = 1.8** for 3-phase and **κ = 1.41 (√2)** for the symmetric-only case are the two numbers you will use 90% of the time. Memorize them as `1.8` and `1.414`.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Switchgear Selection:** Every circuit breaker, LBS, and contactor is rated by `Icw` (1 s or 3 s) and `Icm` (making capacity = `ip`). Under-specification leads to **arc-flash explosions**.
- **Cable Sizing:** The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes that a cable can carry 100 A normally but may need to be oversized purely to *survive* a 25 kA fault for 1 second.
- **Busbar Design:** LT panels in Kerala industrial estates frequently use 100×10 mm copper busbars; their mechanical and thermal ratings dictate panel layout.
- **Transformer Selection:** The transformer's own `Isc` (the current it can deliver into a bolted fault) sets the upper bound for downstream SCWC.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Peak Short Circuit Current

The short-circuit current in a purely inductive network during a bolted three-phase fault at `t = 0` is governed by the differential equation:

$$u(t) = R \cdot i(t) + L \cdot \frac{di(t)}{dt}$$

Substituting `u(t) = U_m \cdot \sin(\omega t + \theta)` where `θ` is the closing angle of the fault:

$$U_m \cdot \sin(\omega t + \theta) = R \cdot i(t) + L \cdot \frac{di(t)}{dt}$$

The general solution has the form:

$$i(t) = i_{ac}(t) + i_{dc}(t)$$

### Step 1 — Steady-State AC Solution

Assuming the circuit had been operating in steady state, the AC part is:

$$i_{ac}(t) = \frac{U_m}{Z} \cdot \sin(\omega t + \theta - \phi)$$

where `Z = √(R² + (ωL)²)` and `φ = tan⁻¹(ωL / R)`.

### Step 2 — DC Transient Solution

To eliminate the DC component in steady state, the natural response of the RL circuit is:

$$i_{dc}(t) = A \cdot e^{-R t / L}$$

The constant `A` is determined by ensuring the current is continuous at `t = 0` (assume pre-fault current = 0):

$$i(0^+) = 0 \Rightarrow A = -\frac{U_m}{Z} \cdot \sin(\theta - \phi)$$

### Step 3 — Combined Expression

$$i(t) = \frac{U_m}{Z} \cdot \left[ \sin(\omega t + \theta - \phi) - \sin(\theta - \phi) \cdot e^{-R t / L} \right]$$

The **worst-case** peak occurs when `θ - φ = -π/2` (i.e., the fault closes at voltage zero, where di/dt is maximum):

$$i(t) = \frac{U_m}{Z} \cdot \left[ -\cos(\omega t) + e^{-R t / L} \right]$$

### Step 4 — Maximum Instantaneous Value (Peak)

The peak occurs at the first half-cycle, approximately at `t = T/2 = 1/(2f)`. With `f = 50 Hz`, the decay factor is `e^{-R · 0.01 / L} = e^{-3R/X}` (since `X = ωL = 2πfL` and `R·T/2 = R/(2f) = πR/X`, but the standard IEC formula uses `3` because of the actual asymmetry definition).

Therefore:

$$i_p = \frac{U_m}{Z} \cdot \left( 1 + e^{-3R/X} \right)$$

Converting `U_m/Z` to RMS:

$$U_m / Z = \sqrt{2} \cdot I_{sc}$$

Hence the **final textbook expression**:

$$\boxed{i_p = \sqrt{2} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot \left( 1 + e^{-3R/X} \right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** The factor `(1 + e^(-3R/X))` is precisely the **peak factor κ** in its mathematically derived form. Many students incorrectly write `i_p = √2 Isc` only — this gives the AC peak, missing the DC component that *doubles* the force. Always include the `1 + e^(-3R/X)` term.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Minimum Cable Cross-Section from I²t Criterion

The Joule heating in a conductor of resistance `R = ρ L / A` during a short circuit of duration `t` is:

$$Q = \int_0^t i^2(\tau) \cdot R \, d\tau = R \cdot I^2_{sc} \cdot t$$

(Using RMS value `Isc` as the equivalent heat source.)

The permissible energy is governed by the adiabatic heating limit:

$$Q_{max} = c \cdot m \cdot \Delta T = c \cdot (\rho_{m} \cdot A \cdot L) \cdot \Delta T$$

Setting `Q ≤ Q_max`:

$$R \cdot I^2_{sc} \cdot t \le c \cdot \rho_{m} \cdot A \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$$

Substitute `R = ρ_e · L / A`:

$$(\rho_e L / A) \cdot I^2_{sc} \cdot t \le c \cdot \rho_{m} \cdot A \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$$

The lengths cancel (adiabatic assumption — no heat loss):

$$I^2_{sc} \cdot t \le (c \cdot \rho_{m} \cdot \Delta T / \rho_e) \cdot A^2$$

Defining the material constant `K`:

$$K = \sqrt{c \cdot \rho_{m} \cdot \Delta T / \rho_e}$$

We obtain the KTU-IEC standard:

$$\boxed{A_{min} = \frac{I_{sc} \cdot \sqrt{t}}{K}}$$

For copper conductor with initial temperature 90 °C, final short-circuit temperature 250 °C:

$$K_{Cu} = 142 \text{ A} \cdot \text{s}^{1/2} / \text{mm}^2$$

For aluminium:

$$K_{Al} = 94 \text{ A} \cdot \text{s}^{1/2} / \text{mm}^2$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Style)

**Problem:** A 415 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz industrial busbar is fed through a 500 kVA transformer with 5% impedance. Calculate (a) the symmetrical short circuit current, (b) the peak short circuit current assuming R/X = 0.1, and (c) the minimum copper cable cross-section if the fault clearance time is 0.5 s.

### Solution

**Step (a) — Symmetrical SC Current**

Full load current of transformer:

$$I_{fl} = \frac{500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 415} = 695.6 \text{ A}$$

Symmetrical short circuit current:

$$I_{sc} = I_{fl} \times \frac{100}{\%Z} = 695.6 \times \frac{100}{5} = 13{,}912 \text{ A} \approx 13.9 \text{ kA}$$

**Step (b) — Peak Current**

Peak factor:

$$\kappa = 1.02 + 0.98 \cdot e^{-3 \times 0.1} = 1.02 + 0.98 \cdot e^{-0.3} = 1.02 + 0.98 \times 0.7408 = 1.746$$

Peak current:

$$i_p = \kappa \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot I_{sc} = 1.746 \times 1.414 \times 13{,}912 = 34{,}348 \text{ A} \approx 34.35 \text{ kA}$$

**Step (c) — Minimum Copper Cable Cross-Section**

$$A_{min} = \frac{I_{sc} \cdot \sqrt{t}}{K} = \frac{13{,}912 \times \sqrt{0.5}}{142} = \frac{13{,}912 \times 0.7071}{142} = \frac{9{,}835}{142} \approx 69.3 \text{ mm}^2$$

Selecting the next standard size: **70 mm² copper cable**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** The university frequently frames this as a *one-shot combined calculation* carrying 7 marks for (a)+(b) and 7 marks for (c). Always show all substitutions numerically — even arithmetic steps must be visible.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How Equipment Ratings Relate to Fault Level

| Equipment | Rating Parameter | Typical Value | Linked to Fault? |
|---|---|---|---|
| HT Circuit Breaker | Breaking capacity (`Icu`, `Ics`) | 25 kA, 40 kA, 63 kA | Must exceed `Ib` |
| LT ACB | Short-time withstand (`Icw`) | 35 kA / 1 s | Must exceed `Isc` for trip time |
| LT ACB | Making capacity (`Icm`) | 2.2 × `Icw` | Must exceed `ip` |
| Cable | I²t withstand | Computed via K | Must exceed `Isc²·t` |
| Busbar (LT panel) | Peak dynamic (`I_pk`) | 50–110 kA peak | Must exceed `ip` |
| CT | Dynamic current (`I_dyn`) | 2.5 × I_th | Must exceed `ip` |
| Contactor | Conditional SC current | 10–50 kA | With back-up fuse/breaker |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Short Circuit Withstand Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Short Circuit Event] --> B[Symmetrical AC Component]
    A --> C[DC Asymmetrical Component]
    B --> D[Isc RMS Value]
    C --> E[i_dc Decays with X and R]
    D --> F[Peak Current ip]
    D --> G[Breaking Current Ib]
    E --> F
    E --> G
    F --> H[Dynamic Withstand Check]
    G --> I[Thermal Withstand Check]
    H --> J[Busbar and Contact Design]
    I --> K[Cable Cross Section Sizing]
    J --> L[Equipment Pass or Fail]
    K --> L
    L --> M{Survives Fault?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Continue Service]
    M -->|No| O[Equipment Damage]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Protection Coordination Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Utility Grid] --> Q[Step Down Transformer]
    Q --> R[Main LT ACB]
    R --> S[Sub Dist Board]
    S --> T[Branch MCB or MCCB]
    T --> U[Load Cable]
    U --> V[End Load]
    P -->|Isc1| W[Fault Point Near Transformer]
    S -->|Isc2| X[Fault Point Mid Network]
    T -->|Isc3| Y[Fault Point Near Load]
    W -->|Highest SC Level| Z[Short Circuit Withstand Demands]
    X -->|Medium SC Level| Z
    Y -->|Lowest SC Level| Z
    Z --> AA[Equipment Must Withstand]
    AA --> AB{Cable Sized for I2t?}
    AB -->|Yes| AC[Safe Operation]
    AB -->|No| AD[Thermal Failure]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Tree — Asymmetry Factor μ for Different Breaker Trip Times

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Symmetrical Isc] --> B1[Trip Time 0.02s]
    A0 --> B2[Trip Time 0.05s]
    A0 --> B3[Trip Time 0.10s]
    A0 --> B4[Trip Time 0.25s]
    A0 --> B5[Trip Time 1.00s]
    B1 --> C1[mu equals 0.95 for small R over X]
    B2 --> C2[mu equals 0.85 for small R over X]
    B3 --> C3[mu equals 0.75 for small R over X]
    B4 --> C4[mu equals 0.55 for small R over X]
    B5 --> C5[mu equals 0.30 for small R over X]
    C1 --> D[Breaking Current Ib equals mu times Isc]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> E[Breaker Breaking Capacity Selection]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Mermaid Diagram:** Each path shows how a single `Isc` value (which is the same for a given network) yields different `μ` (and hence different `Ib`) depending on how *fast* the breaker interrupts. Faster breakers see more DC offset → higher `Ib` → more onerous selection. This is why **current-limiting breakers** are preferred for high SC levels.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Short Circuit Withstand Capacity" of an electrical equipment. Distinguish between peak withstand current and short-time withstand current.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Short Circuit Withstand Capacity is the ability of an electrical equipment to carry, for a specified short time and under prescribed conditions, a current equal to the prospective short-circuit current, without abnormal heating, mechanical stress, or loss of continuity. **(1 mark)**

- **Peak Withstand Current (`ip`):** The instantaneous peak value of the first major half-cycle of the short-circuit current that the equipment can withstand **mechanically** (electrodynamic forces) without damage. **(1 mark)**
- **Short-Time Withstand Current (`Icw`):** The RMS value of the short-circuit current that the equipment can carry for a defined period (typically 1 second) **thermally** without exceeding the permissible temperature rise. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the expression for the peak factor `κ` for a three-phase short circuit. What is its maximum theoretical value and the typical value used in practice?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The peak factor is given by:

$$\kappa = 1.02 + 0.98 \cdot e^{-3R/X}$$

**(1 mark for the formula)**

- **Maximum theoretical value:** When R/X → 0 (purely inductive), `κ → 2.0`. **(1 mark)**
- **Typical practical value:** For systems with low R/X (transformer-fed), `κ = 1.8` is widely used. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** In the End Semester Examination (ESE), each Module carries a single 14-mark question with internal choice (either-or). The question typically splits as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the peak short-circuit current `i_p` in a 3-phase system, clearly showing the role of the DC component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1 MVA, 11 kV / 415 V, 50 Hz, 3-phase transformer with 6% impedance supplies an LT switchgear panel. If the total cable length from the transformer secondary to the main ACB is 10 metres, calculate: **(7 marks)**
  - (i) The symmetrical short circuit current at the ACB terminals.
  - (ii) The peak short circuit current, assuming R/X of the fault loop is 0.15.
  - (iii) The minimum cross-section of the copper cable required for a fault clearance time of 0.2 s.

#### **Model Solution (Question A)**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Write the circuit equation for an RL fault circuit:

$$U_m \sin(\omega t + \theta) = R \cdot i(t) + L \cdot \frac{di(t)}{dt}$$

The general solution is the sum of steady-state AC and transient DC components:

$$i(t) = i_{ac}(t) + i_{dc}(t)$$

The steady-state AC component:

$$i_{ac}(t) = \frac{U_m}{Z} \sin(\omega t + \theta - \phi)$$

The DC transient:

$$i_{dc}(t) = A \cdot e^{-Rt/L}$$

Apply initial condition `i(0) = 0` (no pre-fault current):

$$0 = \frac{U_m}{Z} \sin(\theta - \phi) + A \Rightarrow A = -\frac{U_m}{Z} \sin(\theta - \phi)$$

For the **worst case**, the fault occurs at `θ - φ = -π/2`, which gives the maximum asymmetry:

$$i(t) = \frac{U_m}{Z} \left[ -\cos(\omega t) + e^{-Rt/L} \right]$$

The peak occurs in the first half-cycle. At `t = T/2`, the cosine term equals `+1`. Substituting `R·T/2 = 3R/X` (using the IEC relationship between the time constant and X/R):

$$i_p = \frac{U_m}{Z} \left( 1 + e^{-3R/X} \right) = \sqrt{2} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot \left( 1 + e^{-3R/X} \right)$$

**(7 marks — distribute as: circuit equation 1, AC part 1.5, DC part 1.5, initial condition 1, worst case + final 2)**

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Symmetrical SC current (2 marks):**

Full-load current on LT side:

$$I_{fl} = \frac{1 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 415} = 1391.4 \text{ A}$$

Short circuit current:

$$I_{sc} = I_{fl} \times \frac{100}{\%Z} = 1391.4 \times \frac{100}{6} = 23{,}190 \text{ A} \approx 23.2 \text{ kA}$$

**(ii) Peak current (2 marks):**

Peak factor:

$$\kappa = 1.02 + 0.98 \cdot e^{-3 \times 0.15} = 1.02 + 0.98 \cdot e^{-0.45} = 1.02 + 0.98 \times 0.6376 = 1.645$$

Peak current:

$$i_p = 1.645 \times \sqrt{2} \times 23{,}190 = 1.645 \times 1.414 \times 23{,}190 = 53{,}946 \text{ A} \approx 53.95 \text{ kA}$$

**(iii) Minimum cable cross-section (3 marks):**

$$A_{min} = \frac{I_{sc} \cdot \sqrt{t}}{K_{Cu}} = \frac{23{,}190 \times \sqrt{0.2}}{142} = \frac{23{,}190 \times 0.4472}{142} = \frac{10{,}371}{142} = 73.04 \text{ mm}^2$$

Select the next standard size: **95 mm² copper cable**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the concepts of *dynamic withstand capacity* and *thermal withstand capacity* of a switchgear panel. How are they related to the peak short-circuit current and short-time current respectively? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 415 V LT busbar system must carry a prospective RMS short-circuit current of 30 kA. The protection system clears the fault in 0.5 seconds. Design the copper busbar cross-section and determine the electrodynamic force between two parallel 1 m long busbars spaced 50 mm apart. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (Question B)**

**Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation (7 marks):**

- **Dynamic Withstand Capacity:** This refers to the **mechanical strength** of the busbar system, supports, and breaker contacts to resist the electrodynamic forces generated by the **peak** (first half-cycle) short-circuit current. Since the force is proportional to `i²`, and `i²_p` can be 4× the steady-state value, the dynamic stress is the *first* and *most severe* load. It is checked against `ip` using the **peak current formula** `i_p = κ √2 Isc`. **(3.5 marks)**

- **Thermal Withstand Capacity:** This is the ability of the current-carrying parts to carry the RMS short-circuit current for the duration of the fault **without exceeding the permissible temperature rise**. The energy input is `I²·t`, and the limit is `K²·A²`. It is checked against `Icw` (1-second or 3-second rating) using the **I²t formula**. **(3.5 marks)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Design (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Busbar cross-section (3 marks):**

Using `A_min = Isc √t / K`:

$$A_{min} = \frac{30{,}000 \times \sqrt{0.5}}{142} = \frac{30{,}000 \times 0.7071}{142} = \frac{21{,}213}{142} = 149.4 \text{ mm}^2$$

Select: **25 mm × 6 mm = 150 mm²** copper busbar (standard size).

**Step 2 — Peak current (1 mark):**

Assuming κ = 1.8 (typical):

$$i_p = 1.8 \times \sqrt{2} \times 30{,}000 = 1.8 \times 1.414 \times 30{,}000 = 76{,}371 \text{ A} \approx 76.37 \text{ kA}$$

**Step 3 — Electrodynamic force (3 marks):**

$$F = \frac{\mu_0}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{i_p^2 \cdot L}{d}$$

With `μ₀ / (2π) = 2 \times 10^{-7}` T·m/A:

$$F = 2 \times 10^{-7} \times \frac{(76{,}371)^2 \times 1.0}{0.05}$$

$$F = 2 \times 10^{-7} \times \frac{5.832 \times 10^9}{0.05} = 2 \times 10^{-7} \times 1.166 \times 10^{11} = 23{,}328 \text{ N}$$

Force: **F ≈ 23.3 kN** (or 2333 kgf equivalent).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Critical):**
> - **2 marks lost** if you forget to use the **peak factor κ** in force calculation. Use `ip`, not `Isc`. The force depends on *instantaneous* current, not RMS.
> - **1 mark lost** if `K` value is hard-coded incorrectly. For copper XLPE 90 °C: **142**. For PVC 70 °C: **115**.
> - **1 mark lost** if you skip the next-standard-size selection (e.g., writing 149.4 mm² instead of choosing 150 mm² standard).
> - **1 mark lost** if units are inconsistent (mixing kA and A). Always show one consistent unit set throughout the calculation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 15 Must-Know Bullets for Last-Minute Revision**

- ✅ **SCWC = ability to carry fault current without damage** — the cornerstone definition.
- ✅ **Two parameters** must always be checked: **`ip` (peak/dynamic)** and **`Icw` (RMS/thermal)**.
- ✅ **Peak factor κ = 1.02 + 0.98·e^(-3R/X)**; typical value **1.8**, maximum theoretical **2.0**.
- ✅ **Symmetrical SC current** formula: `Isc = U / (√3 · Zsc)`. For transformer-fed: `Isc = Ifl × 100 / %Z`.
- ✅ **Peak current** formula: `ip = κ · √2 · Isc`. This determines the **mechanical** design of busbars, supports, and CTs.
- ✅ **Breaking current** formula: `Ib = μ · Isc`. Depends on breaker trip delay and R/X ratio.
- ✅ **I²t criterion** for cable sizing: `Isc² · t ≤ K² · A²`. Solve as `A = Isc·√t / K`.
- ✅ **Material constants**: `K_Cu = 142`, `K_Al = 94` (for XLPE-insulated conductors, 90 °C to 250 °C).
- ✅ **Standard SC durations** for Icw rating: **1 second** (most common) or **3 seconds** (per IEC 62271).
- ✅ **Making capacity** of breaker: `Icm ≈ 2.2 × Icw` per IEC; must always exceed `ip`.
- ✅ **%Z method** is a quick engineering approximation; for precise work, use the **Ohmic impedance summation** method.
- ✅ **Electrodynamic force** between parallel conductors: `F = (μ₀/2π) · (i²_p · L / d)`. Force is *transient* and *doubly squared*.
- ✅ **KTU Board Rule:** Always select the **next standard cable size** above `A_min`. Never use undersized cables in final answer.
- ✅ **Asymmetry factor μ** decreases with longer breaker trip times (DC offset decays). Faster breakers = higher μ = larger `Ib`.
- ✅ **Three checks per KTU module 3 problems:** (1) Is `Icw ≥ Isc`? (2) Is `ip ≥ κ√2·Isc`? (3) Is `A_actual ≥ A_min`?

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Short Circuit Withstand Capacity**

topics.title: Standby DG Systems with AMF Panel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Standby DG Systems with AMF Panel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standby Diesel Generator (DG) System:** A Standby DG System is a *secondary/emergency power generating unit* installed in parallel to the *Utility (Mains) Supply* that automatically takes over the connected electrical load whenever the **normal mains supply fails** or its parameters (voltage/frequency) deviate beyond prescribed limits. As per **IS 1460:2000** and **IEEE 446 (Orange Book)** standards, a standby DG set is rated for **emergency/standby power** applications and is *not* intended for continuous duty operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AMF Panel (Automatic Mains Failure Panel):** An *intelligent microprocessor-based control panel* that continuously monitors the **mains (EB) supply**, automatically *starts* the diesel generator upon mains failure, *transfers* the load to the generator, and *re-transfers* the load back to the mains upon restoration, followed by a *controlled cool-down and shutdown* of the DG set — all **without any manual intervention**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the **Standby DG with AMF Panel** like a *fully automatic water pump with a smart controller* connected to an overhead tank (mains) and a sump (DG).

- The **mains supply** is like the **municipal water pipeline (overhead tank)** — it is your primary source.
- The **Diesel Generator (DG)** is the **sump/backup pump** sitting quietly in the basement — ready to start when needed.
- The **AMF Panel** is the **smart controller/sensor** — it constantly checks if overhead tank water (mains) is flowing. The moment the pipeline goes dry, it *automatically switches on* the sump pump, opens the right valves, and when pipeline water returns, it *gently hands back* the supply and shuts the pump down after a cool-down period.

**No human has to go and turn a switch — everything is automatic, reliable, and instantaneous.** That is exactly what an AMF-controlled standby DG system does for your building, hospital, data centre, or factory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Reference Metrics (KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight):**
> - **Voltage Sensing Tolerance:** ± 10% of nominal (e.g., for 415V → 374V to 457V)
> - **Frequency Sensing Tolerance:** ± 3% to ± 5% of 50 Hz
> - **Mains Failure Detection Time:** 0.5 sec to 3 sec
> - **DG Start Time (from command):** 5 sec to 15 sec
> - **Cooling Run Time after load transfer:** 5 min to 10 min
> - **AMF Controller Standard:** **IEC 60947-6-1** for automatic transfer switches
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Block-Level Decomposition of a Standby DG + AMF System

A complete standby power system can be broken into **four major sub-systems**:

1. **Prime Mover Subsystem** — The diesel engine (mechanical heart)
2. **Generator Subsystem** — The alternator (electrical heart) — usually a **brushless, self-excited, self-regulated** synchronous generator of **3-phase, 415V, 50Hz, 0.8 PF lagging**
3. **Fuel & Storage Subsystem** — Day tank (typically 8–10 hour capacity), bulk storage tank, fuel piping
4. **Control & Switching Subsystem** — The **AMF Panel** (the brain)

### 2.2 Why AMF Panel is Mandatory (KTU Favourite Question)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of AMF Justification:**
> 1. **Unattended Operation:** Critical loads (hospitals, IT, lifts, fire pumps) cannot wait for an operator to start a DG manually.
> 2. **Speed of Restoration:** Mains-to-DG changeover must happen within **15–30 seconds** to avoid equipment tripping or data loss.
> 3. **Safety:** Automatic isolation of mains and DG prevents *paralleling* (which is illegal and dangerous without synchronizing gear).

### 2.3 Sizing of Standby DG Set — KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET — DG SIZING**

| S.No | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|------|-----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | DG kVA Rating (Rule-1: Connected Load Method) | $D_{kVA} = \dfrac{P_{total} \times DF}{\eta \times pf \times 1000}$ | kVA | $P_{total}$ = total connected load in W, **DF** = Diversity Factor, $\eta$ = efficiency, pf = power factor |
| 2 | DG kVA Rating (Rule-2: Largest Motor Method) | $D_{kVA} = \dfrac{(X \times P_{L}) + P_{rest}}{\eta \times pf}$ | kVA | $X$ = motor starting multiplier (5–7 for DOL, 1.5–2.5 for Star-Delta), $P_L$ = largest motor rating |
| 3 | DG Recommended Loading | $D_{optimal} = 0.75 \times D_{rated}$ | kVA | DG should run at **75%** of rated capacity for best fuel efficiency & life |
| 4 | DG Voltage Dip Check | $\Delta V \approx \dfrac{X_d^{\prime\prime} \times kVA_{motor}}{kVA_{DG}}$ | % | Should be **< 15%** for motor starting |
| 5 | Fuel Consumption | $F = 0.21 \times P_{kVA} + 4.5$ (approx., for 75% load) | Litres/hr | Empirical for typical 4-stroke diesel |
| 6 | Battery Sizing (DG Starting) | $C = \dfrac{I_{CCA} \times T_{crank}}{1.2}$ | Ah | $I_{CCA}$ = Cold Cranking Amps, $T_{crank}$ = crank time (sec) |
| 7 | Day Tank Capacity | $V_{tank} = F \times H_{reserve}$ | Litres | $H_{reserve}$ = 8 to 10 hours typical |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Note on Pipes in Tables:** In markdown tables, the absolute value or 'such that' symbol is written using **$\vert$** or **$\mid$** to avoid breaking table syntax. Example: $X \vert$ must be written as $X \,\vert\,$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospitals (IS 14435 & IEEE 602):** DG + AMF ensures life-support equipment never loses power even for a fraction of a second beyond tolerable limits.
- **Data Centres (TIA-942):** AMF + UPS + DG forms a tiered redundancy; AMF initiates DG within 30 sec of UPS battery depletion.
- **High-rise Residential (NBC 2016, Part 4):** Mandatory for lifts, fire pumps, common area lighting, and pressurization fans.
- **Industrial Plants:** Protects continuous process industries (pharma, food, chemical) from batch losses during grid outages.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: DG Set Sizing for a Mixed Load

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Numerical):**
> A commercial complex has the following loads:
> - Lighting: **40 kW** (unity pf)
> - General Power (sockets, AC): **80 kW** at 0.8 pf lagging
> - Lift Motor: **15 kW** at 0.8 pf, **DOL starting**
> - Fire Pump Motor: **22 kW** at 0.85 pf, **Star-Delta starting**
> 
> Determine the **Standby DG kVA rating**. Assume DG efficiency = **88%**, Diversity Factor = **0.8**, and that motors are already running (running load, not starting).

**Step 1 — Convert each load to kVA:**

For Lighting (Unity pf):
$$S_{1} = \dfrac{P_{1}}{pf} = \dfrac{40}{1.0} = 40 \text{ kVA}$$

For General Power (0.8 pf):
$$S_{2} = \dfrac{80}{0.8} = 100 \text{ kVA}$$

For Lift Motor (0.8 pf):
$$S_{3} = \dfrac{15}{0.8} = 18.75 \text{ kVA}$$

For Fire Pump (0.85 pf):
$$S_{4} = \dfrac{22}{0.85} = 25.88 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the kVA (Arithmetic for diverse pf loads):**
$$S_{total} = S_{1} + S_{2} + S_{3} + S_{4}$$
$$S_{total} = 40 + 100 + 18.75 + 25.88 = 184.63 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Diversity Factor:**
$$S_{diversified} = S_{total} \times DF = 184.63 \times 0.8 = 147.7 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 4 — Account for DG Efficiency (output to input conversion):**
$$S_{DG,rated} = \dfrac{S_{diversified}}{\eta_{DG}} = \dfrac{147.7}{0.88}$$
$$S_{DG,rated} = 167.84 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 5 — Select the Next Standard Rating (Standard DG sizes as per KTU/CPWD practice are: 25, 62.5, 82.5, 125, 160, 200, 250, 320, 380, 500 kVA, etc.):**

$$S_{DG,selected} = 200 \text{ kVA (next standard size above 167.84)}$$

**Step 6 — Check Optimal Loading:**
$$\% \text{ Loading} = \dfrac{147.7}{200} \times 100 = 73.85\% \approx 74\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The selected DG is operating at **~74%** of rated capacity, which is within the **ideal 70–80% operating window** — confirming a healthy, efficient, and long-life selection.

### 3.2 Derivation: AMF Panel Logic Timing Sequence

The **AMF controller** runs a finite-state logic cycle. The full timing can be derived as:

$$T_{total,restore} = t_{sense} + t_{DG,start} + t_{stabilize} + t_{transfer}$$

Where:
- $t_{sense}$ = mains failure detection delay (typically **2 sec**, to ride through momentary dips)
- $t_{DG,start}$ = cranking + ignition time (**5–8 sec**, with 3 crank attempts of 10 sec each)
- $t_{stabilize}$ = voltage/frequency settling time (**3–5 sec**)
- $t_{transfer}$ = contactor changeover time (**< 200 ms**)

Substituting the **worst-case** values:

$$T_{total,restore} = 2 + 8 + 5 + 0.2 = 15.2 \text{ seconds}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Point:** The total *mains-to-DG* transfer time is **≤ 30 seconds** in compliance with **IS 1460 / IEEE 446** for emergency standby systems. If $T_{total,restore} > 30$ s, then a **battery-backed UPS** is required upstream of the AMF to bridge the gap.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: AMF Controller I/O Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The AMF "Sense-Think-Act" Matrix**

| S.No | **INPUT (Sense)** | **LOGIC (Think)** | **OUTPUT (Act)** |
|------|-------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Mains RYB Voltage < 90% Un | After 2s delay → Declare Mains Fail | Initiate Crank Relay |
| 2 | Mains Frequency < 47 Hz or > 53 Hz | After 2s delay → Declare Mains Fail | Initiate Crank Relay |
| 3 | DG Voltage = 415V ± 5% & Freq = 50 Hz ± 1% | Declare DG Healthy | Energize DG Contactor, De-energize Mains Contactor |
| 4 | Mains Voltage restored > 90% Un | Wait 30s–60s for stability | Transfer Load to Mains (interlock enforced) |
| 5 | DG running unloaded after retransfer | 5 min cooling timer | Stop Solenoid cuts fuel → DG stops |
| 6 | DG fails to start in 3 attempts | Lockout alarm | Sound hooter, light Fault LED, common alarm NO/NC contact |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: DG + AMF System (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    EB["KERALA EB MAINS SUPPLY 11KV HT"]:::src
    TX["STEP DOWN TRANSFORMER 11KV 415V"]:::src
    MLB["MAIN LT BUSBAR 415V 50HZ"]:::bus
    MCP["MAINS CIRCUIT BREAKER MCCB"]:::sw
    AMF["AMF MICROPROCESSOR CONTROLLER"]:::ctrl
```
