```mermaid
    CT1["CTs PTs SENSING"]:::sense
    DC1["MAINS CONTACTOR K1"]:::sw
    BAT["DG BATTERY 24V DC STARTING"]:::aux
    DGCB["DG CIRCUIT BREAKER"]:::sw
    DC2["DG CONTACTOR K2"]:::sw
    ALTR["DG ALTERNATOR 3PH 415V 50HZ"]:::gen
    ENG["DIESEL ENGINE PRIME MOVER"]:::gen
    FUEL["FUEL TANK DAY STORAGE"]:::aux
    LBUS["LOAD BUS BAR 415V"]:::bus
    LD1["LIFT LOAD"]:::ld
    LD2["LIGHTING LOAD"]:::ld
    LD3["FIRE PUMP LOAD"]:::ld
    LD4["HVAC LOAD"]:::ld
    ALARM["HOOTER ANNUNCIATOR FAULT PANEL"]:::aux

    EB --> TX --> MLB
    MLB --> MCP --> CT1
    CT1 --> AMF
    AMF --> DC1
    AMF --> DC2
    AMF --> BAT
    AMF --> ALARM
    DC1 --> LBUS
    DC2 --> LBUS
    LBUS --> LD1
    LBUS --> LD2
    LBUS --> LD3
    LBUS --> LD4
    ALTR --> DGCB --> DC2
    ENG --> ALTR
    FUEL --> ENG
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Legend — Class Definitions:** `src` = Source, `bus` = Busbar, `sw` = Switchgear, `ctrl` = Controller, `sense` = Sensors, `gen` = Generator, `aux` = Auxiliary, `ld` = Load.

### 4.2 Sequence-of-Operation Flow Chart (KTU Board Exam Favourite)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> MainsHealthy
    MainsHealthy --> MainsFailDetected: Voltage or Frequency out of range
    MainsFailDetected --> CrankStart: Wait 2 seconds
    CrankStart --> DGStarted: Engine fires within 3 attempts
    CrankStart --> Lockout: 3 failed attempts
    DGStarted --> DGStabilize: Wait for V and Hz to settle
    DGStabilize --> LoadOnDG: Close K2 Open K1
    LoadOnDG --> MainsRestored: EB supply returns
    MainsRestored --> WaitStabilizeTimer: Wait 30 to 60 seconds
    WaitStabilizeTimer --> LoadOnMains: Close K1 Open K2
    LoadOnMains --> CoolingRun: DG runs unloaded 5 minutes
    CoolingRun --> DGStop: Fuel cutoff
    DGStop --> MainsHealthy: System ready standby
    Lockout --> AlarmActive: Hooter ON
```

### 4.3 Single-Line Diagram (Conceptual SLDF of AMF Panel)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1["EB INCOMING 415V"]:::src
    KM1["MAINS CONTACTOR K1"]:::sw
    CB1["MAINS MCCB"]:::sw
    BUS["COMMON LOAD BUS"]:::bus
    KM2["DG CONTACTOR K2"]:::sw
    CB2["DG MCCB"]:::sw
    DG["DG SET 3PH 415V"]:::gen
    M1 --> CB1 --> KM1 --> BUS
    DG --> CB2 --> KM2 --> BUS
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interlock Mandate:** $K1$ and $K2$ are **electrically and mechanically interlocked** — they can *never* be closed simultaneously. This prevents dangerous **out-of-phase paralleling** of the DG with the live EB mains.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the term "Standby DG System" and state any two situations where it is mandatory as per National Building Code of India (NBC 2016).*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A Standby Diesel Generator (DG) System is a **secondary source of electrical power** that automatically takes over the connected load when the **normal mains (EB) supply fails** or deviates beyond acceptable voltage/frequency limits. It is *not* used for continuous duty but only during emergencies.
>
> **Two mandatory situations as per NBC 2016, Part 4 (Fire & Life Safety):**
> 1. **High-rise residential buildings** (above 15 m height) — for lifts, common area lighting, fire pumps, and staircase pressurization.
> 2. **Buildings of Group A (Residential), B (Educational), C (Institutional), and H (Hazardous)** with occupancy above 300 persons — for emergency lighting and fire-fighting equipment.

*(Marks: 1 definition + 2 situations = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*List any **four** essential components housed inside an AMF Panel.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — any 4 of the following):**

1. **Microprocessor-based AMF controller** (e.g., DSE 7320, Woodward easYgen) — the brain.
2. **Mains contactor (K1) and DG contactor (K2)** — electrically and mechanically interlocked.
3. **MCCBs/MCBs** for short-circuit and overload protection.
4. **Current Transformers (CTs)** for metering and protection.
5. **Battery charger** for maintaining the DG starting battery at float voltage.
6. **Indicators, meters, and annunciator** for V, Hz, Amps, kW, kVA, hours-run, fault lamps.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Note:** As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, Module questions carry internal choice between two full-length 14-mark questions. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

> *A hospital complex has a total connected load of **450 kVA** at **0.8 pf lagging**. The largest single motor is a **75 kW fire pump with Star-Delta starter**. The DG efficiency is **90%** and the Diversity Factor is **0.7**.*
> 
> **(a)** Calculate the required Standby DG rating and select the next standard size. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Draw a labelled single-line diagram of the AMF panel and explain its **sequence of operation** in the event of mains failure and subsequent mains restoration. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Apply Diversity Factor to connected load:**
$$S_{diversified} = 450 \times 0.7 = 315 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 2: Account for DG Efficiency:**
$$S_{DG,input} = \dfrac{315}{0.90} = 350 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 3: Check if largest motor starting is critical (Star-Delta, starting multiplier $X = 1.5$ approx.):**
$$S_{motor,start} = \dfrac{75 \times 1.5}{0.9 \times 0.8} = 156.25 \text{ kVA}$$

Since $156.25 < 350$, the **running diversified load is governing**, and the largest motor starting is *not* the deciding factor.

**Step 4: Select the next standard DG size:**

Standard sizes in kVA: ... 250, **315**, **380**, 500 ...
$$S_{DG,selected} = 380 \text{ kVA (next standard above 350)}$$

**Step 5: Verify optimal loading:**
$$\% \text{ Load} = \dfrac{315}{380} \times 100 = 82.9\% \approx 83\%$$

**Conclusion:** A **380 kVA** DG is selected, operating at ~83% load. *(At the upper edge of the optimal range — acceptable for standby duty.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Single-Line Diagram (Refer Section 4.3 of these notes for the Mermaid SLDF).**

**Sequence of Operation:**

**Phase 1 — Mains Healthy (Normal):**
- EB supply healthy → Voltage and Frequency within ±10% and ±5% bands.
- Mains contactor **K1 CLOSED**, DG contactor **K2 OPEN**, DG engine **OFF**, Battery on float charge.

**Phase 2 — Mains Failure:**
1. AMF controller senses under-voltage / under-frequency on mains → starts a **2-second confirmation timer** to avoid nuisance tripping.
2. After confirmation → **Crank relay** energised → DG battery powers starter motor.
3. Up to **3 crank attempts** of 10 sec each with 10 sec rest.
4. Engine fires → Voltage and Frequency build up to **415V ± 5%, 50 Hz ± 1%**.
5. After **stabilization delay (5 sec)** → Controller closes **K2** and opens **K1** (load transferred to DG in < 200 ms).

**Phase 3 — Mains Restoration:**
6. AMF senses return of mains → starts a **30 to 60-second stabilization timer**.
7. After delay → Controller closes **K1** (Mains) and opens **K2** (DG). Load now back on EB.
8. DG continues to run **unloaded for 5 minutes (cooling run)** to prevent thermal shock.
9. After cooling → Fuel solenoid de-energised → DG shuts down automatically.
10. Battery charger resumes float charge. System returns to Phase 1.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Forgetting the interlock** between K1 and K2 — students often draw them as independent switches, which costs **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Skipping the cooling run** — mentioning only "DG stops" without the 5-minute unloaded cool-down will lose **1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing AMF with ATS** — AMF is a *controller+switchgear combo*; ATS is only the *transfer switch*. AMF ⊃ ATS.
> 4. **Forgetting units** in the numerical — kVA without unit loses **0.5 mark**.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

> *(a)* Explain with a neat block diagram the **functional architecture of a Standby DG System with AMF Panel**, identifying all major sub-systems. **(7 marks)**
> 
> *(b)* Compare **Manual Changeover**, **Semi-Automatic**, and **Fully-Automatic (AMF)** modes of DG operation. State **three advantages** and **two limitations** of the AMF mode. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

*(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

**Major Sub-systems identified:**

1. **Mains (EB) Source:** HT supply → Transformer → LT busbar at 415V, 3-phase, 50 Hz.
2. **AMF Controller:** Microprocessor unit (e.g., Deep Sea, Woodward) with sensing CTs/PTs, programmable timers, and relay outputs.
3. **Switchgear Section:** Mains MCCB + Mains Contactor (K1), DG MCCB + DG Contactor (K2), all interlocked.
4. **DG Set:** Diesel engine (prime mover) coupled to brushless alternator (typically 3-phase, 415V, 50 Hz, 0.8 pf).
5. **Auxiliary Systems:** 24V DC starting battery, battery charger, fuel day tank, exhaust silencer, and control cabling.
6. **Load Distribution:** Final load busbar feeding lifts, lighting, fire pumps, HVAC, IT, and life-safety loads.

**Working Summary:** AMF controller continuously polls the mains parameters. On any deviation beyond set thresholds, it executes a deterministic, time-stamped sequence of starting the DG, stabilizing its output, and transferring the load — all within ≤ 30 seconds.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Manual Changeover | Semi-Automatic | Fully Automatic (AMF) |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------|-----------------------|
| Operator Required? | **Yes** (for start, transfer, stop) | Partial (start manual, transfer auto) | **No** |
| Reaction Time | 1–5 minutes (depends on operator) | 30–60 sec | **5–15 sec** |
| Suitable for | Small shops, isolated houses | Small commercial, schools | **Hospitals, IT, high-rise** |
| Cost | **Lowest** | Medium | **Highest** |
| Reliability | Operator-dependent | Mixed | **Highest** |

**Three Advantages of AMF:**
1. **Zero human intervention** — critical during nights/holidays.
2. **Sub-30-second power restoration** — protects sensitive electronics and life-safety systems.
3. **Built-in engine protection** — automatic shutdown on low oil pressure, high coolant temperature, overspeed.

**Two Limitations of AMF:**
1. **Higher initial cost** (microprocessor controller, contactors, battery, wiring).
2. **Requires periodic testing** — DG must be run on load weekly/monthly; battery health must be monitored, else failure during actual mains failure is possible (the dreaded "DG failed to start" incident).

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Standby DG + AMF**

- **DG** = Diesel Generator; rated in **kVA** (not kW) for 3-phase sets.
- **AMF** = Automatic Mains Failure — a *complete panel* containing controller + switchgear + interlocks.
- **Standard Voltage:** 415V, 3-phase, 4-wire, 50 Hz, 0.8 pf lagging (Indian standard).
- **Optimal DG loading:** **70%–80%** of rated kVA — never run below 30% (wet stacking) or above 90% sustained.
- **DG Sizing rule-of-thumb:** $S_{DG} = \dfrac{\sum P_i / pf_i \times DF}{\eta_{DG}}$ — always use **arithmetic sum of kVA**, not kW.
- **Largest motor check** is *only* needed for DOL starting on a small DG; for star-delta, often ignored.
- **AMF sequence (must memorise in order):** *Mains fail → 2 s delay → Crank (3 attempts) → Stabilize → Close K2 → Open K1 → Load on DG.*
- **Return sequence:** *Mains restored → 30–60 s wait → Close K1 → Open K2 → 5 min cooling → DG stop.*
- **Interlock K1–K2** is *electrical AND mechanical* — never optional.
- **Battery** = 24V DC (two 12V in series), sized by $C = I_{CCA} \times T_{crank} / 1.2$.
- **Fuel tank:** Day tank = 8–10 hour runtime capacity; bulk storage = 24–72 hours.
- **Standards to quote in exams:** **IS 1460**, **IEEE 446 (Orange Book)**, **IEC 60947-6-1**, **NBC 2016 Part 4**, **CPWD General Specifications for Electrical Works Part-II**.
- **DG should NOT be used for** continuous peak-shaving (use gas turbines or grid-tie solar for that) — standby is for **emergency only**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Essential Protections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Essential Protections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Essential Protection" in Electrical System Design?

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme for **Electrical System Design and Estimation (PCEET602)**, **Essential Protections** refers to the mandatory set of safety arrangements engineered into a building's low-voltage (LV) electrical installation to safeguard **human life, livestock, property, and connected equipment** against the predictable electrical hazards that can arise during the operation of the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> Essential protection is the deliberate provision of devices and design measures — including **earthing, protective bonding, automatic disconnection of supply, overcurrent protection, earth-fault protection, and lightning protection** — that operate collectively to detect an abnormal electrical condition and isolate the faulty section of the installation (or reduce the touch voltage to a harmless value) before injury, fire, or equipment damage occurs.

The relevant governing standards referenced in the KTU syllabus are:
- **IS 732:2019** – Code of Practice for Electrical Wiring Installations
- **IS 3043:2018** – Code of Practice for Earthing
- **IS 12640 (Part 1):2016** – Residual Current Operated Circuit Breakers (RCCBs)
- **IS/IEC 60898-1** – MCBs for AC installations
- **IEC 60364** – International wiring-installations standard

## 1.2 The Three Core Hazards That Essential Protections Must Address

| S. No. | Hazard | Human/Equipment Consequence |
|--------|--------|------------------------------|
| 1 | **Electric Shock** (Direct or Indirect Contact) | Ventricular fibrillation, burns, death |
| 2 | **Overcurrent** (Overload or Short Circuit) | Insulation fire, equipment burnout |
| 3 | **Transient Overvoltage / Lightning Surge** | Insulation puncture, electronic damage |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Electrical Bodyguard"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (The Building Is a Human Body Analogy):**
> Imagine your house's wiring system as the **nervous system of a human body**. The wires carry impulses (current) to organs (loads), and the protective devices act as the **reflex arcs of the body**:
> - A **fuse/MCB** is the *nerve that jerks your hand away* when you touch a hot kettle (instant disconnection on overcurrent).
> - **Earthing** is the *skin that bleeds (conducts) harmful charges* safely into the ground.
> - An **RCCB** is the *brain's pain sensor* that detects a tiny mismatch (leakage) and orders a trip before any damage is done.
> - The **lightning arrestor** is the *sweat gland* that releases a sudden burst of energy outwards so the internal organs don't burn.
>
> Without these reflex mechanisms, a small disturbance (like a 30 mA leak) — which is barely noticeable to the wiring — can **kill a human in 0.2 seconds**.

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Module 4 — Scope of Essential Protections

The KTU 2024 syllabus places the following six topics under the umbrella of "Essential Protections":

1. **Earthing** — Equipment earthing, System earthing, Methods of Earthing
2. **Fuses** — HRC type, Rewireable type, Selection
3. **Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCB)** — Types B, C, D
4. **Moulded Case Circuit Breakers (MCCB)** — For higher ratings
5. **Residual Current Circuit Breakers (RCCB / ELCB)** — Earth-leakage detection
6. **Lightning Protection** — Conventional Faraday system, Surge Protection Devices (SPDs)

## 1.5 The Human Body — Why These Protections Cannot Be Optional

| Parameter | Typical Value | Reference |
|-----------|--------------|-----------|
| Body resistance (skin dry) | **100,000 Ω** | IEEE Std 80 |
| Body resistance (skin wet) | **1,000 Ω** | IEC 60479 |
| Let-go threshold current (AC 50 Hz) | **10 mA (women) / 16 mA (men)** | IEC 60479-1 |
| Ventricular fibrillation threshold (≥ 0.5 s) | **~30 mA** | IEC 60479-1 |
| Body current considered safe (≤ 40 ms) | **≤ 500 mA** | IEC 60364-41 |
| Conventional touch voltage limit (AC) | **50 V** (dry), **25 V** (wet) | IS 3043:2018 |

> [!WARNING]
> A current of just **30 mA** passing through the human heart region for more than **0.5 s** can cause ventricular fibrillation. The household mains in India supplies **230 V AC, 50 Hz** — if a person touches a live conductor in a wet bathroom, the resulting current can be **230 mA**, which is **lethal within 0.1 second**. This is exactly why RCCBs of **30 mA sensitivity** are mandatory for domestic installations under IS 732:2019.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchy of Protection Functions

The KTU module treats "essential protection" as a **layered (defence-in-depth) concept**. The layers are:

### Layer 1 — Basic Protection (Against Direct Contact)
- Insulation of live conductors
- Barriers, enclosures, and out-of-reach placement
- **RCDs (Residual Current Devices)** of ≤ 30 mA used as supplementary protection

### Layer 2 — Fault Protection (Against Indirect Contact)
- **Protective Earthing** combined with **Automatic Disconnection of Supply**
- Equipotential bonding of exposed conductive parts (ECPs) and extraneous conductive parts (ExCPs)
- Earth-fault loop impedance $Z_s$ must be low enough to ensure disconnection within **0.4 s** (final circuits ≤ 32 A) and **5 s** (distribution circuits)

### Layer 3 — Overcurrent Protection
- **Fuses** or **MCBs/MCCBs** that trip when $I \ge I_{set}$
- Protects cables from overload and short circuit

### Layer 4 — Surge / Lightning Protection
- **Lightning Arrestor (LSP)** at the building entry
- **Surge Protection Devices (SPDs)** Type 1, 2, 3 at distribution and terminal level

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Essential Protections (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet:** The following formulas are tested repeatedly in KTU University Examinations. Memorise the formula, its units, and the boundary condition.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Typical Boundary / Note |
|---|----------|---------|-------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Resistance of an earth electrode (rod) | $R_e = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln \left( \dfrac{4L}{d} \right) - 1 \right]$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | $\rho$ = soil resistivity (Ω·m), $L$ = rod length (m), $d$ = rod diameter (m) |
| 2 | Resistance of plate electrode | $R_e = \dfrac{\rho}{4 r} + \dfrac{\rho}{4 a}$ | $\Omega$ | $r$ = plate radius, $a$ = depth of burial |
| 3 | Earth resistance (multiple electrodes in parallel) | $\dfrac{1}{R_T} = \sum \dfrac{1}{R_i}$ | $\Omega$ | Ignore mutual resistance for first-order KTU design |
| 4 | Minimum cross-section of earth conductor (Cu) | $A = \dfrac{I^2 t}{k^2}$ (adiabatic) | mm² | $k$ = 143 for Cu with PVC, $k$ = 176 for Cu with XLPE |
| 5 | Prospective short-circuit current at the far end | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{U_0}{Z_s}$ | A | $U_0$ = nominal line-to-neutral voltage (230 V) |
| 6 | Fusing factor | $F = \dfrac{I_f}{I_r}$ | Dimensionless | $F > 1$; 1.4–1.5 typical for semi-enclosed fuses; 1.0 for HRC |
| 7 | MCB tripping condition (overload) | $I^2 t \le k^2 S^2$ | A²s | Adiabatic equation for cable protection |
| 8 | Touch voltage (conventionally safe) | $U_T = U_0 \times \dfrac{R_{body}}{Z_s + R_{body}}$ | V | Must be ≤ 50 V (AC) in dry conditions |
| 9 | Body current on indirect contact | $I_b = \dfrac{U_0}{Z_s + R_{body}}$ | A | Must be < 30 mA; this is why $Z_s$ matters |
| 10 | Lightning cone of protection | $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{1}{k}$ | degrees | k = 1 (45°), 1.5 (33.7°), 2 (26.6°) for air-termination mast |
| 11 | Energy let-through of MCB | $I^2 t \le k_{MCB}^2 \times S^2$ | A²s | MCB selection is governed by **cable protection** |
| 12 | Specific soil resistance (typical values) | Sandy soil: **300 Ω·m**; Loam: **100 Ω·m**; Clay: **40 Ω·m**; Moist soil: **10 Ω·m** | $\Omega$·m | Remember order of magnitude for KTU numericals |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Don't Break the Markdown Table:** Note that absolute-value bars are written using `\vert` or `\mid` — for example, $\lvert Z_s \rvert$ is preferred to $\vert Z_s \vert$ inside a row to prevent table-parser breakage.

## 2.3 The Disconnection Time Equation (The Heart of the KTU Numerical Problem)

For **automatic disconnection of supply** under earth-fault conditions, IS 732:2019 (and IEC 60364-41) requires that the protective device must disconnect in a time such that:

$$I_{fault} \times t_{disconnection} \le \text{let-through energy} \le I^2 t_{allowable}$$

**Real-world utility:** This single principle decides whether you will pass KTU Numerical #7 on earth-fault loop impedance. The complete design procedure is:

1. Calculate $Z_s$ (earth-fault loop impedance) of the circuit.
2. Find $I_{fault} = U_0 / Z_s$.
3. From the MCB / fuse time-current curve, read the disconnection time at that $I_{fault}$.
4. Check $I^2 t \le k^2 S^2$ (cable protection) AND $t \le 0.4 \text{ s}$ (final circuit shock protection).

## 2.4 Why the Fusing Factor Matters

For a fuse to **discriminate properly** — i.e., a 10 A fuse should blow before a 20 A fuse upstream — the **fusing factor** must be controlled. A *semi-enclosed rewireable fuse* (Kit-Kat) has $F \approx 1.4$, meaning it carries **1.4 times** its rated current indefinitely. A *HRC cartridge fuse* has $F \approx 1.0$ — the moment the current equals rating, the element melts within a defined time.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Essential Protection Used |
|-------------|---------------------------|
| Domestic 230 V home | MCB + RCCB (30 mA) + Earthing |
| Hospital OT | IT system + isolated power + 6 mA RCD |
| 11 kV substation | Lightning arrestor + Earth electrodes + differential protection |
| High-rise building (30+ floors) | Faraday cage + Surge arrestors + ELCBs on each floor |
| Industrial motor | MCCB + Overload relay + Earth-fault relay |
| Solar PV installation | DC MCB + DC SPD + Equipotential bonding |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Resistance of a Single Pipe/Rod Earth Electrode

This is the most-frequently asked KTU derivation. The student is expected to show the steps from Laplace's equation of a hemispherical equipotential field in soil.

### Starting Point
When a current $I$ is discharged from a hemispherical electrode of radius $r_1$ buried at a distance $r_2$ away, the current density at the surface of radius $r$ is uniform across the hemispherical surface. By symmetry, the **resistance of the soil shell between $r$ and $r+dr$** is:

$$dR = \rho \cdot \frac{dr}{A} = \rho \cdot \frac{dr}{2 \pi r^2}$$

### Step 1 — Integrate from $r_1$ to $r_2$

$$R = \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \rho \cdot \frac{dr}{2 \pi r^2} = \frac{\rho}{2 \pi} \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \frac{dr}{r^2}$$

$$R = \frac{\rho}{2 \pi} \left[ -\frac{1}{r} \right]_{r_1}^{r_2} = \frac{\rho}{2 \pi} \left( \frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2} \right)$$

### Step 2 — Apply the "Far Electrode" Limit

As $r_2 \rightarrow \infty$ (the "infinite earth" assumption), $1/r_2 \rightarrow 0$:

$$R = \frac{\rho}{2 \pi r_1}$$

For a hemisphere, $r_1$ is the equivalent radius. For a **rod of length $L$ and diameter $d$**, Dwight's empirical formula (which is what KTU accepts) is:

$$R_e = \frac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \left[ \ln \left( \frac{4L}{d} \right) - 1 \right]$$

### Step 3 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

A 2.5 m long, **50 mm diameter** GI pipe electrode is driven into **loam soil** ($\rho = 100 \, \Omega \cdot m$). Calculate the earth resistance.

**Given:**
- $L = 2.5 \text{ m}$
- $d = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- $\rho = 100 \, \Omega \cdot \text{m}$

**Step-by-step substitution:**

$$R_e = \frac{100}{2 \pi \times 2.5} \left[ \ln \left( \frac{4 \times 2.5}{0.05} \right) - 1 \right]$$

$$R_e = \frac{100}{15.708} \left[ \ln \left( \frac{10}{0.05} \right) - 1 \right]$$

$$R_e = 6.366 \times \left[ \ln (200) - 1 \right]$$

$$R_e = 6.366 \times \left[ 5.2983 - 1 \right]$$

$$R_e = 6.366 \times 4.2983$$

$$\boxed{R_e = 27.36 \, \Omega}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Required Value:** IS 3043:2018 mandates that the resistance of any earth electrode **must not exceed 5 Ω** for a domestic installation. Since 27.36 Ω > 5 Ω, the design must include **multiple electrodes in parallel**.

### Step 4 — Multiple Electrodes in Parallel (Neglecting Mutual Resistance)

Using **6 electrodes** in parallel:

$$R_T = \frac{R_e}{n} = \frac{27.36}{6} = 4.56 \, \Omega$$

This now satisfies the IS 3043 limit of ≤ 5 Ω. **Note:** In practice, mutual resistance between electrodes increases the total, so KTU is happy with the simple assumption.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Earth-Fault Loop Impedance & Disconnection Time

### Setup
A fault occurs when a live conductor touches an exposed metal body. Current flows: **Line → fault → body → earth → return to transformer neutral**.

$$I_{fault} = \frac{U_0}{Z_s}$$

where $Z_s$ = $Z_1$ (line impedance) + $Z_2$ (CPC impedance) + $Z_e$ (earth electrode impedance) + $Z_T$ (transformer impedance).

### Numerical Example
A final sub-circuit is protected by a 16 A Type C MCB. The measured $Z_s = 0.85 \, \Omega$. Calculate fault current and disconnection time.

**Step 1 — Fault Current:**

$$I_{fault} = \frac{U_0}{Z_s} = \frac{230}{0.85} = 270.59 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 2 — MCB Multiple of Rating:**

$$n = \frac{I_{fault}}{I_n} = \frac{270.59}{16} = 16.91$$

**Step 3 — Read Time from Type C MCB Curve:**

A Type C MCB trips instantaneously at $5 I_n$ to $10 I_n$. Since $16.91 I_n$ is above 10, **instantaneous (magnetic) tripping** occurs. Disconnection time = **< 0.01 s** (less than 10 ms).

**Step 4 — Verify with Adiabatic Check (cable protection):**

Assume a $2.5 \text{ mm}^2$ Cu cable (PVC insulated, $k = 143$).

$$k^2 S^2 = 143^2 \times 2.5^2 = 20449 \times 6.25 = 127{,}806 \, \text{A}^2\text{s}$$

$$I^2 t = 270.59^2 \times 0.01 = 73{,}219 \times 0.01 = 732.19 \, \text{A}^2\text{s}$$

Since $732.19 \le 127{,}806$, the **cable is safe**.

**Step 5 — Verify Shock Protection:**

$0.01 \text{ s} \le 0.4 \text{ s}$ — **PASS** for a final circuit ≤ 32 A.

$$\boxed{\text{Disconnection time} = 0.01 \text{ s; All KTU conditions satisfied}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Adiabatic Equation for Earth Conductor Sizing

**The KTU standard adiabatic equation is:**

$$A = \frac{I}{\sqrt{t}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{t}}{k} = \frac{I \sqrt{t}}{k}$$

Or equivalently:

$$A^2 = \frac{I^2 t}{k^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = \frac{I \sqrt{t}}{k}$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation
- **Step A:** Heat generated in conductor = $I^2 R t$
- **Step B:** Heat absorbed to raise conductor from ambient to melting = $m c \Delta T$ (and latent heat of fusion for the element)
- **Step C:** Equating and rearranging leads to the standard form $A^2 = I^2 t / k^2$
- **Step D:** $k$ is a material constant defined by the IEC: $k$ = 143 (Cu/PVC), 176 (Cu/XLPE), 80 (Al/PVC), 94 (Al/XLPE), 51 (steel)

### Worked Example
Find the minimum cross-section of a Cu earth conductor for a fault current of 4 kA for 0.5 s. Use $k = 143$ (Cu/PVC).

$$A = \frac{I \sqrt{t}}{k} = \frac{4000 \times \sqrt{0.5}}{143} = \frac{4000 \times 0.7071}{143} = \frac{2828.4}{143} = 19.78 \, \text{mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{\text{Select standard size} = 25 \text{ mm}^2 \text{ Cu}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What Protection

| Fault Type | Typical Magnitude | Required Device | Typical Rating | KTU Standard |
|------------|-------------------|-----------------|----------------|--------------|
| Overload (1–1.5 × $I_n$) | Low | MCB (thermal bimetallic strip) | 6, 10, 16, 20, 25, 32 A | IS/IEC 60898 |
| Short circuit (> 5 × $I_n$) | Very high (1–10 kA) | MCB (magnetic trip) or MCCB | 16–1600 A | IS/IEC 60947-2 |
| Earth leakage (30 mA) | Very low | RCCB (ELCB) | 30 mA / 100 mA | IS 12640 |
| Surge (lightning) | Up to 100 kA, very brief | SPD / Lightning Arrestor | 10/350 µs (Type 1) | IEC 61643-1 |
| Open neutral | Voltage spike | Surge Arrestor | Type 2 SPD | IEC 61643 |
| Fire from leakage | 300 mA on combustible | AFDD (Arc Fault Device) | 30 mA RCBO | IS/IEC 62606 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Layered Essential Protection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef source fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef layer1 fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#06c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef layer2 fill:#e0ffe0,stroke:#060,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef layer3 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#a80,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef layer4 fill:#f0e0ff,stroke:#606,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#cccccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    sourceA[EB 230V SUPPLY 50HZ]:::source
    meterA[ENERGY METER]:::source
    mcbA[MAIN MCB 32A]:::source

    sourceA --> meterA --> mcbA

    mcbA --> spdA[SPD TYPE 1 LIGHTNING ARRESTOR]:::layer4
    spdA --> elcbA[MAIN RCCB 63A 30MA]:::layer2

    elcbA --> branchA[LIGHTING CIRCUIT MCB 10A]:::layer3
    elcbA --> branchB[POWER CIRCUIT MCB 16A]:::layer3
    elcbA --> branchC[Geyser 20A MCB]:::layer3
    elcbA --> branchD[AC 25A MCB]:::layer3

    branchA --> earth1[EARTHING STRIP 25 SQMM CU TO PIT]:::layer2
    branchB --> earth1
    branchC --> earth1
    branchD --> earth1

    earth1 --> earthPit1[EARTH PIT 1 RES LESS THAN 5 OHM]:::layer2
    earth1 --> earthPit2[EARTH PIT 2 IN PARALLEL]:::layer2
    earth1 --> earthPit3[EARTH PIT 3 IN PARALLEL]:::layer2

    branchA --> loadA[LAMP LOAD]:::load
    branchB --> loadB[SOCKET LOAD]:::load
    branchC --> loadC[GEYSER LOAD]:::load
    branchD --> loadD[AC LOAD]:::load

    spdA --> surgeEarth[SURGE EARTH PIT]:::layer4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Block Diagram:** The arrows trace the path of the **supply** (red) entering the building. After the main MCB, the supply is first shunted through the **SPD** (purple) for lightning, then passes through the **RCCB** (green) for earth leakage, then **branches through individual MCBs** (yellow) for overcurrent. The **earthing strip** (green) carries any fault current directly to multiple **earth pits** in parallel, keeping the resistance ≤ 5 Ω.

## 4.2 Flow Diagram — Decision Logic of an MCB vs RCCB

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef start fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#900,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#aa0,color:#000
    classDef branchA_fill fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#06c,color:#000
    classDef branchB_fill fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#060,color:#000
    classDef resultFill fill:#f0e0ff,stroke:#606,color:#000

    nodeA[Fault Occurs in Circuit]:::start
    nodeB{Is it Overload or Short Circuit?}:::decision
    nodeC{MCB Thermal Magnetic Trips}:::resultFill
    nodeD{Is it Earth Leakage?}:::decision
    nodeE[RCCB Detects 30 mA Mismatch]:::resultFill
    nodeF[Load Disconnected Within 0.01 s]:::branchA_fill
    nodeG[Load Disconnected Within 0.04 s]:::branchB_fill
    nodeH[No Trip; If Persistent Could Damage Cable]:::resultFill

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB -- YES --> nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeB -- NO --> nodeD
    nodeD -- YES --> nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeD -- NO --> nodeH
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Lightning Protection (Faraday Cage Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef skyClass fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#06c,color:#000
    classDef meshClass fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#060,color:#000
    classDef downClass fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#c80,color:#000
    classDef earthClass fill:#f0d5d5,stroke:#900,color:#000
    classDef bldgClass fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333,color:#000

    L1[AIR TERMINATION VERTICAL ROD 1 METER]:::skyClass
    L2[HORIZONTAL MESH ON ROOF 10M x 10M]:::meshClass
    L3[DOWN CONDUCTOR 25 x 3 MM GI STRIP]:::downClass
    L4[TEST LINK AT 1.5 M ABOVE GROUND]:::downClass
    L5[EARTH ELECTRODE GI PIPE 50 MM DIA 2.5 M]:::earthClass
    L6[CHARCOAL AND SALT LAYER AROUND PIT]:::earthClass
    L7[PROTECTED BUILDING STRUCTURE]:::bldgClass

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L6
    L3 --> L7
    L5 --> L7
```

## 4.4 Architecture Flow — Earth-Fault Loop (Indirect Contact)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef liveClass fill:#ffb3b3,stroke:#c00,color:#000
    classDef faultClass fill:#ffd5b3,stroke:#c60,color:#000
    classDef earthClass fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#060,color:#000
    classDef tripClass fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#06c,color:#000

    stepA[TRANSFORMER NEUTRAL 230 V PHASE]:::liveClass
    stepB[LINE CONDUCTOR L1]:::liveClass
    stepC[FAULT AT METAL BODY OF APPLIANCE]:::faultClass
    stepD[FAULT CURRENT FLOWS THROUGH EXPOSED BODY]:::faultClass
    stepE[MAIN EARTH TERMINAL]:::earthClass
    stepF[EARTH ELECTRODE 5 OHM]:::earthClass
    stepG[NEUTRAL EARTH OF TRANSFORMER]:::earthClass
    stepH[MCB TRIPS OR FUSE BLOWS WITHIN 0.4 s]:::tripClass
    stepI[SUPPLY DISCONNECTED]:::tripClass

    stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG --> stepH --> stepI
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023, PCEET602]

**Define "let-go voltage" and "let-go current" as applicable to human electrical safety.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Let-go Current (1 mark):** The maximum value of current through the body at which a person can still voluntarily withdraw the hand from contact. For AC 50 Hz: ~10 mA for women, ~16 mA for men (as per IEC 60479).
- **Let-go Voltage (1 mark):** The product of let-go current and the body resistance (skin-wet condition ~1 kΩ). The conventional safe touch-voltage limit adopted in IS 3043 is **50 V (AC) for dry conditions** and **25 V (AC) for wet conditions**.
- **Significance (1 mark):** Any fault that can keep a body voltage above these values for more than 0.4 s is considered lethal; hence essential protections are designed to disconnect within this time.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, PCEET602]

**What is a fusing factor? State the fusing factor range for (a) a semi-enclosed rewireable fuse and (b) an HRC fuse.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Fusing factor $F = I_f / I_r$, where $I_f$ = minimum fusing current, $I_r$ = current rating of fuse.
- **Semi-enclosed rewireable fuse (1 mark):** $F \approx 1.4$ to $1.5$ (i.e., fuse carries 1.4–1.5 × rated current indefinitely).
- **HRC fuse (1 mark):** $F \approx 1.0$ (very close to rated current).
- **Significance:** A lower fusing factor implies better discrimination and faster disconnection.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 4 Internal Choice

### ▶ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, PCEET602]

**(a)** Explain the **different methods of earthing** commonly used in electrical installations. Describe the procedure for preparing a **pipe earthing** with a neat sketch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 16 mm diameter cast iron pipe of length 2.8 m is buried vertically in soil having a resistivity of $80 \, \Omega \cdot m$. Calculate the **(i)** resistance of a single pipe electrode and **(ii)** the number of pipes required (in parallel, neglecting mutual resistance) to obtain a combined resistance of less than $2 \, \Omega$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Methods of Earthing (7 marks):**

The three IS 3043-recommended methods are:

1. **Pipe Earthing (2.5 marks):** A GI or CI pipe (40–100 mm dia) of 2.5–4.5 m length is driven vertically into the ground, leaving 100 mm above the surface. A coal-and-salt alternate layer surrounds the pipe (helps retain moisture). A 25 × 3 mm GI strip runs from the pipe to the main earth terminal through a funnel for water pouring.

2. **Plate Earthing (2 marks):** A copper or GI plate (60 × 60 cm or 60 × 30 cm, 3.15 mm thick) is buried vertically at 3 m depth inside a 4 m deep pit. The earth lead is bolted to the plate.

3. **Rod Earthing (1.5 marks):** A copper-bonded steel rod (12.5–25 mm dia, 1–4 m length) is driven into the earth. Multiple rods are connected in parallel for higher ratings.

4. **Procedure for Pipe Earthing (1 mark):** Dig pit (1.2 m deep) → drive pipe into pit floor until 2.5–4 m is embedded → fill with alternate layers of charcoal and salt (10–15 cm each) up to 1 m above pit floor → pour water via funnel → connect 25 × 3 mm GI strip to pipe and run to main earth terminal.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $d = 16 \text{ mm} = 0.016 \text{ m}$
- $L = 2.8 \text{ m}$
- $\rho = 80 \, \Omega \cdot m$

**Step 1 — Apply Dwight's formula (3 marks):**

$$R_e = \frac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \left[ \ln \left( \frac{4L}{d} \right) - 1 \right]$$

$$R_e = \frac{80}{2 \pi \times 2.8} \left[ \ln \left( \frac{4 \times 2.8}{0.016} \right) - 1 \right]$$

$$R_e = \frac{80}{17.593} \left[ \ln \left( \frac{11.2}{0.016} \right) - 1 \right]$$

$$R_e = 4.547 \left[ \ln (700) - 1 \right]$$

$$R_e = 4.547 \left[ 6.5511 - 1 \right]$$

$$R_e = 4.547 \times 5.5511$$

$$\boxed{R_e = 25.24 \, \Omega}$$

**Step 2 — Number of pipes in parallel (4 marks):**

For $n$ electrodes in parallel (neglecting mutual resistance):

$$R_T = \frac{R_e}{n} \le 2 \, \Omega$$

$$n \ge \frac{R_e}{2} = \frac{25.24}{2} = 12.62$$

$$\boxed{n = 13 \text{ electrodes}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall 1:** Students often forget to convert mm to m in the formula's $d$ term. Using $d = 16$ instead of $d = 0.016$ gives a wildly wrong answer (~1 Ω) — you will lose 2 marks.
>
> **Common Pitfall 2:** KTU expects the *next whole number*. Do not write 12.62 → 12; you must round up to 13.
>
> **Common Pitfall 3:** Mutual resistance is neglected by IS 3043's *first approximation*, but a 20% derating is expected in real design. In a numerical problem, do not include the derating unless explicitly told.

---

### ▶ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PCEET602]

**(a)** Explain the **operation and selection criteria of an HRC fuse** with reference to the time-current characteristics. Compare it with a semi-enclosed rewireable fuse. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 230 V, 5 kW single-phase domestic geyser is supplied through a $2.5 \text{ mm}^2$ Cu PVC-insulated cable from a DB. The cable length from MCB to geyser is 18 m. The MCB is a Type C, 32 A. Compute the **(i)** full-load current, **(ii)** voltage drop, and **(iii)** earth-fault loop impedance if the CPC (earth continuity conductor) has a resistance of $0.6 \, \Omega$ per conductor. State whether the MCB trips within 0.4 s. Take $\cos \phi = 0.85$ and cable resistance per km = $7.41 \, \Omega$/km for phase and $11.9 \, \Omega$/km for CPC. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — HRC Fuse Operation and Selection (7 marks):**

1. **Construction (1.5 marks):** An HRC (High Rupturing Capacity) fuse contains a silver-element fuse wire surrounded by chemically pure quartz sand inside a ceramic body. End caps act as terminals.
2. **Operation (1.5 marks):** When fault current exceeds the rating, the silver element melts and vapourises. The metal vapour reacts with the silica sand to form a high-resistance non-conductive silicate glass compound (fulgurite), which interrupts the arc almost instantly. This enables breaking capacities of 80–100 kA.
3. **Time-current characteristic (1.5 marks):** The T-I curve has three regions: pre-arcing, arcing, and total clearance. For low overloads, it allows inverse-time delay; for high short-circuits, it clears in < 5 ms.
4. **Selection criteria (1 mark):** (i) Voltage ≥ system voltage, (ii) Current rating ≥ load current, (iii) Breaking capacity ≥ prospective short-circuit current, (iv) $I^2 t$ let-through ≤ cable $k^2 S^2$.
5. **Comparison with Rewireable (1.5 marks):**

| Parameter | HRC | Rewireable (Kit-Kat) |
|-----------|-----|-----------------------|
| Breaking capacity | 80–100 kA | 2–4 kA |
| Fusing factor | ~1.0 | 1.4–1.5 |
| Discrimination | Excellent | Poor |
| Maintenance | Replace element | Re-wire |
| Cost | Higher | Cheaper |
| Use | Industrial / main DB | Domestic (legacy) |

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Full-load current (2 marks):**

$$I_{FL} = \frac{P}{U \cos \phi} = \frac{5000}{230 \times 0.85} = \frac{5000}{195.5}$$

$$\boxed{I_{FL} = 25.58 \, A}$$

**Step 2 — Cable resistance and voltage drop (2.5 marks):**

$$R_{phase} = 7.41 \times 0.018 = 0.1334 \, \Omega$$

$$R_{CPC} = 11.9 \times 0.018 = 0.2142 \, \Omega$$

$$R_{total} = 0.1334 + 0.2142 = 0.3476 \, \Omega$$

Voltage drop in the loop:

$$V_{drop} = I_{FL} \times R_{total} = 25.58 \times 0.3476$$

$$\boxed{V_{drop} = 8.89 \, \text{V (3.87\% of 230 V)}}$$

> **Permissible V drop** for a final sub-circuit is 3% (gland) + 3% (sub-mains) = 6% (combined). Here 3.87% < 6% — **acceptable**.

**Step 3 — Earth-fault loop impedance (2.5 marks):**

$Z_s$ = phase loop resistance + CPC loop resistance + transformer (assumed negligible) = $0.1334 + 0.2142 + 0.6 = 0.948 \, \Omega$

$$I_{fault} = \frac{U_0}{Z_s} = \frac{230}{0.948} = 242.6 \, \text{A}$$

Multiple of MCB rating: $242.6 / 32 = 7.58$

From Type C MCB curve, magnetic trip range is $5 I_n$ to $10 I_n$. Since $7.58 I_n$ lies in this range, **instantaneous magnetic trip** occurs.

$$\boxed{\text{Disconnection time} \approx 0.01 \text{ s} \ll 0.4 \text{ s — \textbf{TRIP CONFIRMED}}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall 1:** Many KTU students confuse the **cable conductor resistance per km** with the *loop* resistance. The loop resistance includes **both the phase conductor and the CPC**, both at their respective resistivities. The earth-fault loop = phase + CPC + external Z_e + transformer.
>
> **Common Pitfall 2:** Do not put CPC resistance per km in series with phase resistance per km without adding the **earth electrode resistance and transformer winding resistance** explicitly, otherwise you will be marked down for omitting them.
>
> **Common Pitfall 3:** Some students compute the *steady-state* voltage drop but forget to check the **earth-fault loop impedance** separately. Both are required marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Essential Protections**

- **Definition:** Essential protection = the mandatory set of safety arrangements (earthing, fuses, MCB, MCCB, RCCB, SPD) that prevent shock, fire, and equipment damage.
- **Body current limit:** < **30 mA** for shock protection; **5 mA** trip threshold for medical applications.
- **Earth resistance limit:** < **5 Ω** (domestic), < **1 Ω** (hospital, substation).
- **Earthing Methods (IS 3043):** **Pipe**, **Plate**, **Rod** — always embed in **charcoal + salt** for moisture retention.
- **Dwight's formula (Rod):** $R_e = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln \left( \dfrac{4L}{d} \right) - 1 \right]$
- **Fusing Factor:** **1.0** for HRC, **1.4–1.5** for rewireable. Lower is better.
- **Adiabatic Earth Conductor Sizing:** $A = \dfrac{I \sqrt{t}}{k}$, with $k = 143$ (Cu/PVC).
- **MCB Types (Tripping Curves):**
  - **Type B** = 3–5 $I_n$ (resistive loads, domestic)
  - **Type C** = 5–10 $I_n$ (lighting, small motors)
  - **Type D** = 10–20 $I_n$ (transformers, large motors)
- **RCCB Trip Current:** **30 mA** for human shock, **100 mA** for fire prevention, **300 mA** for industrial earth-fault.
- **Disconnection Time (Final Circuit ≤ 32 A):** ≤ **0.4 s** for shock protection.
- **Disconnection Time (Final Circuit > 32 A):** ≤ **5 s** for fault clearance.
- **Touch Voltage Limit:** **50 V** (AC dry), **25 V** (AC wet) per IS 3043.
- **Lightning Protection (Conventional Faraday):** Air termination rod (10 cm dia GI pipe), horizontal mesh, down conductor (25 × 3 mm GI strip), test link, earth pit with 5 Ω limit.
- **Standards to Remember (KTU expects them):** IS 3043:2018, IS 732:2019, IS/IEC 60898, IS/IEC 60947, IS 12640, IEC 60364, IEC 61643.
- **Soil Resistivity (Typical):** Moist soil 10 Ω·m, Clay 40 Ω·m, Loam 100 Ω·m, Sand 300 Ω·m, Gravel 1000 Ω·m.
- **Cable Adiabatic Constant $k$:** Cu/PVC = **143**, Cu/XLPE = **176**, Al/PVC = **80**, Al/XLPE = **94**.
- **Body Resistance (Human):** Dry **100 kΩ**, Wet **1 kΩ** — this 100× change is why bathrooms need 30 mA RCDs and 25 V touch limit.
- **Fuse Discrimination:** Downstream fuse must blow first; $I_{fuse,down} < 0.5 \times I_{fuse,up}$ for safe discrimination.
- **Number of Electrodes:** Always **round up** to the next integer in KTU numericals; mutual resistance is **neglected** unless specifically asked.
- **Final Word on KTU Marking:** Always show the **substituted value** in the formula box, write down the **unit**, and add a **concluding sentence** comparing against the relevant IS limit — that is where the 1–2 "explanation marks" are awarded.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of KTU Module 4 — Essential Protections Premium Notes.**
> These notes are tuned to the **KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech** assessment pattern, with CO-mapping implied under **CO4** (Design safe electrical installations) and Bloom's levels of **L2 (Understand), L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyse)**. Cross-reference this content with **IS 3043:2018** and **IS 732:2019** for further reading.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: UPS System and Its Design for Residential Application
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** UPS System and Its Design for Residential Application

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS)** is a solid-state, static power conditioning device that provides regulated, continuous, and disturbance-free electrical power to critical loads during normal operation, and sustains the load from its internal energy storage (battery) bank during mains failure, voltage sags, surges, frequency variations, or any other power quality anomaly — *all without any perceptible transfer interruption* to the connected load.

In the context of **residential application design**, the UPS system is engineered as a point-of-use or whole-house backup solution that protects sensitive electronic loads (computers, Wi-Fi routers, CCTV, home automation hubs, medical equipment for home-care patients, refrigerators with electronic controls) from the unreliable Kerala STATE grid profile, which frequently experiences voltage fluctuations, sudden outages, and harmonic distortion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Check:** Per IS 13252 (Part 1) and IEC 62040-3, a UPS is *not* a standby generator. A generator has a transfer time of seconds to minutes; a UPS has a transfer time in **milliseconds (typically 2–10 ms for offline, 0 ms for online/double-conversion)**. This distinction is a frequently tested KTU concept.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of a **UPS as the "heart-lung machine" of your house's electrical system**:

- Your house's wiring is like the **human body's circulatory system** — blood (electricity) must keep flowing continuously.
- The KSEB grid is the **outside world** — it sometimes stops pumping (outage), sometimes pumps too hard (surge), sometimes too weakly (brownout).
- A **generator** is like a slow-responding backup heart — it takes time to start, and during that gap, the patient (your sensitive electronics) dies.
- A **UPS** is like a heart-lung machine that is *already running in parallel* — it senses any disturbance **instantaneously** and seamlessly takes over, keeping the patient alive with stored oxygen (battery energy).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Residential Scenario in Kerala:** During a typical evening thunderstorm in Kochi, the grid may fail 4–5 times within an hour. A properly designed UPS lets your Wi-Fi router stay online, your CCTV keep recording, and your work-from-home laptop finish an important video call without a single drop — *without you even noticing the switchover*.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis)

The following are the **KTU-board-relevant metrics** for residential UPS specification:

| Metric | Typical Residential Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Apparent Power Rating (S)** | **600 VA – 5 kVA** | Total volt-ampere capacity |
| **Real Power (P)** | **480 W – 4 kW** | Actual usable wattage |
| **Power Factor (cos φ)** | **0.8 – 1.0 (lagging)** | Ratio of real to apparent power |
| **Battery Voltage (V_BATT)** | **12 V / 24 V / 48 V / 96 V DC** | DC bus voltage of battery bank |
| **Backup Time (t_B)** | **5 min – 4 hours** | Endurance on battery |
| **Transfer Time** | **< 10 ms (offline)**, **0 ms (online)** | Time to switch to battery |
| **Crest Factor** | **3:1 to 5:1** | Tolerance to peak currents |
| **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** | **< 5% (online)** | Output voltage purity |

## 1.4 Classification of UPS Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[UPS Topologies]:::title --> B[Offline / Passive Standby]
    A --> C[Line-Interactive]
    A --> D[Online / Double-Conversion]
    
    B --> B1["Transfer: 2-10 ms\nCost: Lowest\nUse: PC, Modem"]
    C --> C1["Transfer: 2-4 ms\nCost: Medium\nUse: Home Office"]
    D --> D1["Transfer: 0 ms\nCost: Highest\nUse: Servers, Medical"]
    
    classDef title fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#e94560,color:#ffffff,font-weight:bold
```
*Figure 1.1: UPS Topology Classification for Residential Use*

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state the *type* of UPS in your design — for residential applications, **Line-Interactive UPS** is the most commonly recommended topology in KTU model answers because it balances cost, performance, and protection against Kerala's frequent voltage sags (180 V – 220 V) and surges (250 V+).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

A residential UPS operates on a **dual-mode principle**: **Mains Mode (Online / Bypass Path)** and **Battery Mode (Inverter Path)**. The transition logic is governed by an embedded microcontroller-based control unit (MCU) that continuously samples the incoming mains waveform.

### Step 1 — Mains Sensing and Qualification
- The MCU samples the **RMS voltage**, **frequency**, and **waveform integrity (THD)** of the incoming supply via potential transformers and zero-cross detectors.
- The sampled values are compared against **predefined acceptance windows**:
  - Voltage window: **180 V ≤ V_RMS ≤ 250 V** (for 230 V nominal Indian residential supply)
  - Frequency window: **47 Hz ≤ f ≤ 53 Hz**
  - THD threshold: **≤ 15%**

### Step 2 — Decision Logic
- If the mains lies *within* all windows → **Mains Mode is active**. The load is fed directly (offline/line-interactive) or through the rectifier-inverter chain (online).
- If the mains lies *outside* any window → the static transfer switch (STS) commands the **inverter** to draw power from the battery bank and feed the load.

### Step 3 — Inverter Operation
- The inverter uses **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** with an **IGBT/MOSFET bridge** to synthesize a pure sine wave (or modified sine wave in budget units) from the DC bus.
- For Line-Interactive UPS, an **autotransformer with tap-changer (buck/boost)** corrects voltage sags/surges *without* engaging the battery — extending battery life significantly.

### Step 4 — Battery Charging
- A **constant-voltage / constant-current (CV/CC) charger** maintains the battery at its float voltage (e.g., 13.6 V for a 12 V lead-acid cell) using temperature-compensated charging curves.
- Charging current is typically limited to **C/10 to C/20** of battery capacity to prevent gassing and thermal runaway.

### Step 5 — Return Transfer (Mains Restoration)
- When mains returns within tolerance, the UPS performs a **synchronized retransfer** after a programmable hysteresis delay (typically 3–10 seconds) to confirm the restoration is stable.

## 2.2 Battery Sizing — The Core Design Equation

The heart of residential UPS design is **battery sizing**. The fundamental relationship derives from **Peukert's Law** and basic DC energy balance.

### Key Formula 1 — Energy Demand Equation

The battery must supply the **DC load** for the required **backup time**. The energy balance is:

$$E_{BATT} \geq \frac{P_{LOAD} \times t_B}{\eta_{INV}}$$

where:
- $E_{BATT}$ = Energy required from the battery in **Watt-hours (Wh)**
- $P_{LOAD}$ = Real power drawn by the AC load in **Watts (W)**
- $t_B$ = Desired backup time in **hours (h)**
- $\eta_{INV}$ = Inverter efficiency (typically **0.85 – 0.95**)

### Key Formula 2 — Battery Capacity in Ampere-Hours

The required battery capacity in **Ah** is then computed from the battery terminal voltage:

$$C_{Ah} = \frac{E_{BATT}}{V_{BATT}} = \frac{P_{LOAD} \times t_B}{V_{BATT} \times \eta_{INV}}$$

where:
- $C_{Ah}$ = Battery capacity rating in **Ampere-hours (Ah)**
- $V_{BATT}$ = Nominal battery bank voltage in **Volts (V)**

### Key Formula 3 — VA-to-Watts Conversion

$$P_{LOAD} = S_{UPS} \times \cos\phi$$

where:
- $S_{UPS}$ = UPS apparent power rating in **VA**
- $\cos\phi$ = Combined power factor of the load (typically **0.7 – 0.8** for residential mixed loads)

### Key Formula 4 — Peukert's Correction (For Long Backup Times)

When the discharge time exceeds **1 hour**, the usable capacity reduces below the rated capacity. Apply **Peukert's Law**:

$$C_{actual} = C_{rated} \times \left(\frac{I_{rated}}{I_{actual}}\right)^{n-1}$$

For lead-acid batteries, the Peukert exponent is typically $n = 1.20$ to $n = 1.35$.

### Key Formula 5 — Actual Load Current on DC Side

$$I_{BATT} = \frac{P_{LOAD}}{V_{BATT} \times \eta_{INV}}$$

### Key Formula 6 — Inverter DC Input Power

$$P_{DC} = \frac{P_{AC}}{\eta_{INV}}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | $P_{LOAD} = S \cdot \cos\phi$ | Real power from apparent power | W |
| F2 | $C_{Ah} = \frac{P_{LOAD} \times t_B}{V_{BATT} \times \eta_{INV}}$ | Battery capacity sizing | Ah |
| F3 | $E_{BATT} = V_{BATT} \times C_{Ah}$ | Battery energy storage | Wh |
| F4 | $I_{BATT} = \frac{P_{LOAD}}{V_{BATT} \times \eta_{INV}}$ | DC discharge current | A |
| F5 | $N_{cells} = \frac{V_{BATT}}{V_{cell}}$ | Number of cells in series | count |
| F6 | $V_{DC,total} = N_{cells} \times V_{cell}$ | Total DC bus voltage | V |
| F7 | $C_{actual} = C_{rated} \times (I_{rated}/I_{actual})^{n-1}$ | Peukert correction | Ah |
| F8 | $t_B = \frac{C_{Ah} \times V_{BATT} \times \eta_{INV}}{P_{LOAD}}$ | Achievable backup time | h |
| F9 | $P_{DC} = P_{AC} / \eta_{INV}$ | DC input power to inverter | W |
| F10 | $I_{charge} = C_{Ah} \times 0.1$ | Standard CV/CC charge rate | A |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Notice:** In the formula sheet above, vertical bar symbols have been intentionally replaced with forward slashes or expressed via exponents to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. In your KTU answer scripts, you may freely use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute values.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles above are used in:

- **Data center design** (10 kW – 10 MW UPS rooms with N+1 redundancy)
- **Hospital OT (Operation Theatre) backup** (online UPS with medical-grade isolation transformer)
- **Telecom tower sites** (DC UPS with –48 V battery banks for BTS equipment)
- **Kerala domestic solar hybrid systems** (grid-tied inverter with battery backup, using identical sizing principles)
- **Industrial PLC and SCADA panels** (line-interactive or online UPS for control room protection)

The same design philosophy — *load assessment → sizing → battery selection → charger sizing → protection* — scales from a 600 VA home unit to a 2 MVA industrial UPS.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Residential UPS Battery Sizing

We will derive the complete closed-form expression for **battery bank sizing** for a residential load, step by step, with no skipped algebra.

### Given Inputs (Typical Kerala Residential Application)

Let us define a representative residential load profile:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{UPS} &= 2\ \text{kVA} &&\text{(apparent power rating)} \\
\cos\phi &= 0.8 &&\text{(assumed load power factor)} \\
\eta_{INV} &= 0.90 &&\text{(inverter efficiency)} \\
t_B &= 1\ \text{hour} &&\text{(desired backup time)} \\
V_{BATT} &= 24\ \text{V} &&\text{(nominal DC bus for 2 kVA system)} \\
V_{cell} &= 12\ \text{V} &&\text{(single sealed lead-acid cell)} \\
\text{Depth of Discharge (DoD)} &= 0.6 &&\text{(safe limit for lead-acid)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 — Compute Real Load Power (P_LOAD)

The real power delivered to the connected residential equipment is the product of the apparent power rating and the load power factor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{LOAD} &= S_{UPS} \times \cos\phi \\
P_{LOAD} &= 2000\ \text{VA} \times 0.8 \\
P_{LOAD} &= 1600\ \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Even though the UPS is rated 2 kVA, only 80% of that rating translates to useful real work because residential loads (inductive fans, refrigerator compressors) have a lagging power factor. The remaining 20% (400 VAR) is reactive and circulates between the UPS and the load.

### Step 2 — Compute DC Input Power Required

The inverter must draw **more DC power** from the battery than it delivers as AC, because of conversion losses:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{DC} &= \frac{P_{LOAD}}{\eta_{INV}} \\
P_{DC} &= \frac{1600\ \text{W}}{0.90} \\
P_{DC} &= 1777.78\ \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The extra $177.78\ \text{W}$ of DC power is dissipated as heat in the IGBT switches, the output filter inductors, and the cooling fan. This is the cost of converting 24 V DC to 230 V AC.

### Step 3 — Compute DC Discharge Current

Applying Ohm's law in power form ($P = V \times I$), the current drawn from the 24 V battery bank is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{BATT} &= \frac{P_{DC}}{V_{BATT}} \\
I_{BATT} &= \frac{1777.78\ \text{W}}{24\ \text{V}} \\
I_{BATT} &= 74.07\ \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** A massive current of 74 A flows out of the 24 V bank — this is why residential UPS systems use thick (10–25 mm²) copper cables between the battery and the inverter module, and why a single 12 V 150 Ah battery is **insufficient**; a series-parallel bank is mandatory.

### Step 4 — Compute Required Battery Capacity (Raw Ah)

The raw capacity needed to sustain 74.07 A for 1 hour is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{raw} &= I_{BATT} \times t_B \\
C_{raw} &= 74.07\ \text{A} \times 1\ \text{h} \\
C_{raw} &= 74.07\ \text{Ah}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Apply Depth-of-Discharge (DoD) Derating

Lead-acid batteries should not be discharged below 60% State-of-Charge (SoC), i.e., maximum DoD = 60% = 0.6. Therefore, the **rated capacity** must be larger than the raw capacity by a factor of $1/\text{DoD}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{rated} &= \frac{C_{raw}}{\text{DoD}} \\
C_{rated} &= \frac{74.07\ \text{Ah}}{0.6} \\
C_{rated} &= 123.45\ \text{Ah}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** If we discharge the battery to 100% DoD, its cycle life drops from ~500 cycles to ~150 cycles. By derating to 60% DoD, we get a healthy trade-off between battery size and longevity.

### Step 6 — Select Commercial Battery Size

Round up to the next commercially available rating. The standard lead-acid battery sizes available in Kerala's market are **100 Ah, 120 Ah, 150 Ah, 200 Ah**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{selected} &= 150\ \text{Ah} \quad \text{(next standard size above 123.45 Ah)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Compute Number of Cells and Bank Configuration

A 24 V DC bus is built from **two 12 V batteries in series**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{series} &= \frac{V_{BATT}}{V_{cell}} = \frac{24\ \text{V}}{12\ \text{V}} = 2\ \text{cells}
\end{aligned}
$$

For this design, a single string of 2 × 12 V, 150 Ah batteries is sufficient. (If a larger current rating were required, parallel strings would be added — e.g., 2S2P for 300 Ah.)

### Step 8 — Verify Achievable Backup Time

Substituting the selected 150 Ah battery back into the backup-time formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{B,actual} &= \frac{C_{selected} \times V_{BATT} \times \eta_{INV} \times \text{DoD}}{P_{LOAD}} \\
t_{B,actual} &= \frac{150 \times 24 \times 0.90 \times 0.6}{1600} \\
t_{B,actual} &= \frac{1944}{1600} \\
t_{B,actual} &= 1.215\ \text{hours} \approx 1\ \text{hour}\ 13\ \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

This satisfies the 1-hour backup requirement with a comfortable margin.

### Step 9 — Compute Battery Charger Rating

The standard CV/CC charger current is **C/10**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{charge} &= \frac{C_{selected}}{10} \\
I_{charge} &= \frac{150\ \text{Ah}}{10} \\
I_{charge} &= 15\ \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

The charger should deliver **24 V × 15 A = 360 W** continuously during the bulk-charge phase, tapering to a float voltage of **27.2 V** (13.6 V × 2 cells) for maintenance.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Typical Residential Load Profile

| Appliance | Quantity | Rating (W each) | Total (W) | Avg. Daily Use (h) | Daily Energy (Wh) | UPS-Backed? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| LED Lighting (5×9W) | 5 | 9 | 45 | 6 | 270 | Yes |
| Ceiling Fan (75W BLDC) | 4 | 75 | 300 | 10 | 3000 | Yes |
| Wi-Fi Router + ONU | 1 | 15 | 15 | 24 | 360 | Yes |
| Laptop Charger | 1 | 65 | 65 | 8 | 520 | Yes |
| Refrigerator (Inverter) | 1 | 150 | 150 | 12 | 1800 | Selective |
| CCTV Camera (4) | 4 | 12 | 48 | 24 | 1152 | Yes |
| TV (LED 43") | 1 | 80 | 80 | 5 | 400 | Yes |
| Mobile Chargers (×4) | 4 | 10 | 40 | 4 | 160 | Yes |
| **Critical Subtotal** | — | — | **743 W** | — | — | — |
| **Total Subtotal** | — | — | **743 W** | — | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Decision Point:** A 1 kVA UPS (≈ 800 W) can back up the *critical* subtotal of 743 W with a safety margin. For a *whole-house* approach, a 3–5 kVA UPS is selected, often paired with a separate battery bank and a changeover to bypass non-essential loads.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Full Design Problem

**Problem:** Design a UPS system for a Kerala residential home requiring **1500 W of real power** with a **30-minute backup time**, on a **48 V DC bus**, using **12 V, 100 Ah lead-acid batteries**, with **DoD = 0.5** and **inverter efficiency = 0.92**.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:}\quad I_{BATT} &= \frac{P_{LOAD}}{V_{BATT} \times \eta_{INV}} = \frac{1500}{48 \times 0.92} = 33.97\ \text{A} \\
\text{Step 2:}\quad C_{raw} &= I_{BATT} \times t_B = 33.97 \times 0.5 = 16.98\ \text{Ah} \\
\text{Step 3:}\quad C_{rated} &= \frac{C_{raw}}{\text{DoD}} = \frac{16.98}{0.5} = 33.97\ \text{Ah} \\
\text{Step 4:}\quad N_{series} &= \frac{48\ \text{V}}{12\ \text{V}} = 4\ \text{cells in series} \\
\text{Step 5:}\quad C_{selected} &= 100\ \text{Ah} \quad \text{(next commercial size)} \\
\text{Step 6:}\quad t_{B,actual} &= \frac{100 \times 48 \times 0.92 \times 0.5}{1500} = 1.472\ \text{h} = 1\ \text{h}\ 28\ \text{min} \\
\text{Step 7:}\quad I_{charge} &= \frac{100}{10} = 10\ \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Configuration:** **1S4P (or 4 cells in series, 1 string) of 12 V, 100 Ah batteries** — a single 48 V, 100 Ah battery bank — provides 1.47 hours of backup, exceeding the 30-minute requirement. The charger is rated **48 V, 10 A (≈ 480 W)**.
