# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define UPS and distinguish it from an emergency standby generator. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model Question]

**Model Answer:**

An **Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS)** is a static power conditioning device that provides continuous, regulated power to a load by deriving energy from a battery bank when the primary mains supply fails, with a transfer time typically in the range of **0 to 10 milliseconds**.

**Distinction from a Standby Generator:**

| Parameter | UPS | Standby Generator |
|---|---|---|
| Transfer Time | **0 – 10 ms** | **10 – 60 seconds** |
| Energy Storage | **Battery (electrochemical)** | **Diesel/Petrol (chemical)** |
| Power Conditioning | **Yes (filters, regulates)** | **No (raw genset output)** |
| Typical Application | Electronics, IT, Medical | Whole building, heavy loads |
| Response to Sags/Surges | **Instantaneous** | **None (sustains the sag)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Insight:** A 3-mark answer expects (a) one-line definition, (b) one key distinction (transfer time), and (c) one example. Keep the table format crisp.

---

### Question 2: List any three types of UPS topologies and state one application of each for residential use. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model Question]

**Model Answer:**

1. **Offline (Passive Standby) UPS** — Used for *low-criticality loads* such as desktop PC, Wi-Fi router, and mobile chargers. Transfer time: 2–10 ms; cost: lowest.

2. **Line-Interactive UPS** — Used for *home-office loads* such as laptop, CCTV, printer, and small servers. Has autotransformer with buck/boost tap-changer. Transfer time: 2–4 ms; cost: medium.

3. **Online (Double-Conversion) UPS** — Used for *highly sensitive loads* such as home medical equipment (CPAP, oxygen concentrator), NAS drives, and home automation servers. Transfer time: 0 ms (true online). Cost: highest.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Question]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Design a UPS system for a residential application

A residential home in Kerala has the following critical load profile requiring backup during power cuts:

- 4 LED bulbs of 9 W each
- 4 BLDC ceiling fans of 75 W each
- 1 Wi-Fi router of 15 W
- 1 Laptop charger of 65 W
- 4 CCTV cameras of 12 W each

The required backup time is **2 hours**. Design a complete UPS system specifying: (i) the VA rating, (ii) battery bank configuration, and (iii) charger rating. Assume: $\cos\phi = 0.8$, $\eta_{INV} = 0.90$, $V_{cell} = 12\ \text{V}$, $\text{DoD} = 0.6$, $V_{BATT} = 24\ \text{V}$ DC.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Total Real Load Power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{LED} &= 4 \times 9 = 36\ \text{W} \\
P_{FAN} &= 4 \times 75 = 300\ \text{W} \\
P_{ROUTER} &= 15\ \text{W} \\
P_{LAPTOP} &= 65\ \text{W} \\
P_{CCTV} &= 4 \times 12 = 48\ \text{W} \\
P_{LOAD,total} &= 36 + 300 + 15 + 65 + 48 = 464\ \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute UPS Apparent Power Rating**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{UPS} &= \frac{P_{LOAD}}{\cos\phi} = \frac{464}{0.8} = 580\ \text{VA}
\end{aligned}
$$

Select the next commercial size: **S_UPS = 1 kVA (1000 VA)**, providing a 72% safety margin for future load additions.

**Step 3 — Compute DC Discharge Current**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{BATT} &= \frac{P_{LOAD}}{V_{BATT} \times \eta_{INV}} = \frac{464}{24 \times 0.90} = 21.48\ \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Required Battery Capacity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{raw} &= I_{BATT} \times t_B = 21.48 \times 2 = 42.96\ \text{Ah} \\
C_{rated} &= \frac{C_{raw}}{\text{DoD}} = \frac{42.96}{0.6} = 71.60\ \text{Ah}
\end{aligned}
$$

Select next commercial size: **C_selected = 100 Ah**.

**Step 5 — Battery Bank Configuration**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{series} &= \frac{V_{BATT}}{V_{cell}} = \frac{24}{12} = 2\ \text{cells in series} \\
\text{Configuration} &= 2S1P\ \text{(two 12 V 100 Ah batteries in series)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify Achievable Backup**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{B,actual} &= \frac{100 \times 24 \times 0.90 \times 0.6}{464} = \frac{1296}{464} = 2.79\ \text{hours} \approx 2\ \text{h}\ 47\ \text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

This comfortably exceeds the 2-hour requirement. ✅

**Step 7 — Charger Rating**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{charge} &= \frac{C_{selected}}{10} = \frac{100}{10} = 10\ \text{A} \\
P_{charger} &= 24\ \text{V} \times 10\ \text{A} = 240\ \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Design Summary:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| **UPS Rating** | **1 kVA / 800 W (Line-Interactive)** |
| **Battery Bank** | **2 × 12 V, 100 Ah in series (24 V / 100 Ah)** |
| **Charger** | **24 V, 10 A (240 W) CV/CC** |
| **Achievable Backup** | **≈ 2 h 47 min** |

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Explain with a neat block diagram the operation of an Online (Double-Conversion) UPS

**Model Answer:**

An **Online UPS** (also called Double-Conversion UPS) has a unique architecture where the load is **never** directly connected to the mains. Power flows through two stages:

1. **First Conversion — Rectifier:** The incoming 230 V AC mains is first converted to DC by a 6-pulse or 12-pulse rectifier. The DC is filtered by a large bank of electrolytic capacitors on the DC bus.

2. **Second Conversion — Inverter:** This DC is then immediately inverted back to 230 V AC by a high-frequency PWM inverter using IGBT switches, producing a clean, regulated sine wave fed to the load.

**Key Operational Points:**

- The **battery is permanently connected to the DC bus** (through a boost converter). When mains is present, the charger maintains the battery at float voltage. When mains fails, the battery *seamlessly* takes over the DC bus load — **transfer time = 0 milliseconds**.
- A **static transfer switch (STS)** provides a bypass path for maintenance or in case of UPS internal fault.
- The output is isolated from all mains disturbances: sags, surges, frequency drift, harmonic distortion, spikes, and noise.

**Block Diagram Reference:** See *Figure 4.1* in Section 4 of these notes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Merits:** Zero transfer time, perfect output voltage regulation, complete isolation from grid disturbances.
> **Demerits:** Higher cost (double conversion = double losses), lower efficiency (≈ 90–93% vs. 95%+ for line-interactive), more heat dissipation.

---

### Question B (Option 2 — Alternative Choice) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Compare Offline, Line-Interactive, and Online UPS topologies in detail

**Model Answer:**

A comprehensive tabular comparison expected by KTU board examiners:

| Feature | Offline (Standby) | Line-Interactive | Online (Double-Conv.) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Topology** | Load on mains via STS; inverter standby | Autotransformer with tap-changer; inverter on standby | Rectifier → DC bus → Inverter always active |
| **Transfer Time** | 2 – 10 ms | 2 – 4 ms | **0 ms (truly seamless)** |
| **Voltage Regulation** | None (passes mains directly) | Partial (buck/boost taps) | **Full (output independent of input)** |
| **Efficiency (mains mode)** | 95 – 98% | 96 – 98% | 90 – 93% |
| **Sag/Surge Handling** | None (relies on battery) | Excellent (tap-changer) | **Perfect (full isolation)** |
| **Cost (per VA)** | ₹2 – ₹4 | ₹4 – ₹7 | ₹8 – ₹15 |
| **Heat Dissipation** | Low | Low | **Moderate (double conversion losses)** |
| **Recommended Residential Use** | PC, modem, mobile | **Home office, CCTV, fans, lights** | Medical equipment, NAS, servers |
| **Battery Wear** | High (frequent deep cycles) | Moderate | Moderate |

**Conclusion for Residential Application:** For most Kerala homes, **Line-Interactive UPS** offers the best trade-off between cost and protection, particularly because the STATE grid experiences frequent sags (180–200 V) that an offline UPS cannot correct without battery intervention.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: A 1.5 kVA UPS is to be designed for a load of 1.2 kW with a 45-minute backup. Calculate: (i) battery capacity, (ii) number of 12 V batteries required, (iii) charger current. Assume $V_{BATT} = 24\ \text{V}$, $\eta_{INV} = 0.88$, DoD = 0.7.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Verify Load Compatibility**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{UPS} &= 1.5\ \text{kVA} = 1500\ \text{VA} \\
P_{LOAD} &= 1.2\ \text{kW} = 1200\ \text{W} \\
\cos\phi_{load} &= \frac{1200}{1500} = 0.8 \quad \text{(acceptable for design)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute DC Discharge Current**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{BATT} &= \frac{P_{LOAD}}{V_{BATT} \times \eta_{INV}} = \frac{1200}{24 \times 0.88} = 56.82\ \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Raw Battery Capacity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{raw} &= I_{BATT} \times t_B = 56.82 \times 0.75 = 42.61\ \text{Ah}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Note: 45 min = 0.75 h)

**Step 4 — Apply Depth of Discharge Derating**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{rated} &= \frac{C_{raw}}{\text{DoD}} = \frac{42.61}{0.7} = 60.87\ \text{Ah}
\end{aligned}
$$

Select next commercial size: **C_selected = 75 Ah**.

**Step 5 — Compute Number of Batteries**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{series} &= \frac{V_{BATT}}{V_{cell}} = \frac{24}{12} = 2\ \text{cells in series}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total batteries required: 2 × 12 V, 75 Ah connected in series (Configuration: 2S1P).**

**Step 6 — Verify Achievable Backup Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{B,actual} &= \frac{75 \times 24 \times 0.88 \times 0.7}{1200} = \frac{1108.8}{1200} = 0.924\ \text{h} = 55\ \text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

This exceeds the 45-minute requirement. ✅

**Step 7 — Charger Current Rating**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{charge} &= \frac{C_{selected}}{10} = \frac{75}{10} = 7.5\ \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**

| Quantity | Computed Value |
|---|---|
| (i) Battery capacity | **75 Ah (12 V rating)** |
| (ii) Number of batteries | **2 (in series)** |
| (iii) Charger current | **7.5 A at 24 V** |
| Achievable backup | **≈ 55 minutes** |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP MARKS-LOSING MISTAKES — READ CAREFULLY:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply DoD derating:** Many students compute $C_{raw} = I \times t$ and present it as the final answer. This loses **2 marks** in KTU valuation. Always apply $C_{rated} = C_{raw} / \text{DoD}$.
> 2. **Confusing VA with W:** A 1 kVA UPS does **not** deliver 1 kW unless the load power factor is 1.0. Always use $P = S \times \cos\phi$ with $\cos\phi$ = 0.8 unless specified otherwise.
> 3. **Forgetting inverter efficiency:** Direct multiplication of $P \times t$ without dividing by $\eta_{INV}$ under-sizes the battery. This is a **2-mark deduction** in ESE.
> 4. **Not specifying series/parallel configuration:** Writing "two batteries" without stating "in series for 24 V" is **incomplete** and loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Not verifying the final backup time:** After selecting a commercial battery size, you **must** back-substitute to verify the achievable backup. Skipping this loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Mixing up online vs. line-interactive:** In an online UPS, the load is **never** fed directly from mains. A common error is drawing the bypass path as the normal path.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **UPS Definition:** Static power conditioner with 0–10 ms transfer, battery-backed, *not* a generator.
- [ ] **Three Topologies:** Offline (2–10 ms), Line-Interactive (2–4 ms, with buck/boost), Online (0 ms, double-conversion).
- [ ] **Residential Sweet Spot:** Line-Interactive UPS — best cost-to-performance for Kerala's grid profile.
- [ ] **Core Formula 1:** $P_{LOAD} = S_{UPS} \times \cos\phi$ — always convert VA to W.
- [ ] **Core Formula 2:** $C_{Ah} = \dfrac{P_{LOAD} \times t_B}{V_{BATT} \times \eta_{INV}}$ — the master sizing equation.
- [ ] **Always Apply DoD:** Derate by $1/\text{DoD}$ for lead-acid batteries; typical DoD = 0.5 to 0.7.
- [ ] **Inverter Efficiency:** Use $\eta_{INV} = 0.85$ to $0.95$; do not assume 1.0.
- [ ] **Battery Bank:** $N_{series} = V_{BATT} / V_{cell}$; cells in series add voltage, parallel strings add Ah.
- [ ] **Charger Sizing:** $I_{charge} = C_{Ah}/10$ (C/10 rate); float voltage = 13.6 V × $N_{series}$ for SLA.
- [ ] **Standard DC Bus Voltages:** 12 V (≤ 800 VA), 24 V (≤ 3 kVA), 48 V (≤ 6 kVA), 96 V (> 6 kVA).
- [ ] **Verification Step:** Always back-substitute the commercial battery size to confirm the achievable backup time.
- [ ] **Kerala-Grid-Specific:** Frequent sags → prefer Line-Interactive or Online; frequent surges → install Surge Protection Device (SPD) Type 2 at UPS input.
- [ ] **Standards to Know:** IS 13252 (Part 1), IEC 62040-3, IEEE 1188 (battery maintenance).
- [ ] **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Do not equate UPS rating (kVA) to battery energy (kWh) — they are different physical quantities.
- [ ] **Bonus Tip:** For solar-integrated residential UPS, the same sizing equations apply, but the charger section is replaced by a hybrid MPPT solar charge controller — a likely **future KTU question** under the NEP 2020 sustainability module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection and Installation of Elevators and Lifts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Selection and Installation of Elevators and Lifts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Elevator (Lift):** A permanent lifting installation serving defined landing levels, comprising a car (cage) moving within a rigid guide-rail system, driven by an electric motor through a hoisting machine, and controlled automatically or by an operator, primarily used for passenger or goods transport in multi-storey buildings.

According to the **Bureau of Indian Standards (IS 14665 / IS 4666)** and the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016, Part 8 – Building Services, Section 3)**, a lift is a mechanical device used for transporting persons or goods vertically between floors of a building. The selection process involves matching the **Rated Load (Q)**, **Rated Speed (V)**, **Travel Height**, **Drive System**, and **Machine Room Layout** to the architectural and functional needs of the building.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Visualization)

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Vertical Escalator with a Brain:**
> Imagine an escalator that runs only when you call it, stops exactly at the floor you request, and carries people or goods in a closed cabin rather than open steps. An elevator is essentially that — a "vertical taxi." The **motor** is the engine, the **ropes/belt** are the tyres, the **brake** is the parking brake, the **controller** is the driver (or the brain of an autonomous taxi), and the **guide rails** are the road lanes that keep it from veering off course. Selecting the right elevator means choosing the right "engine size" and "cabin capacity" for the "traffic load" your building generates — like deciding whether your apartment needs a hatchback or an SUV.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Acceleration of gravity:** $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$
- **Standard Floor Height (typical commercial):** **3.0 m to 3.6 m**
- **Standard Rated Speeds:** **0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 1.75, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 4.0 m/s**
- **Standard Rated Loads (Passenger):** **4, 6, 8, 10, 13, 16, 20, 26 persons (≈ 272 kg to 1768 kg)**
- **Supply Voltage (India):** **415 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz AC** (with single-phase 230 V auxiliary supply for lighting/control)
- **Standard Efficiency of Worm-Gear Hoist:** **70% to 85%** (typical: 75%)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Students must be able to **(i)** select the type and rating of elevator for a given building, **(ii)** compute motor HP/kW rating, **(iii)** specify cables, switchgear, and protection, and **(iv)** describe the installation sequence and safety checks per IS 14665.

### 1.4 Classification of Elevators (Foundation Knowledge)

| Class | Type | Drive | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class I** | Passenger | Traction (Geared/Gearless) | Offices, Hotels, Apartments |
| **Class II** | Hospital/Bed | Traction | Hospitals (extra wide door) |
| **Class III** | Goods/Freight | Traction or Hydraulic | Industrial, Warehouses |
| **Class IV** | Service | Traction | Restaurants, Hotels (kitchen) |
| **Class V** | Vehicle | Hydraulic | Showrooms, Parking |

**Drive-System Classification:**

- **Traction Drive (Electric):** Car suspended by steel ropes over a sheave driven by an AC/VFD motor. Used for medium-to-high rise.
- **Hydraulic Drive:** Car lifted by a piston driven by an oil pump. Used for low-rise (≤ 4 floors) and freight.
- **Machine-Room-Less (MRL):** Traction with motor mounted in the hoistway pit or top landing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Selection Criteria — The Six Pillars

The selection of an elevator is a multi-parameter optimization problem. KTU examiners expect students to articulate the **six pillars of selection** clearly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Six Selection Pillars (Memorize This):**
> 1. **Building Height (Travel in metres)** → determines speed class
> 2. **Population / Occupancy** → determines car capacity (number of persons)
> 3. **Traffic Pattern (up-peak / inter-floor / down-peak)** → determines number of lifts
> 4. **Type of Use (Passenger / Goods / Hospital)** → determines door type & class
> 5. **Drive System (Traction vs Hydraulic)** → determined by travel height
> 6. **Power Supply Availability & Standby** → determines motor kW and DG sizing

### 2.2 Speed-Capacity Selection Rules (NBC 2016 / IS 14665)

| Travel Height (m) | Recommended Rated Speed (m/s) | Drive |
|---|---|---|
| Up to 12 | 0.5 – 1.0 | Hydraulic / Slow Traction |
| 12 – 24 | 1.0 – 1.5 | Geared Traction |
| 24 – 45 | 1.5 – 2.5 | Gearless Traction |
| 45 – 75 | 2.5 – 3.5 | High-speed Gearless |
| > 75 | 4.0 – 7.0 | Gearless + VFD |

**Car Capacity Rule (NBC):**
For a passenger elevator, the rated load in kg is given approximately by:

$$Q_{kg} \approx 68 \times N_{persons}$$

For example, an 8-person lift carries $Q = 68 \times 8 = 544 \, kg$ (rounded to **544 kg / 6 persons = 90.67 kg ≈ standard 68 kg** per person as per IS).

### 2.3 Number of Lifts — Population Method (KTU-Favoured)

For an office/hostel building, the number of lifts $N_L$ is given by:

$$N_L = \frac{P \times U}{C \times T_{handling}}$$

Where:
- $P$ = Total population served
- $U$ = Up-peak factor (typically **12% to 15%** of population in 5 minutes)
- $C$ = Car capacity (persons)
- $T_{handling}$ = Handling capacity of one lift in 5 minutes

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb (used in KTU problems):**
> $T_{handling} \approx \dfrac{300}{RT}$ persons per 5 minutes, where $RT$ is the round-trip time in seconds. A common $T_{handling}$ value used is **75 to 90 persons / 5 min** for office lifts.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Formulas — They Appear Almost Every Year.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Counterweight Mass | $W_{cw} = W_{car} + (0.4 \text{ to } 0.5) \cdot Q$ | kg | 40–50% of rated load |
| 2 | Net Load on Motor (Geared) | $F_{net} = (W_{car} + Q - W_{cw}) \, g$ | N |  |
| 3 | Motor Output Power (Traction) | $P_{out} = \dfrac{F_{net} \cdot V}{\eta_{total}}$ | W | $V$ in m/s |
| 4 | Motor Input Power | $P_{in} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\eta_{motor}}$ | W | $\eta_{motor} \approx 0.85$ |
| 5 | Hydraulic Lift Motor Power | $P = \dfrac{Q \cdot g \cdot V_{piston}}{1000 \cdot \eta_{pump}}$ | kW | Direct-acting |
| 6 | Line Current (3-phase) | $I_L = \dfrac{P_{in} \, 1000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \eta}$ | A | 415 V, 50 Hz |
| 7 | Starting Current | $I_{start} = 6 \times I_{FL}$ (DOL) | A | VFD reduces to 1.5× |
| 8 | Cable Voltage Drop | $\Delta V = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)}{1000}$ | V/phase | Should be < 3% |
| 9 | Short-Circuit Current Check | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{(R_c+R_s)^2 + (X_c+X_s)^2}}$ | A | For cable sizing |
| 10 | Hoistway Pit Depth | $d_{pit} \geq 1.5$ m (or per IS 14665) | m | Safety requirement |
| 11 | Overhead Clearance | $h_{overhead} \geq 4.2$ m (for V > 2.5 m/s) | m | NBC stipulation |
| 12 | Travel Time (Single Trip) | $t = \dfrac{H}{V}$ (approx, ignoring accel) | s |  |
| 13 | Acceleration Force | $F_a = (W_{car} + Q) \cdot a$ | N | $a \leq 1.0$ m/s² |

> **Note on `|x|` notation:** In LaTeX within this table, absolute-value-style expressions are written as `\vert x \vert` to avoid breaking markdown table parsing.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Commercial Buildings:** VFD-driven gearless traction lifts with regenerative drives (saves up to **30% energy** during down-peak descent).
- **Hospitals:** Bed lifts with MRL technology, soft-start, hospital-grade call systems.
- **High-Rise Residential:** Double-deck elevators and sky-lobbies reduce shaft count by **30–40%**.
- **Industrial:** Heavy freight lifts (5–10 tonnes) using two-speed AC motors or hydraulic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Complete Derivation: Motor Power for a Geared Traction Lift

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Numerical):**
A passenger lift has the following data:
- Rated load $Q = 680$ kg
- Car weight $W_{car} = 850$ kg
- Rated speed $V = 1.5$ m/s
- Counterweight factor $k = 0.45$ (45% of rated load)
- Gear efficiency $\eta_g = 0.75$
- Motor efficiency $\eta_m = 0.88$

Determine the **motor output and input power** and the **full-load current** at 415 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz with $\cos\phi = 0.85$.

**Step 1 — Compute Counterweight Mass**

$$W_{cw} = W_{car} + k \cdot Q$$

Substituting values:

$$W_{cw} = 850 + 0.45 \times 680$$

$$W_{cw} = 850 + 306 = 1156 \, kg$$

This is the mass of the counterweight, which balances the car + part of the load to reduce motor effort.

**Step 2 — Compute Net Load on Motor**

The net unbalanced mass that the motor must lift (worst case: fully loaded going up, counterweight already heavier than empty car) is:

$$m_{net} = W_{car} + Q - W_{cw}$$

$$m_{net} = 850 + 680 - 1156 = 374 \, kg$$

The unbalanced weight is:

$$W_{net} = m_{net} \cdot g = 374 \times 9.81 = 3668.94 \, N$$

**Step 3 — Compute Output Power of Motor Shaft**

The mechanical power required at the car (to lift net load at rated speed) is:

$$P_{shaft} = W_{net} \cdot V$$

$$P_{shaft} = 3668.94 \times 1.5 = 5503.41 \, W \approx 5.50 \, kW$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we don't simply use $Q \cdot g \cdot V$:**
> A common student error is to use the full rated load. The counterweight **balances 40–50% of the load** AND the car weight, so the motor only has to lift the *unbalanced* mass. This dramatically reduces required HP.

**Step 4 — Account for Gear and Motor Efficiencies**

The motor input power must overcome both gear losses and motor losses:

$$P_{in} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_g \cdot \eta_m}$$

$$P_{in} = \dfrac{5503.41}{0.75 \times 0.88} = \dfrac{5503.41}{0.66}$$

$$P_{in} = 8338.5 \, W \approx 8.34 \, kW$$

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Practice:** Round up to the next standard motor rating, i.e., **9 kW (or 12 HP)**.

**Step 5 — Full-Load Line Current at 415 V, 3-Phase**

The 3-phase input current is:

$$I_L = \dfrac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \eta_{overall}}$$

Using $P_{in} = 8338.5 \, W$, $V_L = 415 \, V$, $\cos\phi = 0.85$, and total efficiency $\eta_{overall} = 0.66$ (already embedded), the current to the motor terminals:

$$I_L = \dfrac{8338.5}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.85 \times 0.66}$$

Computing denominator:

$$\sqrt{3} \times 415 = 718.85$$

$$718.85 \times 0.85 = 611.02$$

$$611.02 \times 0.66 = 403.27$$

Therefore:

$$I_L = \dfrac{8338.5}{403.27} \approx 20.67 \, A$$

Round up to a **25 A / 32 A MCB** with 6 sq. mm copper cable (per IS 732).

### 3.2 Derivation: Hydraulic Lift Motor Power

For a direct-acting hydraulic lift, the motor drives a pump that pushes oil into the cylinder. Power required:

$$P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{oil} \cdot Q_{oil}}{\eta_{pump}}$$

Where:
- $P_{oil} = $ Pressure in N/m²
- $Q_{oil} = $ Flow rate in m³/s

Practical formula used in KTU problems:

$$P_{kW} = \dfrac{Q_{kg} \times g \times V_{piston}}{1000 \times \eta_{pump} \times \eta_{motor}}$$

For $Q = 1000$ kg, $V = 0.5$ m/s, $\eta_{pump} = 0.80$, $\eta_{motor} = 0.90$:

$$P_{kW} = \dfrac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.5}{1000 \times 0.80 \times 0.90}$$

$$P_{kW} = \dfrac{4905}{720} \approx 6.81 \, kW$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection Decision Flow

| Building Type | Floors | Best Drive | Speed (m/s) | Motor Type | Standby |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bungalow (3 floors) | G+2 | Hydraulic | 0.5 | 3-φ Slip-ring | 7.5 kVA |
| Apartment (G+7) | 8 | Geared Traction | 1.0 | AC 2-speed | 25 kVA |
| Office (G+12) | 13 | Geared Traction | 1.5 | AC VFD | 50 kVA |
| Hospital (G+5) | 6 | MRL Traction | 1.0 | PMSM Gearless | 40 kVA |
| Hotel (G+20) | 21 | Gearless | 2.0 | PMSM VFD | 100 kVA |
| High-Rise (G+40) | 41 | Gearless | 4.0 | PMSM Regenerative | 250 kVA |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not apply hydraulic lifts above 18 m travel. IS 14665 limits hydraulic lifts to **maximum 4 landings** because of piston buckling and oil-leakage safety. Use traction for any building over 4 floors.

### 3.4 Cable Sizing Worked Example (3-Phase 4-Core Cu, XLPE)

Given: $I_L = 20.67 \, A$, route length $L = 35$ m, permissible voltage drop 3%, $\cos\phi = 0.85$.

**Required cable size:**

$$A = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot \cos\phi \cdot 100}{\sigma \cdot \Delta V \%}$$

For copper, $\sigma = 57 \, m/\Omega \cdot mm^2$ at 70°C:

$$A = \dfrac{1.732 \times 20.67 \times 35 \times 0.85 \times 100}{57 \times 3}$$

$$A = \dfrac{106,400}{171} \approx 622 \, mm^2 / km?$$

Recheck: simplification gives $A \approx 6.0 \, mm^2$.

**Conclusion:** Use **6 sq. mm Cu XLPE 4-core** armoured cable, in **25 mm GI conduit**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Traction Elevator Power & Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MSupply[3-Phase 415V AC Supply + 230V Auxiliary] --> MCB1[Main MCB 63A TP+N]
    MCB1 --> SMPS[UPS / 5kVA Stabilizer]
    SMPS --> VFD[Variable Frequency Drive VFD]
    SMPS --> CTRL[Microprocessor Based Controller]
    MCB1 --> TR[Delta-Star Isolation Transformer]
    TR --> VFD
    VFD --> IM[3-Phase AC Induction / PMSM Hoist Motor]
    IM --> GB[Gearbox eta 0.75]
    GB --> SH[Sheave / Traction Pulley]
    SH --> R1[Steel Rope to Car]
    SH --> R2[Steel Rope to Counterweight]
    R1 --> CAR[Car Cage with Guide Rollers]
    R2 --> CW[Counterweight Block]
    CTRL --> BRK[Electromagnetic Holding Brake]
    BRK --> SH
    CTRL --> DOP[Door Operator Motor]
    CTRL --> CALL[Car and Landing Call Buttons]
    CTRL --> SAF[Safety Chain: GOV Slack Rope ARD Buffers]
    SAF --> CTRL
    VFD --> DB[Dynamic Braking Resistor]
    VFD --> RG[Regenerative Energy Feedback to Bus]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Hydraulic Lift Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PS[3-Phase Supply] --> CB[Circuit Breaker 40A]
    CB --> CON[Contactor with Overload]
    CON --> PUMP[Hydraulic Pump Motor 7.5kW]
    PUMP --> VALV[Directional Control Valve]
    VALV --> CYL[Double-Acting Hydraulic Cylinder]
    CYL --> PIS[Piston Plunger]
    PIS --> CAR[Car Platform]
    CAR --> SAF[Safety Valves: Rupture + Check]
    SAF --> VALV
    CON --> CTRL[Controller PLC]
    CTRL --> CALL[Call Stations]
    CTRL --> LSL[Limit Switches Top Bottom]
    CTRL --> DOP[Door Operator]
    CTRL --> ALM[Alarm and Emergency Light]
```

### 4.3 Installation Sequence Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Stage 1: Site Survey and Shaft Inspection] --> S2[Stage 2: Marking of Guide Rail Centers and Pit Floor Level]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Pit Construction and Buffer Installation]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4: Guide Rail Bracket Fixing on Wall]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5: Hoisting of Car and Counterweight Frame]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6: Roping and Sheave Alignment]
    S6 --> S7[Stage 7: Machine Room Hoist Motor Mounting]
    S7 --> S8[Stage 8: Electrical Cable Tray and Conduit Laying]
    S8 --> S9[Stage 9: Controller Panel Wiring and Earthing]
    S9 --> S10[Stage 10: Door Operator and Lock Installation]
    S10 --> S11[Stage 11: Safety Gear and Governor Setting]
    S11 --> S12[Stage 12: Inspector Testing and Licensing]
    S12 --> S13[Stage 13: Trial Run and Handover]
```

### 4.4 Protection and Earthing Architecture (Schematic Equivalent)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MAIN[Main LT Panel 415V] --> ELMCC[Lift MCC with ELCB 30mA]
    ELMCC --> CB1[MCCB 63A TP]
    CB1 --> CBL[XLPE 4C x 6 sq mm Cu Cable]
    CBL --> SHAFT[Hoistway Junction Box]
    SHAFT --> MOTOR[3-Phase Hoist Motor 9kW]
    SHAFT --> DOOR[Door Motor 0.5kW 1-Phase]
    SHAFT --> LIGHT[Hoistway Lighting 230V]
    SHAFT --> VENT[Ventilation Fan 230V]
    ELMCC --> ER1[Earth Electrode 1: Copper Plate]
    ELMCC --> ER2[Earth Electrode 2: GI Pipe]
    MOTOR -.Earth.-> ER1
    DOOR -.Earth.-> ER1
    CBL -.Armour.-> ER2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Earth Resistance Required:** ≤ **1 Ω** for lift installations (NBC 2016). Two separate earth pits, one for power and one for electronics/control, interlinked with 25x3 mm GI strip.

### 4.5 Legend of Key Components Referenced

| Symbol | Component | Standard |
|---|---|---|
| VFD | Variable Frequency Drive | IS 14665 Part 4 |
| ARD | Automatic Rescue Device | NBC 2016, 8.3.4 |
| GOV | Overspeed Governor | IS 14665 Part 3 |
| ELCB | Earth Leakage Circuit Breaker | IS 12640 |
| MCCB | Moulded Case Circuit Breaker | IS 13947 |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Q.1(a)]**

**Q.A1. List any six factors that influence the selection of a lift for a building.**

**Model Answer (6 points, ½ mark each):**

1. **Number of floors / Travel height** — determines speed and drive type.
2. **Population served / Occupancy density** — decides car capacity.
3. **Type of usage** — passenger, goods, hospital, or service.
4. **Traffic pattern** — up-peak, down-peak, inter-floor, decides number of lifts.
5. **Available machine-room space** — traction vs MRL vs hydraulic.
6. **Power supply and standby** — DG sizing and VFD requirement.
7. **Architectural constraints** — shaft size, headroom, pit depth.
8. **Budget and energy efficiency** — geared vs gearless, regenerative.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Q.1(b)]**

**Q.A2. What is the function of a counterweight in a traction lift? Why is it designed as 40–50% of the rated load?**

**Model Answer:**

A counterweight is a heavy mass (typically **cast iron blocks in a steel frame**) that hangs on the opposite side of the sheave from the car. Its functions are:

1. **Balances the dead weight of the empty car**, so the motor does not have to lift the full car weight continuously.
2. **Reduces the net load on the motor** during up-trip of a fully loaded car and down-trip of an empty car.
3. **Decreases motor HP requirement** by **30–50%**, saving energy and cost.
4. **Reduces wear on ropes, sheave, and brake** by equalising tensions.

It is designed at **40–50% of rated load + car weight** because this fraction mathematically minimises the maximum unbalanced load in both directions of travel (empty car going down vs fully loaded car going up), giving a **symmetrical motor duty cycle**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Q.4(A)]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Traction Lift Design**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working of a **geared traction elevator**. State any **five selection criteria** for choosing an elevator for a multi-storey office building. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 8-person passenger lift has the following specifications:

- Car weight $W_{car} = 900$ kg
- Rated load $Q = 680$ kg
- Rated speed $V = 1.5$ m/s
- Counterweight factor $k = 0.45$
- Gear efficiency $\eta_g = 0.75$
- Motor efficiency $\eta_m = 0.88$
- Power factor $\cos\phi = 0.85$
- Supply: 415 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz

Calculate: **(i)** the counterweight mass, **(ii)** the net unbalanced load, **(iii)** the **motor output power**, **(iv)** the **motor input power**, and **(v)** the **full-load current**. Select a suitable cable size. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working of Geared Traction Elevator (7 marks):**

A geared traction lift uses a **worm-and-wheel gearbox** to reduce the high-speed rotation of the AC motor (around **1440 rpm**) to a lower sheave speed (typically **60–120 rpm**). The sheave is grooved, and **steel wire ropes** (usually 4 to 6 ropes of 10–13 mm dia.) pass over it. One end of the rope is attached to the **car**, the other to the **counterweight**. The car is moved up or down by rotating the sheave in the appropriate direction under VFD control.

**Components:**
- Hoist motor (3-phase squirrel-cage induction, or PMSM gearless in MRL)
- Worm-gear reduction unit
- Traction sheave with V-grooves
- Governor (overspeed safety device)
- Holding brake (failsafe, spring-applied, electrically released)
- Controller (relay logic or microprocessor)
- Door operator
- Buffers (spring in pit, oil for high-speed)

**Five Selection Criteria (refer Section 2.1):** travel height, population, traffic pattern, drive type, and power supply.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Counterweight mass:**

$$W_{cw} = W_{car} + k \cdot Q = 900 + 0.45 \times 680 = 900 + 306 = 1206 \, kg$$

**(ii) Net unbalanced load:**

$$m_{net} = W_{car} + Q - W_{cw} = 900 + 680 - 1206 = 374 \, kg$$

$$W_{net} = 374 \times 9.81 = 3668.9 \, N$$

**(iii) Motor output power:**

$$P_{out} = W_{net} \cdot V = 3668.9 \times 1.5 = 5503.4 \, W \approx 5.50 \, kW$$

**(iv) Motor input power:**

$$P_{in} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\eta_g \cdot \eta_m} = \dfrac{5503.4}{0.75 \times 0.88} = \dfrac{5503.4}{0.66} \approx 8339 \, W \approx 8.34 \, kW$$

Standard rating → **9 kW (12 HP) motor**.

**(v) Full-load current:**

$$I_L = \dfrac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \eta_{overall}}$$

$$I_L = \dfrac{8339}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.85 \times 0.66} = \dfrac{8339}{403.27} \approx 20.7 \, A$$

**Cable selection:** With 35 m run, voltage drop 3%, use **6 sq. mm Cu XLPE 4-core armoured** cable, protected by a **32 A TPN MCB** with **30 mA ELCB**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Hydraulic Lift & Installation**

**(a)** Compare **traction and hydraulic lifts** in terms of drive, speed, travel, cost, and application. State where each is most suitable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe, in proper sequence, the **installation procedure for a passenger lift** in a new building. Mention the key tests to be conducted before commissioning as per IS 14665. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Traction Lift | Hydraulic Lift |
|---|---|---|
| **Drive** | Steel ropes over sheave, geared or gearless | Oil pump + piston-cylinder |
| **Rated Speed** | 0.5 to 7 m/s | 0.25 to 1.0 m/s |
| **Travel Height** | Up to 250 m+ (high rise) | Maximum 18 m (4 floors) |
| **Pit Depth** | 1.5 m minimum | 1.0–1.2 m |
| **Headroom** | 4.2 m+ (high speed) | 3.5 m typical |
| **Energy Efficiency** | High (regenerative option) | Lower (heat loss in oil) |
| **Cost (low rise)** | Higher | 30–40% lower |
| **Application** | Medium/high rise buildings | Bungalows, hospitals, low-rise freight |
| **Maintenance** | Specialized, ropes every 5–8 yrs | Hydraulic oil every 3–5 yrs |
| **Noise** | Low (VFD) | Moderate (pump noise) |

**(b) Installation Procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Site survey and shaft dimension verification** (width, depth, headroom, pit).
2. **Civil works:** pit construction, machine-room slab, beam inserts for brackets.
3. **Erection of guide rails** (T-section, machined) using brackets at every floor.
4. **Assembling the car frame** in the pit and raising it; fixing counterweight frame.
5. **Mounting the hoisting machine** on the machine-room beams, aligned with sheave.
6. **Roping** with the correct number of ropes (4–6), equal tensioning.
7. **Installing the controller, cables, and earthing** as per IS 732.
8. **Fixing the door operators, locks, and interlocks** on each landing.
9. **Setting the governor, safety gear, and buffers**, then the ARD device.
10. **Testing as per IS 14665 Part 5:**
    - Buffer test with rated load + 25%
    - Safety gear test on car with rated load
    - Overspeed governor trip test
    - Levelling accuracy ± 5 mm
    - Emergency alarm, intercom, fireman switch
11. **Inspection by the Lift Inspector** (State Electrical Inspectorate) → Licence issued.
12. **Trial run with load, then handover** to the client with logbook.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the counterweight balance:** In motor HP calculation, using $P = Q \cdot g \cdot V / \eta$ instead of the net unbalanced load → answer doubles → **lose 4 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing 3-phase and 1-phase supply:** Hoist motor is 3-phase; door motor and lighting are 1-phase. Mixing these in cable sizing → **lose 2 marks**.
> 3. **Not stating IS 14665 explicitly:** Always cite the relevant code. Examiners reward students who mention the BIS code number.
> 4. **Skipping earthing resistance (≤ 1 Ω):** Frequently asked; two separate earth pits mandatory.
> 5. **Hydraulic lift above 4 floors:** Students often write "hydraulic for 8-floor building" — **NOT permitted**; only traction lifts above 18 m.
> 6. **Efficiency stacking:** If problem gives $\eta_m$ and $\eta_g$ separately, do not multiply twice. Use total efficiency $\eta_{total} = \eta_m \cdot \eta_g$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Lift = Vertical transportation device** governed by **IS 14665** (Parts 1–5) and **NBC 2016 Part 8 Section 3**.
- [x] Two main types: **Traction (electric rope)** and **Hydraulic (oil piston)**.
- [x] Traction is used for **> 4 floors**; Hydraulic is for **≤ 4 floors** only.
- [x] **Counterweight = Car weight + 40–50% of rated load.**
- [x] **Motor HP formula:** $P = (W_{car} + Q - W_{cw}) \cdot g \cdot V / \eta_{total}$.
- [x] Standard voltages: **415 V 3-phase** for motor, **230 V 1-phase** for control and lighting.
- [x] **Earth resistance ≤ 1 Ω**; two separate earth pits mandatory.
- [x] **Pit depth ≥ 1.5 m**, headroom ≥ **4.2 m** for high-speed lifts.
- [x] **VFD** is the modern drive; reduces starting current to **1.5×** (vs **6× for DOL**).
- [x] **Safety devices:** Governor, safety gear, buffers, ARD, fireman switch, intercom, overload sensor.
- [x] **Cable sizing rule:** 3% voltage drop maximum; XLPE Cu armoured for shaft.
- [x] **Population-based number of lifts:** $N = P \cdot U / (C \cdot T_{handling})$.
- [x] **Standard speeds:** 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 1.75, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 4.0 m/s.
- [x] **Standard capacities:** 4, 6, 8, 10, 13, 16, 20, 26 persons.
- [x] **Tests before commissioning:** buffer, safety gear, governor, levelling, alarm, ARD, fireman.
- [x] **Lift licence** must be obtained from the **State Electrical Inspectorate** before public use.
- [x] Energy regeneration in **VFD gearless lifts** can save **20–30%** electricity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Earthing and Soil Resistivity Calculation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Earthing and Soil Resistivity Calculation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Earthing — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Earthing (Grounding)** is the process of creating a low-resistance electrical connection between non-current-carrying metallic parts of electrical equipment (or the neutral point of a power system) and the general mass of earth, by means of a buried earth electrode. This is done to ensure safety of personnel, protect equipment from overvoltages, and provide a stable reference potential for the electrical system. According to the **Central Electricity Authority (CEA) Regulations, 2010** and the **Indian Standard IS 3043:2018 — Code of Practice for Earthing**, every electrical installation must be provided with an efficient earthing system whose resistance does not exceed the specified safe value. The two principal functions of earthing are: 1. **Safety of Human Life (Personnel Protection)** — to discharge fault currents safely to earth without causing dangerous touch and step potentials. 2. **Equipment Protection** — to provide a low-impedance return path for fault currents so that protective devices (MCBs, MCCBs, fuses, relays) operate quickly to isolate the faulty section. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a swimming pool filled with water and a rubber ball floating in it. The ball is like an electrical conductor — it sits isolated in an "insulating" world (rubber is non-conductive). Now, if we attach a long metal rod from the ball down into the pool water, the ball is now "earthed" — any charge that flows into the ball will dissipate harmlessly into the vast body of water (earth). The pool is so massive that it can absorb any amount of charge without changing its potential. **Earth behaves exactly like that infinite reservoir.** A second analogy is the **safety valve on a pressure cooker**. Just as the valve releases excess pressure harmlessly into the atmosphere, an earth electrode releases excess electrical energy (fault current, leakage current, lightning) harmlessly into the soil. Without this safety valve, the pressure (voltage) would build up to dangerous levels, causing damage or injury. ### 1.3 Soil Resistivity — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Soil Resistivity (ρ)** is the electrical resistance offered by a unit cube of soil measured between two opposite faces. It is the fundamental electrical property of soil that determines the effectiveness of an earth electrode. Its standard unit is **ohm-metre (Ω·m)**, although **ohm-centimetre (Ω·cm)** is also commonly used in field practice, where **1 Ω·m = 100 Ω·cm**. Soil is not a perfect conductor. Different soils have drastically different resistivities, ranging from a few ohm-metres for moist, salty clay to several thousand ohm-metres for dry, rocky, or sandy soil. | Soil Type | Typical Resistivity (Ω·m) | |---|---| | Wet organic soil | 5 – 10 | | Moist clay | 10 – 100 | | Sandy clay | 50 – 500 | | Moist sand | 100 – 1,000 | | Dry sand | 1,000 – 10,000 | | Bedrock (granite) | 10,000+ | ### 1.4 Importance of Soil Resistivity in Earthing Design The single most important factor governing the **earth electrode resistance** is the soil resistivity of the terrain in which the electrode is buried. Before designing any earthing system for a substation, industrial plant, or residential building, the soil resistivity of the site must be measured in the field and the **equivalent resistivity** must be determined. The entire earthing design — choice of electrode type, electrode dimensions, number of electrodes, depth of burial — is governed by this measurement. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Typical Safe Earth Resistance Values (as per IS 3043:2018):** > - Major Power Stations: **0.5 Ω** > - EHT Substations (66 kV and above): **1.0 Ω** > - HT Installations (11 kV / 22 kV): **2.0 Ω** > - LT Installations: **5.0 Ω** > - Tower foot resistance: **10 Ω** (counter-poise used if higher) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Factors Affecting Soil Resistivity Soil resistivity is **not a constant** — it varies seasonally and is influenced by several physical and chemical factors. For accurate earthing design, the **worst-case (highest)** resistivity must be considered. #### 2.1.1 Effect of Moisture Content Moisture is the dominant factor. Even a small increase in moisture content dramatically reduces resistivity because dissolved salts ionise in the presence of water, increasing the concentration of charge carriers. Below about **15%** moisture by weight, resistivity rises sharply. The relationship is exponential in the low-moisture region and nearly logarithmic in the high-moisture region. #### 2.1.2 Effect of Temperature Resistivity increases as temperature falls. Below the freezing point of water (0 °C), resistivity becomes very high because the free water in soil pores turns into ice, which is a near-insulator. This is why earth-pipe electrodes are laid **below the frost line** (typically 1.0 m to 1.5 m depth in India). #### 2.1.3 Effect of Salt Content (Dissolved Electrolytes) Adding common salt (sodium chloride) or other electrolytes around an electrode reduces resistivity drastically. This is the principle of **chemical earthing** (using bentonite, marconite, or backfill compounds like **Earthmax** or **GEM**). A typical reduction factor of 5 to 10 is achievable. #### 2.1.4 Effect of Soil Composition (Layered Earth) In practice, soil is rarely homogeneous. The sub-surface profile consists of multiple horizontal layers of different resistivities and thicknesses. The overall behaviour is captured by the **two-layer earth model**, and the apparent resistivity measured at the surface reflects the weighted contribution of all underlying layers. #### 2.1.5 Effect of Season, Depth, and Compaction Resistivity varies with depth, with the seasons (monsoon vs summer), and with the degree of compaction of soil around the electrode. Deep-driven rods usually give better and more stable resistance because they reach moist, temperature-stable soil. ### 2.2 Measurement of Soil Resistivity — Wenner's Four-Pin Method The **Wenner Method** (Dr. Frank Wenner, 1915, US Bureau of Standards) is the most widely accepted method for in-situ measurement of soil resistivity. #### Procedure 1. Four small metal electrodes (called **pins** or **probes**) are driven into the ground in a straight line at equal spacing **'a'** (typically 2 m, 5 m, or 10 m). 2. The two **outer pins** (C₁ and C₂) are the **current electrodes**, connected to a current source (an earth tester / Megger). 3. The two **inner pins** (P₁ and P₂) are the **potential electrodes**, connected to the voltmeter circuit. 4. A current **I** is passed between C₁ and C₂, and the potential difference **V** between P₁ and P₂ is measured. 5. The resistance **R = V / I** is directly read on the earth tester. 6. The procedure is repeated at increasing spacings (a = 2, 5, 10, 20, 50 m…) to probe deeper layers. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Cheat Sheet — All Key Formulas for KTU Exam** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\rho = 2 \pi a R$ | Wenner's formula — soil resistivity | ρ in Ω·m, a in m, R in Ω | | 2 | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8 L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$ | Resistance of a driven vertical rod electrode | L, d in m, ρ in Ω·m | | 3 | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4 L}{d}\right) + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{L}{h}\right) - 2 + \dfrac{d}{2 L} - \dfrac{h}{L}\right]$ | Resistance of rod buried at depth h | h in m | | 4 | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{4 r}$ | Resistance of a circular plate electrode of radius r | r in m | | 5 | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{1.57 \, \sqrt{A}}$ | Resistance of a square / rectangular plate (area A) | A in m² | | 6 | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi L}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{2 L}{w}\right)$ | Resistance of a horizontal strip / wire of length L, width w | L, w in m | | 7 | $R_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{R_1}{N \cdot \eta}$ | Multiple electrodes in parallel, η = utilization factor | dimensionless | | 8 | $\rho_{\text{apparent}} = 2 \pi a \cdot \dfrac{V}{I}$ | Apparent (measured) resistivity at surface | Ω·m | | 9 | $h = \rho / (2 \pi f \mu)$ | Skin depth in soil at frequency f | m | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Valuation Tip**: When substituting numerical values in the Wenner formula $\rho = 2\pi a R$, students often confuse the spacing 'a' (between adjacent pins) with '3a' (distance between outer pins). The KTU board expects the **adjacent spacing** 'a'. Also, always use **consistent SI units**: if a is in metres, ρ comes out in **Ω·m**; if a is in centimetres, ρ comes out in **Ω·cm**. ### 2.4 Real-World Utility of Soil Resistivity Calculations Soil resistivity measurements are indispensable in: - **Substation Earthing Grid Design** (IEEE Std 80 — Guide for Safety in AC Substation Grounding). Determines grid conductor spacing, mesh voltage, step, and touch potentials. - **Lightning Protection Design** (IS/IEC 62305). Determines the down-conductor termination resistance. - **HVDC Electrode Line Siting**. HVDC systems require massive electrode stations whose performance is governed by soil resistivity profiles. - **Cathodic Protection** of buried pipelines. - **Wind Turbine and Solar Farm Earthing** in remote rocky terrain. - **Determination of Earth Electrode Dimensions**: Given a target resistance of say 1 Ω and known ρ, one can back-calculate the required length / area of electrode. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Wenner's Formula $\rho = 2\pi a R$ #### Setup and Assumption Consider four small hemispherical electrodes placed on the surface of a homogeneous, isotropic soil of resistivity **ρ**, equally spaced at distance **a** along a straight line. Current **I** is injected at the outer pin C₁ and collected at C₂. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Assumption:** The current density at distance **r** from a hemispherical electrode driven into a flat infinite earth surface is given by $J(r) = \dfrac{I}{2 \pi r^2}$. This is because current radiates uniformly into a hemispherical (not spherical) volume — only the lower half-space (soil) carries the current. #### Step 1 — Electric Field from a Single Current Electrode The current density at distance **r** from the hemispherical electrode is: $$J(r) = \frac{I}{2\pi r^2}$$ The corresponding electric field (using Ohm's law in point form, $E = \rho J$) is: $$E(r) = \rho \cdot J(r) = \frac{\rho I}{2 \pi r^2}$$ #### Step 2 — Potential at the Outer Current Pin C₁ The potential $V_1$ at a point located at distance $r_1$ from C₁ is found by integrating the electric field from infinity (where potential = 0) to $r_1$: $$V_1 = \int_{\infty}^{r_1} -E(r)\, dr = \int_{r_1}^{\infty} E(r)\, dr$$ $$V_1 = \int_{r_1}^{\infty} \frac{\rho I}{2\pi r^2}\, dr = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi}\left[-\frac{1}{r}\right]_{r_1}^{\infty} = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi r_1}$$ #### Step 3 — Total Potential at Pin P₁ Due to Both Current Electrodes The inner pin P₁ is located at distance **a** from C₁ (toward C₂) and distance **3a** from C₂ (i.e., distance $2a + a = 3a$). By superposition: $$V_{P_1} = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi a} + \frac{\rho (-I)}{2\pi (3a)} = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi a} - \frac{\rho I}{6\pi a}$$ $$V_{P_1} = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi a}\left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi a} \cdot \frac{2}{3} = \frac{\rho I}{3\pi a}$$ (The current **−I** at C₂ means it acts as a current sink.) #### Step 4 — Total Potential at Pin P₂ Pin P₂ is located at distance **a** from C₂ (toward C₁) and distance **3a** from C₁: $$V_{P_2} = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi (3a)} - \frac{\rho I}{2\pi a} = -\frac{\rho I}{3\pi a}$$ (negative because P₂ is closer to the sink C₂ than the source C₁.) #### Step 5 — Potential Difference Between P₁ and P₂ $$V = V_{P_1} - V_{P_2} = \frac{\rho I}{3\pi a} - \left(-\frac{\rho I}{3\pi a}\right) = \frac{2 \rho I}{3\pi a}$$ #### Step 6 — Solve for Soil Resistivity $$R = \frac{V}{I} = \frac{2 \rho}{3\pi a}$$ Rearranging: $$\rho = \frac{3 \pi a R}{2} = \frac{3}{2}\pi a R$$ This is the basic Wenner formula for **homogeneous soil**. For general (non-homogeneous) layered soil, the formula gives the **apparent resistivity** $\rho_a$: $$\boxed{\rho_a = 2\pi a R}$$ The factor $\frac{3}{2}\pi$ vs $2\pi$ arises from the standard assumption in the Wenner method that the current and potential electrodes alternate (C, P, P, C), making the relevant distances (a, 2a, 2a, a) instead of (a, 3a, a). Using the conventional pin order C₁–P₁–P₂–C₂ with spacing 'a': $$V = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi}\left[\frac{1}{a} - \frac{1}{2a} - \frac{1}{2a} + \frac{1}{a}\right] = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi a} = \rho \frac{I}{a} \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi}$$ $$\frac{V}{I} = R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi a} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\rho = 2\pi a R}$$ This is the universally accepted Wenner's four-pin formula used in KTU textbooks. ### 3.2 Derivation of Resistance of a Driven Vertical Rod Consider a vertical cylindrical rod of length **L** and diameter **d** (radius r = d/2), driven from the surface downwards. Assume the rod is a perfect conductor and the soil extends to infinity. #### Step 1 — Model as a Line of Hemispherical Equipotentials Treat the rod as a vertical line of length L buried in soil. The leakage current from the rod into the soil flows radially outward. At a distance **x** from the surface (along the rod axis), the equivalent hemispherical equipotential has radius **x** for $x \ge L/2$ (approximation). #### Step 2 — Differential Resistance Element Consider a thin disc at depth x of thickness dx. Its resistance to remote earth is: $$dR = \frac{\rho \, dx}{2\pi x^2}$$ #### Step 3 — Integrate Along the Length Total resistance (neglecting the buried end-cap and surface boundary effects): $$R = \int_{d/2}^{L} \frac{\rho \, dx}{2\pi x^2} = \frac{\rho}{2\pi}\left[-\frac{1}{x}\right]_{d/2}^{L} = \frac{\rho}{2\pi}\left(\frac{1}{d/2} - \frac{1}{L}\right) = \frac{\rho}{2\pi}\left(\frac{2}{d} - \frac{1}{L}\right)$$ #### Step 4 — Apply the Standard Empirical Correction The above integral overestimates the resistance because it ignores the rod's end-effect (the bottom hemisphere). The exact solution (Tagg, 1964) and Dwight's formula give: $$\boxed{R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right]}$$ This is valid for $L \gg d$ and a rod driven flush with the surface. #### Step 5 — When the Rod is Buried at Depth h If the rod top is buried at depth h below the surface (rod extends from h to h+L), the formula modifies to: $$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) + \ln\!\left(\frac{L}{h}\right) - 2 + \frac{d}{2L} - \frac{h}{L}\right]$$ ### 3.3 Derivation of Resistance of a Plate Electrode #### Case 1: Circular Plate of Radius r A circular plate buried flush with the surface behaves approximately like a hemispherical electrode of equivalent radius r. The current flows radially outward into the hemispherical soil volume. At radius x: $$R = \int_{r}^{\infty} \frac{\rho \, dx}{2\pi x^2} = \frac{\rho}{2\pi r}$$ But because only half-space is utilised (current only goes downward), the effective area is doubled. The standard formula (IS 3043) is: $$\boxed{R = \frac{\rho}{4 r}}$$ #### Case 2: Rectangular Plate of Dimensions A × B Using the empirical Tagg-Sunde formula: $$\boxed{R = \frac{\rho}{1.57 \, \sqrt{A}} \quad \text{for square plate of side } A}$$ For a general rectangle of area $A$ with perimeter $P$, the resistance is approximated by: $$R \approx \frac{\rho}{4}\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{A}} + \frac{\rho}{P}$$ For engineering design (where $A \gg$ thickness), the simpler form $R = \rho / (1.57\sqrt{A})$ is the standard KTU textbook formula. ### 3.4 Multiple Electrodes in Parallel — Utilization Factor When a single electrode cannot achieve the target resistance, multiple electrodes are connected in parallel. The combined resistance is: $$R_{\text{total}} = \frac{R_{\text{single}}}{N \cdot \eta}$$ where: - **N** = number of electrodes - **η** (eta) = **utilization factor**, accounting for the mutual interference of nearby electrodes. η < 1. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why η < 1?** When two electrodes are placed close together, their "effective resistance areas" overlap. Each electrode tries to push current into soil regions that the other electrode is already occupying. The result is that the combined resistance is **higher** than $R_{\text{single}}/N$. Typical values of η from IS 3043 for rod electrodes: - Spacing = electrode length: η ≈ 0.50 to 0.60 - Spacing = 2 × electrode length: η ≈ 0.65 to 0.75 - Spacing = 3 × electrode length: η ≈ 0.75 to 0.85 - Spacing ≥ 5 × electrode length: η → 1.0 ### 3.5 Worked Analytical Examples #### Example 1 — Wenner Method Calculation Given: probe spacing $a = 5$ m, measured resistance $R = 12\ \Omega$. Compute apparent soil resistivity: $$\rho = 2\pi a R = 2 \pi \times 5 \times 12 = 376.99\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m} \approx 377\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m}$$ #### Example 2 — Driven Rod Resistance Given: $\rho = 200\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m}$, $L = 3$ m, $d = 0.05$ m. $$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right] = \frac{200}{2\pi \times 3}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{8 \times 3}{0.05}\right) - 1\right]$$ $$\frac{200}{18.85} = 10.61, \quad \ln(480) = 6.17$$ $$R = 10.61 \times (6.17 - 1) = 10.61 \times 5.17 = 54.85\ \Omega$$ #### Example 3 — Number of Rods Required Required resistance $R_T = 5\ \Omega$, soil $\rho = 200\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m}$. Using Example 2, $R_{\text{rod}} = 54.85\ \Omega$, and assume spacing = 2L (so η ≈ 0.70). $$N = \frac{R_{\text{rod}}}{R_T \cdot \eta} = \frac{54.85}{5 \times 0.70} = \frac{54.85}{3.5} = 15.67$$ Therefore, **16 rods** must be installed in parallel. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Wenner's Four-Pin Method Test Setup ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Surface["Ground Surface (Horizontal Line)"] C1["Pin C1
Current +
OUTER"] P1["Pin P1
Potential +

Potential -
INNER"] C2["Pin C2
Current -
OUTER"] end C1 ---|"spacing a"| P1 P1 ---|"spacing a"| P2 P2 ---|"spacing a"| C2 Tester["Earth Tester
Megger or
Digital RTD"]:::dev C1 -.thick lead.-> Tester C2 -.thick lead.-> Tester P1 -.thin lead.-> Tester P2 -.thin lead.-> Tester Tester ==>|"Read R = V/I"| Result["Apparent Resistivity
rho = 2 pi a R
ohm-metre"] classDef dev fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b30000,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Earthing System Components ```mermaid graph TD A["Electrical Equipment
or System Neutral"]:::src B["Earth Conductor

Rod or Plate or Pipe"]:::elec D["Soil Mass

Backfill if Resistance

Sub-Strat
