KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PCEET602]** > > **Q1.** Define **soil resistivity** and state **any three** factors that affect it. > > **Model Answer:** > **Soil resistivity (ρ)** is defined as the electrical resistance offered by a unit cube of soil measured between two opposite faces, expressed in **Ω·m** or **Ω·cm**. > > The three main factors affecting soil resistivity are: > 1. **Moisture content** — Higher moisture drastically reduces resistivity. Below 15% moisture, resistivity rises sharply. > 2. **Temperature** — Lower temperature increases resistivity. Below 0 °C (frozen soil), resistivity becomes very high. > 3. **Salt / dissolved electrolyte content** — Adding salts (NaCl, bentonite) reduces resistivity by increasing ionic conductivity. > *(Other acceptable factors: soil composition, depth, compaction, seasonal variation.)* **[3 Marks]** --- > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PCEET602]** > > **Q2.** State **Wenner's formula** for measuring soil resistivity and explain the meaning of each term. > > **Model Answer:** > The Wenner four-pin formula is: > $$\rho = 2\pi a R$$ > where: > - **ρ** = apparent soil resistivity, in **Ω·m** > - **a** = spacing between adjacent electrodes (pins), in **metres** > - **R** = measured resistance (V/I) between the potential electrodes, in **Ω** > - The constant **2π** has units of metres/Ω and converts geometry into resistivity. > > The formula assumes a **homogeneous** soil; for layered soil it gives the **apparent** resistivity at depth ≈ a. **[3 Marks]** --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PCEET602]** > > ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) > > **(a)** Derive the expression for the resistance of a **driven vertical rod electrode** of length L and diameter d buried in soil of resistivity ρ. State all assumptions. **[7 Marks]** > > **(b)** In a substation earthing design, the soil resistivity measured by Wenner's method is **250 Ω·m**. The target earth resistance is **1 Ω**. A vertical rod electrode of diameter **40 mm** is proposed. Determine: > 1. The required length of a single rod to achieve 1 Ω. > 2. If the maximum rod length available is 3 m, how many such rods are needed in parallel (assume utilisation factor η = 0.75)? **[7 Marks]** --- ### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A #### Part (a) — Derivation **Assumptions:** 1. Soil is homogeneous, isotropic, and extends to infinity. 2. The rod is a perfect conductor (negligible metallic resistance). 3. The rod is vertical, cylindrical, and driven from the surface. 4. Current leaks uniformly along the length of the rod. **Derivation:** Step 1 — Consider a thin disc element at depth x of thickness dx along the rod. The leakage area at distance x is approximately $2\pi x^2$ (hemispherical spreading). Step 2 — The differential resistance of this disc to remote earth is: $$dR = \frac{\rho \, dx}{2\pi x^2}$$ Step 3 — Integrate from the rod surface ($x = d/2$) to its tip ($x = L$): $$R = \int_{d/2}^{L} \frac{\rho \, dx}{2\pi x^2} = \frac{\rho}{2\pi}\left[\frac{1}{d/2} - \frac{1}{L}\right]$$ Step 4 — Apply the **empirical correction** (Dwight's formula) for the rod tip end-effect: $$\boxed{R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right]}$$ This is valid when $L \gg d$. **[7 Marks]** #### Part (b) — Numerical Computation **Given:** $\rho = 250\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m}$, $R_T = 1\ \Omega$, $d = 40\ \text{mm} = 0.04\ \text{m}$, $\eta = 0.75$, $L_{\max} = 3\ \text{m}$. ##### Sub-part (b.1) — Single Rod Length $$1 = \frac{250}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{8L}{0.04}\right) - 1\right]$$ $$2\pi L = 250\left[\ln(200 L) - 1\right]$$ This is a transcendental equation solved iteratively. Let $f(L) = 250[\ln(200L) - 1] - 2\pi L$. **Trial 1:** $L = 50$ m → $250[\ln(10000) - 1] - 100\pi = 250[9.21 - 1] - 314.16 = 2052.5 - 314.16 = 1738 > 0$ (too high). **Trial 2:** $L = 30$ m → $250[\ln(6000) - 1] - 60\pi = 250[8.70 - 1] - 188.5 = 1925 - 188.5 = 1736 > 0$. **Trial 3:** $L = 1$ m → $250[\ln(200) - 1] - 2\pi = 250[5.30 - 1] - 6.28 = 1075 - 6.28 = 1069 > 0$. **Trial 4:** $L = 0.2$ m → $250[\ln(40) - 1] - 0.4\pi = 250[3.69 - 1] - 1.26 = 672.5 - 1.26 = 671 > 0$. The equation $R = 1\ \Omega$ with $\rho = 250$ cannot be achieved with a reasonable single rod — the length required is impractically large (>100 m). Therefore, the design must use **multiple parallel rods**. ##### Sub-part (b.2) — Number of Rods of Length 3 m Resistance of one 3 m rod: $$R_{\text{rod}} = \frac{250}{2\pi \times 3}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{8 \times 3}{0.04}\right) - 1\right] = \frac{250}{18.85}\left[\ln(600) - 1\right]$$ $$R_{\text{rod}} = 13.26 \times [6.397 - 1] = 13.26 \times 5.397 = 71.57\ \Omega$$ Number of rods required: $$N = \frac{R_{\text{rod}}}{R_T \cdot \eta} = \frac{71.57}{1 \times 0.75} = 95.43$$ $$\boxed{N \approx 96 \text{ rods in parallel}}$$ **Engineering Note:** Such a high count indicates the soil is very resistive. The practical solution is to use **chemical earthing** with bentonite/marconite backfill (which can reduce ρ by a factor of 5 to 10), and/or drive rods to greater depth where soil is moister. **[7 Marks]** --- > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PCEET602]** > > ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice > > **(a)** Explain the **Wenner's four-pin method** for measuring soil resistivity with a neat sketch. Derive the formula $\rho = 2\pi a R$. **[7 Marks]** > > **(b)** In a substation site, the following Wenner test data were obtained at probe spacings of 5 m and 10 m respectively: **R = 18 Ω** and **R = 9 Ω**. > 1. Calculate the apparent resistivity at each spacing. > 2. Comment on whether the soil is **homogeneous** or **layered**. > 3. Design a suitable **earthing arrangement** if the target resistance is **2 Ω** and rod electrodes of 3 m length, 50 mm diameter are available. **[7 Marks]** --- ### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B #### Part (a) — Wenner Method Explanation and Derivation **Description:** *(Sketch — refer to diagram in Section 4.1.)* Four electrodes are driven into the ground in a straight line at equal spacing 'a'. The two outer electrodes C₁ and C₂ carry the test current I. The two inner electrodes P₁ and P₂ measure the potential V. An earth tester (Megger or digital RTD) supplies I and reads R = V/I directly. **Derivation of $\rho = 2\pi a R$:** **Step 1:** Potential due to C₁ at distance r in homogeneous soil (hemispherical current spread): $$V_1(r) = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi r}$$ **Step 2:** By superposition, potential at P₁ (distance a from C₁ and 2a from C₂): $$V_{P_1} = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi a} - \frac{\rho I}{2\pi (2a)} = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi a}\left(1 - \frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{\rho I}{4\pi a}$$ **Step 3:** Potential at P₂ (distance 2a from C₁ and a from C₂): $$V_{P_2} = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi (2a)} - \frac{\rho I}{2\pi a} = -\frac{\rho I}{4\pi a}$$ **Step 4:** Potential difference: $$V = V_{P_1} - V_{P_2} = \frac{\rho I}{4\pi a} - \left(-\frac{\rho I}{4\pi a}\right) = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi a}$$ **Step 5:** Resistance and resistivity: $$R = \frac{V}{I} = \frac{\rho}{2\pi a} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\rho = 2\pi a R}$$ **[7 Marks]** #### Part (b) — Numerical and Design Problem ##### Sub-part (b.1) — Apparent Resistivity At $a_1 = 5$ m, $R_1 = 18\ \Omega$: $$\rho_1 = 2\pi \times 5 \times 18 = 565.5\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m}$$ At $a_2 = 10$ m, $R_2 = 9\ \Omega$: $$\rho_2 = 2\pi \times 10 \times 9 = 565.5\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m}$$ ##### Sub-part (b.2) — Homogeneity Check Since $\rho_1 = \rho_2 = 565.5\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m}$ at both spacings, the apparent resistivity is independent of probe spacing. This is the signature of a **homogeneous (single-layer) soil** of resistivity ≈ **566 Ω·m**. (If ρ changed with spacing, the soil would be layered and a curve-fitting / Sunde's method would be required.) ##### Sub-part (b.3) — Earthing Arrangement Design Given: $\rho = 566\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m}$, $R_T = 2\ \Omega$, $L = 3$ m, $d = 50$ mm = 0.05 m. Single-rod resistance: $$R_{\text{rod}} = \frac{566}{2\pi \times 3}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{8 \times 3}{0.05}\right) - 1\right] = \frac{566}{18.85}[\ln(480) - 1]$$ $$R_{\text{rod}} = 30.03 \times [6.173 - 1] = 30.03 \times 5.173 = 155.3\ \Omega$$ Assuming spacing = 2L = 6 m, η ≈ 0.70: $$N = \frac{R_{\text{rod}}}{R_T \cdot \eta} = \frac{155.3}{2 \times 0.70} = \frac{155.3}{1.4} = 110.9 \approx 111 \text{ rods}$$ **Recommended Arrangement:** - Lay out the rods in a **grid pattern** with 6 m × 6 m spacing (driven vertically). - Use **4 groups of 28 rods each**, interconnected by **50 mm × 6 mm GI strip** at 0.5 m depth. - Add **chemical backfill (bentonite + gypsum + salt)** in each pit to reduce effective ρ by factor 3 to 5. - After backfill, ρ reduces to ≈ 100–150 Ω·m, and required N drops to ≈ 20–25 rods. **[7 Marks]** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Unit Inconsistency**: Mixing up 'a' in cm vs metres. If probe spacing a is in centimetres, ρ comes out in Ω·cm; KTU expects **Ω·m**. Always convert before substituting. > 2. **Wrong Constant**: Writing $\rho = 4\pi a R$ or $\rho = \pi a R$ instead of $2\pi a R$. Memorise the constant. > 3. **Ignoring Utilisation Factor**: Students compute $N = R_{\text{rod}}/R_T$ directly. KTU expects $N = R_{\text{rod}}/(R_T \cdot \eta)$. **Marks are deducted for η omission.** > 4. **Rod Length vs Depth Confusion**: When rod is buried at depth h below surface, the simple formula $R = (\rho/2\pi L)[\ln(8L/d) - 1]$ does **not** apply. Use the depth-modified formula. > 5. **Forgetting End-Effect**: Some texts give a simpler $R = \rho/(2\pi L)$ form which underestimates resistance. KTU expects the full formula with $\ln(8L/d) - 1$. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam** > > ✅ **Soil Resistivity ρ** is in **Ω·m**. Typical range: **10 Ω·m** (wet clay) to **10,000+ Ω·m** (dry rock). > > ✅ **Wenner's Formula**: $\rho = 2\pi a R$ — the **single most important formula** for this topic. > > ✅ **Rod Resistance**: $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$ — valid for $L \gg d$, surface-driven. > > ✅ **Plate Resistance (Circular)**: $R = \dfrac{\rho}{4r}$. **Plate Resistance (Square)**: $R = \dfrac{\rho}{1.57\sqrt{A}}$. > > ✅ **Multiple Rods**: $R_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{R_{\text{single}}}{N \cdot \eta}$ where η < 1. > > ✅ **Target Earth Resistance (IS 3043)**: Power Station **0.5 Ω**, EHT **1 Ω**, HT **2 Ω**, LT **5 Ω**. > > ✅ **Factors Increasing ρ (worsening)**: Dry soil, low temperature (<0 °C), sandy/rocky soil, low salt content, frozen subsoil. > > ✅ **Factors Decreasing ρ (improving)**: Moisture, salt/chemical backfill (bentonite), higher temperature, deeper soil (below frost line), multiple electrodes in parallel. > > ✅ **Layered Soil**: Identified when Wenner's ρ varies with probe spacing 'a'. Use Sunde's / curve-fitting method. > > ✅ **Probe Spacing Rule of Thumb**: Spacing 'a' (in metres) ≈ depth being probed. To investigate up to 10 m depth, use a = 10 m. > > ✅ **Wenner Assumption**: Soil is homogeneous and isotropic; current flows as hemispherical waves from each pin. > > ✅ **Always quote final answers with appropriate units** — KTU examiners are strict on units.

topics.title: Earth Electrodes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Earth Electrodes # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) An **Earth Electrode** is defined under **IS 3043:2018** (Code of Practice for Earthing) as a conductor or a group of conductors in intimate contact with the earth, providing an electrical connection to the general mass of the earth. In the context of **PCEET602 – Electrical System Design and Estimation**, an earth electrode is the physical hardware component that dissipates fault current or leakage current into the surrounding soil mass, thereby stabilizing the system's reference potential to **0 V (earth potential)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Memorize this exact phrasing):** > *"An earth electrode is a metal plate, pipe, rod, or conductor embedded in the soil and connected to the non-current carrying metallic parts of an electrical installation, the purpose of which is to maintain the potential of these parts as close to the earth's general potential as possible."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Drainage Pipe" Mental Model Imagine a building's plumbing system. When water overflows or a pipe leaks, the **drainage pipe** carries the excess water safely into the ground (sewage system) so that it doesn't accumulate and damage the structure. An earth electrode works on the **exact same principle**, but instead of water, it drains **stray electrical currents (fault/leakage currents)**: - **Building/Equipment** → The plumbing system - **Stray Fault Current** → Excess/leaking water - **Earth Electrode + Soil** → The drainage pipe + ground - **Earth Pit** → The inspection chamber - **Soil Resistivity** → The "absorbing capacity" of the ground Without this "drainage," stray current would accumulate, causing shocks, fires, or equipment damage — just as stagnant water would cause structural damage. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Physical Quantities | Quantity | Symbol | Unit | Typical Value (Reference) | |---|---|---|---| | Soil Resistivity | ρ | Ohm-metre (Ω·m) | **10 Ω·m (moist clay)** to **1000 Ω·m (dry sandy soil)** | | Earth Electrode Resistance | R | Ohm (Ω) | Should be **< 1 Ω** (for large substations), **< 5 Ω** (for HT), **< 10 Ω** (for domestic LT) | | Depth of Burial | d | metre (m) | Typically **2.5 m to 4 m** | | Length of Rod | L | metre (m) | **1.2 m / 2 m / 3 m** (standard GI/Copper bonded rod) | > [!NOTE] > **Why two values of resistance?** IS 3043 specifies different maximum permissible earth resistance values depending on the type of installation. For a domestic LT installation, ≤ 5 Ω is acceptable, but for a major power station, the design target is often ≤ 0.5 Ω, achieved by using multiple electrodes in parallel. ## 1.4 Classification of Earth Electrodes (Foundation Concept) Earth electrodes are broadly classified as follows (this is a high-frequency KTU short-answer area): 1. **Natural Earth Electrodes** – Existing metal objects already in contact with earth (e.g., water mains, building steel, well casings, metal sheaths of cables). 2. **Artificial / Made Electrodes** – Specifically installed for the purpose: - **Rod Electrode** (driven into the soil) - **Pipe Electrode** (buried vertically) - **Plate Electrode** (buried vertically or horizontally) - **Strip / Conductor Electrode** (buried in trenches) - **Watering Pipe Electrode** 3. **Special Electrodes** – For high-resistivity soils: - **Chemical Electrode** (salt-treated) - **Marconite / Bentonite-based backfilled electrodes** - **Maintenance-free (gel-filled) electrodes** > [!TIP] > KTU examiners love the phrase "effective earthing" — meaning the combined resistance of the electrode **plus** the soil contact resistance. A perfect copper plate still has high resistance if the surrounding soil is bone-dry! # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Earth Electrode Resistance The resistance of an earth electrode is **not** the resistance of the metal itself. Copper has a resistivity of ~1.7 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m, which is essentially **zero** compared to soil. The resistance comes from the **gradual divergence of current lines** as they fan out from the electrode into the vast mass of soil. **Operational Logic (Step-by-step):** 1. Current leaves the electrode through its surface. 2. At the electrode surface, current density is **maximum** (small cross-section of soil to flow through) → high resistance. 3. As current moves away, it spreads over a **larger hemisphere of soil** → resistance drops sharply. 4. Beyond a distance of approximately the **largest dimension of the electrode**, adding more soil does not significantly reduce the resistance. 5. Therefore, ~90% of the earth resistance is concentrated within a **hemisphere of radius equal to the electrode's largest dimension** surrounding the electrode. > [!IMPORTANT] > This is why **increasing the length** of a rod electrode is more effective than increasing its diameter, and why multiple electrodes must be spaced at least equal to their length to avoid overlapping resistance zones (mutual resistance effect). ## 2.2 Standard KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL TABLE FORMATTING RULE:** No vertical pipes `|` are used inside formula cells; instead, the LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` delimiter is used to represent mathematical bars, ensuring the markdown table renders perfectly. | # | Electrode Type | Formula for Resistance (R) | Variables Explained | KTU Standard Reference | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Rod / Pipe Electrode** (driven vertically) | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \left[ \ln\left( \dfrac{4L}{d} \right) - 1 \right]$ | $\rho$ = soil resistivity (Ω·m), $L$ = length of rod (m), $d$ = diameter of rod (m) | IS 3043 Cl. 9.3.2 | | 2 | **Plate Electrode** (buried vertically) | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2 A} \left[ \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{A}} \cdot \ln\left( \dfrac{2\sqrt{A}}{0.10} \right) \right]$ ...simplified: $R = \dfrac{\rho}{4r}$ where $r = \sqrt{A/\pi}$ | $A$ = area of plate (m²), $r$ = equivalent radius | IS 3043 Cl. 9.3.1 | | 3 | **Plate Electrode** (simplified empirical) | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{4} \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{A}}$ | $A$ = area of plate in m² | IS 3043 Cl. 9.3.1 (alternative form) | | 4 | **Horizontal Strip Electrode** (length $L$, depth $h$) | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \left[ \ln\left( \dfrac{2L}{h} \right) + \ln\left( \dfrac{L}{d} \right) \right]$ | $L$ = length, $h$ = depth of burial, $d$ = strip width/2 | IS 3043 Cl. 9.3.3 | | 5 | **Multiple Rods in Parallel** (n rods, spacing $s$) | $R_n = \dfrac{R_1}{n} \cdot \eta$ | $R_1$ = single rod resistance, $\eta$ = utilization factor (0.55 to 0.9) | IS 3043 Cl. 9.4 | | 6 | **Two Electrodes in Series** | $R = R_1 + R_2$ (if far apart) | Negligible mutual resistance | Conceptual | | 7 | **Soil Resistivity (Wenner 4-probe)** | $\rho = 2 \pi a \cdot R$ | $a$ = probe spacing (m), $R$ = measured resistance (Ω) | IS 3043 Cl. 7.2.3 | | 8 | **Current Density criterion (Step/Touch voltage)** | $E = \rho \cdot J \le E_{\text{permissible}}$ | $J$ = current density, $E_{\text{permissible}}$ based on body weight, time, footwear | IEEE 80 | > [!NOTE] > The **utilization factor (η)** in parallel rod calculations is always **less than 1** because of mutual interference between the resistance zones of adjacent electrodes. A common student mistake is to use η = 1 (which would falsely suggest perfect paralleling). ## 2.3 Influence Factors on Earth Electrode Resistance 1. **Soil Resistivity (ρ)** – Directly proportional; the dominant factor. 2. **Moisture Content** – Wet soil = low resistance. A 10% increase in moisture can halve ρ. 3. **Soil Temperature** – Below 0°C, resistivity rises sharply (ice is a poor conductor). 4. **Depth of Burial** – Deeper electrodes reach moist, stable soil layers. 5. **Salt Content / Chemical Treatment** – Adding charcoal + salt (NaCl) reduces ρ dramatically (e.g., from 100 Ω·m to ~10 Ω·m), but requires periodic recharging. 6. **Electrode Material & Surface Coating** – Copper-bonded rods (G.I. core + copper coating) are the most cost-effective for most soils. 7. **Multiple Electrode Spacing** – Spacing ≥ rod length avoids mutual resistance overlap. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Earth electrodes are not an academic concept — they are a **legal safety mandate** under: - **IE Rules 1956 (India)** – Rule 61 mandates earthing. - **National Electrical Code (NEC) 2011 (SP 30)** – Section 9. - **CEA (Central Electricity Authority) Safety Regulations 2010**. - **IEEE Std 80** – For substations (Step & Touch voltage design). - **IS 3043:2018** – Indian Standard code of practice (the most referenced document in KTU exams). - **IEC 62561** – Lightning Protection Component Earthing. In practical projects (KTU Estimation context), the **size and quantity of earth electrodes** directly drive the **BOQ (Bill of Quantities)** — so designers must justify the chosen configuration by calculation. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Resistance of a Pipe/Rod Electrode (Vertical, Driven into Soil) This is the **most-tested derivation** in KTU Board examinations. **Starting Setup:** Consider a vertical rod of length $L$ metres and diameter $d$ metres, driven into soil of uniform resistivity $\rho$ Ω·m. Current $I$ amperes flows radially outward from the rod into a hemisphere of soil. **Step 1 — Differential Resistance Element:** Consider a thin cylindrical shell of soil at radius $x$ from the rod axis, of thickness $dx$. The current flows **radially outward (perpendicular to the shell)** through the hemispherical cross-section of area $2 \pi x^2$. $$ dR = \dfrac{\rho \, dx}{2 \pi x^2} $$ **Step 2 — Explanation of the area term:** Since the current radiates from a vertical rod (one half of a full 3D sphere is in the soil, the other half is in air), the effective cross-section is a hemisphere of radius $x$: $A_{\text{hemisphere}} = 2 \pi x^2$. **Step 3 — Integration from rod radius to far end:** The total resistance is obtained by integrating $dR$ from $x = d/2$ (rod surface) to $x = L$ (where the current effectively "ends" — beyond this distance, resistance contribution becomes negligible). Beyond $x = L$, the cross-section grows so large that additional soil offers near-zero resistance. $$ R = \int_{d/2}^{L} \dfrac{\rho \, dx}{2 \pi x^2} $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the integral:** $$ R = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi} \int_{d/2}^{L} x^{-2} \, dx $$ **Step 5 — Apply power rule for integration:** $$ R = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi} \left[ \dfrac{x^{-1}}{-1} \right]_{d/2}^{L} = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi} \left[ -\dfrac{1}{x} \right]_{d/2}^{L} $$ **Step 6 — Substitute limits:** $$ R = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi} \left[ -\dfrac{1}{L} - \left( -\dfrac{2}{d} \right) \right] = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi} \left[ \dfrac{2}{d} - \dfrac{1}{L} \right] $$ **Step 7 — Apply the engineering approximation:** Since the length $L$ of a practical rod is much greater than its diameter $d$, the term $\dfrac{1}{L}$ is **much smaller** than $\dfrac{2}{d}$ (e.g., for $L = 2$ m, $d = 0.02$ m: $1/L = 0.5$ vs $2/d = 100$ — a 200× difference). Thus, $\dfrac{1}{L}$ is dropped as a simplifying engineering approximation. $$ R \approx \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{2}{d} = \dfrac{\rho}{\pi d} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Valuation Mistake:** Writing $R = \dfrac{\rho}{\pi d}$ is the **oversimplified form** and is technically wrong for finite-length rods. The **correct, accepted IS 3043 formula** is the logarithmic form below, which accounts for the finite rod length: **Step 8 — Refined logarithmic form (the actual KTU-accepted formula):** When full rigor is applied (considering the rod as a hemispherical source and using the method of images), the result is the classic expression: $$ \boxed{R = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \left[ \ln\left( \dfrac{4L}{d} \right) - 1 \right]} $$ This is the formula examiners expect. The "$-1$" arises from the natural log simplification of the hyperbolic function, and the $\dfrac{4L}{d}$ term includes the assumption that current effectively reaches a hemisphere of radius $L$. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style) **Problem Statement:** A substation requires an earth electrode of resistance ≤ 1 Ω. The available soil has resistivity 80 Ω·m. Determine the required length of a 50 mm diameter GI pipe electrode. If two such pipes are connected in parallel, what would be the resulting resistance? (Take utilization factor = 0.8) **Given Data:** - $\rho = 80 \, \Omega\cdot m$ - Target $R \le 1 \, \Omega$ - $d = 50 \, \text{mm} = 0.05 \, \text{m}$ - Number of electrodes $n = 2$, $\eta = 0.8$ **Step 1 — Solve for L using the rod formula:** $$ R = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \left[ \ln\left( \dfrac{4L}{d} \right) - 1 \right] = 1 $$ $$ \dfrac{80}{2 \pi L} \left[ \ln\left( \dfrac{4L}{0.05} \right) - 1 \right] = 1 $$ $$ \dfrac{12.73}{L} \left[ \ln(80L) - 1 \right] = 1 $$ **Step 2 — Iterative substitution (since the equation is transcendental):** Try $L = 3.0 \, \text{m}$: $$ \dfrac{12.73}{3.0} \left[ \ln(80 \times 3.0) - 1 \right] = 4.244 \times \left[ \ln(240) - 1 \right] $$ $$ = 4.244 \times \left[ 5.481 - 1 \right] = 4.244 \times 4.481 = 19.02 \, \Omega \quad (\text{too high}) $$ Try $L = 6.0 \, \text{m}$: $$ \dfrac{12.73}{6.0} \left[ \ln(80 \times 6.0) - 1 \right] = 2.122 \times \left[ \ln(480) - 1 \right] $$ $$ = 2.122 \times \left[ 6.174 - 1 \right] = 2.122 \times 5.174 = 10.98 \, \Omega \quad (\text{still too high}) $$ Try $L = 30.0 \, \text{m}$: $$ \dfrac{12.73}{30.0} \left[ \ln(80 \times 30) - 1 \right] = 0.424 \times \left[ \ln(2400) - 1 \right] $$ $$ = 0.424 \times \left[ 7.783 - 1 \right] = 0.424 \times 6.783 = 2.88 \, \Omega \quad (\text{still high}) $$ Try $L = 60.0 \, \text{m}$: $$ \dfrac{12.73}{60.0} \left[ \ln(80 \times 60) - 1 \right] = 0.212 \times \left[ \ln(4800) - 1 \right] $$ $$ = 0.212 \times \left[ 8.477 - 1 \right] = 0.212 \times 7.477 = 1.585 \, \Omega $$ Try $L = 90.0 \, \text{m}$: $$ \dfrac{12.73}{90.0} \left[ \ln(80 \times 90) - 1 \right] = 0.141 \times \left[ \ln(7200) - 1 \right] $$ $$ = 0.141 \times \left[ 8.882 - 1 \right] = 0.141 \times 7.882 = 1.111 \, \Omega $$ Try $L = 100 \, \text{m}$: $$ \dfrac{12.73}{100} \times \left[ \ln(8000) - 1 \right] = 0.1273 \times \left[ 8.987 - 1 \right] $$ $$ = 0.1273 \times 7.987 = 1.017 \, \Omega \quad (\text{approximately target}) $$ **Step 3 — Conclusion for single rod:** $\boxed{L \approx 100 \, \text{m of a single 50 mm pipe is impractical.}}$ This shows why **single rod electrodes are inadequate** for high-current substations. **Step 4 — Two rods in parallel with utilization factor:** $$ R_1 (\text{single rod of, say, 5 m}) = \dfrac{80}{2 \pi (5)} \left[ \ln\left( \dfrac{4 \times 5}{0.05} \right) - 1 \right] $$ $$ = 2.546 \times \left[ \ln(400) - 1 \right] = 2.546 \times \left[ 5.991 - 1 \right] = 2.546 \times 4.991 = 12.71 \, \Omega $$ $$ R_n = \dfrac{R_1}{n} \cdot \eta = \dfrac{12.71}{2} \times 0.8 = 5.08 \, \Omega $$ **Step 5 — Engineering insight:** Even with 2 rods, the resistance is high — you'd need **~25 rods in parallel** or **a plate electrode** of much larger area, OR the soil must be treated with salt/charcoal to drop ρ to ~10 Ω·m, which would make $R_1$ drop to ~1.6 Ω. This is why **soil treatment is standard practice** in KTU estimation problems. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Electrode to Choose? | Condition / Constraint | Best Choice | Reasoning | |---|---|---| | Loose, rocky soil (rod cannot be driven) | **Plate electrode** | Rods need driving; plates can be placed in a pit. | | Deep, soft, moist alluvial soil | **Pipe / Rod electrode** | Easy to drive; reaches deep moist layers. | | Urban site with restricted area | **Maintenance-free chemical electrode** | Compact; no need for watering. | | High-resistivity desert / rocky soil | **Multiple rods + soil treatment (salt/charcoal)** | Combined approach; one rod insufficient. | | Large substation (fault current 50+ kA) | **Grid of strip electrodes (Earthing Mat)** | Distributes current; controls step/touch voltage. | | Cost-sensitive domestic LT installation | **GI pipe electrode with charcoal + salt** | Lowest cost; acceptable ≤ 5 Ω. | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Earth Electrode Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Electrical Equipment / Panel] -->|Fault Current Path| B[Earth Conductor GI/Cu Strip] B --> C{Test Link Inspection Chamber} C --> D[Earth Electrode Hardware] D --> D1[Rod Type] D --> D2[Pipe Type] D --> D3[Plate Type] D --> D4[Strip / Mat Type] D1 --> E[Soil Mass General Earth] D2 --> E D3 --> E D4 --> E E -->|Return Path| F[Source Neutral / Star Point] F --> G[Step and Touch Voltage Safety Zone] G --> H[Protection Relay Trips Fault] F --> I[IS 3043 Compliant Resistance less than 5 Ohm] I --> J[Equipment is Safely Earthed] C --> K[Periodic Maintenance Watering Salt Recharge] K --> D ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Resistance Layer Distribution Around a Rod Electrode ```mermaid flowchart LR M1[Core Resistance R1
Negligible
Metal of Rod] --> M2[Contact Resistance R2
Metal to Soil Interface] M2 --> M3[Soil Resistance R3
Dominant Component
90 percent of total] M3 --> M4[Far Field Resistance R4
Negligible Contribution] M4 --> M5[Total Earth Electrode Resistance R equals R1 plus R2 plus R3 plus R4] style M3 fill:#fbb,stroke:#900 style M5 fill:#bfb,stroke:#090 ``` ## 4.3 Functional Layout: Standard Pipe Electrode Earth Pit (IS 3043 Cross-Section) ```mermaid flowchart TB P1[Surface Ground Level 0 m] --> P2[Top Funnel with Mesh Cover 0 to 0.3 m] P2 --> P3[Alternate Layers of Charcoal and Salt 0.3 to 2.0 m] P3 --> P4[GI Pipe Electrode 40 to 50 mm dia 2.0 to 2.5 m length] P4 --> P5[Bottom of Pit 2.5 m below GL Moist Soil Zone] P6[GI Strip Earthing Lead 25x6 mm] -.->|Welded / Brazed| P4 P6 --> P7[Test Link at Inspection Chamber] P7 --> P8[Main Equipment / Panel Earth Bus] style P4 fill:#ffd,stroke:#a80 style P3 fill:#fdb,stroke:#a80 style P6 fill:#ddf,stroke:#008 ``` ## 4.4 Process Flow: Wenner 4-Probe Method for Soil Resistivity Measurement ```mermaid flowchart LR W1[Lay out 4 Probes C1 P1 P2 C2 equally spaced at distance a] --> W2[Drive Probes 0.5 to 1 m into Soil] W2 --> W3[Inject Current I between Outer Probes C1 and C2] W3 --> W4[Measure Voltage V between Inner Probes P1 and P2] W4 --> W5[Compute Apparent Resistance R equals V by I] W5 --> W6[Apply Wenner Formula rho equals 2 pi a R] W6 --> W7[Plot Resistivity Profile versus Probe Spacing] W7 --> W8[Identify Deep Layer Resistivity for Final Electrode Design] style W6 fill:#bfb,stroke:#090 style W8 fill:#bbf,stroke:#008 ``` ## 4.5 System Architecture: Multiple Electrode Paralleling (Mutual Resistance) ```mermaid flowchart TD E1[Electrode 1 Resistance R1] -->|Mutual Resistance R12| E2[Electrode 2 Resistance R2] E2 -->|Mutual Resistance R23| E3[Electrode 3 Resistance R3] E3 -->|Mutual Resistance R34| E4[Electrode N Resistance RN] E1 --> HUB[Common Earth Bus] E2 --> HUB E3 --> HUB E4 --> HUB HUB --> OUT[Equivalent Resistance Rn times eta] HUB --> RULE1[Spacing Rule Minimum spacing equals Length of each rod] RULE1 --> RULE2[Typical Spacing 3 m to 6 m for 2.5 m rods] RULE2 --> RULE3[Utilization Factor eta 0.55 close spacing to 0.9 wide spacing] style HUB fill:#fdb,stroke:#a80 style OUT fill:#bfb,stroke:#090 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions (Direct Definition Type) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define an earth electrode. List any two types with one application of each.** **Model Answer:** An **earth electrode** is a metal conductor or a system of conductors in intimate contact with the earth, intended to provide a low-impedance electrical connection between the non-current carrying metallic parts of an electrical installation and the general mass of the earth (IS 3043:2018). **Two Types with Applications:** 1. **Pipe Electrode** – A 2.5 m long, 40–50 mm diameter GI pipe driven vertically into a pit. Used for **domestic and small commercial LT installations** where soil is soft and moisture-retaining (e.g., alluvial/coastal soil). 2. **Plate Electrode** – A 600 mm × 600 mm × 6.3 mm copper plate (or 600 mm × 600 mm × 6 mm GI plate) buried vertically at ≥ 2.5 m depth. Used for **HT substations and large industrial installations** where fault current is high and low resistance (≤ 1 Ω) is mandatory. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** **State the factors affecting the resistance of an earth electrode.** **Model Answer:** The resistance of an earth electrode depends on the following factors: 1. **Soil Resistivity (ρ)** – The dominant factor; resistance is directly proportional to soil resistivity. ρ varies with soil type, moisture, salt content, and temperature. 2. **Length and Diameter of the Electrode** – Resistance is inversely proportional to length; doubling the length nearly halves the resistance, while diameter has a logarithmic effect. 3. **Depth of Burial** – Deeper electrodes access more stable, moist soil layers, reducing resistance. 4. **Moisture and Salt Content of Soil** – Wet, saline soil has lower resistivity; hence, charcoal + salt treatment is standard practice. 5. **Temperature of Soil** – Below 0 °C, ice formation sharply increases resistivity. 6. **Number and Spacing of Multiple Electrodes** – More electrodes in parallel reduce resistance, but with diminishing returns due to mutual resistance (governed by utilization factor η). 7. **Shape and Surface Area of the Electrode** – Larger surface contact with soil lowers contact resistance. --- ## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > In KTU's End Semester Evaluation (ESE), Module 4 carries either a 14-mark full question or a 7-mark sub-part. The format below mirrors the exact 2024 Scheme template (part-a: 7 marks + part-b: 7 marks). --- ### ⭐ CHOICE QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme Pattern]** **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and installation procedure of a **pipe earth electrode** as per IS 3043. (7 Marks) **(b)** A pipe electrode of length 2.5 m and diameter 50 mm is driven into soil having resistivity 100 Ω·m. Calculate the resistance of the electrode. If the same soil is treated with salt and charcoal such that the effective resistivity reduces to 30 Ω·m, what is the new resistance? Comment on the effectiveness of the treatment. (7 Marks) --- **Solution:** **Part (a) – Construction & Installation:** **Materials Required:** - 1 × GI Pipe (Class B, ISI marked) — 50 mm nominal bore, 2.5 m long. - 1 × GI Funnel with wire mesh — for watering. - 1 × GI Strip 25 mm × 6 mm — for the earth lead. - Charcoal (coke breeze) and common salt (NaCl) — for soil treatment. **Installation Procedure (Step-by-step):** 1. **Excavation** — Dig a pit of dimensions 0.6 m × 0.6 m × 2.75 m deep at the designated earth station location. 2. **Electrode Placement** — Drive the GI pipe vertically into the soil at the centre of the pit, with its top 50 mm above the pit bottom for the funnel attachment. 3. **Earthing Lead Connection** — Weld or braze the 25 mm × 6 mm GI earth lead firmly to the top of the pipe above ground level. The joint must be protected with bitumen paint. 4. **Soil Treatment (Alternate Layers)** — Fill the pit with **alternating layers of charcoal and salt** (each layer ~100 mm thick). The bottom layer is charcoal, followed by salt, repeating until the pit is filled up to 150 mm below ground level. 5. **Funnel Attachment** — Fit the GI funnel with mesh over the top of the pipe to allow periodic watering. 6. **Cover Plate & Test Link** — Cover the pit with a 6 mm thick CI/MS cover plate. Install a **test link** in an accessible inspection chamber to permit disconnection of the earth lead for periodic resistance testing. 7. **Watering** — Pour 20–40 litres of water through the funnel at least once a week (in dry seasons) to maintain low soil resistivity. 8. **Commissioning Test** — Measure earth resistance using a digital earth tester (fall-of-potential method). Acceptable value: **≤ 5 Ω** (domestic), **≤ 1 Ω** (substation). --- **Part (b) – Numerical Solution:** **Given:** - $L = 2.5 \, \text{m}$ - $d = 50 \, \text{mm} = 0.05 \, \text{m}$ - $\rho_1 = 100 \, \Omega\cdot m$ (untreated) - $\rho_2 = 30 \, \Omega\cdot m$ (after treatment) **Step 1 — Apply the pipe electrode formula:** $$ R = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \left[ \ln\left( \dfrac{4L}{d} \right) - 1 \right] $$ **Step 2 — Compute the dimensionless term:** $$ \dfrac{4L}{d} = \dfrac{4 \times 2.5}{0.05} = 200 $$ $$ \ln(200) = 5.298 $$ $$ \ln(200) - 1 = 4.298 $$ **Step 3 — Case 1 (Untreated, ρ = 100 Ω·m):** $$ R_1 = \dfrac{100}{2 \pi \times 2.5} \times 4.298 $$ $$ R_1 = \dfrac{100}{15.708} \times 4.298 = 6.366 \times 4.298 = 27.36 \, \Omega $$ **Step 4 — Case 2 (Treated, ρ = 30 Ω·m):** $$ R_2 = \dfrac{30}{15.708} \times 4.298 = 1.910 \times 4.298 = 8.21 \, \Omega $$ **Step 5 — Comment on effectiveness:** | Parameter | Untreated | Treated (Charcoal + Salt) | |---|---|---| | Resistance | 27.36 Ω | 8.21 Ω | | % Reduction | — | **~70% reduction** | | Compliance (≤ 5 Ω) | ❌ Fails | ❌ Marginal (still above 5 Ω) | **Comment:** The salt–charcoal treatment reduces soil resistivity by 70% and the electrode resistance from 27.36 Ω to 8.21 Ω. However, even after treatment, the value exceeds the 5 Ω target for a domestic LT installation. **Conclusion:** For this soil type, the designer should either (i) use a larger plate electrode, (ii) add a second pipe in parallel, or (iii) consider chemical/maintenance-free electrodes for permanent compliance. Treatment alone is **not sufficient** for high-resistivity soils — it only delays the inevitable. --- ### ⭐ ALTERNATIVE CHOICE QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme Pattern]** **(a)** Differentiate between **pipe and plate earth electrodes** with respect to construction, formula, typical resistance obtained, and applications. (7 Marks) **(b)** Describe with a sketch the **plate earth electrode** and explain how soil resistivity is measured using the **Wenner Four-Probe Method**. (7 Marks) --- **Solution:** **Part (a) – Comparison Table:** | Sl. No. | Parameter | Pipe Electrode | Plate Electrode | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Shape** | Vertical cylindrical pipe (GI/CI) | Square/Rectangular plate (Cu/GI) | | 2 | **Typical Size** | 40–50 mm dia × 2.5 m long | 600 mm × 600 mm × 6.3 mm (Cu) or 6 mm (GI) | | 3 | **Installation** | Driven vertically into soil with a sledgehammer | Placed vertically at the bottom of an excavated pit | | 4 | **Resistance Formula** | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \left[ \ln\left( \dfrac{4L}{d} \right) - 1 \right]$ | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{4} \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{A}}$ | | 5 | **Typical Resistance** | 5 – 25 Ω (single pipe) | 1 – 10 Ω (single plate) | | 6 | **Soil Required** | Soft, alluvial, drivable soil | Any soil (no driving required) | | 7 | **Best Application** | Domestic LT, small commercial, soft soils | HT substations, industrial, rocky soils | | 8 | **Maintenance** | Requires periodic watering (high) | Requires periodic watering (moderate) | | 9 | **Cost** | Low (₹500–₹1500 per electrode, 2024 rates) | Higher (₹3000–₹8000 per electrode, 2024 rates) | | 10 | **Space Required** | Less (vertical, point source) | More (excavation for pit) | --- **Part (b) – Plate Electrode & Wenner 4-Probe Method:** **Plate Earth Electrode — Description:** A **plate electrode** is a flat metallic plate — typically **600 mm × 600 mm × 6.3 mm copper** (preferred for corrosive soils) or **600 mm × 600 mm × 6 mm GI** (cost-effective for non-corrosive soils). It is buried **vertically** at the bottom of a pit excavated to a depth of **2.5–3 m below ground level**, with its face perpendicular to the ground surface. The plate is surrounded on all sides by **alternating layers of charcoal and salt (NaCl)** to lower the surrounding soil resistivity. A 25 mm × 6 mm GI (or 25 mm × 2.5 mm copper) earth lead is brazed/welded to the plate and brought up to the inspection chamber, where a test link is provided. **Wenner 4-Probe Method for Soil Resistivity Measurement:** **Principle:** A known current $I$ is passed between two outer probes, and the resulting potential difference $V$ is measured between two inner probes. The apparent resistivity is calculated using the geometry of probe spacing. **Procedure:** 1. Lay out **four probes C1, P1, P2, C2** in a straight line, equally spaced at distance $a$ (typically $a = 2, 5, 10, 15, 20$ m) depending on the depth of interest. 2. Drive the probes **20–50 cm** into the soil. 3. Connect the **outer probes (C1, C2)** to the current terminals of a **soil resistivity meter (Earth Tester)**. 4. Connect the **inner probes (P1, P2)** to the potential terminals. 5. Inject a known current $I$ and measure the resulting voltage $V$ between P1 and P2. 6. Compute the apparent resistance: $R_a = V / I$. 7. Apply the **Wenner formula**: $\rho = 2 \pi a \cdot R_a$. 8. Repeat the test for various values of $a$ (2 m, 5 m, 10 m, 20 m) to obtain the **resistivity profile** vs. depth. 9. The resistivity at the deepest spacing corresponds to the layer where the actual earth electrode will be installed. **Standard Formula:** $$ \boxed{\rho = 2 \pi a \cdot R_a = \dfrac{2 \pi a \cdot V}{I}} $$ This is a **7-mark descriptive answer** in KTU valuation; the sketch should be a clear labelled diagram showing the 4 probes, meter connections, and the formula. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the "−1" inside the log bracket** of the rod formula — this is the #1 valuation complaint, costing 1–2 marks per question. > 2. **Using η = 1 for parallel electrodes** — physically wrong and triggers deduction. > 3. **Mixing up units** (cm vs. m) in the diameter $d$ term of the log formula. > 4. **Skipping the sketch** in 7-mark descriptive questions — IS 3043 diagrams are mandatory. > 5. **Not mentioning IS 3043** clause numbers — examiners expect "as per IS 3043 Cl. 9.x.x" for full marks. > 6. **Confusing step voltage and touch voltage** in mat electrode design — both are required to be defined separately. > 7. **Writing the formula for plate electrode with $A$ as "area of one face"** vs. the correct "area of both faces" in some IS interpretations — always quote the standard. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Print this section and stick it on your study wall before the KTU exam.** - [ ] **Definition:** Earth electrode = conductor in intimate contact with earth for safety, as per IS 3043:2018. - [ ] **Key Resistance Values (KTU Favourite):** ≤ 5 Ω (LT domestic), ≤ 1 Ω (HT substation), ≤ 0.5 Ω (major power station). - [ ] **Types:** Natural (water mains, structural steel) + Artificial (rod, pipe, plate, strip, chemical, maintenance-free). - [ ] **Master Formula — Rod/Pipe:** $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \left[ \ln\left( \dfrac{4L}{d} \right) - 1 \right]$ — **never forget the −1.** - [ ] **Master Formula — Plate:** $R = \dfrac{\rho}{4} \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{A}}$. - [ ] **Wenner 4-Probe Formula:** $\rho = 2 \pi a \cdot R_a = \dfrac{2 \pi a \cdot V}{I}$. - [ ] **Parallel Electrodes:** Use **utilization factor η < 1**; spacing ≥ rod length; typical η = 0.55–0.9. - [ ] **Soil Treatment:** Charcoal + salt reduces ρ by 50–80% but is **temporary** (6–12 months) and requires **re-watering**. - [ ] **IS 3043 Reference:** Cl. 9.3.1 (Plate), 9.3.2 (Pipe/Rod), 9.3.3 (Strip), 9.4 (Multiple Electrodes), 7.2.3 (Wenner). - [ ] **Depth of Burial:** Minimum 2.5 m below GL; deeper → better (reaches moist layer). - [ ] **Hemisphere Concept:** ~90% of resistance is within a hemisphere of radius = largest electrode dimension. - [ ] **Cost Note (2024 rates):** GI Pipe ₹800–₹1500; Cu Plate ₹5000–₹8000; Maintenance-free ₹6000–₹12000. - [ ] **Common Material:** Copper-bonded GI rods (≥ 250 micron Cu coating) are the modern KTU-standard choice. - [ ] **Step vs. Touch Voltage (IEEE 80):** Both must be checked for mat/substation design; formula: $E_{\text{step}} = (1000 + 6 \rho_s) \times 0.116 / \sqrt{t}$ (for 70 kg body). - [ ] **Last-Month KTU Mantra:** Practice **3 numerical problems** on rod formula + **1 problem** on parallel electrodes + **1 sketch** of pipe electrode pit + **1 definition** of Wenner method.

topics.title: Methods of Earthing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Methods of Earthing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Earthing (Grounding)** is the deliberate electrical connection of a non-current-carrying metal part of an electrical installation (e.g., equipment body, neutral point, structural frame) to the general mass of earth through a low-impedance electrode buried in soil, such that any fault current or leakage current is safely discharged into the earth without causing injury, fire, or equipment damage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (per IS 3043:1987 & IEEE 80):**
> "Earthing is the process of making a physical connection between any conductor and the earth by means of an earth electrode, so that the earth electrode remains at earth potential (0 V reference) under all operating and fault conditions."

### 1.2 Classification Snapshot

The **Methods of Earthing** are broadly classified by the **geometry and material of the electrode** used to make contact with the soil. Under the KTU 2024 syllabus for PCEET602, the following five methods are prescribed:

1. **Plate Earthing**
2. **Pipe Earthing**
3. **Rod / Driven Pipe Earthing**
4. **Strip / Conductor Earthing**
5. **Water Main Earthing** (limited application)

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Lightning Rod of Your Washing Machine"**
>
> Imagine a tall glass of water (your charged appliance body) sitting on a wooden table. If you accidentally touch the glass with a wet hand, you get a shock because the water has nowhere to "drain." Now, suppose we connect a copper wire from the glass to a damp sponge buried in the garden. The moment any leakage current appears, it instantly escapes into the soil through the wire–sponge system. The sponge (earth electrode) provides a vast surface area of contact with the earth, and its dampness (moisture + salt) lowers the "drainage resistance."
>
> In this analogy: the **glass = equipment body**, the **wire = earth continuity conductor**, the **sponge = earth electrode**, and the **garden soil = general mass of earth**. Methods of earthing differ only in **what shape and material we use for the "sponge."**

### 1.4 Purpose & Need for Earthing (KTU 2024 High-Yield)

| Sl. No. | Purpose | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Safety of human life** | Limits touch voltage to ≤ **50 V** (IEEE 80) under fault |
| 2 | **Protection of equipment** | Operates fuses/MCBs by providing a low-impedance fault path |
| 3 | **Voltage stabilization** | Provides a stable 0 V reference for the system neutral |
| 4 | **Lightning protection** | Dissipates surge currents of up to **200 kA** safely |
| 5 | **Noise reduction** | Reduces electromagnetic interference in electronic circuits |
| 6 | **Fire prevention** | Prevents arcing faults from igniting insulation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Statutory Basis (KTU Reference):** As per the **Central Electricity Authority (CEA) Regulations 2010, Regulation 41**, every electrical installation above **650 V** must have a **dedicated earthing system** with earth resistance not exceeding **5 Ω** for large substations and **1 Ω** for major generating stations.

### 1.5 Standard Quantities (Bold per KTU Norms)

- **Soil resistivity (ρ)** typically ranges from **10 Ω·m** (moist loam) to **1000 Ω·m** (dry sandy/gravelly soil). Acceptable design value: **ρ ≤ 100 Ω·m**.
- **Maximum permissible earth electrode resistance** for domestic installations = **5 Ω** (IS 3043).
- **Maximum touch potential** under fault = **50 V** AC (IEEE C2 / NEC).
- **Standard earth pit dimensions** for plate earthing = **3.0 m × 3.0 m × 3.0 m** (depth ≥ 3 m in dry soil).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Examination of Each Earthing Method

#### 2.1.1 Plate Earthing

**Constructional Detail:**
- A **GI (Galvanized Iron) plate** of size **600 mm × 600 mm × 6.35 mm** OR a **Copper plate of 600 mm × 600 mm × 3.15 mm** is buried vertically in the ground at a depth of **not less than 3 m** below the surface.
- The plate is surrounded by a **layer of alternate charcoal and salt** (each layer 75–100 mm thick) to maintain moisture and reduce soil resistivity.
- A **GI pipe of 19 mm dia** with a funnel at the top is provided for periodic watering of the pit to keep the surrounding soil moist.
- A **masonry inspection chamber** with a cast iron cover is built at the surface for maintenance.
- The earth wire (usually GI for plate earthing) is bolted to the plate by means of **nuts, bolts, and washers**.

**Operational Logic:**
- The plate presents a **large surface area** to the surrounding soil.
- Salt (NaCl) is hygroscopic — absorbs moisture from the air and retains it.
- Charcoal is porous — holds the moisture and salt solution.
- Combined effect: **soil resistivity around the plate is reduced by a factor of 5 to 10**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Charcoal + Salt?**
> The charcoal and salt create a low-resistivity **"earthing compound"** around the electrode. This is essentially a manually engineered **hemispherical shell of low-resistivity soil**, simulating an electrode of much larger effective radius.

---

#### 2.1.2 Pipe Earthing (KTU's Most-Favoured Method)

**Constructional Detail:**
- A **GI pipe of 38 mm (1.5 inch) diameter** and **2.75 m (9 ft) length** is the standard (per IS 3043).
- For rocky soil, **40 mm dia × 3 m long** copper pipe may be used.
- Multiple pipes (2–3 in parallel at 3 m spacing) are installed when the required resistance is very low.
- A **funnel-shaped watering cap** with wire mesh is provided at the top.
- The pipe is surrounded by a **75 mm thick layer of charcoal and salt** in the pit.

**Why Pipe Earthing is Superior:**
1. The pipe's cylindrical surface provides a **larger contact area per unit length** than a flat plate.
2. Pipe length can be increased easily to reach moist soil strata below dry topsoil.
3. It maintains a **constant moisture gradient** because the watering pipe reaches the bottom of the electrode.
4. **Cost-effective** for rocky terrain where a deep pit for plate earthing is impractical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Fact:** Pipe earthing is the **most widely used** method in Indian domestic and small industrial installations because of its low cost, simple construction, and ease of maintenance.

---

#### 2.1.3 Rod / Driven Pipe Earthing

- A **solid copper rod of 12.5 mm to 25 mm diameter** (or GI rod of similar size) is **driven directly into the ground** without excavating a pit.
- Length: **1.2 m to 3 m** for residential use; up to **6 m** for high-resistivity soil.
- Multiple rods are **driven in parallel, spaced at twice the rod length**, and connected in parallel to achieve low resistance.
- Used extensively in **rocky terrain, hilly regions, and transmission tower footings**.

---

#### 2.1.4 Strip / Conductor Earthing

- A **GI strip of 25 mm × 3 mm** (or 50 mm × 6 mm) is laid horizontally in trenches of depth **0.5 m to 1.0 m**.
- The strip is laid in a **star or radial pattern** from the equipment to be earthed.
- Particularly used in **substation earth grids** and for **lightning protection down-conductors**.
- Provides **low surge impedance** for high-frequency lightning currents.

---

#### 2.1.5 Water Main Earthing

- Uses the **underground metallic water pipeline** as the earth electrode.
- A GI wire is clamped to the water pipe with a brass saddle clamp.
- **No longer recommended** for new installations as plastic PVC pipes are replacing metallic water mains.
- Resistance depends on **length of pipe in contact with soil** and **moisture condition**.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (CRITICAL — Memorize Before Exam)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **definitive cheat sheet** for Module 4 calculations. All questions in KTU 2024 exams requiring numerical answers are based on these formulas.

| # | Earthing Method | Resistance Formula (R) | Variables & Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Plate Earthing (Circular plate, radius $a$)** | $$R_{plate} = \dfrac{\rho}{4a}$$ | $\rho$ = soil resistivity ($\Omega \cdot m$); $a$ = plate radius (m) | $a \ll$ depth; $a \ll$ spacing |
| 2 | **Plate Earthing (Square plate, side $a$)** | $$R_{plate} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \sqrt{\pi}}{2a}$$ | $a$ = side of square plate (m) | Edge effects neglected |
| 3 | **Pipe Earthing (Single pipe, length $L$, dia $d$)** | $$R_{pipe} = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) \right]$$ | $L$ = pipe length (m); $d$ = pipe diameter (m) | $L \gg d$; $L \leq 6$ m |
| 4 | **Rod Earthing (Driven rod, length $L$, dia $d$)** | $$R_{rod} = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$$ | Same as pipe | $L \gg d$ |
| 5 | **Strip / Conductor (Horizontal, length $L$, depth $h$)** | $$R_{strip} = \dfrac{\rho}{\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\dfrac{2L}{w}\right) + \ln\left(\dfrac{L}{h}\right) \right]$$ | $w$ = width of strip (m); $h$ = burial depth (m) | $L \gg h \gg w$ |
| 6 | **Multiple Electrodes in Parallel ($n$ identical)** | $$R_{n} = \dfrac{R_{single}}{n} \times \text{Utilisation Factor}$$ | $n$ = number of electrodes | Spacing $\geq 2L$ |
| 7 | **Earth Resistance from a Hemisphere (general)** | $$R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi r}$$ | $r$ = radius of hemisphere (m) | Conductor of radius $r$ buried flush |
| 8 | **Approximate for GI Plate (600 mm × 600 mm)** | $$R \approx 2.5 \text{ to } 5 \, \Omega$$ | Empirical | Dry/moderate soil |

### 2.3 Factors Influencing Earth Electrode Resistance (KTU 2024 Core Concept)

The earth electrode resistance has **three contributing components** acting in series:

$$R_{total} = R_e + R_{c} + R_{d}$$

Where:

- $R_e$ = **Resistance of earth electrode** (metal — negligible, $< 0.01 \, \Omega$)
- $R_c$ = **Contact resistance** between electrode and soil (depends on surface condition)
- $R_d$ = **Dissipation resistance** of the soil mass surrounding the electrode (the dominant term)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The **dissipation resistance $R_d$** accounts for ~ **90 % of the total earth resistance**. This is why all engineering design effort (longer electrode, more salt/charcoal, multiple parallel pits) is directed at reducing $R_d$, not at the metal itself.

### 2.4 Selection Criterion — Which Method When?

| Soil Condition / Application | Recommended Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Normal moist loam, domestic use | **Pipe earthing** | Cost-effective, easy maintenance |
| Hard rocky terrain, hilly areas | **Rod earthing (driven)** | No excavation needed |
| Heavy fault currents, substations | **Plate earthing** (Cu) | Large contact area, low R |
| High-salt or coastal soil | **Strip earthing + Cu** | Resistance stable in moisture variation |
| Lightning protection of tall structures | **Strip earthing (radial)** | Low surge impedance, distributed |
| Temporary installations | **Rod earthing** | Quick installation, no pit |
| Dry sandy soil (ρ > 300 Ω·m) | **Multiple pipe electrodes in parallel** | Reduces R to < 5 Ω |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Domestic Safety:** Every geyser, washing machine, and refrigerator body in your home is earthed through pipe earthing. Without it, a single insulation failure could be lethal.
- **Industrial Safety:** In a 415 V three-phase workshop, an unearthed motor body can develop 240 V on its frame during a single phase-to-body fault. Pipe earthing drops this to < 10 V within a few milliseconds.
- **Lightning Protection:** The Empire State Building uses 68 strip earthing electrodes radiating from its base to handle lightning strikes of up to **200 kA**.
- **Substation Grounding:** A 220 kV substation uses a **grid of strip electrodes** (earth mat) of total length > 5 km, designed to keep step and touch potentials within **IEEE 80** limits.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Earth Resistance for a Buried Hemisphere Electrode

This is the **fundamental reference derivation** from which all other earthing formulas are derived. We begin with a perfectly conducting hemisphere of radius $r$ buried flush with the earth's surface.

**Step 1 — Model Setup**
Consider the electrode as an **equivalent hemisphere of radius $r$**. The current diverges radially outward through the soil.

**Step 2 — Elemental Shell Resistance**
At a radial distance $x$ from the centre, take a thin hemispherical shell of thickness $dx$ and radius $x$.

The cross-sectional area through which the current flows is:

$$A(x) = 2\pi x^{2}$$

The resistance of this shell is given by:

$$dR = \rho \cdot \dfrac{dx}{A(x)} = \rho \cdot \dfrac{dx}{2\pi x^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Integration from Inner to Outer Radius**
The total resistance from radius $r$ (electrode surface) to infinity (effectively, to a far-away equipotential surface) is:

$$R = \int_{r}^{\infty} dR = \int_{r}^{\infty} \dfrac{\rho \, dx}{2\pi x^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluating the Integral**

$$R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi} \int_{r}^{\infty} x^{-2} \, dx = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi} \left[ -x^{-1} \right]_{r}^{\infty}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting the Limits**

$$R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi} \left[ 0 - \left(-\dfrac{1}{r}\right) \right] = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi r}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{R_{hemisphere} = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi r}}$$

This is the **master formula** of earthing engineering. All other methods (plate, pipe, rod) are derived by **equating their geometry to an equivalent hemisphere**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Plate Earthing Resistance (Circular Plate)

**Step 1 — Equivalence Principle**
A circular plate of radius $a$ has a **disc-like geometry**. If we model it as a **hemisphere of equivalent radius $r_{eq}$**, we need the same surface area for current emission.

For a plate, the area is $\pi a^{2}$ (one face). For a hemisphere, the area is $2\pi r_{eq}^{2}$.

Setting them equal:

$$\pi a^{2} = 2\pi r_{eq}^{2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; r_{eq} = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Master Formula**

$$R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi r_{eq}} = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{a} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \sqrt{2}}{2\pi a}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the Constant**

$$\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{R_{plate} = \dfrac{\rho}{4a}}$$

*(Note: In the limit of "buried hemisphere" of radius equal to the plate radius, the formula $\rho/(4a)$ emerges. The $\sqrt{\pi}/2$ form for a square plate comes from the equivalent-radius analysis of a square geometry.)*

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Square Plate Earthing Resistance

**Step 1 — Equivalent Radius of a Square**
A square plate of side $a$ has the **same area as a circular plate** of radius $r_{eq}$:

$$a^{2} = \pi r_{eq}^{2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; r_{eq} = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{\pi}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Master Formula**

$$R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi r_{eq}} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \sqrt{\pi}}{2\pi a}$$

**Step 3 — Final Simplified Form**

$$\boxed{R_{plate,square} = \dfrac{\rho \, \sqrt{\pi}}{2a}}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Pipe Earthing Resistance

This is the **most important derivation for KTU 2024 exams**.

**Step 1 — Geometric Model**
A vertical pipe of length $L$ and diameter $d$ is modeled. The current is assumed to flow **radially outward from the cylindrical surface**, with the bottom end (hemispherical cap) contributing a small additional path.

**Step 2 — Empirical Approximation (Emde's Formula)**
For a long thin electrode ($L \gg d$), Emde (1904) derived:

$$R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Physical Interpretation of Each Term**

- $\dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}$ — This is the **"characteristic resistance"** of a soil cylinder of radius $L$ and unit depth. As $L$ increases, the surrounding "earthing volume" increases, reducing $R$.
- $\ln(4L/d)$ — This is the **"shape factor"**. A thinner pipe (small $d$) gives a **larger logarithm** and thus **higher resistance**. A fatter pipe (large $d$) gives lower resistance.

**Step 4 — Final Result**

$$\boxed{R_{pipe} = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) \right]}$$

---

### 3.5 Complete Worked Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A **GI pipe of 50 mm outer diameter** and **3.0 m length** is used for earthing. The soil resistivity is **80 Ω·m**. Calculate:
> (a) the earth resistance of the pipe,
> (b) the number of such pipes in parallel (spacing 3 m apart) required to bring the resistance below 1 Ω, assuming a utilisation factor of 0.85.

**Given Data:**

$$d = 50 \, \text{mm} = 0.050 \, \text{m}, \quad L = 3.0 \, \text{m}, \quad \rho = 80 \, \Omega \cdot m$$

---

**Part (a) — Single Pipe Resistance**

$$R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right)$$

Substitute the values:

$$R = \dfrac{80}{2 \pi \times 3.0} \ln\left(\dfrac{4 \times 3.0}{0.050}\right)$$

Compute the argument of the logarithm:

$$\dfrac{4L}{d} = \dfrac{12.0}{0.050} = 240$$

Compute the natural logarithm:

$$\ln(240) = 5.4806$$

Compute the denominator:

$$2 \pi L = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 3.0 = 18.8496$$

Now compute the resistance:

$$R = \dfrac{80}{18.8496} \times 5.4806$$

$$R = 4.244 \times 5.4806$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 23.26 \, \Omega}$$

---

**Part (b) — Parallel Combination for $R < 1 \, \Omega$**

For $n$ identical electrodes in parallel, with utilisation factor $\eta$ (which accounts for interference between electrodes):

$$R_{n} = \dfrac{R_{single}}{n \times \eta}$$

Required: $R_n \leq 1 \, \Omega$, with $\eta = 0.85$.

Rearranging:

$$n \geq \dfrac{R_{single}}{R_{required} \times \eta} = \dfrac{23.26}{1.0 \times 0.85} = 27.36$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{n = 28 \text{ pipes}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Achieving 1 Ω in typical loam soil requires approximately **28 parallel pipes of 3 m length each**, spaced at least 3 m apart. This is why large substations use an **earth mat** (grid of strips) rather than discrete electrodes.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Plate vs Pipe vs Rod

| Parameter | Plate Earthing | Pipe Earthing | Rod Earthing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Standard size** | 600 mm × 600 mm × 6.35 mm (GI) | 38 mm dia × 2.75 m (GI) | 12.5 mm dia × 1.2 m (Cu) |
| **Material cost** | High (large plate) | Low | Medium |
| **Installation effort** | Excavate deep pit (3 m³) | Excavate small pit | Drive with hammer — no pit |
| **Suitable soil** | Soft, deep loam | Normal loam/clay | Rocky, hilly |
| **Maintenance** | Periodic watering required | Periodic watering | Minimum |
| **Achievable R** | 2.5–5 Ω (single) | 5–25 Ω (single) | 20–80 Ω (single) |
| **Life (years)** | 25–30 | 15–20 | 10–15 |
| **Best for** | Heavy fault installations, substations | Domestic, small industry | Rocky soil, towers |
| **Standard reference** | IS 3043 Cl. 9.3 | IS 3043 Cl. 9.2 | IS 3043 Cl. 9.4 |

---

### 3.7 Step-by-Step Derivation of the Earth-Resistance Concept (Physical Intuition)

**Question:** *Why does doubling the pipe length NOT halve the resistance?*

**Answer:** From the pipe formula:

$$R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right)$$

If $L \to 2L$:

$$R_{2L} = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi (2L)} \ln\left(\dfrac{4(2L)}{d}\right) = \dfrac{\rho}{4\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) + \ln 2 \right]$$

Therefore:

$$\dfrac{R_{2L}}{R_L} = \dfrac{1}{2} \left[ 1 + \dfrac{\ln 2}{\ln(4L/d)} \right]$$

For $L = 3 \, m, \, d = 0.05 \, m$:

$$\dfrac{\ln 2}{\ln(240)} = \dfrac{0.6931}{5.4806} = 0.1265$$

So:

$$\dfrac{R_{2L}}{R_L} = 0.5 \times (1 + 0.1265) = 0.5633$$

Doubling the length **reduces resistance by only ~ 44 %** (not 50 %). The logarithmic gain in shape factor partially offsets the linear halving.
