## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]**
*What is earthing? List any **three** purposes of providing an earthing system in a domestic electrical installation.*

**Model Answer:**

**Earthing** is the process of connecting the non-current-carrying metal parts of an electrical installation (such as equipment bodies, conduits, and structural steel) to the general mass of earth through a low-impedance electrode, so that any leakage or fault current is safely discharged to earth, keeping the system at earth potential.

**Three Purposes:**

1. **Safety of human life** — By keeping the touch voltage below 50 V, earthing prevents fatal electric shocks in case of insulation failure.
2. **Protection of equipment** — A low-impedance earth path allows sufficient fault current to flow, which causes the fuse or MCB to operate quickly, isolating the faulty circuit.
3. **Stabilization of system voltage** — Earthing provides a stable 0 V reference for the system neutral, ensuring balanced phase voltages and protecting sensitive electronic equipment.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*State the **empirical formula** for earth resistance of a pipe electrode. Identify each variable and its SI unit.*

**Model Answer:**

The empirical formula for earth resistance of a **pipe electrode** is:

$$R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right)$$

Where:
- $R$ = earth resistance of the pipe electrode, in **ohms ($\Omega$)**
- $\rho$ = soil resistivity at the location, in **ohm-metre ($\Omega \cdot m$)**
- $L$ = length of the pipe electrode buried in the ground, in **metres (m)**
- $d$ = outer diameter of the pipe, in **metres (m)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### 🔷 **Question A** (14 Marks) — Pipe Earthing Focus

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]**

**(a)** Explain the **constructional details of pipe earthing** with a neat block diagram. State **four advantages** of pipe earthing over plate earthing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **GI pipe electrode of 50 mm diameter and 3 m length** is buried vertically in soil of resistivity **90 Ω·m**. Calculate:
- (i) the **earth resistance** of a single pipe, and
- (ii) the **number of such pipes** (spacing 3 m apart) required in parallel to reduce the resistance below **2 Ω**, assuming a **utilisation factor of 0.8**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a) — Constructional Details (7 marks):**

**Construction of Pipe Earthing:**

1. **Excavation of Pit:** A pit of dimensions approximately **0.3 m × 0.3 m × 3.0 m** is excavated in the ground. The depth is kept at least 3 m below the natural surface level.

2. **Placement of GI Pipe:** A **GI pipe of 38 mm (1.5 inch) diameter and 2.75 m length** (per IS 3043 standard) is placed vertically in the centre of the pit. For higher current installations, a **50 mm diameter pipe** may be used. The lower end of the pipe is **tapered or plugged** to prevent clogging.

3. **Charcoal and Salt Layers:** A layer of **75 mm thickness** consisting of **alternating layers of charcoal (coke) and common salt (NaCl)** is filled around the pipe. The proportions are typically **5 parts charcoal : 1 part salt** by volume. This layer reduces the effective soil resistivity by 5–10 times.

4. **Watering Arrangement:** A **19 mm dia GI pipe** with a **funnel and wire mesh** at the top is placed alongside the main electrode. Water is poured periodically (every 2–3 months in dry season) to keep the charcoal-salt layer moist.

5. **Earth Wire Connection:** The earth continuity conductor (GI wire of appropriate size as per IS 732) is connected to the GI pipe using **nuts, bolts, and washers** above the ground level.

6. **Masonry Chamber:** A **concrete or brick masonry chamber** of size **0.3 m × 0.3 m × 0.3 m** is built around the top of the electrode. A **cast iron inspection cover** is provided for periodic maintenance.

**Four Advantages of Pipe Earthing over Plate Earthing:**

1. **Lower cost and easier installation** — Pipe earthing requires a smaller pit and less excavation work compared to plate earthing, which needs a 3 m × 3 m × 3 m pit for a 600 mm × 600 mm plate.
2. **Easier maintenance** — Watering through the funnel keeps the surrounding soil moist consistently, whereas in plate earthing, watering has limited penetration to the plate's bottom.
3. **Higher mechanical strength** — A pipe electrode is mechanically robust and can withstand soil pressure, ground movement, and accidental impacts, whereas a flat plate can bend or warp.
4. **Suitable for deeper soil strata** — The pipe can be extended by coupling additional lengths to reach moist soil layers below dry topsoil, ensuring stable low resistance throughout the year.

---

**Solution to Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**

$$d = 50 \, \text{mm} = 0.050 \, \text{m}, \quad L = 3.0 \, \text{m}, \quad \rho = 90 \, \Omega \cdot m, \quad \eta = 0.8, \quad R_{req} < 2 \, \Omega$$

**(i) Earth Resistance of Single Pipe:**

$$R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right)$$

Compute $4L/d$:

$$\dfrac{4L}{d} = \dfrac{4 \times 3.0}{0.050} = \dfrac{12.0}{0.050} = 240$$

Compute $\ln(240)$:

$$\ln(240) = \ln(2.4 \times 100) = \ln(2.4) + \ln(100) = 0.8755 + 4.6052 = 5.4806$$

Compute denominator:

$$2 \pi L = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 3.0 = 18.8496 \, m$$

Compute $R$:

$$R = \dfrac{90}{18.8496} \times 5.4806 = 4.775 \times 5.4806$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 26.17 \, \Omega}$$

**(ii) Number of Pipes in Parallel:**

For $n$ pipes with utilisation factor $\eta$:

$$R_{n} = \dfrac{R_{single}}{n \cdot \eta}$$

Required: $R_{n} \leq 2 \, \Omega$. Solve for $n$:

$$n \geq \dfrac{R_{single}}{R_{req} \cdot \eta} = \dfrac{26.17}{2 \times 0.8} = \dfrac{26.17}{1.6} = 16.36$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{n = 17 \text{ pipes}}$$

**Verification:**

$$R_{17} = \dfrac{26.17}{17 \times 0.8} = \dfrac{26.17}{13.6} = 1.924 \, \Omega < 2 \, \Omega \quad \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Pipe Earthing Numericals:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error:** Students frequently forget to convert $d$ from mm to m. If $d = 50$ is plugged in directly, the resistance comes out absurdly small. **Always convert to SI units first.**
> 2. **Forgetting the Utilisation Factor:** When calculating parallel resistance, students often write $R_n = R_{single}/n$ and ignore the utilisation factor. This **causes a 2-mark penalty** in KTU evaluation.
> 3. **Rounding Up vs Down:** When $n = 16.36$, students often write 16 (incorrect). The correct answer is **17 (always round up to next integer)** to satisfy the inequality $R_n \leq 2 \, \Omega$.
> 4. **Wrong Formula Substitution:** Some students confuse the plate formula with the pipe formula. **Memorize the two formulas distinctly** — the plate formula has $\rho/(4a)$, while the pipe formula has $\rho/(2\pi L) \times \ln(4L/d)$.

---

#### 🔷 **Question B** (14 Marks) — Plate vs Pipe Comparison (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**(a)** Compare **plate earthing and pipe earthing** under the following heads: (i) electrode material and size, (ii) installation procedure, (iii) cost, (iv) maintenance, (v) earth resistance achieved, (vi) typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **square plate electrode of side 600 mm** is buried in soil of resistivity **100 Ω·m**. Calculate the **earth resistance**. If the same installation is replaced by a **circular plate of equal area**, what is the **percentage change** in earth resistance? **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Plate Earthing | Pipe Earthing |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Electrode material and size** | GI plate 600 mm × 600 mm × 6.35 mm OR Cu plate 600 × 600 × 3.15 mm | GI pipe 38 mm dia × 2.75 m length (per IS 3043) |
| 2 | **Installation procedure** | Dig a large pit 3 m × 3 m × 3 m, place plate vertically, fill with charcoal-salt layers | Dig a small pit 0.3 m × 0.3 m × 3 m, drive/place pipe, surround with charcoal-salt |
| 3 | **Cost** | High — large plate + large pit + more charcoal/salt | Low — small pipe + small pit + less charcoal/salt |
| 4 | **Maintenance** | Watering has limited reach to plate's deep bottom; resistance may rise in dry season | Easy watering through funnel; consistent low resistance year-round |
| 5 | **Earth resistance** | 2.5 – 5 Ω (single plate) | 5 – 25 Ω (single pipe), but parallel pipes give 1 – 2 Ω |
| 6 | **Typical applications** | Generating stations, EHV substations, heavy industrial fault currents | Domestic installations, small commercial buildings, light industrial loads |

---

**Solution to Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**

For square plate: $a = 600 \, \text{mm} = 0.6 \, \text{m}$, $\rho = 100 \, \Omega \cdot m$

**Step 1 — Earth Resistance of Square Plate**

$$R_{sq} = \dfrac{\rho \, \sqrt{\pi}}{2a} = \dfrac{100 \times 1.7725}{2 \times 0.6}$$

$$R_{sq} = \dfrac{177.25}{1.2} = 147.71 \, \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent Radius of Circular Plate of Same Area**

For a square of side $a$, the area is $A = a^{2}$. For an equal-area circular plate, $\pi r^{2} = a^{2}$:

$$r = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{\pi}} = \dfrac{0.6}{1.7725} = 0.3385 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Earth Resistance of Circular Plate**

$$R_{circ} = \dfrac{\rho}{4r} = \dfrac{100}{4 \times 0.3385} = \dfrac{100}{1.354} = 73.86 \, \Omega$$

**Step 4 — Percentage Change in Resistance**

$$\Delta R = R_{sq} - R_{circ} = 147.71 - 73.86 = 73.85 \, \Omega$$

$$\% \text{ change} = \dfrac{\Delta R}{R_{sq}} \times 100 = \dfrac{73.85}{147.71} \times 100 \approx 50\%$$

**Conclusion:** A circular plate of the same area gives **half (50 % less)** the earth resistance compared to a square plate. This is because the circular geometry has a **shorter effective radius of current emission** and a more uniform field distribution.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes in This Question:**
> 1. **Confusing $a$ and $r$:** Students often plug in $a = 0.6$ into the circular plate formula $\rho/(4a)$, treating the side as the radius. This gives $R = 41.67 \, \Omega$ — **wrong**. The circular plate must first be converted to an **equivalent radius** $r = a/\sqrt{\pi}$.
> 2. **Missing the percentage change step:** Many students compute both resistances but forget the **"percentage change"** requirement, losing 2 marks.
> 3. **Sign convention error:** When asked "percentage change," always use the **original (square plate) value** as the denominator, not the average or the final value.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this section as your last-minute revision sheet before the KTU exam.**

#### ✅ Definition Master Points
- [x] Earthing = intentional connection of non-current-carrying parts to earth via low-impedance electrode.
- [x] Earth electrode resistance should be **< 5 Ω** for domestic; **< 1 Ω** for substations.
- [x] Touch voltage limit = **50 V** (IEEE); Step voltage limit = **varies with soil resistivity**.

#### ✅ Five Methods — Quick Memory Mnemonic
**"P-P-R-S-W"** = **P**late, **P**ipe, **R**od, **S**trip, **W**ater Main.

#### ✅ Formula Mastery (3 must-know formulas)
- [x] Hemisphere: $R = \rho/(2\pi r)$
- [x] Plate (circular): $R = \rho/(4a)$
- [x] Pipe: $R = (\rho/2\pi L) \ln(4L/d)$

#### ✅ Material Specifications (IS 3043 Standards)
- [x] GI plate: 600 mm × 600 mm × 6.35 mm
- [x] GI pipe: 38 mm dia × 2.75 m length
- [x] Cu plate: 600 mm × 600 mm × 3.15 mm
- [x] Cu rod: 12.5 mm to 25 mm dia × 1.2 m to 6 m length

#### ✅ Soil Resistivity Range
- [x] Moist loam: 10 Ω·m (excellent)
- [x] Average soil: 100 Ω·m (typical)
- [x] Dry sandy/gravel: 1000 Ω·m (poor)
- [x] **Rule of thumb:** ρ × (desired R) ≥ 25,000 → use multiple electrodes.

#### ✅ Numerical Problem Solving Protocol
1. **Read the question carefully** — note whether it's pipe, plate, or rod.
2. **Convert all units to SI** — mm → m, cm → m, etc.
3. **Write the formula first** — partial marks for correct formula even if calculation fails.
4. **Show every step** — KTU awards step marks.
5. **Round up to next integer** for number of electrodes.
6. **Apply utilisation factor** for parallel electrodes.

#### ✅ Comparison Pointers for Theory Questions
- [x] Pipe earthing > Plate earthing in **cost, maintenance, ease**.
- [x] Plate earthing > Pipe earthing in **achievable single-electrode resistance**.
- [x] Rod earthing > Pipe earthing in **rocky terrain**.

#### ✅ Common Examiner Traps to Avoid
- [x] Don't forget to **multiply by utilisation factor** in parallel resistance.
- [x] Don't use $4a$ for a square plate — use the $\sqrt{\pi}$ correction.
- [x] Don't confuse the **log term** — pipe has $\ln(4L/d)$, rod has $\ln(4L/d) - 1$.
- [x] Always specify **electrode depth** in design problems (3 m standard).

#### ✅ Last-Line Defence — If Question is Ambiguous
- [x] Default to **pipe earthing** (most common in Indian practice).
- [x] Default to **GI material** (cheaper than copper).
- [x] Default to **ρ = 100 Ω·m** if not given.

> [!TIP]
> **Golden Rule for KTU 2024 PCEET602 Module 4:** *Memorize the three formulas (hemisphere, plate, pipe), understand the derivation, and practice at least five numerical problems on parallel electrodes. This topic is worth **~ 12–15 marks** in a typical KTU end-semester exam, and 80 % of the marks come from the pipe earthing formula.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plate Earthing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Plate Earthing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Plate Earthing** is a type of earthing (grounding) system in which a metallic plate — typically made of **Copper (Cu)** or **Galvanized Iron (GI)** — is buried vertically in a pit at a depth below the earth's surface, surrounded by alternate layers of **charcoal and salt**, to provide a low-resistance dissipation path for fault/leakage currents into the general mass of earth. It is the most commonly specified earthing method in KTU-evaluated residential, commercial, and small industrial installations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Plate Earthing is the **foundational standard method** the examiner tests against. You MUST know the plate dimensions, backfill composition formula, and the standard resistance equation (≤ 1 Ω for large installations, ≤ 5 Ω for domestic) — these are guaranteed 3-mark questions.

## 1.2 The "Why" — Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are watering a huge garden. A small bucket of water poured onto dry, hard soil will simply **pool on the surface** and run off — it never reaches the roots. But if you dig a pit, fill it with absorbent material (charcoal acts like a sponge), and pour the water in, it **seeps deep and spreads out** to nourish the entire garden.

Plate earthing works the **exact same way**:

| Garden Analogy | Plate Earthing System |
|---|---|
| Bucket of water | Fault current from a live conductor |
| Dry, hard topsoil | Natural dry earth (high resistivity, 100–1000 Ω·m) |
| Pit with absorbent sponge | Pit with **charcoal + salt** (low resistivity, 5–10 Ω·m) |
| Water seeping to roots | Current dissipating into the general earth mass |
| Garden soil | The general mass of earth |

The **plate** is the collection point. The **charcoal + salt backfill** is the **low-resistivity bridge** that lets current spread out. Without the backfill, the contact resistance between plate and dry earth would be so high that the system would be **useless**.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (Kerala/Indian Standard Practice)

- **Standard plate size (Copper):** **600 mm × 600 mm × 3.15 mm thick** (IS 3043)
- **Standard plate size (GI):** **600 mm × 600 mm × 6 mm thick**
- **Burial depth of plate:** **Top edge at least 1.5 m to 3 m below ground level**
- **Backfill layers:** Alternate layers of **charcoal (coke)** and **common salt (NaCl)**, each ~150 mm thick
- **Earth pit diameter:** ~1.0 m (so that backfill covers plate on all sides with ≥ 100 mm cover)
- **Watering arrangement:** A **GI pipe (20 mm dia)** with a funnel at the top, perforated at the bottom, runs down to the plate — this allows periodic watering to keep the salt moist (NaCl is hygroscopic, so the moisture is retained).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why salt and charcoal?** Charcoal retains moisture for months (it does not rot or dissolve). Salt, when dissolved in trace moisture, ionizes into Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions, **drastically lowering the resistivity** of the surrounding soil. Together, they keep the pit's resistivity at **5–10 Ω·m** versus the natural 100–1000 Ω·m of dry Kerala laterite soil.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The plate earthing system functions through a deliberate chain of electrical and chemical actions:

1. **Fault Occurs:** A live conductor (phase) touches the metal body of an appliance (e.g., a washing machine chassis) due to insulation failure. The body becomes energized to ~230 V AC.
2. **Current Seeks Earth:** The fault current flows through the protective earth conductor (the green/yellow wire) → down to the **earth electrode (the plate)**.
3. **Plate as Collection Node:** The buried plate acts as a large surface area collector — a **600 mm × 600 mm** plate provides **0.36 m²** of contact area versus a pipe electrode's much smaller contact patch.
4. **Backfill as Resistivity Reducer:** The charcoal-salt mixture immediately surrounding the plate is engineered to have a resistivity of **~5–10 Ω·m**, so the current easily leaves the metal plate and enters the backfill.
5. **Diffusion into General Earth Mass:** The current then spreads radially outward from the pit into the surrounding natural soil. As the cross-sectional area of current flow increases (a sphere of dissipation), the resistance drops rapidly.
6. **Protective Device Operation:** The high fault current trips the MCB/MCCB/ELCB within **0.1 to 0.4 seconds**, isolating the faulty circuit and preventing electrocution.
7. **Equipotential Bonding:** The earth pit maintains the body of the appliance at **near-zero potential** relative to earth, so a person touching it does not get shocked.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Design Choice

- **Why a plate, not just a rod?** A plate provides **much larger surface area in contact with the backfill** → lower contact resistance. A 600×600 mm plate has 6× the area of a typical 40 mm dia × 2.4 m rod.
- **Why vertical burial?** Vertical burial allows the plate to reach **moist sub-soil layers** below the dry surface. Moisture content is the single biggest factor in soil resistivity.
- **Why alternate layers?** It prevents the salt from being washed away in one heavy rain. Layered with charcoal, it stays in place for years.
- **Why the GI watering pipe?** In summer, the pit dries out → resistivity rises → resistance fails. Periodic pouring of water through this pipe dissolves the salt and rehydrates the charcoal.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These four equations are the **bread-and-butter** for any plate earthing numerical in KTU exams.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary / KTU Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Resistance of a plate electrode** | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{4} \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{A}}$ | $\rho$ = soil resistivity (Ω·m); $A$ = plate area (m²) | Earth resistance **≤ 1 Ω** (large substation), **≤ 5 Ω** (domestic) |
| 2 | **Area of plate** | $A = a \times b$ | $a$ = length (m), $b$ = width (m) | Copper: 0.6 × 0.6 = 0.36 m² |
| 3 | **Resistivity of backfill (approx.)** | $\rho_{\text{bf}} \approx 5$ to $10 \, \Omega\!\cdot\!m$ | — | Versus natural soil: 100–1000 Ω·m |
| 4 | **Total earth resistance (parallel plates)** | $\dfrac{1}{R_T} = \dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2} + \dots$ | $R_i$ = individual plate resistance | Use when multiple plates in same pit |
| 5 | **Current density at electrode** | $J = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | $I$ = fault current (A); $A$ = area (m²) | Must be ≤ tolerable limit for the metal |
| 6 | **Voltage of earth electrode** | $V_e = I_g \times R_e$ | $I_g$ = fault current (A); $R_e$ = earth resistance (Ω) | $V_e$ must be ≤ 50 V for human safety (IEC) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical bar trap in KTU answer sheets:** When you write absolute value (like $\vert R \vert$) in a markdown table, **DO NOT** use the keyboard pipe `|`. The markdown parser will break your table. Use the LaTeX command `\vert` or `\mid` instead, as done above. The KTU online portal is also strict about this.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Plate earthing is the **workhorse** of low-voltage distribution. You will find it in:
- **Domestic houses** (KSEB standard for all new connections in Kerala — single-plate domestic earth).
- **Commercial complexes** (multiple plates in parallel for IT parks in Trivandrum and Kochi).
- **Hospitals** (medical IT earthing — plate + salt + charcoal + bentonite for ultra-low resistance).
- **Transformer neutral earthing** (up to 250 kVA distribution transformers in Kerala use plate earthing as per IS 3043).

The design you study in this module is **directly implemented** in every KSEB service connection estimate sheet you will draft in your design lab.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Resistance of a Plate Electrode

The plate earthing formula is **derived from first principles using the theory of a current-flow hemisphere in a homogeneous conducting medium**. This is the most-asked derivation in KTU Module 4.

### Setup
Imagine a perfectly hemispherical electrode of radius $r$ buried flush with the earth's surface. Current $I$ flows radially outward from the electrode into the infinite, homogeneous earth of resistivity $\rho$. We want the resistance between the electrode and "infinity."

### Step 1 — Resistance of a Thin Hemispherical Shell
Consider a thin hemispherical shell of radius $x$ and thickness $dx$ concentric with the electrode.

The **cross-sectional area** through which current flows at radius $x$ is the area of the hemisphere:

$$
A(x) = 2\pi x^{2}
$$

The **resistance** of this thin shell is given by $dR = \rho \, \dfrac{dx}{A(x)}$:

$$
dR = \rho \, \dfrac{dx}{2\pi x^{2}}
$$

### Step 2 — Integrate From the Electrode Surface to Infinity
The total resistance of the hemisphere of earth is the integral of $dR$ from the electrode radius $r$ to infinity:

$$
R = \int_{r}^{\infty} dR = \int_{r}^{\infty} \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi x^{2}} \, dx
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi} \int_{r}^{\infty} x^{-2} \, dx
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Integral
Using the standard power rule $\int x^{-2} \, dx = -x^{-1}$:

$$
R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi} \left[ -\dfrac{1}{x} \right]_{r}^{\infty}
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi} \left( 0 - \left(-\dfrac{1}{r}\right) \right)
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi r}
$$

> This is the classical **hemispherical electrode resistance**.

### Step 4 — Convert Hemisphere to an Equivalent Plate
For a plate of dimensions $a \times b$, we define an **equivalent radius** $r_{eq}$ such that the hemispherical surface area equals the plate's dissipating surface area projected as a hemisphere:

$$
2\pi r_{eq}^{2} = A = a \times b
$$

$$
r_{eq}^{2} = \dfrac{A}{2\pi}
$$

$$
r_{eq} = \sqrt{\dfrac{A}{2\pi}}
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute $r_{eq}$ into the Hemisphere Formula

$$
R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi \, r_{eq}} = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{A / 2\pi}}
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2\pi}{A}}
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{2\pi}}{\sqrt{A}}
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{2\pi}}{\sqrt{A}} = \dfrac{\rho \sqrt{2\pi}}{2\pi \sqrt{A}}
$$

Since $\dfrac{\sqrt{2\pi}}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{2} / \sqrt{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{4\pi}} = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{\pi}}$:

$$
R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\sqrt{\pi}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{A}} = \dfrac{\rho}{4} \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{A}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final boxed formula (memorize this — it appears in nearly every KTU numerical):**
> 
> $$\boxed{R = \dfrac{\rho}{4} \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{A}}}$$
> 
> where $\rho$ is in Ω·m, $A$ is in m², and $R$ is in Ω.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — KTU Standard Style

**Problem:** A copper plate of size 600 mm × 600 mm is used for plate earthing in a region where the soil resistivity is **80 Ω·m**. The backfill reduces the effective resistivity near the plate to **10 Ω·m**. Calculate the resistance to earth. If two such plates are connected in parallel in the same pit, what is the combined resistance?

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Given:**
- Plate size: $0.6 \, \text{m} \times 0.6 \, \text{m}$
- Natural soil resistivity: $\rho_{\text{soil}} = 80 \, \Omega\!\cdot\!m$
- Backfill effective resistivity near plate: $\rho = 10 \, \Omega\!\cdot\!m$ (we use this as the controlling resistivity within one plate-radius of the plate)

**Step 1 — Plate area:**

$$
A = 0.6 \times 0.6 = 0.36 \, \text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Single plate resistance:**

$$
R = \dfrac{\rho}{4} \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{A}} = \dfrac{10}{4} \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{0.36}}
$$

Compute the inner square root first:

$$
\dfrac{\pi}{0.36} = \dfrac{3.1416}{0.36} = 8.7267
$$

$$
\sqrt{8.7267} = 2.9541
$$

Therefore:

$$
R = \dfrac{10}{4} \times 2.9541 = 2.5 \times 2.9541 = 7.385 \, \Omega
$$

> **Result (single plate):** $R \approx 7.39 \, \Omega$

**Step 3 — Two plates in parallel:**

For two identical plates in parallel:

$$
R_T = \dfrac{R}{2} = \dfrac{7.385}{2} = 3.69 \, \Omega
$$

> **Result (two plates parallel):** $R_T \approx 3.69 \, \Omega$ — this is below the KTU domestic limit of 5 Ω. ✅

### Analytical Matrix — What the Examiner Looks For

| Step | What the Examiner Awards | Marks (out of 7) |
|---|---|---|
| Listing given data with units | 1 | 1 |
| Correct formula citation | 1 | 1 |
| Area computation | 1 | 1 |
| Substitution with units | 1 | 1 |
| Numerical evaluation (intermediate) | 1 | 1 |
| Final answer with unit | 1 | 1 |
| Parallel combination step (Part b) | 1 | 1 |
| **Total** | | **7** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

Since plate earthing is a **physical installation buried in soil**, a literal cross-sectional drawing is the ideal. However, to keep the diagram fully Mermaid-renderable and to ensure the Mermaid compiler never chokes on reserved keywords, we map the physical structure to a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** that conveys the same engineering information — the layered construction, the connections, and the current path.

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of a Plate Earthing Installation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SURFACE["GROUND SURFACE LEVEL"]
        ACCESS["ACCESS PIT WITH CAST IRON COVER"]
        FUNNEL["FUNNEL TOP OF WATERING PIPE 20 MM DIA GI"]
    end

    subgraph BACKFILL["ALTERNATE LAYERS OF CHARCOAL AND SALT EACH LAYER 150 MM THICK"]
        L1["LAYER 1 CHARCOAL"]
        L2["LAYER 2 SALT NaCl"]
        L3["LAYER 3 CHARCOAL"]
        L4["LAYER 4 SALT NaCl"]
        L5["LAYER 5 CHARCOAL"]
    end

    subgraph PLATE["PLATE ELECTRODE 600 MM x 600 MM x 3.15 MM COPPER OR 6 MM GI"]
        PLATE_A["PLATE BURIED VERTICALLY TOP EDGE 1.5 M TO 3 M BELOW GROUND"]
    end

    subgraph PIPE["WATERING PIPE PERFORATED LOWER 500 MM"]
        PIPE_A["GI PIPE 20 MM DIA RUNS FROM FUNNEL TO PLATE"]
    end

    subgraph LUG["EARTHING CONDUCTOR CONNECTION"]
        LUG_A["COPPER OR GI LUG BOLTED TO PLATE VIA NUT AND BOLT"]
        LUG_B["EARTHING CONDUCTOR 6 SWG GI WIRE FOR DOMESTIC OR 25 x 3 MM GI STRIP FOR INDUSTRIAL"]
    end

    subgraph EARTH["GENERAL MASS OF EARTH"]
        EARTH_A["CURRENT DISSIPATION SPHERICAL REGION OF SOIL RADIUS 5 TO 10 M"]
    end

    FUNNEL --> PIPE_A
    PIPE_A --> PLATE_A
    ACCESS -.INSPECTION.-> PLATE_A
    PLATE_A --> LUG_A
    LUG_A --> LUG_B
    L1 --> PLATE_A
    L2 --> PLATE_A
    L3 --> PLATE_A
    L4 --> PLATE_A
    L5 --> PLATE_A
    PLATE_A --> EARTH_A
    LUG_B --> EARTH_A

    style PLATE_A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style EARTH_A fill:#8B4513,stroke:#3E2723,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    style FUNNEL fill:#4682B4,stroke:#1A237E,color:#FFF
```

## 4.2 Construction Sequence (Block-Level Process Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["STEP 1 EXCAVATE PIT 1 M DIA x 3 M DEEP"] --> S2["STEP 2 PLACE PLATE VERTICALLY AT BOTTOM"]
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3 BOLT LUG AND EARTHING CONDUCTOR TO PLATE"]
    S3 --> S4["STEP 4 PLACE 150 MM CHARCOAL LAYER"]
    S4 --> S5["STEP 5 PLACE 150 MM SALT LAYER"]
    S5 --> S6["STEP 6 REPEAT CHARCOAL SALT UNTIL PIT FILLED"]
    S6 --> S7["STEP 7 INSERT PERFORATED GI WATERING PIPE"]
    S7 --> S8["STEP 8 INSTALL FUNNEL AT TOP AND CI COVER"]
    S8 --> S9["STEP 9 POUR 20 TO 40 LITRES OF WATER THROUGH FUNNEL"]
    S9 --> S10["STEP 10 MEASURE EARTH RESISTANCE WITH MEGGER OR EARTH TESTER"]

    style S1 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    style S2 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    style S3 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    style S4 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style S5 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    style S6 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style S7 fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    style S8 fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    style S9 fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    style S10 fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Reading the Diagram — Engineering Interpretation

- The **gold-shaded block (plate)** is the only metallic conductor in direct contact with the surrounding backfill. Everything above it is a **chemical/physical aid** to its function.
- The **brown earth block** is the dissipation region — note that current does not stop at the pit boundary; it continues to spread in a roughly spherical region of radius 5–10 m depending on soil type.
- The **blue funnel-to-pipe path** is a **maintenance access** — it is not energized in normal operation. It exists to allow the homeowner/electrician to add water during dry months.
- The **lug and conductor** are the only **current-carrying** path during a fault — they must be sized to carry the maximum earth-fault current without fusing.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define plate earthing. List the standard dimensions of a copper plate and a GI plate as per IS 3043.**

**Model Answer:**

Plate earthing is a method of earthing in which a metallic plate (Copper or GI) is buried vertically in a pit at a depth of 1.5 m to 3 m below the ground level, surrounded by alternate layers of charcoal and salt, to provide a low-resistance path for fault currents to dissipate into the general mass of earth.

Standard plate dimensions as per IS 3043:
- **Copper plate:** 600 mm × 600 mm × 3.15 mm thick
- **Galvanized Iron (GI) plate:** 600 mm × 600 mm × 6 mm thick

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**State the formula for the resistance of a plate electrode and explain the role of charcoal and salt in the backfill.**

**Model Answer:**

$$
R = \dfrac{\rho}{4} \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{A}}
$$

where $R$ = earth resistance (Ω), $\rho$ = effective resistivity of the backfill soil (Ω·m), $A$ = area of the plate in m².

**Role of charcoal:** Charcoal is highly porous and retains moisture for long periods without decaying. It maintains a moist conductive zone around the plate throughout the year.

**Role of salt (NaCl):** Common salt is hygroscopic and ionizes in the trace moisture to release Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions, which drastically reduce the resistivity of the surrounding soil from ~100 Ω·m to ~5–10 Ω·m.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU ESE Pattern: Choose EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.]**

---

### ❓ Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the constructional features of a plate earthing system. Label all the components clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the resistance of a plate electrode, $R = \dfrac{\rho}{4} \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{A}}$, starting from the resistance of a hemispherical electrode. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Constructional Features (7 marks):**

A plate earthing system consists of the following components installed in a step-by-step manner:

1. **Excavation:** A pit of approximately 1 m diameter and 3 m depth is dug at the selected location (preferably in moist soil, away from building foundations and water pipes).

2. **Plate electrode:** A copper plate (600 mm × 600 mm × 3.15 mm) or a GI plate (600 mm × 600 mm × 6 mm) is placed **vertically** at the bottom of the pit.

3. **Earthing conductor connection:** A copper lug (for Cu plate) or GI lug (for GI plate) is bolted to the plate using nut-bolt-washer. A 6 SWG GI wire (domestic) or 25 × 3 mm GI strip (industrial) is connected to the lug and run up to the equipment/main panel.

4. **Backfill layers:** Alternate layers of **charcoal (coke)** and **common salt (NaCl)**, each layer approximately 150 mm thick, are filled around the plate until the pit is full. The layers are lightly watered to dissolve the salt and compact the backfill.

5. **Watering pipe:** A 20 mm diameter GI pipe, perforated at the lower 500 mm, is installed vertically with its bottom touching the plate. The top of the pipe is fitted with a funnel and a cast iron (CI) cover for periodic watering.

6. **Access pit:** A masonry/inspection chamber is built around the top of the pit with a CI cover at ground level, allowing future access for testing and watering.

**(Mark allocation: 2 marks components list, 3 marks sketch, 1 mark labelling, 1 mark role explanation)**

**Part (b) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to the exhaustive derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The key steps the examiner will check:

| Step | Result | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| Set up: hemispherical shell, $A(x) = 2\pi x^2$ | — | 1 |
| Element resistance: $dR = \rho \, dx / (2\pi x^2)$ | — | 1 |
| Integrate from $r$ to $\infty$ | $R = \rho / (2\pi r)$ | 2 |
| Equivalent radius for plate: $r_{eq} = \sqrt{A / (2\pi)}$ | — | 1 |
| Substitute and simplify to final boxed form | $R = (\rho/4)\sqrt{\pi/A}$ | 2 |

---

### ❓ Question B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** A copper plate of size 600 mm × 600 mm is used for plate earthing. The effective resistivity of the backfill is 12 Ω·m. Calculate the resistance to earth. If the maximum permissible earth resistance is 5 Ω, determine how many such plates (in parallel) are required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare plate earthing and pipe earthing. State at least four points of difference. State two advantages and one limitation of plate earthing. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Plate size: $a = b = 0.6 \, \text{m}$
- Backfill resistivity: $\rho = 12 \, \Omega\!\cdot\!m$
- Permissible resistance: $R_{\text{perm}} = 5 \, \Omega$

**Step 1 — Plate area:**

$$
A = 0.6 \times 0.6 = 0.36 \, \text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Resistance of a single plate:**

$$
R_1 = \dfrac{\rho}{4} \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{A}} = \dfrac{12}{4} \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{0.36}}
$$

$$
\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{0.36}} = \sqrt{8.7267} = 2.9541
$$

$$
R_1 = 3 \times 2.9541 = 8.86 \, \Omega
$$

> Since $R_1 = 8.86 \, \Omega > 5 \, \Omega$, **one plate is NOT sufficient**.

**Step 3 — For $n$ plates in parallel:**

$$
R_n = \dfrac{R_1}{n} \leq 5 \, \Omega
$$

$$
n \geq \dfrac{8.86}{5} = 1.77
$$

> Since $n$ must be an integer, we round up: $\boxed{n = 2}$ plates in parallel.

**Verification:** $R_2 = 8.86 / 2 = 4.43 \, \Omega$ ≤ 5 Ω. ✅

**Part (b) — Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| # | Parameter | Plate Earthing | Pipe Earthing |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Electrode | Rectangular plate (600 × 600 mm) | GI pipe (40 mm dia × 2.5–3 m long) |
| 2 | Contact area with soil | Large (0.36 m²) | Smaller (surface area of pipe) |
| 3 | Earth resistance | Lower for the same depth | Higher for the same depth |
| 4 | Installation | More excavation, more material | Less excavation, easier in rocky soil |
| 5 | Cost | Higher | Lower |
| 6 | Best suited for | Domestic, large substations, soft soil | Rocky terrain, where deep boring is easier |
| 7 | Maintenance | Needs periodic watering | Less frequent watering needed |

**Two advantages of plate earthing:**
1. Provides very low and stable earth resistance due to large plate area.
2. Long service life (30+ years) when properly maintained with salt + water.

**One limitation of plate earthing:**
1. Requires deep excavation (3 m) which is impractical in rocky terrain or in built-up urban areas with underground services.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on plate earthing questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the area formula in m².** A common mistake is to plug in 600 × 600 = 360000 and forget to convert mm to m. Always convert to SI units FIRST. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
> 2. **Using the natural soil resistivity instead of backfill resistivity.** The plate resistance depends on the **backfill** resistivity (~5–10 Ω·m), NOT the natural soil. The examiner WILL deduct 1 mark for this confusion.
> 3. **Forgetting to round up $n$ in parallel-plate problems.** If the calculation gives $n = 1.77$, the answer is **2 plates**, not 1.77. Part-plates don't exist. **(−1 mark)**
> 4. **Writing the formula $R = \rho / (2\pi r)$ for a plate.** This is the **hemispherical** formula. The plate formula is $R = (\rho/4)\sqrt{\pi/A}$. They are NOT the same.
> 5. **Not drawing the watering pipe and CI cover in the sketch.** KTU evaluators specifically check for the GI pipe and the cast iron cover. **(−1 mark)**
> 6. **Writing the unit as "Ω·m" for resistance.** Resistance is in **Ω (ohms)**, NOT Ω·m. The latter is resistivity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Plate earthing** = vertical metallic plate (Cu or GI) buried at **1.5–3 m depth**, surrounded by **charcoal + salt** layers.
- ✅ Standard Cu plate: **600 mm × 600 mm × 3.15 mm**. Standard GI plate: **600 mm × 600 mm × 6 mm**.
- ✅ **Master formula (memorize verbatim):** $R = \dfrac{\rho}{4} \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{A}}$ — derived from hemispherical electrode equivalence.
- ✅ $A$ is **area in m²**; $\rho$ is **backfill resistivity in Ω·m**; $R$ is in **Ω**.
- ✅ Backfill resistivity ≈ **5–10 Ω·m**; natural soil resistivity ≈ **100–1000 Ω·m** in Kerala laterite.
- ✅ **Permissible earth resistance:** ≤ 1 Ω (substations), ≤ 5 Ω (domestic/commercial), ≤ 0.5 Ω (hospital/sensitive electronics).
- ✅ **Multiple plates in parallel:** $1/R_T = \sum (1/R_i)$. Round UP the number of plates to the next integer.
- ✅ Components you MUST draw in the sketch: **plate, lug, earthing conductor, charcoal layers, salt layers, GI watering pipe, funnel, CI cover**.
- ✅ **Maintenance:** Periodic watering through the GI pipe (20 L every 2–3 months in Kerala's dry season).
- ✅ **Code reference:** IS 3043:1987 (Reaffirmed 2018) — Code of Practice for Earthing.
- ✅ KSEB (Kerala State Electricity Board) standard requires **a single plate earth** for domestic connections up to 5 kW; **two plates in parallel** for connections above 5 kW.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pipe Earthing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pipe Earthing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pipe Earthing** is a type of earthing (grounding) system in which a **Galvanized Iron (GI) pipe** is buried vertically into the ground, with its top end projecting above the ground surface, to serve as the **earth electrode**. The pipe is surrounded by alternating layers of **charcoal (coke)** and **salt** to maintain low earth resistance by retaining moisture, and is connected to the electrical installation's earth continuity conductor through a GI wire.

According to **IS 3043:2018** (Code of Practice for Earthing) and standard electrical engineering practice, pipe earthing is classified as a **driven earth electrode** system, where the electrode is driven or buried vertically to a depth sufficient to reach permanently moist soil strata.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Pipe earthing is the **most commonly used method** for domestic and small commercial installations in India, as it provides a low and stable earth resistance, is easy to install, and is cost-effective for voltages up to **650V** in normal soil conditions.

## 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Earth Electrode** | A conductor or group of conductors in intimate contact with the earth, providing an electrical connection to the general mass of earth. |
| **Earth Resistance** | The resistance offered by the earth electrode to the passage of fault current into the surrounding soil, measured in **ohms (Ω)**. |
| **GI Pipe** | Galvanized Iron pipe — a steel pipe coated with zinc to prevent corrosion; serves as the primary earth electrode. |
| **Charcoal (Coke)** | Carbon-rich material that retains moisture around the earth electrode and improves electrical conductivity of the soil. |
| **Salt (NaCl)** | Sodium chloride that absorbs moisture from the atmosphere and the surrounding soil, reducing soil resistivity. |
| **Alternate Layer** | Successive horizontal layers of charcoal and salt placed around the pipe electrode in the pit. |
| **Earthing Pit** | A pit dug into the ground (typically 1.25 m to 4.5 m deep) where the pipe electrode and its surrounding layers are placed. |
| **GI Wire / Strip** | The connecting conductor between the earth electrode and the equipment's earth terminal. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Imagine a Tree in Soil:**

Think of pipe earthing like a **tree's root system planted in a special "moisture garden"**:

- The **GI pipe** is like the **trunk of the tree** — the main structure that channels electrical current (the "water/nutrients" in this analogy) into the ground.
- The **charcoal and salt layers** around the pipe are like **rich, moist soil** packed around the tree's roots. Just as a tree needs moist soil to absorb water, the earth electrode needs a moist, conductive environment to dissipate fault current efficiently into the ground.
- The **earth wire** (the GI wire or strip from the pipe to the equipment) is like the **pipeline** that carries water from the source to the tree's leaves — it safely transports the fault current to the earth electrode.
- The **ground (soil)** itself is the **vast ocean** into which all the water eventually flows — the infinite reservoir that absorbs all the electrical energy safely.

If you wrap the pipe in dry, sandy soil (high resistivity), the "tree" cannot effectively transfer current to the ground, just as a tree in a desert would wither. By packing the pipe with **charcoal and salt**, we artificially create a **high-moisture, high-conductivity zone** that ensures the earth resistance remains low and stable throughout the year, even in dry seasons.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why charcoal and salt together?** Charcoal is **porous** and retains moisture for long periods without decomposing. Salt is **hygroscopic** — it actively absorbs moisture from the air and soil. Together, they form a **self-sustaining moist environment** around the earth electrode, keeping the soil resistance low for **3 to 5 years** before requiring maintenance (replenishment of salt).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of Pipe Earthing — Detailed Breakdown

A complete pipe earthing installation consists of the following components, each with a specific engineering purpose:

### 2.1.1 GI Pipe (Earth Electrode)

- **Material:** Galvanized Iron (mild steel pipe with hot-dip zinc coating)
- **Standard Diameter:** **40 mm (1.5 inches)** for ordinary soil conditions; **50 mm to 100 mm** for rocky or high-resistivity soil
- **Standard Length:** **2.75 m (9 feet)** for normal soil; may extend up to **4.5 m** in dry or rocky terrain
- **Holes:** The bottom end of the pipe is drilled with **12 mm diameter holes** (typically 6 to 8 holes) to allow moisture seepage into the pipe
- **Top End:** A **GI reducing socket** with a **GI funnel** is fitted on top of the pipe above ground level for periodic water pouring (maintenance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Point:** The standard pipe dimensions for domestic installations are **40 mm diameter × 2.75 m length** for soil with resistivity up to **300 Ω-m**. Always quote this standard in numerical problems unless specified otherwise.

### 2.1.2 Charcoal (Coke Breeze)

- **Layer Thickness:** Approximately **75 mm to 100 mm** thick around the pipe
- **Function:**
  1. Retains moisture for long durations due to its **porous structure**
  2. Increases the effective surface area of the electrode
  3. Prevents chemical reaction between the GI pipe and the salt layer
  4. Maintains a **stable low-resistance zone** around the electrode

### 2.1.3 Salt (Sodium Chloride — NaCl)

- **Layer Thickness:** Approximately **75 mm to 100 mm** thick, placed as alternate layers with charcoal
- **Function:**
  1. Absorbs atmospheric moisture (hygroscopic property) → keeps soil moist
  2. Dissolves in moisture → creates a **conductive electrolyte** that reduces soil resistivity
  3. The ionic dissociation (Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions) dramatically lowers the electrical resistance of the surrounding soil
- **Maintenance:** Salt must be **replenished every 2 to 3 years** as it gradually washes away with rainwater

### 2.1.4 Alternate Layer Arrangement

The layers are placed in the pit in the following sequence from bottom to top:

1. **Bottom layer:** Charcoal (~100 mm thick)
2. **Second layer:** Salt (~100 mm thick)
3. **Third layer:** Charcoal (~100 mm thick)
4. **Fourth layer:** Salt (~100 mm thick)
5. **Top layer:** Charcoal (~100 mm thick)

> The pipe sits in the **center** of these layers, surrounded on all sides by this moisture-retentive medium.

### 2.1.5 GI Wire / Earth Continuity Conductor

- **Material:** Galvanized Iron wire (for pipe earthing specifically, unlike plate earthing which uses GI strip)
- **Size:** For a 40 mm pipe: **No. 6 SWG (≈ 5 mm diameter)** GI wire is standard
- **Connection:** The wire is firmly bolted to the pipe using a **GI clamp and nut-bolt assembly** at the top of the pipe, above ground level
- **Routing:** The wire is run through a **GI pipe sleeve** (conduit) from the earth pit to the equipment being earthed, providing mechanical protection

### 2.1.6 Earthing Pit

- **Depth:** At least **1.25 m** below ground level, but the pipe is driven to **2.75 m** or more
- **Diameter:** Approximately **300 mm to 500 mm** wider than the pipe diameter
- **Cover:** A **CI (Cast Iron) cover or concrete slab** is placed at ground level over the pit for safety and protection

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Engineering Logic

| Step | What Happens | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fault current flows from the equipment body to the earth wire | The earth wire provides a low-impedance path to safely carry fault current to the earth electrode |
| 2 | Current enters the GI pipe through the bolted connection | The pipe acts as a large-surface-area conductor, distributing current radially into the surrounding soil |
| 3 | Current passes through the salt-charcoal layers | The moist, ion-rich environment provides a **low-resistance transition zone** between the metallic electrode and the bulk soil |
| 4 | Current dissipates into the general mass of earth | The earth acts as an infinite current sink with negligible potential rise, ensuring the equipment body remains at near-zero potential relative to earth |

## 2.3 Factors Affecting Earth Resistance in Pipe Earthing

The earth resistance of a pipe electrode depends on:

1. **Soil resistivity (ρ):** Higher ρ → Higher resistance. Clay soil (low ρ) is best; rocky/sandy soil (high ρ) is worst.
2. **Depth of burial:** Deeper electrode reaches moist strata → Lower resistance.
3. **Diameter of pipe:** Larger diameter → Slightly lower resistance (minor effect).
4. **Length of pipe:** Longer pipe → Lower resistance (major effect, roughly inversely proportional).
5. **Moisture content:** Higher moisture → Lower resistance.
6. **Temperature:** Below 0°C, moisture freezes → Resistance increases sharply.
7. **Salt and charcoal layers:** Dramatically reduce effective soil resistivity near the electrode.
8. **Number of electrodes:** Multiple pipes in parallel → Lower combined resistance.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$$ | Resistance of a single **rod/pipe electrode** driven into uniform soil | $R$ in Ω, $\rho$ in Ω-m, $L$ in m, $d$ in m |
| $$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{8L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$$ | Resistance when the **top of the electrode is at ground level** (more commonly used) | Same as above |
| $$\rho = 2\pi a R$$ | Apparent soil resistivity (Wenner method, 4-probe) — $a$ = electrode spacing, $R$ = measured resistance | $\rho$ in Ω-m |
| $$R_{\text{total}} = \frac{R_1 \times R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$ | Combined resistance of **two** pipe electrodes in parallel (if spaced > 2× length apart, no mutual resistance) | Ω |
| $$R_n = \frac{R_1}{n}$$ | Combined resistance of $n$ identical electrodes in parallel (ideal case, no mutual coupling) | Ω |
| $$R_n = \frac{R_1}{n} \left(1 + \frac{\lambda a}{R_1} \cdot k \right)$$ | More accurate formula with mutual resistance correction; $\lambda$ = mutual resistance factor, $a$ = spacing, $k$ = correction coefficient | Ω |
| $$V_{\text{touch}} = I_{\text{fault}} \times R_{\text{earth}}$$ | Touch potential (voltage across body from equipment to earth surface at the fault time) | Volts |
| $$V_{\text{step}} = I_{\text{fault}} \times R_{\text{step}}$$ | Step potential (voltage between two feet, 1 m apart) | Volts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Reference — Standard Dimensions for KTU Problems:**
> - Pipe diameter: **40 mm (0.04 m)**
> - Pipe length: **2.75 m**
> - Pit depth: **3.5 m to 4.5 m** (pipe embedded with at least 1.25 m below ground)
> - Charcoal layer: **75–100 mm thick**
> - Salt layer: **75–100 mm thick**
> - GI wire: **No. 6 SWG** for 40 mm pipe

## 2.5 Standard Earth Resistance Values (As per IS 3043)

| Installation Type | Maximum Permissible Earth Resistance |
|---|---|
| Major power stations | **0.5 Ω** |
| Major substations | **1.0 Ω** |
| Small substations / industrial installations | **2.0 Ω** |
| Commercial buildings | **5.0 Ω** |
| Domestic installations (LT) | **8.0 Ω** (or less) |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

Pipe earthing is the **workhorse of low-voltage electrical installations** in India:

- **Residential buildings:** Every home meter board has a pipe earth for the neutral grounding and equipment body earthing.
- **Commercial complexes:** Used for earthing distribution boards, lifts, HVAC panels.
- **Small industries:** Motor bodies, control panels, welding machines.
- **Telecom towers:** BTS sites use pipe earthing in conjunction with chemical earthing.
- **Solar PV installations:** Lightning protection and inverter earthing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In modern practice, **chemical earthing** (using conductive backfill compounds like bentonite or Marconite) is increasingly replacing traditional charcoal-salt pipe earthing due to lower maintenance and more consistent performance. However, **KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests pipe earthing** with charcoal and salt — master the traditional method first.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Earth Resistance Formula for a Pipe Electrode

The standard formula for the resistance of a vertically driven pipe (or rod) electrode is derived from the concept of **radial current flow** in a semi-infinite homogeneous soil medium. We use the principle that current flows radially outward from the electrode surface into the surrounding earth.

### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Problem

Consider a pipe electrode of:
- Length = $L$ (m)
- Diameter = $d$ (m), so radius $r = d/2$
- Buried with its **top at the ground surface** in a homogeneous soil of resistivity $\rho$ (Ω-m)

At a distance $x$ from the center of the pipe, the current flows through a **cylindrical shell** of soil with:
- Surface area: $A = 2\pi x L$ (lateral surface of a cylinder of length $L$ and radius $x$)
- Thickness: $dx$

The resistance of this thin cylindrical shell is:

$$dR = \frac{\rho \, dx}{2\pi x L}$$

### 3.1.2 Integration

The total resistance from the pipe surface (radius $r = d/2$) to infinity is found by integrating:

$$R = \int_{d/2}^{\infty} \frac{\rho \, dx}{2\pi x L}$$

$$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \int_{d/2}^{\infty} \frac{dx}{x}$$

$$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln(x) \right]_{d/2}^{\infty}$$

$$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln(\infty) - \ln\left(\frac{d}{2}\right) \right]$$

This is infinite because we integrated to infinity. In practice, current effectively flows within a finite "effective radius." For a pipe electrode with the top at the surface, empirical and rigorous analysis give the corrected formula:

$$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{8L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$$

### 3.1.3 Deriving the Alternative Form (Top of Pipe at Ground Level)

If the top of the pipe is **at ground level**, rigorous analysis using the method of images gives:

$$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$$

The **difference of $\ln(2) \approx 0.693$** between the two formulas accounts for the image electrode effect (the actual electrode has an "image" below the ground surface, effectively doubling the dissipation path).

### 3.1.4 Worked Example 1 — Basic Calculation

**Problem:** A pipe electrode of **40 mm diameter** and **2.75 m length** is driven into soil of resistivity **100 Ω-m**. The top of the pipe is at ground level. Calculate the earth resistance.

**Given:**
- $d = 40 \text{ mm} = 0.04 \text{ m}$
- $L = 2.75 \text{ m}$
- $\rho = 100 \text{ Ω-m}$

**Step 1:** Select the correct formula (top at ground level):

$$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 2:** Compute the argument of the logarithm:

$$\frac{4L}{d} = \frac{4 \times 2.75}{0.04} = \frac{11.0}{0.04} = 275$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the logarithm:

$$\ln(275) = \ln(100) + \ln(2.75) = 4.6052 + 1.0116 = 5.6168$$

**Step 4:** Subtract 1:

$$5.6168 - 1 = 4.6168$$

**Step 5:** Compute the denominator:

$$2\pi L = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 2.75 = 17.279$$

**Step 6:** Calculate R:

$$R = \frac{100}{17.279} \times 4.6168 = 5.787 \times 4.6168 = 26.72 \text{ Ω}$$

**Result:** $R \approx 26.7 \text{ Ω}$

This is **too high** for domestic use (< 8 Ω), so multiple electrodes or longer pipe would be needed.

### 3.1.5 Worked Example 2 — Effect of Pipe Length

**Problem:** If the pipe length is increased to **4.0 m** (keeping diameter and soil same), what is the new resistance?

**Given:**
- $d = 0.04$ m, $L = 4.0$ m, $\rho = 100$ Ω-m

**Step 1:**

$$\frac{4L}{d} = \frac{4 \times 4.0}{0.04} = 400$$

**Step 2:**

$$\ln(400) = \ln(100) + \ln(4) = 4.6052 + 1.3863 = 5.9915$$

**Step 3:**

$$5.9915 - 1 = 4.9915$$

**Step 4:**

$$2\pi L = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 4.0 = 25.133$$

**Step 5:**

$$R = \frac{100}{25.133} \times 4.9915 = 3.9789 \times 4.9915 = 19.86 \text{ Ω}$$

**Result:** $R \approx 19.9$ Ω — Still high. **Length alone is insufficient**; we need multiple pipes or better soil treatment.

### 3.1.6 Worked Example 3 — Multiple Pipes in Parallel

**Problem:** Two identical pipe electrodes, each with $R_1 = 26.7$ Ω (from Example 1), are placed in parallel at a spacing of **6 m** (which is > 2L = 5.5 m, so mutual resistance is negligible). Find the combined resistance.

**Given:**
- $R_1 = R_2 = 26.7$ Ω
- Mutual resistance negligible (spacing > 2L)

**Step 1:** Apply parallel resistance formula:

$$R_{\text{total}} = \frac{R_1 \times R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{26.7 \times 26.7}{26.7 + 26.7}$$

$$R_{\text{total}} = \frac{712.89}{53.4} = 13.35 \text{ Ω}$$

**Result:** $R_{\text{total}} \approx 13.35$ Ω

This is better but still above 8 Ω. For **4 pipes** in parallel:

$$R_4 = \frac{R_1}{4} = \frac{26.7}{4} = 6.675 \text{ Ω}$$

This now satisfies the domestic earthing requirement of < 8 Ω.

### 3.1.7 Worked Example 4 — Effect of Soil Treatment (Effective Resistivity)

**Problem:** After adding salt and charcoal layers, the effective soil resistivity around a 40 mm × 2.75 m pipe drops from **100 Ω-m** to **40 Ω-m**. Calculate the new earth resistance.

**Given:**
- $d = 0.04$ m, $L = 2.75$ m, $\rho_{\text{eff}} = 40$ Ω-m

**Step 1:**

$$R = \frac{40}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 2.75} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4 \times 2.75}{0.04}\right) - 1 \right]$$

$$R = \frac{40}{17.279} \times 4.6168$$

$$R = 2.3148 \times 4.6168 = 10.69 \text{ Ω}$$

**Result:** $R \approx 10.69$ Ω

The salt-charcoal treatment reduced the resistance from **26.7 Ω to 10.69 Ω** — a **60% reduction** — demonstrating the critical importance of the alternate layer system.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Parameters

| Parameter | Change | Effect on Earth Resistance | Quantitative Impact (Example 1 base: 26.7 Ω) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pipe length (L) | Increase 2.75 → 4.0 m | **Decreases** | 26.7 Ω → 19.9 Ω (−25.5%) |
| Pipe diameter (d) | Increase 40 → 50 mm | **Decreases** (minor) | 26.7 Ω → 24.1 Ω (−9.7%) |
| Soil resistivity (ρ) | Decrease 100 → 40 Ω-m (treatment) | **Decreases** (major) | 26.7 Ω → 10.7 Ω (−60%) |
| Number of pipes (n) | 1 → 2 (parallel, no mutual R) | **Decreases** | 26.7 Ω → 13.4 Ω (−50%) |
| Number of pipes (n) | 1 → 4 (parallel, no mutual R) | **Decreases** | 26.7 Ω → 6.7 Ω (−75%) |
| Moisture content | Increase (after rain/treatment) | **Decreases** | Can reduce by 30–50% |

## 3.3 Numerical Problem — KTU Board Exam Pattern

**Problem:** A pipe earthing installation uses a **GI pipe of 50 mm outer diameter and 3.0 m length**, driven vertically with its top at ground level. The soil resistivity is **200 Ω-m**. Calculate:

(a) The earth resistance of a single pipe.
(b) The combined resistance if **three** such pipes are connected in parallel with spacing of **8 m** (assume negligible mutual resistance).
(c) Comment on whether this installation meets the IS 3043 standard for a small substation (≤ 2 Ω).

### Part (a): Single Pipe Resistance

**Given:** $d = 0.05$ m, $L = 3.0$ m, $\rho = 200$ Ω-m

**Formula:**

$$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 1:** Compute $\frac{4L}{d}$:

$$\frac{4 \times 3.0}{0.05} = \frac{12.0}{0.05} = 240$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate logarithm:

$$\ln(240) = \ln(24 \times 10) = \ln(24) + \ln(10) = 3.1781 + 2.3026 = 5.4807$$

**Step 3:** Subtract 1:

$$5.4807 - 1 = 4.4807$$

**Step 4:** Compute denominator:

$$2\pi L = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 3.0 = 18.850$$

**Step 5:** Calculate R:

$$R = \frac{200}{18.850} \times 4.4807 = 10.610 \times 4.4807 = 47.54 \text{ Ω}$$

**Answer (a):** $R_{\text{single}} \approx 47.5$ Ω

### Part (b): Three Pipes in Parallel

**Given:** $n = 3$, spacing = 8 m, $2L = 6$ m, so spacing > $2L$ → mutual resistance is negligible.

**Formula:**

$$R_{\text{total}} = \frac{R_1}{n} = \frac{47.54}{3} = 15.85 \text{ Ω}$$

**Answer (b):** $R_{\text{total}} \approx 15.85$ Ω

### Part (c): Compliance Check

For a small substation, IS 3043 requires $R \leq 2.0$ Ω. The achieved value of **15.85 Ω** is **7.9 times higher** than the standard. 

**Conclusion:** The installation **does NOT meet** the IS 3043 requirement. **Recommendations:**
1. Increase pipe length to 5–6 m, or
2. Add more pipes (need $n = 47.54/2 \approx 24$ pipes — impractical), or
3. Apply soil treatment with salt and charcoal to reduce $\rho$ to ~20 Ω-m, which would give:

$$R_{\text{new}} = \frac{20}{200} \times 47.54 = 4.75 \text{ Ω} \quad \text{(still not sufficient)}$$

4. Use **chemical earthing** with bentonite backfill ($\rho \approx 2$ Ω-m):

$$R_{\text{new}} = \frac{2}{200} \times 47.54 = 0.475 \text{ Ω} \quad \text{(meets standard)}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete Pipe Earthing Installation — Cross-Sectional View

The following block diagram represents the **layered architecture** of a pipe earthing system, showing all components, their spatial relationships, and the flow of fault current from the equipment to the general mass of earth.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EQUIP["ELECTRICAL EQUIPMENT (e.g., Motor / DB / Panel)"]
        EQ[Equipment Metal Body / Neutral Point]
    end

    subgraph ABOVE["ABOVE GROUND LEVEL"]
        WIRE1[GI Earth Wire — No. 6 SWG]
        CLAMP[GI Clamp + Nut-Bolt Connection]
        FUNNEL[GI Funnel with Mesh Cover]
        SOCKET[GI Reducing Socket]
        SLEEVE[GI Conduit Sleeve — Mechanical Protection]
    end

    subgraph PIT["EARTHING PIT (Below Ground)"]
        PIPE[GI Pipe 40mm dia x 2.75m length<br/>with 12mm holes at bottom]
        L5[Charcoal Layer — 100mm]
        L4[Salt Layer — 100mm]
        L3[Charcoal Layer — 100mm]
        L2[Salt Layer — 100mm]
        L1[Charcoal Layer — 100mm — Bottom]
        SOIL[Natural Soil — General Mass of Earth]
    end

    subgraph CURRENT["FAULT CURRENT FLOW PATH"]
        DIR1[Step 1: Equipment body to GI wire]
        DIR2[Step 2: GI wire to pipe via clamp]
        DIR3[Step 3: Pipe through salt-charcoal layers]
        DIR4[Step 4: Dissipation into general earth]
    end

    EQ -->|Fault Current| WIRE1
    WIRE1 -->|Through Sleeve| CLAMP
    CLAMP -->|Bolted Joint| SOCKET
    SOCKET --> PIPE
    FUNNEL -->|Water Pouring for Maintenance| SOCKET
    SLEEVE -->|Protects| WIRE1

    PIPE --> L5
    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
    L1 --> SOIL

    EQ -.-> DIR1
    DIR1 -.-> DIR2
    DIR2 -.-> DIR3
    DIR3 -.-> DIR4

    style EQ fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    style PIPE fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style L5 fill:#2F2F2F,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style L4 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#0000FF,color:#000
    style L3 fill:#2F2F2F,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
```

## 4.2 Step-by-Step Construction Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Mark Location and Dig Pit<br/>300mm dia x 3.5 to 4.5m deep]
    S2[Step 2: Drive GI Pipe<br/>40mm dia x 2.75m vertical, top above ground]
    S3[Step 3: Drill 12mm holes<br/>at bottom of pipe for moisture]
    S4[Step 4: Add Bottom Charcoal Layer<br/>100mm thick]
    S5[Step 5: Add First Salt Layer<br/>100mm thick]
    S6[Step 6: Add Alternate Layers<br/>up to ground level]
    S7[Step 7: Connect GI Wire<br/>No. 6 SWG via clamp]
    S8[Step 8: Fit Funnel and Socket<br/>for water pouring maintenance]
    S9[Step 9: Cover Pit<br/>with CI cover or concrete slab]
    S10[Step 10: Test Earth Resistance<br/>using earth tester]

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 --> S9 --> S10

    style S1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style S2 fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style S3 fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style S4 fill:#2F2F2F,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style S5 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#0000FF,color:#000
    style S6 fill:#2F2F2F,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style S7 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style S8 fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style S9 fill:#808080,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style S10 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Equivalent Electrical Circuit Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[AC Mains / Phase Supply] -->|Line Conductor| EQUIP[Equipment Terminal]
    EQUIP -->|Body to Earth Wire| R_WIRE[Resistance of GI Wire — Negligible]
    R_WIRE -->|Clamp Joint| R_PIPE[Pipe-to-Soil Contact Resistance]
    R_PIPE -->|Salt-Charcoal Zone| R_TREAT[Treated Soil Resistance]
    R_TREAT -->|Bulk Soil| R_BULK[Bulk Earth Resistance]
    R_BULK -->|Neutral Plane| NEUTRAL[General Mass of Earth — 0V Reference]
    EQUIP -.->|Fault Path| R_FAULT[Body Fault Resistance]
    R_FAULT -.-> R_WIRE

    style SRC fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style EQUIP fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style R_WIRE fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style R_PIPE fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style R_TREAT fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style R_BULK fill:#8B7355,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style NEUTRAL fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style R_FAULT fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The total earth resistance $R_{\text{earth}} = R_{\text{wire}} + R_{\text{pipe}} + R_{\text{treat}} + R_{\text{bulk}}$. The **dominant component** is $R_{\text{bulk}}$ (bulk soil resistance) and $R_{\text{treat}}$ (which we minimize with salt and charcoal). The wire and pipe metallic resistances are negligible compared to soil resistance.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q: Define Pipe Earthing. List any four standard components used in a pipe earthing installation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Pipe Earthing** is a method of earthing in which a **Galvanized Iron (GI) pipe** of standard dimensions (typically **40 mm diameter** and **2.75 m length**) is buried vertically into the ground to act as the earth electrode. The pipe is surrounded by alternate layers of **charcoal and salt** to maintain low earth resistance.

**Four Standard Components:**
1. **GI Pipe** (40 mm dia × 2.75 m) — serves as the earth electrode
2. **GI Wire** (No. 6 SWG) — connects the pipe to the equipment earth terminal
3. **Charcoal (Coke)** — retains moisture around the pipe
4. **Salt (NaCl)** — absorbs moisture and reduces soil resistivity

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q: Why are alternate layers of charcoal and salt used around the pipe electrode in pipe earthing? Explain the role of each.**

**Model Answer:**

The alternate layers of charcoal and salt are used to **maintain a low and stable earth resistance** by creating a moisture-retentive, conductive zone around the pipe electrode.

**Role of Charcoal:**
- It is **porous** and retains moisture for long periods without decomposing
- It increases the effective surface area of the electrode
- It prevents direct chemical reaction between the salt and the GI pipe (which would cause corrosion)

**Role of Salt (NaCl):**
- It is **hygroscopic** — actively absorbs atmospheric moisture
- When dissolved in moisture, it **dissociates into Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions**, creating a conductive electrolyte
- This **reduces the effective soil resistivity** around the electrode, thereby lowering earth resistance

Together, the two materials ensure the earth resistance remains within permissible limits (< 8 Ω for domestic) even during dry seasons.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of **Pipe Earthing**. State the standard dimensions of the GI pipe used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A GI pipe electrode of **50 mm diameter** and **3.0 m length** is driven into soil of resistivity **150 Ω-m**, with the top of the pipe at ground level. Calculate:
  (i) The earth resistance of the single pipe. **(3 marks)**
  (ii) The combined resistance if **two** such pipes are connected in parallel with a spacing of **7 m**. **(2 marks)**
  (iii) Comment on whether the installation in part (ii) meets the IS 3043 standard for a domestic installation. **(2 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Construction and Working of Pipe Earthing

**Construction:**

A pipe earthing installation consists of the following components arranged as shown in the schematic:

1. **GI Pipe (Earth Electrode):** A **Galvanized Iron pipe of 40 mm outer diameter and 2.75 m length** is the standard size. For difficult soil, 50 mm to 100 mm diameter pipes of length up to 4.5 m may be used. The bottom end of the pipe is drilled with **12 mm diameter holes** to allow moisture seepage inside.

2. **Earthing Pit:** A pit of **300 mm to 500 mm diameter** is dug to a depth of **1.25 m to 4.5 m** below ground level. The pipe is placed vertically in the center of the pit.

3. **Alternate Layers of Charcoal and Salt:** Around the pipe, layers of **charcoal (coke)** and **salt (NaCl)** are placed alternately, each layer being approximately **75 mm to 100 mm thick**. Starting from the bottom: Charcoal → Salt → Charcoal → Salt → Charcoal.

4. **GI Wire (Earth Continuity Conductor):** A **No. 6 SWG GI wire** is firmly connected to the top of the pipe using a **GI clamp and nut-bolt assembly**. This wire runs through a **GI conduit sleeve** to the equipment being earthed.

5. **GI Funnel and Socket:** A **reducing socket** with a **GI funnel** is fitted on top of the pipe above ground level. Water can be poured periodically through the funnel to maintain moisture around the electrode.

6. **CI Cover:** A **Cast Iron cover or concrete slab** is placed at ground level to cover the pit and prevent accidental contact or ingress of debris.

**Working:**

When a fault occurs (e.g., a live wire touches the metal body of the equipment), the fault current flows from the equipment body → through the **GI earth wire** → to the **GI pipe electrode** via the clamp → through the **moist salt-charcoal layers** (which provide a low-resistance path) → dissipates into the **general mass of earth**. This keeps the equipment body at near-earth potential, protecting users from electric shock and preventing damage to equipment.

The earth resistance depends on:
- Soil resistivity ($\rho$)
- Length ($L$) and diameter ($d$) of the pipe
- Effectiveness of the salt-charcoal treatment
- Moisture content

> **Standard Dimensions Summary (for exam):**
> - Pipe: 40 mm dia × 2.75 m length
> - Pit: 300 mm dia × 3.5–4.5 m deep
> - Wire: No. 6 SWG GI
> - Layers: 75–100 mm each (charcoal/salt)

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation

**Given:**
- $d = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05$ m
- $L = 3.0$ m
- $\rho = 150$ Ω-m
- Top of pipe at ground level
- Spacing between two pipes = 7 m; $2L = 6$ m, so spacing > 2L → mutual resistance negligible

**Formula (top at ground level):**

$$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**(i) Single Pipe Resistance:**

Step 1 — Compute $\frac{4L}{d}$:

$$\frac{4L}{d} = \frac{4 \times 3.0}{0.05} = \frac{12.0}{0.05} = 240$$

Step 2 — Evaluate logarithm:

$$\ln(240) = 5.4807$$

Step 3 — Subtract 1:

$$5.4807 - 1 = 4.4807$$

Step 4 — Compute denominator:

$$2\pi L = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 3.0 = 18.850$$

Step 5 — Calculate R:

$$R = \frac{150}{18.850} \times 4.4807 = 7.958 \times 4.4807 = 35.66 \text{ Ω}$$

**Result (i):** $R_{\text{single}} \approx 35.66$ Ω

**(ii) Two Pipes in Parallel:**

$$R_{\text{total}} = \frac{R_1 \times R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{35.66 \times 35.66}{35.66 + 35.66} = \frac{35.66}{2} = 17.83 \text{ Ω}$$

**Result (ii):** $R_{\text{total}} \approx 17.83$ Ω

**(iii) Compliance Check:**

For a **domestic installation**, IS 3043 requires earth resistance **≤ 8 Ω** (or less).

The achieved value of **17.83 Ω** is **more than twice** the permissible limit. 

**Conclusion:** The installation **DOES NOT meet** the IS 3043 standard for domestic earthing.

**Recommended improvements:**
1. Increase the number of pipes to **at least 5** (since $5 \times 35.66/5 = 7.13$ Ω, which is acceptable)
2. Apply salt and charcoal treatment to reduce effective soil resistivity
3. Use longer pipes (5–6 m) to reach permanently moist strata

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **factors affecting earth resistance** in a pipe earthing system. Derive the expression for the earth resistance of a pipe electrode. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pipe earthing system uses a **40 mm diameter, 2.75 m long** GI pipe. The soil resistivity is **80 Ω-m** initially. After treatment with salt and charcoal, the effective resistivity drops to **30 Ω-m**. Calculate:
  (i) The earth resistance before treatment. **(2 marks)**
  (ii) The earth resistance after treatment. **(2 marks)**
  (iii) The **percentage reduction** in earth resistance due to treatment. **(1 mark)**
  (iv) If **two such treated pipes** are connected in parallel (spacing 6 m, no mutual resistance), what is the combined resistance? **(2 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Factors and Derivation

**Factors Affecting Earth Resistance of Pipe Electrode:**

1. **Soil Resistivity (ρ):** The most significant factor. Clay soil (low ρ) gives low resistance; sandy/rocky soil (high ρ) gives high resistance. Soil resistivity varies with moisture, temperature, and mineral content.

2. **Length of Pipe (L):** Earth resistance is **inversely proportional** to length. Doubling the length approximately halves the resistance. Longer pipes reach deeper, more permanently moist soil.

3. **Diameter of Pipe (d):** Resistance varies as $\ln(1/d)$ — a logarithmic dependence. Increasing diameter has a **minor effect** compared to length.

4. **Moisture Content:** Higher moisture in soil → lower resistivity → lower earth resistance. Below 0°C, moisture freezes and resistance increases sharply.

5. **Temperature:** Extreme cold (frozen soil) increases resistance; high temperatures cause moisture evaporation, also increasing resistance.

6. **Salt and Charcoal Treatment:** Adding salt and charcoal layers around the pipe dramatically reduces the **effective soil resistivity** in the immediate vicinity of the electrode, lowering total earth resistance.

7. **Number of Electrodes in Parallel:** Multiple pipes in parallel reduce the combined resistance (ideally $R/n$ for $n$ identical pipes, if spaced > 2L apart).

8. **Depth of Burial:** Deeper burial ensures the electrode is in contact with permanently moist subsoil.

9. **Seasonal Variations:** Resistance is lower in wet seasons and higher in dry seasons; salt-charcoal treatment helps stabilize this variation.

**Derivation of Earth Resistance Formula:**

[Refer to the detailed derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes]

The final formula for a pipe electrode with its top at ground level:

$$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**Brief Derivation Steps:**

Consider current flowing radially outward from a cylindrical electrode of length $L$ and radius $r = d/2$ in soil of resistivity $\rho$. The resistance of a thin cylindrical shell of radius $x$ and thickness $dx$ around the electrode is:

$$dR = \frac{\rho \, dx}{2\pi x L}$$

Integrating from the electrode surface to an effective radius and applying the method of images correction (for the semi-infinite soil domain above the electrode), we obtain:

$$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation

**Given:**
- $d = 40 \text{ mm} = 0.04$ m
- $L = 2.75$ m
- $\rho_1 = 80$ Ω-m (before treatment)
- $\rho_2 = 30$ Ω-m (after treatment)
- Top at ground level

**Formula:**

$$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**Pre-computation (common to all sub-parts):**

$$\frac{4L}{d} = \frac{4 \times 2.75}{0.04} = \frac{11.0}{0.04} = 275$$

$$\ln(275) = 5.6168$$

$$5.6168 - 1 = 4.6168$$

$$2\pi L = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 2.75 = 17.279$$

**Common factor:**

$$K = \frac{4.6168}{17.279} = 0.26721 \text{ (units: 1/m, when multiplied by ρ in Ω-m gives Ω)}$$

**(i) Earth Resistance Before Treatment ($\rho_1 = 80$ Ω-m):**

$$R_1 = K \times \rho_1 = 0.26721 \times 80 = 21.38 \text{ Ω}$$

**Result (i):** $R_1 \approx 21.38$ Ω

**(ii) Earth Resistance After Treatment ($\rho_2 = 30$ Ω-m):**

$$R_2 = K \times \rho_2 = 0.26721 \times 30 = 8.02 \text{ Ω}$$

**Result (ii):** $R_2 \approx 8.02$ Ω

**(iii) Percentage Reduction:**

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{R_1 - R_2}{R_1} \times 100 = \frac{21.38 - 8.02}{21.38} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{13.36}{21.38} \times 100 = 62.49\%$$

**Result (iii):** Percentage reduction $\approx 62.5\%$

**(iv) Two Treated Pipes in Parallel:**

$$R_{\text{parallel}} = \frac{R_2}{2} = \frac{8.02}{2} = 4.01 \text{ Ω}$$

**Result (iv):** $R_{\text{parallel}} \approx 4.01$ Ω — **This now meets the IS 3043 standard for domestic installations (≤ 8 Ω).**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Formula Selection Error:** Students often confuse the two formulas — with top at surface vs. top below surface. **Always read the problem carefully.** If the problem says "top of pipe is at ground level," use $\ln(4L/d) - 1$. If it says "pipe is fully buried with top below ground," use $\ln(8L/d) - 1$.
>
> 2. **Unit Conversion:** Forgetting to convert **mm to m** for diameter is the most common error. 40 mm = 0.04 m, NOT 40 m. Always check units.
>
> 3. **Logarithm Base:** $\ln$ is natural log (base $e$). Some students use $\log_{10}$ by mistake, which gives wrong answers. KTU expects $\ln$ in the earth resistance formula.
>
> 4. **Parallel Pipes — Mutual Resistance:** If spacing is **less than 2L**, the mutual resistance between electrodes cannot be neglected. The simplified $R/n$ formula is only valid when spacing **> 2L**.
>
> 5. **Standard Dimensions:** In 7-mark descriptive questions, students must state **40 mm diameter and 2.75 m length** as the standard, not vague "a pipe of suitable size." Examiners specifically look for these exact numbers.
>
> 6. **IS 3043 Limits:** Memorize the standard permissible earth resistance values (0.5 Ω for power stations, 1 Ω for substations, 2 Ω for small substations, 5 Ω for commercial, 8 Ω for domestic). A "comment" question without these numbers scores zero.
>
> 7. **Sketch in Descriptive Answers:** For the 7-mark construction question, **always draw a labelled diagram**. A well-labelled sketch carrying component names (GI pipe, charcoal, salt, GI wire, clamp, funnel, pit) can earn **2–3 marks** by itself even if the text explanation is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Pipe Earthing**

### 📌 Core Concepts
- [ ] Pipe earthing uses a **vertical GI pipe** as the earth electrode, surrounded by **alternate layers of charcoal and salt**.
- [ ] It is the **most common and economical** method for domestic and small commercial installations in India.
- [ ] The system is classified as a **driven earth electrode** under IS 3043:2018.

### 📌 Standard Dimensions (Memorize!)
- [ ] **GI Pipe:** 40 mm outer diameter × 2.75 m length (for normal soil)
- [ ] **Pit Depth:** 3.5 m to 4.5 m (pipe embedded at least 1.25 m below ground)
- [ ] **Pit Diameter:** 300 mm to 500 mm
