<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Substation Earthing** is the deliberate and engineered process of connecting the non-current carrying metallic parts of electrical equipment and a designated point in the power system (neutral of transformer, generator, etc.) to the general mass of earth through a low-impedance conducting path. This is achieved using **earth electrodes**, **earth conductors**, and an **earth mat (grid)** buried in soil.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (As per IE Rule 61, IS 3043 & IEEE Std 80):**
> Earthing is defined as "the connection of a conducting body to the earth so that charges may flow to and from the earth, thereby keeping the body at earth potential under normal and fault conditions."

### 1.2 Purpose of Substation Earthing

1. **Safety of Operating Personnel** – limits touch and step potentials to safe values.
2. **Equipment Protection** – provides low-impedance path for fault currents, enabling fast relay operation.
3. **Voltage Stabilization** – stabilizes system voltages with respect to ground.
4. **Overvoltage Protection** – drains lightning and switching surges safely to earth.
5. **Reference Point** – provides a stable zero-potential reference for the entire system.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – The "Water Drain" Concept:**
> Imagine a swimming pool. Earthing is exactly like the **drain pipe at the bottom of the pool**. Even if the pool fills up with excess water (fault current), the drain safely takes all the extra water away so that no one (person or equipment) gets harmed. The drain pipe must be **wide, short, and unobstructed (low resistance)** so that water flows out fast and smoothly. If the drain is clogged (high resistance), the pool overflows dangerously.
>
> Similarly, an **earth electrode** is like the "drain mouth," the **earth conductor** is the "drain pipe," and the **soil** acts as the "wastewater reservoir." A high-quality earthing design keeps the entire substation from becoming electrically "flooded" during a fault.

### 1.4 Key Terminology for KTU Examinations

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Earth Electrode** | A metal rod/plate/pipe buried in soil to make direct contact with earth |
| **Soil Resistivity (ρ)** | Resistance offered by soil of unit cross-section and unit length (Ω·m) |
| **Earth Resistance** | Resistance between the electrode and remote earth (Ω) |
| **Touch Potential** | Voltage between an energized object and the feet of a person touching it |
| **Step Potential** | Voltage between the feet of a person standing near an energized grounded object |
| **Earth Mat / Grid** | A network of horizontal conductors (rods/rods + plates) buried in the substation yard |
| **Equipotential Bonding** | Connecting all metallic parts to bring them to the same potential |

### 1.5 Physical Constants to Remember

- Standard human body resistance assumed = **1000 Ω**
- Body weight considered for step potential = **50 kg**
- Standard fault clearing time for design = **0.5 s** (or 1 s for slower breakers)
- Soil resistivity typical range = **10 Ω·m (moist clay) to 1000 Ω·m (dry sandy soil)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Earthing Systems

#### A) System Earthing (Neutral Earthing)
The deliberate connection of the **neutral point** of a power system to earth.

| Type | Description | Application |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Solid Earthing** | Neutral directly connected to earth without impedance | LV systems (415V), generators |
| **Resistance Earthing** | Neutral connected through a resistor (RE) | Generators, 11kV/33kV systems |
| **Reactance Earthing** | Neutral connected through a reactor (XE) | High-voltage transmission |
| **Arc Suppression Coil (Peterson Coil)** | Tuned to cancel capacitive earth-fault current | 33kV – 132kV systems |
| **Earthing Transformer Earthing** | Zig-zag or Star-Delta transformer used to create neutral | Systems without natural neutral |

#### B) Equipment Earthing (Protective Earthing)
Connecting the **non-current carrying metallic parts** (body of transformer, switchgear frame, panel enclosure, cable sheath, etc.) to earth for personnel safety.

### 2.2 Types of Earth Electrodes

1. **Pipe Electrode** – vertical galvanized iron (GI) pipe, 40 mm dia, 2.75–3 m long.
2. **Plate Electrode** – GI / Copper plate (60 cm × 60 cm), buried vertically.
3. **Rod Electrode** – copper-clad steel rod, 12.5–25 mm dia, 1.5–3 m long.
4. **Strip / Conductor Electrode** – GI strip of cross-section 25 mm × 3 mm, buried horizontally.
5. **Earth Mat / Grid** – combination of horizontal + vertical electrodes for substations.

### 2.3 Soil Resistivity – The Controlling Parameter

Soil resistivity depends on:
- **Moisture content** (decreases sharply with moisture up to 15–20% by weight, then stabilizes).
- **Temperature** (increases dramatically below 0°C due to ice formation).
- **Salt content / Mineral composition.**
- **Compactness** of soil (compact = lower ρ).
- **Seasonal variation** (winter > summer).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Why is soil resistivity measured? What factors affect it?"*
> Always mention that two-electrode (Wenner) or four-electrode method is used. Typical formula for **Wenner Method**:
>
> $$\rho = 2\pi a R$$
>
> where **a** is electrode spacing (m) and **R** is measured resistance (Ω).

### 2.4 IEEE Std 80 – The Substation Earthing Bible

For substation earthing, the design philosophy revolves around ensuring that the **touch potential (E_touch)** and **step potential (E_step)** experienced by a person during a fault are **within tolerable human limits**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEEE Std 80 Tolerable Limits (assumed body weight 70 kg, body resistance 1000 Ω, fault duration 0.5 s):**
> - Tolerable Step Potential ≈ **1170 V**
> - Tolerable Touch Potential ≈ **537 V**

These values are derived from the **Dalziel's formula** for ventricular fibrillation threshold.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Remarks |
|---|-----------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | Earth resistance of **Pipe Electrode** | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$ | L = length, d = diameter, ρ in Ω·m |
| 2 | Earth resistance of **Circular Plate** | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{4r}$ | r = radius of plate |
| 3 | Earth resistance of **Rectangular Plate** | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{8r}\left[1 + \dfrac{r}{\sqrt{A}}\right]$ | A = area, r = equivalent radius |
| 4 | Earth resistance of **Rod Electrode** | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$ | For a single driven rod |
| 5 | Earth resistance of **Horizontal Strip** | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{2L}{w}\right) + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{L}{h}\right) - 2 + \dfrac{2h}{L}\right]$ | w = width, h = depth of burial |
| 6 | Earth Grid resistance (Buried mesh) | $R_g = \rho\!\left[\dfrac{1}{L} + \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{20A}}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{1 + h\sqrt{20/A}}\right)\right]$ | A = grid area, L = total conductor length |
| 7 | Tolerable **Touch Potential** (IEEE 80) | $E_{\text{touch}} = \dfrac{1000 + 1.5\,C_s\,\rho_s}{\sqrt{t}}$ | $C_s$ = surface layer derating factor, $t$ = fault duration |
| 8 | Tolerable **Step Potential** (IEEE 80) | $E_{\text{step}} = \dfrac{1000 + 6\,C_s\,\rho_s}{\sqrt{t}}$ | Same parameters as above |
| 9 | Touch Potential inside substation (mesh) | $E_m = \dfrac{\rho\,I_g\,K_m\,K_i}{L}$ | $K_m$ = mesh factor, $K_i$ = irregularity factor |
| 10 | Step Potential inside substation | $E_s = \dfrac{\rho\,I_g\,K_s\,K_i}{L}$ | $K_s$ = step factor |
| 11 | Derating factor (Crushed rock) | $C_s = 1 - \dfrac{0.09\!\left(1 - \rho/\rho_s\right)}{2h_s + 0.09}$ | $h_s$ = thickness of surface layer |
| 12 | Maximum Grid Current | $I_g = D_f \times 3I_0$ | $D_f$ = decrement factor, $I_0$ = zero sequence current |
| 13 | Number of rods in parallel (if grid alone not sufficient) | $R_n = \dfrac{R_1 R_2}{R_2 + n R_1}$ (approx.) | $R_1$ = single rod resistance |
| 14 | Wenner Method Soil Resistivity | $\rho = 2\pi a R$ | a = probe spacing, R = measured resistance |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Utilities** – every generating station, EHV substation, and distribution transformer must be earthed.
- **Industrial Plants** – process plants use earth mats to ensure worker safety near switchgear.
- **Telecom Towers** – lightning protection depends on low-impedance earthing.
- **Hospital Operation Theatres** – TN-S earthing system for medical IT systems.
- **Data Centres** – equipotential bonding for sensitive IT loads.
- **Railway Traction** – return current earthing and OHE mast earthing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A well-designed substation earth electrode typically has an **earth resistance ≤ 0.5 Ω** for EHV substations, **≤ 1 Ω** for 33/11 kV substations, and **≤ 5 Ω** for distribution transformers. Lower is always safer!

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Earth Resistance of a Pipe Electrode

**Statement:** To derive the expression for the resistance offered by a vertical pipe electrode of length **L** and diameter **d** buried in soil of resistivity **ρ**.

**Assumptions:**
- The pipe is a perfect cylindrical conductor of negligible resistance.
- Current flows radially outward from the pipe into a homogeneous, isotropic soil of infinite extent.
- Remote earth is at zero potential.

**Step 1 – Consider a thin cylindrical shell of soil at radius *x* from the pipe axis, of thickness *dx* and length *L*.**

The current **I** flows outward through this shell. The cross-sectional area through which current flows is:
$$A = 2\pi x L$$

**Step 2 – The resistance *dR* of this elementary shell is given by:**

$$dR = \rho \cdot \dfrac{dx}{A} = \dfrac{\rho \, dx}{2\pi x L}$$

**Step 3 – Integrate from the surface of the pipe (at radius = d/2) to infinity (remote earth, at radius = ∞):**

$$R = \int_{d/2}^{\infty} \dfrac{\rho \, dx}{2\pi x L}$$

**Step 4 – Perform the integration:**

$$R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \int_{d/2}^{\infty} \dfrac{dx}{x} = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln(x)\right]_{d/2}^{\infty}$$

**Step 5 – Evaluate limits:**

$$R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln(\infty) - \ln(d/2)\right]$$

Since infinity is unbounded, the actual current does not extend to infinity. The effective distance is approximated as **4L** (a standard empirical result based on field practice and Dwight's formula). Substituting upper limit as 4L:

$$R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{d/2}\right)\right] = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right)\right]$$

**Step 6 – For a pipe of finite length, the empirical correction (accounting for end effects) reduces the expression to:**

$$\boxed{R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) - 1\right]}$$

This is the **standard IS 3043 / IEEE formula** for pipe electrode resistance.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Touch Potential (Simplified, IEEE 80)

**Concept:** Touch potential is the potential difference between the **hand** (touching a faulty structure) and the **feet** (standing on soil surface 1 m away).

**Step 1 – Voltage Division at the Body:**

A human can be modelled as a resistor. The voltage between hand and feet = $(I_b \times R_b)$, where $I_b$ = current through body, $R_b$ = 1000 Ω.

**Step 2 – Maximum permissible body current (Dalziel's Equation):**

$$I_b = \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{t}}$$

where K = 0.116 for body weight 70 kg (ventricular fibrillation threshold).

**Step 3 – Body current limits assuming the worst-case scenario (1 hand, 2 feet contact) is scaled:**

$$E_{\text{touch,tol}} = (R_b + 1.5\,C_s\,\rho_s) \times \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{t}}$$

**Step 4 – Substituting R_b = 1000 Ω and K = 0.116 A·s^0.5:**

$$E_{\text{touch,tol}} = \dfrac{1000 + 1.5\,C_s\,\rho_s}{\sqrt{t}} \quad \text{Volts}$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{touch}} = \dfrac{1000 + 1.5\,C_s\,\rho_s}{\sqrt{t}} \;\; \text{Volts}}$$

**Step 5 – Similarly for step potential (current path is foot-to-foot through 2 feet resistors in series):**

$$E_{\text{step,tol}} = \dfrac{1000 + 6\,C_s\,\rho_s}{\sqrt{t}} \quad \text{Volts}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

> **Problem:** A 33/11 kV substation has a grid area of 30 m × 20 m. Soil resistivity ρ = 200 Ω·m. Surface layer (crushed rock, 0.1 m thick) has ρ_s = 3000 Ω·m. Fault current = 5000 A, fault duration = 0.5 s. Verify if the design is safe.

**Solution Table:**

| Step | Computation | Result |
|------|-------------|--------|
| 1. Total grid area A | 30 × 20 | 600 m² |
| 2. Total conductor length L (assumed 4 parallel rods of 6 m + grid) | 4 × 6 = 24 m (assume 500 m total) | 500 m |
| 3. Grid resistance $R_g$ | Using formula | ≈ 4.5 Ω |
| 4. Decrement factor $D_f$ (typical for t = 0.5 s) | Standard tables | 1.0 |
| 5. Maximum grid current $I_g$ | 1.0 × 3 × 5000 / 3 ≈ | 5000 A |
| 6. GPR (Ground Potential Rise) | $I_g \times R_g$ = 5000 × 4.5 | **22,500 V** |
| 7. Derating factor $C_s$ | $1 - \dfrac{0.09(1 - 200/3000)}{2(0.1) + 0.09}$ | ≈ 0.74 |
| 8. Tolerable touch potential | $(1000 + 1.5 \times 0.74 \times 200) / \sqrt{0.5}$ | ≈ 1728 V |
| 9. Tolerable step potential | $(1000 + 6 \times 0.74 \times 200) / \sqrt{0.5}$ | ≈ 2670 V |
| 10. Actual mesh (touch) potential | $E_m = \rho I_g K_m K_i / L$ (assume $K_mK_i \approx 0.5$) | = 2000 V |
| 11. **Safety check** | Actual touch (2000 V) < Tolerable (1728 V) | ⚠️ Marginal! |

**Conclusion:** Design is marginal. Either increase conductor length L (add more grid rods), reduce grid resistance by using deeper ground rods, or add more surface layer thickness (crushed rock) to increase $C_s$ derating.

---

### 3.4 Quick Reference Matrix for KTU Exam

| Component | Earth Resistance Target (Ω) |
|-----------|-----------------------------|
| Large power station (EHV) | 0.1 – 0.5 |
| Major substation (132/66 kV) | 0.5 – 1.0 |
| 33/11 kV substation | 1.0 – 2.0 |
| Distribution transformer (11/0.4 kV) | 1.0 – 5.0 |
| Tower footings | 5 – 10 |
| Residential / commercial | ≤ 5 |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]

**"What is the purpose of substation earthing? List any four types of earth electrodes."**

**Model Answer:**

**Purpose of Substation Earthing:**
1. To provide safety to operating personnel by limiting touch and step potentials.
2. To provide a low-impedance path for fault currents so that protective relays operate quickly.
3. To stabilize system voltages and prevent overvoltages.
4. To drain lightning and switching surges safely to earth.
5. To prevent damage to equipment due to insulation failure.

**Four Types of Earth Electrodes:**
1. **Pipe electrode** – vertical GI pipe (40 mm dia, 2.75–3 m long).
2. **Plate electrode** – GI/Cu plate buried vertically (60 × 60 cm).
3. **Rod electrode** – copper-clad steel rod (12.5–25 mm dia, 1.5–3 m).
4. **Strip / conductor electrode** – GI strip (25 × 3 mm) buried horizontally.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]

**"Define soil resistivity. Explain briefly the Wenner four-electrode method for measuring it."**

**Model Answer:**

**Soil Resistivity (ρ):** The electrical resistance of a unit cube of soil (1 m × 1 m × 1 m), measured in Ω·m. It represents how strongly the soil opposes the flow of electric current.

**Wenner Four-Electrode Method:**
- Four electrodes are equally spaced in a straight line at distance **a** (typically 5–20 m).
- Outer two electrodes carry the test current **I**; inner two electrodes measure the potential **V**.
- The resistance **R = V / I** is measured.
- Soil resistivity is calculated using the formula:
$$\rho = 2\pi a R$$
- The probe spacing **a** is varied to obtain resistivity at different depths (deeper soil = larger 'a').

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Wenner method?** It eliminates the resistance of the measuring electrodes themselves, giving a more accurate value of true soil resistivity.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern]

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat sketch, explain the **plate earthing** method. Derive the expression for the earth resistance of a circular plate electrode.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A substation has a soil resistivity of 250 Ω·m. A plate electrode of size 60 cm × 60 cm is used. Calculate the earth resistance offered by the plate.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) – Plate Earthing Method & Derivation:**

**Plate Earthing Construction:**
- A GI (Galvanized Iron) or copper plate of size **60 cm × 60 cm × 6.35 mm** thick is used.
- The plate is buried vertically in the ground at a depth of **3 m** from the ground level.
- A GI pipe of 19 mm dia is bolted to the plate and brought above the surface for connection.
- The pit around the plate is filled with **alternate layers of charcoal and salt** (to reduce soil resistivity and retain moisture).
- Water is poured regularly to keep the surrounding soil moist.

**Diagram of Plate Earthing:**

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Equipment Body] --> B[GI Strip Earth Conductor]
    B --> C[GI Pipe Riser 19mm dia]
    C --> D[GI or Copper Plate 60cm x 60cm buried vertically]
    D --> E[Charcoal and Salt Layers around plate]
    E --> F[General Mass of Earth]
    G[Inspection Chamber] --> C
    H[Water Inlet Pipe] --> E
    
    style A fill:#2563eb,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#fbbf24,color:#000000
    style E fill:#78716c,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
```

**Derivation for Circular Plate Resistance:**

Consider a circular plate electrode of radius **r** buried in soil of resistivity **ρ**. Current flows radially outward from the plate.

Step 1: Consider a hemispherical shell of soil at radius **x** from the plate (acting as a centre).

Step 2: Surface area of the hemispherical shell:
$$A = 2\pi x^2$$

Step 3: Resistance of elementary shell of thickness *dx*:
$$dR = \rho \cdot \dfrac{dx}{2\pi x^2}$$

Step 4: Integrating from the surface of the plate (radius = r) to infinity:
$$R = \int_{r}^{\infty} \dfrac{\rho \, dx}{2\pi x^2} = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi}\left[-\dfrac{1}{x}\right]_{r}^{\infty}$$

Step 5: Evaluating limits:
$$R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi}\left[0 - \left(-\dfrac{1}{r}\right)\right] = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi r}$$

Step 6: In practice, due to field non-uniformities, the empirical formula used is:
$$\boxed{R = \dfrac{\rho}{4r}}$$

**Part (b) – Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:**
- Soil resistivity, ρ = 250 Ω·m
- Plate size = 60 cm × 60 cm
- Equivalent radius of square plate, $r = \dfrac{a}{2}\sqrt{2} = \dfrac{0.6}{2}\times\sqrt{2} = 0.3\sqrt{2} = 0.4243$ m
  (Or use r ≈ 0.6 m for square plate with empirical correction)

**Using $R = \rho / 4r$:**
$$R = \dfrac{250}{4 \times 0.4243} = \dfrac{250}{1.6972}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 147.3 \;\; \Omega}$$

**If the actual size of square plate is used with corner-to-centre radius (0.4243 m), the resistance is ~147 Ω. To bring it below 1 Ω (substation standard), we would need multiple plates in parallel.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:**
> - **DO NOT** directly use the square plate formula without converting to equivalent radius. Students often write `R = ρ/4a` and lose 2 marks.
> - Always convert square plate to equivalent circular radius first: **r = a/√2 × 1.13** or use the half-diagonal approach.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks – Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **pipe earthing** method with a neat diagram. List the materials used and state any two advantages of pipe earthing over plate earthing.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A 40 mm diameter, 3 m long GI pipe is used as an earth electrode. The soil resistivity is 300 Ω·m. Calculate the earth resistance. If the same resistance is to be achieved using a 6 mm diameter rod, what should be the length of the rod?

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) – Pipe Earthing Method:**

**Construction Details:**
- A GI pipe of **40 mm diameter, 2.75 m to 3.6 m long** is used.
- The pipe is placed vertically in the ground, with the top at least 20 cm below ground level.
- A tapered end (cone-shaped) is provided at the bottom for easy driving.
- Multiple holes are drilled at the bottom portion of the pipe to allow seepage of water.
- A **GI strip / wire** is securely bolted to the top of the pipe using a nut-bolt arrangement.
- The pit is filled with layers of **charcoal, salt, and sand/moist earth** for moisture retention.
- A **cast iron inspection chamber** with a cover is provided at the top for maintenance.

**Materials Used:**
1. GI pipe (40 mm dia, 2.75–3 m long)
2. GI nut, bolt, and washer
3. Charcoal (carbon)
4. Common salt (sodium chloride)
5. Sand / moist earth
6. GI earth strip (25 mm × 3 mm)
7. Cast iron inspection cover

**Diagram:**
```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Equipment Body] --> B[GI Earth Strip 25mm x 3mm]
    B --> C[Nut Bolt Connection at Top of Pipe]
    C --> D[GI Pipe 40mm dia 2.75 to 3m long]
    D --> E[Multiple Holes at Bottom for Water Seepage]
    D --> F[Tapered Cone at Bottom for Easy Driving]
    E --> G[Charcoal Salt Sand Layers]
    G --> H[General Mass of Earth]
    I[Inspection Chamber with Cover] --> C
    J[Water Pipe for Periodic Pouring] --> G
    
    style A fill:#2563eb,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#78716c,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#92400e,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
```

**Two Advantages of Pipe Earthing over Plate Earthing:**
1. **Easier installation** – can be driven into the ground by hammering; no need for deep excavation as in plate earthing.
2. **Lower resistance achievable** – longer contact length with soil; rods can be extended deeper.
3. **Better for rocky/compact soil** – rod can penetrate where a plate cannot.
4. **Less material, lower cost** – pipe requires less excavation and backfill.

**Part (b) – Numerical Calculation:**

**Given for Pipe:**
- d = 40 mm = 0.04 m
- L = 3 m
- ρ = 300 Ω·m

**Pipe Resistance Formula:**
$$R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$$

Substituting values:
$$R = \dfrac{300}{2\pi \times 3}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4 \times 3}{0.04}\right) - 1\right]$$

$$R = \dfrac{300}{18.849}\left[\ln(300) - 1\right]$$

$$R = 15.915 \times [5.7038 - 1]$$

$$R = 15.915 \times 4.7038$$

$$\boxed{R_{\text{pipe}} \approx 74.86 \;\; \Omega}$$

**For Rod to achieve same resistance (~74.86 Ω):**
- d = 6 mm = 0.006 m
- ρ = 300 Ω·m
- Use rod formula:
$$R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right] = 74.86 \;\; \Omega$$

Let **L = unknown**. Then:
$$\dfrac{300}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8L}{0.006}\right) - 1\right] = 74.86$$

$$\dfrac{300}{6.283 L}\left[\ln(1333.33\,L) - 1\right] = 74.86$$

$$\dfrac{300}{6.283 L} = 47.74 \quad \Rightarrow \quad L \approx 1.05 \;\; \text{m (initial guess)}$$

**Iterating by trial:**
- Try L = 1.5 m: R = (300 / 9.425) × [ln(2000) - 1] = 31.83 × [7.60 - 1] = 31.83 × 6.60 = 210 Ω
- Try L = 3 m: R = (300 / 18.85) × [ln(4000) - 1] = 15.92 × [8.29 - 1] = 15.92 × 7.29 = 116 Ω
- Try L = 4 m: R = (300 / 25.13) × [ln(5333) - 1] = 11.94 × [8.58 - 1] = 11.94 × 7.58 = 90.5 Ω
- Try L = 5 m: R = (300 / 31.42) × [ln(6667) - 1] = 9.55 × [8.80 - 1] = 9.55 × 7.80 = 74.49 Ω ✓

**Therefore: Length of rod required ≈ 5.0 m**

$$\boxed{L_{\text{rod}} \approx 5 \;\; \text{m}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:**
> - **Pipe vs Rod Formula**: Many students wrongly use the same formula for both! The correct formulas are:
>   - **Pipe**: $\ln(4L/d) - 1$
>   - **Rod**: $\ln(8L/d) - 1$
>   - The factor of 2 difference (4 vs 8) comes from different boundary conditions (pipe is hollow, rod is solid with no inner field).
> - **Iteration method**: For non-linear equations, **always show two or three iterations** to justify the answer.

---

|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Installation | Easy (driven) | Difficult (excavation) |
| Resistance obtained | Moderate | Lower (for same depth) |
| Best suited for | Distribution transformers | Power stations |
| Maintenance | Easy | Difficult |

✅ **KTU Exam Tip (Last-Minute Pointers):**
- Always draw a **neat labelled diagram** (4–5 marks weightage).
- Mention **IS code numbers** and **IEEE Std 80** wherever applicable.
- Show **derivation steps** clearly – not just the final formula.
- For numerical problems, write **Given → Formula → Substitution → Answer** in tabular form.
- Use **units** at every step – examiners love this.
- Remember: **Fault current is calculated at 3 × I₀** for line-to-ground faults, and decrement factor $D_f$ multiplies this.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Substation Lightning Protection (Theory Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Substation Lightning Protection (Theory Only)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Substation Lightning Protection?

**Lightning Protection of a Substation** is a coordinated engineering system designed to **intercept, conduct, and safely dissipate** the enormous electromagnetic energy of direct and indirect lightning strokes into the earth, while simultaneously shielding the substation equipment (transformers, busbars, switchgear, CTs, PTs, etc.) from destructive overvoltages.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (per IS/IEC 62305 & IEEE 998):**
> "A complete lightning protection scheme comprises an **external protection system** (air termination network, down conductors, and earth termination), an **internal protection system** (surge arresters/shields, equipotential bonding, and earthing grid), and **insulation coordination** — all designed to keep the impulse voltages and currents within the Basic Insulation Level (BIL) of the protected equipment."

### 1.2 The Two Enemies of a Substation

| Type of Lightning | Mechanism | Threat Level |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct Stroke** | Lightning directly hits the substation structure, busbar, or equipment | Catastrophic — full stroke current (typically 30 kA – 200 kA) flows through substation |
| **Indirect Stroke** | Lightning strikes the incoming/outgoing transmission line or a nearby ground point; a travelling surge wave enters the substation | Severe — high-frequency impulse wave (1.2/50 µs standard) with amplitude up to 1000 kV |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Substation as a Fortress

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Substation is a Medieval Castle, and Lightning is the Siege Catapult.**

Think of a substation as a **highly fortified medieval castle** protecting its most valuable treasure (the transformer and switchgear — worth crores of rupees). The castle has **three concentric defensive walls**, each with a specific job:

1. **Outer Wall (Shield Wires / Earth Screen / Air Termination)** — These are the **archers on the castle ramparts** and the **outermost thatched roof**. Their job is to *attract* the enemy's projectile (lightning) and intercept it **before** it enters the inner precincts. They are the *sacrificial layer* — designed to get hit.

2. **Middle Wall (Surge Arresters / Lightning Arresters)** — These are the **armored knights standing at the gates**. Even if a projectile slips past the outer wall, the knights (arresters) intercept it at the *entrances* of critical rooms (transformer terminals, line entries). They dump the surge to earth.

3. **Inner Wall (Earthing Grid & Equipotential Bonding)** — This is the **stone floor and drainage system** of the castle. Any leaked energy (fault current, residual surge) is harmlessly dispersed into the surrounding earth, with the **touch and step potentials** kept below human-tolerable limits (typically < 430 V touch, < 700 V step under IS 3043).

Without all three walls, the castle is vulnerable. If you only have shield wires but no arresters, the surge sneaks in through line entries. If you have arresters but a poor earth grid, the arrester *dumps energy back into the substation soil*, creating deadly step voltages for personnel.

## 1.4 Why Lightning Protection is Non-Negotiable

- **A single direct stroke carries 10⁶ to 2×10⁶ volts and 30,000 to 200,000 amperes.**
- **A 1 cm gap in air breaks down at ≈ 30 kV.** A substation busbar at 11 kV or 110 kV has insulation levels far below the lightning voltage.
- **Unprotected substations** suffer transformer winding failures, CT/PT insulation puncture, insulator flashover, control cable damage, and fire hazards.
- **Indian Standard IS 2309 / IEC 62305** mandates lightning protection for substations above 66 kV and recommended for all outdoor substations.

## 1.5 Key Terminology (Syllabus-Highlighted Terms)

> [!NOTE]
> - **BIL (Basic Insulation Level):** The peak value of the standardized 1.2/50 µs impulse voltage wave that the equipment insulation must withstand.
> - **Surge Arrester:** A nonlinear resistive device that limits overvoltages by conducting surge current to earth and resealing against follow current.
> - **Earth Screen / Shield Wire (OHGW):** Overhead grounded wire strung above the substation busbars to intercept direct strokes.
> - **Cone of Protection:** The conical volume of space beneath a vertical mast or shield wire that is statistically protected against direct strokes.
> - **Follow Current:** The power-frequency current (50 Hz) that flows through the arrester to ground *after* the surge has been diverted.
> - **Creepage Distance:** The shortest path along the surface of an insulator between two conductive parts; must be adequate to prevent surface flashover in polluted conditions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Lightning Phenomenon — Engineering Perspective

A lightning stroke is **NOT** a simple DC current. It is a complex transient event with the following standardized parameters (per IEEE Std 4 / IEC 60060-1):

| Parameter | Standard Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Peak current** $I_p$ | 30 kA (median), up to 200 kA (extreme) | Maximum amplitude of the return stroke |
| **Wave shape** | 1.2/50 µs | Front time / Tail time to half-value |
| **Stroke duration** | ≤ 500 µs (typical) | Time for the entire return stroke |
| **Rate of rise** | $di/dt$ | Up to 100 kA/µs (extreme) |
| **Charge** | 0.5 – 5 C (typical stroke) | Time-integrated current |
| **Flash density** | $N_g$ (strokes/km²/year) | Site-specific, from IS 2309 maps of India |

## 2.2 Components of a Complete Substation Lightning Protection System

### A. External Protection (Direct Stroke Shielding)

**1. Overhead Shield Wires (Earth Wires / OHGW — Overhead Ground Wires)**

- Strung **horizontally** above the substation busbars at heights greater than the equipment.
- Made of **galvanized stranded steel** (typically 7/3.15 mm or 7/4.00 mm) with high mechanical strength and adequate conductivity to carry the stroke current.
- Connected to the **earth grid** at multiple points via down-conductors.
- **Critical design rule:** The shield wire must be at a height such that the **protective angle** (typically 30° to 45° from vertical) covers all equipment below.

**2. Lightning Masts / Rods (Air Termination Masts)**

- Vertical galvanized steel masts (typically 50 mm GI pipe, 10–30 m tall).
- Provide **point-discharge** initiation of upward streamers, which attract the downward leader of the lightning.
- Used in substations where **shield wires are impractical** (e.g., small 11 kV substations, corners of large substations).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Electrogeometric Model (EGM) — Modern Protection Design:**
> Modern KTU and IS/IEC practice uses the **Electrogeometric Model** (developed by Young, Clayton, and Hileman) rather than the older "cone of protection" (Forte's rule). The EGM states that the striking distance $r_s$ is related to the prospective stroke current $I$ by:
>
> $$r_s = A \cdot I^{0.65}$$
>
> where $A = 8$ for 50% probability of interception. This gives a **striking distance of ≈ 50 m for a 30 kA stroke** and **≈ 200 m for a 200 kA stroke**. The implication: a tall mast with a small protection angle actually provides a *larger* protection radius for *higher* current strokes — counter-intuitively, small strokes are harder to attract.

### B. Internal Protection (Surge / Lightning Arresters)

**Lightning Arresters (Surge Arresters)** are the most critical *active* protection device. They are installed at:

- **Line entrances** (incoming/outgoing line terminations)
- **Transformer HV and LV bushings**
- **Bushings of HV switchgear (CBs, CTs, PTs)**
- **Interconnections between bus sections**

**Working Principle (Nonlinear V-I Characteristic):**

$$V = K \cdot I^{\beta}$$

where $\beta \ll 1$ (typically 0.02–0.05 for ZnO). This means:

- At **normal system voltage** → arrester is an **insulator** (only µA of leakage current).
- At **surge voltage** → arrester becomes a near-**short circuit**, dumping kiloamperes of surge current to earth.
- After the surge → arrester returns to its high-impedance state and blocks the 50 Hz follow current.

### C. Earth Termination System (Earthing Grid)

- A mesh of **buried GI/Cu conductors** (40 mm GI flat or 50 mm² Cu) at 0.5–1.0 m depth.
- **Earth electrodes** (rods, plates) at corners and intersections.
- **Target earth resistance:** ≤ 0.5 Ω for large EHV substations, ≤ 1 Ω for 33/11 kV, ≤ 2 Ω for LT panels.
- **Soil resistivity** $\rho$ is the key parameter (varies from 10 Ω·m in coastal clay to 10,000 Ω·m in rocky/dry soil).

## 2.3 Types of Lightning Arresters (Historical to Modern)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus requires knowledge of all major arrester types and their evolution.**

| Type | Construction | Active Element | Follow Current | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rod Gap** | Two rods with adjustable gap in air | Air | Not limited — gap must be self-clearing | Obsolete — used only as back-up |
| **Horn Gap** | Two horn-shaped electrodes with small series resistance | Air + resistor | Limited by resistor | Obsolete for HV |
| **Expulsion Type** | Arc-quenching chamber with fibre liner | Gas generation + arc | Self-extinguishes | Used on 11/33 kV lines |
| **Valve Type (SiC)** | Silicon carbide (SiC) blocks in series with spark gap | SiC nonlinear resistor | Limits to ≈ 100–300 A | Phased out |
| **Gapless Metal Oxide (ZnO)** | Zinc-oxide blocks directly across terminals, no series gap | ZnO varistor | < 1 mA (capacitive leakage) | **Modern standard** |
| **Series Gapped Metal Oxide** | ZnO blocks in series with a controlled gap | ZnO + gap | Very low | Used for very high voltages |

## 2.4 Selection of Arrester Rating

**Step 1: Determine the Maximum Continuous Operating Voltage (MCOV)**

$$\text{MCOV} \geq \frac{V_{LL} \times \text{TOV factor}}{\sqrt{3}}$$

For a 110 kV system: $\text{MCOV} = 110 / \sqrt{3} \times 1.05 \approx 78 \text{ kV (rms)}$.

**Step 2: Arrester Voltage Rating (Ur)**

$$U_r \geq 1.4 \times \text{MCOV}$$

(Per IEC 60099-4 for 10-second temporary overvoltage withstand.)

**Step 3: Discharge Capacity**

- Class 1 (Station class): 10 kA (8/20 µs) nominal discharge, 100 kA high-current impulse, 5 kA long-duration.
- Class 2 (Intermediate): 5 kA.
- Class 3 (Distribution): 5 kA.

**Step 4: Energy Absorption**

$$W = U_{res} \times I_{surge} \times t_{surge}$$

The arrester must absorb this energy without thermal runaway.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS FOR KTU EXAM — Memorize These.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_{rod} = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{a}\right)-1\right]$ | Earth resistance of a single vertical rod (length L, radius a) | Ω |
| 2 | $R_{plate} = \dfrac{\rho}{4r}$ | Earth resistance of a circular plate (radius r) | Ω |
| 3 | $R_{grid} \approx \dfrac{\rho}{4r_{eq}}$ | Approximate resistance of a square grid (equivalent radius $r_{eq}$) | Ω |
| 4 | $E_{touch} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot K_m \cdot I}{L_s}$ | Mesh (touch) voltage at substation surface | V |
| 5 | $E_{step} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot K_s \cdot I}{L_s}$ | Step voltage at substation surface | V |
| 6 | $K_m = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\left[\ln\left(\dfrac{D^2}{16 h d}+\dfrac{(D+2h)^2}{8 D d}-\dfrac{h}{4d}\right)+\dfrac{K_{ii}}{K_h}\ln\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi(2n-1)}\right)\right]$ | Mesh factor per IEEE 80 | — |
| 7 | $K_s = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left[\dfrac{1}{2h}+\dfrac{1}{D+h}+\dfrac{1}{D}\left(1-0.5^{n-2}\right)\right]$ | Step factor per IEEE 80 | — |
| 8 | $Z_{surge} = 60 \ln\left(\dfrac{2h}{r}\right)$ | Surge impedance of a single vertical conductor (h = height, r = radius) | Ω |
| 9 | $Z_{line} = 60 \ln\left(\dfrac{D_{avg}}{r}\right) + 30 \ln\left(\dfrac{2H_{avg}}{D_{avg}}\right)$ | Surge impedance of a single horizontal wire above ground (simplified) | Ω |
| 10 | $V_{refl} = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \cdot V_{inc}$ | Reflection coefficient for a travelling wave at discontinuity | — |
| 11 | $V_{trans} = \dfrac{2 Z_2}{Z_2 + Z_1} \cdot V_{inc}$ | Transmission coefficient for a travelling wave | — |
| 12 | $\text{Protective ratio} = \dfrac{\text{BIL}}{\text{Arrester residual voltage}}$ | Indicates margin of protection | — |
| 13 | $r_s = 8 \cdot I^{0.65}$ | Electrogeometric model striking distance (50% probability, A=8, I in kA) | m |
| 14 | $V_{cfo} = N \cdot d \cdot E_{cfo}$ | Critical flashover voltage of an insulator string (N units, gap d, gradient E) | kV |
| 15 | $W = \int V(t) \cdot I(t) \, dt$ | Energy absorbed by arrester during surge | kJ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility of these formulas:**
> - **Equations 4–7** are used by every practicing substation design engineer in India (per IS 3043 and IEEE Std 80) to design safe earthing grids for personnel safety.
> - **Equation 11** is the foundation of insulation coordination — explains why steep-fronted surges double at open-circuited transformer terminals.
> - **Equation 13** is the basis of IS/IEC 62305 lightning risk assessment for modern substations.

## 2.6 Insulation Coordination — The Bigger Picture

The **Goal of Insulation Coordination** is to ensure that the **protective characteristic of the arrester** lies *below* the **withstand characteristic of the equipment** at all relevant times, for all surge waveforms.

$$\text{Equipment BIL} > \text{Max arrester residual voltage} \times \text{Safety margin (typically 1.15–1.4)}$$

The 1.15 factor accounts for **separation distance** between the arrester and the protected equipment (each meter of lead adds roughly 1–2 kV of inductive voltage drop on a steep wave).

> [!WARNING]
> **The Steep-Front Problem:**
> For waves with rise time < 10 µs (typical of nearby direct strokes), the inductive voltage drop in the arrester lead $L$ becomes significant:
>
> $$V_{drop} = L \cdot \frac{di}{dt}$$
>
> A 1 m lead of 1 µH/m with $di/dt = 100$ kA/µs produces **100 kV of additional voltage** — which may *exceed* the BIL of the protected equipment. This is why arresters must be installed **as close as physically possible** to the transformer bushings (separation < 5 m for EHV).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Earth Resistance of a Vertical Rod

**Physical Setup:** A single vertical cylindrical rod of length $L$ and radius $a$ is driven vertically into soil of uniform resistivity $\rho$. We wish to find the resistance between the rod and a remote hemispherical shell of soil.

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry:**

Consider a thin hemispherical shell of soil at radius $r$ from the rod, of thickness $dr$. The current $I$ flows radially outward from the rod through the entire hemispherical equipotential shell.

**Step 2 — Write the resistance of a hemispherical shell:**

The current crosses the shell through a hemispherical area $A = 2\pi r^2$. The resistance of the shell is:

$$dR = \frac{\rho \cdot dr}{A} = \frac{\rho \, dr}{2\pi r^2}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from the rod surface to infinity:**

$$R = \int_{a}^{\infty} \frac{\rho \, dr}{2\pi r^2} = \frac{\rho}{2\pi} \left[-\frac{1}{r}\right]_{a}^{\infty} = \frac{\rho}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{1}{a} = \frac{\rho}{2\pi a}$$

This is the *ideal* formula. However, in practice, the current does not flow uniformly from the entire rod — most of the current crowds out from the **bottom tip** of the rod. An empirical correction due to Tagg gives:

$$\boxed{R_{rod} = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\left(\frac{4L}{a}\right)-1\right]}$$

**Step 4 — Real-world evaluation:**

For a 3 m rod, 25 mm radius, $\rho = 100 \, \Omega\cdot\text{m}$ (typical clay):

$$R_{rod} = \frac{100}{2\pi \times 3}\left[\ln\left(\frac{4 \times 3}{0.025}\right)-1\right]$$

$$= \frac{100}{18.85}\left[\ln(480) - 1\right] = 5.305 \times (6.173 - 1) = 5.305 \times 5.173$$

$$R_{rod} \approx 27.45 \, \Omega$$

This is still too high for a substation. Hence, **multiple rods in parallel** are used:

$$R_{n \, \text{rods}} = \frac{R_{rod}}{n \cdot \eta}$$

where $\eta$ is the **utilization factor** (typically 0.6–0.8 for rods spaced 1× their length apart). For 10 rods in parallel, $\eta = 0.7$:

$$R_{10} = \frac{27.45}{10 \times 0.7} = \frac{27.45}{7} \approx 3.92 \, \Omega$$

Adding **earthing compounds** (bentonite, charcoal, salt) or longer rods (e.g., 6 m) can further reduce this to < 1 Ω.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reflection & Transmission at a Transformer Termination

**Problem Setup:** A surge wave of amplitude $V_{inc}$ travelling on a line of surge impedance $Z_1$ arrives at the terminals of a transformer (which appears as an open circuit, $Z_2 \to \infty$). What is the voltage that appears across the transformer terminals?

**Step 1 — Boundary conditions:**

At the junction, by Kirchhoff's voltage law:

$$V_{inc} + V_{refl} = V_{trans}$$

By Kirchhoff's current law (continuity of current into the line):

$$\frac{V_{inc}}{Z_1} - \frac{V_{refl}}{Z_1} = \frac{V_{trans}}{Z_2}$$

**Step 2 — Solve the system:**

From the first equation: $V_{trans} = V_{inc} + V_{refl}$

Substitute into the second equation:

$$\frac{V_{inc}}{Z_1} - \frac{V_{refl}}{Z_1} = \frac{V_{inc} + V_{refl}}{Z_2}$$

Multiply through by $Z_1 Z_2$:

$$Z_2 V_{inc} - Z_2 V_{refl} = Z_1 V_{inc} + Z_1 V_{refl}$$

Collect $V_{refl}$ terms:

$$-Z_2 V_{refl} - Z_1 V_{refl} = Z_1 V_{inc} - Z_2 V_{inc}$$

$$V_{refl}(Z_1 + Z_2) = V_{inc}(Z_1 - Z_2)$$

$$\boxed{V_{refl} = \frac{Z_1 - Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2} \cdot V_{inc}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply to the transformer case ($Z_2 \to \infty$):**

$$\lim_{Z_2 \to \infty} V_{refl} = \lim_{Z_2 \to \infty} \frac{Z_1 - Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2} \cdot V_{inc} = \frac{-Z_2}{Z_2} \cdot V_{inc} = -V_{inc}$$

And:

$$V_{trans} = V_{inc} + V_{refl} = V_{inc} + (-V_{inc}) \cdot \text{(NO, recalculate)}$$

$$V_{trans} = V_{inc} + V_{refl} = V_{inc} - V_{inc} = 0 \quad \text{(this is wrong — let's re-evaluate)}$$

**Re-derivation with proper transmission coefficient:**

$$V_{trans} = \frac{2 Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2} \cdot V_{inc}$$

For $Z_2 \to \infty$:

$$V_{trans} = \frac{2 Z_2}{Z_2(1 + Z_1/Z_2)} \cdot V_{inc} \xrightarrow{Z_2 \to \infty} 2 V_{inc}$$

**Physical Result:** At an open-circuited transformer terminal, a travelling surge wave **doubles in voltage**. This is the famous "1+1 = 2" or **"doubling effect"** of lightning surges at open ends.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** A 500 kV incoming surge on a 110 kV line becomes **1000 kV** at the open-circuited transformer terminal. This is why surge arresters are **absolutely essential** at every transformer HV terminal — without them, the doubling effect would destroy the winding insulation.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mesh Voltage (Touch Voltage) Inside a Substation Grid

**IEEE 80 Formula for mesh voltage (simplified derivation):**

The **mesh voltage** $E_m$ is the maximum touch voltage a person experiences when standing at the center of a grid mesh and touching a grounded structure during a fault.

**Step 1 — Geometry:**

Consider a square grid mesh of side $D$ buried at depth $h$ in soil of resistivity $\rho$. The grid conductor has diameter $d$. The fault current entering the grid is $I$ (typically the worst-case single-line-to-ground fault current, or for lightning protection, the stroke current discharged to earth).

**Step 2 — Empirical formula per IEEE Std 80:**

$$E_{mesh} = \frac{\rho \cdot K_m \cdot K_i \cdot I_g}{L_s}$$

where:

- $K_m$ = mesh factor (depends on geometry)
- $K_i$ = irregularity factor (typically 1.0–1.3)
- $I_g$ = maximum grid current (in amperes)
- $L_s$ = total length of buried grid conductor (in metres)

**Step 3 — Simplified mesh factor:**

For a uniform square grid with $n$ parallel conductors per side:

$$K_m = \frac{1}{2\pi}\left[\ln\left(\frac{D^2}{16 h d} + \frac{(D+2h)^2}{8 D d} - \frac{h}{4d}\right) + \frac{K_{ii}}{K_h}\ln\left(\frac{8}{\pi(2n-1)}\right)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Worked numerical example:**

Suppose a 110 kV substation has:
- Grid area: 50 m × 50 m
- Grid depth: $h = 0.6$ m
- Conductor diameter: $d = 0.01$ m (10 mm)
- Mesh spacing: $D = 5$ m
- Number of parallel conductors: $n = 10$
- Soil resistivity: $\rho = 200 \, \Omega\cdot\text{m}$
- Maximum fault current: $I_g = 10{,}000$ A

**Calculate $K_m$:**

$$\frac{D^2}{16 h d} = \frac{25}{16 \times 0.6 \times 0.01} = \frac{25}{0.096} = 260.4$$

$$\frac{(D+2h)^2}{8 D d} = \frac{(5+1.2)^2}{8 \times 5 \times 0.01} = \frac{38.44}{0.4} = 96.1$$

$$\frac{h}{4d} = \frac{0.6}{0.04} = 15$$

$$\ln(260.4 + 96.1 - 15) = \ln(341.5) = 5.83$$

For the second term, with $K_{ii}/K_h \approx 1.0$ for typical grids:

$$\ln\left(\frac{8}{\pi(2 \times 10 - 1)}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{8}{59.69}\right) = \ln(0.134) = -2.01$$

$$K_m = \frac{1}{2\pi}[5.83 + 1.0 \times (-2.01)] = \frac{1}{6.283} \times 3.82 = 0.608$$

**Calculate $L_s$** (total conductor length for an 11 × 11 grid of conductors in a 50 × 50 m area, with 5 m spacing):

$$L_s = 11 \times 50 + 11 \times 50 = 1100 \, \text{m}$$

(11 conductors in each direction, 50 m long each, minus overlap; approximated.)

**Calculate $E_{mesh}$:**

$$E_{mesh} = \frac{200 \times 0.608 \times 1.0 \times 10000}{1100} = \frac{1{,}216{,}000}{1100} \approx 1105 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with tolerable touch voltage:**

Per IEEE 80, the tolerable touch voltage for a 50 kg body, 1 second fault clearance:

$$E_{touch,tol} = (1000 + 1.5 C_s \rho_s) \times \frac{0.116}{\sqrt{t_s}}$$

For a crushed rock surface layer of resistivity $\rho_s = 3000 \, \Omega\cdot\text{m}$, $C_s = 0.8$, $t_s = 0.5$ s:

$$E_{touch,tol} = (1000 + 1.5 \times 0.8 \times 3000) \times \frac{0.116}{\sqrt{0.5}}$$

$$= (1000 + 3600) \times \frac{0.116}{0.707} = 4600 \times 0.164 = 754 \, \text{V}$$

**Conclusion:** $E_{mesh} = 1105 \, \text{V} > E_{touch,tol} = 754 \, \text{V}$ → **NOT SAFE**. The grid must be redesigned: increase $L_s$ (finer mesh), increase surface layer resistivity, or reduce $I_g$ (current splitting).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often use the *raw* body weight formula or forget the **crushed-rock surface layer** correction. IS 3043 mandates a 100–150 mm thick layer of wet crushed rock (resistivity 2000–5000 Ω·m) on top of the substation floor — this single measure increases tolerable touch voltage by 4–6× and is the most cost-effective safety intervention.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Arresters

| Property | Rod Gap | Horn Gap | Expulsion | SiC Valve | **ZnO (Gapless)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Operating principle** | Air breakdown | Air + arc elongation | Gas blast | SiC nonlinearity | **ZnO varistor** |
| **Residual voltage (5 kA)** | N/A (no limiting) | High | High | Medium | **Low** |
| **Follow current** | Uncontrolled | Limited by R | Self-cleared | 100–300 A | **< 1 mA** |
| **Maintenance** | Frequent | Frequent | Periodic | Moderate | **Almost nil** |
| **Service life** | Months | Months | 1–3 years | 5–10 years | **> 25 years** |
| **Pollution performance** | Poor | Poor | Poor | Moderate | **Excellent** |
| **Cost (per kV)** | Very low | Low | Low | Medium | **High (but declining)** |
| **Modern use** | Back-up only | Almost extinct | Rural 11 kV | Phased out | **Standard** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Substation Lightning Protection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EXT[External Protection Layer]
        direction TB
        CLOUD[Atmospheric Charge Cloud]
        LH[Lightning Leader Channel]
        SW[Overhead Shield Wires / Earth Screen]
        LM[Lightning Masts at Corners]
        SW -->|intercepts stroke| LH
        LM -->|intercepts stroke| LH
    end

    subgraph INTC[Internal Protection Layer]
        direction TB
        LN1[Incoming Line 110 kV]
        SA1[Surge Arrester at Line Entry]
        BUS[Busbar 110 kV]
        CB[Circuit Breaker]
        TR[Power Transformer HV Bushing]
        SALV[Surge Arrester at Transformer LV]
        LN1 --> SA1
        SA1 --> BUS
        BUS --> CB
        CB --> TR
        TR --> SALV
    end

    subgraph GRD[Earth Termination Layer]
        direction TB
        DC[Down Conductors from Masts and Shields]
        EG[Buried Earth Grid Mesh]
        ER[Earth Rods at Intersections]
        SK[Soil Crushed Rock Surface Layer]
        DC --> EG
        EG --> ER
    end

    LH -->|stroke current diverted| DC
    SA1 -->|clamp surges to earth| DC
    SALV -->|clamp surges to earth| DC
    SK -->|personnel safety via touch step control| EG
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Surge Arrester Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Normal Condition 110 kV system voltage] -->|leakage current approx 1 mA| B[ZnO blocks in HIGH impedance state]
    B --> C[Surge arrives steep front]
    C --> D[ZnO blocks transition to LOW impedance in approx 1 ns]
    D --> E[Surge current 5 kA to 10 kA flows to earth]
    E --> F[Surge passes, system voltage returns]
    F --> G[ZnO blocks return to HIGH impedance state]
    G --> H[Follow current approx 1 mA]
    H --> I[Steady state normal operation]
    I -->|repeat cycle| A
```

```mermaid
        direction TB
        MA[Lightning Mast A NW corner height 25 m]

    end

    MA --- SW1
    MB --- SW1
    MC --- SW2
    MD --- SW2
    TR1 --> BB1
    TR2 --> BB1
    TR1 --> BB2
    TR2 --> BB3
    AX --> BB1
    AY --> BB2

    MB --> EG
    MC --> EG
    MD --> EG
    TR1 --> EG
    TR2 --> EG
    AX --> EG
    AY --> EG
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Selection of Arrester Type

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START[Choose Lightning Arrester for Substation] --> Q1{System Voltage Level}
    Q1 -->|11 kV or 33 kV| A[Distribution Class ZnO]
    Q1 -->|66 kV or 110 kV| B[Station Class ZnO]
    Q1 -->|220 kV and above| C[Station Class Heavy Duty ZnO]
    A --> Q2{Need back up protection}
    Q2 -->|Yes| D[Add Rod Gap in parallel]
    Q2 -->|No| A1[Final Selection Distribution Class ZnO]
    B --> Q3{High energy TOV risk}
    Q3 -->|Yes| E[Use Series Gapped ZnO]
    Q3 -->|No| F[Use Gapless ZnO with thermal disconnector]
    C --> Q4{Contaminated coastal site}
    Q4 -->|Yes| G[Use Polymer Housed Heavy Duty ZnO]
    Q4 -->|No| H[Use Porcelain Housed Heavy Duty ZnO]

    E --> A1
    F --> A1
    G --> A1

```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modeled strictly on the **KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech** assessment pattern. Part A carries 3 marks each (no choice). Part B carries 14 marks each with **internal choice** between two questions of equal cognitive demand. Bloom's Levels: L1–L2 (Remember/Understand), L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyze), L5 (Evaluate).

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Explain the function of an overhead shield wire in a substation. How is its protective zone determined?"** [3 Marks | L2]

**Model Answer:**

An **overhead shield wire (OHGW)** is a grounded conductor strung horizontally above the substation busbars and equipment at a height greater than the equipment. Its function is to **intercept direct lightning strokes** that would otherwise strike the energized busbars or transformer terminals, and to provide a low-impedance path for the stroke current to flow to the earth grid via down-conductors.

The **protective zone** of a shield wire is determined by the **Electrogeometric Model (EGM)** in modern practice, where the striking distance $r_s$ is given by $r_s = 8 \cdot I^{0.65}$ (for 50% probability, $I$ in kA, $r_s$ in metres). For a 30 kA stroke, $r_s \approx 50$ m, which means a single shield wire at 15 m height with sag of 2 m provides protection over a horizontal span of $2\sqrt{r_s^2 - (h-r)^2}$. The older "cone of protection" method using a 45° protective angle is conservative and still acceptable for small substations (per IS 2309).

[Examiner's note: 1 mark for function, 1 mark for protective zone principle, 1 mark for EGM/cone method — total 3 marks.]

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between a surge arrester and a lightning arrester. Why are gapless metal-oxide arresters preferred for HV substations?"** [3 Marks | L3]

**Model Answer:**

**Surge arrester** is a general term for a device that limits any *abnormal overvoltage* (switching surge, lightning surge, or temporary overvoltage). A **lightning arrester** is a more specific term, historically used for the older gapped arresters designed primarily for direct lightning protection. In modern parlance, both terms refer to the same device — the **Metal-Oxide Surge Arrester (MOSA)**.

**Gapless ZnO arresters** are preferred for HV substations because:

1. **No series spark gap** → the response time is reduced from microseconds to nanoseconds, providing faster surge clamping.
2. **Negligible follow current (< 1 mA)** → the arrester can be connected across the equipment continuously without a need for a gap to extinguish the power-frequency follow current.
3. **Excellent V-I nonlinearity** ($\beta \approx 0.025$ for ZnO) → very low residual voltage at high discharge currents.
4. **Maintenance-free** operation, with service life exceeding 25 years.
5. **Better pollution performance** — the housing is typically polymer silicone rubber, which sheds water and resists surface flashover in coastal/industrial environments.

[Examiner's note: 1 mark for distinction, 2 marks for reasons ZnO is preferred.]

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]

**"Design the external lightning protection system for a 110/33 kV substation. The substation layout is 60 m × 40 m. The site has an isokeraunic level of 60 thunderstorm days/year. Discuss the selection of shield wires, masts, and down-conductors. Calculate the required earth resistance of the grid for a tolerable step voltage of 700 V."** [14 Marks | L4/L5]

**Sub-parts:**

**(a) Selection of Shield Wires and Masts. [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Determine ground flash density:**

The isokeraunic level $T = 60$ days/year corresponds to a ground flash density (per IS 2309 / IEEE 998):

$$N_g = 0.04 \cdot T^{1.25} = 0.04 \times 60^{1.25} = 0.04 \times 153.0 \approx 6.12 \text{ strokes/km}^2/\text{year}$$

**Step 2 — Determine the maximum expected stroke current:**

For a 99% protection level (only 1% of strokes exceed this value), the median stroke current $I_m = 31$ kA and the standard deviation in log-normal distribution is $\sigma = 0.484$:

$$I_{max} = I_m \cdot e^{2.33 \sigma} = 31 \times e^{2.33 \times 0.484} = 31 \times e^{1.128} = 31 \times 3.09 \approx 95.8 \text{ kA}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate striking distance using EGM:**

$$r_s = 8 \cdot I_{max}^{0.65} = 8 \times 95.8^{0.65} = 8 \times 22.1 \approx 177 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Determine mast height:**

For a four-corner mast arrangement with corner masts only, the diagonal of the 60 m × 40 m substation is $\sqrt{60^2 + 40^2} = \sqrt{5200} = 72.1$ m. To protect the center of the substation, each mast must be at a height $H$ such that:

$$\sqrt{r_s^2 - (d/2)^2} \leq H$$

where $d = 72.1$ m. However, since $r_s = 177$ m $\gg 72.1$ m, a mast of modest height suffices.

**Selection:** Use **4 corner masts of 22 m height** (GI pipe 150 mm NB, 6 mm thick) interconnected by **shield wires** at 18 m height. The shield wire provides intermediate protection over the busbars between masts.

**Step 5 — Down-conductors:**

Use **50 × 6 mm GI flat** down-conductors from each mast, one per mast, with a test link at 1.5 m above ground for periodic earth resistance measurement.

**(b) Earth Grid Resistance Calculation. [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Determine tolerable step voltage:**

Given: $E_{step,tol} = 700$ V. From IEEE 80:

$$E_{step,tol} = (1000 + 6 C_s \rho_s) \cdot \frac{0.116}{\sqrt{t_s}}$$

For a body weight 70 kg, surface material of resistivity $\rho_s = 3000 \, \Omega\cdot\text{m}$, $C_s = 0.8$, $t_s = 0.5$ s:

$$700 = (1000 + 6 \times 0.8 \times 3000) \cdot \frac{0.116}{\sqrt{0.5}}$$

$$700 = 15400 \times 0.164 = 2526 \text{ V (tolerable)}$$

Since 2526 V > 700 V, the surface layer provides adequate margin. ✓

**Step 2 — Calculate required grid resistance:**

For a fault/stroke current of $I = 10{,}000$ A flowing to earth, the maximum permissible earth resistance $R$ such that the potential rise (GPR) does not exceed safe limits is given by:

$$V_{GPR} = I \times R$$

For a tolerable touch voltage of 430 V and assuming the worst case where the person is at the corner of the grid (where grid potential equals GPR):

$$R \leq \frac{V_{GPR,max}}{I} = \frac{V_{touch,tol} + V_{drop}}{I}$$

Using $V_{touch,tol} = 430$ V, $V_{drop}$ (foot-to-earth, negligible if surface layer present) $\approx 0$:

$$R \leq \frac{430}{10000} = 0.043 \, \Omega$$

This is extremely low. In practice, a target of **$R \leq 0.5 \, \Omega$** is set for EHV substations, achieved using:

- A **buried grid** of 50 × 6 mm GI flat at 0.6 m depth
- **30 earth rods** of 3 m length, 25 mm diameter, at grid intersections
- **Chemical earthing** with bentonite/copper compound

**Step 3 — Final grid design:**

- Grid area: 60 m × 40 m = 2400 m²
- Conductor: 50 × 6 mm GI flat
- Mesh size: 5 m × 5 m → 12 × 8 = 96 meshes
- Total conductor length: $L_s = 13 \times 40 + 9 \times 60 = 520 + 540 = 1060$ m
- Earth rods: 30 rods of 3 m length at outer perimeter intersections

**Final Required Earth Resistance: $R \leq 0.5 \, \Omega$.**

[Examiner's note: 7 marks for (a), 7 marks for (b). Marks allocated: 2 for ground flash density, 2 for EGM striking distance, 2 for mast selection, 1 for down-conductor spec; 2 for tolerable voltage check, 2 for resistance calculation, 2 for grid spec, 1 for final answer.]

---

### Question B (Option 2 — Internal Choice) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**"Explain with neat sketches the working of a metal-oxide surge arrester. With reference to its V-I characteristic, derive the protective ratio. A 132 kV substation has equipment with a BIL of 650 kVp. The arrester's residual voltage at 10 kA discharge current is 380 kVp. Calculate the protective ratio and the safety margin offered by the arrester."** [14 Marks | L3/L4]

**Sub-parts:**

**(a) Construction and Working of a ZnO Surge Arrester with V-I Characteristic. [7 Marks]**

**Construction:**

A modern gapless metal-oxide surge arrester consists of the following stacked elements inside a sealed porcelain or polymer housing:

1. **ZnO varistor blocks** — cylindrical discs of zinc-oxide ceramic doped with bismuth, cobalt, manganese, and antimony oxides. Typical disc dimensions: 50 mm diameter × 25 mm thick, rated at 3–5 kV per disc.
2. **Top and bottom metal end fittings** — aluminium or brass terminals with bolted connections to the line and earth.
3. **Silicone rubber polymer housing** (modern) or porcelain housing (older) — provides weather protection, mechanical strength, and external insulation.
4. **Pressure relief diaphragm** — vents internal hot gases in the event of arrester failure, preventing explosive rupture.
5. **Spring-loaded contacts** — maintain uniform pressure on the varistor stack to prevent partial discharge.

**Working Principle:**

Under **normal operating voltage** (line-to-ground), the ZnO blocks have a very high impedance, allowing only a capacitive leakage current of < 1 mA to flow.

When a **surge voltage** (from lightning or switching) arrives, the voltage across the ZnO blocks exceeds the **reference voltage** (knee point) of the V-I characteristic, and the blocks switch to a **low-impedance state** within **< 100 nanoseconds**, conducting kiloamperes of surge current to earth.

After the surge passes, the system voltage drops below the arrester's **continuous operating voltage**, and the ZnO blocks return to their high-impedance state, blocking any 50 Hz follow current.

**V-I Characteristic (Nonlinear):**

$$I = K \cdot V^{\alpha}$$

where $\alpha$ (the nonlinearity exponent) is approximately 25–50 for ZnO. This means for every doubling of voltage, the current increases by $2^{\alpha} \approx 3 \times 10^7$ times — making the V-I curve nearly **L-shaped** on a log-log plot.

**Three Regions of the Curve:**

- **Region 1 (Low field):** Below the **knee voltage** $V_{ref}$ — leakage current is capacitive, in microamperes.
- **Region 2 (Intermediate):** Near the knee — transition region, microamperes to milliamperes.
- **Region 3 (High field / Up-turn):** Above the reference voltage — high conduction, surge currents of kiloamperes flow with only modest voltage increase.

**(b) Protective Ratio Calculation. [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- Equipment BIL = 650 kVp (1.2/50 µs wave)
- Arrester residual voltage at 10 kA = 380 kVp

**Step 1 — Apply the safety margin for separation distance:**

For a separation distance of 5 m between arrester and protected equipment, the inductive voltage drop across the leads is:

$$V_{drop} = L \cdot \frac{di}{dt}$$

For a 1.2/50 µs wave with 10 kA peak: $di/dt = 10000 / (1.2 \times 10^{-6}) = 8.33 \times 10^{9}$ A/s. With $L = 5 \text{ m} \times 1 \text{ µH/m} = 5 \text{ µH}$:

$$V_{drop} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8.33 \times 10^{9} = 41{,}667 \text{ V} \approx 42 \text{ kV}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the effective voltage at the protected equipment:**

$$V_{eff} = V_{residual} + V_{drop} = 380 + 42 = 422 \text{ kVp}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the protective ratio:**

$$\text{Protective Ratio} = \frac{\text{BIL}}{V_{residual}} = \frac{650}{380} \approx 1.71$$

Or, including the lead separation effect:

$$\text{Effective Protective Ratio} = \frac{\text{BIL}}{V_{eff}} = \frac{650}{422} \approx 1.54$$

**Step 4 — Safety Margin:**

$$\text{Safety Margin (\%)} = \left(\frac{\text{BIL}}{V_{residual}} - 1\right) \times 100\% = (1.71 - 1) \times 100\% = 71\%$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

A safety margin of 71% (without considering lead drop) or 54% (with lead drop) is **acceptable** per IEC 60099-4, which recommends a minimum margin of 20–40% depending on the system voltage. Therefore, this arrester is **adequately rated** for the 132 kV substation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Many students lose marks in the following ways:
> 1. **Confusing BIL with BSL or SIL** — BIL is the *lightning impulse* level; BSL is the *basic switching impulse* level (different wave shape: 250/2500 µs).
> 2. **Forgetting the lead separation effect** — for substations with long lead lengths between arrester and transformer, the safety margin is significantly reduced.
> 3. **Using rms instead of peak values** — the BIL is always in **kVp (peak)**, not kV rms.
> 4. **Forgetting units in the protective ratio** — it is dimensionless.
> 5. **Not specifying the wave shape** when comparing BIL to arrester residual voltage — they must be for the same wave (1.2/50 µs for lightning, 8/20 µs for discharge current).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

### Core Concepts (Must Memorize)

- ☐ Lightning is a transient with standardized **1.2/50 µs** voltage wave and **8/20 µs** current wave.
- ☐ Substation lightning protection has **3 layers**: external (shields/masts), internal (arresters), and earth termination (grid).
- ☐ The **Electrogeometric Model (EGM)** $r_s = 8 I^{0.65}$ is the modern method (replacing the 45° cone of protection).
- ☐ The **doubling effect** at open-circuited transformer terminals: $V_{trans} = 2 V_{inc}$ when $Z_2 \to \infty$.
- ☐ **Earth resistance target**: ≤ 0.5 Ω for EHV, ≤ 1 Ω for HV, ≤ 2 Ω for distribution substations.

### Key Devices (Must Be Able to Draw & Explain)

- ☐ **ZnO gapless arrester** construction (varistor blocks, housing, pressure relief).
- ☐ **V-I characteristic** with three regions (low-field, knee, high-field up-turn).
- ☐ **Earthing grid layout** with mesh pattern, rods at corners, test links.

### Critical Numerical Must-Knows

- ☐ Tagg's rod resistance formula: $R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln(4L/a) - 1\right]$.
- ☐ IEEE 80 mesh and step voltage formulas with $K_m$ and $K_s$ factors.
- ☐ Reflection/transmission coefficients at line discontinuities.
- ☐ Ground flash density from isokeraunic level: $N_g = 0.04 T^{1.25}$.
- ☐ Protective ratio and safety margin calculation with lead drop.

### KTU-Examiner-Traps (Pitfalls)

- ☐ Don't confuse **MCOV** (Maximum Continuous Operating Voltage) with the arrester **voltage rating** — they are different by a factor of ~1.4.
- ☐ The "**cone of protection**" is **NOT** a rigorous modern design method — always mention EGM for full marks.
- ☐ For **ZnO arresters**, the **follow current is negligible** — this is the key advantage, not just "low residual voltage."
- ☐ **Crushed rock surface layer** is mandatory for substation personnel safety — never omit it from designs.

### Standards to Reference

- ☐ **IS 2309**: Code of practice for protection of buildings and allied structures against lightning.
- ☐ **IS 3043**: Code of practice for earthing.
- ☐ **IEC 60099-4**: Metal-oxide surge arresters without gaps for AC systems.
- ☐ **IEC 62305**: Protection against lightning (international).
- ☐ **IEEE Std 80**: Guide for safety in AC substation grounding.
- ☐ **IEEE Std 998**: Guide for direct lightning stroke shielding of substations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar PV Power Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET602 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN AND ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Solar PV Power Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Solar Photovoltaic (PV) Power Generation** is the direct conversion of sunlight (solar irradiance) into electrical energy using semiconductor devices called **solar cells**, which exhibit the **Photovoltaic Effect** — the generation of a voltage and electric current in a material upon exposure to light. The basic building block is the **PV cell** (typically a silicon p-n junction diode); multiple cells are encapsulated into a **PV module** (panel), and several modules connected in series and/or parallel form a **PV array** capable of producing usable DC power ranging from a few watts (calculators) to several megawatts (utility-scale solar farms).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 4 (Solar PV Power Generation):**
> The syllabus expects students to clearly understand the PV cell working principle, the I–V and P–V characteristics, fill factor, efficiency, the role of **MPPT**, the difference between **Standalone (Off-grid)**, **Grid-Connected**, and **Hybrid** PV systems, and the basic design philosophy of array sizing, inverter selection, and battery bank sizing for an electrical installation project.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **solar cell as a one-way escalator for electrons**. Sunlight (photons) is the "push" that gives electrons in the silicon crystal enough energy to climb from the lower energy level (valence band) to a higher level (conduction band). The built-in electric field at the p-n junction acts as a slanted ramp: once the electrons climb up, the field immediately pushes them down the ramp in only one direction, sweeping them to the external circuit as **direct current (DC)** electricity.

A single cell is like a single water droplet — useful to understand the principle, but practically invisible. To get useful power, we bundle **cells into a module (panel)**, modules into an **array (a field of panels)**, and then use power electronic converters (MPPT charge controllers, inverters) and energy storage (batteries) to transform this raw DC into clean, usable AC for our homes, offices, and industrial loads.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for quick recall):**
> - **Solar Constant (I₀) ≈ 1361 W/m²** — the extraterrestrial irradiance perpendicular to the sun's rays outside Earth's atmosphere.
> - **Standard Test Conditions (STC)**: **1000 W/m²** irradiance, **25 °C** cell temperature, **AM 1.5** air mass spectrum.
> - **1 kWp** of a PV system under STC produces **1 kWh** of energy per peak sun hour.

### 1.3 Why Solar PV Matters in Electrical System Design

For an electrical design engineer, solar PV is no longer "alternative energy" — it is mainstream generation. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly embeds PV in design and estimation because:

- **Net-metered rooftop PV systems** require correct sizing of array, inverter, and protection gear.
- **Hybrid systems** (PV + battery + DG) need careful load analysis and energy yield estimation.
- **Standalone systems** (remote agricultural pumps, telecom towers, village microgrids) need battery bank sizing, autonomy days, and depth-of-discharge (DoD) calculations.
- **Electrical estimation** for PV projects involves module count, BOS (Balance of System) costs, cable sizing, and earthing — all of which fall under this module.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Photovoltaic Effect — Step-by-Step Physics

1. **Photon Absorption**: A photon with energy $E_{ph} = h \nu$ greater than the band-gap energy $E_g$ of the semiconductor (e.g., $E_g \approx 1.12$ eV for crystalline silicon) is absorbed in the depletion region or quasi-neutral region of the p-n junction.
2. **Electron–Hole Pair Generation**: The absorbed photon excites an electron from the valence band to the conduction band, creating a free electron–hole pair.
3. **Charge Separation**: The built-in electric field at the junction sweeps electrons toward the n-side and holes toward the p-side.
4. **Collection at Terminals**: If an external load is connected, the separated charges flow through it, producing a **photo-current** $I_{ph}$ proportional to the incident irradiance $G$.

### 2.2 Equivalent Circuit of a Practical PV Cell

A practical PV cell is modelled as:

- A **current source** ($I_{ph}$) proportional to irradiance $G$ in parallel with
- A **diode** (representing the p-n junction dark current $I_D$),
- A **series resistance** $r_s$ (resistive losses in contacts, semiconductor bulk), and
- A **shunt (parallel) resistance** $r_{sh}$ (leakage paths at edges, manufacturing defects).

The governing I–V equation is:

$$
I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V + I r_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + I r_s}{r_{sh}}
$$

where $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q}$ is the thermal voltage (~25.85 mV at 300 K), $n$ is the diode ideality factor (1–2), $I_0$ is the reverse saturation current, and $I$ is the terminal current.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The series resistance $r_s$ mainly reduces the **fill factor (FF)** and the open-circuit voltage slightly. The shunt resistance $r_{sh}$ mainly reduces the **short-circuit current** and the **slope of the I–V curve near $V_{oc}$**. A high-quality cell has very low $r_s$ and very high $r_{sh}$.

### 2.3 I–V and P–V Characteristics — What the Graphs Tell Us

On the **I–V curve**:
- **Short-circuit current $I_{sc}$** — current when terminals are shorted ($V = 0$); nearly linearly proportional to irradiance.
- **Open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$** — voltage when terminals are open ($I = 0$); logarithmically dependent on irradiance, decreases with temperature.

On the **P–V curve** (product $P = V \times I$):
- Power rises, reaches a **Maximum Power Point (MPP)** at $(V_{mp}, I_{mp})$, then falls sharply to zero at $V_{oc}$.
- The MPP shifts with **irradiance** (proportionally) and **temperature** (inversely — hotter cell → lower $V_{mp}$).

### 2.4 Fill Factor (FF) and Conversion Efficiency ($\eta$)

These two parameters define the "quality" of a PV cell/module.

- **Fill Factor** is the "squareness" of the I–V curve:
$$
FF = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}
$$

- **Cell/Module Efficiency** is the ratio of maximum electrical power output to the incident solar power on the active area:
$$
\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{A \cdot G} = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{A \cdot G} = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{A \cdot G}
$$

where $A$ is the module area in m² and $G$ is the incident irradiance in W/m².

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical values you MUST memorize for KTU exams:**
> - $FF$ of a good crystalline silicon cell: **0.70 – 0.82**
> - $\eta$ of monocrystalline Si cell: **18 – 22%**
> - $\eta$ of polycrystalline Si cell: **15 – 18%**
> - $\eta$ of thin-film (CdTe / CIGS / a-Si): **10 – 14%**
> - $\eta$ of commercial module (encapsulated): **2 – 4% lower** than the bare cell efficiency.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Photon energy | $E = h \nu = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda (\text{nm})}$ | eV | $\lambda$ in nm, $h$ = Planck's constant |
| 2 | Thermal voltage | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85$ mV | V | At 300 K (T = 27 °C) |
| 3 | Open-circuit voltage | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{n k T}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | V | Increases with $G$, decreases with T |
| 4 | Short-circuit current | $I_{sc} \approx I_{ph} \propto G$ | A | Linearly proportional to irradiance |
| 5 | Fill Factor | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$ | dimensionless | Higher is better (0.7–0.82) |
| 6 | Cell/Module efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc} I_{sc} FF}{A \cdot G}$ | % | Under STC: $G = 1000$ W/m² |
| 7 | Daily energy from PV | $E_{PV} = P_{peak} \times PSH \times \eta_{system}$ | Wh/day | $PSH$ = Peak Sun Hours |
| 8 | Number of modules (energy basis) | $N = \dfrac{E_{load}}{\eta_{sys} \cdot P_{module,STC} \cdot PSH}$ | integer | Round-up to next whole number |
| 9 | Modules in series (string) | $N_s = \dfrac{V_{DC,bus}}{V_{module}}$ | integer | Match inverter MPPT window |
| 10 | Modules in parallel (strings) | $N_p = \dfrac{N}{N_s}$ | integer | Match inverter max input current |
| 11 | Battery capacity (Ah) | $C = \dfrac{E_{load} \times D}{V_{bus} \times DoD \times \eta_{bat}}$ | Ah | $D$ = autonomy days |
| 12 | Inverter rating | $P_{inv} = \dfrac{P_{PV,peak}}{1.25}$ (typical) | kW | 1.25 = safety/oversizing factor |
| 13 | Charge controller rating | $I_{ctrl} = 1.25 \times I_{sc,array}$ | A | 25% safety margin |
| 14 | Temperature-corrected $V_{oc}$ | $V_{oc}(T) = V_{oc,STC} \big[1 + \beta(T_{min} - 25)\big]$ | V | $\beta$ in V/°C (negative) |
| 15 | Temperature-corrected $P_{max}$ | $P_{max}(T) = P_{max,STC}\big[1 + \gamma(T_{cell} - 25)\big]$ | W | $\gamma \approx -0.4$ %/°C for c-Si |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Recall — System Efficiencies used in estimation:**
> - Module derating (soiling, mismatch, wiring): ~**0.90**
> - Battery round-trip efficiency (lead-acid): ~**0.80 – 0.85**
> - Inverter efficiency: ~**0.95 – 0.98**
> - Overall system derating factor: ~**0.75 – 0.85**

### 2.6 Types of PV Cells / Modules

- **Monocrystalline Silicon (mono c-Si)**: Single-crystal silicon, high efficiency (18–22%), uniform dark colour, expensive.
- **Polycrystalline Silicon (multi c-Si)**: Cast multi-crystal ingot, blue speckled look, 15–18% efficiency, lower cost.
- **Thin-Film**:
    * **Amorphous Silicon (a-Si)**: Flexible, low cost, ~6–9% efficiency, suffers from Staebler–Wronski degradation.
    * **Cadmium Telluride (CdTe)**: ~10–12% module efficiency, dominant in utility-scale (e.g., First Solar).
    * **Copper Indium Gallium Selenide (CIGS)**: ~12–14% efficiency, good in low-light.
- **Emerging**:
    * **Mono PERC / TOPCon / HJT / Bifacial** — passivated contacts, bifacial gain adds 5–25% extra energy.
    * **Perovskite tandems** — lab efficiencies > 33%, not yet commercial at scale.

### 2.7 Types of PV Power Systems

| System Type | Battery Storage | Grid Connection | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Standalone (Off-grid)** | Yes (essential) | No | Remote homes, telecom, water pumps, rural electrification |
| **Grid-Connected (On-grid)** | No (optional) | Yes (utility) | Rooftop solar, solar farms, net-metering |
| **Hybrid (PV + DG + Battery)** | Yes | Optional | Commercial buildings, islands, microgrids |
| **Pumped-storage / Solar-thermal** | Mechanical | Yes | Utility-scale peak shaving |

### 2.8 MPPT — Maximum Power Point Tracking

Since the MPP shifts continuously with irradiance, temperature, and load, an electronic **MPPT controller** forces the PV array to operate at its instantaneous $V_{mp}$ by adjusting the duty cycle of a DC–DC converter (typically buck, boost, or buck-boost topology).

Common MPPT algorithms:
- **Perturb & Observe (P&O)**: Periodically perturbs $V$ and observes $\Delta P$; simple, oscillates around MPP.
- **Incremental Conductance (IncCond)**: Compares $dI/dV$ with $-I/V$; more accurate at steady state.
- **Fractional Open-Circuit Voltage**: Approximates $V_{mp} \approx k \cdot V_{oc}$ with $k \approx 0.7–0.8$; cheap, no sensors.
- **Fuzzy Logic / Neural Network / PSO**: AI-based, used in research-grade systems.

> [!TIP]
> **Why MPPT matters:** Without MPPT, a PV array may deliver 20–40% less energy. With MPPT, you can recover this loss and dramatically improve ROI — this is **high-yield for KTU 14-mark questions**.

### 2.9 Real-World Utility of Solar PV

- **Utility-scale solar farms** (10 MW – 2 GW): Connected via step-up transformers to EHV grid; central inverters.
- **Rooftop solar** (1 kW – 500 kW): Behind-the-meter; net metering.
- **Solar water pumps** (0.5 HP – 10 HP): Replace diesel pumps in agriculture; PM-KUSUM scheme in India.
- **Building-Integrated PV (BIPV)**: PV glass façades, rooftops, parking canopies.
- **Floating solar (Floatovoltaics)**: On reservoir surfaces; cooler modules → higher efficiency.
- **Space applications**: Highest-efficiency multi-junction cells (30–40%) for satellites.
